question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's Peatland Strategy.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Peatland Strategy was published in September 2025 and has a commitment to publish a Delivery Plan and reporting framework.  The first Delivery Plan, covering the period April 2026 to December 2027 was published on 22 April and sets 14 short-term targets to help deliver peatland conservation, restoration and management during this period.Subsequent Peatland Delivery Plans will cover a five year time period to align with the NI Climate Action Plan cycle.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how the re-classified £15.8 million for the community and voluntary sector will be spent, following the introduction of the Local Growth Fund and imposed 70-30 capital-revenue split.
answer_text: The Resource / Capital split for the Local Growth Fund was determined by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government and the Executive has no power to amend it. Decisions on how funding will be allocated have not yet been made. The NIO will shortly commence a public consultation on the proposed use of the Local Growth Fund, which will inform future decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the proportion of applications for Legal Aid related to Housing Executive damp and mould cases that have been unsuccessful in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency NI records and administers legal aid cases on the basis of specific Natures and Matters. It is not possible to identify or extract cases associated with terms such as “damp and mould” from the legal aid case management system. This is due to the way data are formatted and the absence of any such bespoke categorisations within the database. As a result, attempting to retrieve this information would require a manual, case-by-case review of all supporting documents associated with non-granted applications over the past five years.Accordingly, it is not possible to detail the proportion of applications for legal aid that have been unsuccessful exclusively in these types of cases.  However, the table below sets out cases in which applicants sought to bring proceedings against the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (for any matter), in each of the last five years:2021/222022/232023/242024/252025/26Applications Granted4869466855Applications Refused55115626950Applications resulting in cost to the legal aid fund1917102113Applications resulting in no cost to the legal aid fund23201391Outcome currently unknown 632233841Source: LAMS (Legal Aid Case Management System)Notes:Figures presented in the above table constitute Management Information and may be subject to change.Applications have been identified where the opponent was recorded as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, or any variation or alias of the organisation’s name (e.g. NIHE, NI Housing Executive, The NI Housing Executive etc.), and across all matters, not limited solely to cases concerning damp and mould.If an application is refused, the decision can be appealed, or a fresh application submitted, that may be subsequently granted.Applications that result in a cost to the legal aid fund could be considered ‘unsuccessful’ from the perspective of the applicant.As a legal aid case can span multiple years, expenditure authorised in one financial year will not necessarily reflect legal aid cases granted, or work undertaken, in that same year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 40083/22-27, to detail the number of people that were treated in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area for these conditions in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department’s planned closure of the Ardlough Road, Derry, for tree cutting between 30 March 2026 and 3 April 2026 is in compliance with wildlife legislation on the bird nesting season.
answer_text: My Department received a request to close Ardlough Road, Derry, to allow a private landowner to carry out tree-cutting. An arboriculture consultant inspected the trees and found that several were in poor condition and could fall onto the road, posing a risk to road users. The landowner also confirmed that the trees will be checked for nesting birds before any felling takes place.My Department is aware that hedge and tree cutting is not permitted between 1 March and 31 August, as this is the recognised bird nesting season in the North. This closed period is set out in DAERA’s cross-compliance rules, which exist specifically to protect birds, nests and eggs and to conserve wildlife habitats. There is also, however, a single, clearly defined exception, whereby hedge and tree cutting during this period may take place where there is a demonstrable health and safety issue and this exception ensures that my Department can comply with its duty to maintain the road network under The Roads (NI) Order 1993.The planned road closure will allow the defective trees along Ardlough Road to be cut down safely minimising the risk to road users and site operatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the allergy awareness training that is provided to people that work in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Special Education Needs Inclusion Service Medical Needs team operates a central point of contact for schools and parents who have questions around inclusion of children with medical needs including those with allergies and anaphylaxis and provides a range of resources on its website to support teachers, non-teaching staff and parents in allergy management.  Since 2022 all EA catering staff receive general Food Safety training, alongside separate Allergen Management training. In addition to this, a Special Diets Management training module was introduced for all catering staff in August 2025. Each module is delivered to new staff upon appointment to their job, and the training is refreshed annually for all catering staff. Special diets and allergens have also been the subject of recent awareness-raising initiatives, including toolbox talks and quality assurance assessments. Individual pupils with an Anaphylaxis Care Plan or Individual Healthcare Plan will have specific support and training identified in these plans.   The pupil and their family should be known to the Paediatric Anaphylaxis Nursing Specialists team who can provide specific support, if required. Schools currently arrange completion of the relevant training via the respective Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the enforcement notices served on Lough Neagh sand traders by her Department on 4 February 2026 were accompanied by Stop Notices.
answer_text: Current operations associated with the commercial dredging of sand on Lough Neagh by the Lough Neagh Sand Traders (LNST) benefit from planning permission. The activities subject to the notice served on 4 February 2026 are historical in nature and a stop notice was not necessary.

This includes development of the business case, staffing considerations, accommodation and office infrastructure, and preparing the documentation required to run a public appointments process in accordance with the CPANI Code of Practice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the proportion of funding allocated for tackling violence against women and girls that has been ringfenced in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The first Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Delivery Plan launched on 16 September 2024 for the period 2024-2026. The launch was the start of community investment funding.  The 2025/26 financial year was the first year in which ringfenced funding was received for EVAWG. At the start of 2025/26, £2million was specifically ringfenced for EVAWG. In June 2025, a further £2.2million was ringfenced by the Department of Finance as part of June Monitoring round.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the implications of Westminster’s Medical Training (Prioritisation) Bill for the North.
answer_text: The Medical Training (Prioritisation) Act received Royal Assent on 5 March 2026. The Act introduces a UK-wide duty on all providers of medical training to prioritise applications from UK or RoI medical schools. The policy aim of the Bill is to ensure that there is an employment pathway for UK and RoI graduates into UK medical training programmes.The Act aims to implement the UK government’s commitment to prioritise UK medical graduates for foundation training places and specialty training places. Doctors outside of this group, but with significant NHS/HSC experience will also be prioritised for specialty training places. Graduates from medical schools in the Republic of Ireland will receive the same prioritisation as those from UK institutions.This Act will ensure UK-wide consistency and will enhance career pathways for UK and RoI medical graduates, protect UK-wide recruitment practices, and ensure medical foundation places are sufficient for the output of all local Higher Education Institutions. It will also ensure a more sustainable medical workforce that can meet the health needs of the population, will mean we are less reliant on an unpredictable labour market and can make best use of the substantial taxpayer investment in medical training. It will reduce competition for places and give homegrown talent a clear path to become the next generation of HSC doctors.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) who the deputy First Minister held meetings with in Washington over the St. Patrick's Day period; and (ii) whether minutes were taken of the meetings.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the bodies that sit on the Multi-Agency Review Agency; and (ii) how it makes decisions.
answer_text: The Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) were established under Article 50 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland Order) 2008 and operate in accordance with statutory guidance - Guidance to agencies on multi-agency review arrangements (mara) | Department of Justice.MARA confers a statutory duty on the Department of Justice, Northern Ireland Prison Service, Probation Board for Northern Ireland and Police Service of Northern Ireland to share information and work together to support the assessment and management of risk posed by Terrorist Risk Offenders (TROs).  Staff from His Majesty’s Prisons and Probation Service (HMPPS) are also deployed on behalf of the Department of Justice to assist with the supervision of TROs. The multi-agency risk assessment panel (MARAP) enables agencies to share information, assesses risk, develop and review risk management plans on an ongoing basis.  Decision-making within MARAP where applicable, will be in accordance with the Article 50 Guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases the Multi-Agency Review Agency has observed since its inception.
answer_text: The Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) were established under Article 50 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland Order) 2008 and operate in accordance with statutory guidance - Guidance to agencies on multi-agency review arrangements (mara) | Department of JusticeSince commencement of MARA in September 2021, 47 individuals have been classified as eligible for management under MARA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of occasions the Multi-Agency Review Agency has recommended further detention of prisoners.
answer_text: The Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) were established under Article 50 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland Order) 2008 and operate in accordance with statutory guidance - Guidance to agencies on multi-agency review arrangements (mara) | Department of JusticeSince commencement of MARA in September 2021,                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               four TROs have had their licences revoked and been recalled to prison custody; two TROs have been recalled twice and two TROs recalled once.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current opportunities for career development for specialist, associate specialist and speciality doctors.
answer_text: Since the development of a new specialist contract and a reformed speciality doctor contract in 2021, career progression and development as a SAS doctor have become more clearly defined.SAS doctors progress clinically, and by contributing to wider roles within healthcare, for example, teaching and training of others, innovation, service development and modernisation, leadership or management and representative work.Career development is supported in the contracts of SAS doctors with opportunities to take professional and study leave for study, research, teaching, examinations, visiting clinics and attending professional conferences and training.The NIMDTA SAS Career development programme, which is funded by my Department, designs, delivers and quality assures an annual educational programme for Specialty, Associate Specialist and Specialist doctors substantively employed in HSCNI.The SAS Career Development Programme is influenced by findings from regional and national SAS training needs and career development reports and national requirements for career progression. The annual educational programme incorporates events, training, workshops, webinars and eLearning to enhance SAS knowledge and skills and to support their continuing professional development.Courses delivered through the SAS Career Development Programme are designed to meet the GMC Generic Capabilities Framework competencies. These are required to be evidenced for career progression within SAS posts from Specialty doctor to Specialist and for Portfolio Pathway route to consultant posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to expand capacity within anaesthetics, in light of his Department's Elective Care Framework and Reset Plan for waiting lists.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the circumstances under which a person previously detained for treatment under the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 on Article 15 in a specialist care home, who is subsequently moved to be subject to a deprivation of liberty authorisation under the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, become liable for means-tested charges for a specialist care home placement where their underlying clinical needs and treatment needs remain substantially unchanged.
answer_text: Under the provisions of the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 an individual can be detained in hospital for treatment and within these periods the Responsible Medical Officer may grant periods of leave under Article 15 to the patient on specified occasions and/or for specified periods which can include conditions. Article 15 leave can be used to trial a period of leave from hospital to a care or accommodation placement and if relapse occurs the patient can be recalled to hospital. When on Article 15 trial leave a financial assessment does not take place. When the criteria for the use of the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 no longer applies, care professionals involved ascertain if any legal authority is required to provide the care, support and supervision the individual requires. If the individual is assessed as lacking capacity under The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016, a Deprivation of Liberty authorisation can be sought.In respect of continuing health care, if the needs of the individual can be met in any other setting than a hospital, discharge will take place and the individual will be subject to the relevant charging policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he received any correspondence from the former British Ambassador to the United States, Peter Mandelson, in relation to Dalradian’s mining application.
answer_text: I have not received any correspondence from Peter Mandelson on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasoning behind his decision not to make developer contributions mandatory in the near future.
answer_text: I have decided to progress developer contributions on a phased basis, beginning with a voluntary scheme.  I have made this decision based on my consideration of all of the evidence available to me including the responses to the March-June 2025 public consultation on whether there should be a pathway to introduce developer contributions either on a voluntary or compulsory basis, and taking account of factors such as affordability concerns, budget impacts and sustainability of services. A point made by many of those who responded to that consultation was that there are many aspects of a levy on which more detail or further consultation would be needed before they could comment. I have, therefore, asked officials to continue work on the development of a potential levy so that options for mandatory contributions can be fully considered in future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any ethical procurement guidelines the PSNI considered when awarding an Israeli company a £5.5 million contract in 2019.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail each time he has contacted a representative of the British Government in relation to upcoming cuts to Local Growth Fund employability support programmes.
answer_text: The Department of Finance leads on engagement with the UK Government on behalf of the Executive in relation to the Local Growth Fund and other UK-wide funds. I have written to the Minister of Finance to highlight my concerns about the impact of the UK Government’s proposed funding allocations for 2026/27 on employability services and I also supported the issue of letters on behalf of the Executive Departments to the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government and to the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to invest in anaesthetics, including by expanding the number of training places.
answer_text: To address identified recruitment and retention challenges in the anaesthetic workforce, I have made additional money available from elective care allocations to expand the number of anaesthetic training posts. Seven new training posts have been appointed during 2025/2026, and a further seven posts are planned to be filled before March 2028. Four of these will be for entry in August 2026.Department officials will shortly be submitting proposals for my consideration in relation to the 2026/27 commissioning of pre and post-registration training across the range of healthcare programmes supported by my Department.  The provision of additional training places will be dependent on the availability of funding. Whilst I expect the next financial year to be extremely challenging, I will keep making the case for essential long-term investment in education and training programmes, to deliver an optimum workforce across all the professions contributing to our health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to change Commissioner roles to Statutory Officers of the relevant Department.
answer_text: I have no plans to change any Commissioner roles to Statutory Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail each time he has contacted a representative of the British Government in relation to upcoming cuts to Local Growth Fund employability support programmes.
answer_text: Since taking up post, I have repeatedly sought clarity from Westminster and pressed for changes to the approach to the Local Growth Fund. I was informed of a Capital heavy profile of the Local Growth Fund at the end of September 2025. Since then, I have raised this issue with representatives of the British Government on the following occasions:DateLetter to:30 September 2025Letter to Secretary of State, Hilary Benn23 October 2025Letter to James Murray Chief Secretary to the Treasury23 December 2025Letter to Miatta Fahnbulleh MP Minister for Devolution, Faith and Communities.22 January 2026Letter from the Executive to Steve Reed MP Secretary of State for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government.25 February 2026Letter from the Devolved Governments to Darren Jones MP Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister.19 March 2026Letter from the Executive to Hilary Benn, Secretary of State, NIO.I have also held several meetings and discussions regarding the cuts to the Local Growth Fund as listed below: Date Meeting with: 01 October 2025Secretary of State, Hilary Benn17 October 2025Chief Secretary to the Treasury James Murray at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee22 October 2025Secretary of State, Hilary Benn, and Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government19 March 2026Chief Secretary to the Treasury James Murray following the Finance Interministerial Standing CommitteeMy officials have also been in regular contact with their counterparts in Whitehall on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the PSNI plans to renew its £5.5 million contract with an unnamed Israeli company, upon its expiration in November 2026.
answer_text: The procurement of services by the PSNI for operational purposes is a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has carried out an assessment of the PSNI’s £5.5 million contract with an unnamed Israeli company.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any assessment of the Police Service of Northern Ireland’s £5.5 million contract with an unnamed Israeli company.The procurement of services by the PSNI for operational purposes is a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has estimated (i) the total annual value of tips and service charges in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the proportion that is administered through third-party tronc systems or companies outside the jurisdiction.
answer_text: The regulatory impact assessment carried out by my Department in 2024 provided an annual estimate of tips and service charges of just over £150 million received in restaurants, pubs and bars.I have no estimates on the proportion that is administered through third-party tronc systems or companies.  Scrutiny of troncs and other organisations operating PAYE services is a matter for HMRC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether her Department has identified any companies that use regulatory loopholes to divert tips and service charges out of the local economy; and (ii) whether there will be any repercussions or enforcement action against companies.
answer_text: My Department has no regulatory or enforcement function in that regard.  Any monies that may be subject to income tax or national insurance are, as excepted matters, for HMRC to investigate and address. However, I intend to include provisions in my “Good Jobs” Employment Rights Bill that will require employers to ensure that there is a fair allocation of tips, gratuities and service charges to workers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any responsibility or oversight for ensuring that arm's-length bodies, such as the PSNI, engage in ethical and human rights-compliant procurement processes.
answer_text: The procurement of services by the PSNI for operational purposes is a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any differences that exist in oversight or worker protections between traditional hospitality businesses and pop-ups; and (ii) how any gaps are being addressed.
answer_text: The same statutory employment protections apply to all workers, including traditional hospitality businesses and pop-up or temporary operations.Workers experiencing unfair treatment can seek confidential advice from the Labour Relations Agency and, if necessary, pursue enforcement through relevant bodies such as HMRC for breaches of the minimum wage. In respect of other statutory rights such as those relating to working time, holidays, leave and unlawful deductions from wages, individuals may seek remedy through an industrial tribunal.Measures to be included in the proposed ‘Good Jobs’ Employment Rights Bill are intended to strengthen worker protections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any oversight that exists to ensure hospitality workers can take their statutory breaks and ensure access to break and welfare facilities.
answer_text: Across all sectors, oversight of employment rights operates primarily through a complaints-led model.In relation to statutory rest breaks, workers can seek advice from the Labour Relations Agency, which also provides an early conciliation service which seeks to resolve the issue. A worker may ultimately bring a claim to an industrial tribunal.Welfare facilities are dealt with under health and safety legislation and may be investigated and enforced by the relevant enforcing authority, which for hospitality purposes is the local district council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether her Department has assessed the extent to which customer tips and service charges collected are routed through UK-based third-party tronc companies; and (ii) any estimates available for the total funds leaving the local economy each year.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in determining how employers operate their tronc arrangements.  HMRC is responsible for determining whether troncs are operating with sufficient independence of employers and ensuring the correct statutory deductions are made.  I have no estimates available on the total funds that are administered via troncs.  My proposed legislation, along with a subsequent statutory code of practice, will aim to ensure that all tips and service charges go to workers in a fair and transparent manner and that only statutory deductions will be permissible from those amounts.  This will apply irrespective of whatever tronc operator is used by an employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action being taken to stop employers topping up staff wages with tips routed through third-party tronc systems.
answer_text: A tronc is a special pay arrangement used to distribute tips, gratuities and service charges. HMRC is responsible for ensuring that troncs operate within its rules.  My proposed “Good Jobs” Employment Rights Bill, along with a subsequent statutory code of practice, will aim to ensure that all tips and service charges go to workers in a fair and transparent manner and that only statutory deductions will be permissible from those amounts.  This will apply irrespective of whatever tronc operator is used by an employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils in mainstream education currently awaiting a statement of special educational needs, with suspected speech and language issues.
answer_text: The number of pupils in mainstream education currently awaiting a statement of special educational needs, with suspected speech and language issues is 130.For this request, pupils awaiting a statement of special educational needs are defined as those who have received a proposed statement but have not yet been issued with a final statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the last review of the Factories Act (Northern Ireland) 1965 took place.
answer_text: Since the Factories Act (Northern Ireland) 1965 was made, it has been extensively amended, and some parts have been repealed due to more modern and updated health and safety legislation.I have recently approved a public consultation which issued on 9th March 2026 seeking views on removing further parts of the Factories Act which are considered redundant. The consultation, which closes on 11th May 2026, can be found on the HSENI website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department issues any guidance to the PSNI on instances when tasers should not be used.
answer_text: The use of Conducted Energy Devices (CED) by the PSNI is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Additionally, the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland is notified of all instances where CED is used.  It would not be appropriate for my Department to issue guidance to PSNI on this matter.The College of Policing compiles and provides comprehensive guidance on the use of CED such as TASERs, including use of the National Decision Making model and highlighting Risk Factors that may influence the operational use of CED. A non-exhaustive list can be viewed at https://www.college.police.uk/app/armed-policing/conducted-energy-devices-taser#risk-factorshttps://www.college.police.uk/app/armed-policing/conducted-energy-devices-taser#risk-factors

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the Women’s Health Action Plan will ensure improved awareness and understanding of women’s health issues across a range of healthcare settings, not just specialist services.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action Plan, when published, will highlight and consider the need for improved awareness, training and understanding of women’s health issues across a range of healthcare settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether education and training on menstrual health and endometriosis for healthcare practitioners will be included as a key priority within the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action Plan, when published, will highlight and consider the need for improved awareness, training and understanding of women’s health issues across a range of healthcare settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of PSNI potential use of the T10 taser model.
answer_text: The use of Conducted Energy Devices (CED) by the PSNI is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Additionally, the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland is notified of all instances where CED is used.I understand that assessment of the use of the TASER 10 Conducted Energy Device by police services was conducted by the Home Office including by: external consultancy; the College of Policing; Defence Science and Technology Laboratory UK (Dstl); and the Scientific Advisory Committee on the Medical Implications of Less-Lethal Weapons (SACMILL).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has reconsidered allowing recipients of Universal Credit to avail of the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: While West Belfast has the lowest number of Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) claims in absolute terms, this is not due to lower uptake among eligible families. The pattern is primarily explained by the relationship between Universal Credit (UC), TaxFree Childcare (TFC), and eligibility for NICSS.Access to NICSS is based on eligibility for TFC. Families in receipt of UC are not eligible for TFC and therefore cannot access NICSS. Areas with higher levels of UC claimants, such as West Belfast, will therefore have a smaller pool of families eligible for NICSS. Department for Communities statistics show that West Belfast has the highest concentration of UC claimants of any constituency in Northern Ireland. There is no justification to change the eligibility as UC provides its own, more generous childcare support, covering up to 85% of eligible costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to the negative implications of removing the social disadvantage criteria for admission into the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: I understand that the member has provided further clarification in regard to his question, confirming that he is referring to the statutory socially disadvantaged circumstances criterion that funded pre-school education settings are required to adhere to when considering applications received from children during the pre-school admissions process.  My Department carried out a public consultation in relation to this criterion in 2021 from which it was clear that overall there is no longer support for the indefinite retention of a statutory criterion.  I also regularly receive correspondence from MLAs, parents and others in regard to the current statutory criterion, voicing concerns about the impact it has on children being able to access pre-school places in their preferred settings.I am of the view that this current legislative requirement is no longer fit for purpose in the context of universal pre-school provision.   It is my intention to revisit the issue in the context of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which includes a proposed action to carry out a further consultation this year on legislative changes to remove the statutory admissions criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount allocated to the Women's Centre Childcare Fund in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: From 1 April 2016 to 31 March 2026, Voluntary and Community Division will have awarded over £9 million in funding via the Women's Centre Childcare Fund.The table below sets out the amount allocated to the WCCF in each of the last ten years:Women's Centre Childcare FundYearAmount2016/17£796,589.682017/18£795,355.932018/19£764,876.29*2019/20£761,145.972020/21£762,871.612021/22£877,693.412022/23£823,982.81**2023/24£823,982.812024/25£841,128.522025/26£960,159.68*The Northern Ireland Assembly was suspended in January 2017 and was not restored until January 2020, which resulted in reduced budgets being implemented in the absence of a Minister.	**Magherafelt Women’s Centre withdrew from the WCCF Funding Programme due to a decline in eligible applicants, which subsequently reduced the annual funding award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the Strategic Childcare Investment Fund, referenced in the draft Childcare Strategy, will increase capacity and support for rural childcare providers.
answer_text: The aim of the Strategic Childcare Accessibility Fund, proposed within the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, is to support increased provision in areas where the demand for childcare places exceeds supply. Progress on this proposal, including eligibility requirements for the fund, is subject to Executive agreement of the actions within the final strategy and   dependent on the level of funding allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Toybox scheme will keep its exclusive focus on the specific developmental needs of Traveller, Roma and Newcomer children in the event of its expansion, as outlined in the draft Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: My Department currently provides funding for the Toybox project which supports Traveller, Roma and Newcomer children who, as identified in ‘A Fair Start’, are at particular risk of educational underachievement.The Budgets for 2026-27 or subsequent periods have not yet been formally set by the Executive; therefore, it would be premature to comment on any future development or expansion of the Toybox project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of childcare providers in Belfast that avail of Tax Free Childcare (TFC) that do not have children below primary school age using their provision that are eligible for TFC.
answer_text: TaxFree Childcare (TFC) is a UKwide scheme administered by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC). The specific information requested is not available from published HMRC statistics and cannot be provided by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the organisations in Belfast that have availed of the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: 304 childcare providers across Belfast have submitted claims under the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme in 2025/26. 149 of these are Childminders and Approved Home Childcare providers, a list of the remaining 155 organisations has been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total figure his Department has provided through the childcare subsidy scheme.
answer_text: As of 06 March 2026, my Department has paid out £25.2 million in subsidy claims under the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has taken of people who work shifts in the development of the childcare strategy.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy sets out proposed actions to expand the availability of Approved Home Childcare (AHC).AHC enables parents to access approved childcare in their own home rather than in a childminder’s home, providing an option that may be particularly helpful for those with atypical working patterns. This is part of the broader commitment within the draft Strategy to improve the flexibility and accessibility of provision.The full draft Strategy is available here. draft ELC Strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any costings in relation to a universal public childcare system.
answer_text: In developing the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, the Department completed highlevel indicative costings of a range of support models. The provision of universal publicly funded childcare was not one of them as it would be unaffordable in the current context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the total financial figure that will be provided for energy support payments in 2026.
answer_text: The north was originally excluded from the British Government’s decision to apply a discount to electricity bills in Britain. However my Department immediately challenged this decision, and through these efforts secured funding for a comparable scheme here. This is estimated to equate to £81m over three years. As I have said, once we have legislative cover from Westminster, my Department will be in a position to implement the comparable scheme, which is a £30 reduction per year on household electricity bills. Separately, £17million has been made available to people who rely on oil to heat their homes. In England this is being delivered through the Crisis and Resilience Fund. The local equivalent is the Department for Communities’ Discretionary Support scheme. I understand that Department is working up proposals to utilise the funding. I recognise that this funding is not sufficient to deal with what is a global energy crisis, and I am therefore working with Executive colleagues to secure more support from the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any work to determine the average cost of childcare in Belfast.
answer_text: My Department collects fee data from childcare providers registered for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (Northern Ireland CSS).  Based on this source, at September 2025, the average daily fee across all provider types in Belfast, was £47.  For day nursery providers only, the average daily fee in Belfast was £57. These figures reflect the total cost of a place before any subsidies are applied including through Universal Credit, the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme and Tax-Free Childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to capping the cost of fees in nursery settings.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out an assessment as to the low uptake of the childcare subsidy scheme in West Belfast.
answer_text: While West Belfast has the lowest number of Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) claims in absolute terms, this is not due to lower uptake among eligible families. The pattern is primarily explained by the relationship between Universal Credit (UC), TaxFree Childcare (TFC), and eligibility for NICSS.Access to NICSS is based on eligibility for TFC. Families in receipt of UC are not eligible for TFC and therefore cannot access NICSS. Areas with higher levels of UC claimants, such as West Belfast, will therefore have a smaller pool of families eligible for NICSS. Department for Communities statistics show that West Belfast has the highest concentration of UC claimants of any constituency in Northern Ireland. There is no justification to change the eligibility as UC provides its own, more generous childcare support, covering up to 85% of eligible costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of workers in childcare settings that are in receipt of the living wage.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the role that gender will play in shaping a childcare strategy.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy is the Executive’s draft plan to improve outcomes for all children; support working families and carers with the cost of their childcare and support the sustainability of the early learning and childcare sector.Whilst acknowledging that decisions around entering or remaining in employment are complex and are not dependent on childcare availability or affordability alone, the proposed actions within the draft strategy do however have the potential to particularly benefit women by supporting their return to, or remaining in, the workplace.The majority of employees in the Early Learning and Childcare are women. Addressing affordability of childcare may support the sustainability of a predominantly female workforce. Through this strategy, the Executive is also seeking to support gender diversification of the workforce and ensure that it reflects the children and families it supports.The draft Strategy is open for consultation until 24 March 2026. The draft Strategy is available  here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 39249/22-27, to detail the percentage of total operations that were carried out within (i) NHS settings; and (ii) private health care providers.
answer_text: Of the number of hernia and cataract operations provided within AQW 39249/22-27, 100% were carried out within NHS settings, within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, the Cataract Day Procedure Centre network and the regional Day Procedure Centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children living in poverty in West Belfast that are availing of childcare financial support from his Department.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS), which provides financial support with the cost of childcare for eligible working parents.There are a number of recognised approaches to assessing whether individuals are living in poverty, typically based on equivalised household income and wider household circumstances. The data collected through NICSS is not designed to determine whether a child is living in poverty, and therefore this cannot be determined from NICSS administrative data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Residents' Parking Zone in the Iveagh area.
answer_text: Proposals associated with the residents’ parking zone in the Iveagh area are being finalised to enable officials to carry out the formal consultation. Further progress will be subject to the satisfactory completion of the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial support his Department provides to people with disabilities beyond Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: Financial support for people with disabilities may also be available through Universal Credit, Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit, Employment and Support Allowance, Attendance Allowance and Disability Living Allowance subject to meeting the necessary eligibility criteria. Additional financial support may also be available through the Discretionary Support scheme, which can help those in need of short-term assistance in crisis or emergency situations. My Department’s Make the Call service is available to ensure people are receiving all of the benefits, services and supports they are entitled to.Further information on Universal Credit, Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit, Employment and Support Allowance, Attendance Allowance and Disability Living Allowance and the Discretionary Support scheme can be found at www.nidirect.gov.uk Contact information for the Make the Call service is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the documentation that is required to be presented when a person who is not the gender assigned at birth is applying for a provisional driving licence.
answer_text: All applicants applying for a provisional licence must provide the standard proof of identity documentation required for first time licence applications. This may include a valid British or Irish passport, a share code confirming digital immigration status, or a British or Irish birth, adoption or naturalisation certificate.Where an applicant has newly taken up residence in the north or has previously lived outside the jurisdiction, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) may also require proof of residency such as a utility bill, bank statement, hospital appointment letter, court notice, HMRC or rates correspondence, or confirmation of enrolment at a university based in the north.When the applicant’s gender is not the gender assigned at birth, at least one of the following additional supporting documents must be provided:a Gender Recognition Certificate;a letter from a healthcare professional confirming that the individual is undergoing or has undergone treatment for gender dysphoria; ora deed poll or updated birth certificate reflecting the acquired gender.Applicants must also demonstrate that they are living in the acquired gender for all practical purposes. One piece of documentary evidence, such as a bank statement, payslip, utility bill or similar document, is sufficient when provided alongside one of the documents listed above.These arrangements ensure that the DVA can maintain accurate and legally compliant identity records while appropriately supporting applicants who have transitioned or whose gender identity has changed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of reports that the BBC has set a new target of £500 million in savings over two years.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any discussions he or his officials have had about providing women’s refuge support service in West Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Supporting People (SP) team has not been involved with discussions regarding the provision of a women’s refuge support service in West Belfast.The Housing Executive recently completed public consultation on the Supporting People Strategic Intent 2026–2031, to help shape the SP Programme’s future direction.In parallel, a programme of work to modernise the SP Programme has commenced. A key element of which is the development of a Strategic Needs Assessment (SNA), which will provide a robust evidence base of current and projected housing-related support needs. The findings of the SNA will inform future SP Programme priorities, including the consideration of gaps in provision and the potential reprofiling of services to better meet identified needs.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the measures the PSNI require to implement live facial recognition.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to ringfence apprenticeship funding for direct use by employers.
answer_text: Currently the Apprenticeship Levy is a reserved matter and my Department has no remit in its administration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 41347/22-27, whether the current Non-Molestation Order legislation protects victims of violence.
answer_text: Statistical data provided by the NI Policing Board in response to a Written Question from Cheryl Brownlee MLA contains the following information on the number of Non-Molestation Orders issued between 2021 and 2025:		20212022202320242025Total 20151621165014151444The above figures demonstrate that practitioners and victims of violence understand the protection Non-Molestation Orders can provide, and that legal aid is available, where appropriate, to assist in obtaining that protection.  Non-Molestation Orders can also work alongside Occupation Orders and criminal law remedies to offer further protection to victims, for example under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act 2021. In relation to criminal law protections, I note that the Justice Minister has provided recent information on work within her Department to develop a pilot to test the use of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders in this jurisdiction in her response to question 41178/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in West Belfast that received puberty blockers for issues not related to gender-affirming care in the last ten years.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will liaise with the Home Office to request a moratorium on evictions from Mears accommodation during Ramadan.
answer_text: I have no plans to liaise with the Home Office to request a moratorium on evictions from Mears accommodation during Ramadan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any efficiency savings being considered by Translink.
answer_text: Translink continually assesses its cost base for further savings.  Existing efficiencies that will be continued in 2026-27 include a successful fuel hedge providing price certainty in a volatile energy market and a programme of cost reductions to offset emerging cost pressures.  Translink is currently finalising its 2026-27 Corporate Plan to be submitted to the Department for consideration. This budget will incorporate further efficiencies that will build on existing cost savings achieved in 2025-26 and previous years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 39975/22-27, to detail the number of staff employed to maintain roads in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Road maintenance is largely delivered by my Department’s Road Asset Maintenance (RAM) Directorate, and the staff in post as at February 2026 is set out in the table below.Total Staff Employed in RAM DirectorateNumber of Non Industrial StaffNumber of Industrial StaffFeb 2026732502230The recording and management of historical staff data is the responsibility of People and Organisational Development within the Department of Finance (DoF), it is however worth saying that there has been a number of reorganisations within this work area over the last ten years and the figures may not be readily available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Metro journeys that fail to operate each week due to operational fleet issues.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his officials have had any discussions with the Kids Operating Room charity about supplying children’s medical services in Hebron, Jericho and Bethlehem.
answer_text: My department has not had any discussions with this charity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress made by her Department in complying with the recommendations contained in the Public Accounts Committee report, Planning in Northern Ireland 2022.
answer_text: Eight out of the twelve recommendations contained in the Public Accounts Committee report on planning for which my Department is either fully or partially responsible, have been implemented.  Work continues on the remaining 4 recommendations. which are:Recommendation 4: The Department considers ways to streamline the remaining LDP processes.Recommendation 7: The Department should ensure rural housing policy is agreed and implemented equally across the system.Recommendation 8: The Department urgently considers how it exercises its oversight of the planning system; and Recommendation 11: The planning system must be financially sustainable, and this requires an appropriate, long-term funding model.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when AQW 29333/22-27 will be answered.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26260/22-27, and in light of the sentencing on Mobuoy, why no one is being held responsible for around one million tonnes of illegally dumped waste at the site.
answer_text: The criminal proceedings, in relation to the Mobuoy site, concern the unauthorised disposal of an estimated 635,000 tonnes of controlled waste. However, site investigations for the development of the draft strategy indicate that there is up to 1.6 million m3 of waste present at the site. This estimated volume includes historical waste, which was not deposited by the current defendants and some of which dates back to deposits made in the 1960s.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an outline of discussions during her joint meeting with the Minister for Justice, Home Affairs and Migration in Cavan on 2 March 2026.
answer_text: I had a short meeting with Jim O’Callaghan, Minister for Justice, Home Affairs and Migration, on the margins of the annual crossborder police conference on organised and serious crime on 2 March 2026. Our discussion covered a number of issues of mutual interest including organised crime, cross border human trafficking, ongoing work to address domestic violence and sexual abuse such as Operation Encompass and the response to online violent pornography legacy matters, and the work being led by Fleur Ravensbergen in her role as Independent Expert on paramilitary group transition to disbandment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the visit to Washington on St. Patrick’s Day has been reconsidered following recent events involving the United States of America in Iran.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister will be travelling to Washington DC to participate in a series of engagements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the advice their Department can provide to people from here currently living in UAE, Qatar, Bahrain and Iran, given recent geopolitical events.
answer_text: We are fully engaged with both the UK and Irish governments on this matter and stand ready to assist in helping people from here who are currently impacted in the region in any way we can.People can check Foreign Office travel advice updates - GOV.UK for the latest advice from the UK Government and Consular updates | Ireland.ie for the latest advice from the Irish Government.  This advice has also been summarised on our new NI Direct pages https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/news/middle-east-travel-advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with (i) anti-racist organisations; and (ii) anti-slavery historians in relation to America 250 events.
answer_text: NoneNone

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department has had in relation buying oil in bulk following the recent rise in energy costs.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department will give to including the fight around abolitionism as part of the America 250 celebrations.
answer_text: My Department has incorporated the fight around abolitionism as part of the America 250 commemorations. In 2024, the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland produced a Guide to Slavery, Ulster & Slavery:  The Story from the Archives to mark #Douglass Week in Belfast. The updated guide includes a foreword by Kenneth B. Morris, Jr., the great-great-great-grandson of Frederick Douglass, co-founder and President of the Frederick Douglass Family Initiatives. PRONI will reprint and promote the guide during 2026.Histories of enslavement, including abolitionism, are central to National Museums NI’s interpretive approach at the Ulster American Folk Park. In recent years the Museum has been committed to a programme of ‘Developing the Narrative’, through which we bring to the fore historical experiences that were previously marginalised, including those of Indigenous Peoples, enslaved people and women. In terms of enslavement, National Museums have worked closely with a consultation group to develop and discuss several new interpretive interventions and public activities. These have included new graphics in the ‘Emigrants’ gallery, which recover and personalise the stories of enslaved individuals, and the planting of tobacco and creation of a provision ground at the Rogan House, which was owned by a known enslaver, Francis Rogan (at the time of the American Civil War in 1861, 71 people were enslaved by the Rogan family).The story of abolitionism has mainly been explored through programming, with regular events focussed on the stories of notable abolitionists Mary Ann McCracken and Frederick Douglass. This work on enslavement and abolitionism will continue to be further developed at the Ulster American Folk Park, both as the Museums marks and considers the 250th anniversary of the United States and plans for the future at the site. The projects supported through the grant call that the Ulster-Scots Agency (U-SA) managed, as well as projects being taken forward by U-SA directly, are focused on the American Revolutionary period, which happened about 50 years before the anti-slavery movement started to develop. This makes it less likely that projects will feature abolition as a specific issue, but there is ample scope to demonstrate inclusive approaches and different perspectives through people and events in that period, through discussion of rights, liberty and freedom, which were key tenets underlying the Revolution. The historical complexity of the period is demonstrated by Hercules Mulligan from Coleraine, who was one of George Washington's top spies. Mulligan owned an enslaved man called Cato, who also spied for the Revolutionaries and used his status to avoid suspicion from the authorities. Cato was freed in recognition of his services in the Revolution and Mulligan went on to found an early anti-slavery organisation, but it is undeniable that Mulligan, like many people in that period, was still a slave holder.  People of Ulster-Scots and Irish desce

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 40529/22-27, to detail why there was a drop in people in work claiming Universal Credit from 2024/25.
answer_text: The number of people claiming Universal Credit changes daily as new people claim and existing claims end. In addition, the circumstances of existing claimants change from period to period, including movements into and out of work. There are many reasons why the number of people claiming Universal Credit, who are also working, will be different from one period to another and it is not possible to detail the exact changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of recommendations featured in the Policing Board's most recent 5-year Human Rights Review.
answer_text: PSNI is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) for its response to recommendations in the Policing Board’s Human Rights Review.However, my department continues to engage with partners to monitor the progress and actions being taken to ensure that the recommendations are fully reflected in ongoing policy and operational development. This is a sustained programme of work, and further updates will be provided as implementation of recommendations continues.My sincere thanks go to the Board and all those involved, in the production of this insightful report, including the Human Rights Advisor, John Wadham. Their dedication and expertise have been instrumental in bringing these important issues to light.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to changing the law to remove protests not related to the Protestant, unionist and loyalist and Catholic, nationalist and republican identity from the remit of the Parades Commission.
answer_text: The legislation governing the operation of the Parades Commission – The Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 – is reserved.  Any changes to this legislation are a matter for the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Office.  The Commission is a body independent of the Northern Ireland Executive, appointed and reviewed by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how their Department ensures law and policy in relation to protest rights comply with the European Convention on Human Rights, given there has been no assessment of whether notification and authorisation requirements have deterred people from organising protests.
answer_text: The regulation of protests and public processions in Northern Ireland is governed by the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, a framework that remains reserved and operates independently of departmental policy functions.Responsibility for operational decisions on these matters rests with the PSNI, with oversight provided by the Northern Ireland Policing Board, which monitors the discharge of policing duties including compliance with Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Parades Commission also functions as an independent statutory body tasked with receiving notifications and determining conditions for parades and related protests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department ensures that law and policy in relation to protests complies with the European Convention on Human Rights.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not set legislative or policy direction on protests, moving or static.  These matters are reserved, and the associated legislation is a matter for the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Office. The European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR), including Article 10 (Freedom of expression) and Article 11 (Freedom of assembly and association), is incorporated into UK law through the Human Rights Act 1998 (HRA).  This ensures that any legislation enacted by Westminster is compatible with ECHR standards, including laws and policies relating to protests.However, decisions and determinations of the PSNI (including on protests) are subject to human rights monitoring, to ensure compliance with the HRA, as a statutory function of the Northern Ireland Policing Board under the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.In respect of moving protests, the decision-making process of the Parades Commission is set out in the Public Processions and Related Protest Guidelines produced as a statutory duty under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998.  The Guidelines provide a framework for the use of the Commission’s statutory powers, including recognising Human Rights, as expressed in the ECHR.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has carried out an assessment of the current 28-day notification period for moving protests and the impact it has on protest rights.
answer_text: The 28-day notification for moving protests or processions (submitted to the Parades Commission either online or via the PSNI) is a statutory requirement under the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, which is a reserved matter.  While I have a general interest in the potential impact of parading on policing, justice and the community, the Parades Commission is independent of Government, appointed by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and, therefore, not within my remit.As such, no formal assessment has been undertaken by the Department in relation to the 28-day notification period or its operation in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether he will make a request to the Home Office to instruct Mears to enact an eviction moratorium for asylum-seekers during the Ramadan period.
answer_text: Immigration, including the Move On policy, is not devolved and is a matter for the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Diocese of Down and Conor received payment for the transfer of land at Milltown Cemetery that is due to form part of the new greenway.
answer_text: The land was transferred to my Department under a Deed of Dedication for a nominal fee of £1. My Department also paid the legal fees associated with the transfer of lands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of complaints, queries or review of educational settings the Education Authority has received in relation to children with special educational needs being placed in Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has indicated that neither their complaint system nor the general correspondence logging system can be configured to filter correspondence by complaints, queries or reviews according to the parameters outlined in your request. Retrieving this information would require a manual review of individual complaint records, correspondence files and pupil files. Conducting a review of the requested information would require a disproportionately large amount of time and resources. Unfortunately, we are therefore unable to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been made of the National Statistics’ Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings that shows we have some of the highest rates of low pay.
answer_text: The latest statistics from the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings for 2025 shows that 17.3% of employees in the North aged 18 and over were paid below the Real Living Wage. This represents an improvement over recent years, with a reduction of almost three percentage points in the past year and a fall of around nine percentage points since 2020, demonstrating sustained progress in reducing low pay.		Although we continue to have one of the higher proportions of lowpaid jobs compared with other regions in Britain, progress has been made in narrowing regional disparities. The gap between the North and Scotland (the region with the lowest proportion of employee jobs earning below the Real Living Wage) has reduced by almost 50 per cent since 2020.I am committed to creating more good jobs and to increasing the number of Real Living Wage employers. The Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will be a major step towards that goal. This legislation will secure fairer conditions for all workers, including those on lower incomes. My Department will also continue to provide funding for the Real Living Wage franchise in the North. That funding, along with the Good Jobs Bill and a planned Good Jobs Charter will help to drive up the number of people in good jobs and the number of employers that pay the Real Living Wage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the aspects of Awaab’s Law that will be included in the Decent Homes Standard.
answer_text: Pillar A of the current Decent Homes Standard requires that, as part of the minimum Fitness Standard for housing, all homes must be free from any form of damp that may be prejudicial to the health of occupants.As set out in the published consultation on the Decent Homes Standard, it is proposed that the revised standard will also include:Damp and mould as a standalone element under Health and Safety (Pillar B), recognising the seriousness of the issueAdditional safety requirements that are central to tenant health, safety and wellbeing, such as fire safety and risk, electrical safety, carbon monoxide detection and risk of fallsNew requirements to risk assess and triage repairs, including defined timeframes for investigation and completion, with appropriate prioritisation of emergency and urgent repairsSome provisions already exist in guidance but incorporating them into the revised Decent Homes Standard will set a clearer, more explicit expectation in one standard.This approach will ensure similar expectations and protections for tenants within existing standards, with faster implementation than legislation would allow, while recognising differing regulatory structures in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the Housing Executive’s Action Plan for Damp and Mould has resulted in (i) a reduction in the risks posed by damp and mould; (ii) more timely and effective response maintenance; and (iii) a more targeted planned scheme works programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The organisational action plan has delivered a more structured, data-informed and preventative approach to damp and mould, resulting in measurable improvements across risk reduction, response maintenance, and planned investment targeting.(i)	The introduction of a cross-divisional action plan, supported by enhanced reporting and monitoring arrangements, has strengthened the NIHE’s ability to identify, assess and mitigate risks associated with damp and mould.Key actions include:•	Improved risk identification and oversight through the development of dashboards, regular performance reporting have enabled earlier identification of emerging issues, including hotspot areas.•	Adoption of a more proactive, preventative approach including property inspections during routine visits (e.g. voids, grant inspections, maintenance visits) and pilot sampling of low-engagement households have increased early detection of issues before escalation.•	Enhanced risk assessment through data and vulnerability profiling by implementation of a risk matrix considering factors such as the nature of report and tenant vulnerability will support prioritisation of high-risk cases and more appropriate interventions.•	Increased tenant awareness and early reporting through targeted communications (leaflets, digital content, and communication campaigns) have improved tenant understanding of damp and mould including how to help prevent it, and how to report concerns.•	Stronger links to planned investment through cross departmental collaboration and use of Stock Condition Survey data ensures that underlying structural causes are identified and addressed. (ii)	The action plan has improved both the speed and quality of response maintenance services through standardisation and better processes.	Key actions include:•	Standardisation of response processes by creation of Damp & Mould Inspection guidance has ensured a more consistent best-practice approach across the organisation.•	Improved case tracking through the introduction of damp and mould dashboard and reporting frameworks allows cases to be tracked helping to reducing delays and ensuring follow-up actions are completed efficiently.•	Enhanced staff capability and responsiveness by delivery of training and awareness initiatives have enabled both technical (maintenance) and non-technical (housing) staff to better understand and identify damp and mould issues and trigger appropriate responses at an earlier stage.•	Better co-ordination between teams achieved through cross-divisional working (including housing, maintenance, and grants teams) has improved communication and ensured a more joined-up response as necessary. (iii)	The action plan has enabled a more intelligence-led approach to planned investment, ensuring resources can be directed to areas of greatest need and impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of occasions that Environmental Health carried out inspections for damp, mould and disrepair in West Belfast in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for environmental health inspections relating to damp, mould or disrepair.  These statutory inspection and enforcement functions are carried out by district councils, including Belfast City Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of occasions that Environmental Health carried out inspections for damp, mould and disrepair in the Belfast City Council area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer you to the response provided to AQW 41533 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will publish the Housing Executive’s Action Plan for Damp and Mould, resulting from the work of the Task and Finish group formed in November 2022.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The document is an internal Action Plan which has not been published externally.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether she will implement all of the recommendations in the NIAO Report Active Travel in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the timelines for implementation.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report on active travel. My Department has accepted all recommendations contained within the report and the NIAO were formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A copy of the Department’s response to each of the recommendations, including target implementation dates in 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly library.Whilst my Department has not carried out stakeholder engagement specifically on the NIAO report, officials have given oral evidence to the Committee for Infrastructure and my Department has responded to correspondence from a number of elected representatives and interest groups on the topic. I recognise the importance of stakeholder engagement and my Department has agreed to set up an Active Travel Engagement Stakeholder Forum by May 2026.  Going forward, this forum will provide an opportunity for regular engagement with key stakeholders on a range of issues that affect the delivery of active travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any consideration he has given to issuing a Ministerial Direction to introduce an Independent Environmental Protection Agency.
