question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 22693/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people admitted to hospitals in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust with a diagnosis of inadequate housing in the last five years.
answer_text: In relation to the requested details of the number of people admitted to hospitals in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust with a diagnosis of inadequate housing in the last five years, I would like to advise that this data is not collected by my Department. Therefore, my Department cannot provide the information requested in response to this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out an assessment on where and how addicts will acquire alcohol if minimum unit pricing is implemented.
answer_text: MUP targets consumption of cheap, strong alcohol products, which are disproportionately consumed by hazardous and harmful drinkers. Those with alcohol dependence are a small subgroup of those who drink at harmful levels and have specific needs. It is likely that people with dependence will continue to purchase and consume alcohol following any introduction of MUP, and that is why they need timely and evidence-based treatment and wider support that addresses the root cause of their dependence.The evaluations from the introduction of MUP in Scotland and in Wales have been considered in detail in the policy development for MUP in Northern Ireland. A detailed evaluation conducted by Public Health Scotland ‘Evaluating the impact of minimum unit pricing for alcohol in Scotland: Final report A synthesis of the evidence’ found limited evidence to suggest that MUP was effective in reducing consumption for those people with alcohol dependence. Should MUP be taken forward, we would monitor the policy for any adverse impacts, particularly for dependent drinkers.However, as MUP targets the wider cohort of hazardous and harmful drinkers, it can play a role in preventing them becoming dependant drinkers in the future.It is acknowledged that minimum unit pricing of alcohol is not the sole answer to addressing alcohol related harms. As such, I am also focused on improving the access to quality substance misuse services across Northern Ireland. To this end, the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan 2024-28, co-developed following considerable stakeholder engagement, will inform the development of substance use related services, subject to required additional funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out any assessment of minimum unit pricing for alcohol in Scotland, specifically whether its introduction has lead to an increase in the use of illegal drugs.
answer_text: A minimum unit price (MUP) for the sale of alcohol is not a tax or a levy, so no funds would be raised directly that could be subsequently ringfenced if one were to be set in Northern Ireland. An MUP would set a rate per unit of alcohol below which a product containing alcohol could not be sold. It should be noted that alcohol is already the subject to taxation and duty at the UK level and therefore the powers for additional taxation within Northern Ireland would likely be limited.Modelling of the impact of MUP has been undertaken in Northern Ireland, with the most recent estimates available here: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/health/doh-mup-revised-modelling.PDF. Overall, the modelling shows that heavier drinkers buy cheaper alcohol consistently across all drink types and channels. This is particularly true for cider, for which higher risk drinkers pay an average of 44p/unit compared to 78p/unit for moderate drinkers and spirits, for which higher risk drinkers pay an average of 67p/unit compared to £2.42/unit among moderate drinkers. The picture is similar for drinkers in poverty, compared to those not in poverty. MUP targets consumption of cheap, strong products, which are disproportionately consumed by hazardous and harmful drinkers regardless of income status.The MUP is therefore targeted to those who drink alcohol at hazardous and harmful levels, rather than at specific income groups. That is why MUP is more effective than taxation, which impacts all drinkers rather than those who drink the most and come to the greatest harm.  The evaluations from the introduction of MUP in Scotland have been considered in detail in the policy development for MUP in Northern Ireland. The evidence does not support an increase in the use of illegal drugs and illicit alcohol following the introduction of MUP in Scotland. A detailed evaluation carried out by Public Health Scotland ‘Evaluating the impact of minimum unit pricing for alcohol in Scotland: Final report A synthesis of the evidence’ found no consistent evidence of either positive or negative impacts on social outcomes, such illicit drug use, at a population level. This analysis is available online at:https://publichealthscotland.scot/media/20366/evaluating-the-impact-of-minimum-unit-pricing-for-alcohol-in-scotland-final-report.pdf. This is consistent with the wider evidence base that shows alcohol and other drugs can act as complimentary products, rather than substitutes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any responsibility his Department has in relation to Non-Molestation Orders legislation.
answer_text: My Department retains responsibility for the substantive law in this area and the provisions contained in the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998, which set out the statutory criteria relating to Non-Molestation Orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any communication he has had with Treasury officials in relation to the £30 billion surplus in the British economy.
answer_text: I am aware that the Office of National Statistics has recently published figures estimating a £30.4 billion public sector surplus for January 2026. I am currently engaging with Treasury in advance of the Spring Forecast, which is scheduled for 3 March 2026. I anticipate any changes to Whitehall spending decisions, and resulting Barnett consequentials for the Executive, will be set out at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of staff in her Department that are working on improving disability access.
answer_text: The challenges faced by people with disabilities cut right across my Department, including transport planning; public and community transport services and policy; maintenance of existing roads infrastructure and development of new roads projects; active travel planning; parking policy and enforcement; Blue Badge Scheme; and taxi policy and legislation, amongst others. As a result, I have identified “Including People” as one of the key strands of my “Foundations for a Better Future” programme and require that all staff in my Department and in other key delivery organisations, such as Translink, actively work towards improving disability access and addressing the transport challenges experienced by older, Deaf, and disabled users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered conducting a review of Translink’s management structure, including salaries paid to senior staff.
answer_text: Translink’s management structure and renumeration arrangements to senior staff is an operational matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any contracts and associated costs across the Health and Social Care Trusts with the Liaison Group.
answer_text: In the most recent full financial year, 2024/25, Health and Social Care Trusts spent £5k with the Liaison Group in relation to a VAT recovery exercise. The fee was offset against VAT recovered.3 Trusts have existing contracts with the Liaison Group.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the number of journalists that have had restricted access to Parliament Buildings imposed in the last ten years.
answer_text: Members of the Assembly Commission are not involved in these decisions but I understand that one journalist has had their Parliament Buildings media accreditation suspended in the last ten years. This sanction was imposed under the Assembly Commission Media Handbook following the exceptional situation of a deliberate breach of an Assembly media embargo on a committee report in February 2026.  I understand that following the imposition of the sanction, the matter was satisfactorily resolved and the suspension lifted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of adults that received and ADHD diagnosis and support privately in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i)	The Department of Health does not hold information on the number of adults that received an ADHD diagnosis and support privately.(ii)	The BHSCT can confirm that 62 adults are currently known to the Adult ADHD services as having an ADHD diagnosis and residing in the West Belfast area.  However, this figure reflects only those individuals actively known to and managed within the specialist ADHD service.  There may be additional adults with an ADHD diagnosis who are receiving care and treatment through core Mental Health services, and these individuals cannot be reliably identified through current data systems.  Therefore, the true number of adults with an ADHD diagnosis in West Belfast is likely to be higher than the figure recorded specifically by the Trust ADHD service.(iii)	The use of private clinics to complete ADHD assessments has increased as has private prescribing.  It is recognised that, in the absence of commissioned services for ADHD across Trusts, some individuals have opted to seek private diagnosis and treatment.  Clinics providing assessments have been notified to advise service users that they will remain under private care until they reach the top of the waiting list.  My department is unable to be responsible for any arrangements agreed between patients and private providers. 	Shared care arrangements are voluntary and not part of a GP’s core contractual obligations. GP’s can decline if they feel they lack the capacity or clinical assurance to safely manage prescribing and monitoring responsibilities. This may be because of reservations about the robustness of assessments carried out by certain private providers. Where there is uncertainty about diagnostic standards or continuity of specialist oversight, GP’s may be reluctant to assume responsibility for ongoing care.	The Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, aims to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.	The ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025 and is currently being considered, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter. In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support available to local councils that are struggling to regularly collect household bins on time.
answer_text: Article 20 of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 places a statutory requirement on district councils here to arrange for the collection of household waste in its district.  This requirement forms part of the wider legislative framework governing the proper management, handling and collection of waste by councils. As such, it remains the responsibility of each council to ensure that household waste collections are carried out regularly and in line with their legal obligations and consequently my Department does not provide any support to assist councils that are struggling to collect household bins on time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of adults that are recorded as having ADHD in West Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the shared care arrangements in place for patients in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total amount spent on investigating benefit fraud in 2024/25.
answer_text: My Department spent £2.6million on salary costs investigating benefit fraud in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether a list of organisations will be funded through their Department as part of Clause 7 of the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill (NIA Bill 19/22-27).
answer_text: Clause 7 of the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill does not change how funding is currently allocated.  Whilst current funding is primarily provided to the NI Refugee Resettlement Consortium partners and local councils, there is no list of potential future funded organisations. Funding will continue to be used to provide services which meet needs in line with Managing Public Money NI (MPMNI)

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the immigration status of people who will be eligible to receive support under Clause 7 of the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill (NIA Bill 19/22-27).
answer_text: Clause 7 of the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill enables support to be given to an individual who is an asylum-seeker or a dependant of an asylum-seeker; or has humanitarian protection or refugee status; or has permission to enter or remain in the UK under a UKG Scheme because of adverse circumstances in another place; or outside the immigration rules, because of adverse circumstances in another place.The clause allows the Executive Office to regularise spend of UKG funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of how the £16.7 million allocation for tackling benefit fraud and error will be spent.
answer_text: My Department invests significant resources each year to prevent, detect and correct fraud and error in the benefit system, with staff carrying out investigations, compliance checks and case reviews, supported by the systems and processes needed to ensure payments are accurate. In 2025-26, £16.7million will have been spent on this work. The following table details the allocation across Benefit Security Division business areas.Benefit Security Business Area2025-26 Budget AllocationFraud, Compliance & NINo Operations£5.9millionCase Preparation and Intervention Teams£3.1millionError Reduction, Decision Making and Information Security£2.9millionStandards Assurance Unit£2.9millionCorporate and Operational Delivery Team£1.9millionTotal£16.7million

answer_text: My Department’s benefit fraud and error estimates are published in the Department for Communities Annual Report & Accounts. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the value of unclaimed social security in each of the last five years, broken down by benefit type.
answer_text: My Department does not produce statistics on the amount of unclaimed social security benefits, other than Pension Credit.For Pension Credit, the most recently published statistics were released on 01 September 2022 and provided estimates for the 2019/20 financial year. The analysis estimates that there was £1.1m per week in unclaimed Pension Credit in 2019/20. The Department is not in a position to update these statistics at present due to data constraints relating to State Pension data. The Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to resolve these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the development of an Irish medium early years workforce strategy.
answer_text: I am aware of the recommendation in the research recently launched by Altram regarding the development of an Irishmedium early years workforce strategy. This recommendation will be considered as part of the wider strategic work currently underway within the Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any plans their Department has to ringfence funding in the 2026/27 financial year for work to prevent violence against women and girls.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered alternative approaches from that taken by the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to benefit fraud and error, in favour of focusing on simplifying the benefits system.
answer_text: My Department has not considered alternative approaches from that taken by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) as Northern Ireland operates under parity with DWP under the statutory parity framework set out in Section 87 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, which provides for aligned social security systems across the UK. Parity ensures that taxpayers and National Insurance contributors in Northern Ireland receive the same rights and benefits as those in Great Britain. Any tailored variations from DWP policy would breach parity and require funding from the NI Executive’s own budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered introducing legislation for an Environmental Protection Agency without Executive approval.
answer_text: The Ministerial Code, made under section 28A of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, provides the statutory framework within which Ministers must operate. Under paragraph 2.4 of the Ministerial Code, “any matter which is significant or controversial or which cuts across the responsibilities of two or more Ministers must be brought to the attention of the Executive Committee.”Given the significant and crosscutting nature of environmental governance in Northern Ireland, establishing an independent Environmental Protection Agency will require Executive consideration and agreement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of staff in his Department that work solely on supporting Irish medium education.
answer_text: Five Department of Education staff work primarily on supporting Irish-medium Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in the absence of new legislation, to outline any wraparound support he plans to put in place for school leavers with special educational needs.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any training opportunities provided to teachers and education workers in Irish medium settings when they are in post.
answer_text: All professional learning delivered by the Education Authority, and through the Department’s TransformED NI Strategy, is fully accessible to practitioners in Irishmedium settings. This includes the full continuum of support for teachers and leaders at all stages of their career, ensuring that Irishmedium educators benefit from the same highquality development opportunities available across statutory nursery, primary and postprimary schools.In addition to this universal offer, the EA provides a range of programmes tailored specifically for the Irishmedium sector. These include a dedicated Irishmedium strand within the Early Career Teacher Programme, enhanced support for Teacher Tutors through the medium of Irish, and bespoke leadership programmes at both primary and postprimary level. Further assistance is provided through specialist training in pedagogy, SEND, digital technology and immersive education, as well as access to the EALearn platform and an annual conference, delivered with St Mary’s University College, focused on digital and AI approaches within Irishmedium education.Additional training opportunities are available for Irish-medium teachers and education workers through a number of organisations outside DE and the EA.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether minutes were taken at the meeting in the UK Embassy in Washington on 14 March 2025,which included the deputy First Minister, the Minister for Communities and the Minister of Health.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any discussions with the UK Government prior to the launch of the National Cancer Plan for England, particularly in relation to the new £10 million travel fund for young cancer patients; and (ii) whether any agreement has been made as to whether the fund will be available to patients in Northern Ireland, in particular those who travel to England for their treatment and care.
answer_text: I welcome the announcement of a travel fund for young cancer patients in England, however, health is a devolved responsibility in Northern Ireland and decisions on the design and funding of local support schemes must therefore be taken within the context of the Northern Ireland Executive’s budget and priorities.Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly.While my Department has considered the possibility of a similar scheme in NI, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.For children and young people who are approved and require access to specialist care and treatment outside Northern Ireland, my Department’s travel policy offers support and financial assistance. This includes flights, accommodation, onward road transport costs and funding for a patient escort based on clinical need. Both parents are approved and funded to travel with paediatric patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment made of evidence and modelling by Young Lives vs Cancer on the financial impact of travel costs to treatment being experienced by children and young people with cancer and their families, including those missing or delaying treatment as a result of these costs; and (ii) whether they will work with Young Lives vs Cancer to ensure all under 25s with cancer are supported with travel costs following commitments from UK Government to do this via a £10 million travel fund.
answer_text: I welcome the announcement of a travel fund for young cancer patients in England, however, health is a devolved responsibility in Northern Ireland and decisions on the design and funding of local support schemes must therefore be taken within the context of the Northern Ireland Executive’s budget and priorities.Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly.While my Department has considered the possibility of a similar scheme in NI, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.For children and young people who are approved and require access to specialist care and treatment outside Northern Ireland, my Department’s travel policy offers support and financial assistance. This includes flights, accommodation, onward road transport costs and funding for a patient escort based on clinical need. Both parents are approved and funded to travel with paediatric patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of the savings from benefit fraud will be retained by his Department.
answer_text: My department submitted a Business Case to HM Treasury ahead of the Autumn Budget, seeking approval for NI to retain a proportion of the Annually Managed Expenditure (AME) savings generated through my Department’s fraud and error reduction initiatives. This will require a £9m upfront investment in 2026-27, with recurrent funding from then on. It is envisaged this will generate £47m of AME savings from 2027-28 onwards.Clearly this will require HM Treasury approval to (i) the business case; (ii) the quantum of the savings that can be retained and used by the Executive and (iii) the reclassification of the AME savings to Resource DEL. In line with the Fresh Start Agreement, I will be seeking a 50% share of the savings.HM Treasury has confirmed that UK Government will work with the Executive over the coming weeks and months to explore options for tackling welfare fraud and error. This will include funding models and the potential to share a portion of the resulting savings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether total income, rather than profit, is a more accurate method of assessing rates for a hospitality venue.
answer_text: Rates for licensed and hospitality premises are assessed using the Receipts and Expenditure method, in line with longstanding rating and industry practice and the statutory requirement to determine rental value at the valuation date. A detailed explanation of the methodology has been deposited in the Assembly Library under Deposited Paper Number 2034, and is available through the Assembly’s Deposited Papers collection, found here:Deposited Papers

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact of potholes on the health and safety of (i) wheelchair users; (ii) pedestrians; and (iii) cyclists.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a series of targeted footway improvement works across Belfast in partnership with our environmental contractor.The works involved clearing back the edges of footways to remove debris and overgrown vegetation which was reducing the space available for pedestrians and making it difficult to navigate. The work has now restored the full width of footways, making them safer, more accessible, and easier to use.Routine maintenance activities such as carriageway, footway and cycleway defect repair, grass cutting and weed control, gully emptying and maintenance of street lighting and traffic signals. all contribute to road safety, including for pedestrians, wheelchair users and cyclists, and have been affected by underfunding over many years.  I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. As part of this, I have recently allocated an additional £7.85 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work his Department has carried out to calculate how much is unclaimed in social security annually.
answer_text: My Department does not produce statistics on the amount of unclaimed social security benefits, other than Pension Credit.For Pension Credit, the most recently published statistics were released on 01 September 2022 and provided estimates for the 2019/20 financial year. The analysis estimates that there was £1.1m per week in unclaimed Pension Credit in 2019/20. The Department is not in a position to update these statistics at present due to data constraints relating to State Pension data. The Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to resolve these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of hospitality venues that were due to have reduced rates payments under the paused Reval 2026.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide the number of hospitality properties that would have seen a reduction in rates under the draft Reval 2026 as the rates payment calculation includes the regional and district rate, and for 2026/7 these have now been set for the current (2023) values. In the absence of those Reval 2026 district and regional rates, it is not possible to calculate the rates impact or identify which properties would have experienced a reduction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the financial impact the pause in Reval 2026 will have on public finances.
answer_text: Stopping Reval 2026 will have no impact on public finances. Revaluations are revenue-neutral, meaning they do not change the overall amount of rates collected; they only redistribute liability across the valuation list. As a result, the decision to stop Reval 2026 simply means that rate bills from April will be based on the existing Reval 2023 valuation list, with no effect on total revenue raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether (i) small businesses; and (ii) large hospitality venues, will benefit from the pause in Reval 2026.
answer_text: The decision to stop Reval 2026 means that the rates liability for all non-domestic properties, including small businesses and large hospitality venues will continue to be assessed using the existing Reval 2023 valuation list from April. Revaluations are revenue-neutral, so stopping the process does not alter the overall amount of rates collected; it simply maintains the current valuation basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his response to the Independent Review of Education, Investing in a Better Future report that states that education in Northern Ireland is highly underfunded.
answer_text: I concur with the findings of the Independent Review of Education which identified systemic and long-term underfunding as a central factor driving the financial crisis in Northern Ireland’s education system. My Department has endured historic underfunding for many years, operating from an inadequate baseline that has only been sustained through reliance on in-year allocations.This represents a profound structural weakness.  Based on the draft Executive Budget 2026-2029/30, my Department’s financial position is only set to worsen over this next three-year spending period.  This cannot continue to be ignored nor can we continue to neglect education through three more years of underfunding. Whilst I will continue to advocate for additional resources for the education sector, I also urge the Member to join me in making representations to the Finance Minister to provide a fair and realistic budget settlement for education.  For more information in terms of what the Draft Budget means for Education, please refer to my Written Ministerial Statement of 6 January Written Ministerial Statement of 6 January.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education his assessment of the current income thresholds for education maintenance allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 39019/22-27, given that tree cutting on the bank of the River Faughan and Tributaries Special Area of Conservation continues, whether his Department will intervene to stop further risk of harm to the integrity of this internationally important designation and the protected species.
answer_text: My Department became aware of the potential unauthorised removal of trees along the River Faughan and Tributaries Special Area of Conservation and Area of Special Scientific Interest on 20 January 2026.My Department treats any potential damage to designated sites with the utmost seriousness and, in accordance with Article 46 of the Environment (NI) Order 2002, as amended, has initiated an investigation into the alleged matter.  NIEA officers have since visited the site on a number of occasions and are liaising with the relevant parties and other agencies.As this investigation is ongoing, it would be inappropriate to comment further on the detail of at this stage, as doing so may prejudice any potential future proceedings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of an independent review process for people who have been de-registered by their GP.
answer_text: I welcome the Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman’s publication of the Overview Report on the Removal of Patients from their General Practice Service and the consideration given to this important issue.I fully recognise the importance of appropriate access to General Medical Services provided by GP practices under the GMS Contract, and the vital service delivered by GPs across Northern Ireland.I acknowledge NIPSO’s recognition of steps already taken by my Department to address some of the concerns identified in the report. My Department will give due regard to the three recommendations detailed in the NIPSO report as part of its ongoing work to ensure that all patients have access to the General Practice services they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 39019/22-27, to detail (i) where in the Forestry Act the felling of trees in hedgerows is excluded from the requirement for a tree felling license; and (ii) any environmental protection that is afforded to Northern Ireland Priority Habitats, such as hedgerows.
answer_text: My Department’s responsibilities in relation to protecting woodland trees are governed under the Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010. Felling licences are required to fell trees in areas 0.2 hectares or more where the land is a woodland. Trees within hedgerows are not within the scope of the act.Northern Ireland does not have specific legislation preventing the removal of hedgerows. However, environmental protection is afforded to hedgerows and priority habitats where they occur within designated sites, (Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs); Special Areas of Conservation (SACs); Special Protection Areas (SPAs) and Ramsar sites). Additionally, in some circumstances hedgerow trees may be protected though other legislation such as planning controls, Tree Preservation Orders and under the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985 due to species utilising habitats, e.g. nesting birds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions that GP practices have not acted in accordance with statutory processes or guidance in relation to striking patients off their register in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Registration to, and removal from, GP Practice lists is facilitated on a regional basis through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) under direction from GP Practices. BSO is commissioned to provide this service on behalf of my Department.  BSO do not have a record of numbers removed which are not in accordance with regulations. The table below shows the number of patient removal request transactions by Northern Ireland GP Practices, by subcategory.Table 1 – Patient removal request transactions by subcategory by Northern Ireland GP Practices.Description20212022202320242025Outside area removal requests8,7187,2489,0339,1599,334Removal at Surgery Request259315359338297Removal as the result of an incident reported to the police) 4043453648Removals information is based on data held by BSO relating to registered patients.‘Outside area removal requests’ are submitted when a patient moves to an address outside the Practice catchment area.‘Removal at Surgery Request’ refers to removal requests submitted after a breakdown of the Doctor/Patient relationship’.‘Removal as result of an incident reported to the police’  – these patients are registered as violence patients under paragraph 21 of the Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004.Removal request transactions do not always result in a patient being removed from a GP Practice via the same transaction type.  For example, in the case of outside area removal requests a patient may have registered with another GP Practice before the removal request has been actioned.Patients removed at doctor's request retain entitlement to healthcare but are not immediately re-registered with a GP practice.Individual Patients may have more than one removal transaction request over the period.Removal figures have been derived from a live administrative database updated as new information is received and hence may not perfectly reconcile with published statistics.Patients may be removed for reasons other than at Doctors request e.g. Death, moving to another part of the UK, leaving the UK, whereabouts unknown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider allowing families of asylum-seeking children with severe learning difficulties, who are enrolled in a special school, to submit a statement of special educational needs in lieu of a diagnosis from a health trust.
answer_text: Children who are seeking asylum in Northern Ireland are fully entitled to access all health and social care services on the basis of professionally assessed need using the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) framework. The assessment of need determines the support a child or family are assessed as required, and the social work team who is best placed to provide the support assessed as being needed by the child or family.The pathways available for a child in the asylum process to access  disability support are the same as for all children, Trusts will have eligibility criteria to access services, however assessed need would be met through the service that child or family are referred to. There are multiple pathways to access disability support, including through GPs, and by direct referral to the appropriate Health and Social Care Trust.   Assessments of children with learning difficulties are undertaken by the Department of Education. The Department of Education will ask Department of Health colleagues for information to inform any assessment, input from family would be part of the assessment process but would not be accepted in lieu of professional Health Input.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of pedestrian crossing projects that have been completed in the Belfast City Council area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I can advise the Eastern Division’s controlled crossing priority list remains live, and as such is subject to constant change. However, at present there are currently 76 sites that meet the initial threshold for further consideration on the division’s priority list, however this list remains live and is subject to change. Details of all planned schemes are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Within the past five years there has been 11 pedestrian crossings installed in the Belfast City Council area in the last five years, including developer led schemes and controlled pedestrian phases within a junctions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the advice his Department would give to patients who have been struck off their GP register.
answer_text: THE DEPARTMENT OF HEALTHMIKE NESBITT MLACastle Buildings, Ballymiscaw,Stormont, Belfast BT4 3SQGerry Carroll MLANI AssemblyParliament Buildings BallymiscawStormont20 February 2026AQW 39858/22-27Gerry Carroll MLA asked:To ask the Minister of Health to detail the advice his Department would give to patients who have been struck off their GP register. ANSWERGP Practices are permitted under The Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 along with their General Medical Services contract, to remove patients from their practice lists for a range of reasons. If a patient believes their removal is not in line with regulations, they should approach the practice in the first instance to try and resolve the matter locally. If resolution isn’t possible, the Primary Care Complaints Team, in my Department’s SPPG can act as intermediary or honest broker between a patient and a practice, where both parties agree. Registration to, and removal from, GP Practice lists is facilitated on a regional basis through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) under direction from GP Practices. BSO is commissioned to provide this service on behalf of my Department.  BSO has advised that the process to register with a new practice is as follows for all new patients:Patient presents at a surgery and requests to be registered.Patient completes registration form and provides required documentation to the GP Practice.GP Practice register patient on their clinical system and send the paperwork to BSO.If a patient is refused registration at 3 or more practices, they can contact the BSO directly, at that stage the BSO will allocate the patient to a GP practice within the patient’s locality.For general registration enquiries the BSO manage a helpline – 028 9536 0333.MIKE NESBITT MLABACKGROUND NOTE…………………………… AQW 39858/22-27This question follows the publication of the NIPSO report on 3 February 2026 “The removal of patients from their General Practice service | NIPSORegulations relating to patient removals are included in the GMS Regulations (N Ireland) 2004. There is a range of reasons for removal by the contractor, such as the patient moving outside the practice boundary including abroad, a breakdown in the doctor-patient relationship, violent patients and, on occasion, the householders at the home of a violent patient as the doctor or their staff could be assaulted again on a home visit. Registration to, and removal from, GP Practice lists is facilitated through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) under direction from GP Practices. BSO provide this service on behalf of SPPG.  FOR ANSWER ON: Drafted by: Elaine Fitzsimons, SPPG – 9 February 2026Cleared by Head of Division Dr Caren Walsh, GP Medical Adviser, SPPG – 10 February 2026 Cleared by Special Adviser: For use by Private OfficeCopy distribution List:

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process by which an asylum-seeking child can avail of a disability support worker, in the absence of a diagnosis from a local health trust.
answer_text: Children who are seeking asylum in Northern Ireland are fully entitled to access all health and social care services on the basis of professionally assessed need using the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) framework. The assessment of need determines the support a child or family are assessed as required, and the social work team who is best placed to provide the support assessed as being needed by the child or family.The pathways available for a child in the asylum process to access  disability support are the same as for all children, Trusts will have eligibility criteria to access services, however assessed need would be met through the service that child or family are referred to. There are multiple pathways to access disability support, including through GPs, and by direct referral to the appropriate Health and Social Care Trust.   Assessments of children with learning difficulties are undertaken by the Department of Education. The Department of Education will ask Department of Health colleagues for information to inform any assessment, input from family would be part of the assessment process but would not be accepted in lieu of professional Health Input.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of times the PSNI has made use of the facial matching facility contained within the Police National Database since November 2021.
answer_text: Use of the facial searching capability within the Police National Database is an operational matter for the PSNI. However, PSNI has confirmed they have used the facial searching capability within the Police National Database 2153 times since November 2021. As the PSNI is operationally independent and is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board you may wish to contact them directly for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the PSNI has ever used live facial recognition technology.
answer_text: Use of Live Facial Recognition (LFR) technology by the PSNI is an operational matter. The PSNI is operationally independent and is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Any questions in relation to the PSNI’s use of LFR should be directed to the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the circumstances under which facial recognition software is used by the PSNI.
answer_text: Use of facial recognition software by the PSNI is an operational matter. The PSNI is operationally independent and is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Any questions in relation to the use of facial recognition software by the PSNI should be directed to the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support in place for artists who are from deprived areas.
answer_text: The Arts Council, an arm’s length body of my Department, is the responsible body for the distribution of funding and development of the arts in Northern Ireland which includes supporting artists from all areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures that will be included in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill that will benefit those working in the hospitality sector.
answer_text: A wide range of the measures that will be included in the Employment Rights Bill will benefit all workers, including those working in the hospitality sector. The Bill will include provisions requiring the fair and transparent allocation of tips, gratuities and service charges, which will be of specific benefit to workers in this sector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the steps they have taken to engage with those who have been subject to sexual abuse in church settings.
answer_text: Historical clerical child abuse (HCCA) and all forms of sexual abuse in faith settings have had a devastating impact on those affected and we express our deepest sympathy and solidarity to all victims and survivors of this terrible abuse. Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors, listening to their stories and hearing their views on what is needed.  This has included attending meetings of the Reference group of victims and survivors in September 2024 and May 2025 and meeting with individual victims and survivors.  Junior Ministers have recently invited some victims and survivors to meet them in the coming weeks.In addition, officials have liaised closely with the Reference group of victims and survivors to develop the terms of reference for the 3 research projects that were completed last year, regularly update on progress of the work, and discuss the findings and recommendations. We are very grateful to all those who have participated and shared their experiences.We remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors, whose views we will take into account as we move forward in our deliberations on this complex and sensitive issue.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Climate Commissioner.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 were affirmed by the Assembly and made by The Executive Office (TEO) on 8 April 2025, and came into force on 9 April 2025, providing legal authority to TEO to appoint the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner (NICC). From April, work has been ongoing on the establishing stage of the NICC which includes the business case process, staffing, accommodation and office infrastructure, and the preparatory work on the draft documents required to run a public appointment process in accordance with the CPANI Code of Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any cross-departmental consideration that has been given to a fund from all departments to protect the organisations impacted by the changes to the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided to previous question AQW 39456/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in West Belfast that have been struck off their GP register in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of patient removal request transactions by West Belfast Assembly Area GP Practices, by subcategory, can be found in Table 1 below.Table 1 – Patient removal request transactions by subcategory – by West Belfast Assembly Area GP Practices.Description20212022202320242025Outside area removal requests452373467458476Removal at Surgery Request2828395227Para21 (Removal as the result of an incident reported to the police) Removal0*4**GP Practices have been assigned to Assembly Area 'West Belfast' based on Practice Postcode.Removals information is based on data held by BSO relating to registered patients.‘Removal at Surgery Request’ refers to removal requests submitted after a breakdown of the Doctor/Patient relationship’.Counts of less than 3 have been replaced with an *.Removal request transactions do not always result in a patient being removed from a GP Practice via the same transaction type.  For example, in the case of outside area removal requests a patient may have registered with another GP Practice before the removal request has been actioned.Patients removed at doctor's request retain entitlement to healthcare but are not immediately re-registered with a GP practice.Individual Patients may have more than one removal transaction request over the period.Removal figures have been derived from a live administrative database updated as new information is received and hence may not perfectly reconcile with published statistics.Patients may be removed for reasons other than at Doctors request e.g. Death, moving to another part of the UK, leaving the UK, whereabouts unknown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the appointment of the Just Transition Commission.
answer_text: My Department undertook an extensive 10-week public consultation which sought views on the policy options to establish the Just Transition Commission. The proposals, including seeking views on potential additional sectors to be represented on the Commission beyond those mandated by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 were positively received and overwhelmingly supported. A summary report of the consultation responses and findings from the engagement sessions was published on the DAERA website on 21 May 2025.I am pleased to advise that following engagement with my Executive colleagues that agreement for the draft Regulations to be laid in the Assembly was recently obtained. Subsequently, the draft Regulations were shared with the AERA Committee on 5 February 2026, and officials are scheduled to brief the Committee in early March following which arrangements will be made to lay the draft Regulations in the Assembly for further scrutiny and debate. Once the Regulations are agreed by the Assembly and become law my Department will then be able to commence the process to appoint members to the Commission in line with the Commissioner for Public Appointments Northern Ireland (CPANI) guidance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the (i) budgetary allocation; and (ii) timeline relating to the implementation of recommendations formulated by the inter-departmental working group, survivors and civic society groups on clerical sexual abuse.
answer_text: We are considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse and have not yet taken any decisions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of an independent, public inquiry into clerical sexual abuse.
answer_text: We are currently considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse reports. We have not yet completed our assessment of any proposals.We are committed to progressing work on this complex and sensitive issue, and our officials will continue to keep victims and survivors informed of developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has liaised with the Public Health Agency in relation to the use of air filtration, including portable air filters, in schools, particularly in light of the Public Health Agency and Business Services Organisation using this technology in their offices.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) relies on the guidance of the Public Health Authority (PHA) which has the expertise to take a scientific approach to determining the merits of air filtration and its applicability to school settings. All actions taken by the EA to date have been guided entirely by the advice provided by the PHA and Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the provision for environmental education in the post-primary curriculum.
answer_text: Environmental education is currently embedded throughout the post-primary curriculum, particularly within the Environment and Society Area of Learning at Key Stage 3, and through subjects such as Science and Technology and Learning for Life and Work, where pupils explore issues including climate systems, sustainability and global responsibility.As part of the wider curriculum reform programme, the Curriculum Taskforce is now developing a new statutory curriculum framework for Northern Ireland. This work will include consideration of the future role and positioning of environmental education within the revised curriculum. A draft of the new framework will be issued for public consultation and wider stakeholder engagement in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will publish the three reports by independent experts on clerical sexual abuse, including the set of recommendations agreed by the inter-departmental working group, survivors and public interest groups
answer_text: We are considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse, and our officials will shortly be engaging with the Committee for The Executive Office on this matter.  No decision has been taken on the publication of the research reports.Officials remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors to ensure they are kept informed of developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding provided by his Department to target economic inactivity in West Belfast in each of the last five years.
answer_text: All employability support provided by and through my department is focused on maximising employment, increasing economic activity and reducing economic inactivity.  This includes a wide range of provision, from one-to-one support provided by work coaches across my Jobs and Benefits offices, recruitment events, and tailored programmes including JobStart, Access to Work, Workable and the Condition Management Programme, as well as responsive flexible activity funded through the local Labour Market Partnerships and Neighbourhood Renewal programmes. The total West Belfast Neighbourhood Renewal funding provided to organisations with projects whose primary aim is to target economic inactivity in the last 5 financial years is:YearAmount2020/21£543,9192021/22£587,0962022/23£623,6462023/24£578,8542024/25£607,680The total funding provided to Belfast Labour Market Partnership (LMP), since LMPs were established in December 2021, is:Belfast Labour Market PartnershipYearAmount2021/22£1,116,6002022/23£1,258,2442023/24£1,123,6972024/25£1,204,7182025/26£1,397,378

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the British Government’s proposed cuts to the Local Growth Fund on small charities, particularly those operating in economically deprived areas.
answer_text: I am aware of the significant impacts that the reduced financial allocations proposed by the UK Government under the Local Growth Fund will have on community and voluntary organisations delivering important provision to support those people furthest from the labour market.The Finance Minister is leading on engagement with the UK Government to request a reversal of the proposed capital and revenue split under this Fund and I will continue to work with other Executive Ministers in seeking to address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to providing match funding for the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: Whilst the Department of Finance has published a draft three-year Budget for consultation, it is clear that both the Resource and Capital funding position remain extremely constrained. I will, however, continue to discuss this issue with Executive colleagues to explore solutions to address the significant impacts that the reduced budgets proposed by the UK Government under the Local Growth Fund will have on community and voluntary organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has engaged with the British Government over schemes similar to getting Britain working being made available.
answer_text: I and my officials engage with the UK Government on a regular basis. Employability provision is a devolved matter for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with officials about plugging the gap for Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: I engage regularly with my officials on the Local Growth Fund, who in turn lead an interdepartmental working group on future funding that meets regularly to monitor the British Government’s position on the fund, and consider the implications of that. I have been very clear that the current Resource funding shortfall is a direct consequence of a succession of bad decisions by the British Government in how this fund will be delivery here. I have also made it clear that it is unreasonable for Westminster to expect the Executive to step in and address the harm that it has caused with its failed approach to Local Growth. This is particularly the case at a time when the Executive’s Budget position is already highly constrained and falls short of what is needed to deliver sustainable public services. I have repeatedly raised issues around the Local Growth Fund, including the need for a change to the Capital / Resource funding split, with a succession of British Government Ministers. The most recent correspondence being with the Ministry of Housing, Communities, and Local Government (MHCLG) Minister, Miatta Fahnbulleh, on 23 December 2025. The Executive also collectively wrote to Steve Reed, MHCLG Secretary of State on 22 January 2026 calling for this to be urgently addressed.  While we await Mr Reed’s response, I wrote to Executive Colleagues on 12 February asking them to consider if there are opportunities within their departments to support the sector in other ways and suggesting they meet with sector representatives to discuss this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Housing Executive became aware of problems with cladding at Carnet House tower block.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In June 2022, the Housing Executive commissioned Fire-SERT from the University of Ulster to assess our tower blocks against the new legislation and guidance, this included testing a sample of the cladding system at Carnet House in a controlled environment. In February 2023, following receipt of the initial report, it identified concerns around the performance of the cladding in the event of a fire. The Housing Executive then proactively commissioned a Fire Risk Appraisal of the External Wall of Carnet House which took place in October 2023, and the final report issued with their findings in February 2024.  Following the receipt of this report, the Building Safety Team implemented a series of mitigations immediately in respect of the recommendations. NIFRS, residents and local representatives were informed, and kept briefed throughout.  More recently, the Housing Executive reviewed longer term options, following separate deterioration of the fabric of the tower block, due to water ingress from the roof, and this comprehensive review of options led to the decision to clear the block.   The residents where continually updated throughout this period.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding the REACH programme received in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Education allocated funding to the Education Authority for the REACH programme in previous financial years as follows:2025/26	£920,0002024/25	£857,0002023/24	£991,0002022/23	£944,0002021/22	£899,000

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work being completed by the steering group for speech and language.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised representatives from Health and Social Care Trusts and Public Health Agency have worked with the EA to carry out a regional review of all specialist provision for speech and language.   Once final proposals for a regional pathway to access a consistent model of speech and language specialist provision have been completed and approved by the Department of Education and Department of Health, agreement from each Health Trust will be required to resource any additional Speech and Language Classes prior to them being set up.
