question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of statutory deadlines his Department missed in the relation to the climate change in the last four years.
answer_text: In the last four years my Department has missed six statutory deadlines specified by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. In the majority of these cases, the absence of a sitting Executive and Assembly prevented my Department from progressing to specified deadlines in the Act.I am pleased however to advise that since coming into office legislation has now been made to progress three of these missed deadlines. Specifically, Regulations were made for Public Body Reporting, the setting of our 2030 /2040 emissions reduction targets and the setting of our first three carbon budgets covering the period from 2023 to 2037. I am also pleased to report that for the remaining requirements substantive progress has been made. As required by the Act 16-week consultations have been completed on our first Climate Action Plan (CAP) and the setting of our Fourth Carbon Budget which covers the period 2037-2042. I intend to bring these both to the Executive shortly for agreement with the remaining deliverable relating to progress reporting on the CAP which is dependent upon its publication. Work however has been undertaken in the background in preparation for this.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the conditions of care workers in the private, or independent, sector.
answer_text: Data quality within encompass is monitored by the Trusts, and corrective action is taken where issues are identified. As unauthorised parallel or informal records can create inconsistencies and pose potential patient safety risks, Trusts and the regional encompass team continue to ensure there are structured feedback routes and clear mechanisms for staff to raise concerns or request further support to reduce the likelihood of these occurring. As with any clinical system, the quality of outputs depends on the quality of information entered. Trusts will continue to support staff to use encompass safely, consistently and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of care workers that are on a zero hours contract in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Just Transition fund.
answer_text: Work on developing the high-level policy framework for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture is ongoing. Initial stakeholder engagement was carried out in autumn 2025.  Capital DEL of £12.3m for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture was secured in 2025-26.I expect to consider a draft policy framework for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture in early spring 2026 and, subject to my approval, that will be followed by a full public consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) hernia; and (ii) cataract, operations that took place within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details the number of hernia and cataract operations that took place within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, over the last five years.YearHernia operationCataract operation2021188 patients724 patients2022280 patients1510 patients2023434 patients1063 patients2024393 patients463 patients2025439 patients671 patientsIn order to increase productivity, efficiency and activity around low-complexity cataract cases, the majority of cataract operations are carried out by visiting Belfast HSCT surgical teams within the dedicated Cataract Day Procedure Centre (DPC) network.  Therefore, the above figures do not include patients outsourced to the regional Day Procedure Centres at South Tyrone Hospital and Downe Hospital.  These figures are detailed below.YearSouthern HSCTSouth Eastern HSCT2020/21466 patients1332 patients2021/221127 patients2498 patients2022/231706 patients2601 patients2023/241672 patients2276 patients2024/251279 patients2384 patientsThe Cataract DPC network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to manage lists on a regional basis. The Day Procedure Centre cataract service has had a positive impact on waiting times. Total regional cataract surgical activity, including at the three speciality centres for cataracts at Downe, South Tyrone and Mid Ulster Hospitals, has treated over 30,000 patients treated since December 2019. This approach was designed to provide a more equitable service across Northern Ireland, while minimising short-notice cancellations that can occur in acute hospital settings when emergency cases arise. Further initiatives are also underway to expand capacity within core HSC ophthalmology services. This includes recent investment to deliver an additional 1,500 cataract assessments and treatments through an insourced independent sector arrangement at the South Eastern HSC Trust’s Cataract Day Procedure Centre in Downe Hospital. This additional activity will directly benefit patients currently waiting within the Belfast HSC Trust, with procedures scheduled to take place during the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of occasions that planning applications have been called in over the last five years.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications be referred to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council.  A similar ‘call in’ power exists for conservation area, listed building and hazardous substance consent applications. The ‘call in’ function provides a necessary safeguard where there are applications that raise issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level. A planning application can be ‘called in’ by my Department at any time up until a council has issued a decision. Whilst it is a discretionary power, it is generally only used in exceptional circumstances as it is recognised that councils are best placed to deal with the vast majority of planning applications within their own council areas. Table 1 below provides details on the number of planning applications ‘called in’ by my Department since April 2021.Year (01 April - 31 March)No. of applications ‘called in’ by  Department 2021/202272022/202312023/202422024/202502025-Present0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of occasions his Department offered sustainability advice to small farmers in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department’s College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) has responsibility for delivering knowledge transfer to all farm businesses in Northern Ireland, irrespective of farm size, through awareness sessions, training and peer to peer learning. Sustainability advice is embedded in all the knowledge transfer programmes offered by CAFRE. In the past five years over 52,000 farmers and farm business members have availed of this training and advice. In addition, all-farm businesses have access to sustainability advice through the CAFRE website which can be accessed at any time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether standardised environmental assessments exist for local councils in relation to planning applications.
answer_text: Local councils have to undertake Habitat Regulation Assessments prior to determining planning applications.Planning applicants may have to undertake Environmental Impact Assessments to accompany planning applications. A Biodiversity Checklist is avaliable for planning applicants to complete. These assessments provide information to help inform local council planning decisions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding the living with water programme has received in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NI Water determines how much of its annual budget is allocated to the development and delivery of its Living With Water projects. The total funding allocated to Living With Water in each of the last five years is outlined below. 	Year	21/22	22/23	23/24	24/25	25/26		£M	£M	£M	£M	£M	Total	21.70	22.76	30.05	35.96	1.25These figures comprise funding for DfI and NI Water, Living With Water in Belfast Plan projects and also include the development of the Living With Water in Derry Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any plans are in place for rodent control at Casement Park when building works commence.
answer_text: This is a matter for the UCGAA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether a tree felling licence (i) is required; and (ii) has been issued by his Department, for the removal of mature broadleaf trees and woodland taking place around Brookhill House, Ardlough Road, Derry, and along the banks of the River Faughan and Tributaries Special Area of Conservation and Area of Special Scientific Interest.
answer_text: My Department has not received a felling licence application for cutting trees at Brookhill House, Ardlough Road, Derry. A forestry official checked the area where tree felling had taken place and found that they were single trees and trees in hedgerows. Therefore, a felling licence was not required as the trees cut do not fall within the scope of the forestry act, which applies to the felling of woodlands of 0.2 hectares and above.NIEA is investigating possible unconsented removal of trees/hedges at this location within the boundary of River Faughan and Tributaries Special Area of Conservation and Area of Special Scientific Interest under the Environment (NI) Order 2002 and later amendments.My Department’s Water Pollution Response & Enforcement Team was notified on the 20 January 2026 of tree removal at Walkers Burn, Ardlough Road, Drumahoe, Co. Londonderry. A Water Quality Inspector attended to assess risk from suspended solid ingress to the waterway. Inspection confirmed no pollution to waterway from tree removal activities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has raised the issue of data centres and their intensive use of water and energy at any North South Ministerial Council meetings since taking up post.
answer_text: I have not raised the issue of data centres and their intensive use of water and energy at either of the Transport Sectoral or Inland Waterways North South Ministerial Council meetings I have attended since taking up post.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of asbestos removal that has been carried out at Casement Park in the last ten years.
answer_text: This is a matter for the UCGAA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work that has been completed by contractors at Casement Park to ensure that boundary walls at properties adjacent to Casement Park are protected following the commencement of works.
answer_text: UCGAA have restarted their site clearance and demolition works at Casement Park under their own contract and expense and all works must comply with statutory obligations.  I refer you to Ulster GAA for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how Japanese Knotweed is being treated at the Casement Park site.
answer_text: UCGAA have restarted their site clearance and demolition works at Casement Park under their own contract and expense and all works must comply with statutory obligations.  I refer you to Ulster GAA for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work ongoing to provide bilingual voice messaging on the Metro network.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on repairing roads along the Glider route damaged by the Glider itself since its introduction.
answer_text: My officials undertake regular maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, are noted. However, my Department does not hold data in such a format as to identify the information requested, as we could not attribute the reason for requiring repairs solely to the Glider.Road repairs are prioritised to address those posing the most serious risk to road users. Repairs necessary for continued road safety are also carried out subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times he has met with representatives of independent and private healthcare providers since taking up post.
answer_text: In my role as Health Minister, I have met with a wide range of stakeholders, including representatives from independent and private sector organisations, in the course of official business. Given the volume and breadth of engagements undertaken during my term, it is not possible to provide a comprehensive breakdown of every individual or organisation met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any communication the Education Authority has with schools in relation to air ventilation; and (ii) how often this information is communicated.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) issued ‘Ventilation for Schools Guidance’ in December 2021. The guidance was issued alongside the provision of carbon dioxide (CO2) monitors to schools. Although originally developed in response to Covid-19, the guidance focuses on ventilation more broadly and remains relevant for ongoing use. Schools can also seek further ventilation advice by contacting either the EA Health and Safety team or EA Maintenance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the (i) Secretary of State; and (ii) Prime Minister in relation to the US President’s tariff proposals.
answer_text: We have engaged directly with the Prime Minister and with the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, Jonathan Reynolds on the issue of US tariffs. We continue to argue the case for local businesses to ensure that our economy and businesses here are protected as far as possible in both trade negotiations and in terms of any countermeasures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the roll-out of schemes such as Drive Smart and their impact on the right to privacy of individuals with a disability.
answer_text: My department takes seriously its commitment to data privacy and adheres to data protection legislation and related guidance published by the Information Commissioners Office. All handling of personal information is conducted in accordance with the data protection principles of lawful, fair, and transparent processing.Drive Smart is a telematics initiative used on the Motability Scheme, which assists people with their mobility. My department has no role in the Motability Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the banks that attend the banking roundtable.
answer_text: My Banking Roundtable in December 2025 was attended by UK Finance. As the industry body for banking and financial services, it collectively represented the local banks. A full list of its members is available here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any Equality Impact Assessments have been carried out on pensions, including investment strategies and their investments in arms companies, and how this relates to racial and religious minorities.
answer_text: My Department has direct responsibility for the Civil Service Pensions schemes only. Any new legislative change or update to the schemes is routinely screened for equality impacts with reference to the requirements set out in Equality Commission guidance on duties for public authorities under section 75 of the NI Act 1998.The main Civil Service Pension Scheme is an unfunded defined benefit scheme and operates on what is known as a 'pay-as-you-go' basis. This means that there is no investment fund or strategy.The Civil Service also provides employees with access to two alternative workplace pension defined contribution arrangements: The Partnership pension account  A Stakeholder Pension accountScottish Widows and Standard Life are the current independent pension providers for the Civil Service defined contribution arrangements. Money invested in either of the available defined contribution options is the responsibility of the provider.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount their Department spent on legal counsel representation in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of access to bank accounts his Department has when making decisions relating to people receiving benefits.
answer_text: My Department does not have direct access to the bank accounts of benefit claimants. However, claimants can be asked to provide bank statements to confirm they are receiving the correct benefit entitlement where they have declared capital or as part of a review. In addition, Officers from my Department’s Benefit Security Division may request information from banks during criminal investigations into suspected benefit fraud.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount his Department spent on legal counsel representation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The amount spent by my Department on legal counsel representation in each of the last five years is detailed in the table below: Financial YearAmount21/22£63,414.0822/23£37,499.4223/24£66,579.5024/25£39,425.6625/26 (YTD End December)£270,018.74This table represents the costs of Barrister / KC / QC engaged to represent my Department and excludes plaintiff’s barristers’ costs, solicitors’ costs and DSO costs. The increase to the fees for 2025/26 is the result of the Covid Inquiry (£166k);  Judicial Reviews (£78k) and other Legal counsel advice (£24k).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department spent on legal counsel representation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The amount spent on legal counsel representation by my Department in each of the last five financial years is provided in the table.  2024-252023-242022-232021-222020-21£000£000£000£000£000Department485389354299214DVA21727732117TOTAL702416427320231The spend on legal counsel representation will vary annually depending upon the nature of the legal advice sought.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount her Department spent on legal counsel representation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The format in which financial information is recorded does not allow legal counsel representation costs to be readily identified and separately reported upon. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of grammar schools that received RAISE funding in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme consists of two key elements, Locality Led Projects and Cross Cutting Initiatives. Whilst 61 successful locality projects have been identified following an open call, a process to agree Letters of Offer is underway and will need to be completed in advance of funding being released under the Locality Led Projects element of the RAISE Programme.  No funding has been released at this point. The RAISE Programme also includes a number of Cross Cutting Initiatives, of these, four Voluntary Grammar schools are participating in the Reading with AI Research Project, and 18 Voluntary Grammar schools participated in the Halloween Revision Scheme as part of the Holiday Revision Support Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in Lagan Valley that availed of RAISE funding in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme consists of two key elements, Locality Led Projects and Cross Cutting Initiatives. Whilst 61 successful locality projects have been identified following an open call, a process to agree Letters of Offer is underway and will need to be completed in advance of funding being released under the Locality Led Projects element of the RAISE Programme.  No funding has been released at this point.The RAISE Programme also includes a number of Cross Cutting Initiatives, of these, four schools in Lagan Valley are participating in the Reading with AI Research initiative and, two schools in Lagan Valley participated in the Halloween Revision Scheme as part of the Holiday Revision Support Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether pre-schools will be eligible for the Pre-school Inclusion Fund if they do not have any children with special educational needs in their setting.
answer_text: Following a review of the eligibility criteria, all Department of Education (DE) funded pre-school education settings are eligible for the Pre-School Inclusion Fund in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the gradient of an incline is an accurate way of determining whether an area requires a grit box.
answer_text: Salt bins may be provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on roads or streets which do not qualify for inclusion in the gritting schedule, provided that the necessary criteria are met:  the location in question must be on the publicly maintained road network;the gradient of the road in question must be over 5%;no reasonable alternative route shall be available; andthe subject road attains a minimum overall score, derived using a specific formula, where points are awarded depending on road geometry, residential usage, community welfare and commercial usage.It should be noted that the gradient of an incline is only one element of this criteria which is assessed when determining if an area requires a salt bin. Further guidance and advice is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/clearing-ice-and-snow-footways

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of occasions her Department received a request from the Lady Chief Justice for mediation in relation to the criminal barristers strike.
answer_text: The Lady Chief Justice wrote to me on the 10 November 2025 suggesting the appointment of a mediator. I understand she wrote to the Chair of the Bar Council in similar terms and on 12 November 2025 she wrote again sharing his subsequent response. The number of occasions on which a proposal has been raised does not, however, determine its legitimacy and, as I advised the Lady Chief Justice, I do not consider mediation necessary or appropriate in these circumstances. Decisions on the use of public resources cannot be subject to negotiation but must instead be evidence-based and able to withstand scrutiny in terms of fairness, proportionality and affordability. I have also placed significant weight on the independent view of His Honour Judge Burgess that mediation would not significantly progress resolution. Instead, I have proposed a way forward that builds on the work already done through the Working Group Chaired by Judge Burgess and which provides an opportunity to address remaining issues of concern to the Bar and the Solicitors Criminal Bar at pace while meeting the requirements of good governance. The proposed approach is a matter of ongoing discussion, and so it would be inappropriate to comment further at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is on track to deliver the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme.
answer_text: Resetting the justice system is a long-term objective that will necessarily extend beyond the current mandate; however, work on the Enabling Access to Justice Programme is continuing at pace and good progress is being made. That  includes the recently implemented 16 percent uplift in fees for advice and representation across criminal, civil and family cases; the introduction of new fees for advice and representation in applications for immigration and asylum; the establishment of a Working Group to consider other fee changes and the consequent introduction of  enhanced fees for PACE and parole applications; the introduction of interim payment of fees and disbursements for public representation in criminal, civil and family cases which were introduced in the interests of  the viability and sustainability of the legal profession and career progression for junior and female practitioners in particular; and development of a proposal for a refresher fee pilot. I will also shortly publish discussion papers on options to reform financial eligibility rules for legal aid and new approaches to merits testing to ensure the proper purpose of legal aid, namely protecting the most vulnerable, is fulfilled.Some anticipated actions under the draft Programme Delivery Plan have necessarily been delayed as we have sought to respond to views expressed during consultation on the Delivery Plan and in other engagement with stakeholders including the Criminal Legal Aid Working party. I expect to publish a revised Delivery Plan shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Bar has supplied submissions and proposals to her Department, including independent economic appraisals, in relation to an uplift payment for legal aid.
answer_text: The Bar has made a number of submissions to the Department, via the Independent Working Group chaired by His Honour Judge Burgess, over the last year but submissions to date have not been sufficiently robust to meet the evidential requirements for additional expenditure from public funds. The Bar supplied timecards for 15 cases and results of an internal survey from self-selected barristers which is not representative of the 219 barristers who practiced in the crown court last year. Unfortunately, the report commissioned by the Bar can also not be relied upon. My Department commissioned an independent review of that report and it was found to have a number of significant deficits in terms of the methodology and the data used. It was also not an economic appraisal as it did not, for example, consider overall cost or affordability.  In addition, the report contained a number of assumptions and unevidenced costs and is based on a very small, self-selected sample size of cases. The Bar is aware of that assessment. Assurance on public expenditure requires more detailed and more vigorous evidence. We have been making the same ask of the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association and they are delivering the necessary data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average wage for criminal barristers receiving legal aid rates.
answer_text: As barristers undertaking legal aid work are self-employed, they do not receive a ‘wage’ in the conventional meaning of the term. Barristers undertaking criminal cases funded by legal aid are remunerated in keeping with the relevant statutory schemes. Table 1 below sets out the sums paid to barristers for all criminal cases in the period 1 April 2022 to 31 December 2025. The Table bands the payments barristers received and provides an annualised average per band.As barristers undertaking criminal cases may also receive payments for cases funded by civil legal aid, the Table also indicates the annualised average payment per band for legal aid work in the same period across. Barristers may also receive payment for work which was not funded by legal aid and for which we would not hold figures.The annualised mean payment to barristers in criminal cases over the period was £71,424.74. As the focus of recent engagement on payments to barristers has been in respect of Crown Court cases, Table 2 sets out the corresponding information for payments made in respect of that court tier. The annualised mean payment to barristers in criminal cases over the period was £48,313. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the date on which (i) the Bar wrote to her Department in advance of the criminal barristers strike commencing; and (ii) her Department responded to that correspondence.
answer_text: Intensive engagement with the Bar and the Criminal Bar Association has been ongoing since before service withdrawal began in November 2024. I met with them again on 5 November 2025 and it was agreed, by all parties, that the meeting was constructive. Officials had further meetings with representatives of the CBA and Bar on 28 November and 8 December 2025, when the roadmap to address remaining issues was agreed. The Chief Executive of Bar Council then wrote to officials by email on Friday 12 December 2025 on the subsequent decision of the CBA to withdraw services.  Officials responded on Monday 15 December 2025. Officials then received a further email from the Chief Executive of the Bar on 17 December. I wrote to the CBA membership directly on the 5 January 2026 and in response received further clarity on the latest CBA ask. That enabled me to invite CBA representatives to further meeting on 15 January 2026 at which I proposed an approach which I believe will both allow the concerns of the CBA to be addressed at pace and fulfil our requirements in terms of appropriately managing public money. Constructive engagement on the proposal has been ongoing since.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the rates for payment to barristers undertaking criminal legal aid work were last set.
answer_text: Rates for advice and representation for criminal legal aid were last set on 7 January 2026 when a sixteen percent uplift in fees, backdated to 1 December 2024, came into effect. The 16 percent uplift was applied to a framework that was agreed with the Criminal Bar Association through mediation in 2016 and it has resulted in additional £6.15m being paid to criminal practitioners in recent weeks, £1.8m of which went directly to barristers. The increase in rates means, based on current demand, annual spend on legal aid will increase by £11.5m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average waiting time for a trial to commence for cases involving defendants receiving legal aid in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information for the average time from date offence was reported to first court date after a decision by Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to prosecute a person had been taken has been provided, as not all cases result in a trial and a significant proportion are dealt with by way of a guilty plea.Time taken for cases from date offence reported to first court date after PPS decision for legal aid cases, 2020/21 to 2024/25YearAverage (Median) days2020/211122021/221562022/231512023/241412024/25153Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the listed dates.  Court recess dates are not taken into account.2. Figures relate to cases disposed at court during the year specified. Each period is a financial year, running from 1st April to the following 31st March.3. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.4. Figures do not include cases where the case was dismissed but a caution was administered, fixed penalty registrations, penalty notices for disorder or cases resulting from a breach of a court order. Successful youth engagement cases were also excluded.5. Figures relate to cases marked on the LSA Case Management System as being a legally aided case.6. Figures relate to the average (median) days from date offence was reported to police to the first court date after PPS decision in a case.  This is not necessarily the same as first date of trial.7. The form of average used is the median, as this removes any skew caused by cases where particular circumstances may have caused them to remain incomplete for a longer than normal period of time.8. The majority of cases are dealt with at magistrates’ courts, which tend to move through the justice system more quickly than those that end up in the Crown Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of graves or burial plots the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has purchased at Milltown Cemetery, including dates of purchase.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has advised that, in circumstances where the Trust takes responsibility for funeral arrangements for an individual who dies without a next of kin or any known relatives, Patient and Client Support Services make those arrangements via a Funeral Director.  In those circumstances, the Trust directs the Funeral Director to cremate remains, unless the wishes of the individual are known to state otherwise.  Where those wishes are known to be a burial, the Trust plans for burial at Roselawn via the Funeral Director.  They advised that they have no records which would relate to burials at Milltown.My Department asked the Trust to apply the above question to a 40 to 50 year time frame. The Trust requested any information which would relate to graves or burial plots purchased by BHSCT at Milltown Cemetery from the Diocese of Down and Connor to ascertain if they had any records.  The Trust advised that, since their request, they have not received a response from the Diocese.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust sold grave or burial plots at Milltown Cemetery back to the Diocese of Down and Connor; and (ii) much much these plots were sold for.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has advised that they have not sold any graves or burial plots since the establishment of the Belfast Trust (April 2007), and have no legacy records prior to this that indicate any such sales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she can confirm that Judge Burgess described the 16 per cent uplift in legal aid fees award to criminal barristers as being an interim position only.
answer_text: When Judge Burgess recommended a 16 percent uplift in criminal legal aid fees in the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid he was clear that was what the evidence supported and he did not have evidence to go further at that time. The Judge has since confirmed that he used the term interim in that context to mean an uplift in advance of further evidence being gathered by a working group that he was also recommending be established to determine whether any other changes to the fee regime could be justified. I have been clear that I will consider any such additional changes if that is what the evidence supports, and I set up the working group recommended by Judge Burgess so statistically viable data could be developed to that end. The group has proved effective and has already allowed us to deliver additional changes to PACE and parole fees. A range of standard Crown Court fees are already scheduled for further consideration within the agreed working party roadmap and I have suggested a mechanism for building on that work and accelerating progress which I believe meets both the concerns of the Criminal Bar Association and the requirements of managing public money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has received communication from the Department of Health as to what the £495 million spend for Waiting Lists and Elective Care, detailed in the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30, will be spent on.
answer_text: The Health Minister outlined his department’s intended use of the proposed £495 million allocation as part of his return to the budget 2026-29/30 information gathering exercise. The Department of Health has advised that £85m per year (£255m over the budget period) is required to support red flag, cancer and time critical treatments. This funding will be directed toward commissioning, expanding and enhancing services across the region to address red current demand and capacity pressures. It is expected to increase the number of consultants, specialist nurses and associated support staff required to deliver these essential services.A further £80m per year (£240m over the budget period) is required to increase elective case capacity and respond to rising demand. The investment will support improvements in patient throughput through initiatives such as strengthening the GP Federation Primary Care Elective Service, expanding the preoperative assessment nursing workforce, providing additional training posts for anaesthetists and developing phlebotomy hubs for both cancer and non-cancer patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what the £21 million spend for Skills, as detailed in the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30, will be spent on.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy has advised that the £21 million (£7 million per year) will be used to support Skills within the Higher Education sector, through the Higher Education Teaching Grant. This will help to protect student places and sustain teaching quality and subject breadth over the Budget period.Protecting the teaching grant supports the skills pipeline for priority sectors and helps safeguard the research and innovation partnerships that drive productivity and growth across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of revenue that would be raised if non-domestic rates were raised by 5 per cent over the next three years.
answer_text: It is forecasted that a 5% increase in the current non-domestic regional rate over the next three years would raise an additional £19 million in 2026/27, £38 million in 2027/28 and £59 million in 2028/29 in regional rate income.
