question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine’s December 2025 survey showing that almost half of patients awaiting admission in Northern Ireland Emergency Departments were treated in corridors or other non-clinical spaces, whether his Department will commit to ending the practice of corridor care.
answer_text: I am acutely aware that the current level of use of non-designated clinical spaces to provide care is too high and must be reduced. While I accept it is reflective of the current pressures on hospitals and the wider health system, the impact on those providing and receiving care in that environment is not acceptable as routine, nor is it sustainable.However, Emergency Departments (EDs) operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces have their place in being used flexibly by clinicians to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. My Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. This includes:Risk assessments and infection prevention measures for any escalation area;Senior clinical oversight to ensure patient safety and dignity; andContinuous monitoring of patient flow and escalation triggers as part of Trust surge plans.Reducing the routine use of corridors and non-clinical spaces for patient care is a matter of improving hospital and system-wide flow, and my Department has taken initiatives to provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance, ensuring they are seen and treated in the most appropriate place for their needs. Working in partnership with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and Trusts, my Department has also strengthened consultant capacity in EDs across Northern Ireland. Eighteen additional emergency medicine consultants have already taken up posts, with a further five due to start in the coming months. I am actively engaging with representatives of the RCEM following their recent survey on care in EDs.  I regard this as an important piece of work and I am engaging directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to (i) mitigate delays in neurodevelopmental assessments for children; and (ii) provide pre-diagnostic and post-diagnostic support for their families.
answer_text: My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework endorses an inclusive, needs-based approach to neurodiversity designed to help reduce stigma and the perceived need to obtain a diagnosis in order to access support through health and education services. The Framework will include options for support pre and post assessment whether a diagnosis is confirmed or not. The development of this Framework has been informed by a review carried out of existing pathways across both Children’s Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) services. The Framework will also seek to clarify the roles and responsibilities of everyone tasked with working to support children and young people presenting with neuro-developmental and emotional health and wellbeing needs. Responses to the consultation have been very positive and officials are currently considering feedback received. It is clear that the development of a consistent approach to neurodiversity is an area of work that straddles both health and education. Work is also being undertaken with colleagues from the PHA and Child Health to develop the definitions and requirements that will underpin this pathway, and which will support commissioning going forward. My Department has also commissioned a regional needs assessment in respect of ADHD demand across all Trusts This initiative represents an important point of departure in establishing the basis upon which a future commissioning model in support of ADHD services across Trusts might be based. In approaching this exercise, the primary objectives are to gauge the overall demand for an ADHD service based upon a model of provision across the lifespan, and to provide detailed recommendations to inform discussion. It is envisaged that these recommendations will offer a view upon the proposed structure of a future clinical model, the necessary steps that might be required to ensure regional consistency and equity of access, an exploration of operational feasibility and proposals associated with implementation and staffing costs.  Paediatric Autism and ADHD services currently offer post diagnostic support including personalised needs-based care plans. At the end of the assessment process for Autism and/or ADHD, parents and children/young people; depending on age, will have a clinical discussion following diagnosis. This discussion will include options of treatment for ADHD and post-diagnostic support and intervention for ASD. The assessing Clinician may also refer to other services; based on the individual needs identified during the assessment; for example, Occupational Therapy (OT), Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) and Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS). Children and young people may also be referred to OT, SLT and Psychological services by their GP or another clinician involved in their care, however it is recognised that th

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how often grit boxes are refilled on average each year.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins and 52,300 grit piles for use by the public on a self-help basis on selected routes not included on the salting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.All grit boxes and piles were replenished prior to the start of the winter season, however during spells of ice and snow, grit boxes and piles can be consumed within a few hours. Given the limited resources available to the Department, it can then take some time to replenish them all, at a time when priority must be given to treating the main road network. However, salt bins and piles are always replenished as soon as possible.As conditions vary depending on location, not all bins require replenishment at the same time or at the same frequency, therefore it would take a disproportionate amount of time to work out yearly averages. The Winter Service Policy is available to view on my Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Society of Radiographers’ view that, in order to improve cancer services for patients, it is imperative to strengthen the radiographer workforce by investing in recruitment and equipment.
answer_text: Allied Health Professionals (AHP) are increasingly recognised as essential to the stabilisation of services and supporting transformation across HSC.  My Department has secured important expansions over recent years in the pre-registration AHP degree programmes commissioned annually from Ulster University.I announced in May 2025, as part of an overall £160m investment in HSC workforce commissioning across all professions, an additional cohort of 100 pre-registration AHP training positions, for commencement in September 2025, representing a significant step forward. The Diagnostic Radiography and Radiotherapy & Oncology AHP programmes delivered by UU have both seen significant increases in recent years. Diagnostic Radiography places have increased from 78 in 2022/23 to 94 in 2025/26, an increase of 20%, whilst Radiotherapy & Oncology places have increased from 16 in 2022/23 to 25 in 2025/26, an increase of almost 60%.In recent months, officials have been working in partnership with staff and managers at both radiotherapy sites, and in consultation with other key stakeholders, to undertake a Review of Radiotherapy Services in Northern Ireland (NI). The first phase of the review will be published shortly and will underscore the need for strategic investment in the radiotherapy service including the need for continued investment, not only in routine replacement of capital equipment, but also the adoption of new technologies that will increasingly allow for greater precision and more effective radiotherapy treatment.  Equally it will recognise the need to develop a resilient, well-supported multidisciplinary workforce supporting both Cancer Centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any plans he has to introduce statutory rights for young people with disabilities between the ages of 19-25; and (ii) a timeline for his Department's review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
answer_text: I am committed to reviewing the Chronically Sick & Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 and any related legislation during the first year of implementing the three-year Service Delivery Plan (SDP) for the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM), to determine whether there is a need for legislative change which better reflects changing demographics and modern models of support for people with lifelong complex needs. In relation to statutory rights for young people with disabilities aged 19-25, I have no plans at this stage to introduce new age-specific statutory entitlements. However, while I am fully supportive of the case for exploring legislative change, I have previously indicated the need to be mindful of the practical challenges of progressing new primary legislation within the remainder of the current mandate.My Department recently completed a 14-week public consultation on the LDSM and the SDP which closed on 25 November 2025. The LDSM sets out clear ambitions for young people with a learning disability, which includes individuals with complex needs, to receive lifelong, co-ordinated support, tailored to their needs and enabling choice and control. My officials are undertaking an analysis of the responses to the consultation which will inform any required amendments to the LDSM and SDP, including the commitment to carry out a legislative review.In terms of timescale, officials are aiming to publish an initial summary report of the consultation in January 2026, with the full analysis report to follow in March 2026. The findings from this will be used to further refine and finalise the LDSM and SDP by Spring 2026. Once finalised, they will inform decisions on the future reconfiguration of services for people with a learning disability and any additional investment required. Delivery of the ambitions outlined within the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department. As part of developing the LDSM, my officials undertook a comparative analysis of policy and frameworks across England, Scotland and Wales. This work concluded that, if fully implemented, the LDSM, the SDP and associated transition reforms have the potential to meet and, in some respects, exceed the frameworks published in other jurisdictions particularly where legislation has not been matched by service capacity or effective delivery arrangements.However, until the LDSM and SDP are finalised, it is not possible to provide an exact timeframe for this review or to assess whether legislative reform will be required. I am clear however that any legislative reform must be properly sequenced, evidence-based, and aligned with the system reform already underway. In addition, as the legislation relates to a number of Health and Social Care programmes of care and will also require cross Governmental involvement (spanning Health, Education, Economy, and Communities), a working group will need to be established which will determine the terms of reference, scope and timeframe

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of protests that have been restricted or prevented from proceeding by the (i) Parades Commission; and (ii) PSNI in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold operational data on decisions made by the Parades Commission or PSNI.  However, the Member will wish to note the number of parades or parade-related protests with conditions imposed is set out in each Parades Commission Annual Report and Financial Statement, under Parading Activities, on the Parades Commission website.  	While I have an interest in the potential impact of protests on policing, justice and the community, I am not responsible for the Parades Commission or PSNI decisions when determining public procession and protest issues. The Parades Commission is a body independent of Government, appointed by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland. Determinations on demonstrations are an internal matter for the Police Service of Northern Ireland, and I am committed to respecting their operational independence.  I would recommend that the Member writes to the Chief Constable for this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any guidance that exists in relation to information that local councils provide to campaigners around public liability insurance requirements before a protest takes place.
answer_text: The Department for Communities does not provide guidance to councils regarding public liability insurance for protests.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to their policy of supporting social change, whether they have held any discussion about amending the Public Order Act 1986 in relation to making it easier for people to organise and attend protests.
answer_text: The Public Order Act 1986 is a reserved matter, and any potential amendments would be for the UK Parliament to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his officials have held any discussions with Belfast City Council in relation to their permit scheme for public gatherings in the city centre.
answer_text: The development, consultation and implementation of any such scheme are matters solely for Belfast City Council under its statutory powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of protests, rallies or assemblies that have been declared sensitive by the (i) Parades Commission; and (ii) PSNI in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold operational data on decisions made by the Parades Commission or PSNI.  However, the Member will wish to note the number of parades or parade-related protests declared sensitive is set out in each Parades Commission Annual Report and Financial Statement, under Parading Activities, on the Parades Commission website.  While I have an interest in the potential impact on policing, justice and the community, I am not responsible for the Parades Commission or PSNI decisions when determining the sensitivity of public procession and protest issues. The Parades Commission is a body independent of Government, appointed by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland. Determinations on demonstrations are an internal matter for the Police Service of Northern Ireland, and I am committed to respecting their operational independence.  I would recommend that the Member writes to the Chief Constable for this information.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to their policy of supporting social change, whether any assessment has been made as to whether a notification to police before a protest takes place has deterred people from organising protests.
answer_text: The Executive Office has not carried out any assessment on whether the requirement to notify the police before a protest has deterred people from organising protests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 36982/22-2, whether 11 is the total number of staff on temporary contracts in the NIEA.
answer_text: At the end of November 2025 the Northern Ireland Environment Agency had 11 staff on temporary contracts.  These staff are Sandwich Course Students.  The Northern Ireland Environment Agency also engages the services of agency workers who are not employed by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency but are engaged through third party providers on temporary contracts. At the end of November 2025 the agency had engaged 79 agency workers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the commitment given by her Department to the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee, as set out in paragraph 279 of the Report of the Compliance Committee on decision VII/8s, to detail (i) when her officials engaged with the Planning Appeals Commission; and (ii) her Department’s assessment of the basis and weight that was attached to PAC decision 2021/A0081.
answer_text: My officials engaged with the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) in March 2025 regarding legal principles established in case law in relation to the management of unauthorised development which should be subject to environmental impact assessment (EIA) and which are set out in Development Management Practice Note 9A, published by my Department in December 2021.  The PAC confirmed that appeal decisions are taken in the relevant evidential context; and advised that the Commission is fully aware of the legislative requirements underpinning the EIA regime.  As an independent appellate body, the PAC sits outside of the two-tier planning system and outside of the remit of my Department (its sponsor body being the Department of Justice).  The weight the PAC applies to material considerations in its decision making is a matter for that organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any screening completed under (i) the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; and (ii) other Section 75 groups, in relation to planning applications for wind turbines being erected in the Sperrins.
answer_text: Two applications for wind turbines within the Sperrins AONB are currently under consideration by my Department. Rural Needs and Equality Impact screening will be carried out for these proposals at an appropriate time in the planning application process.  It is important to note that screening cannot be carried out before there is an indication of what the decision might be, i.e. an approval or a refusal.  In reaching a decision, full consideration will be given to the impacts on section 75 groups and rural needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that the findings and recommendations set out in paragraphs 4(a) and 4(b) of the Report of the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee on decision VII/8s against the UK arose from work within her Department, why this jurisdiction has made the least progress towards compliance.
answer_text: The case ACCC/C/2013/90, to which recommendations 4(a) and (b) in Decision VII/8s relate, did not arise from work within the Department for Infrastructure, but dates back to the previous unitary planning system in the North.  Progress towards compliance has been made, including the publication of Development Management Practice Note 9A: Unauthorised Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Development. Engagement with the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee will continue as necessary going forward, in conjunction with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, which is the co-ordinating Whitehall department on Aarhus Convention issues, and the planning regimes in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to reducing general and acute bed occupancy to the Royal College of Emergency Medicine-recommended safe level of 85 per cent.
answer_text: The Education (Student Support) (No.2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 preclude students who already hold a primary degree awarded by an institution in Northern Ireland or Great Britain, from obtaining fee loans or maintenance grants for a second primary degree. This approach is based on the principle that the limited financial resources available should be targeted at primary degree level, thereby allowing a greater number of students to experience third level study. My officials are conducting an ongoing review of the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an overview of discussions he has had with the Treasury in relation to further fiscal devolution.
answer_text: At the Spending Review last year, I secured a commitment from the British Government that it would begin discussions on a full Fiscal Framework for the North, inclusive of fiscal devolution. I have consistently raised the issue of a full Fiscal Framework in my regular engagement with British Government ministers including the Chancellor, the Secretary of State, and the Chief Secretary to the Treasury. In these discussions, I have made clear the importance of the Executive having the right fiscal levers at its disposal. My officials have been engaging with their Treasury colleagues in recent months on the potential scope of a full Fiscal Framework.I also corresponded with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury late last month on the importance of negotiations on a full Fiscal Framework being progressed. It will take time to negotiate and deliver the right deal, as it did in Scotland and Wales. I will do everything in my power to secure a full Fiscal Framework that delivers for people, businesses and public services in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his preferred options for further fiscal devolution.
answer_text: I have been clear that it is my objective to secure a full Fiscal Framework, inclusive of fiscal devolution, which provides the Executive with the levers we need to deliver more sustainable public finances and public services. In my view, further fiscal devolution would allow the Executive increased control to make different choices and manage our own priorities locally. This is something which is already happening in Scotland and Wales. I tabled an Executive Paper on 6 November 2025, outlining to Executive colleagues my pathway to a full Fiscal Framework.The recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission will form the basis upon which my department will engage with the Treasury on these matters. These are detailed in the Commission’s final report, which is available to read here. I am aware there will be different views, but I will work with Executive colleagues, the British Government, and my officials to secure a Fiscal Framework which delivers for people, businesses and public services in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to regular reporting to the Assembly on corridor care in Emergency Departments so that progress can be scrutinised.
answer_text: My Department does not currently hold data in a format that would allow me to report the number or percentage of Emergency Department (ED) patients receiving care in escalation or corridor areas, or indeed the improvement in this field. These areas are not routinely recorded as a distinct category in existing datasets, which focus primarily on overall attendances, waiting times, and clinical outcomes.I recognise the importance of understanding the use of escalation and corridor spaces as part of quality and safety monitoring. My Department is working closely with the EPIC Team and Health and Social Care Trusts to develop a standardised data collection process that will capture this information consistently across all EDs. This work will form part of a broader programme to enhance transparency and support evidence-based decision-making.EDs operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces are used flexibly to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. While specific data on escalation areas is not currently available, my Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. I am actively engaging with representatives of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) following their recent survey on care in EDs.  I regard this as an important piece of work and I am engaging directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) measurable targets; and (ii) timeframes set, for eliminating the routine use of corridors and other non-clinical spaces for patient care.
answer_text: My Department does not currently hold data in a format that would allow for the setting of targets or timeframes for the elimination of routine use of corridors and other non-clinical spaces for patient care.EDs operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces are used flexibly to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. These areas are not routinely recorded as a distinct category in existing datasets, which focus primarily on overall attendances, waiting times, and clinical outcomes.My Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. This includes:Risk assessments and infection prevention measures for any escalation area;Senior clinical oversight to ensure patient safety and dignity; andContinuous monitoring of patient flow and escalation triggers as part of Trust surge plans.Reducing the routine use of corridors and non-clinical spaces for patient care is a matter of improving both hospital and system-wide flow. I am actively engaging with representatives of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) following their recent survey on care in EDs. I regard this as an important piece of work and I am engaging directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether any systemic failings have been identified with the crisis response model for people experiencing a mental health crisis; (ii) whether he will consider reviewing the crisis response model for people experiencing a mental health crisis, with a view to include (a) trauma-informed practice; (b) compassionate communication; (c) de-escalation training; (d) consistency across all Health and Social Care Trusts; (e) safe alternatives to unnecessary police involvement; and (f) modernising outdated legislation and risk only models.
answer_text: It is widely accepted there is variation in mental health crisis services across Northern Ireland but this variation does not equate to systemic failings. To address variation, development of a new regional crisis service has been prioritised in the delivery of the Mental Health Strategy. To achieve this a Care Network has been stood up, inclusive of community and voluntary sector colleagues. Staff across various areas of service provision are trained in trauma informed practice, compassionate care and de-escalation techniques.Each Trust and service area has robust mechanisms in place to consider all safe alternatives and reduce the need for police involvement where possible.In the delivery of crisis services a range of legislative frameworks are utilised and relied upon. Policy colleagues and those across the Health and Social Care sector alongside the Judiciary and allied areas will highlight legislative issues where evident.Risk assessment is an essential component of interventions and the basis for analysis of risk, individual care planning, safety planning, recovery interventions, outcome and recovery monitoring. It is one aspect of a holistic assessment of an individual need and not relied upon in isolation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the NI Audit Office October 2025 report, Performance of Restricted Procedures by Health Trusts; and (ii) whether he has any plans to allocate additional funds for this service.
answer_text: I acknowledge the findings of the recent NIAO report on the performance of restricted procedures, particularly the gaps identified in monitoring processes within both Trusts and the Department. Work is already underway to address these issues, with a focus on ensuring that future monitoring is effectively embedded within the new digital infrastructure currently in development.To take forward the recommendations in the report, a dedicated project team has been established. Over the next 12 months, this team will strengthen the systems and processes that support the Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy. This work will enhance consistency across Trusts, improve financial accountability, and ensure that commissioning decisions continue to be based on the best available clinical evidence.In relation to funding, there are no plans to allocate additional resources as this report relates to the oversight and implementation of a policy not a service. The EUR Policy is designed to ensure that existing resources are used to deliver the greatest possible clinical benefit. The focus therefore remains on improving monitoring, governance, and adherence to the established commissioning criteria, rather than expanding funding for procedures that are considered restricted or not routinely commissioned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine’s December 2025 survey showing that almost half of patients awaiting admission in Emergency Departments were being treated in corridors or other non-clinical spaces, to detail (i) whether he will commit to ending corridor care; and (ii) the immediate actions he is taking this winter to deliver that.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by emergency departments (EDs) and is working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays.To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has introduced targeted initiatives, including the expansion of urgent care centres, Phone First services and minor injury units. These measures provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance.My Department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and the Trusts to ensure that additional emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. To date, 18 additional consultants have taken up posts, with a further five commencing work in the coming months once they have completed their training. There are plans to recruit a further four posts into early 2026 once training is completed. This additional capacity is intended to increase senior decision making into evenings and weekends where consultant cover has previously been low.Ultimately, these measures are designed to improve patient flow throughout hospitals, reduce congestion in EDs and enhance waiting times. They form part of a broader, system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.I will be meeting representatives of the RCEM on 22 January 2026 to discuss their recent survey on overcrowding and corridor care in EDs.  I regard this as an important piece of work and I am keen to engage directly with RCEM on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that delayed discharges are identified as a major driver of corridor care, to detail any immediate steps is he taking to reduce the number of medically-fit patients unable to leave hospital this winter.
answer_text: I recognise that additional demand over the winter months places hospitals under severe strain, often pushing them well beyond their capacity.  I also recognise the short-term necessity of ensuring that those individuals who present to our hospitals are prioritised and offered assessment and treatment in line with presenting need. Within this context it is also important to ensure that once the requirement for a period of consultant-led care has concluded, patients are able to return to community settings in a timely fashion. I was conscious that this winter would be difficult for our Health and Social Care system and accordingly earlier this year, under the leadership of the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, I brought together senior leaders from across the Health and Social Care system to learn from last winter, and deliver a winter preparedness plan for winter 2025/26. I was clear that I wanted a whole system approach looking at what could be achieved both in advance of this winter and in the longer-term.   There is a recognised need to improve patient flow within the hospital setting from a systemic perspective which; understands the drivers for increasing demand on acute hospital services; seeks to prevent and reduce avoidable admissions to acute hospital; enacts measures to avoid overcrowding in ED departments and delays in ambulance handovers through improved alternative pathways to ED; and which works toward reducing avoidable delays in discharge from hospital. In approaching this cluster of challenges, a particular focus is required on interventions which might improve the care of those older people living with frailty, who have been identified as some of the most vulnerable to the risks associated with the winter period.  Identified critical ‘red lines’ included corridor care; prolonged ambulance delays; overcrowded Emergency Departments; poor experience; and emotional toll on both patients and staff.  These insights directly shaped the development of seven targeted improvement projects which focus on reducing hospital demand by supporting older people to stay well and receive appropriate care in the community. This includes identification and risk stratification of frailty in our over 65 population, enhanced clinical care arrangements in care homes, admission avoidance for end of life care through advance care planning, and the development of a frail elderly falls pathway to reduce deconditioning and avoiding unnecessary conveyance to hospital.Each of the Health and Social Care Trusts developed unscheduled care improvement plans to introduce additional measures this winter from a small funding resource from my Department, which targeted actions across a range of areas from pre-hospital care through to acute care and into community care. Measures enacted to improve system flow include the prioritisation of early reviews of home care packages, a measure which has already delivered a significant number of hours of care bein

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of a levy on streaming services to fund grassroots arts organisations.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of a levy on streaming services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether commitments in the Disability Strategy adequately progress the rights of deaf and disabled people in line with the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.
answer_text: The Executive’s Draft Disability Strategy will drive continuous improvement targeted at making a real difference to the lives of Deaf and disabled people, helping to ensure that obligations under the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities are fulfilled.    The draft Strategy sets out a series of proposed commitments, one of which will involve an undertaking to review current disability legislation including how best to incorporate the UN Convention within the existing legal framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made as to whether the Disability Strategy will receive adequate funding.
answer_text: The funding of actions contained within the Disability Strategy remain the responsibility for each Executive Minister.I am confident that all actions falling to my Department will receive the necessary funding to be progressed within the ten-year lifespan of the Strategy.Additional actions may be considered throughout the lifespan of the Strategy as additional funding and resource becomes available to relevant departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Disability Strategy will be accessible in (i) easy read format; (ii) British Sign Language; and (iii) Irish Sign Language.
answer_text: The draft Disability Strategy currently out for public consultation has been available since launch in easy read format, accompanied by British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language videos.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their engagement with the US Government will change in light of the recent military operation in Venezuela.
answer_text: There is no agreed position on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work his Department undertook to co-produce the Disability Strategy with deaf and disabled people.
answer_text: An extensive Disability Strategy co-design process involving Deaf and disabled people has been ongoing since 2021 when Departmental officials first began meeting with individuals and organisations, including those in hard-to-reach communities, who could articulate the issues involved and who reflected the full range of disabilities in Northern Ireland.An Expert Advisory Panel was established comprising of members drawn from the disability sector. They prepared a report setting out their recommendations on the key actions the Disability Strategy should include. In addition, a Disability Strategy Co-design Group was established, made up of key stakeholders from the Disability sector. They have provided valuable advice on the development of the Strategy throughout the development process.The process was refreshed in 2024 when I directed my officials to further reach out to ensure as many people as possible covering the full spectrum of disability were involved.I would encourage everyone with an interest to input to the current consultation on the draft Strategy, including Deaf and disabled people, their families and carers, representative organisations, service providers and members of the public. Feedback received during the consultation will inform the final Disability Strategy which will be brought forward for Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of the number of Home to School bus services provided, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport which includes route planning and determining the most appropriate mode of transport for each pupil. Transport assistance is delivered through provision of a seat on dedicated EA, Translink or private operator bus services, a bus pass for use on Translink public bus services and by means of  train, ferry, taxi, and parental payments. Many pupils use more than one mode or service on their journey to school.Because of the complex and varied nature of transport provision, the EA is unable to provide a breakdown of individual home to school bus services in each of the last five years. However, it has supplied information in the table below which outlines the overall level of school transport delivered by bus. EA School BusesPrivate Operator BusesTranslink Buses2YearNumber of pupils Number of school buses1Number of pupils Number of bus Companies contractedNumber of pupils 2025/26unavailable823unavailable75unavailable2024/2521,0059,2237451,9992023/2422,1058,5606449,5392022/2321,4628,7816949,5732021/2221,5778,7137248,628The EA’s Fleet Management System is a live data set meaning figures for previous years are not available.  The EA does not hold the number of buses utilised by Translink for dedicated school transport and it is important to note that many pupils are allocated a bus pass for use on public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive plans to use funds in its Landlord Reserve account to purchase accommodation to add to its stock of permanent social housing.
To ask the Minister of Education who invited him to return to Israel in early 2026.
answer_text: Yoav Kisch, Minster of Education, for the State of Israel invited me to a Ministerial Education Conference on AI Powered Personalised Learning which has been scheduled for 2-4 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will provide employees with rights to claim unfair dismissal from day one of their employment.
answer_text: The proposed broad content of the Employment Rights Bill was outlined in my April 2025 Statement to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the differences in healthy life expectancy across areas with and without social deprivation.
answer_text: The Life Expectancy in Northern Ireland 2022-24 report highlighted that between 2018-20 and 2022-24, the male healthy life expectancy deprivation gap widened by 2.9 years from 12.0 years to 14.9 years. Over the same period, there was no notable change in the female healthy life expectancy deprivation gap which stood at 14.6 years in 2022-24.These gaps relate to the difference in healthy life expectancy between the 20% ‘most’ and 20% ‘least’ deprived areas and not areas ‘with’ and ‘without’ social deprivation. Assessments are made on a scale of deprivation according to the NI Multiple Deprivation Measure.Addressing health inequalities is one of my key areas of focus and I am keen my Department takes a long-term approach to addressing this issue.Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. Through MLB, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally. A reinvigorated MLB Thematic Plan is currently being developed.I also tested a new targeted, place-based approach called Live Better to help address health inequalities. Delivery was from January to June 2025 across 3 key themes: Starting Well, Living Well, and Ageing Well, supplemented by Health Fairs and Checks, Health Literacy Training and Health Inequalities Training. While the evaluation and recommendations reports are being finalised, they are being used to inform the development of the Neighbourhood Model of Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to alleviate the pressures of the Universal Credit Act on (i) deaf; and (ii) disabled, people.
answer_text: The Universal Credit Act 2025 reduces the rate of the limited capability for work related activity element (LCWRA) for Universal Credit claimants from 6 April 2026. However, the Act includes important safeguards for certain categories of claimant. The reduction in the rate will not apply to:existing claimants who were entitled to the LCWRA element before 6 April 2026; severe conditions criteria claimants (those with severe, lifelong conditions); andclaimants who are terminally ill.  My Department will continue to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions to ensure that the needs of deaf and disabled people are considered in future policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Executive’s temporary accommodation stock acquisition scheme, announced on 26 March 2025.
answer_text: The Department provides funding to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive for the Supporting People Programme; the Department does not break the funding down by thematic group. This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The below table shows the level of funding provided through the Supporting People Programme, in each of the last five financial years, including 2025-2026, broken down by thematic group:Please note:Figures are based on actual payments and exclude accruals.Special Needs Management Allowance (SNMA) figures are excluded.2025-2026 figures are for the financial year-to-date.Thematic Group2020-21 (£m)2021-22  (£m)2022-23  (£m)2023-24 (£m)2024-25 (£m)2025-26 to date (£m)Disability29.828.930.732.232.428.3Homeless27.227.529.730.932.029.0Older People8.68.38.78.79.17.8Young People5.55.56.06.16.45.6Total71.170.275.277.979.970.8”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have been proscribed Ribociclib for breast cancer treatment, in the last five years.
answer_text: Ribociclib has been approved for use by NICE for three indications:TA496: Ribociclib with an aromatase inhibitor for previously untreated, hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative, locally advanced or metastatic breast cancerTA687: Ribociclib with fulvestrant for treating hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer after endocrine therapyTA1086: Ribociclib with an aromatase inhibitor for adjuvant treatment of hormone receptor-positive HER2-negative early breast cancer at high risk of recurrence255 patients have been treated with ribociclib within the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any media organisations which (i) are eligible for; and (ii) have been awarded, funding for the latest road safety campaign.
answer_text: (i) My department’s advertising contract was awarded to Ardmore Advertising in September 2025 and the agency is responsible for planning and placing advertising across all channels for the latest road safety campaigns.  My officials do not hold the information you have requested but should you wish to discuss this further with the Agency, please contact the Media Director, Tory Anderson: tory.anderson@ardmore.co.uk. (ii)  For the most recent Anti-Drug Driving campaign, media channels included:TV & Video on Demand: UTV, Channel 4, Sky AdSmart, ITVXOutdoor: Poster sites, forecourts, ATM digital screensRadio & Audio: Cool FM, Q Radio, Podcasts, DAX, SpotifySocial Media: Instagram, Facebook, Snapchat, TelegramDigital: Programmatic, YouTube, Twitch, TinderArdmore bases its media strategy and channel selection on audience insight, media habits, and budget. Ardmore remains open to proposals from outlets on how they can help maximise campaign reach and frequency and, can be contacted directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the lack of emergency surgery services at South West Acute Hospital, and its impact on the rural industrial and farming population.
answer_text: In November 2022, the WHSCT made the decision to temporarily suspend Emergency General Surgery (EGS) in SWAH.  This was due to issues experienced by the Trust in securing sufficient substantive consultant general surgeons to reliably sustain an out of hours rota for emergency general surgery at SWAH. The temporary suspension of services was implemented from 18th December 2022 and new clinical pathways were put in place for Emergency General Surgery.Subsequently the Trust ran a public consultation exercise on the temporary suspension with a findings report published in July 2023. In line with other providers, it is for the Western Trust as a provider to determine how it will configure its General Surgery services to meet the standards on a safe and sustainable basis to meet the needs of the population. The issue of impact on particular populations would be subject to further consultation by the Trust rather than the Department. If a further consultation is performed I would expect the Trust to undertake another rural impact assessment in line with DOH guidelines.Any permanent change to the delivery of EGS at SWAH will require Ministerial approval in line with the ‘Guidance on the Change and Withdrawal of Services’.The experience of the temporary EGS pathways and supporting outcome data, along with the ongoing work on the vision for the future health and care services for Fermanagh and West Tyrone, will inform the plans for the Western Trusts’ future model for general surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be any investment in the South West Acute Hospital, so that it will be sufficiently staffed and regain its emergency surgical capacity.
answer_text: In November 2022, the WHSCT made the decision to temporarily suspend Emergency General Surgery (EGS) in SWAH.  This was due to issues experienced by the Trust in securing sufficient substantive consultant general surgeons to reliably sustain an out of hours rota for emergency general surgery at SWAH. The temporary suspension of services was implemented from 18th December 2022 and new clinical pathways were put in place for Emergency General Surgery.Subsequently the Trust ran a public consultation exercise on the temporary suspension with a findings report published in July 2023. In line with other providers, it is for the Western Trust as a provider to determine how it will configure its General Surgery services to meet the standards on a safe and sustainable basis to meet the needs of the population. The issue of impact on particular populations would be subject to further consultation by the Trust rather than the Department. If a further consultation is performed I would expect the Trust to undertake another rural impact assessment in line with DOH guidelines.Any permanent change to the delivery of EGS at SWAH will require Ministerial approval in line with the ‘Guidance on the Change and Withdrawal of Services’.The experience of the temporary EGS pathways and supporting outcome data, along with the ongoing work on the vision for the future health and care services for Fermanagh and West Tyrone, will inform the plans for the Western Trusts’ future model for general surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of transport connectivity and a lack of adequate services in rural areas, in relation to how these impact on health inequalities.
answer_text: I appreciate that health issues faced in rural areas can be quite different to those of urban areas. While in general, people in our rural areas tend to experience better health outcomes, this can mask issues faced by local communities, such as transport connectivity, lack of and issues of access to services, pockets of deprivation and isolation.Health inequalities is one of my key areas of focus areas and through Making Life Better (MLB), the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled and supported to lead healthy lives, the Live Better approach and the new Neighbourhood Model of Care for Northern Ireland I hope those who live in rural locations will see an improvement in access to services and this will, in turn, have a positive impact on health inequalities.In partnership with DAERA, my Department and the Public Health Agency have supported the Farm Families Health Checks Programme for a number of years. This consists of a mobile unit which is available at local livestock markets and additional community events to offer on-the-spot health checks consisting of diabetic and heart attack risk assessments, BMI checks and offers support around mental health. In addition, individual lifestyle advice is given on a range of health issues, and onward referral completed to local support services as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of his Department’s December Monitoring Round bid for Decent Homes funding not being met, to detail any impact this will have on (i) the retrofitting of social homes; and (ii) tenants living in damp and mouldy conditions.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s funding for its mainstream stock investment programmes and services is largely derived from its rental income, reflecting the fact that its Landlord body operates substantively on a self-financing basis. Whilst the Department’s December Monitoring Round bid of £14.4m for the Landlord’s Decent Homes capital programme was not met, this will not have any adverse impact on current year stock investment delivery, as these costs will instead be funded directly from the Landlord Reserve. This will result in a reduced level of funding available in future years for various stock investment programme initiatives.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trusts have reported an increase in health-related issues in relation to people living next to inland wind turbines.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Health and Social Care Trusts have not reported to the Public Health Agency any increase in health-related issues in people living next to inland wind turbines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any international guidance there is on the health impacts from noise being emitted from inland wind turbines.
answer_text: The World Health Organization (WHO) has published Environmental Noise Guidelines for the European Region (2018). These guidelines were developed by the WHO Regional Office for Europe, based on the growing understanding of health impacts of exposure to environmental noise. The main purpose of these guidelines is to provide recommendations for protecting human health from exposure to environmental noise originating from various sources including wind turbine noise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the legislation that monitors the noise of inland wind turbines is fit for purpose.
answer_text: My Department does not hold policy responsibility for legislation that monitors the noise of inland wind turbines in Northern Ireland and, as such, I have not assessed its effectiveness.  Similarly, my Department is not responsible for legislation governing the granting of planning approval for inland wind turbines.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of whether his Department’s December Monitoring Round allocation is sufficient for the timely progression of housing adaptations needed for sick, and disabled people.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any screening completed under (i) the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; and (ii) other Section 75 groups, in relation to wind turbines being erected in the Sperrins.
answer_text: My Department is not a determining planning authority for wind turbine applications.My Department has not undertaken any screening under (i) the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; and (ii) other Section 75 groups, in relation to planning applications for wind turbines being erected in the Sperrins.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 35930/22-27, whether any batteries were found in the Mobuoy illegal dump.
answer_text: Numerous intrusive ground investigations have been carried out at the Mobuoy site to identify the types of waste present. Detailed ground investigations, including the excavation of 95 trial pits and the drilling of 140 boreholes, have not identified batteries buried at the Mobuoy site. Detailed information on the ground investigations carried out, the type(s) of waste buried, and the estimated volume of waste buried at the Mobuoy site is published on the DAERA Mobuoy Remediation Project Website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/mobuoy-remediation-project-waste-type-and-waste-volume-factual-report.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current average house price of empty properties.
answer_text: The NI House Price Index and associated prices are calculated based on sales recorded by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) on Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) returns.  No information regarding the occupancy/vacancy of the property is recorded against this data, therefore it is not possible to identify empty properties to calculate an average house price for this subset of property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why wastewater treatment works' capacity sheets, received from NI Water, are treated as officially sensitive information for public bodies, including local councils.
answer_text: As this is an Information Management issue for NI Water, I have requested a response from NI Water on this matter.NI Water has advised that it shares capacity information with Councils’ Local Development Planning (LDP) teams for planning purposes, in order to best reflect an accurate picture of the current capacity situation if capacity statuses diverge from the annual information provided.However, despite these documents highlighting that the information contained is subject to fluctuation, NI Water is aware that such capacity information has been shared more widely by some Councils outside of the LDP teams without NI Water’s knowledge/agreement, or any request for updated information being made to NI Water. This has resulted in NI Water staff being publicly challenged on outdated information. As such, NI Water has taken the decision to protectively mark such documents to discourage the disclosure of this information for purposes other than is intended. NI Water has advised that current and accurate information can be obtained by making the appropriate information access requests to NI Water’s Information Management at imu@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of concerns raised by the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations regarding changes made to funding allocated to housing associations, and the impact on new social housing projects.
answer_text: The analysis provided by NIFHA shows the financial impact on a set of schemes of the new grant rate, should no efficiencies be found by Associations. However, it does not address the actual viability of these schemes or the ability of Housing Associations to fund them. NIHE do not expect that the changes to TCI Allowances/Grant Rates which took effect on 1st December 2025 will impact on delivery of the revised Social Housing Development Programme Starts target of 1,750 units for the 2025/26 year. However, they do not have detailed information of the impact on the 2026/27 programme year, which will depend on scheme-by-scheme financial viability review by Housing Associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount that his Department has spent on agency staff, in the last five years.
answer_text: The table below sets out the total agency costs for my Department over the last five years, spread across responsibilities that rest with me and also those that rest with the Defra Secretary of State since the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024. 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Agency Staff Costs£'000£'000£'000£'000£'000DAERA Core Department2,7714,0996,3656,6179,327NIEA2,6703,1103,3853,127 3,632Forest Service100335345391541
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of overspend requests he has received from Ministers, this year.
answer_text: Currently both the Department of Health and the Department of Education are forecasting to overspend in this financial year. The Executive has approved Ministerial Directions issued by the Health and Education Ministers to their Accounting Officers to allow pay awards to be implemented in these circumstances Teachers and health workers workers make a vital contribution to our society, and these decisions will ensure they are properly recognised for the essential work they do.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 36705/22-27, to detail the number of occasions she discussed this issue with her officials, since taking office.
answer_text: In addition to receiving regular written briefing, I have discussed this important topic with officials on at least 5 occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider introducing legislation to allow two-seat mobility scooters to be used on pavements, at low speed.
answer_text: I can confirm that legislation here does not allow for two-seat powered wheelchairs or mobility scooters to be used on public pavements or roads here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance for my Department, and I am committed to working proactively, along with key partners in the Strategic Road Safety Forum to make our roads and pavements safer for everyone. As my resources are limited, I will continue to focus them on interventions where evidence shows they will have the greatest impact.  At this time this does not include plans to review the existing legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how agency workers in the NI Environment Agency can receive permanent contracts.
answer_text: Page 18 of the "NICS Agency Worker User Guide for Managers v 5.0 Revised November 2025", states:	"There is no facility to ‘convert’ Agency Workers to permanent members of NICS, the only avenue open to Agency Workers is through external open competition."
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the length of time served, for those currently held in custody at Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre.
answer_text: On 9 December 2025 there were 3 children in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC), all of whom have been in JJC for less than 6 months.  Due to the low numbers currently in custody, it is not possible to provide a present breakdown of circumstances in order to protect personal information. The numbers of children in custody at this time of the year is typically low.To give a fuller picture, data for the 2024/2025 was published recently by the Northern Ireland Statistics Agency in the Youth Justice Agency Annual Workload Statistics (available on the Department of Justice website).  In that year, there were 180 admissions to the JJC with 265 movements in and out of custody.  The total average daily population was 7.7 with numbers of children in custody ranging on any given day from 3 to 21.  Most admissions into the JJC in that year were in relation to the Police and Criminal Evidence Order 1989 (PACE) at 83.3%; remand admissions accounted for 15% and sentenced admissions 1.7%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of staff in the NI Environment Agency on temporary contracts.
answer_text: At the end of October 2025 the Northern Ireland Environment Agency had 11 staff on temporary contracts.  These staff are Sandwich Course Students.  The Northern Ireland Environment Agency also engages the services of agency workers who are not employed by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency but are engaged through third party providers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of occasions her Department or sub-contractors were involved in works at Broadway, West Belfast, over the last five years; and (ii) what those works involved.
answer_text: My officials or sub contractors acting on behalf on my department have been involved in works at Broadway on 35 occasions over the last 5 years.  10 occasions relate to carriageway surface defects, 20 to footway surface defects, 4 for road markings and 1 for a traffic sign defect.Officials are also aware of a recent report of surface water ponding between Broadway roundabout and the entrance to the Royal Victoria Hospital. Arrangements have been made with the internal contractor to clean the gullies and jet the drainage lines at this location prior to the Christmas holidays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Queen's University report, Religion and Worldviews Education for All: Possibilities for Change to Religious Education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Religion and Worldviews Education for All research is a report produced by Queen’s University Belfast. While this report has not been formally shared with my Department for comment, officials are aware of its publication and the issues it raises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any role his Department plays in (i) regulating; and (ii) monitoring, Artificial Intelligence, across the civil service.
answer_text: (i) I recognise the potential benefits of using AI in the workplace and my department has implemented several measures to support the civil service with the careful adoption of AI tools.In providing and managing the shared IT infrastructure for civil service departments and the wider public sector, my department provides corporate software, some of which will incorporate AI functionality. For example, Microsoft Copilot Chat is permitted for use because its secure design ensures that prompts and associated data remain within the defined corporate boundary.For accessing AI tools that are not corporately provided, clear procedures and guidance are in place and business areas must complete a risk assessment and gain approval from the relevant Departmental Senior Information Risk Owner before access can be granted.To ensure that AI software procured for use across the civil service is resilient against cyber threats, my department has strengthened the security provisions within the Terms and Conditions of ICT contracts. These security provisions provide clarity to suppliers on the baseline security requirements that must be implemented to protect AI systems. Compliance with the AI Cyber Security Code of Practice is mandatory for all ICT suppliers and is monitored through contract management arrangements.(ii) While my department can assist in the regulation of the adoption of AI software in the civil service, responsibility for monitoring its use and any content generated, rests with the departments, their Information Asset Owners and the business areas utilising these tools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any considerations being given to the roll out of Artificial Intelligence across the health service, in the next five years.
answer_text: AI is already being applied in several clinical and operational areas with upcoming plans include testing AI algorithms to support referral and decision-making processes, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage, and the generation of alerts for results acknowledgement. However, these capabilities must be carefully balanced against potential risks particularly in relation to clinical efficacy and information governance. It is essential that any AI tools deployed are trustworthy and uphold the privacy and rights of patients and service users.The successful adoption of advanced technologies such as AI and Generative AI depends on several key enablers:High-quality, codified data that accurately reflects service users’ needs and historical treatments, which is essential for training AI models.Robust clinical safety and ethical governance frameworks to oversee the safe and responsible introduction of AI tools.Specialist skills in cloud engineering and cybersecurity, recognising that the computational demands of AI are often best met through cloud-based infrastructure.These considerations are informed by the HSC AI Steering Group, which has been established to support system-wide oversight and strategic guidance to ensure AI adoption is safe, ethical, and aligned with health service priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any provisions her Department has in place in relation to regulating Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: My Department has no statutory role in relation to the regulation of Artificial Intelligence. Businesses operating in the North must comply with the British Government’s sector-led principles-based AI framework, meanwhile the requirements of the EU’s risk-based AI Act are applicable for any business, here or anywhere in the world, providing AI products or services into the EU market.	Both Invest NI and the AI Collaboration Centre, funded by my Department, provide a range of practical guidance, support and advice on compliance with Britain and EU requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her Department's assessment of the use of Artificial Superintelligence systems.
answer_text: Artificial Superintelligence is a hypothetical technology and therefore has no practical application currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any areas of health and social care that Artificial Intelligence is currently used.
answer_text: There is significant work underway across Health and Social Care (HSC) to explore and apply Artificial Intelligence (AI) in ways that support both clinical decision-making and operational efficiency. Some examples include:Bone Image Fracture Scanning: Boneview AI uses an AI-enhanced algorithm to assist clinicians in reading X-rays within Emergency Departments. It helps identify acute fractures, effusions, and dislocations in patients from the age of two, reducing waiting times and improving accuracy.Brain Image Stroke Scanning: AI is being applied to CT Brain Perfusion scans to support clinicians in identifying patients suitable for disability-saving interventions such as thrombolysis and intra-arterial therapy (IAT), helping to speed up critical decision-making.Ambulance Service Planning: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) uses AI within its Integrated Analysis & Prediction model and Computer-Aided Dispatch system to improve demand and capacity planning. These initiatives demonstrate how AI can enhance patient care and operational processes, while maintaining a strong focus on safety, ethics and governance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her officials will consider meeting the group, ControlAI.
answer_text: My officials meet with a wide range of stakeholders in the normal course of business. Every invitation is considered on its merits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of Invest NI paying £800,000 to four senior leaders who left the organisation.
answer_text: The payments were made pursuant to contractual obligations following a restructure and redundancy process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the merits of the Housing Executive implementing a formal fast-track process for individuals with progressive conditions, such as motor neurone disease, who are on the waiting list for housing.
answer_text: The Housing Selection Scheme currently provides for applicants with specialist needs, including those with progressive conditions. Priority among eligible applicants is determined by Housing Selection Scheme points, which reflect an individual’s ability to manage in their current home and any social or medical factors. Additional points may be awarded for mobility challenges, complex care requirements, or the need to live near support services. Complex cases requiring bespoke solutions are managed by dedicated Housing Support Officers through an options appraisal process, which may include new builds or supported accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Housing Executive waiving the means test for housing adaptations for people living with progressive and life-limiting conditions.
answer_text: The Housing Executive are legally required under Article 61 of the ‘Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 2003’ to carry out a means test, known as the ‘Test of Resources’, for housing adaptations funded by a Disabled Facilities Grant. My Department is leading on a review of the Disabled Facilities Grant as part of the ongoing NIHE Administered Private Sector Grant review. I intend to bring forward proposals for public consultation in Spring 2026. I will assess and consider proposed legislative changes in line with the outcomes of this consultation and wider review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Housing Executive being required to keep and maintain a register of accessible social homes.
answer_text: I fully support the development and maintenance of an Accessible Housing Register.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of increasing the mandatory Disabled Facilities Grant cap to at least £70,000.
answer_text: My Department is leading on a review of the Disabled Facilities Grant as part of the wider NIHE Administered Private Sector Grant review. The maximum grant award for the Disabled Facilities Grant is being assessed as part of this review. I intend to bring forward proposals for public consultation in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the regulations for the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I plan to have the necessary legislation in place before the end of the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who is responsible for maintaining the streetlights on Finaghy Road North, leading to Malone College and Cranmore Integrated Primary and Nursery School.
answer_text: Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) schools, such as Malone Integrated College, and Cranmore Integrated Primary and Nursery School are responsible for their own maintenance. Each GMI school is allocated an additional amount in their delegated budget to recognise these costs.  If works at a GMI school extend beyond routine maintenance, then the school can submit an Unavoidable Minor Work Application to the Department, accompanied by relevant supporting evidence. Malone College has submitted a Minor Works application for external lighting works, which is currently being reviewed

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in custody that are currently awaiting treatment for addiction issues.
answer_text: Healthcare in Prison have a Clinical Addiction Team (CAT) which is a Tier 3 service, and commission Start 360 to provide the Alcohol and Drugs Empowering People through Therapy services (AD:EPT) which is a Tier 2 service.There are currently 49 patients awaiting treatment for Tier 3 Clinical Addiction Services for addiction issues across all prison sites.There are currently 1,236 patients on the waiting lists for interventions offered by AD:EPT Tier 2 services across all prison sites. Please note that AD:EPT services offer multiple interventions, and this is the total number of patients on all of the AD:EPT waiting lists. Patients who are on multiple lists will be counted multiple times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools he has visited since 1 November 2025.
answer_text: From 1 November 2025 I have visited eight schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of (a) children; (b) families; and (c) households, impacted by the two-child limit policy, in Derry; and (ii) the percentage of children in Derry who have received (a) child tax credit; and (b) universal credit, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: At August 2025, there were 4,850 children in 1,420 households impacted by the two-child limit policy in Derry City and Strabane local government district. The below table shows the percentage of children in Derry City and Strabane local government district in households that received Universal Credit. Note that requests for data relating to Tax Credits must be directed to HMRC.YearTotal children (aged 0-16)  in households receiving UCTotal children (aged 0-16) from Mid-Year Estimates Percentage of total Children in households receiving UCAug-17034,6000%Aug-181,44034,4704%Aug-193,61034,34011%Aug-206,28033,95018%Aug-217,93033,84023%Aug-229,70033,99029%Aug-2311,20033,84033%Aug-2415,59033,74046%Notes:Data Source: Universal Full Credit System (UCFS) at August 2025. Due to retrospection of UCFS data these figures are subject to change.Universal Credit Child Element is paid at household level. For this analysis we have considered ‘families’ and ‘households’ to be the same unit.	We have used mid-year population estimates for people aged 0-16, however some children over this age are still eligible for UC child element, for example those in full time education, and will not be captured here.	Mid-year population estimates are only available up to 2024.Due to instances of missing data around children declarations and dates of birth please treat these figures as estimates.Not all children who are the third or subsequent child will be impacted by the two-child policy. The number of children born before the policy will determine how many children in the household are counted towards the UC award calculation. Some children may also be exempt from the policy.Geographical breakdowns are allocated based on the postcode listed on claimant record at data extract date. Not all records can be correctly allocated using this method and some cannot be allocated at all.All figures are rounded to the nearest ten. Totals may not sum due to rounding.

answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) does not maintain a register of 'empty homes'. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the adequacy of Irish language (i) literature; and (ii) educational resources, in prisons.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Irish language literature and educational resources available to prisoners are adequate and are reviewed regularly by the Northern Ireland Prison Service.  Irish Language is offered as part of the core educational curriculum in prisons.  Individuals can engage in a leisure capacity or undertake qualifications up to Level 3/A Level with all necessary materials provided to support their studies.  In addition, prison libraries hold a range of Irish language materials and individuals can request specific books to be sourced if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people arrested for having their face covered at a protest, over the last five years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for using a face covering at a protest and the Department does not hold information on the circumstances relating to arrests made at protests.  The recording of information on persons arrested by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to number of people arrested by police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.
