question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 35833/22-27, to detail the organisations that availed of the £191 million media benefit.
answer_text: The media benefit of £191 million from hosting the 153rd Open is expressed in gross advertising value terms, derived from worldwide exposure on linear television, digital platforms and online press. This means that if local agencies were to pay for this level of exposure it would cost £191 million. The independent research conducted by YouGov Sport recorded exposure to 625 million households globally.The benefits of achieving such an impressive media impact extend right across the north for many organisations in the tourism and hospitality sector. This global promotion of the destination attracts visitors to come to play golf, visit other attractions and experiences, and avail of hospitality and accommodation, all of which delivers significant longer-term benefits for the tourism industry.  Since hosting the Open Championship in 2019, the value of Golf Tourism has increased from £52 million to £86.2 million - growth supported by the level media exposure generated from the 148th Open, combined with the efforts of our marketing agencies and local businesses.The global exposure also demonstrates to the world our ability to host major events, showcasing the destination as a potential host for future major events with all the benefits that brings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink workers, operating the late-night services over the Christmas period, will be renumerated for working unsociable hours.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Drivers who work past midnight will be remunerated for working unsocial hours over the Christmas period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any powers the Housing Executive has to compel private landlords providing single-let housing to implement repairs and maintenance work.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not have any statutory powers to compel Single Let providers to carry out repairs and maintenance to their properties.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who makes the decisions for Environmental Impact Assessment screening on planning recommendations, that come before local council planning committees.
answer_text: Under the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 screening decisions in relation to environmental impact assessment are a matter for the relevant district council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her Department's assessment of the number of people who are victims of human trafficking.
answer_text: The National Referral Mechanism (NRM) is the Home Office managed UK wide system for identifying potential victims of slavery and trafficking. In 2024 in Northern Ireland there were 652 potential victims referred to the NRM.  Of those referred, 442 were male and 210 were female. At the time of referral 43 were children. Of those referred, approximately 92% claimed they were exploited solely overseas, 4.5% in the UK and overseas, and 3.5% solely in the UK. In the first half of 2025 there were 280 potential victims referred to the NRM in Northern Ireland. Of those referred, 178 were male and 102 were female. At the time of referral 51 were children. Of those referred, approximately 84% claimed they were exploited solely overseas, 8% in the UK and overseas, and 8% solely in the UK. Following the publication of the Department’s Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027 in July 2024, while there is much more to do, in conjunction with strategic partners, the increase in identification of victims of slavery and trafficking represents a positive directional change in identifying and reducing the harm experienced within Northern Ireland by some of the most vulnerable in our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether there are any gaps in the provision of medication for people with (i) pancreatic illness; and (ii) pancreatic cancer.
answer_text: There has been a national shortage of Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT).  PERT is medication that is used to manage pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, a condition that can occur in patients with pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer.In response to the national shortages of PERT, the Chief Medical Officer issued National Patient Safety Alerts in May and December 2024.  The alerts were disseminated across primary and secondary care providers, as well as other relevant health organisations.  They outlined the extent of the supply issues and set out detailed actions for clinicians, prescribers and dispensers of PERT.  The actions include limiting dispensing to one month’s supply at a time, implementing clinical prioritisation, and considering the use of unlicensed PERT only when licensed alternatives are unavailable.  The actions are to remain in place only until the supply issues have resolved.The Belfast Trust as the Specialist Centre for pancreatic cancer, has confirmed that, although the supply issue has been challenging to manage, they have continued to maintain access to PERT products for inpatients.  They have also, on occasion, supported patients who have been unable to obtain  PERT products within primary care.Supply issues for PERT are expected to continue until January 2027.  My Department will continue to work collaboratively across organisations to ensure that any impact on patients is kept to a minimum.

answer_text: During the period 2020 - 2024, there were 11 prosecutions at courts for offences prosecuted under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015. These resulted in 7 convictions. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any action he is taking to review maintenance fees paid by leaseholders of former Housing Executive properties.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is responsible for determining maintenance charges and fees paid by leaseholders of former NIHE properties.These charges and fees form part of a legal contract between the NIHE and leaseholder and ensure that necessary services are provided and that planned works are undertaken. I am not taking action to review these fees or charges at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of homes currently valued above £2million.
answer_text: There are currently 19 properties with a Capital Value above £2 million based on the valuation date for the domestic Valuation List of 1 January 2005.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of extra tax that pensioners will be expected to pay, as a result of the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the Autumn Budget was set by the British Government and not my Department nor the Executive.My officials have engaged with HMRC on this issue. HMRC has confirmed that pensioners whose sole income is the basic and full new State Pension will not be subjected to any Income Tax in 2026/27. However, beyond that date it remains unclear what exact arrangements will be in place. HMRC has stated that the British Government will ease the administrative burden for pensioners whose sole income is the basic or new State Pension so that they do not have to pay small amounts of tax via Simple Assessment from 2027/28, and that more detail will be set out next year. HMRC have not provided any estimate of the amount of tax that pensioners with other sources of income, in addition to their State Pension, will have pay on that income.I will be pressing the British Government to ensure that our pensioners are not having their basic or new State Pension taxed as a result of these changes, and that clarity is provided as soon as possible to give pensioners the security they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of classes provided to support people who don’t have English as their first language, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department for Communities does not directly deliver English language classes and therefore does not hold a centralised record of the number provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of extra funding local councils are expected to receive as a result of the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: Additional funding received following the Autumn Budget through Barnett consequentials is unhypothecated and it will be for the Executive to determine how the amount of extra funding will be allocated.Any additional funding provided to my Department will be assessed against competing priorities within the context of a constrained financial position.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the contribution it has made to the exchange of knowledge and adoption of best practice as a result of MLAs' attendance at conferences hosted by the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association.
answer_text: The Assembly passed a motion in May 2007 to agree that it wished to seek membership of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA) and the Assembly Commission has therefore provided the resources necessary to support the Assembly branch of the CPA since that time.  The Assembly Commission provides staff and resources to support a number of inter-parliamentary bodies with which the Assembly is involved, including the CPA, but the Assembly Commission has no role in, or responsibility for, the operation or decision making of those bodies.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether there is a register of (i) MLAs'; or (ii) Assembly staff, attendance at conferences hosted by the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association in the last five years.
answer_text: In accordance with the motion passed by the Assembly in 2007, the Assembly Commission provides staff and resources to support the work of the Assembly branch of the CPA, as well as a number of inter-parliamentary bodies.  The Assembly Commission has no role in, or responsibility for, the operation or decision making of those bodies, including determining those Members who have attended.  Therefore, the Assembly Commission retains records of costs it has provided for travel undertaken by Members and staff, including to support the attendance of the official Assembly CPA delegate at CPA conferences.  However, specific records of attendance at CPA conferences would be held by the Assembly branch of the CPA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people with fetal valproate spectrum disorder; and (ii) any work his Department is undertaking to assist those with the condition.
answer_text: Information on the number of people with Fetal Valproate Spectrum Disorder is not currently available. However, the Cumberlege report estimates that 20,000 women were affected across the UK, with a corresponding estimate of between 550-600 patients harmed by Sodium Valproate in Northern Ireland.My Department continue to work with Health & Social Care system partners to improve the safety of medicines in pregnancy more widely and ensure that Sodium Valproate is only prescribed where clinically appropriate.	Patients can access care and support based on clinical need through normal referral processes. However, I recognise that further enhancements can be made to improve the care and support for the individuals and families affected by a range of medicines taken during pregnancy, including exposure to Sodium Valproate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35398 22-27, to detail where the empty homes in Central council ward are located.
answer_text: Vacant addresses are considered personal information, disclosure would contravene the first principle of the Data Protection Act 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the rising rate of suicide, with 290 registered in 2024 compared to 221 registered in 2023.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each and every suicide, not only in lives lost but also the impact on loved ones and communities. I believe every suicide is preventable up to the point of completion, so while we may set realistic targets, the only acceptable number is zero. In light of the high levels of suicide recorded in 2024 I recently directed the lifting of the embargo of awareness raising for the Lifeline service. The ‘Talking Really Helps’ mass media campaign commenced on 17 November and will run until March 2026. I would wish to emphasise that for anyone who is in distress or despair, the Lifeline helpline is available free of charge 24 hours a day, 7 days a week where trained counsellors are available to take your call on 0808 808 8000. It is disappointing to note the further increase in the numbers of recorded suicide deaths. I am particularly concerned by the 3 year rolling average showing a small upward trend. This average is much more accurate than looking at single year differences which can have bigger fluctuations from year to year. The 3 year average has remained very stable for a long time. Any increase in that is concerning and highlights we must redouble our efforts to provide support to those most in need.We continue to see suicide as the leading cause of death in men under 50 and 79% of all suicide deaths registered in 2024 were male.Of particular concern is the increase in suicide of those aged over 65. Further research would be required to determine the reasons behind this. It is very clear that further support is required for those in older age groups and research undertaken to establish what is driving this increase. We will work with NISRA on this in the near future.It is also worth highlighting that in the suicide deaths recorded in 2024: 61% were single, 8% were divorced and 6% were widowed. Family is a very strong protective factor against suicide where early support can be provided.Despite our strategic approach focusing on reducing the differential between the most deprived communities and the least deprived it is also very concerning to see the disparity widening between suicides registered in the most deprived and the least deprived areas in 2024.With regard to Protect Life 2 we will continue to focus resources on the most deprived communities. However, I recognise that many of the issues affecting those in the most deprived communities are outside the direct control of this Department. This underlines the importance of a cross government approach to the issue and full delivery of the action plan which cuts across all Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any police powers under the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008, to seize vehicles after dangerous use.
answer_text: Under Articles 65 and 66 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008, the police have certain powers relating to the removal, retention and disposal of vehicles seized because the vehicle is being driven carelessly, inconsiderately or illegally off-road and is causing, or is likely to cause, alarm, distress or annoyance to members of the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, allows for the seizure of vehicles for issues of (i) trespass; or (ii) nuisance.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people under the age of 16, that have self harmed whilst in custody, in the last ten years.
answer_text: Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC) does not hold data on self-harm by age.  The figures provided are for all children (i.e. aged 10-17) held in custody in the JJC. Due to technical issues with a database, the data for the last two years are not currently available.  No. of IncidentsNo. of children2015/1644242016/1770232017/1894242018/1964212019/20111192020/2154212021/2280202022/23179The table highlights the number of incidents recorded per year and the number of individual children involved, hence some children will have self-harmed more than once. Significant historic trauma and underlying complexities often underpin self-harm incidents and there is a multi-disciplinary team in place in Woodlands that provides tailored care that emphasises the need for compassion, empathy and an effective clinical response to improve therapeutic outcomes for children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the use of Terrorism Act 2000 legislation at protests.
answer_text: I have always been clear that it is essential to safeguard the right to peaceful and lawful protest and that all protest activity must take place within the law.  Terrorism remains an excepted matter.The policing of protests is an operational matter that falls exclusively within the remit of the Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of both organisations, it would not be appropriate for me to comment further on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of life sentences, handed out to young people under the age of 16, over the last ten years.
answer_text: Data held by my Department indicates that, for cases dealt with in the years 2015 to 2024, there were no young people aged under the age of 16 who received a life sentence as a result of a conviction at court.Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at court.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to age at date participant was dealt with.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of times they met with the Catholic Church regarding burials at Milltown Cemetery; and (ii) the agendas of these meetings, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: We have not met with the Catholic Church regarding burials at Milltown Cemetery.However, under our direction Departmental officials have engaged with the Diocese of Down and Connor regarding works at Milltown Cemetery, which was conducted under an archaeological license from the Historic Environment Division in DfC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the UK Supreme Court judgement in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 35884/22-27, AQW 35885/22-27 and 35886/22-27 published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 28th November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of young people held in custody, in each of the last five years, who (i) had previously been in contact with a social worker; and (ii) had recorded mental health problems.
answer_text: Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC) records where a child is involved with Social Services at the time of admission.  The percentage of individual children admitted to the JJC who were involved with Social Services (in any capacity) at their time of admission for each financial year from 2020/21–2024/25 is shown below.Financial YearPercentage Involved with Social Services2020/2181.5%2021/2269.8%2022/2371.8%2023/2477.9%2024/2576.8%Figures are taken from Youth Justice Agency (YJA) data on individuals held in the JJC at any point during the financial year in question.  A child may, therefore, be counted under more than one financial year.ii)  Woodlands JJC works in partnership with the South-Eastern HSC Trust to provide on-site services equivalent to Step 3 Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS).  Every child admitted to Woodlands (for more than 24 hours) is offered a healthcare assessment and, if needed, an onward referral to on-site CAMHS.Services broadened in 2024 to include both CAMHS and therapeutic services through the Acorn multi-disciplinary team and all children admitted to Woodlands are now offered an Acorn assessment.Following assessment, therapeutic interventions are provided to meet individual needs.  This can also include ongoing CAMHS input if required and transition to appropriate community services on release. Woodlands do not collate data in relation to mental health problems, however, the table below sets out the number of referrals made to CAMHS (up to 2023) and the number of assessments carried out by Acorn (in 2024).  The data covers referrals or assessments rather than individual children.YearReferrals through primary care to CAMHS202065202166202260202358Assessments carried out by Acorn202485

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34200/22-27, whether he will provide a copy of the correspondence shared with his Department in February 2024.
answer_text: I would like to clarify that the Belfast Trust alerted my Department in February 2025, not February 2024 as previously stated. Please find below a copy of the correspondence shared with my Department.SPPG / PHA EARLY ALERT POSITION REPORTUNIQUE INCIDENT IDENTIFICATION NO. / REFERENCE: BHSCT/EA/25/016DATIX ID (SPPG REF): BEA29859DATE EARLY ALERT NOTIFIED:14 February 2025	INCIDENT DATE: 7 February 2025		DATE CLOSED: 17 February 2025PROGRAMME OF CARE: Mental Health	LEAD OFFICER:EARLY ALERT CATEGORY: Regional media interestDESCRIPTION OF INCIDENT *Location - The Mews, Supported Living Facility*The Trust was contacted by RQIA on 07 February 2025 and notified that intelligence had been received about patient safety and the quality of care within The Mews Supported Living Facility (Cedar Foundation).Subsequently, an unannounced quality visit was undertaken by the Trust on 07 February 2025.No immediate service user safety issues were identified at that time.Further intelligence was shared by RQIA with the Trust on 12 February 2025 which highlighted a further range of quality and safety concerns within this facility.The Trust has commenced an adult safeguarding investigation for a number of service users.An Interim Protection Plan has been agreed with the Cedar Foundation.The Trust will continue to work with RQIA on a range of other issues.BHSCT, Executive Director, Social Work briefed the Chief Social Worker on 14th February 2025.17.04.2025 Update:RQIA completed an inspection on the Mews facility provided by the Cedar foundation, RQIA have issued enforcements to the provider. RQIA have written to the BHSCT to invite the BHSCT to a serious concern meeting as the commissioner and the deliverer of therapeutic services to the service users.This information will be made public and will attract media interest a communication strategy will be agreed with Cedar RQIA and BHSCT.18.08.2025 Update:BHSCT attended the closure meeting of the Mews investigation by RQIA. The Trust provided an update and was asked to update the early alert with the following information:- The Trust has completed its Adult Safeguarding Investigation in relation to care and an action plan is in place.- The Trust had asked internal audit to review the management of personal monies and concerns were identified particularly in relation to the receipting of spend. Also concerns in relation *identifiable information redacted.- During the course of the Trust review of placement costs, it was identified that annual uplifts for domiciliary care are being applied as a matter of routine for high cost care packages, which significantly impacts on the hourly rate over a period of time. The Trust is implementing a process for re-view of annual uplifts on high cost packages, considering value for money requirements.Criteria under which event is being notified:- regional media interestIMMEDIATE ACTION TAKEN BY REPORTING ORGANISATION: IMMEDIATE ACTION TAKEN BY SPPG/PHA: SPPG-PHA ACTION TAKEN: 14/02/2025 -

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how often officers, in each Department responsible for ensuring Ministerial code of ethics is adhered to, received complaints regarding each Minister, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I can provide information in relation to the Department of Finance. I can confirm that, for each of the last five years, no concerns have been raised regarding the adherence to the Ministerial Code of Ethics by the Minister of Finance. Information relating to other Departments would need to be provided by those Departments directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of young people, under the age of 16, who have been held in custody, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (the JJC) is the sole custodial facility for children (aged 10 to 17) in Northern Ireland.  The number of individual young people under the age of 16 held in custody over the last five financial years is set out below.Financial YearUnder 162020/21412021/22442022/23412023/24302024/2532Figures are taken from YJA data on individuals held in the JJC at any point during the financial year in question.  A young person may therefore be counted under more than one financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the level of reliance of antibiotics in the pig industry, in relation to improving living conditions for pigs.
answer_text: Since 2014, the UK and NI pig industries have developed with the UK Responsible Use of Medicines in Agriculture Alliance (RUMA) pig sector specific targets to record and reduce antibiotic use. 		The recently published UK Veterinary Antimicrobial Resistance and Sales Surveillance Report (VARSS) 2024 has evidenced 69% reduction in total antibiotic use and 99% reduction in use of antibiotics of highly critical importance in human treatment since 2015. DAERA research into the NI pig sector usage confirms these UK trends with the NI sector. 	The industry has achieved these reductions through a combination of antibiotic use recording and benchmarking, farmer training, improved pig health through husbandry, infrastructure, biosecurity and preventative medicine measures such as vaccination. 	My Department continues to monitor Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in the NI pig industry for trends or emerging resistance and coordinate the delivery of NI actions within the UK National Action Plan for combatting AMR.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to improve conditions and welfare of farm animals, including pigs.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 provide strong protection for all farmed animals. My Department enforces farmed animal welfare requirements and has a Code of Practice for pigs, to reinforce standards of care and husbandry, as well as promote best practice.I am taking steps towards introducing legislation mandating the use of CCTV in all slaughterhouses across Northern Ireland. My officials are also reviewing the recent Animal Welfare Committee report on CO2 stunning of pigs and considering further actions to improve pig welfare at slaughter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support given by his Department to reduce antibiotic use in the pig industry, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Strategic objectives relating to combatting AMR are outlined in the UK National Action Plan (NAP), which provides the overarching framework for addressing risks associated with antimicrobial use in agriculture.		Whilst DAERA does not provide direct and specific financial support to the pig industry for tackling antimicrobial resistance (AMR), knowledge transfer programmes have been delivered to pig businesses across Northern Ireland which included strategies focussed on benchmarking, prevention and reduction in antimicrobial usage. Additional strategies and measures are implemented across the pig industry to reduce endemic diseases and improve animal health and welfare, which serve as an indirect but significant contribution to support UK NAP AMR objectives.  This approach reflects DAERA’s commitment to a One Health strategy, across animal, public and environmental health.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the water quality in the River Faughan at the Mobuoy illegal dump is regularly tested for lithium.
answer_text: NIEA have an extensive Environmental Monitoring Programme in place for the Mobuoy site. Lithium has not been identified as a contaminant of concern at the Mobuoy site and therefore water quality monitoring does not include testing for lithium. Monitoring of the River Faughan includes monitoring for parameters identified as contaminants of concern for the Mobuoy site as well as statutory monitoring under the Water Framework Directive. There is no current statutory requirement to test for lithium in surface waters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of staff in the NI Environment Agency on temporary contracts; and (ii) the average number of years they are on temporary contracts for.
answer_text: At the end of October 2025 the Northern Ireland Environment Agency had 11 staff on temporary contracts.  These staff are Sandwich Course Students.  The Northern Ireland Environment Agency also engages the services of agency workers who are not employed by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency but are engaged through third party providers.    		The 11 staff are engaged on temporary contracts for a maximum period of just under one year (51 weeks) through the Student Placement Scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34952/22-27, to detail how the figure of £7.29 was established.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The process adopted by Translink for Station Access charges, was originally developed by the Competition Commission (now incorporated within the Competition and Markets Authority) for use in Britain. This uses a methodology for calculating charges which provides fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory access to bus stations for Bus Operators. Translink now applies this methodology to determine the access charge for Bus Operators accessing Belfast Grand Central Station and the current charge is £7.29 per departure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the use of temporary engagement forms to avoid formal recruitment processes, as this relates to special educational needs support staff.
answer_text: Sick leave and other unforeseen circumstances can lead to vacancies arising unexpectedly and immediately in special educational needs support positions.  The use of temporary engagement forms in these cases is the only realistic way for schools to respond quickly to these circumstances.However, the EA has taken a range of steps to improve the process for staff, and to support schools in continuing to provide staffing for children and young people with special educational needs.  This includes:Reviewing the current temporary engagement process to improve management, control and compliance with employment and equality requirements. This means that schools can now only use the temporary engagement process for short term emergency cover.Developing a streamlined process to move long-term temporary support staff onto a permanent contract of employment, recognising their commitment and contribution to the education of our young people. This will support the stabilisation of the workforce. Piloting a programme of regional recruitment for ASEN and SEN Classroom Assistants, which has extended the candidate pool for these posts and reduced the recruitment time by at least four weeks.  The pilot ran across Belfast, Newtownabbey and Antrim council areas and feedback has been positive. It will now be extended to other areas as quickly as possible to further support schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s work to end the use of temporary engagement forms, to employ school support workers.
answer_text: Given the size of the workforce, there will always be a need for the Education Authority (EA) to recruit some temporary staff.  In the case of schools, sick leave and other unforeseen circumstances will lead to vacancies arising unexpectedly and immediately.  The use of temporary engagement forms in these cases is the only realistic way for schools to respond quickly to these circumstances.However, the EA has taken a range of steps to improve the process for staff, and to support schools in continuing to provide appropriate staffing. This includes:Reviewing the current temporary engagement process to improve management, control and compliance with employment and equality requirements. This means that schools can now only use the temporary engagement process for short term emergency cover.Developing a streamlined process to move long-term temporary support staff onto a permanent contract of employment, recognising their commitment and contribution to the education of our young people. This will support the stabilisation of the workforce. Piloting a programme of regional recruitment for ASEN and SEN Classroom Assistants, which has extended the candidate pool for these posts and reduced the recruitment time by at least four weeks.  The pilot ran across Belfast, Newtownabbey and Antrim council areas and feedback has been positive. It will now be extended to other areas as quickly as possible to further support schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any work being undertaken by his Department to assess why antidepressants are being dispensed at a higher rate to people living in the most deprived areas; and (ii) how he will address this.
answer_text: Antidepressants are clinically indicated in a number of mental health conditions such as anxiety and depression. They are also used in other conditions, such as relief of pain.The Mental Health Strategy (MHS), published in 2021, highlights that people on low incomes have higher rates of mental health conditions, particularly severe and enduring problems, than high-income groups. It also recognises that the legacy of the Troubles has had a significant impact on mental health in Northern Ireland. Deprivation and high rates of mental and physical illness co-occur in the areas most impacted by the violence.These points are echoed in the Valuing Medicines Strategy, published in September 2025, which recognises there is a well-established link between social deprivation and mental and physical ill-health.  It has identified that in primary care, the number of prescription items dispensed is 45.9% higher, and the total ingredient cost is 21.4% higher in the most deprived areas of NI compared with the least deprived. As in the MHS, the legacy of the Troubles is highlighted, and how this has influenced medicine use in NI, with significantly higher prescribing rates for anxiety and depression medicines.The MHS fully recognises the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports, for example talking therapies and social prescribing, for individuals struggling with their mental health, including those impacted by depression.Talking therapies are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, as well as through various community and voluntary sector (C&V) organisations. Actions 5 and 19 of the MHS respectively commit to expanding hubs for talking therapies and to further embedding psychological therapies across all steps of care. Additionally, Action 4 of the Strategy commits to the provision of social wellbeing supports, such as social prescribing, as interventions for those struggling with their mental health. Social prescribing is a way to link medical care to non-clinical, locally delivered support services, and is typically available through the C&V sector.Action 16 recognises the importance of Recovery Colleges, already well established in each Health and Social Care Trust. These are facilities where recovery-focused mental health courses are jointly delivered by individuals with lived experience of mental distress and practitioners in the field. They are free and open to all.While all of these actions remain extremely important, in the absence of additional funding for the Mental Health Strategy, our focus in the immediate term has to be on workforce, crisis provision and tackling inequity in provision.  However, subject to availability of future funding, delivery of all these actions will ensure that a full range of non-pharmaceutical options is available to promote good mental health and aid recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31490/22-27, whether this information will be available with the introduction of the encompass system.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how Artificial Intelligence is currently used in educational settings; and (ii) any safeguards in place to protect individuals when data is collected and stored.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is already being used in many schools across Northern Ireland to support teaching and learning. This includes personalised learning tools, literacy support, and real-time feedback that helps to identify pupil development and areas for improvement. A Generative AI (GenAI) pilot is being progressed by the Education Authority involving approximately 200 schools, using two AI tools, MS Copilot and Google Gemini through the school IT system EdIS.  These tools provide safeguards regarding the collection and storing of data which other GenAI products do not. Work is continuing on developing guidance for education settings on the use of AI/GenAI involving the Education Authority, Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment and other key Arms Length Bodies. My Department is aiming to launch its guidance in parallel with the EA’s launch of its GenAI resources in early 2026.

answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is already being used in many schools across Northern Ireland to support teaching and learning. My Department is aiming to launch its guidance in parallel with the EA’s launch of its GenAI resources in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the figure of £280 million raised from he 153rd Open at Royal Portrush was calculated.
answer_text: The figure of £280 million generated by The Open at Royal Portrush in July 2025 is comprised of £89 million economic impact and £191 million media benefit.Independent research was commissioned by The R&A, in partnership with Tourism NI, to assess the economic impact.  It was undertaken by the Sport Industry Research Centre (SIRC) at Sheffield Hallam University. The research is calculated based on primary research undertaken with spectators along with complementary desk research, coupled with analysis of event documentation. Further research was also commissioned by The R&A to assess the media benefit from hosting The 153rd Open and broadcasting to a global audience. This research was undertaken independently by YouGov Sport. This is expressed in gross advertising value terms and derived from worldwide exposure on linear television, digital platforms and online press. The methodology applied to collate these figures is consistently used to measure The Open Championships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of care workers in West Belfast who don’t currently receive the real Living Wage.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the number of care workers who do not currently receive the Real Living Wage (RLW).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any aspects of the Renters' Right Act 2025 will apply to tenants here.
answer_text: No aspects of the Renters’ Right Act apply in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34693/22-27,  whether data exists for money paid prior to April 2023.
answer_text: Data exists but is stored across multiple operational systems and claim records.  In addition, some of the data is subject to a Privacy Notice whilst the sharing of other data has not been previously agreed with employers.  For example, under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, the Department’s contractual arrangements are with the Training Providers and not the employer.  As part of the Apprenticeship Action Plan’s commitment to improve equity of access to the apprenticeship system, my Department is looking at how more information, such as that requested, can be put into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to reduce private sector rent.
answer_text: Earlier this year I commenced legislation to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12 months, with a three-month notice given. I have no plans to control private sector rent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the results of Northern Ireland Civil Service interviews are released at the same time for those who applied (i) with extra time; and (2) without extra time, for interviews.
answer_text: Generally, all interview results issue at the same time unless there has been a rescheduled interview at a later date to accommodate a reasonable adjustment, in which case results may issue at a later date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34693/22-27, to detail the organisations that availed of payment.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide a consolidated list of all organisations that have received funding from the Department in support of employing an apprentice.  This is due to a range of factors including the different methods of collecting data over the past ten years, the need to adhere to GDPR rules in respect of individuals, the fact that any data sharing agreements under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme are with training providers and not employers and that privacy notices preventing disclosure of data in respect of the Apprenticeship Recruit and Apprenticeship Return Retain Result schemes.As part of the Apprenticeship Action Plan’s commitment to improve equity of access to the apprenticeship system, my Department is looking at how more information, such as that requested, can be put into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34693/22-27. whether there are any requirements for employers to pay the living wage, as a result of receiving government support.
answer_text: Employers are required to pay apprentices at least the National Minimum Wage for Apprentices, which is set by HMRC. There is no requirement for employers participating in the ApprenticeshipsNI or Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes to pay the Real Living Wage as a condition of receiving government support.  The Living Wage Foundation itself, also does not require apprentices to be paid the Real Living Wage as part of its accreditation criteria for employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34693/22-27, to detail any requirements placed on employers, to offer permanent jobs after apprenticeships are completed,
answer_text: It is a requirement of both the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes that, from the outset of training, the apprentice must be a new employee in (or about to take up) a permanent remunerated employment offer.  In addition, the apprentice must be paid at least the National Minimum Wage, be employed by a company that has operations in the North, and which is registered as an employer with HMRC.  The apprentice must be contracted to work a minimum of 21 hours per week with one employer and day release to undertake directed training must be paid as part of the apprentice’s contracted hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of meetings he or his officials have had with British Government representatives in relation to the two-child benefit cap, in the last two years.
answer_text: The two-child limit has been regularly discussed both formally and informally by me and by my officials during engagement with UK Government representatives and Department for Work and Pensions’ officials. There is no definitive record of the number of occasions when the two-child limit has been discussed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any arrangements the PSNI has to support a person with a disability, applying for a post in the PSNI.
answer_text: The legislative framework is in place to support the PSNI recruitment processes and encourage applications from under-represented groups. Recruitment to the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and the practical arrangements in place to support applicants are operational matters for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Policing Board. The member may therefore wish to direct their question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the Education Authority’s total annual bill for taxi use has more than doubled in the past five years.
answer_text: The increasing cost of taxis to transport pupils to school is of significant concern to myself and the Education Authority (EA).The EA has advised me that the increase in taxi costs is due to the demand for pupils with Special Educational Needs to travel by taxi rising by 33% in the last 5 years, whilst costs increased by 105%, in the same time frame. The number of licensed taxi drivers has also fallen by nearly 50%, reducing availability and the EA’s strict safeguarding requirements further limit the pool of suitable providers. The EA’s preference is for all pupils to travel via its own vehicles, or by a bus to reduce costs and improve efficiency however some pupils will always require individualised transport arrangements, which means that taxis will remain necessary for certain cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of home to school transport taxi journeys that were completed, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport and has advised me that although it monitors the completion of taxi journeys each month, it does not document the actual number of home to school taxi journeys completed each year.   Along with their invoice, taxi operators must submit a hard-copy form listing all individual journeys completed during the month and each pupil’s school endorses the form to confirm the pupil’s attendance on the invoiced dates.  There is maximum number of 185 operational days per year, but the number of journeys could be impacted by school closures, child illness, or other factors.The number of pupils receiving taxi transport assistance in the last five years, along with the number of taxi contracts are detailed in the table below.Academic YearNumber of Taxi Operators Awarded ContractsNumber of Pupils travelling by taxi2020/2021683,9132021/20221034,2862022/20231004,6342023/20241264,5982024/20251315,230

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any custody arrangements in place for people accused of violence by and ex-partner and who have children with the ex-partner.
answer_text: Where parents are unable to agree arrangements for children after they separate, either parent may make an application to the court for an order in relation to residence or contact in respect of a child. The determination of such an application is a judicial function. However, the legislative framework (in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995) provides that the child’s welfare should be the court’s paramount consideration and lists a number of factors to which the court should have particular regard. These include the child’s wishes and feelings, the child’s physical, emotional and educational needs, how capable each parent is of meeting the child’s needs, and any harm (not limited to physical harm) the child has suffered or is at risk of suffering. The definition of harm covers cases where a child has been affected by domestic abuse. Judges should apply the welfare principle with reference to the evidence before the court and relevant case law. In addition to the legislative framework, the Lady Chief Justice has issued guidance for family judges on dealing with applications for residence and contact orders where domestic abuse is alleged or admitted. The guidance advises that where allegations of abuse are disputed, the court should determine, as soon as possible, whether it is necessary to conduct a fact-finding exercise. The guidance also underlines that, where domestic abuse is admitted or proven, the court should ensure the safety and wellbeing of the child is protected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any role his Department plays in monitoring the accounts of Housing Associations.
answer_text: My Department carries out annual regulatory assessments on registered housing associations (RHAs) in Northern Ireland to ensure that they are complying with the finance standard contained within the Regulatory Framework for RHAs in Northern Ireland - https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-regulatory-framework-housing-associations-northern-ireland.pdf. The Department monitors the published accounts of RHAs to help assess whether they are managing resources effectively to ensure financial viability is maintained in the short, medium and long term, and that RHAs are delivering a strategic approach to achieving value for money.Regulatory Judgement Reports for RHAs are published on the Department’s https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/registered-housing-associations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department plans to remove the 2011 posthumous date for payments under the proposed redress scheme established under the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA Bill 15/22-27).

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the Union of Shop, Distributive and Allied Workers Freedom from Fear campaign, regarding retail workers; and (ii) to detail any plans her Department has to address this issue.
answer_text: (i) No one should go to work fearing that they will face abuse. Although the USDAW Freedom from Fear survey results do show a small decrease in number of instances reported, the high levels of abuse, threats and assaults faced by those working in retail remain unacceptable.    (ii) I am fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, PSNI, as well as other Departments and organisations, to ensure that we continue to reduce these numbers.   My Sentencing Bill will legislate for a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service.    This will include attacks on retail workers.  	This new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty on conviction than the sentences currently available for similar less serious assault offences.    	Additionally, if a more serious assault offence is charged, I will make the fact that the victim is such a worker a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect this specifically in the sentences imposed.   These higher penalties will act as a significant deterrent and send the message that there is no place for these types of offences in our society.  	I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill in January 2026, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of health care workers that have been off on work-related stress, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Firstly, I want to acknowledge the vital role our HSC workforce plays in delivering health and social care services across Northern Ireland. Their dedication, often under significant pressure, is deeply appreciated. With the aim of keeping absence to a minimum all HSC Trusts currently provide a range of health and wellbeing services and resources that are available to all staff.  Services include the provision of telephone support helplines and counselling provided through Employee Assistance Programmes and on-line resources to support healthy bodies and minds.  Where further specific support for staff is required, including psychological support, this is also available through referral to HSC Trust Occupational Health Services.  The metric used to measure sickness absence in HSC organisations is the percentage of hours lost to sickness absence including industrial injury. The percentage of hours lost due to sickness absence reasons recorded as ‘work-related stress’, in the last five years, is provided in the table below.Percentage of HSC hours lost due to ‘work-related stress’: Financial YearPercentage of hours lost due to sickness reason ‘work-related stress’2020/210.33%2021/220.40%2022/230.35%2023/240.41%2024/250.45%Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of safe staffing in maternity units.
answer_text: Women and babies in Northern Ireland have the right to safe, equitable, quality maternal and newborn health care and services. Across the five HSC Trusts there are currently seven maternity units that provide intrapartum care, each unit is staffed over a 24/7 period.  Each Trust has a continuity plan in place to manage the available resource against the demand and capacity required. There is regional collaboration in time of peaks in demand to ensure that women have access to safe care.My Department has been overseeing a major programme of work on maternity and neonatal services, with an immediate focus on improving governance and safety. This includes the independent review of midwifery services led by Professor Renfrew, “Enabling Safe, Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland”, published last year. Collectively, this work will culminate in a comprehensive, consolidated Regional Action Plan for improving maternity and neonatal services, and improving outcomes for mothers, babies and their families in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average price for a visit to a private dental practice.
answer_text: My Department does not collect data on private dental treatment, therefore I am unable to provide a figure for the average cost of a visit to a private dental practice.Private dental practices operate independently and set their own prices/charges for treatment. My department has no powers to control the fees charged by private dentists outside of the Health Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any powers his Department has to limit the costs charged by private dentistry.
answer_text: My Department does not collect data on private dental treatment, therefore I am unable to provide a figure for the average cost of a visit to a private dental practice.Private dental practices operate independently and set their own prices/charges for treatment. My department has no powers to control the fees charged by private dentists outside of the Health Service.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times the Enterprise train service from Belfast to Dublin (i) was delayed; (ii) was cancelled; and (iii) did not leave the station, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Review Period November 2024 to October 2025 Total Enterprise Services from Belfast to Dublin = 5021 A total of 331 delays (including extreme weather, delays on the Irish Rail network and other delays outside of Translink’s control) were recorded in the 12-month period. In accordance with the Public Service Agreement parameters, 69 delays were categorised as delayed in the period requested, equating to 1.37% of all Dublin bound trains. 14 Enterprise train services were cancelled, equating to 0.27% of all Dublin bound services. Same as above. 14 services did not leave the station (i.e. cancelled). For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: Since the introduction of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland, 21,480 households have been impacted by the two-child limit policy. There were 75,780 children living in these households, 27,250 of whom were not eligible for the additional child element at some point during the period. Notes:Data Source: Universal Full Credit System (UCFS) at May 2025. Due to retrospection of UCFS data these figures are subject to change.Due to instances of missing data around children declarations and dates of birth please treat these figures as estimates.Not all children who are the third or subsequent child will be impacted by the two-child policy. Some children may also be exempt from the policyAll figures are rounded to the nearest ten.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures he is taking to prevent the cost increase of school dinners.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage closely with the Education Authority (EA)  in seeking to ensure that the EA Catering Service operates as efficiently as possible. A key aspect of this work is to maximise footfall of pupils paying for a school meal. The additional income from more pupils choosing to take a school meal reduces the overall net cost to the EA of operating its school meal service. The EA Catering Service has been successful in recent years in increasing pupil footfall through a range of initiatives informed by a detailed engagement exercise undertaken with pupils, parents, schools and catering staff. This has seen revised menus, promotions, branding, theme days, improved communication with parents and efforts to reduce queuing time – all aimed at creating a more appealing school meals offering to pupils. As a result, there has been a sustained increase in pupils choosing a school meal. An example of this has been the “Eat Smart” theme week operated by the EA Catering Service in September 2025 across more than 500 primary schools. While these initiatives have been successful in increasing footfall and therefore the EA’s income from school meals, the scale of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year has required it to make a number of difficult and unpalatable decisions aimed at reducing this shortfall. Having maintained its school meals prices at the same level since 2017/18, despite the significant rises in its production costs over that period, the EA determined that a price increase for school meals was unavoidable in the current financial circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of occasions he, or his officials, have met with representatives of the Israeli government, or military, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: I met with members of the Israeli government during my recent visit to Israel. There is no record of any official meeting with representatives in a departmental capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of strengthening inspection frameworks to include monitoring of quality-of-life outcomes and safeguarding measures beyond paperwork compliance, for adults with learning disabilities in domiciliary care settings.
answer_text: At present Supported Living services in Northern Ireland are regulated under the framework of domiciliary care. The legislative context for this is the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003, the Domiciliary Care Agencies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007, and the associated Domiciliary Care Agencies Minimum Standards. These collectively define and enforce the regulatory requirements for supported living services as a subset of domiciliary care provision. While the Regulations and Minimum Standards were not explicitly crafted for supported living type settings, they nonetheless include several fundamental principles that align with the ethos of supported living. My Department is carrying out a comprehensive review of the Domiciliary Care Agencies Standards, and has engaged with a range of key stakeholders, including the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC), to inform this. The purpose of this exercise is to review the existing Standards (and relevant supporting documentation) against recent developments in best practice guidance, policy and procedures. This will then inform any potential amendments that may be required to the Standards and Regulations. It is intended that a formal consultation will be undertaken prior to the finalisation and implementation of the revised Standards, ensuring transparency and the opportunity for wider sectoral input.However, I recognise that further work is needed for supported living type settings which better reflect the uniqueness of this model of care and the increasing complexity of need among service users living within such settings. Accordingly, a Supported Living Implementation Group has been established as part of work being taken forward by the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board (SCCRB) to reform adult social care. The aim of the Implementation Group is to develop proposals and recommendations to reshape how Supported Living services are commissioned, regulated and delivered. The Implementation Group will propose options to better regulate Supported Living services, including the development of standards and regulations if appropriate, and develop a standardised commissioning framework for Supported Living services.As part of their remit, the RQIA is mandated to inspect all Domiciliary Care Agencies at least once annually while maintaining a risk-based approach which may necessitate additional inspections, as needed. Guidance for registered providers concerning the statutory requirement to notify RQIA of events affecting service users and others is outlined within the RQIA's Statutory Notification of Incidents and Deaths Guidance for Registered Providers and Managers of Regulated Services (January 2023). This guidance is not intended to replace the professional judgement of registered providers who are still expected to comply with

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost to her Department for the new Enterprise fleet.
answer_text: The provision of a replacement Enterprise Fleet and associated infrastructure is being delivered jointly by my Department and the Department of Transport. It is also being supported by the PeacePlus Fund.The two Departments are considering the Full Business Case for the project, and it would not appropriate to detail the costs until this process has been completed.
