question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions her Department has had with Queens University Belfast, over the last two years, regarding its commitments to implementing divestment from Israel.
answer_text: As an autonomous institution, decisions on investment and divestment are matters for Queen’s University itself, however I fully welcome any steps taken by the university to implement divestment from Israel in line with ethical and human rights considerations.  The university’s activities relating to divestment from Israel are included in its 2025/26 Outcome Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the cost of her Department's office in Dublin.
answer_text: My Department does not have an office in Dublin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the costs charged to private bus companies using Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The current departure charge for bus companies using Belfast Grand Central Station is £7.29. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his officials have had with HMRC, regarding people travelling home through Dublin airport, and having benefits stopped or frozen.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I provided for AQW 34052/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any agreements that exist between his Department, and HMRC, regarding the monitoring of people’s benefits, for alleged fraud.
answer_text: My Department uses the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) IT systems for benefit administration. Consequently, my Department is named as a third party in a Memorandum of Understanding between DWP & His Majesty’s Revenue & Customs, which governs the exchange of information for benefit related purposes, including the prevention and detection of fraud. Disclosures must have a clear legal basis and adhere to data protection and human rights legislation.As outlined in my Statement to the Assembly on 11 February 2025, I am committed to a zero-tolerance approach to benefit fraud. I welcome my Department’s collaboration with other government bodies to help protect the integrity of the benefit system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of implementing mandatory reporting of all significant incidents in domiciliary care settings for adults with learning disabilities, including restraint, medication errors, allegations of abuse, and other safeguarding concerns at supported living services directly to the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority.
answer_text: At present Supported Living services in Northern Ireland are regulated under the framework of domiciliary care. The legislative context for this is the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003, the Domiciliary Care Agencies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007, and the associated Domiciliary Care Agencies Minimum Standards. These collectively define and enforce the regulatory requirements for supported living services as a subset of domiciliary care provision. While the Regulations and Minimum Standards were not explicitly crafted for supported living type settings, they nonetheless include several fundamental principles that align with the ethos of supported living. My Department is carrying out a comprehensive review of the Domiciliary Care Agencies Standards, and has engaged with a range of key stakeholders, including the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC), to inform this. The purpose of this exercise is to review the existing Standards (and relevant supporting documentation) against recent developments in best practice guidance, policy and procedures. This will then inform any potential amendments that may be required to the Standards and Regulations. It is intended that a formal consultation will be undertaken prior to the finalisation and implementation of the revised Standards, ensuring transparency and the opportunity for wider sectoral input.However, I recognise that further work is needed for supported living type settings which better reflect the uniqueness of this model of care and the increasing complexity of need among service users living within such settings. Accordingly, a Supported Living Implementation Group has been established as part of work being taken forward by the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board (SCCRB) to reform adult social care. The aim of the Implementation Group is to develop proposals and recommendations to reshape how Supported Living services are commissioned, regulated and delivered. The Implementation Group will propose options to better regulate Supported Living services, including the development of standards and regulations if appropriate, and develop a standardised commissioning framework for Supported Living services.As part of their remit, the RQIA is mandated to inspect all Domiciliary Care Agencies at least once annually while maintaining a risk-based approach which may necessitate additional inspections, as needed. Guidance for registered providers concerning the statutory requirement to notify RQIA of events affecting service users and others is outlined within the RQIA's Statutory Notification of Incidents and Deaths Guidance for Registered Providers and Managers of Regulated Services (January 2023). This guidance is not intended to replace the professional judgement of registered providers who are still expected to comply with

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any safeguards and accountability mechanisms there are to ensure that police are only called as a last resort during a mental health crisis when there is immediate risk to life.
answer_text: Within Northern Ireland the Health and Social Care Trusts along with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service are cognisant of the acute demands on all service areas to meet the needs of those requiring support at times of mental health crisis.Front line staff including those in Trusts, NIAS and Lifeline engage with and support individuals at times of mental health distress or crisis in a range of locations throughout the region.Lifeline and Trust’s have robust mechanisms in place to assess calls from individuals in mental health crisis, this assessment will determine the level of perceived or actual risk of serious harm to the individual or others and enable a determination if police involvement is necessary. Contact with the PSNI is only made when information indicates that there is a clear and immediate risk to the safety of the individual or others. Police assistance may be required when contact is lost with a high-risk individual, when there is clear risk to health and social care staff or when a crime has been reported.When a vulnerable person in mental health crisis requires the legal authority of The Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 to be used, the Approved Social Worker, following assessment, may request police assistance to ensure safety and public protection. All Trusts have reconvened interagency meetings inclusive of PSNI representation to ensure close interface working arrangements, appreciation of each agency’s risk assessment models and formulation, and to support greater utilisation of resources to meet the needs of those in mental health crisis. This will be consolidated further with the interagency working arrangements under Right Care, Right Person (RCRP).Health and social care staff ensure all options to reduce the need for police involvement have been considered and a request for police assistance is only made when assessed as necessary and proportionate to the risk identified and as a last resort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered implementing a review of the adequacy of domiciliary care regulations, given that they apply to supported living for adults with learning disabilities.
answer_text: At present Supported Living services in Northern Ireland are regulated under the framework of domiciliary care. The legislative context for this is the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003, the Domiciliary Care Agencies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007, and the associated Domiciliary Care Agencies Minimum Standards. These collectively define and enforce the regulatory requirements for supported living services as a subset of domiciliary care provision. While the Regulations and Minimum Standards were not explicitly crafted for supported living type settings, they nonetheless include several fundamental principles that align with the ethos of supported living. My Department is carrying out a comprehensive review of the Domiciliary Care Agencies Standards, and has engaged with a range of key stakeholders, including the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC), to inform this. The purpose of this exercise is to review the existing Standards (and relevant supporting documentation) against recent developments in best practice guidance, policy and procedures. This will then inform any potential amendments that may be required to the Standards and Regulations. It is intended that a formal consultation will be undertaken prior to the finalisation and implementation of the revised Standards, ensuring transparency and the opportunity for wider sectoral input.However, I recognise that further work is needed for supported living type settings which better reflect the uniqueness of this model of care and the increasing complexity of need among service users living within such settings. Accordingly, a Supported Living Implementation Group has been established as part of work being taken forward by the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board (SCCRB) to reform adult social care. The aim of the Implementation Group is to develop proposals and recommendations to reshape how Supported Living services are commissioned, regulated and delivered. The Implementation Group will propose options to better regulate Supported Living services, including the development of standards and regulations if appropriate, and develop a standardised commissioning framework for Supported Living services.As part of their remit, the RQIA is mandated to inspect all Domiciliary Care Agencies at least once annually while maintaining a risk-based approach which may necessitate additional inspections, as needed. Guidance for registered providers concerning the statutory requirement to notify RQIA of events affecting service users and others is outlined within the RQIA's Statutory Notification of Incidents and Deaths Guidance for Registered Providers and Managers of Regulated Services (January 2023). This guidance is not intended to replace the professional judgement of registered providers who are still expected to comply with

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount paid to employers in support of them taking on apprenticeships, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: Under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, employers are eligible for an employer incentive payment ranging from £670 to £2,008 per apprentice who successfully completes a Level 2 or 3 framework, depending on the occupation. This incentive aims to encourage employers to support apprentices through to completion and is paid through the training provider to the employer.  Data before April 2023 is not readily available.  Payments made to training providers before this date combined the employer incentive payment alongside payment to the training provider following full framework achievement by the apprentice under the AppsNI 2013 and AppsNI 2017 programmes; and across a wide sectoral range of apprenticeship frameworks.  The AppsNI 2021 programme introduced new payment coding to separate out the Employer Incentive payment. Table 1 below details the ApprenticeshipsNI (AppsNI) funding made to employers under the AppsNI 2021 programme for the period April 2023 to 7 November 2025.  Table 1: Employer Incentive payments made under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme 2023/24 to 2025/26Financial YearTotal Employer Incentive Paid (£,000)2023/243,6892024/253,7592025/26 (to 7 Nov 2025)1,644In addition to the above, the Department funded apprentice employers for a limited period under the Covid Recovery Recruit scheme and the Return, Retain, Result (RRR) scheme.  These initiatives were developed to provide targeted support for employers during the Covid impacted period by facilitating both the recruitment of new apprentices and their retention, including assistance with the reintegration of apprentices returning from furlough.  Table 2 details the funding provided by the Department to employers with apprentices during the COVID period to support business continuity.Table 2: Payments made to employers under the Department’s Covid incentive schemes20/2121/2222/2323/2424/2525/26Total Funding  Scheme£,000£,000£,000£,000£,000£,000£,000Recruit9176,6379,70041300	17,667RRR 1592,9767025101611	4,509Total1,0769,61310,402923161122,175Notes: Figures rounded to the nearest one thousand

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a formal review will be carried out into how mental health crisis calls and appointments are currently managed, as a first point of call.
answer_text: Each service area that receives mental health crisis calls and arranges appointments have robust mechanisms in place to provide oversight and accountability for managing calls for people in mental health crisis.Lifeline is Northern Ireland’s 24/7 crisis helpline for people experiencing distress or despair. It is provided by Belfast Trust (on behalf of the wider HSC) who are responsible for the day to day operational management and governance of the service. The service is accountable to the PHA as commissioners, who provide oversight and assurance of service delivery. All calls to Lifeline are managed and data analysis completed to monitor the service and provide opportunity for review. Trusts implement the regional, national and clinical guidance for mental health services which are monitored via local governance procedures, audits, RQIA inspections and incident reviews.With the introduction of Encompass as a region wide recording database, information in relation to mental health crisis contacts with an individual will be regionally available to the practitioner managing a call; thus providing greater oversight, prompt access to patient contacts and monitoring of service delivery.The Regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework (August 2025) is being adapted across the health and social care sector in order to enhance care through evidence-based practice. Implementation of outcome measures within mental health services can significantly enhance the quality of care, ensuring that treatment strategies are not only effective but also aligned with the needs and goals of service users.Two pilot services supporting individuals in mental health need and crisis presentations are the PSNI/Trust MATT Service and NIAS/Trust Hear and Treat. Both services aim to reduce the number of individuals in mental health need and crisis being conveyed to emergency departments by offering an alternative where their specific needs can be met.Evaluation of these services and review of how they manage mental health crisis calls and appointments is expected by March 2026.Work is progressing on the development of a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service. All stakeholders are engaged in this work. As this work progresses, mental health crisis calls and appointments will continue to be reviewed for service development. A formal review of how mental health crisis calls and appointments are currently managed is not indicated at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how the walkway, over Benbradagh Mountain, could be used to further promote tourism and economic regeneration of the local area.
answer_text: Outdoor Tourism in all its forms plays a key role in the developing the North’s tourism offering; encouraging longer stays and increasing tourism spend in our regions. Developed in the right manner, it showcases our heritage and natural environment and offers benefits for communities to enjoy year-round.   Visitor research continues to show that landscapes and scenery remain the key reason visitors choose to visit Ireland. Furthermore, walking is the top outdoor activity many visitors enjoy whilst here.Tourism NI works with a range of partners to realise the potential of Outdoor Tourism and is supported in this area by the Outdoor Tourism Partnership, with over forty representatives from across this sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will introduce mandatory trauma-informed and compassionate crisis response training for all GPs, including out of hours clinicians.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including mental health.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of PBL is to encourage dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GPs and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then Health & Social Care Board (HSCB) for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) commissions the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the employers who received financial support for supporting apprentices, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: I apologise for the delay in responding. I refer the Member to the reply I provided to AQW 35601/2227 on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether it is standard practice for her Department to provide officials, employed by arms-length bodies, such as Invest NI, with opportunities to redact further information from Freedom of Information responses, beyond the level of redaction already undertaken by Departmental officials.
answer_text: In line with the FOI Act’s Section 45 Code of Practice, it is standard practice for my Department to consult with third parties, including arms-length bodies, when requested information originates from them or may affect their interests. This consultation ensures compliance with the Act and allows those bodies to make representations, which may include identifying further information for redaction under relevant FOI exemptions. However, the final decision on disclosure and any redactions rests solely with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 30645 22 77, whether Invest NI has ever provided any financial support towards production, as opposed to manufacturing costs, of F-35 components to (i) RLC Group; (ii) Survitec; (iii) Moyola Precision Engineering; and (iv) Electric Automation Engineers.
answer_text: Invest NI provides targeted financial support for defined investment projects, that support the business to increase its overall capabilities and competitiveness. All funding is provided in accordance with all relevant State Aid and Subsidy Control regulations. Examples of eligible activities include R&D, skills development, sustainability and export support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the distinction between manufacturing and production costs, in relation to Invest NI funding.
answer_text: Invest NI funding is provided for defined investment projects that enable the business to increase its overall capabilities and competitiveness. These are investments in areas such as R&D, Skills and job creation. Funding is provided in accordance with all relevant State Aid and Subsidy Control regulations. Invest NI does not provide financial support to subsidise manufacturing costs as this is not permitted under EU state aid rules or the UK Subsidy Control Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the estimated cost of a data breach reported at a number of GP surgeries in June 2025 during an IT systems upgrade; and (ii) who will meet this cost.
answer_text: My Department is assisting those GP Practices affected by this incident. As you will appreciate, this matter is subject of an ongoing PSNI investigation and my Department is unable to release any further information at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the March 2025 NI Audit Office report Health and Social Care Imaging Services deemed that 16 per cent of imaging equipment is effectively obsolete, whether an assessment has been made of the cost to replace out of date equipment.
answer_text: The Capital Subgroup of the Regional Medical Imaging Board monitors the use, configuration, and age of all imaging equipment across the Health and Social Care system. The group meets quarterly and maintains an inventory of radiology equipment requiring replacement, including estimated project costs, prioritised in line with population needs and available budget. This priority list is reviewed regularly.£3,310,000 has been made available to the Subgroup to date in 2025/26, which funded nine replacement capital imaging equipment projects. The inventory indicates that further projects could be completed by year-end if capital funding becomes available in the next monitoring round.However, while my Department acknowledges that some HSC imaging equipment has been in use for over 10 years and would ideally be replaced more quickly, all equipment remains safe and fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s Live Better initiative, in West Belfast, including what impact this has had on outcomes for dentistry.
answer_text: Health inequalities is one of my key areas of focus and I introduced the targeted, place-based approach, Live Better, to help address this issue by bringing targeted health support to communities which needed it most. Live Better was tested in the Court DEA, Lower Shankill, Lower Falls and Grosvenor Road, and is currently being evaluated.The outcome areas agreed for the Court DEA were:Childhood Immunisations;Smoking in Pregnancy;Social Isolation; andHealth Fairs/Checks and Improving Health Literacy/health inequalities awareness.Oral Health was not included in the outcomes for Live Better in the Court DEA, however efforts are being made to improve children's oral health across Northern Ireland with the continuation of funding of the Enhanced Child Examination scheme for children aged 0-10 years and the expansion of Happy Smiles oral health improvement programme to include children in school years Primary one to Primary three in the 20% most deprived wards in each Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) with whom he met with in Israel, on his recent visit; (ii) whether minutes were taken at the meetings; and (iii) whether any departmental officials accompanied him on the trip.
answer_text: Racheli Yitzhak Principal, Ofek School.Nina Zeltzman, Senior Deputy Director General and Head of the Pedagogical Administration at Israel’s Ministry of Education.Dr Dalit Atrashchi, Director of the Division for International Relations and UNESCO Affairs, Ministry of Education.No. No.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what proportion of the red flag or time-critical patients who have been seen, diagnosed or treated as part of his Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme since April 2025 were seen, diagnosed or treated by an independent sector provider.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32800/22-27, to detail the breakdown of the categorisation of spend.
answer_text: The investment information provided in response to AQW 32800/22-27 is for staff costs within local community organisations who support the delivery of a range of programmes with a focus on health inequalities including emotional wellbeing, mental health, obesity, physical activity, healthy eating, smoking cessation and ageing well. It is not possible to break this down to investment amounts per programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount spent on public-private partnerships, in each of the last 15 years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) a breakdown of the categorisation of costs, including but not limited to (a) carpark charges; (b) loan repayments on new healthcare buildings; (c) facilities; (d) machinery.
answer_text: Details of public-private partnerships, including spend, are collated annually by The National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority (NISTA) on behalf of HM Treasury and this information is accessible online. A list of Public Finance Initiative (PFI) and Private Finance (PF2) projects as at 31 March 2022 was published by HM Treasury in July 2023, which includes projects relating to Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in Northern Ireland. This list is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/private-finance-initiative-and-private-finance-2-projects-2022-summary-data which provides data on the level of unitary charge payments per project per year.A high-level summary of the projects, broken down by HSC Trust and category, is provided in the table below.Project IDProject NameProcuring AuthorityCategoryContract expiry dateCapital Value (£m)497Cancer Centre equipmentBelfast Health and Social Care TrustEquipment30/12/203036.70499Managed Equipment Service Belfast Health and Social Care TrustEquipment30/9/202352.00532Services Centre Western Health and Social Care TrustBuildings25/4/203215.45646Enniskillen HospitalWestern Health and Social Care TrustBuildings19/5/2042223.912119Lisburn Primary & Community Care Centre 3PD South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustBuildings15/4/204633.38The latest data published in February 2025 is available at https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/pfi-and-pf2-projects-2024-summary-data . This does not include the Managed Equipment Service project which has expired.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the importance of Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council asserting, and recording, the public right of way on Benbradagh mountain.
answer_text: Asserting and recording public rights of way is a statutory responsibility of district councils under the Access to the Countryside (Northern Ireland) Order 1983; this is an operational matter for Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he plans to introduce a statutory duty on the Housing Executive, to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: All legislation is kept under review.  I have not made any commitment to specific legislative changes to the Homelessness Legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the public right of way over Benbradagh mountain can be protected, whilst windfarm installation is taking place in the Sperrin Mountains.
answer_text: Under Article 3 of the Access to the Countryside (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (Access Order) “A district council shall assert, protect and keep open and free from obstruction or encroachment any public right of way.”  District councils can also consider the temporary diversion or closure of public paths.If a landowner or developer requests the permanent diversion or closure of a public right of way to enable development to be carried out in accordance with planning permission, the Department considers this request under Article 16.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered placing a moratorium on any further developments of wind turbines in the Sperrins.
answer_text: Development of wind turbines is controlled by the planning system and development management. DAERA does not have any powers within the planning system to place a moratorium on any further wind turbine developments in the Sperrins.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there is a management plan for the development and installation of wind turbines in the Sperrins area.
answer_text: The local councils can produce spatial planning policy associated with turbine development in the Sperrins and AONBs. Within the planning system DAERA only has a statutory advisory role in relation to environmental impacts of proposed developments. As such, there is no management plan for the development and installation of wind turbines in the Sperrins area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33269/22-27, to detail the amount of funding that will be attached to the Irish Medium Education strategy.
answer_text: The development of a bespoke Irish-Medium Education strategy is ongoing with officials in my Department and relevant stakeholders. As actions have not yet been fully developed, no decisions have been made regarding funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is aware of any concerns raised about the use of private police at Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: AQO 2665 22-27 - ANSWER The Airports (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 (the 1994 Order) provided for the privatisation of Belfast International Airport and enabled the appointment of Airport Police Officers as a private Police Service employed and controlled by the airport operator.   I received correspondence from a private individual in May 2024 raising concerns about the licensing arrangements and policing of alcohol related disturbances at Belfast International Airport; however, alcohol licensing does not fall under the remit of the Department of Justice.  I am also aware that Criminal Justice Inspection published An Inspection of Airport and Harbour Policing in Northern Ireland on 15 October 2025. While the report did not comment on the use of a private police force, it raised a number of concerns about the policing of Belfast International Airport by the Belfast International Airport Constabulary (BIAC). The report also made a number of recommendations to address the issues raised. BIAC has accepted all the recommendations and provided an action plan setting out the measures it will take in response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when his Department were first made aware of complaints around the care of the Cedar Foundation's The Mews, in West Belfast.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust alerted my Department in February 2024, regarding concerns of safeguarding and governance issues within the Mews Supported Living Facility. The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and Northern Ireland Social Care Council (NISCC) were also alerted. The Mews is registered with RQIA who assure the quality of services provided by my Department through statutory and independent sector bodies. It is RQIA’s responsibility to ensure that every aspect of care reaches the standards set by my Department and expected by the public.RQIA issued two failure to comply notices and a notice of proposal in respect of new admissions to The Mews Supported Living Facility on 17th May 2025. In addition, my Department continues to liaise with the BHSCT who have statutory responsibility to ensure the social and medical needs of residents are met. The BHSCT have reported that they have a contract in place with the Cedar Foundation and an associated care management process in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will legislate for an Independent Environmental Protection Agency before the end of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of health and social care staff trained in Belfast for positive behaviour support, in the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there has been any assessment made by his Department on the impact of the overproliferation of wind turbines in the northern part of the Sperrins.
answer_text: The Department is a statutory consultee in the planning system and can provide advice, on a case by case basis, about the landscape and visual impact of windfarm planning proposals within AONBs, including the Sperrins. As such, my Department has not undertaken any overarching assessment on the impact of wind turbines in the northern part of the Sperrins.	Windfarm developers usually provide an assessment of cumulative impacts as part of their planning applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how calls from vulnerable people in a mental health crisis are assessed, to prevent unnecessary police involvement.
answer_text: Each Trust has robust mechanisms in place to assess calls from vulnerable people in mental health crisis, this assessment will determine the level of perceived or actual risk of serious harm to the individual or others and a determination if police involvement is necessary. Contact with the PSNI is made only when a comprehensive risk assessment determines that there is a clear and immediate risk to the safety of the individual or others. On occasion police assistance may be required when contact is lost with the individual in high-risk context, when there are safeguarding concerns or when a crime has been reported.When a vulnerable person in mental health crisis requires the legal authority of the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 to be used, the Approved Social Worker, following assessment, may request police assistance to ensure safety and public protection. All other options to reduce the need for police involvement will have been considered and such a request is only made when assessed as necessary and proportionate to the risk identified. All Trusts have reconvened interagency meetings inclusive of PSNI representation to ensure close interface working arrangements, appreciation of each agency’s risk assessment models and formulation and to support greater utilisation of resources to meet the needs of those in mental health crisis. This will be consolidated further with the interagency working arrangements under Right Care, Right Person (RCRP).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of workers in education who have received LGBT+ anti-bullying training, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT), within the Education Authority provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents/carers to help them understand the requirements of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act). ABSIT promotes inclusive, school-wide approaches to prevent and respond to bullying and offers tailored support for complex cases to aid inclusion. Training delivered by ABSIT is not exclusively centred on LGBT+ matters, rather it addresses the implementation of the Act and how the Act applies to all Section 75 categories of children and young people, including those who identify as LGBTQ+.Before the Act was implemented in 2021, ABSIT provided training for schools which covered the Act’s statutory implications and prepared staff to support pupils showing or experiencing bullying-type behaviours. The Education Authority delivered the programme to ensure pupils from all Section 75 categories receive appropriate support.Although the exact figure is not recorded, the Education Authority has advised that the vast majority of schools availed of this training. Those that did not avail of the training still had access to the associated resources.In 2023/24, ABSIT launched introductory online training for all school staff and a modular leadership programme for school leads which include specific reference to Section 75 groups. The figures for this training are as follows – ABSIT Introductory Online Training (All School Staff)School YearNumber of Staff Certified2023/243,8602024/259,4382025/26 (to date)3,191Total16,489 of 19000 who have completed some aspectsABSIT 8-Module Comprehensive Leadership Programme (Train the Trainer Model)School YearNumber of School Leaders Certified2023/24250 schools (some of which had more than one participant)2024/25625 participants2025/26 (to date)397 participants (booked for completion)Total1,272In addition, ABSIT has delivered Governor Training through EA Governance - Format2023/242024/25ABSIT Governors specific Face-to-face / Live Online Seminars130402Governors have also availed of Level 1 training and the bespoke training for complex cases delivered by ABSIT, however, the figures for these are not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what specific protocols there are to ensure trauma-informed care when patients reach out for assistance.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any oversight or accountability there is for managing calls for people in a mental health crisis.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children who have withdrawn from sports in school, because they have not been allowed to use toilets and changing facilities that match their pronouns, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information. It is for pupils, parents or carers and schools to work together to agree on the support required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any facilities and resources in place to encourage non-binary and transgender students to not withdraw from physical education.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information. It is for pupils, parents or carers and schools to work together to agree on the support required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how wage stagnation has led to an increase in poverty.
answer_text: I am aware that during recent years, wages in Northern Ireland did not keep up with inflation. In 2022, real earnings saw the largest annual decrease on record, falling by 3.7%. However, the latest data shows improvement, with real weekly earnings in Northern Ireland increasing by 3.1% over the year to April 2025. This is the second consecutive increase in real earnings following two consecutive annual decreases. As stated in the draft Anti-poverty Strategy, work is one of the most effective long-term ways out of poverty. The Strategy therefore includes an outcome of “A strong and growing economy with a wide range of good jobs across both the public and private sector providing opportunities that are accessible to those in poverty.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the Anti-Poverty Strategy has considered the merits of retrofitting, as a way to reduce poverty and fuel costs.
answer_text: I have presented a new Fuel Poverty Strategy to the Executive. The new Strategy will be addressing the need to make homes more energy efficient through measures such as retrofitting homes with better insulation and heating to reduce fuel bills. My officials are also working on a new more ambitious fund to replace the existing Affordable Warmth Scheme, which will aim to tackle fuel poverty by improving energy efficiency and delivering long-term sustainable solutions for low income households.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work his Department has done to include a homelessness strategy, alongside the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: Article 6A of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988 gives the Housing Executive a duty to formulate and publish a homelessness strategy. The Homelessness Strategy 2022-2027 can be reviewed here. https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of poverty, after housing costs, in West Belfast, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for NI are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Both measures can be presented on a before and after housing costs basis. Due to the uncertainty around estimates at lower levels, the Department does not present results for poverty at Parliamentary Constituency level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether out of hours GPs receive any mandatory training in responding to psychiatric crises, psychosis, or schizophrenia.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including mental health issues such as psychosis and schizophrenia.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the identification and management of psychosis and schizophrenia in adults updated in July 2025.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of PBL is to encourage dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GPs and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then Health & Social Care Board (HSCB) for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) commissions the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints received by the Housing Executive, from tenants, about contractors not showing up to carry out works, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Strategic Investment Board is working on a paper of options, involving the further use of the private sector, in the provision of housing.
answer_text: My Department has tasked the Northern Ireland Housing Executive with implementing the proposals from the fundamental review of social housing allocations. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Department for Communities’ Fundamental Review of Allocations (FRA) proposed that the NIHE can meet its statutory duty to homeless applicants on a tenure-neutral basis, provided that the accommodation meets certain conditions. The Department’s Consultation Outcome Report noted the importance of understanding the complexities associated with this proposal and that the conditions for discharge in the private rented sector must be carefully considered. With that, the Housing Executive, under the FRA Implementation Project, has been tasked with undertaking a scoping exercise to consider the practicalities and safeguards necessary to enable the Housing Executive to move from the long-established custom and practice of discharging the statutory duty through an allocation of social housing to discharge on a tenure-neutral basis. The FRA Implementation Project has commissioned the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) to carry out consultancy into how tenure-neutral discharge models are operating in the UK and RoI and how tenure-neutral discharge can be implemented in Northern Ireland. This work is currently underway and is intended to help identify potential models that are practical, fair, sustainable, and suited to the Northern Ireland context. These models and potential options will then be critically appraised with relevant stakeholders to identify a preferred approach for submission to the Department for Communities.”The Department will consider recommendations from the Housing Executive as to the implementation of this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any differences in those acting under, a Power of Attorney and appointed advocates, in relation to matters concerning the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“From a legal perspective an individual acting under a Power of Attorney has a different status in law than an individual who is an appointed advocate* on behalf of another. In Northern Ireland, there are two types of Power of Attorney. They are General Power of Attorney (GPA) and Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA). They can be used to give one or more people the authority to manage a person’s property and financial affairs for a limited time or if they lose their mental capacity. They have legally binding authority in respect of certain financial and property matters. The material difference with an advocate is that an advocate has no legal authority to make decisions for a person. Their role is representational, ensuring that the customer has a voice in the particular process and that their wishes and views are heard. NIHE will and does engage with persons as advocates in their representational capacity, when requested by the customer and in line with data protection principles. Examples include where a representative is appointed to act on a customer’s behalf in the complaint process, or an advocate accompanying a vulnerable customer during a housing needs assessment.*The use of the term ‘appointed advocate’ is noted. This response does not deal with the appointment of independent mental capacity advocates under the Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016. For the purposes of that Act an “independent mental capacity advocate” a means a person who has been appointed by an HSC trust, in accordance with regulations under the Act parts of which are not yet in effect and broadly relate primarily to medical and safeguarding matters.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to issues connected to housing, as part of the implementation of the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy recognises the significance of Housing, particularly in minimising the impacts of poverty, and includes a strategic commitment to ensure that everyone has access to good quality, affordable and sustainable homes.  It cross-references the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy and associated Action Plan, which will be integral to this work. Following the close of the Public Consultation on the draft-Strategy, my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received. I will report the outcome of this analysis, including any additional considerations raised relating to housing, to my Executive colleagues.Decisions on the final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to agreement by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected cost of the Housing Executive’s plans to purchase 600 homes.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.	I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:		“The projected cost associated with the purchase of 600 homes is circa £70.2m.  This includes the expected initial purchase price, professional fees, upfront repairs and furnishing costs. Note that this excludes costs associated with the ongoing management of the properties once the houses are occupied, as such costs are expected to be funded from rental income receipts.”		This proposal is focused on reducing NIHE’s current exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation (particularly hotels and B&Bs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of tenancy fraud cases his Department or the Housing Executive have been involved with, in the last five years; and (ii) the outcome of those cases.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Please find below the tenancy fraud statistics for the last 5 financial years in line with the agreed departmental reporting protocol, showing:New cases reported to the Housing ExecutiveClosed cases where lawful tenant residency is confirmed i.e. no fraud Recovered properties that were subject to fraud. This includes abandoned properties recovered via abandonment procedures (Article 41/19a). NB: Fraud numbers are fluid in nature; cases can be closed and reopened at a later date or can cross from one category into another during the course of an investigation. At any given time there will be a number of cases under investigation and these can cross annual reporting boundaries. These statistics reflect the picture as of the 31st March of each respective year:Year Reported casesClosed- Residence confirmed Properties recovered2024/257523831692023/257513902122022/23730 4042332021/225992722252020/21480310181

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount currently in the landlord reserve fund of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive held circa £265m in its Landlord rental income reserve as at 31/3/2025, to fund future housing stock investment. It is currently anticipated that circa £69m of this will be utilised in 2025/26 to fund Housing Executive stock investment, which will leave a balance of circa £196m at the end of March 2026. This may be subject to change as the year progresses. It is important to state that while this figure appears large, this balance amounts to less than the cost of one year’s total investment programme, and amounts to approximately £2300 reserve per home to fund long term investment. This underscores the very significant challenge associated with financial sustainability of public housing in Northern Ireland.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of refusals of housing offers that can be made by those who have been denied a succession of a Housing Executive properties.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive who has advised as follows:“Anyone who receives an offer of accommodation is, of course, entitled to refuse it if they so wish.In the case of applications for (re)housing under the Rules of the Housing Selection Scheme, all Applicants will be entitled to receive a maximum of two reasonable offers. This number of offers is not connected to any decision(s) or offers made in respect of a separate application or request to succeed to a social housing tenancy.In circumstances where the outcome of a succession request is that succession to the subject property is refused but that an offer of suitable alternative accommodation should be made, one offer of accommodation will be made. This offer is separate from any offers which have or may be made as part of the individual’s separate housing application. More detail about when a request to succeed may result in an offer of suitable alternative accommodation is contained in Rule 74 of the Housing Selection Scheme which sets out the criteria around policy succession in full.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the Primary Social Needs review will be completed.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 33376/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, under the public procurement thresholds, which are being amended by the British Government on 1 January 2026 with his consent, to detail the defence companies that are impacted by this decision.
answer_text: I have no detail on those companies that are impacted by this decision by the British Government as my department does not commission defence and security contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive is considering extending the use of private rented sector accommodation to reduce homelessness.
answer_text: My Department has tasked the Northern Ireland Housing Executive with implementing the proposals from the fundamental review of social housing allocations. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“While there are no means by which the Housing Executive can currently discharge the statutory homelessness duty via the Private Rented Sector, the Fundamental Review of Allocations approved Proposal 4, which will allow the Housing Executive to meet its duty to homeless applicants on a tenure-neutral basis.While work is ongoing on this proposal there are no immediate plans to use private rented sector accommodation aligned to Proposal 4.”	The Department will consider recommendations from the Housing Executive as to the implementation of this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of (i) the tribunal ruling of the case between a transgender postal worker and Royal Mail; and (ii) the implications it may have for transgender rights in the workplace.
answer_text: My Department provides funding and administrative support for the Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal in Northern Ireland; however, they are independent judicial bodies and it would be inappropriate for me as Minister for the Economy to comment on a tribunal ruling.  I understand the ruling referred to in the question was made by a tribunal in England.		The Department for Communities is responsible for equality legislation in relation to gender.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, as part of the projected £1.44 billion spend on the 2026 Agency Worker Contract, to detail how the projected £1 billion for the delivery of the Department for Communities' administration roles and corporate functions, will be spent.
answer_text: The projected £1 billion Department for Communities expenditure on the Agency Worker 2026 Contract will be spent on the provision of agency staff to enable delivery of my Department’s requirement to provide benefit services and support to citizens across the UK. The projected expenditure relates to the entire contract period if extension options are utilised.The services delivered include Finance Support, Universal Credit and  Employment Support Allowance which are directly administered by my Department to Northern Ireland citizens.The contract also ensures delivery of a range of services and support for the Department of Work and Pensions in Whitehall, as set out in the concordat between both Departments. Those services include:• Child Maintenance Service;• Universal Credit;• Working Age Services; • Retirement Services;• Disability Services including Personal Independence Payment.This use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of my Department in achieving those objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how the projected spend of £1.44 billion for the 2026 Agency Worker Contract matches with his Department committing to reduce the number of people on agency contracts.
answer_text: ANSWERThe projected value for the 2026 Agency Worker Contract is £1.44 billion (upper threshold). This figure is indicative and an estimate only and does not represent committed expenditure. £1bn of this projected value is estimated expenditure for the delivery of Department for Communities work which includes the delivery of services by DfC on behalf of the Department for Works and Pensions.As at month ending September 2025 there were 3872 agency workers on assignment in the Department for Communities, 2539 of these workers (65.5%) were assigned to roles to deliver the Department for Work and Pensions contract. As the Department for Communities have a concordat with the Department for Work and Pensions to recover these costs this expense is considered cost neutral. The new 2026 contract will cover a potential eight-year duration and includes in the estimated value allowance for increases in Department for Work and Pensions contract expected demand, Consumer Price Index, National Living Wage and NICS Annual Pay Awards.The use of agency staff is a cost effective and necessary way to manage some time bound and project aspects of temporary work and to meet particular business needs. Where budgets are uncertain it can also provide an ability to flex and adapt to changing fiscal circumstances.      While agency staff help maintain service delivery, our focus remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the report by the Criminal Justice Inspection, on its inspection of airport and harbour policing, in particular the security at Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: I note the findings of the report by Criminal Justice Inspection with concern. Policing of Belfast International Airport is carried out by a private police force, employed and controlled by the airport operator. Belfast Harbour Police is accountable to the Harbour Commissioners. Neither are accountable to the Department of Justice.The report recommended that a fundamental review of the policing of airports be carried out by the Police Service of Northern Ireland, in partnership with Belfast International Airport Constabulary and Belfast Harbour Police. I look forward to sight of the report when that work is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the security provided to asylum seekers or migrants arriving at Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: Responsibility for meeting and processing asylum seekers or migrants arriving at Belfast International Airport rests with Border Force, a law enforcement command within the Home Office.Immigration is a reserved matter and I, therefore, have no role or responsibility in Border Force operations or the security arrangements it has in place.

answer_text: Landlord allowance operates as a reduction in the rates payable and is not a payment made to the landlord. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether a memorandum of understanding exists between her Department, the Northern Ireland Office and the security services.
answer_text: My department does not have a Memorandum of Understanding with the Northern Ireland Office and the Security Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the £650,000 being allocated to Translink for late-night bus services.
answer_text: The £650,000 required to support the extension of late-night services for one year is made up of a collaborative funding approach whereby the First and Deputy First Minsters, the Minister for Communities, the Justice Minister, the Minister for Agriculture and Rural Affairs and Belfast City Council have each committed £92,300 towards the overall cost.  Minister Archibald and I have each committed £94,900 towards the cost of extending late night public transport services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any rights that private rented sector tenants have, with regards to keeping pets in their home.
answer_text: There is no legislation regarding pets in the private rented sector. Private landlords are under no obligation to agree to a request from tenants about keeping or acquiring pets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of trees planted in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
