department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of current standards of temporary accommodation provided by the Housing Executive, and its external contractors.
answer_text: In its acquisition of Single Lets, the Housing Executive will inspect the property at the point of acquisition and if the inspection is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not use the property.For Housing Executive-secured placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels), the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI.The development of a temporary accommodation standards framework is a commitment within the Housing Executive’s Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation. It is anticipated that work on this framework will conclude in line with the conclusion of this Action Plan in March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out a cost analysis for the roll-out of a women’s health hub.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report, Gynaecology across Northern Ireland, one of the key recommendations was to establish women’s health hubs to improve access to services and streamline care for women.Whilst work is ongoing to improve gynaecology services across the region, no formal cost analysis has yet been undertaken for women’s health hubs. It is envisaged that further work to develop appropriate service models and enhance existing services across Trusts will be subject to available staff capacity and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average time taken by the Housing Executive to come to a decision on homelessness assessments, for each of the past five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not report on the average time taken by the Housing Executive to come to a decision on homelessness assessments.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any legislation and policy governing standards of temporary accommodation, provided by the Housing Executive and its external contractors.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There is no legislative duty on the Housing Executive to provide standards for temporary accommodation. In its acquisition of Single Lets, the Housing Executive will inspect the property at the point of acquisition and if the inspection is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not use the property.For Housing Executive-secured placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels), the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when his Department’s review of primary social needs criteria and points will be completed.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the review of Primary Social Needs points will be completed by autumn 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work his Department has undertaken to connect rates to household income.
answer_text: ANSWERDomestic rating is a devolved property tax, it is not an income tax.As a key part of the system, however, there are several means-tested reliefs available which are among the most generous property tax supports available on these islands. The full detail of that assistance is detailed at the link below:-https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/rates-help

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment on whether students in Irish Medium Education are placed at a disadvantage, due to lack of funding for resources in Irish.
answer_text: The Department recognises the challenges faced by the Irish-Medium Education (IME) sector, including in relation to the availability of curriculum resources in Irish. Recent research commissioned by Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) has highlighted the additional demands placed on IME educators in adapting and developing materials.The Department is aiming to address this issue, and other issues in the sector, through the current ongoing development of an Irish-Medium Education Strategy. Engagement with stakeholders is continuing to focus on the production of an achievable action plan. We remain committed to supporting IME and ensuring all pupils have access to high-quality education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of land the Crown Estate holds within Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Land Registry holds information in relation to interests in land and property, whichis held in both the Land Registry and the Registry of Deeds. It is not currently possibleto provide detailed reports on ownership by public sector organisations. Land & Property Services (LPS) has already commenced as part of the broader LPS modernisation agenda to implement a new digital solution for Land Registration. The intention is that the new digital solution will include the structuring of the data held to make it more accessible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his department has carried out an assessment of the number of staff across the Civil Service, and the public sector, who are able to speak Irish.
answer_text: No such assessment has been carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for on update on any work to develop the rail lines between Derry, the North West and Dublin.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) sought to provide a strategic vision for rail across Ireland, including how rail could contribute to the decarbonisation of transport, promote sustainability connectivity into and between major cities, enhance regional accessibility and support balanced regional development. I have ring-fenced £1m in Translink’s 2025/26 capital budget to progress projects identified in the AISRR including the Portadown to Derry rail link. Translink has submitted the feasibility study into the Portadown to Derry rail link to my Department and I expect to publish the outcomes later in the Autumn. My Department has also approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest. My budget for 2025/26 includes ring fenced funding of £5.79m, which will allow Translink to progress this project. Work has continued including the production of the detailed design of the required track improvements and preparation for the procurement phase of the project. It has also included the manufacture of railway sleepers in advance of the main works. Subject to the availability of future funding, construction work could be carried out in two phases:February – May 2027 for physical intervention work on 6 miles of track.April – June 2028 to facilitate ballast cleaning of 14 miles of track.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Queen’s University Belfast’s Cancer Epidemiology Research published in October 2025 which found that air pollution is estimated to cause more than 130 lung cancer cases each year in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the recently published research led by scientists in Queen’s University Belfast, which provides an estimate of the number of lung cancer cases attributable to air pollution in Northern Ireland. Poor air pollution is the largest environmental risk to public health in the UK. Long-term exposure to air pollution can cause chronic conditions such as cardiovascular and respiratory diseases as well as lung cancer, leading to reduced life expectancy.The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is the lead Department responsible for air quality policy and my officials work with colleagues in DAERA in relation to public health aspects. The findings of this research will provide useful information to support ongoing work in this area.As part of the three-year Health and Social Care Plan, I have committed that by April 2027, and subject to additional funding, to have a new targeted lung screening programme in Northern Ireland. This aligns with action 10 of the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland to implement all UK National Screening Committee recommendations.The lung screening programme will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services, which will require input and collaboration from multiple partners. We are still at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be, however, the Department continues to work with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to learn lessons, to establish the resources required and determine a likely timeline to implement the programme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has received any correspondence from local councils in relation to its work on the International Relations Strategy in the last six months.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the outstanding pay claim for Translink platform grade workers; and (ii) the impact this may have on the roll-out of night-time bus services.
answer_text: The responsibility for resolving any outstanding pay claim is with Translink and the relevant trade unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department have given to people with disabilities, who are carers, with the announcement of the JobStart scheme.
answer_text: My department is committed to creating inclusive work opportunities, particularly for those who face barriers to employment, and I fully welcome participation from those with disabilities who are carers.  This phase of the JobStart Scheme will offer flexibility for those facing additional barriers to employment, with the option for these people to gradually increase their working hours.  Prior to starting an opportunity, a Work Coach will fully discuss individual needs with participants, identify what strand of the Scheme is most appropriate to meet their needs, and discuss any other assistance they may require.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the JobStart Scheme will include financial, and other practical support, for people with disabilities, to travel to interviews and work.
answer_text: My department offers assistance to all working age benefit claimants including those with disabilities.  Work Coaches across NI provide advice and support to overcome barriers to employment, including assistance for travel costs through the Adviser Discretion Fund.  Work Coaches will discuss tailored support requirements with the individual wishing to participate on the JobStart Scheme.  My department also funds the Access to Work (NI) programme that provides individual support to assist disabled people who wish to take up and remain in employment, including travel costs, through development of personalised work-based solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when Translink management plans to engage with the GMB and Unite unions, regarding the pay claim submitted in June.
answer_text: The responsibility for resolving any outstanding pay claim is with Translink and the relevant trade unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to climate change, whether he will consider setting sectoral and quantified milestones, including start dates and expected emissions reduction.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and associated legal framework includes specific milestones for GHG emissions reductions in the form of targets for a 48% reduction by 2030 and a 77% reduction by 2040, compared to the Base Year. There are also start and end dates for each of the 5 yearly carbon budgets. These operate at an overall Northern Ireland level. My focus is on implementing the existing legislation and ensuring that Northern Ireland achieves the economic and environmental benefits associated with climate action and a just transition. I do not intend to set targets or milestones at sectoral level. Climate action is a challenge which we must deliver on collectively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of transgender and non-binary young people, who have been contacted by their school to discuss their support plan, since the removal of Guidance on Supporting Transgender Young People from the Education Authority website on  8 September 2025.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of support plans in place across schools for (i) transgender; and (ii) non-binary, students.
answer_text: It is for pupils, parents or carers and schools to work together to agree the most appropriate support. If there is a complaint as to how this is handled at a school level, this should be raised via the school’s internal complaints process. My department does not have a record of the number of support plans in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline where he would direct students and pupils to go for advice, if their school wanted to change their support plan for transgender and non-binary students, without their consent.
answer_text: It is for pupils, parents or carers and schools to work together to agree the most appropriate support. If there is a complaint as to how this is handled at a school level, this should be raised via the school’s internal complaints process. My department does not have a record of the number of support plans in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Ann’s Care Group providing services, including the opening of a children’s residential home at Derry, given that the June 2023 Northern Ireland Review Of Children's Social Care Services Report stated that there should be an explicit commitment that private for-profit companies will not be allowed to provide foster and residential care for children within Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials within my department have inquired with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) on 10 October 2025 regarding new registrations for Children’s Homes in the Western Trust area. At this time there have been no applications from the provider mentioned in your query.  My Department will continue to liaise with RQIA in relation to new applications for registration within the children’s social care sector in Northern Ireland.While currently it is possible for a for-profit operation to register as a provider of a children’s home in Northern Ireland, only a small number have done so.  At 30 June 2024, there were 55 Children’s Residential Homes in Northern Ireland, 46 homes were statutory and 9 were independent sector providers, including providers operating on a not-for-profit and for-profit basis.  Statutory homes provided 268 places at an average of six places per home; independent homes provided 27 places at an average of three per home.An Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland was published in June 2023. That Review made a recommendation not to allow the privatisation of the care of children. In its response to the Review, the Department partially accepted that recommendation, recognising the need to keep private sector provision of children’s social care to a minimum, but also acknowledging the small number of for-profit providers currently operating here and the increasing pressure on services. My Department has established a programme of reform which is leading on the implementation of a number of the Review’s recommendations.  The Residential Placement Capacity Reform Workstream has a clear focus on increasing capacity of the statutory sector to meet the residential needs of children and young people now and in the future. As shown in the table below, the number of children in care in Northern Ireland has increased year on year.  My Department will continue to strive to ensure that children in care receive the best package of care available to them.31 March 20193,28131 March 20203,38331 March 20213,53031 March 20223.62431 March 20233,80131 March 20243,999My Department publishes Official Statistics on the number of children in care in the annual report ‘Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland’ (Children’s social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).  The next release of these Official Statistics for year ending 31 March 2025 is due later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when her Department’s review of Invest NI’s projects and links to Israel will be published.
answer_text: The review has been completed and was published on 16th October 2025 - Review of Investments Report | Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the latest Review of Environmental Governance.
answer_text: The final report from the independent panel, setting out its proposals to strengthen environmental governance in Northern Ireland, was published on 21 October 2025 and is now available to view on the DAERA website.  I plan to bring my proposed way forward to the Executive shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) whether it is a criminal offence to dispose of non-hazardous wastes without the necessary permit or licence; and (ii) when unregulated dumping has taken place, whether his Department has the discretion not to enforce or prosecute against that illegal activity.
answer_text: It is an offence under Article 4 of the Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997 (the 1997 Act) as amended to keep, treat, deposit or dispose of controlled waste without having been authorised to do so under a waste management license issued by the Department or in contravention of said license. In line with the DAERA enforcement policy, each incident received by the NIEA is assessed and prioritised in terms of risk and harm to the environment and health before deciding on the most appropriate action.Both the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and District Councils can use a number of provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to take enforcement action in respect of illegal dumping. Under the joint Fly-tipping Protocol NIEA takes responsibility for investigating large deposits of non-hazardous wastes of 20m³ or more and hazardous materials such as asbestos and Fuel Laundered Waste that cannot be accepted by Councils. Councils take responsibility for dealing with all other deposits such as low-level ad-hoc dumping often referred to as ‘flytipping’.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on work to establish the Independent Climate Commissioner Office, since the approval of the draft Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 on 25 March 2025.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 were affirmed by the Assembly and made by The Executive Office (TEO) on 8 April 2025, and came into force on 9 April 2025, providing legal authority to TEO to appoint the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner (NICC). It is envisaged that it may take between 12 and 24 months from that date to fully establish the NICC.Work to do so commenced in April and is ongoing and well advanced to operationalise the office of the NI Climate Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update of work to establish a Just Transition Commission before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My Department is progressing the development of regulations to establish a Just Transition Commission for Northern Ireland.  I am currently engaging with Executive colleagues on draft regulations for their agreement to lay them in the Assembly for debate and approval.Subject to the Executive and Assembly agreement of the regulations, my officials will progress a public appointments process to recruit members to the Just Transition Commission.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount that his Department has invested in preventive health measures to reduce women’s health inequalities in West Belfast, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last 5 years the Public Health Agency has provided the following investment to preventative health measures to reduce health inequalities in West Belfast. These investments are for the community as a whole, including women’s health:2021/22 - £450,6272022/23 - £501,9592023/24 - £499,3682024/25 - £505,0432025/26 - £543,017There may be additional investment, via the Belfast Heath and Social Care Trust, but given the nature of this spend it is harder to categorise.Tackling health inequalities remains a key focus for me as Health Minister. Unfortunately, given the nature of funding within the health and social care system, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of funding focussed just on addressing health inequalities that women face – as this will cut across many programmes of care and prevention budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how her Department's spend on active travel is meeting departmental targets under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 stipulates that “The Department for Infrastructure must develop sectoral plans for transport which set a minimum spend on active travel from the overall transport budgets of 10%.” My department is working on the basis of ramping up overall investment in active travel to meet this requirement by 2030 and have taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Act towards achieving that. Progress to date since the introduction of Act can be found on my department’s website at Departmental spend on active travel | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of creating women’s health hubs to help address women’s health inequality.
answer_text: While there are a number of different approaches to women’s health hubs across the UK, the concept aims to provide a range of services with rapid, local access within a single visit, provide assessment and treatment where possible and reduce referrals into elective waiting lists. Following the publication of the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report, Gynaecology across Northern Ireland, one of the key recommendations was to establish women’s health hubs to improve access to services and streamline care for women.It is envisaged that further work to develop appropriate service models and enhance existing services across Trusts will be subject to available staff capacity and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the current health and social care system meets the needs of older women.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that the term “health and social care needs” encompasses a broad range of factors including physical and mental health, support with daily living, access to community services, and protection from isolation and economic hardship. Similarly, “older women” is not a homogenous group, and needs may vary significantly depending on age, health status, socioeconomic background, and caregiving responsibilities.The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland provides a range of services aimed at supporting older people, including older women. These include domiciliary care, residential care, community nursing, mental health services, and access to aids and adaptations. Personalised care planning and direct payments are also available to support individual choice and independence.Older women are more likely to live alone, experience poverty in later life, and take on unpaid caregiving roles. Despite this, there is limited gender-disaggregated data available to inform service design and delivery. My Department recognises the importance of addressing these gaps and is committed to improving the evidence base and ensuring services are responsive to the diverse needs of older women.Strategic frameworks such as Delivering Together, my Three Year Plan and the development of the Integrated Care System all aim to improve coordination across services and shift care closer to home. Work is also under way to develop a Neighbourhood Model for health delivery which will further support such a shift. These strategies do not currently differentiate between the needs of older men and older women, however my Department is also supporting a large scale women’s health listening exercise, currently being conducted by Derry Well Women and Queens University Belfast, which aims to listen to the healthcare needs of women and girls across the life course. It is important that women’s voices, including older women, are listened to and enabled to inform future policy and service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how women taking on the care load of other family members impacts on the health of women themselves.
answer_text: I acknowledge the central and indispensable role women play in family caregiving.  Research from Carers NI indicates that approximately 60% of unpaid carers in Northern Ireland are women. Furthermore, women face a 70% probability of undertaking caregiving responsibilities during their adult lives, often beginning earlier and continuing across their lifespan.The intersection of caregiving with other responsibilities such as employment, parenting, and education places significant demands on women. Young carers and young adult carers may be required to balance care for siblings or parents alongside their educational and training commitments. These pressures can constrain women’s access to paid employment, education, and training opportunities, resulting in long-term financial disadvantage. Additionally, caregiving responsibilities can lead to social isolation and limit participation in recreational and community activities.The cumulative impact of caregiving can result in substantial physical and psychological strain, adversely affecting carers’ mental health and overall wellbeing. It is therefore imperative that government departments and relevant sectors recognise and respond to the needs of carers. This includes identifying carers early, providing appropriate support, and ensuring that policies and services are designed to protect and promote their welfare.  To continue improving support for carers, the Department has established the Social Care Collaborative Forum Supporting Carers workstream and a Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has a low-carbon agricultural model that could be rolled out across the sector.
answer_text: The Schemes being introduced as part of my Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) will be essential levers in contributing to Northern Ireland’s statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and achieving a genuinely Just Transition. Their implementation and delivery will be key to meeting the targets set out for the NI Agriculture Sector in the draft Climate Action Plan (2023-2027).Within SAP, the Farming for Carbon workstream is focussed on actions to reduce carbon emissions on farms and offset carbon emissions through carbon sequestration. These actions currently include: Reductions in older livestock numbers through the Beef Sustainability Package and through Knowledge Transfer.Breeding of more environmentally efficient cattle through the Bovine Genetics Project. The use of urease treated fertilisers, the optimal timing of fertiliser and slurry applications and the establishment of grassland swards with legumes and herbs to reduce fertiliser nitrogen use, promoted through applied research and knowledge transfer initiatives.Dietary interventions in livestock diets, for example, the use of methane reducing feed additives and reductions in nitrogen, and phosphorus outputs by collaborative industry research through the AFBI led, Dairy Carbon Network project.Novel livestock slurry treatments to reduce GHG emissions.Development of a Carbon Footprinting Project to provide all farm businesses in Northern Ireland with their whole farm carbon footprint and training on actions that can be taken to reduce GHG emissionsA Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme is also planned within the SAP which will assist farm businesses to adopt technology to help reduce GHG emissions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to bringing the Initial Teacher Education bursary scheme forward for current PGCE students.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme, and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot, is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages.Extending the scheme to include current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the scheme. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.  I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of investment his Department has allocated in the last five years to addressing health inequalities faced by women.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of implementing Friends of the Earth’s Fairness Test to address the climate crisis.
answer_text: The Friends of the Earth’s Fairness Test is primarily focussed around influencing the UK Government’s revised climate plan. While not directly mirroring the Fairness Test, I am pleased that the principle of just transition, with its 11 objectives is at the heart of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. These include objectives, as set out in section 30 of the Act, to support green jobs, reduce poverty and engage with workers and communities.My Department is currently progressing the development of Regulations to establish a Just transition Commission for Northern Ireland which, once operational, will play a key role in advising and overseeing the implementation of the just transition principle. With responsibilities placed on all Northern Ireland Departments under the Act, I am committed to working collaboratively with other Ministers, local government, businesses and the wider public to ensure a fair and just transition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the need for a Just Transition investment fund.
answer_text: I support the need for investment to help meet the targets set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  The Act requires my Department to establish a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture and I was pleased that the Executive agreed to allocate £12.3m Capital DEL for this fund in the Final Budget exercise for 2025-26.  In addition, my Department has recently submitted Capital DEL bids of £47.8m / £77.3m / £106.0m / £121.6m over the four years from 2026-27 to 2029-30 in relation to the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture as part of the ongoing multi-year Budget exercise commissioned by the Department of Finance following the outcome of the 2025 Spending Review. In tandem with this, my officials have commenced work on the development of a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture policy framework. I will continue to engage with Executive colleagues and the UK Government around broader funding for climate action and investment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the affordability of the new Belfast Bikes scheme.
answer_text: ‘Belfast Bikes’ is a Belfast City Council led initiative with my Department providing capital funding to facilitate the introduction of 100 new e-bikes and 300 pedal bikes into the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in Belfast with fit-for-purpose playgrounds, and appropriate outdoor space.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information specifically on playgrounds which are fit for purpose, as this is a subjective issue and one which is impossible to measure consistently.Most schools have adequate space to provide for outdoor play, however there will always be opportunities to improve facilities for our children and young people. Where a need for improvement is identified, my Department will try to meet the need through its capital investment programmes.I was pleased to announce my Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme in December 2024, which has a specific focus on the provision of outdoor sports facilities. Following this, the Outdoor Learning Project launched in early 2025, which provided funding for outdoor play equipment to all nursery (93), primary (776) and special (40) schools. A further call was issued to all primary school, inviting applications for the provision of outdoor covered sports space.Through these programmes, I am striving to provide enhanced outdoor facilities for schools, however it is up to schools to determine how best to incorporate these facilities into their delivery of the curriculum.I look forward to launching further calls for investment in outdoor facilities in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Active Travel in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report into active travel in Northern Ireland, which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North.  I can confirm that the report will be considered very carefully and a response to the recommendations will be provided in line with the established process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of kilometers of additional greenway routes, expected to be in place by March 2027.
answer_text: Greenways are generally council led with my department providing grant funding of up to 50% of the capital costs of approved projects. I recently launched a Greenway Grant funding round inviting councils to apply for capital funding to support delivery of their priorities, spanning the 2025-26 and 2026-27 financial years.The Strategic Plan for Greenways was published in 2016 and positive progress is being made. For example, Derry City and Strabane District Council led on the delivery of over 46km of greenway in recent years as part of the North West Greenway Network, and I recently attended an official launch event to mark completion of the Carlingford Lough Greenway, which has delivered over 11km of cross-border greenway connecting Newry to Carlingford, via Omeath.Ards and North Down Borough Council are currently progressing construction of the 5.4km Bangor to Newtownards Greenway, and my officials are engaged with the Council to progress their plans for the 9km Comber to Newtownards Greenway towards construction in the coming years.Belfast City Council are leading on phase 2 of the Lagan Gateway project, with construction works anticipated to commence in the coming months. My officials are also actively engaged with various other councils on a number of planned future greenway projects including:Fermanagh and Omagh District Council - Sligo to Enniskillen Greenway;Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council - Portrush to the Giant’s Causeway Greenway; andDerry City and Strabane District Council – Strabane to Sion Mills Greenway. As part of my department’s delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network, a number of active travel projects are also being progressed along proposed traffic free routes. For example: the first phase West Belfast Greenway is due to commence in October 2025 upgrading 0.5km of paths within the Bog Meadows to greenway standard, and design of further phases of the West Belfast Greenway and Sydenham Greenway will also be progressed over the period.Progression of Greenways through the design and planning phases to construction can take a number of years and include many complexities that can impact on delivery. As councils generally lead on Greenways, they are best placed to advise on progress on individual projects.I acknowledge the positive work that has taken place in recent years through partnership working and look forward to further progress being made across a range of projects so that as many people as possible can avail of the numerous benefits Greenways can deliver for local communities and visitors alike.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's planned spend on greenway routes, from September 2025 to March 2027.
answer_text: Greenways are generally council led with my department providing grant funding of up to 50% of the capital costs of approved projects. I recently launched a Greenway Grant funding round inviting councils to apply for capital funding to support delivery of their priorities, spanning the 2025-26 and 2026-27 financial years.There are currently a number of multi-year greenway projects being led by councils which are part funded by my department. For example: Letters of Offer have been issued to Ards and North Down Borough Council for the Newtownards to Green Road, Bangor Greenway and Comber to Newtownards Greenway of £2.8m and £2.4m respectively. My department also recently issued a capital funding Letter of Offer to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council for £0.1m in 2025-26 to support geotechnical survey work as part of the scheme development of the Sligo to Enniskillen Greenway.As part of my department’s delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network, a number of active travel projects are also being progressed along proposed traffic free routes. For example: West Belfast Greenway Phase 1a is due to commence in October 2025, with an estimated construction cost of £0.5m, and design of further phases of the West Belfast Greenway and Sydenham Greenway will also be progressed over the period. I also refer the member to the AQW 32579/22-27 which refers to work ongoing in relation to greenways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department records data on unsuitable or non-suitable school placements.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised:The EA does not hold the data requested.The Educational Needs & Disability (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 sets out under 7 (3) that if a statement is maintained they will be educated in ab ordinary school unless that is incompatible with:The wishes of parentsThe provision of efficient education of othersSchools need to take all reasonable steps, with support from services if required, to meet the needs of children and young people. The suitability of a placement therefore is contextual and can change. If a placement is not meeting the child/young person’s Special Education Needs, then a change of placement can be requested via the Annual Review process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the incorporation of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child into domestic law.
answer_text: There are no plans to incorporate the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child into domestic law in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in West Belfast with sensory or quiet rooms available for pupils with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: My department does not hold a record of the number of sensory or quiet rooms in schools.  All specialist provision classes will have access to a sensory or breakout area, and minor works are delivered to provide such spaces for mainstream pupils if recommended by an occupational therapist to support a pupil(s).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any processes in place to ensure his Department realises the educational rights of pupils with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: The Education Authority adheres to the statutory timeframes and guidance set out in The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, The Special Educational Needs and Disability (Northern Ireland) Order 2005, The Education (SEN) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 and the Code of Practice on the identification and assessment of SEN (1998), including its supplement (2005) when considering the educational rights of pupils with special educational needs and disabilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they plan to write to the British Government regarding the proposed plans for a digital ID scheme, requesting that the North is exempt from the implementation of such a scheme.
answer_text: We have raised our objections to the proposed Digital ID scheme directly with Darren Jones, the Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will publish (i) the responses to the Being Open Framework consultation; and (ii) a summary of responses report.
answer_text: My Department is working to publish the Being Open Framework and a Departmental Consultation Analysis Report in October 2025. This will be followed by a period of implementation with HSC Trusts and others to embed the use of the Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28045/22-27, when the Being Open Framework will be published.
answer_text: My Department is working to publish the Being Open Framework and a Departmental Consultation Analysis Report in October 2025. This will be followed by a period of implementation with HSC Trusts and others to embed the use of the Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have written to the UK Foreign Secretary on the recent attacks on the Global Sumud Flotilla ships, and to encourage the UK Government to use its diplomatic powers to guarantee the safe passage of those on the flotilla.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is helping people facing hunger or food insecurity to afford food, and basic needs.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 31296/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is legally obliged to carry out a Habitats Regulations Assessment, in accordance with the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995, as amended, before it can issue a Schedule 6 consent to discharge to a (i) Special Area of Conservation; or (ii) Special Protection Area.
answer_text: Under the provisions of the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (NI) 1995, as amended, my Department acts as a competent authority. In this capacity the Department must determine if a plan or project may affect the protected features of a Special Area of Conservation (SAC) or Special Protection Area (SPA), before giving consent under schedule 6 of the Drainage Order (1973).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, under the proposed redevelopment of Belfast City Airport, what is the estimated expected increase in aircraft (i) arrivals; and (ii) departures.
answer_text: Under the current planning agreement, the Airport is restricted to 48,000 ATMs (Air Traffic Movements) in any period of 12 months. ATMs includes both arrivals and departures. The recently launched Draft Belfast City Airport Master Plan includes a proposal to increase the ATM limit to accommodate the projected growth in passenger demand to 7 million passengers per annum by 2040 in order to allow for delivery of the Master Plan. The Department has not received any requests to amend the current Planning Agreement to allow for an increase in ATMs.I would encourage interested parties to engage draft Master Plan consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, under the proposed redevelopment of Belfast City Airport, to detail (i) whether there are any plans to extend current curfew for night time arrivals; and (ii) the expected time flights will arrive at night.
answer_text: Under the current planning agreement, the Airport is restricted to the operating hours of 6.30am to 9.30pm with delayed aircraft only allowed to operate later and up to 11:59pm if there are exceptional circumstances. As part of the consultation on the recently launched Draft Belfast City Airport Masterplan 2040, the Airport state they are exploring adjustments to their operating hours to allow for some additional scheduled flights beyond the current 15 hour daily limit, but within the existing shoulder period for delays to 23:59. It further states that subject to feedback, they will consider how best to accommodate increased flexibility whilst also ensuring that local communities are protected and receive the benefits of cleaner and quieter aircraft sooner. I would encourage interested parties to engage in this consultation process.The Department has not received any requests to amend the current Planning Agreement to allow for flights after 21:30.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether increased car parking is expected as part of the Belfast City Airport redevelopment.
answer_text: Belfast City Airport launched their draft Master Plan 2040 on 16 September 2025 with public consultation running until 11 November 2025.  The Plan contains proposals for the growth of the Airport over the next 15 years, including proposals to improve surface access facilities and reduce the proportion of people accessing the airport by car.  The draft Master Plan states that it is likely upgrades to the existing access, parking and forecourt facilities will be required but that parking levels will be capped to encourage greater use of public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the funding mechanism for the cost of redeveloping Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to our three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  Belfast City Airport is privately owned and is expected to fund their own operational and facility improvements independently. I recommend contacting Belfast City Airport directly with this query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a greater number of early morning flights are expected with the redevelopment of Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The Department has not received any requests to amend the current Planning Agreement to allow for an increase in ATMs. I would encourage interested parties to engage in the draft Master Plan consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline in what situations would GPs or their staff contact the PSNI after a patient call.
answer_text: GPs and their practice staff may contact the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) following patient calls in specific circumstances where they have attained patient consent, the disclosure is legally required, or it is in the public interest. The decision to contact the PSNI will be the responsibility of the GP practice assisted by the available guidance. Patient confidentiality is fundamental to the doctor-patient relationship but is not absolute. There are a number of specific circumstances where GP practices must disclose information to the PSNI. The points outlined below are not exhaustive, however by way of example, GP practices must disclose information about terrorism under the Terrorism Act 2000, report a ‘relevant offence’ including serious crime such as murder, manslaughter, rape and child abuse under the Criminal Law (Northern Ireland) Act 1967 and when legally compelled by court order.In the absence of consent from the patient, disclosure occurs only when legally required or justified in the public interest, for example to prevent serious harm or death, following credible threats of serious violence to identifiable individuals, risk of child abuse or neglect, risk of serious harm to vulnerable adults or serious threats to public safety.GP practices can also contact the PSNI where patients consent to police involvement (e.g., victims of assault requesting police involvement, fitness to be detained documentation).The Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 require practices to report incidents to the police if they are removing a patient on grounds of violence or threatening behaviour. This provision relates to the practice's duty of care to staff and other patients. GP practices must balance confidentiality with protection of others from serious harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust's out of hours GP policy when responding to calls from patients who have schizophrenia.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) Out of Hours service follow the 2024/2025 Urgent Care Regional Specification for Out of Hours issued by my Department.Patients phone calls are triaged in line with the specification guidance.  The calls are taken by the call handler, prioritised, and then passed to the clinician.  The outcome of the call could result in advice, a prescription, a base visit (at either of the Trust’s bases in Belfast) or a house visit.Following the initial triage and outcome, the call could be passed to BHSCT’s Unscheduled Mental Health Team or to the Approved Social Worker for a joint mental health assessment with the medical clinician.The Out of Hours period covers from when the GP practices close each day, at the weekend and bank holidays.The Out of Hours Teams include call handlers, drivers, General Practitioners, MDT teams including nurses and pharmacists who can access Social Workers and on-call specialists.  The teams work closely with the Emergency Departments, Urgent Care Centre and the 24-Hour Nursing teams.All the teams have ongoing medical training covering aspects of Urgent Care through in-house training, regional GP educational courses, as well as commissioned bespoke GP training courses. Each clinician has an annual appraisal, which can help to identify any training needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what aspects of the health service have a trauma-informed approach to care.
answer_text: The use of trauma-informed approaches has become increasingly common across a range of sectors, including health and social care. The main objective of a trauma-informed approach is to ensure the physical, psychological and emotional safety of individuals who have experienced a traumatic event. It is intended to prevent the re-traumatisation of victims/survivors when they recall their experiences.The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland embeds a trauma-informed approach through strategic frameworks, service design, and workforce development. This is reflected in key policies such as the Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031, Delivering Together (2026), and Protect Life 2 (Suicide Prevention), which collectively promote person-centred care, early intervention, and integrated service delivery. These strategies ensure that trauma awareness informs how services are planned and delivered, fostering environments that are safe, responsive, and supportive for individuals affected by trauma.While the extent of implementation may vary across settings, the overarching aim is to embed trauma awareness into service planning, delivery, and workforce development wherever possible.The Regional Trauma Network (RTN), overseen jointly by my Department and The Executive Office, involves collaborative working with statutory, community and voluntary sector organisations to address trauma related to the Troubles/conflict. It operates through dedicated teams embedded within each HSC Trust. These teams are governed by Trust-specific policies and procedures, including trauma-informed policies.  All staff delivering RTN services are expected to apply a trauma-informed approach in their practice. The Network actively promotes trauma-informed care across the region and includes representation from three Trusts, RTN clinical leads and the RTN lead psychologist on the Safeguarding Board of Northern Ireland Trauma Informed Practice Committee.Notably, the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) RTN team has led a quality improvement initiative to create a trauma-informed waiting area and has contributed to staff training efforts, reinforcing the Network’s commitment to embedding trauma-informed culture in service delivery.My Department remains committed to embedding trauma-informed approaches in policy development to address the pervasive and long-term impact of trauma.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans to introduce a transitional bursary or financial support package for current PGCE students who are outside the remit of the Initial Teacher Education Bursary Scheme.
answer_text: The Department recognises the dedication of students who have commenced Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes, including the financial commitment required. Their dedication to pursue a career in teaching is deeply valued and appreciated. As announced, the bursary scheme will apply to new entrants of relevant subjects, beginning their ITE programmes from September 2026 onwards. The purpose of the bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages. Introducing a transitional or hardship funding arrangement would not increase applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the pilot. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only. I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.Students experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to explore discretionary hardship support that may be available from their Higher Education Institution (HEI), which includes access to the Department for the Economy’s Higher Education Support Fund. HEIs typically have support mechanisms in place to assist students facing unexpected financial pressures, and students should contact their university’s student support or finance office for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the McCullough Review report into the conduct by the PSNI; and (ii) to outline the actions she will take following the publication of the report.
answer_text: I wish to place on record my thanks to Angus McCullough KC and his team for what is a thorough and detailed review and to the group of experts and stakeholders for their contribution.While the McCullough Review found no evidence of systemic surveillance of journalists, which is to be welcomed, it did not amount to a clean bill of health, with several findings of poor or unlawful practice.  This is not solely a matter of operational policing, for which the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, but also impacts on wider issues of confidence. The Policing Board has stated that it views the Report as part of fulfilling its duties under Section 59 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.  The Board has made clear it wishes to carefully review the findings of the McCullough Review, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for implementing the recommendations, before deciding on next steps.  I believe it is important that the Board is given the time and space to fully consider the report and its preferred way forward. As I have said previously, I share the serious concerns which have been expressed by the Board in relation to this issue and stand ready to support the Board in whatever actions it considers necessary.  In addition, the Investigatory Powers Tribunal has yet to report, after which a further update to McCullough will be made. Ahead of the Tribunal findings and the Board’s considerations, it would be premature to comment on what actions may be required on my part.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will introduce emergency or hardship funds for current PGCE students.
answer_text: The Department recognises the dedication of students who have commenced Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes, including the financial commitment required. Their dedication to pursue a career in teaching is deeply valued and appreciated. As announced, the bursary scheme will apply to new entrants of relevant subjects, beginning their ITE programmes from September 2026 onwards. The purpose of the bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages. Introducing a transitional or hardship funding arrangement would not increase applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the pilot. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only. I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.Students experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to explore discretionary hardship support that may be available from their Higher Education Institution (HEI), which includes access to the Department for the Economy’s Higher Education Support Fund. HEIs typically have support mechanisms in place to assist students facing unexpected financial pressures, and students should contact their university’s student support or finance office for further information.

answer_text: My officials are aware of the flooding issues in Denewood Drive, Belfast having attended the site on five occasions in the last five years.  A recently completed investigation of the drainage system has identified issues with the drainage lines.Works to remediate the issue have been identified and are programmed to commence mid November. The works will take approximately two weeks to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of commissioned mental health learning disability beds there are in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Individuals with Learning Disabilities can access assessment and treatment for their mental health needs through a stepped care model. Services are provided in every Health and Social Care Trust within the community, through clinics and social care settings, such as beds in care home facilities. This level of assessment and treatment is provided where it has been assessed that inpatient service provision is not required.When a person’s needs are primarily associated with mental health and inpatient treatment is required, a person who has low level needs associated with their Learning Disability, can have their needs met in a mental health inpatient facility. This is available in all Trusts. Excluding the remaining commissioned beds within Muckamore Abbey Hospital, with consideration of imminent closure of the hospital site, the Department of Health currently commissions 23 specialist inpatient Learning Disability beds in the Region. These beds are accessed as part of a network and are available to all Health and Social Care Trusts. These beds are geographically located throughout the Region with 10 beds in the Western HSCT, 10 in the Southern HSCT and 3 in the Northern HSCT.

My Department is not involved in the issuing, selling or regulation of Israeli war bonds.The purpose of my department’s Roundtable in November 2024 was to provide a platform for key stakeholders to highlight local issues and concerns around access to cash and banking services with my predecessor, Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA, which were in turn communicated to Treasury and the regulators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department calculates future projections of spending on private healthcare providers.
answer_text: The Department has been faced with single year budgets since 2015/16, which has impeded long-term financial planning. Uncertainty around sustainable funding means that is not possible to calculate future projections of spending. On 30 May 2025, I announced the publication of an Implementation Plan for my Department’s Elective Care Framework. The Plan describes a combination of measures to tackle our waiting lists including maximising the existing infrastructure, expansion in core capacity in a range of specialties and partnership working with the Independent Sector (IS). Given the scale of the current backlog across both outpatients and treatments, and the time required to establish additional in-house capacity, it will be necessary for Health and Social Care services to continue making use of IS capacity.

BHSCT Dermatology Service currently have a number of initiatives to tackle current red flag waiting times;  Ongoing recruitment to replace a vacant dermatology consultant; Use of independent sector- approx.  500 patients sent out per quarter;Photo triage hub pilot - to reduce the number of patients being automatically added to the Red Flag waiting list;  Advice and guidance pilot for all incoming referrals;Pilot for ‘direct to nurse-led surgery’ from photo triage hub for any confirmed cancers;Use of mega clinics and WLI funding; and,Investment in nursing workforce (ANP in skin cancer funding required).I hope that this addresses your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the health impact on an individual marked for red flag dermatology treatment when they have not been treated until after 18 weeks.
answer_text: The health impact on an individual marked for red flag dermatology treatment when they have not been treated until after 18 weeks, will be specific to the individual patient’s condition and diagnosis.A patient may be referred as a red flag with suspected cancer symptoms, but this may not be the case when they are seen in a dermatology clinic.Therefore, the health impact will depend on the outcome of diagnosis, and will be specific to the patient and the condition. However, such long waits are not what I want for any patient with suspected cancer symptoms. To address these unacceptable waiting times, the Department of Health has been working with Trusts to increase productivity, improve efficiency, identify new ways of working and secure additional funding for the HSC as a whole.  On this last point, you may be aware that I recently announced £215m for additional outpatient appointments and treatments that will prioritise the most urgent patients waiting to receive care.  This follows investment in new initiatives under the Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals the Homeless Health Nurse has worked with in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust since the post was created.
answer_text: Homelessness in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area is managed by the Belfast Inclusion Health Service.  Between 1 January 2018 and 30 August 2025, there have been 7,667 referrals.  Each referral may have multiple patient contacts recorded against it, with some individuals being referred more than once.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any plans to amend the eligibility criteria for the RAISE programme.
answer_text: The methodology used to select localities for the RAISE programme was objective and data informed, with all areas having to meet a threshold of need under a wide criteria.  I have no plans to revisit or amend the methodology.The RAISE Programme seeks to raise achievement and address educational disadvantage through a whole community approach to education. Strategic Area Plans for the 18 RAISE localities across Northern Ireland were published on 5 September. These plans set the context and challenges in terms of educational disadvantage in each area, along with a number of objectives focused on addressing these challenges, raising attainment and raising aspirations.  An open call was launched on 5 September inviting project proposals to deliver against the objectives set out in the Strategic Area Plans. I have also announced a series of cross-cutting initiatives that will span across RAISE areas and beyond to address common issues and complement locality led projects. Work to progress these initiatives is being progressed by my officials.The RAISE Programme presents a real opportunity for organisations to take a fresh look at the challenges caused by deprivation and to be part of a transformative, whole community approach to tackling disadvantage. By working together, we can remove the barriers to learning and help children and young people across Northern Ireland reach their full potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to make it easier for people who are homeless to register with a GP.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland homeless people have the same access to primary care as other people.Under the General Medical Services Contract, GP practices provide services to patients who are registered with them, to temporary residents or to patients where emergency or immediate necessary treatment is required. Those who have been already registered with a GP before becoming homeless can have a C/O address within a Practice area.Northern Ireland, as with the rest of the UK, has a system whereby anyone can get an emergency appointment or immediately necessary treatment with a GP in the locality where they are staying. This also applies to those with no fixed address.Business Services Organisation has developed a GP registration form for those who are experiencing homelessness to make the process as straight forward as possible. In the circumstances where a patient would not be in possession of photographic identification for example, those experiencing homelessness, the GP surgery can discuss with the patient or their representative(s) what alternative documents are available that would be acceptable.In instances where the Practice may contact Business Services Organisation about a case like this, the Practice would be advised to make contact with the homeless shelter as they would normally be in a position to verify the person and supply a letter to this effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the Scottish government's decision to pause all public funding to arms companies involved in making parts for Israel's weaponry; and (ii) whether her Department is considering introducing a similar measure.
answer_text: I support the Scottish Government’s position. My position on this issue is available here: https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-investments-report

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any action his Department has taken to ensure public money has not been used to facilitate Israeli military operations.
answer_text: My Department is not involved in the funding of Israeli military operations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals who availed of anti-psychotic medication in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Belfast HSC Trust have confirmed that unfortunately its electronic care records cannot extrapolate this specific information, so are unable to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total population who aren’t registered with a GP.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not available in the format you have requested.The following table presents the most recent available comparable data to show variance of Northern Ireland registered patients with latest published estimated overall population size.(NISRA –30 June 2024)Figures Published 19 Sept 2024Registered Patients 2,058,672Mid-Year Population Estimate 1,927,855Source: Business Services Organisation, Information and Analysis Directorate, Department of Health There are differences between the figures for GP Registered Patients and residential population of approximately 6.8% according to the mid-year estimates.This discrepancy can arise from a number of factors, for example, time lags in removing patients from GP lists following death, patient movement outside of Northern Ireland with delayed or no notification to GP, Frontier Workers registered with a Northern Ireland GP, or registered patients not completing the 2021 Census (which is used a basis for population estimates).GP Practices will register patients and remove patients from their list in line with the provisions of the General Medical Services (GMS) Contract Northern Ireland, ensuring compliance with the contractual and regulatory requirements.  Under the terms of the GMS Contract, patients are entitled to seek registration, and to have that registration accepted, at any GP practice of their choice, as long as the practice list is open and the patient lives within the practice’s catchment area (and is entitled to receive health care within Northern Ireland).  Northern Ireland, as with the rest of the UK, has a system whereby anyone can get immediately necessary treatment with a GP in the locality where they are staying. This also applies to those with no fixed address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how the health of people who are homeless is recorded and monitored.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland those experiencing homelessness have the same access to primary care as other individuals.Under the General Medical Services Contract, GP practices provide services to patients who are registered with them, to temporary residents or to patients where emergency or immediate necessary treatment is required and patient records in these practices would be recorded under existing processes.  Belfast Inclusion Health Service also currently records information regarding all interventions they carry out using the Encompass System, in line with the Belfast Trust Guidelines. Other Inclusion Nurses across the remaining Trusts would record their interventions in a similar way. Belfast Inclusion Health Service take referrals from various statutory and voluntary sector agencies and see individuals via outreach clinics at various locations within the Belfast Trust area. Staff have referral pathways with various teams within the Belfast Trust such as Genitourinary Medicine, Sexual & Reproductive Health, Hepatology, Tissue Visabilty Nurse and Emergency Departments to ensure that service users are seen in a timely manner and receive appropriate care when needed. Service users at risk of Blood Borne Viruses receive regular screening to ensure timely detection of viruses and onward referral for treatment to help reduce the onward spread of infection and those with ongoing wounds/ infections will also be monitored regularly until the wounds are healed. Service users on treatment for HIV/ Hepatitis C Virus also receive regular support from the Support Workers within the team to ensure medication concordance. Staff also facilitate health promotion events within hostels to help improve overall health of those currently experiencing homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the role and remit of the Homeless Health Nurse.
answer_text: The Homeless Health Nurse is crucial in supporting the unique healthcare needs of homeless individuals.  Whilst specific roles will vary according to the needs of the individual, their remit is to engage with the vulnerable homeless population, working in collaboration with other statutory, community and voluntary partners, to provide essential and holistic health services and to promote overall well-being.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals in temporary accommodation that have experienced depression, anxiety or other mental health related illness in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not hold this information.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the role her Department has in implementing the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, including (i) prevention of causing death or grievous bodily injury by careless or inconsiderate driving; (ii) power of entry; (iii) stopping on verges or in dangerous positions; (iv) parking of heavy commercial vehicles on verges, central reservations and footways.
answer_text: Operational implementation of the offences within the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 is carried out by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  The Member may therefore wish to contact the PSNI directly for further information, or alternatively the Department for Infrastructure on parking matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company's (i) revenue; (ii) gross profit; (iii) net profit, for all holdings in (a) land; (b) property; and (c) assets, over the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested by the Member in relation to Translink’s Group Accounts is publicly available on the Translink website at  www.translink.co.uk/corporate-publications

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when Translink will become an accredited Living Wage employer.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink is unable to confirm when it will become an accredited Living Wage employer.   Whilst progress has been made in this area, Translink recognise some job roles continue to be paid below this level.  We continue to actively engage with Real Living Wage NI and Trade Unions on this matter. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the roles within Translink, which can avail of a car allowance; and (ii) for a breakdown of car allowance spend, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:(i)  Roles within Translink which can avail of a car allowance   The payment of a car allowance is part of contractual terms and conditions for a number of specified roles within the organisation, to enable the duties of the post to be carried out in full.   Unfortunately, Translink is unable to provide all the information requested, specifically, unable to provide the roles within Translink which can avail of a car allowance.   As a data controller, Translink is obliged to consider its obligations under the Data Protection Act 2018 and the UK GDPR. Car allowances form part of the remuneration package for designed roles.  The details sought relate to easily identifiable individuals and so constitute personal data. The disclosure of this information would contravene the data protection rights of the individuals involved. A recent ICO decision notice reconfirmed that staff salary details like these are personal data, and that their disclosure would breach the data protection principles.   (ii) for a breakdown of car allowance spend, in each of the last five years A car allowance is part of contractual terms and conditions of employment for specified roles and as such would not be considered as ‘spend’.  Translink can however provide the total value of car allowances and the total number of staff with this contractual entitlement broken down by year.   Year Amount No of Staff 2020/21    329,429          74 2021/22    335,203          80 2022/23    338,559          91 2023/24    327,749          80 2024/25    327,189          80 For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of staff within Translink, who do not receive the living wage or above.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised that Translink have 179 staff who currently receive a rate of pay less than the real living wage.  This is based on basic hourly rate and excludes shift premium or other allowances that may be payable.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.
