question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the Implementation of the My Right to Access Healthcare Card, similar to that in use in London.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland homeless people have the same access to primary care as other people.Under the General Medical Services Contract, GP practices provide services to patients who are registered with them, to temporary residents or to patients where emergency or immediate necessary treatment is required. Those who have been already registered with a GP before becoming homeless can have a C/O address within a Practice area.Northern Ireland, as with the rest of the UK, has a system whereby anyone can get an emergency appointment or immediately necessary treatment with a GP in the locality where they are staying. This also applies to those with no fixed address.Business Services Organisation has developed a GP registration form for those who are experiencing homelessness to make the process as straight forward as possible. In the circumstances where a patient would not be in possession of photographic identification for example, those experiencing homelessness, the GP surgery can discuss with the patient or their representative(s) what alternative documents are available that would be acceptable.In instances where the Practice may contact Business Services Organisation about a case like this, the Practice would be advised to make contact with the homeless shelter as they would normally be in a position to verify the person and supply a letter to this effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of individuals who have attended the Homeless Hub in Belfast since its foundation.
answer_text: The Belfast Inclusion Health Service was founded in 2018 and was previously located on Townsend Street, Belfast. In the period from 1 January 2018 until 31 May 2024 the team delivered 8965 interventions. However, it is important to note that the number of interventions does not equate to the number of individuals engaged, as some individuals will have visited the Hub on more than one occasion.The service is currently temporarily based in Shankill Health and Wellbeing Centre, following an arson attack in July 2024, on the adjoining premises of the Welcome Organisation, and continues to seek alternative premises.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to AQW 28560/22-27, to detail how Ministers ensure the views and concerns of asylum seekers are aired at meetings with the Home Office.
answer_text: Officials gather information on the views and concerns of asylum seekers directly and from partner organisations working with those seeking to settle here on a regular basis. This informs discussion at the Strategic Planning Group, of which Home Office is a member, and ongoing engagement with Home Office on issues locally.In addition, last year our department commissioned research on the lived experiences of refugees and asylum seekers, which built on research commissioned previously.  This research, conducted by Red Cross, is intended to inform our work, including delivery of the Refugee Integration Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who availed of the Maternity Psychological Service at the Royal Maternity Jubilee Hospital, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Maternity Psychological Service provides psychological assessment and treatment for patients during the antenatal and postnatal periods. Patients who receive antenatal care typically continue to receive psychological support after birth.  The Trust also offers specialist psychological support for bereaved parents who present with additional vulnerabilities, such as a history of mental health difficulties, fertility issues, previous perinatal loss or trauma symptoms alongside their grief.In addition to an outpatient service, the Trust provides a specialist inpatient psychology service for patients on the Maternity Ward.  This includes providing consultation to maternity staff regarding patient’s psychological wellbeing.  A core aim of the inpatient service is preventative: identifying potentially vulnerable families at risk of being negatively impacted by their maternity experience, for example, due to a complex, difficult or traumatic birth, a prolonged hospital stay, or baby loss. The service offers psychological assessment and treatment for birth partners, mothers and fathers experiencing psychological distress related to their maternity care experience.  This often involves couples therapy, but individual sessions are also provided if needed. The service contributes to staff support and provides supervision to specialist midwives who lead on mental health, social complexity and bereavement.The Maternity Psychological Service is currently funded for 2.4wte Clinical Psychology staff.  Longstanding high demand for the service outweighs capacity and unfortunately there are waiting lists within the service. Established in 1999, it is acknowledged that the service requires additional funding.  As a tertiary centre, the Trust experiences higher rates of complex pregnancies and a greater likelihood of psychological issues and complaints.  As such, the limited capacity does not adequately meet demand, leading to long waiting times and gaps in care.  For example, there is currently no Clinical Psychology provision for the Regional Neonatal Intensive Care Unit based in the Trust, which is a gap in care for the most vulnerable babies and their families, as well as the staff who support for them. The need for fully commissioned clinical psychology services within maternity and neonatal settings is clearly outlined in Recommendation 1 of the RQIA Perinatal Mental Health Review in 2017.  The service has introduced initiatives to attempt to manage the high demand and gaps in service, while also highlighting challenges to Departmental officials and PHA colleagues.Patients Availing of the Maternity Psychological Service in each of the last five years.2021*2022*2023*20242025**TotalPatients Referred to Service2292262162572031131Patients Availed of Service192190159180123844*Years with reduced capacity due to planned staff absences (usually staff maternity leave)** Patients Referred/Availed of Service as of 19/9/2025The table above includes the total number of

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children who have been put at risk via a lack of safeguarding because of delayed inspections by the Education and Training Inspectorate, over the last five years; and (ii) how the Department measures this.
answer_text: It is precisely because we do not know how many children are at risk - or whether those risks are being identified - that it is essential that action is taken to ensure that inspections can operate effectively and as intended.   As inspections have proceeded largely unobstructed in the last academic year, serious shortfalls in child protection arrangements have been identified at a small number of schools.  The inspection evidence has ensured that these weaknesses were identified and addressed, with support provided by the Education Authority’s Child Protection Support Services.  In these cases, there is evidence that the shortcomings have existed for a sustained period prior to the inspection.During periods of Action Short of Strike, the Inspectorate was unable to evaluate fully the outworking of safeguarding arrangements in schools or engage meaningfully with staff and pupils - interactions which are vital in identifying potential child protection concerns.  Published reports confirm this limitation across almost 400 inspections of statutory schools.  It remains unknown how many of these schools may have similar shortcomings, potentially placing young people or staff at risk.To provide further context, I can advise that in the last five full academic years, the Inspectorate identified eight organisations with unsatisfactory child protection arrangements. In contrast, during the five years prior - when inspection was not obstructed to the same extent - 52 organisations were identified and supported to improve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of bereaved parents who have availed of mental health treatment via the private sector, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Please be advised that my Department does not collate information on private sector mental health treatment for bereaved parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures in place to ensure management in (i) his Department; and (ii) the Housing Executive are obligated to declare whether they or immediate family members have multiple properties, in order to avoid conflict of interests.
answer_text: My Department implements a Declaration of Interests Policy.  The policy is applicable to all staff and is supported by a bi-annual Certificate of Assurance process that monitors compliance.  Aligned to the NICS Code of Ethics, Registers of Interest capture potential conflicts for all staff, Senior Civil Servants, Non-Executive Board and Departmental Audit and Risk Assurance Committee members.  These are regularly reviewed and published annually on the Department’s website. (ii) 	The Housing Executive also has a well-established Code of Conduct and annual Declaration of Interests process in place.  Declarations are subject to review, and perceived conflicts investigated.  It should be noted that, owning multiple homes does not, automatically, constitute a conflict of interest.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there are any restrictions on ex-prisoners, jailed before 1998, from obtaining home insurance.
answer_text: It has been highlighted at the Ex-Prisoner Working Group that some ex-prisoners have encountered issues with their applications based on the information required to be provided to insurance companies regarding convictions. We are advised that the Ex-Prisoner Working Group have lobbied both the British Insurance Brokers Association (BIBA) and the Association of British Insurers for change. BIBA has developed an online search facility https://insurance.biba.org.uk/find-insurance which has been optimised for the key words - ‘Northern Ireland’ and ‘Conflict Convictions’ providing clients with details of brokers offering insurance cover for home, motor, travel and small business. This facility is ‘live’ and allows for up-to-date information to be accessed digitally. This includes a local list of insurance companies and maintenance of an up-to-date digital search for brokers with access to a variety of products, giving a more competitive market for groups including ex-prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what steps her Department has taken to ensure Operation Begrain is proceeding as a robust inquiry; (ii) why no senior officials from the Belfast Trust or the Department of Health have been questioned in connection with withheld (a) records; (b) patient harm; or (c) governance failings; and (iii) what her Department is doing to promote transparency and justice for affected patients.
answer_text: I would refer you to the published response to AQW 30029/22-27.The Police Service of Northern Ireland’s (PSNI) Operation Begrain is a live police investigation into the clinical practices of the former consultant neurologist Michael Watt.  The PSNI has operational independence in its leadership of this inquiry. The Department of Health does not comment on live police investigations or operational policing matters.In common with all parts of the Health and Social Care system, my Department will cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations. On 11 April 2025 an Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI to this end. The PSNI’s website does provide updates and points of contact in regard to Operation Begrain – the following link may be helpful to you  -  www.psni.police.uk/latest-news/detectives-police-services-major-investigation-team-issue-update-operation-begrain

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is monitoring the development and progression of illnesses related to asbestos for people living or working in the area surrounding the July 2025 bonfire on the Donegall Road.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths in West Belfast in which addiction was cited as the main cause of death, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Official statistics on death registration numbers, including causes of death, are produced by NISRA using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO).As part of the statistical process, the cause of death information recorded by the certifying medical professional on the death certificate is converted to codes based on the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-10) which are used for monitoring and comparison purposes. Each case is assigned an underlying cause of death but may also have multiple contributing causes of death. Deaths due to addiction fall within the ICD-10 category F10-F19 ‘Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use’. Each 3-digit code in this group relates to different types of substances, (drugs, alcohol, solvents etc), and within each of those is a subcode that identifies ‘dependence syndrome’. For completeness the following tables include all deaths due to dependence syndrome for all substances, and deaths due to ‘Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use’ generally, which encompasses intoxication, harmful use, withdrawal, psychotic disorders as a result of psychoactive substance use, amnesic syndrome caused by substance use and other mental and behavioural disorders linked with substance use, in addition to dependence syndrome.The data presented shows both West Belfast Assembly Area and an NI total for context and covers the years 2013 to 2023 - the most recent year for which this data is publicly available. Table 1 counts deaths where the relevant codes were identified as the underlying cause, while Table 2 includes deaths where the ICD-10 codes of interest were contributory to death.Table 1 – Deaths where underlying cause of death was a mental and behavioural disorder due to psychoactive substance use  Registration YearWest BelfastNIMental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use F10 to F19Dependence Syndrome linked to psychoactive substance use(F10.2, F11.2, F12.2, F13.2, F14.2, F15.2, F16.2, F17.2, F18.2, F19.2)Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use F10 to F19Dependence Syndrome linked to psychoactive substance use(F10.2, F11.2, F12.2, F13.2, F14.2, F15.2, F16.2, F17.2, F18.2, F19.2)20134445412014223828201553533720163251332017317243201896855520194375572020717140202132694520227673422023425931 Table 2 - Deaths where a contributing cause of death was a mental and behavioural disorder due to psychoactive substance use  Registration YearWest BelfastNIMental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use F10 to F19Dependence Syndrome linked to psychoactive substance use(F10.2, F11.2, F12.2, F13.2, F14.2, F15.2, F16.2, F17.2, F18.2, F19.2)Mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use F10 to F19Dependence Syndrome linked to psychoactive substance use(F10.2, F11.2, F12.2, F13.2, F14.2, F15.2, F16.2, F17.2, F18.2, F19.2)20132710236101201424924082201521102571112016225267104

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of why service providers have not installed protective fencing around 5G masts to prevent them from being set on fire.
answer_text: Mobile infrastructure networks are currently deployed on a commercial basis here by the infrastucture providers. In installing new infrastructure, providers must adhere to standards which are defined by a mix of legislation, codes of practice, and guidance from bodies such as Ofcom. I understand that industry is considering preventative measures to help protect their infrastructure in light of the recent spate of attacks in West Belfast. My officials lead on the Barrier Busting Taskforce, the delivery vehicle for the Mobile Action Plan. One work stream being progressed is a programme of information to help address any perceived concerns regarding mobile technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail under which circumstances the NI Environment Agency is not responsible for air quality monitoring and related issues.
answer_text: The NI Environment Agency (NIEA) will only monitor air quality where it has a statutory responsibility to do so.Industrial Pollution and Radiochemical Inspectorate (IPRI), within NIEA, has powers under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 to require operators it regulates to monitor air emissions to ensure they comply with the emission limits contained in their permits. They also have powers under these regulations to carry out monitoring of regulated sites to ensure the conditions contained in the permit are being met.To monitor air pollution impacts to sensitive ecosystems across NI, NIEA has co-ordinated and funded atmospheric nitrogen monitoring and ecosystem surveys across sensitive habitat types and a range of pollution gradients. Other than that stated above, NIEA does not have further air quality monitoring responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are safe alternatives to creosote.
answer_text: The Health and Safety Executive NI has advised that there are chemical and non-chemical alternatives to creosote available on the market. Authorised chemical alternatives include; copper-based biocides, which are safer than creosote, according to their chemical classification. However, these alternatives have not yet been fully proven to be as effective as creosote in particular areas of use. Controls and restrictions on the use of creosote and creosote treated wood are currently in place to reduce the likelihood of exposure or harm. The possible non-chemical alternatives include the replacement of wood with metal, concrete or other manmade composite materials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the safety of using creosote for public use.
answer_text: Assessment of the safety of substances such as creosote for public use is not within the remit of my department. However, an assessment of the safety of creosote has been undertaken in the European Union’s Biocidal Products Committee and the European Chemicals Agency. As a result of this assessment the European Commission renewed the approval of creosote as an active substance for use in biocidal products of product-type 8, (wood preservatives), in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 528/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council.To ensure a high level of safety for human health, animal health and the environment, the placing on the market of wood treated with creosote was subject to conditions. These conditions include creosote products not being made available on the market to the general public.  Creosote-containing products are only authorised for the treatment by vacuum pressure impregnation of wood in industrial installations to make railway sleepers or utility poles for electricity or telecommunications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the steps he and his Department have taken to ensure Operation Begrain is proceeding as a robust inquiry; (ii) why no senior officials from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust or his Department have been questioned in connection with withheld records, patient harm, or governance failings; and (iii) what he is doing to promote transparency and justice for affected patients.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland’s (PSNI) Operation Begrain is a live police investigation into the clinical practices of the former consultant neurologist Michael Watt.  The PSNI has operational independence in its leadership of this inquiry. The Department of Health does not comment on live police investigations or operational policing matters.In common with all parts of the Health and Social Care system, my Department will cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations. On 11 April 2025 an Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI to this end. The PSNI’s website does provide updates and points of contact in regard to Operation Begrain – the following link may be helpful to you  -  www.psni.police.uk/latest-news/detectives-police-services-major-investigation-team-issue-update-operation-begrain

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 27929 22-27, to define work, as used in this context, including the type of manufacturing or production activity this includes or excludes.
answer_text: The reference to "work" in response to AQW 27929 refers to the manufacturing of products or components.  Invest NI does not provide financial support to subsidise the costs of manufacturing products or components. This type of support is not allowable under EU state aid rules or UK Subsidy Control Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 23086/22-27, whether Invest NI has ever provided any financial support towards production costs of F-35 components to (i) RLC Group; (ii) Survitec; (iii) Moyola Precision Engineering; and (iv) Electric Automation Engineers.
answer_text: Invest NI has not provided any financial support towards the manufacturing costs of F-35 products or components to these companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale behind the removal of frontline rates service staff at Lanyon Plaza.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what departmental groups are in conversation with the PSNI regarding the (i) removal; and (ii) limiting of flags on lampposts.
answer_text: When a complaint is received regarding the erection of a flag, or a request for removal of an attachment and the location having been established, my officials will always contact the PSNI to determine if removal is likely to increase the risk of local community tension.The problem of Illegally erected flags is totally unacceptable.I can assure you that I am committed to working with all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the health problems related to creosote; and (ii) whether anyone has been hospitalised because of it in the last five years.
answer_text: (i) Creosote was banned for consumer use in the UK in 2003 through ‘The Creosote (Prohibition on Use and Marketing) Regulations’, but can still be used in certain circumstances by trained professionals. Creosote is classified as a category 1B carcinogen under the GB Regulation on Classification Labelling and Packaging of Chemicals (CLP Regulations).  This means that there is evidence that it has the potential to cause cancer in humans.  It can also be toxic if swallowed or if it comes into contact with skin. It can cause skin burns and eye damage and may cause allergic skin reactions, according to hazard information provided by manufacturers. However, the impact of creosote on public health, as with all chemicals, depends on how and where creosote is used.(ii) It is not possible to provide information as to ‘whether anyone has been hospitalised because of it in the last five years’ as this information is not routinely collected by Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of occasions after suitability reviews were carried out by the Housing Executive did tenants move to new temporary accommodation, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The MaSN for 2025/26 has not yet been finalised however it is expected to be 26,002. This is up from 25,584 in 2024/25.Due to the significant level of public funding provided to the universities, the Department has to control spending to ensure it stays within its budget allocation. The Maximum Student Number (MaSN) limits the Department’s financial exposure and ensures that funding per place is maintained at a level appropriate to sustain quality. The MaSN, therefore, plays a crucial role in balancing the growth of educational institutions with the available public funding.However, recognizing the need for educational institutions to grow and thrive, my Department maintains a dialogue with all of the higher education institutions on the full range of issues affecting their sustainability and growth. My Department has increased the number of funded undergraduate student places at local universities in 2024/25 and again in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of requests for suitability reviews of temporary accommodation carried out by the Housing Executive, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, and she has responded as follows.“The Housing Executive can provide this information for the last three years. Please see below information regarding temporary accommodation suitability review requests: Statutory Reviews: Suitability of TARegion2022/232023/242024/25Belfast513246South Region7912North Region141422Total725580Please note that reviews are categorised by year based on the date the review request was received.The last three years of data are stored in an accessible format, making them both easily identifiable and verifiable. For earlier years, retrieving the information would require extensive manual collection across multiple teams. As a result, only three years’ worth of data can be provided.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average life expectancy for people who are chronically homeless.
answer_text: My Department currently holds no information regarding the average life expectancy for those who are chronically homeless. There is no way to identify persons that were homeless at the time of their death within the death registration data.However, it is widely recognised that those experiencing homelessness often have complex and multi-faceted needs, many of which would impact on life expectancy. Addressing health inequalities has been and continues to be a priority of mine but it is vital that this is a priority across all parts of Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of surgeries that have been cancelled for patients suffering from haemophilia in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust responded as per Table 1 below:Table 1Belfast Health & Social Care TrustFiscal YearNumber of Surgeries Cancelled2020/21<52021/22<52022/23<52023/24<52024/25<5In order to protect patient confidentiality, small numbers have been replaced with a “<5”.All surgery on patients with haemophilia is performed in Belfast Trust unless it is deemed an emergency and the patient can’t be transferred.The other four Trusts stated that their theatre information systems cannot gather this data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number alleyways which have been adopted by Belfast City Council in West Belfast, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold any information relating to alleyways adopted by Belfast City Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road safety speed signs, in place on Dunmurry Lane, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department does not hold a record of the number of speed limits signs on Dunmurry Lane. However, the road is subject to a 30 and 40 mph speed limit and as such will have terminal and repeater signs in place within the 40mph stretch. I can also advise that road safety speed indicator devices are provided by the Policing and Community Safety Partnership (PCSP) in conjunction with local councils. Officials are not aware of any on Dunmurry Lane.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will implement a my right to access card to facilitate access to health services for homeless people.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland homeless people have the same access to primary care as other people.Under the General Medical Services Contract, GP practices provide services to patients who are registered with them, to temporary residents or to patients where emergency or immediate necessary treatment is required. Those who have been already registered with a GP before becoming homeless can have a C/O address within a Practice area.Northern Ireland, as with the rest of the UK, has a system whereby anyone can get an emergency appointment or immediately necessary treatment with a GP in the locality where they are staying. This also applies to those with no fixed address.Business Services Organisation has developed a GP registration form for those who are experiencing homelessness to make the process as straight forward as possible. In the circumstances where a patient would not be in possession of photographic identification for example, those experiencing homelessness, the GP surgery can discuss with the patient or their representative(s) what alternative documents are available that would be acceptable.In instances where the Practice may contact Business Services Organisation about a case like this, the Practice would be advised to make contact with the homeless shelter as they would normally be in a position to verify the person and supply a letter to this effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of rejected applications for grit boxes, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of grit box applications rejected in West Belfast in the last five years is detailed in the table below:YearNumber of rejected applications2021620222820231202432202532Grit box applications are assessed under criteria set out in my Department’s policy and procedure guide RSPPG E022 Winter Service.Applications that are currently under consideration have not been included in the numbers provided above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of alleyways which have been adopted by her Department, in West Belfast, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has not adopted any alleyways in West Belfast in the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what installations of green infrastructure have been considered to reduce air pollution in the area of Stockman’s Lane, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has no remit to plant additional trees at Stockman’s Way and Stockman’s Avenue.My Department often works in partnership with local councils and Department for Communities on environmental improvement initiatives such as this and can confirm that we are not aware of any plans at present in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the full powers within her Department's remit to reduce or limit on-street parking.
answer_text: The Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997 provides my Department with the powers to prohibit or restrict on-street parking in the North. The Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 also provides powers to issue Penalty Charge Notices for any contraventions in relation to on-street parking listed within the Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact that medical misogyny has on healthcare waiting times for women.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the safety inspections her officials carried out in relation to Our Lady Queen of Peace chapel in Dunmurry, Belfast: and (ii) whether a pedestrian road crossing is required.
answer_text: I have been advised that my officials are not aware of a particular issue at this location. A review the collision history data provided by the PSNI has been carried out and this has not highlighted any safety concerns.I have asked my officials to undertake a pedestrian crossing assessment which will be completed in the coming weeks in line with the Department policy to determine the need and priority of any crossing facility at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out an assessment on whether HGV vehicles can travel through Dunmurry Lane, Belfast.
answer_text: There is currently no restriction on the type of vehicle which is permitted to use Dunmurry Lane. My Department normally only considers restricting HGV movements where there is evidence of abnormal damage to the structure of a road or there is a weak culvert or bridge. As officials are not aware of any underlying structural problems at this location there are no plans to restrict vehicle movements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road maintenance and repairs issues, pertaining to West Belfast, that have been waiting over five years to be completed.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2023-24 publication can be found at Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics 2023-24 | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department's Active Travel Section or an appropriate section has carried out an assessment of how safe it is to travel by (i) foot; and (ii) wheels, on Upper Dunmurry Lane, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that when requests are received to consider the enhancement of pedestrian measures, officials complete assessments in line with Departmental policy. This can include assessments for the introduction of pedestrian crossings.I can confirm my Department recently installed a new pedestrian crossing on Upper Dunmurry Lane within the vicinity of Cherry Road to improve the safety of pedestrians. The crossing went live on 30 May 2025.In addition, officials have advised that Upper Dunmurry Lane has also been assessed for a pedestrian crossing at Kilwee Lane on 14 June 2023 and it is currently ranked 58th on the Departments prioritised list of potential sites for a pedestrian crossing.Dedicated Active Travel investment in the Belfast area is primarily focussed on the delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (BCNDP). Work is currently progressing on many of the schemes on the short-term list. A section of Upper Dunmurry Lane near the railway station will be considered in as part of longer-term plans on the BCNDP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) how the traffic volume is being monitored at Dunmurry Lane, Belfast; and (ii) what steps her Department are taking to reduce traffic volumes.
answer_text: As Dunmurry Lane is a public road, it is maintained and inspected in line with my Department’s policy.Traffic volumes are not monitored on a routine basis, rather surveys are commissioned to help in the decision making process when considering potential schemes in a particular area.The Department does monitor the highway network on an annual basis with regards to road traffic collisions for the consideration of improvement schemes. A review of the most recent data available from the PSNI does not highlight any particular areas of concern on Dunmurry Lane.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what is the average time from when an issue in West Belfast is reported online to her Department until the job is completed, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain statistics in the format requested.Following reports from the public or public representative of potholes it may be necessary for my officials to carry out a site investigation to enable a decision on whether the pothole meets our intervention criteria and the creation of an instructed surface defect record.  My Department receives a high volume of surface defect related enquiries, and regrettably it is not possible to investigate all enquiries immediately. It is likely that some faults will not be investigated until the next routine inspection of the area.If, after investigation, it is found that the reported pothole requires action, our current standards specify a range of response times for the repair of defects, depending on factors such as their severity and the volume of traffic on the road. They range from one calendar day for the most serious defects, to periods of five working days and four weeks for less serious defects. Surface defects data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect category, geographical area, and status.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of her Department's spend on (i) road maintenance; and (ii) road safety measures in West Belfast, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information in the format required, however I can provide you with spend since the inception of Department for Infrastructure by Roads Eastern Division, of which West Belfast falls within. Structural Maintenance Expenditure for Eastern DivisionFinancial YearResource £MCapital £M2016-171.79.22017-182.06.72018-192.213.32019-201.912.92020-212.610.42021-222.713.92022-232.814.92023-243.714.12024-255.619.8Local Transport and Safety Measures Expenditure for Eastern DivisionFinancial Year£M2016-173.42017-183.52018-194.42019-204.82020-215.32021-225.62022-233.42023-244.92024-256.1The Department’s wider road safety responsibility also includes expenditure advertising awareness campaigns, which address the main road safety causations factors impacting road traffic collisions here.  The main causation factors are careless / inattentive driving, access speed for the road or the conditions, drink or drug driving. The campaign advertising also addresses road safety issues impacting vulnerable road users including children, pedestrians, cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclist. Seat belt wearing is also promoted as these are lifesaving if involved in a collision. All communities across the north, including West Belfast, will have benefited from these campaigns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure under what conditions will her Department install wide-angle mirrors to help with visibility of cars emerging from side streets onto main streets.
answer_text: The Department does not currently have the legal authority to provide traffic mirrors or approve their provision by others and at present there are no plans to introduce the required regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the scheduled road repair works for Summerhill Road, West Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department holds data on the number of traffic collisions occurring in particular areas; and (ii) to detail the steps that elected representatives should take to gain access this data.
answer_text: Data in relation to the number of traffic collisions occurring at particular sites is owned and held by the PSNI.  The PSNI share this information with officials in my department for specific uses, including the prioritisation of sites for safety interventions.As the data is not in the ownership of my department, considered to be sensitive information and shared with my officials for specific purposes, I cannot provide this information elected representatives.  I would suggest that the Member contacts the PSNI to discuss access to this data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department is aware of the restricted road signage on the Creighton Road, Dunmurry; and (ii) to detail when the issue will be repaired.
answer_text: My officials are not aware of restricted road signage on Creighton Road, Dunmurry. However, following site inspections of Creighton Road and the adjoining footways and verges it has been noted that some sections would benefit from having foliage cut back. These works have been instructed and are programmed to be completed on Sunday 5 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered instigating the creation of a national trauma plan, informed by survivors, trauma experts, mental health practitioners, and educators to help address post-traumatic stress disorder.
answer_text: There is significant awareness of the impact of trauma across society, and it is included in the strategic direction of my own and other Government Departments.  It is woven through a number of existing strategy and policy documents, including:Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, Substance Use Strategy 2021 – 2031.Regional policy on the use of Restrictive Practices in Health and Social Care Settings 2023.Autism Strategy 2023-28 To Respect, To Listen, To Involve.Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework 2023.and the approach is also included in those policy initiatives currently out to consultation:Children and Young Peoples Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. We Matter, Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland.A trauma informed approach is most effective when integrated into everything we do, which is why it is embedded in these strategies and policies. When individuals are referred with a potential diagnosis of Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), there are a range of existing services which can provide assessment, formulation and intervention as appropriate to the presentation. This includes Community Mental Health Teams, Psychological Therapy Services and the Regional Trauma Network Team in each Trust.The Regional Trauma Network, overseen jointly by my Department and The Executive Office, adopts an innovative approach working collaboratively with statutory, community and voluntary sector organisations to provide specialist trauma care to individuals.   Phase 1 focuses on victims and survivors of the Troubles and formally commenced on 1 April 2023 for a period of at least 3 years.  Phase 1 is subject to evaluation to consider any unmet needs or ongoing needs, with lessons learned from the evaluation informing the roll out of Phase 2. The Phase 1 evaluation is scheduled to commence in April 2026.Community and Voluntary agencies also provide a wide range of interventions and support for individuals who experience trauma.There is currently limited data on the prevalence of PTSD in Northern Ireland. However, I am mindful that there may be unmet need in this area with people not presenting to services or being within services but not yet having received a diagnosis of PTSD.   I remain committed to the continued integration of a trauma informed approach throughout my Department’s work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the definition of active travel spend has changed since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 received Royal Assent.
answer_text: Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 states that my department must develop sectoral plans for transport which set a minimum spend on active travel from the overall transport budgets of 10%.My department has not changed what it considers to be active travel, which is travelling by physically active means such as walking, wheeling, or cycling. What has changed since the introduction of the Climate Change Act is that, for the first time, there is a need to capture and report all active travel expenditure funded from overall transport budgets across my department in line with Section 22 of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what work her Department is undertaking to reduce vehicles parking on the footpath on Upper Dunmurry Lane, Belfast.
answer_text: Upper Dunmurry Lane is part of my Department’s regular parking enforcement patrol within the Dunmurry area and Traffic Attendants take enforcement action against any vehicle parked in contravention of specified decriminalised parking restrictions or prohibitions. My Department cannot take enforcement action against parked vehicles causing an obstruction where there are no parking restrictions marked on the carriageway; this is a matter for the PSNI.My Department has sought the views of the public on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and a Findings Report was published on 6 December 2023. My officials have developed potential next steps for my consideration, and I plan to issue a formal response once I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her officials carried out a safety assessment at Grange Park, Dunmurry, to ascertain whether a salt box is required.
answer_text: I am advised that a safety assessment for a salt box at Grange Park, Dunmurry has not previously been undertaken. I have asked my officials to undertake an assessment at this location in the coming days and to issue a response with regard to the outcome directly to your constituency office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how often her Department is legally committed to investigate that road safety and speed sings are visible and maintained.
answer_text: The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a legal duty on my Department to maintain all public roads in reasonable condition, having regard to the character of the road and traffic expected to use it.My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for over a decade. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated additional funding to deliver an enhanced programme of road marking renewal and sign maintenanceDefective, damaged or missing signs, as well as those needing to be cleaned, are categorised as urgent or non-urgent work and budget allocated for their replacement/repair. Priority is given to mandatory and warning signs for safety purposes with the. cleaning of other signs carried out as permitted by availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he intends to implement new legislation in relation to Education and Training Inspectorate inspections given that teaching unions are against his proposals.
answer_text: It is not surprising that those who have engaged in non-cooperation with inspection as part of industrial action wish to retain that right. However, this is a question for wider society to consider.  Let us be clear: do we, as a society, believe that schools should be inspected?  If the answer is yes - and it must be - then ask yourself: should that inspection be voluntary?  Of course not.  No serious education system in the world leaves inspection to chance. Inspection is not a negotiable workplace duty.  We need a legal framework that guarantees consistent, independent oversight of educational quality, child protection and safeguarding.At the heart of any education system that truly promotes social mobility is a simple but powerful principle: every child deserves access to a good school.  Yet in Northern Ireland today, that principle is under threat.Due to prolonged industrial action, many schools have gone without inspection - without the external scrutiny support and challenge that are essential to driving improvement and safeguarding children. This is not a minor administrative issue. It is a systemic failure that puts standards and equity at risk.We cannot continue with the constant threat - and reality - of disruption. Disruption that is unmatched anywhere else in the UK or Ireland. Northern Ireland stands alone as an outlier. Every other jurisdiction has clear, enforceable legislation requiring cooperation with school inspection. We do not. That must change.Whilst I note the views of the Teaching Unions, I will proceed with this legislation. The rights of teachers to take industrial action must not override the rights of children to high-quality education and robust safeguarding procedures.I initially brought proposals on inspection to the Executive last November.  I am now preparing to return to the Executive with further evidence and to seek permission to introduce the inspection legislation to the Assembly. This is a critical step in restoring confidence, raising standards and protecting children.I trust both Executive Colleagues and Members will give this Bill the careful consideration it deserves and support its passage.  Together, we must close this legislative gap and protect the inspection process - and, in doing so, protect the children and young people we serve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail a breakdown of frontline staff working in Lanyon Plaza providing advice on rates in (i) 2019; and (ii) 2025.
answer_text: To support the collection of over £1.6billion in rates revenue each year, rates administration is delivered by operational staff within 2 Divisions of Land & Property Services (LPS), Revenue & Benefits (R&B) Division and Valuation Division.  LPS implemented the NICS Hybrid Working Policy in 2022, whereby staff may be working at home, in Lanyon Plaza or in another NICS building at a given point in time. All staff have an assigned office location, and the assigned office location has been used to provide a response to this question.I would highlight that staffing levels change over time, linked to changes in the operational delivery environment. For example, staffing levels in 2019 include LPS staff involved in the administration of Housing Benefit for owner occupiers, however this function moved to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) in 2022.  Similarly, office locations have changed over time. In 2019, LPS Belfast based staff worked from both Lanyon Plaza and Queens Court, however in 2025 all Belfast based staff work from Lanyon Plaza.  The table below provides a breakdown of Belfast based LPS staff involved in rates administration in (i) 2019 and (ii) 2025. Division - Location (i) 2019(ii) 2025R&B - Queen’s Court 1010R&B - Lanyon Plaza217270Valuation - Queens Court00Valuation - Lanyon Plaza2826TOTAL 346296

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how many times and (ii) on what dates Ministers put a paper regarding funding for North South bodies on the agenda for the Executive, over the last ten years.
answer_text: Since October 2024, when the Alternative Funding proposal was agreed by the then Finance Minister Caoimhe Archibald, its inclusion on the Executive’s agenda has been requested on 14 occasions. To date the paper has not been included on the Executive’s agenda for consideration.As this is a cross-cutting issue, the Alternative Funding proposal requires Executive approval. I believe it is incumbent on the Executive to consider the proposal, and I will continue to press for this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any correspondence he has had with the UK Cabinet Office to request that any companies are added to the debarment list for public procurement contracts.
answer_text: I have had no correspondence with the Cabinet Office to request that any companies are added to the debarment list for public procurement contracts. The decision to add a supplier to the debarment list can only be taken by a British Government Minister.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any plans their Department has to ringfence funding for work to prevent violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Programme was provided with a flat cash Programme Resource DEL budget of £1.441m for 2025/26. As a result of a successful bid at the start of the 2025/2026 financial year, a further £2m has been provided by DoF, specifically ringfenced for EVAWG. An additional ringfenced £2.2m has been allocated in the June monitoring round. Funding this year will be used to meet the objectives of the first Delivery Plan, which includes proposed local and regional community-based actions. The additional funding will enable TEO to deliver on a larger scale and across a broader spectrum of platforms at both local and regional levels

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how trauma is considered in policy development, ensuring decisions are made with an understanding of the long-term unaddressed trauma.
answer_text: My Department is committed to embedding trauma-informed approaches in policy development to address the pervasive and long-term impact of trauma. Trauma exposure occurs in a range of contexts, including adverse childhood experiences, bereavement, injury and sickness, all of which can have impacts on mental health. The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is the primary policy framework for mental health in Northern Ireland, which includes addressing trauma’s impact on mental health and wellbeing. The Strategy provides a robust framework to promote mental wellbeing, deliver early intervention, and ensure specialist support for those with mental health needs.However, I acknowledge that significant challenges remain, including workforce shortages, long waiting times for psychological therapies, and funding constraints. My assessment is that achieving the Mental Health Strategy’s ambitions requires ongoing investment, enhanced workforce capacity and a sustained commitment to trauma-informed policymaking to promote mental wellbeing and resilience across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the rise in pre-tax profits of the Henderson Group.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an assessment of this company's commercial position.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of recent media reports about the broadcast contract in the Dáil, whether it will commission a review of the £3.23 million contract with Pi Communications for broadcasting services.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission’s operation and maintenance contract for broadcasting and related sound and vision services was awarded to Pi Communications in May 2022, following an open procurement process. This is a fixed fee contract, which provides the structure, scope of works, timetable and fixed income for the provision of a core team retained by the contractor on a dedicated basis. No material factors have arisen since contract award in relation to the services required by the Assembly which would suggest or require that this decision needs to be revisited.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, following media reports in relation to working conditions and pay issues for Pi Communications workers in the Dáil, to detail any plans it has to (i) bring broadcasting services in-house; and (ii) directly employ broadcast workers.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission’s operation and maintenance contract for broadcasting and related sound and vision services was awarded to Pi Communications in May 2022, following an open procurement process. This is a fixed fee contract, which provides the structure, scope of works, timetable and fixed income for the provision of a core team retained by the contractor on a dedicated basis. Prior to the award of this contract, the Assembly Commission considered the business case for the operation and maintenance contract for broadcasting and related sound and vision services. The business case considered three options:1. Do nothing i.e. allow the contract to lapse.2. Bring services ‘in house’.3. Procure a new contract.Following an options appraisal, the Assembly Commission agreed that option 3 should be progressed. No material factors have arisen since contract award in relation to the services required by the Assembly which would suggest or require that this decision needs to be revisited.Prior to the present contract ending in June 2028, these options for the operation and maintenance contract for broadcasting and related sound and vision services will be considered again.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many (i) arrests; (ii) stop and searches; and (iii) other activities, were carried out involving the PSNI's Armed Response Unit vehicles, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on persons arrested or stopped and searched by police in its datasets.  The recording of information on the number of persons arrested for an offence by the police or in relation to those stopped and searched, or in relation to other activities conducted by the PSNI Armed Response Unit is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to persons arrested or stopped and searched by a police Armed Response Unit, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of recorded instances of restrictive practices in educational settings, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: DE Circular Number: 2021/13 - Interim Guidance on the Use of Restraint and Seclusion in Educational Settings provides advice that all instances of the use of restraint should be recorded. This information is held at school level.  There is currently no requirement for schools to report to the Education Authority or DE. In the last five years fewer than five incidents have been raised directly with DE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce a legislative requirement for schools to record and report all instances of the use of restraint in educational settings.
answer_text: My Department is currently taking forward work to develop statutory guidance on the use of restraint in educational settings. Work is at an advanced stage, and subject to my approval, it is hoped that statutory guidance will be in place in the 2025/26 school year.It is my intention that the statutory guidance will issue under Article 18(1)(c) of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 which relates to the welfare and protection of pupils. Article 18(1)(c) places a duty on the Board of Governors of a grant-aided school to have regard to this guidance when determining the measures to be taken at the school (by the Board of Governors, the staff of the school or other persons) to protect pupils from physical or mental abuse, whether at school or elsewhere and includes Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) provision. The statutory guidance will provide for the recording and reporting of the use of restraint in educational settings.I have no plans to introduce a separate legislative requirement for schools to record and report all instances of the use of restraint in educational settings

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail a breakdown of the amount spent on the prevention of violence against women and girls, in each of the previous five years.
answer_text: A breakdown of total TEO expenditure from December 2021 to March 2025 is shown below:December 2021 – March 2022 		£191,790April 2022 – March 2023 		£1,563,497April 2023 – March 2024			£1,644,549April 2024 – Mar 2025*			£3,255,863*Figures to end of March 2025 are provisional as not all final billing has been received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department monitors the (i) scale; and (ii) nature of the use of restraint in educational settings, including instances that are not reported and recorded.
answer_text: DE Circular Number: 2021/13 - Interim Guidance on the Use of Restraint and Seclusion in Educational Settings provides advice that all instances of the use of restraint should be recorded. This information is held at school level.  There is currently no requirement for schools to report to the Education Authority or DE. My Department is currently taking forward work to develop statutory guidance on the use of restraint and seclusion in educational settings. Work is at an advanced stage, and subject to my approval, it is hoped that statutory guidance will be in place in the 2025/26 school year.Additional monitoring arrangements are likely to be put in place in the new statutory guidance. The intention is that Boards of Governors should conduct a periodic review of all incidents to ensure overarching monitoring is conducted, and records should be made available on request to the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI), the EA, and the Department in line with all relevant data protection legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their Department’s current work on preventing violence against women and girls.
answer_text: There has been significant progress on the actions contained in the first Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan since it was launched last September.The Local and Regional EVAWG Change Funds represent a £3.2 million investment for community and voluntary sector organisations in partnership with local councils, and we have been delighted to see the impact of the funding at grassroots community level.As a result of Momentum funding provided at the start of this year, Councils are embedding action to end violence against women and girls into their service delivery, and developing signposting and resources for local communities. The Local Change Fund will support community networks and provide opportunities for grassroots organisations to play their part in preventing violence, harm and abuse against women and girls.We have funded eight community and voluntary sector expert organisations through the Regional Change Fund who are now delivering activity that will strengthen the impact and reach of EVAWG expertise and programmes which support schools, communities and workplaces.                         The Power-to-Change campaign launched in January 2025, in partnership with the Department for Justice and the PSNI, aims to challenge unacceptable attitudes and reduce harmful behaviours of men and boys.  The campaign uses the Active Bystander approach to challenge them to step away from negative attitudes, challenge their mates; and have the confidence to step in safely to put a stop to behaviour that might escalate and put their friends at risk later of offending. It was publicised across a wide range of channels including social media campaigns.We are currently working on a whole of society campaign which will raise awareness and increase understanding of what coercive control is and will provide training and the tools to take action to prevent it.We have established a number of key-sectoral working groups which are identifying key actions to improve outcomes for women and girls in educational, workplace, social and nighttime settings. They are working towards developing actions plans for those settings.An Insight and Reference Network has been established to continue the collaborative focus of the co-design process which shaped the Strategic Framework. This is intended to give strong connection to and support collaboration with the Community and Voluntary sector, and other partners. It will promote the work and impact of these organisations, share learning, forge new connections, and provide a mechanism to inject service provision expertise and lived experience into delivery of the programme.A key objective of the Strategic Framework is the development of a whole of government approach to Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.  Work is progressing across departments and their agencies in the development of a coordinated approach towards ending violence against women and girls.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of parents who availed of Infant Feeding Leads in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is not collected consistently across the Health and Social Care Trusts and is therefore unavailable.
