question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether any research has been carried out into why the prison population increased by 11.4 per cent in 2023-24; and (ii) whether there has been any strategy to reduce the prison population.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison keeps under close review all changes to the prison population.  The most significant factor driving an increased prison population in Northern Ireland is a sustained high level of remand prisoners: however, similar increases have been noted right across these islands, even where remand levels are somewhat lower.  Whilst no specific research has been undertaken to identify the reasons for this trend, there is a significant existing body of research identifying the societal and personal drivers for offending behaviour.The Working Group on Remand, chaired by the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service and with membership drawn from across justice, is continuing to explore viable alternatives to remand to offer the Judiciary. One option it has identified and commissioned is the development of a Bail Support pilot scheme for women, initially, with a view to scaling to the full adult cohort, subject to efficacy and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether there would be any conflict of interest on the Adult Protection Board if all members are employed by his Department or the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The clauses in the Adult Protection Bill relating to the Adult Protection Board (Part 2, clauses 30 to 37) are based on the legislation which established the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) and provide for the appointment and functions of the Adult Protection Board. Clause 30 provides details on membership of the Board.  Statutory members would be employed by their respective organisations. Other members of the Board will be appointed by the Department; the processes in relation to this refer to the public appointments process, which is standard for appointing the members of an arm’s length body.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the cost for (i) training; and (ii) hiring, the 68 new prison officers.
answer_text: Training costs for the sixty-eight new Officers to the Northern Ireland Prison Service total £110,783.83. This includes additional equipment and administrative costs associated with this large intake. The cost of recruitment and assessment of new Prison Officers in 2025 is £201,342.72.  This recruitment continues, and will provide further appointments throughout 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of the schools that receive (i) education; or (ii) health advice, on menstruation issues.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor The Education Authority holds a list of schools receiving education or health advice on menstruation issues.  Menstrual education is taught through the curriculum, using resources from CCEA, including the Period Dignity Hub. These materials are available to all schools and help promote menstrual understanding and reduce stigma.  Some schools also invite health professionals to give extra support however, this information is not collected centrally and my Department does not provide funding in respect of any such support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) actual; and (ii) estimated costs, associated with the forensic investigation into NI Water, carried out by PricewaterhouseCoopers.
answer_text: The cost of the forensic investigation is withheld as disclosure would be likely to prejudice the commercial interests of the external provider carrying out the investigation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what work is being carried out to (i) regulate or (ii) monitor, large private businesses purchasing private homes to further their own expansion plans.
answer_text: None. It is not the duty of my department to regulate or monitor business in this manner,

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether Capita will be eligible to bid for any contract to administer redress payment schemes, as set out in the Inquiry Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses and Redress Scheme Bill.
answer_text: The Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill establishes a Redress Service which will administer the Standardised Payment (SP) Scheme. This will be an independent body, and as such they will be responsible for the procurement of any contracts.  TEO will be responsible for funding the Redress Service and will retain responsibility for the overall policy.The Standardised Payment (SP) Scheme is based on admittance to an institution only, and does not require assessment of any medical condition, therefore the types of services currently on other redress schemes in that regard would not be necessary for this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the potential of using empty, government owned buildings to accommodate artists being displaced from Vault Artist Studios & Gallery, Marlborough House.
answer_text: The management of empty government owned buildings is a matter for the Department of Finance which is responsible for the management and strategic planning of the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) Office Estate, details of which can be found in the NICS Office Estate Strategy.  I am aware that Belfast City Council is undertaking work on Creative Workspace Provision in Belfast/Greater Belfast.  This work is ongoing with the Arts Council engaged as a key partner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department will consider leasing former offices at Clarence Court to Vault Artist Studios and other arts organisations, who will soon lose access to Marlborough House.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance is in the process of selling Clarence Court on the open market. An external Estate Agent is managing the sale and a number of prospective purchasers have viewed the building and are undertaking due diligence. The Department plans to dispose of this asset at pace to make capital funding available for investment in public services elsewhere. As the disposal is advertised on the basis of vacant possession, the Department is not considering leasing options at this time. This will be kept under review should the sale not progress as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a list of all vacant or unoccupied buildings owned by Executive departments.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has a specific and statutory role in relation to the collection of information as it relates to the assessment and collection of rates.I refer to the response to AQW 20861/22-27 which set out the limited circumstances in which a vacant property is identified for the purposes of rates administration. LPS will update the occupancy status of a property within rating records where this has been reported in the context of a change in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates. These activities are carried out in response to a request from a ratepayer as part of general rates administration activities. A list of all properties listed as vacant within rating records as at 30 June 2025, where the ratepayer is one of the Executive Departments or Properties Division, has been provided in Annex 1.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of buildings owned by the Northern Ireland Civil Service that are currently not in use, in Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible for the strategic management of the NICS Office Estate. Below are details on the office buildings owned by DoF that are currently not in use, in Belfast City Centre.BuildingPlan for BuildingVictoria Hall, May Street, BelfastFor sale on the open market.34 College Street, BelfastFor sale on the open market.Clarence Court, Adelaide Street, BelfastFor sale on the open market.Fermanagh House, Ormeau Avenue, BelfastFor sale – Discussions are ongoing with a public sector prospective purchaser.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what work his Department has carried out to tackle homelessness amongst children and young people.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has developed a Youth Homelessness Action Plan which contains 18 actions targeted at young people: Who have been part of the care system prior to reaching 16 years of age and become homeless at a later point. Aged 16 or 17, not known to Social Services, or without care experience, who present as homeless. Aged 18 - 25, who present to the Housing Executive as homeless.The Housing Executive is also working collaboratively with statutory sector partners to develop a pilot programme, called ‘New Foundations’, that will support young people leaving care and help to prevent this group from the risk of homelessness. Other projects being supported by the Housing Executive include, a Young Person Person’s Peer Support Project delivered by the Northern Ireland Youth Forum, Housing First 4 Youth delivered by Simon Community, and the Shared Tenancies Project delivered by MACS.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the operational (i) budget; and (ii) spend, on contractors by NI Water, over the last 10 years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. The breakdown per year, for the last 10 years is as follows:Operational Budget £mOperational Spend £m2015-1621.7622.732016-1723.7924.352017-1823.6724.512018-1924.4725.942019-2025.1327.362020-2127.3332.602021-2233.3232.342022-2332.0233.122023-2435.1135.472024-2542.0841.02For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all correspondence between Belfast City Council and the Executive Office in relation to the motion passed at Belfast council about Belfast International Airport's use by United States airplanes, including how many times council officials have been in touch.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the health impacts of homelessness on children and young people.
answer_text: Children and young people experiencing homelessness can often have complex and intersecting health and social care needs, across a range of areas, that may be contributing factors to becoming homeless as well as the consequences of homelessness. The link between good housing and health is recognised across government and access to suitable homes will have a positive impact on those who are homeless, as well as reducing stress on the health and social care system.My Department continues to work collaboratively with cross-Departmental officials in a number of workstreams to address the impact of homelessness, including for children and young people.Under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Children Order), Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts have a statutory duty to carry out an assessment of the needs of all eligible 16 and 17 year olds who are preparing to leave care, with a view to determining what advice, assistance and support the Trust should provide to the young person, both while they remain a looked after child and when they have left care. The outcome of this assessment must inform the preparation of the young person’s pathway plan, which sets out the actions required to meet the needs of the young person, and which is kept under regular review. The plan must make provision about the health needs of the individual, including mental health, and how they will be met, as well as the provision of accommodation which is suitable in terms of their health and any disability.  Homelessness is a complex issue, which cuts across a wide range of areas and impacts on our most vulnerable individuals as a society. My Department will continue to work collaboratively with stakeholders to further understand how to address the impact of homelessness across that cohort.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans to transfer ownership of car parking facilities on North Street, Newry, from the Department to Newry and Mourne District Council.
answer_text: The land at North Street has been earmarked for social housing and there are no plans to transfer ownership of the land. Newry Mourne & Down District Council is considering leasing the land for the provision of a temporary surface carpark, until such times as construction commences on the social housing, and this process is ongoing. The land is currently leased to the Newry Business Improvement District, who operate the land as a free to use car park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the public announcement system at Belfast Grand Central Station is owned (i) by Translink; (ii) by the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company; or (iii) privately.
answer_text: The public announcement system is owned by the NI Transport Holding Company, which operates under the trading name of Translink

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration he has given to allowing community and arts organisations to utilise empty public buildings owned by the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible for the strategic management of the NICS Office Estate. When DoF’s buildings are declared surplus, they first enter the public sector property disposal process managed by Land & Property Services. Details of a property are first circulated to public sector bodies and then to third sector organisations, via Development Trusts NI, giving them the opportunity to acquire a surplus public sector asset before it is listed on the open market. The DoF properties listed on the open market are being sold on the basis of vacant possession and a number of prospective purchasers have viewed the buildings and are undertaking due diligence at present. The Department intends to move these disposals at pace to allow the capital income to be made available for investment in public services elsewhere. Therefore, no mean time use has been considered. This will be kept under review should the sales not progress as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether car parking facilities on North Street, Newry, will remain free to use if ownership is transferred to Newry and Mourne District Council.
answer_text: A decision has yet to be made as to whether to lease the land at North Street to Newry and Mourne District Council, it is therefore too early to determine the operating arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in the absence of proposals to regulate property management companies, to detail any consideration he will give to establishing a first-tier tribunal similar to that in existence in England and Wales.
answer_text: ANSWERThe potential for a tribunal system, similar to the First-tier Tribunal in England and Wales, was among the matters touched upon in the recent scoping report on managing shared property. Depending on the nature of such a tribunal, this is an issue that may attract the interest of the Department of Justice and the Courts Service. I will take account of the various options available when further work is taken forward and I am considering with my officials how best that can be done in the context of current and competing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will implement extreme weather legislation to protect workers during spells of extreme weather conditions.
answer_text: The current health and safety legislation contains wide reaching protections. However, environmental and other factors can create new and complex challenges that impact on all of society. I have already made a commitment to working with Trade Unions and a range of relevant stakeholders to make improvements where possible and appropriate. This work will be progressed over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 28302/22-27, to outline how her Department’s legal responsibility to consider any application for a Mineral Prospecting Licence is compatible with her commitment to introduce a moratorium on mineral licencing.
answer_text: Legal advice has confirmed that a moratorium would not be legal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount paid to date to BAM Ireland for construction of the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: The contract for the New Children’s Hospital was awarded to John Graham Construction Limited. There has been no payment made to BAM Ireland for the construction of the New Children’s Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail which public bodies are able to investigate corporate manslaughter.
answer_text: The matter raised is not entirely within my remit but my understanding is that all work-related deaths should be reported to the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), and to the Enforcing Authority, which, depending on the circumstances, is either the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland or local council.The PSNI will first investigate to see whether a serious criminal offence has been committed.  They will also work with the relevant regulators as appropriate. In Northern Ireland, the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions is needed before a case of corporate manslaughter can be taken to court.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what protections exist in hospitality workplaces to protect workers during hot temperature conditions.
answer_text: There is no specific workplace temperature (maximum or minimum) set in legislation in the north. However, employers are legally required to manage high temperatures as a workplace hazard under the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993. These Regulations require, “During working hours, the temperature in all workplaces inside buildings shall be reasonable.”  While no maximum temperature is defined in the Regulations, an employer must ensure indoor temperatures do not create health risks or discomfort. Enforcement of these Regulations for the Hospitality sector rests with local councils. The Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland has general workplace guidance on their website relating to temperature in the workplace – Temperature and Work | Health and Safety Executive

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have been waiting for over a year from their first meeting with their consultant, for elective orthopaedic surgery in West Belfast, each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of patients waiting longer than one year for elective orthopaedic surgery in Belfast HSC Trust who were resident in the West Belfast Constituency are shown below. This includes specific initiatives to reduce the waiting times for primary hip and knee surgery.While I am pleased to see that the number of patients waiting over one year has reduced steady over the last five years it should be noted that reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17326/22-27, to outline (i) why the expected publication date of late 2024 early 2025 was missed for publication; and (ii) by what date the Housing Executive will publish the report.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As noted in AQW 17326/22-27, the Housing First research was subject to engagement with a Research Advisory Group. During this engagement, some points of clarification arose which have since been addressed. Whilst this resulted in a delay in publication, it is anticipated that the report will be published in August 2025.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he is ensuring musculoskeletal conditions are treated as a healthcare priority.
answer_text: My strategic priorities are tackling health inequalities, shifting care into the community and keeping people safe and well in their homes. Progressing these priorities ensures those with musculoskeletal conditions are prioritised also. Musculoskeletal services cut across many different services in Health and Social Care. Work that officials are progressing that will ensure musculoskeletal conditions are treated as a priority include:Expanding Multi-Disciplinary Teams in Primary Care, which are vital for prioritising musculoskeletal care and include first contact physiotherapists who support patients to self-manage and require less support from other health services: The expansion will bring Multi-Disciplinary Teams to all 11 GP Federations.Developing a Northern Ireland Rehabilitation Framework: Led by the Chief Allied Health Professions Officer, this framework will help ensure fair, patient-focused rehabilitation is available to everyone who needs it.Improving the Orthotist Service: The Chief Allied Health Professions team is working on proposals for a new service delivery model for the Orthotist service.I have also recently announced an unprecedented increase in the number of commissioned undergraduate training places for allied health professions, bringing the total number of commissioned places to 505 in 2025/2026 from 405 in 2024/2025. This will help ensure that we have the Allied Health Professions workforce within HSCNI that is key to ensuring the successful delivery of musculoskeletal services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what preventative work around musculoskeletal conditions is his Department engaged in.
answer_text: Prevention of the deterioration of musculoskeletal conditions, as with any chronic condition aligns with my strategic priorities of tackling health inequalities, shifting care into the community and keeping people safe and well in their homes. When someone develops a musculoskeletal condition therapeutic treatments and musculoskeletal rehabilitation, including prehabilitation play a key role in preventing health decline in people with these conditions. My Chief Allied Health Professions Officer is currently engaging with key stakeholders to produce a Rehabilitation Framework for Northern Ireland. A wide range of allied health professions are integral in helping to prevent deterioration in people living with musculoskeletal conditions. I have recently announced an unprecedented increase in the number of commissioned undergraduate training places for allied health professions, bringing the total number of commissioned places to 505 in 2025/2026 from 405 in 2024/2025. Multi-disciplinary primary care teams are also key to preventative work around musculoskeletal conditions. These teams are providing vital additional capacity in general practice settings, often without the need for a patient to first see a GP. In 2024/25 first contact physiotherapists in the multi-disciplinary teams provided additional capacity for 70k patients and an additional 136k consultations. The public health approach of first contact physiotherapists means people are supported to self-manage and require less support from other health services. Through recently secured £61m transformation funding, the multi-disciplinary teams programme will further expand to all 11 General Practice Federations across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it holds any records of the activities conducted by Politics Plus Ltd within Parliament Buildings, including events, courses, workshops and meetings.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission does not hold any records relating to the operational work of Politics Plus. The Assembly Commission holds a number of reports which were provided to it by Politics Plus outlining its activities in respect of Member development covering the following periods: June 2015 to June 2016, January 2018 to December 2018 and January 2019 to December 2019.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, aside from Politics Plus Ltd, whether any other charities have (i) been hosted in Parliament Buildings; and (ii) received access to public resources for the delivery of their programmes.
answer_text: From June 2002 to August 2023 the Assembly Commission supported the work of the Northern Ireland Assembly and Business Trust through the provision of administrative support. Individual Members also sponsor a wide range of events in Parliament Buildings, where many of the sponsoring organisations are charities.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the criteria that governs how a charity can access Assembly premises and resources for events and engagement with Members.
answer_text: Under the Assembly Commission’s ‘Sponsored Events in Parliament Buildings Policy’, Members can sponsor events in Parliament Buildings, including where the sponsoring organisation is a charity. The Assembly Commission also has a Charity Policy, which sets out the Commission's commitment to harness the collective efforts of the employees and the organisation itself in supporting the voluntary and community sector. This policy also reflects the will of Assembly Commission staff to demonstrate their social responsibility by supporting charitable endeavours.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any administrative resources or public infrastructure was made available to support Politics Plus Ltd objectives.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 27853/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail how it assisted and facilitated the work of Politics Plus Ltd, including and whether this included the use of Assembly property.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 27853/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any requirements for updates, evaluations or summaries from Politics Plus Ltd on the use of (i) public resources; (ii) Assembly Commission staff; and (iii) parliamentary premises.
answer_text: Politics Plus was established in 2011 to promote the strengthening of democracy in Northern Ireland for the public benefit, in particular by:promoting engagement between elected members of the Northern Ireland Assembly, representatives of political parties and wider civil society;promoting engagement between all those involved in all tiers of public service (including elected representatives, civil servants, other public officials and representative bodies);strengthening the role of women in political and public life; andengaging with young people and young adults in respect of the political process, democratic process and peace building in society.The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission has been supportive of these aims from the outset. As a result, two memoranda of understanding (MoU) were agreed between the Assembly Commission and Politics Plus in January 2015 and 2017. Both MoUs included a requirement for Politics Plus to provide the Assembly Commission with an annual report of activity and outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail a breakdown of duties or responsibilities carried out by Assembly Commission staff assigned to Politics Plus Ltd.
answer_text: Politics Plus was established in 2011 to promote the strengthening of democracy in Northern Ireland for the public benefit, in particular by:promoting engagement between elected members of the Northern Ireland Assembly, representatives of political parties and wider civil society;promoting engagement between all those involved in all tiers of public service (including elected representatives, civil servants, other public officials and representative bodies);strengthening the role of women in political and public life; andengaging with young people and young adults in respect of the political process, democratic process and peace building in society.The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission has been supportive of these aims from the outset. As a result, two memoranda of understanding (MoU) were agreed between the Assembly Commission and Politics Plus in January 2015 and 2017. The duties and responsibilities of staff assigned to, or working directly for, Politics Plus were a matter for Politics Plus.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any alternative structures to Politics Plus Ltd were considered prior to its establishment in 2010.
answer_text: Politics Plus was established in 2011 to promote the strengthening of democracy in Northern Ireland for the public benefit, in particular by:promoting engagement between elected members of the Northern Ireland Assembly, representatives of political parties and wider civil society;promoting engagement between all those involved in all tiers of public service (including elected representatives, civil servants, other public officials and representative bodies);strengthening the role of women in political and public life; andengaging with young people and young adults in respect of the political process, democratic process and peace building in society.The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission has been supportive of these aims from the outset. As set out in the response to AQW 27854/22-27, when the Northern Ireland Assembly Legislative Strengthening Trust was first incorporated in 2010, as an interim measure, the then Speaker and a number of members of the Assembly Commission agreed to act as its initial Trustees. The Assembly Commission does not hold any records on whether any alternative structures to Politics Plus Ltd were considered prior to its establishment in 2010.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it holds any records relating to the decision to establish Politics Plus Ltd in 2010, including any assessments of legal, reputational or governance risks at the time.
answer_text: Politics Plus was established in 2011 to promote the strengthening of democracy in Northern Ireland for the public benefit, in particular by:promoting engagement between elected members of the Northern Ireland Assembly, representatives of political parties and wider civil society;promoting engagement between all those involved in all tiers of public service (including elected representatives, civil servants, other public officials and representative bodies);strengthening the role of women in political and public life; andengaging with young people and young adults in respect of the political process, democratic process and peace building in society.The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission has been supportive of these aims from the outset. As set out in the response to AQW 27854/22-27, when the Northern Ireland Assembly Legislative Strengthening Trust was first incorporated in 2010, as an interim measure, the then Speaker and a number of members of the Assembly Commission agreed to act as its initial Trustees.  The Assembly Commission does not hold any records relating to the decision to establish Politics Plus Ltd in 2010.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26838/22-27, (i) what specific measures are included in the strategy Implementation Plan 2025-28 to improve cancer services for children and young people; and (ii) to detail the (a) timeline; and (b) funding allocated to paediatric, teenage and young adult cancer provisions within the strategy Implementation Plan 2025-28.
answer_text: The NI Cancer Strategy carries a range of actions that will impact on all patients whatever age.  Significant work has been taken forward relating to children and adolescent and young adult (AYA) cancer services.Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) is the designated principle treatment centre (PTC) in Northern Ireland for children from birth to 16 years diagnosed with cancer and manages a workload of approximately 60 newly diagnosed cancer patients a year.  The age range for the AYA service includes those diagnosed with cancer between their 13th and 25th birthday bridging both children’s and adult services.  As a result of my Department’s work over the period 2022-2025 on action 33 of the NI Cancer Strategy, standards have been developed and a new regional service model for Adolescents and Young adults with cancer is being implemented in 2025/2026.  £100k has been allocated to support this work.  This funding will support the further development of the Regional AYA Multi-disciplinary team in line with the regionally agreed  standards: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-04/AYA%20NI.pdfPlans are also in place to stabilise the AYA Clinical Nurse Specialist CNS workforce to ensure that all children and young people, diagnosed with cancer, will have access to a CNS throughout their entire pathway of care. This includes 5 CNS 0.4 wte posts to ensure that the AYA CNS role is accessible across all Trusts including Children’s Hospital. Further to address Action 53 of the cancer strategy an Advanced Communication Skills Training programme bespoke to those who work with Children and Young people has been delivered in 2024/2025 and there are plans for 4 further courses in the financial year of 25/26.  £50k has been secured through Cancer Programme funding to support this training across Northern Ireland with additional financial support received from the Children’s Cancer Unit Charity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with Ulster University in relation to the future of the Riverside Theatre.
answer_text: The Department recognises the cultural and historical significance of the Riverside Theatre as the oldest professional theatre outside of Belfast. The decision to close the theatre while regrettable, is outside the remit of my Department.The University have not contacted me seeking engagement; however my Department maintains an ongoing dialogue with the University and I am happy to discuss any issues affecting the institution and its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to provide guidance to schools on the responsibilities of staff for intimate care practices, to ensure staffing ratios support this.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has made guidance available to all schools on Intimate Care and this can be accessed at Intimate Care Policy - 11 November 2008.  Where a child or young person has a statement of special educational needs further support, if required, can be requested through the annual review of the statement.My department’s guidance on Safeguarding & Child Protection in Schools (September 2024).pdf indicates that schools have a duty to ensure that safeguarding permeates all activities and functions, this includes a requirement for a policy in respect of intimate care.  Advice and guidance is available for schools via the EA Child protection Support Services helpline on 028 9598 5590 (Monday – Friday 9am-4.30pm) or the child’s relevant Special Educational Needs Link Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of (i) the possible closure of Riverside Theatre; and (ii) its impact on access to local art and culture.
answer_text: Much like the closure of any local venue, it is my assessment that, should it materialise, the impacts will be felt across the local area and beyond.There is work to be done to better understand the way arts funding is distributed across Northern Ireland. This will include an informed assessment of the social and economic impact of the arts. My officials are currently examining this as part of the development of a new Arts Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to improve the resources and support available to the parents of a child who is going through the process to obtain a statement of Special Education Needs, with a focus on (i) clarity; and (ii) accessibility.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that, as part of the EDiS (Education Information Solutions) project, the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) is developing a digital case management system that will allow parents to seek up to date information on the EA Connect platform.  It is anticipated that this will be available from January 2026.Children undergoing statutory assessment of Special Educational Needs (SEN) will have an allocated SEN Link Officer who will co-ordinate advice from all relevant agencies and also from parents.  For those parents whose children are in transition years and have not received an allocated placement by 30th June, a Family Support Link Officer will maintain contact with parents through telephone calls, emails, text messages and home visits, as per parents’ wishes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any guidance or support which is available to (i) parents; and (ii) schools on the introduction of a reduced timetable.
answer_text: The Education (Curriculum) (Temporary Exceptions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1990 (SR 1990 No 288) allows principals to make directions excepting pupils from the statutory curriculum and assessment requirements on a temporary basis. The Education Authority would only advocate reducing a child’s timetable if it was assessed as being in the child’s best interests, for example to assist with improving mental health and wellbeing.  There are occasions where a health professional will advocate for this, and this will be actioned in agreement with child, parent and school.  This should be for the shortest time possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans for a mechanism for a student to transfer from a mainstream school to a special school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that placements in Special Schools are primarily for pupils with complex Special Educational Needs (SEN) and/or medical needs, who require access to a range of specialist expertise, facilities and equipment on site.  These pupils will present with a primary need of severe learning difficulties and/or profound and multiple learning difficulties.The EA must be satisfied therefore that placement of a pupil in a special school is appropriate to their age, ability, aptitude and special educational needs.All statements of SEN are required to be reviewed on an annual basis, and this is the mechanism for any change of provision or placement on the Statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to train higher numbers of permanent staff in special educational needs specialisms.
answer_text: A number of actions within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda demonstrate my commitment to investing in the educational workforce, recognising that the quality of support provided to children with SEN is directly linked to the skills, confidence and wellbeing of our educational staff. The Department recognises the importance of building SEN awareness and competence from the outset of teacher and classroom assistant careers and maintaining that development throughout their professional journey. The SEN Reform Agenda commits to ensuring that frontline educational staff are consistently supported in developing their expertise therefore allowing them to adapt to evolving needs and implement best practices as these emerge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a long term strategy for meeting the needs of students with special education needs with school-based services and support.
answer_text: My department’s Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan were published on 4 February 2025 and set out an ambitious programme to transform provision for children and young people with SEN over the next five years. Public Sector Transformation funding of £27.5m has been secured to commence this programme of SEN Reform.  Nine projects will be delivered which aim to test the effectiveness of a range of early interventions including in-school support services, alternative models of support for children with a statement of SEN and professional learning for teachers and support staff.  The evidence from these ‘test and trial’ projects will inform the longer term direction of travel for SEN provision and support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to reduce the waiting time for a Statement of Special Educational Needs in situations where children have available medical records and a diagnosed condition.
answer_text: The statutory assessment process involves a detailed assessment of a child’s educational needs and the Education Authority (EA) will seek and consider advice from a range of sources.  Medical advice is only one part of the detailed advice collated.  The EA must seek the following:•	Educational advice;•	Psychological advice from an educational psychologist regularly employed by the EA or engaged by the EA in the case in question; and•	Medical advice from a registered health care professional identified as appropriate by the relevant HSC Trust.The new draft SEN Framework will reduce the overall statutory timescale for assessment through to the making of a statement (if appropriate) from 26 to 22 weeks.  In order to streamline the process and ultimately reduce the timescale for assessment, the EA is currently leading the way on digitisation of the process through the EA Connect platform which should result in a faster, more transparent and responsive assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans for funding of one-to-one support, if required, to allow children to fully access the curriculum while the Statement of Special Educational Needs process is ongoing.
answer_text: The school, in partnership with the parents and support services, remains responsible for a child’s education, including special education provision, during the process of statutory assessment.All schools are provided with a delegated budget via the Common Funding Formula (CFF).  The majority of CFF funding is on a per pupil basis, with core funding via the Age Weighted Pupil Unit.  As necessary, schools should consider funding additional support as required for pupils from their delegated budget.The pilot Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund was launched in November 2024 and provides additional resource to Department of Education funded pre-school education settings that have at least one child in their setting who is currently undergoing statutory assessment of their special educational needs.  Settings have flexibility on how they use the funding; it may be used to fund additional staff hours, therapeutic or developmental interventions or specialist equipment, resources or training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment on (i) whether a planning project, falling below the thresholds set out in Schedule 2 of the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, can have a significant effect on the environment; and (ii) whether her Department's Development Management Practice Note 9B: Environmental Impact Assessment Screening, published in 2023, adequately addresses how councils must assess sub-threshold developments that are likely to have significant effects on the environment.
answer_text: The Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 (“the EIA Regulations”) effectively transposed the requirements of the EIA Directive as amended.The focus of Development Management Practice Note (DMPN) 9B is very much on advice to support informed decision-making by relevant planning authorities in relation to the legal obligations under the EIA Regulations.  It is a matter for each determining authority to meet its statutory obligations on a case-by-case basis.Where a development proposal does not engage the statutory requirements of the EIA Regulations in respect of Schedule 2 requirements, a planning authority may still consider that a development proposal could have an impact on the environment which might be mitigated through a planning condition or design change proposed as part of the normal development management process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26260/22-27, and in light of the sentencing on Mobuoy, why no one is being held responsible for around one million tonnes of illegally dumped waste at the site.
answer_text: My Department is exploring all actions and enforcement options available in relation to pollution at this site, I am therefore unable to provide any further comment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the estimated costs of the preferred remediation option for the Mobuoy illegal landfill; and (ii) where he envisages the money will come from.
answer_text: There is no agreed preferred remediation option for the Mobuoy site and there will not be until the public consultation has been completed. Therefore, at this time my department does not have a cost estimate which has been assessed and approved through the required public finance processes, such as an approved business case.  Funding will be considered as part of those processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Royal College of Radiologists 2024 census report, for his assessment of the 14 per cent shortfall of clinical oncologists in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will meet with Rostrevor Action Respecting the Environment to discuss issues pertaining to (i) site boundary breaches; and (ii) waste issues regarding Narrow Water.
answer_text: I and my officials are happy to consider any requests for meetings on matters that fall within the responsibility of my Department and I am happy to receive further details from the Member on these particular issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what evidence is used to determine that an applicant for a post within the Northern Ireland Civil Service used notes or other materials in their video interview.
answer_text: Decisions were based on panel observations of the candidates' behaviour in the videos. In cases where the panel noted the candidate referred to notes or received proxy help, an additional NICS HR panel conducted a further review of each of the candidate's video recordings to verify the decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of individuals that applied for Administrative Officer posts within the Northern Ireland Civil Service that have been informed their application was rejected because they were using notes or other material in the interview process, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of people applying for Administrative Officer posts in NICS that have been informed their application was rejected because they were using notes or other material in the interview process, in each of the last five years is shown below:Year					No. of candidatesJun 2020 – Jun 2021			0Jun 2021 – Jun 2022			0Jun 2022 – Jun 2023			0Jun 2023 – Jun 2024			135Jun 2024 – Jun 2025 			200

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount (i) his Department; and (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts took from pharmacies as part of the claw back scheme, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how much was paid to pharmacies; and (ii) how many pharmacies availed of payment for supplying medication to care homes, in the last financial year.
answer_text: The Department of Health reimburses community pharmacies for medication supplied on all prescriptions presented within their pharmacy irrespective of where the prescription originates. There are no separate payments for prescriptions that originate from a care home.The Community Pharmacy Care Home Support Service (CPCHSC) is an additional service which the Department of Health commissions through community pharmacies and which aims to utilise the skills and expertise of community pharmacists and their teams as the experts in medicines to contribute to the optimisation of medicines use in care homes and supporting the reduction of waste.In 24/25, over 400 care homes received the Community Pharmacy Care Home Support Service across Northern Ireland and the Department of Health paid community pharmacies £666,000 to deliver this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what plans he has to ensure the current shortfall of clinical oncologists does not increase to 22 per cent, or 11 consultants, by 2029, as forecast by the Royal College of Radiologists.
answer_text: My department is aware of the recent forecast by the Royal College of Radiologists stating that the current shortfall of clinical oncologists will increase to 22 per cent, or 11 consultants, by 2029. Since 2019/20, a key priority of my Department is to address ongoing resilience issues with regard to oncology and haemato-oncology and services stabilisation and the delivery of Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT).  By 2024/25 my Department invested a total of £10.8m across all five health and social care Trusts for the Oncology & Haematology Stabilisation Plans.   This has funded 167.76 whole time equivalent (WTE) additional staff across a range of health professionals in oncology services including 9.75 WTE oncology consultant posts. These will improve consultant capacity and supportive skills mix.  An additional £2.5m recurrent funding has been identified in the 2025/26 Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan to help complete stabilisation plans.My department is also conducting a review into radiotherapy services which will include a review of current workforce and future needs.There are no current plans to increase the local clinical oncology, medical specialty training programme in Northern Ireland, as several training positions remain unfilled.  I acknowledged it is important we fund positions in our local service to retain our oncology consultants.   My department continues to work with all health and social care trusts to inform regional workforce planning requirements including Registrar training places and associated allocation.  Trusts continue to do all they can to fill posts with ongoing recruitment strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of funding provided for baby loss and stillbirth in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not budget for services at the level requested. Therefore, as we do not have disaggregated information available specifically for baby loss and stillbirth services, we have provided total planned expenditure for the Maternity and Child Health Programme of Care (POC). Please note this will include services and supports wider than this specific request. The table below provides the total planned expenditure in HSC NI, for the relevant Programme of Care (Maternity & Child Health), with further detail on the most relevant services  included therein: 2020/21£’m2021/22£’m2022/23£’m2023/24£’m2024/25£’mProgramme of Care 2: Maternity & Child Health181.5188.9205.3221.0234.0Inclusive of Obstetric CareHospital Obstetric Care105.1110.2119.7132.3143.2Community Midwifery14.515.115.116.817.9Notes:Figures are sourced from the Strategic Resources Framework (SRF) report.It should be noted that these figures represent planned expenditure. They are completed at the beginning of the financial year based on the initial funding allocation and will not include in-year or non-recurrent allocations.  Figures represent planned expenditure of SPPG and PHA funding.The PHA funding assumed for 2022/23 is an estimate based on the prior year.As services are not always organised within HSC Trusts on a Programme of Care basis there is always an element of apportionment and mapping of costs to allow reporting at Programme of Care level. This means that some services may be recorded in different programmes of care in Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to remove barriers which prevent women from cycling.
answer_text: I am committed to the delivery of an active travel network that is as inclusive as possible, so that everyone, not just women, can feel confident choosing active travel for many of their shorter everyday journeys.Travel attitude surveys consistently report that feelings of safety and lack of infrastructure are some of the main barriers which prevent women from cycling. My department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan for the North to complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways providing a firm basis for prioritising delivery of connected active travel infrastructure within our towns and cities into the future.My department has also updated its design guidance for active travel to ensure that the infrastructure we deliver is high-quality, appropriate to the situation, and has safety at its heart.  The provision of such infrastructure can help create places that are welcoming and accessible to all.I recognise that street lighting it is an important piece of active travel infrastructure that contributes significantly to feelings of personal security, particularly for women and girls, and my department will consider the merit of providing lighting on key active travel routes on a scheme-by-scheme basis as part of the design process. My department also delivers road safety and promotion programmes such as the Cycling Proficiency Scheme and the Active School Travel Programme which play an important role in encouraging young people to walk, wheel and cycle by providing them with the necessary skills and confidence to feel safe when making active travel choices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the total number of surface defects and potholes in West Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years. In response, my Department has had to reduce its road maintenance activities to a limited service that prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest-priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.Recognising the vital need for investment in our road infrastructure, £89 million of capital funding was allocated to structural maintenance in the 2024/25 financial year.   In addition, £38 million was allocated to resource funding in support of the delivery of a limited service which prioritises the work of greatest road safety risk for the benefit of all citizens including those in the West Belfast area.My Department continues to repair the highest priority defects across the road network however, I am well aware of the frustrations and, at times, inconvenience that poor road conditions have caused.My Department also carries out a full programme of road inspections on a cyclical basis throughout the West Belfast area, recording defects that meet current intervention levels for appropriate repair. From the 1 January 2025 some 1,859 surface defects, including potholes, have been recorded for repair in the West Belfast area which equates to around 80% of those picked up in total in the previous year.To date 1,172 of the 1,859 defects have been completed, with my external contractor working through the remainder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of the impact of deprivation and poverty on baby loss and stillbirth.
answer_text: Research consistently shows that poverty and deprivation are significant risk factors for stillbirth and pregnancy loss. Women living in deprived areas are more likely to experience poor nutrition, chronic stress, inadequate housing, and limited access to quality healthcare—all of which contribute to adverse pregnancy outcomes. Socioeconomic inequality has been attributed to nearly a quarter of stillbirths in England, with reductions in stillbirth rates being more pronounced in the least deprived areas.I would like to highlight some of the targeted initiatives currently being implemented in Northern Ireland to reduce stillbirth and pregnancy loss, particularly among women living in areas of social deprivation.Implementation of Continuity of Midwifery Carer (CoMC)This model ensures that women receive care from the same midwife or small team throughout pregnancy, birth, and the postnatal period. Evidence shows that CoMC reduces the risk of stillbirth, preterm birth, and other complications by fostering trust, improving communication, and enabling early identification of risks—particularly beneficial for women facing social disadvantage.Family Nurse Partnership (FNP)FNP is a structured, evidence-based home visiting programme for first-time young mothers. It provides intensive support from early pregnancy until the child is two years old, focusing on improving maternal health, child development, and economic self-sufficiency. The programme is particularly impactful in deprived communities where young mothers may lack support networks.Social Complexity MidwivesThese specialist midwives work with women experiencing multiple vulnerabilities, including domestic abuse, substance misuse, homelessness, or mental health challenges. They provide tailored care plans and coordinate with social services to ensure holistic support throughout the pregnancy journey.Perinatal Mental Health (PNMH) TeamsRecently launched across Northern Ireland, these multidisciplinary teams offer specialist mental health care to women during pregnancy and the first postnatal year. Mental health challenges are more prevalent in deprived areas and are closely linked to poor pregnancy outcomes. PNMH teams help address this critical gap in care.Smoking Cessation MidwivesThese midwives provide dedicated support to help pregnant women quit smoking—a major risk factor for stillbirth, particularly prevalent in deprived communities.Infant Feeding LeadsWorking under the extended Breastfeeding Strategy for Northern Ireland, these leads promote and support breastfeeding, which is linked to improved infant health outcomes. The strategy specifically targets lower-income groups, where breastfeeding rates are lowest.Healthy Start SchemeThis UK-wide programme provides eligible low-income families with vouchers for healthy food and vitamins, helping ensure women are nutritionally prepared for pregnancy and babies get the best start in life.Weigh to a Healthy PregnancyThis initiative supports women wit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to address recruitment, training and retention of the diagnostic and cancer workforce.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the challenges faced by the diagnostic and cancer workforce and is working to address this.Significant investment has been made to address recruitment, training and retention of the diagnostic and cancer workforce.  This has included  expansion of the the future diagnostic and imaging workforce in Northern Ireland over the last 10 years, with commissioned radiology medical speciality training places being expanded from 37 (2015/16) to 56 (2025/26).My Department is also currently developing the business case for a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy with the aim of significantly increasing further the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists (growing capacity from 56 to 100 training places) and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing. Progress is subject to business case approval and the availability of recurrent funding. Over the last four years my Department has provided an additional £10.9m for Health & Social Care Trusts to stabilise oncology & haematology services. This investment has led to an additional 167.6 wte posts regionally across a range of disciplines including consultants, nurses, clinical technologists, clinical scientists and pharmacists and administrators. Trusts maintain continuous recruitment drives to fill vacancies, including international recruitment when feasible. However, vacancies persist and recruitment and retention in oncology is an issue. The longer-term solution to these issues will require additional long-term investment in service transformation and new ways of working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of bereavement nurses in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is variation across Trusts in relation to the number of bereavement nurses (some may also be Bereavement Services Coordinators).  The table below outlines the provision in each:TrustBereavement Services Coordinator WTEBereavement NurseWTEBereavement MidwifeWTEFamily Liaison NurseWTEBelfast HSC Trust12 1South Eastern HSC Trust12Northern HSC Trust11Southern HSC Trust11Western HSC Trust 11 (currently funded until end of September 2025)0.72The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.You may also be aware that the Department of Finance is introducing ‘The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill’ in the Assembly which includes provision for the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland.  This initiative, currently under public consultation, would enable a pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  Departmental officials will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance, around the consultation process and development of the scheme. The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, oppor

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for its assessment of the terms and conditions provided by Pi Communications to their staff providing services to the Assembly Commission.
answer_text: Under the terms and conditions of the contract, staff employed for the delivery of broadcasting and related services are directly employed by Pi Communications. Therefore, terms and conditions of employment are a matter solely for Pi Communications.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 27236/22-27, to detail the amount it has paid to Pi Communications in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: In each of the last five years the Assembly Commission has paid Pi Communications:2020-21£624,2122021-22£648,0922022-23£592,5432023-24£641,5532024-25£594,056These amounts exclude VAT.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any monitoring undertaken of deportations carried out by (i) PSNI; and (ii) Border Force.
answer_text: Immigration, including immigration enforcement, is not devolved and is a matter for the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how regularly (i) they; and (ii) officials meet Home Office representatives, in relation to immigration issues.
answer_text: Whilst immigration is an excepted matter, we do recognise the significant impacts within the devolved space and regularly engage at Ministerial level on these matters. This has included participating in the Inter-Ministerial Group for Safety, Security and Migration and other 4 nations Ministerial meetings. Topics covered have included the recently published Immigration White Paper and the implications of this locally; asylum and refugee accommodation; changes to the Afghan Resettlement Programme and local delivery challenges; and the roll out of the recent Ukraine Permission Extension Scheme.Our officials meet regularly with Home Office on matters relating to the support and integration of refugees and asylum seekers, to ensure local needs and concerns are represented. Home Office officials are also members of the multi-agency Strategic Planning Group for Race Relations and Integration, the principal strategic planning and governance group within the devolved sphere here on immigration matters. It is chaired by our Department and meets quarterly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of attempted school inspections that could not be completed due to industrial action, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) does not attempt school inspections. Inspection is a statutory requirement. Article 102 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 sets out the duties of inspectors to monitor, inspect and report on the standard of education being provided in schools and other relevant education and training establishments. Inspections were suspended in 2020 in the context of the pandemic. Subsequent to Covid, ETI engaged in a significant transformation programme to revise and develop the processes for how its statutory duties are carried out, with a key aim of ensuring that the work of ETI has a positive and sustainable impact on school and organisation improvement. The implementation of the new inspection framework from September 2024, following extensive consultation, is receiving strong endorsement from the leaders and organisations involved to date, and from ongoing engagement. During periods of Action Short of Strike (ASoS), including non-cooperation with inspection, the number of inspections carried out was substantially lower than would otherwise be the case. Where inspection operates as intended, ETI would aim to carry out approximately 3 to 4 individual school inspections per week in each academic term. Since the implementation of the new inspection model, ETI has undertaken 91 school inspections from September 2024 to June 2025. It is worth noting that during the most recent period of ASoS, only 3 notified school inspections were completed from 17 February to 4 April 2025, instead of a potential 21. I trust this demonstrates the importance of statutory inspection functions operating as intended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for launching a consultation on the criminalisation of non-cooperation with school inspections before the independent Review of Teacher Workload has been completed.
answer_text: Reducing unnecessary workload is a priority for both my Department and the Independent Teacher Workload Panel.  I am fully committed to addressing teacher workload, ensuring that teachers have the time and space to focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils within the working day. I am also committed to reforming education support services and to significantly investing in teacher professional learning.  I am bringing forward change to improve the system for the benefit of learners, teachers, school leaders and the wider community.  However, I believe we can only do this in a context where inspection operates as intended.All Action Short of Strike (ASoS) directives to union members, including earlier this year, have included refusal to engage with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) on inspection activity.  The proposed legislation is necessary to ensure that inspection is again embedded in our system and operates effectively.The new inspection framework developed by ETI has been welcomed by those who have been through an inspection since its introduction.  The new process “lowers the stakes and raises the impact”.  It is in this context of a reformed and fit for purpose inspection process that I have moved forward with the consultation on proposals to protect this statutory and critical process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26594/22-27, to detail the number of acres of land owned by the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company, including land that is (i) developed; (ii) considered to have development potential; and (iii) without development potential.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The whole operational estate will fall under developed lands. In total NITHCo estimate c.3000 acres which would include the railway corridor. In terms of development land this would comprise c.18.25 acres of potentially developable land across various sites. This includes Weavers Cross that has outline planning approval. NITHCo have c.250 acres of landlocked agricultural conacre lands which are undevelopable but will be used for sustainability purposes. As stated in my previous answer, for any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27500/22-27, whether there are plans for the Regional Obesity Management Service to expand the provision of surgery for skin removal for those who have lost weight without the use of medical injections.
answer_text: The aim of the Regional Obesity Management Service is to provide support to individuals who are struggling with obesity or being overweight. Work is currently being taken forward by my Department to develop a service specification which will set out access criteria for the service. In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, subject to funding.Body contouring procedures are commissioned in Northern Ireland for patients who meet the Effective Use of Resources Policy criteria for treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the implications of criminalising non-cooperation with inspections on the wider education sector.
answer_text: I am fully committed to introducing legislation to ensure inspection is protected during periods of industrial action, as it is in every other UK jurisdiction and the Republic of Ireland. It has not undermined lawful union activity in these jurisdictions, nor has it led to a single fine or conviction rather it has ensured that inspection can operate consistently and effectively to safeguard our children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW27706/22-27, to detail he total number (i) of staff (ii) transgender staff; and (iii) non-binary staff, in the Civil Service who have expressed concerns about the recent Supreme Court decision around transgender rights.
answer_text: Staff have contacted my officials about the Supreme Court decision and queries have also been raised by staff via the Civil Service LGBTQ+ Staff Network.  It is also possible that staff may have expressed concerns directly to line managers in their department(s). Therefore, my department does not hold the total number of queries raised by Civil Service staff. Consequently, it is not possible to provide the breakdown requested.In considering the implications of the Supreme Court judgment on transgender rights and the legal framework here, it is important that this is approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) any strategies his Department will implement in 2025 to increase the number of new social home starts; and (ii) how his Department will move away from relying on in-year monitoring allocations to provide more certainty for social housebuilding funding.
answer_text: I brought forward the first Executive Housing Supply Strategy.  Work is now well underway on the first Strategy action plan and I was successful in securing housing as a priority in the Programme for Government.  My Department continues to work with NIHE to bring forward measures to maximise the Social Housing Development Programme.  One of the critical issues is the provision of suitable land in areas of housing need and my Department is working to identify public lands held by other departments that can potentially be used for the development of social housing.  We are also looking at ways in which we can make delivery timelier and bring more surety to the programme. My officials and I have also engaged with the housing sector and key stakeholders to explore how current issues and pressures associated with the existing new build social housing model might be addressed to maximise delivery in areas of acute housing need, particularly in the current challenging budget environment.I have always said that Multi Year Budgets are needed and I welcome this approach with the Spending Review 25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his statement to the Assembly that he will be bringing proposals to the Executive on how we can do more with the money we have to build more homes, to provide more detail on these proposals.
answer_text: Work underway on the first Housing Supply Strategy action plan will include consideration of potential alternative and new forms of finance to support supply across the housing system. This will be an Executive action plan with details to be delivered in the coming months.I am not only focussed on identifying alternative financial options, but making better use of all available assets. I therefore also intend to bring a paper to the Executive regarding how we can better use public land to support the supply of affordable housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department’s review of the findings of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 22857/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of hospitality venues that have (i) registered for Ask for Angela training; and (ii) received Ask for Angela training.
answer_text: Ask for Angela is a joint venture between the PSNI and Hospitality Ulster and as such my Department does not hold this information. A list of local venues which have received training and registered as part of the Northern Ireland Ask for Angela scheme is helpfully provided by PSNI via their website. For any further information you may wish to direct your query towards the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the £100 million discrepancy, specifically allocated for social housing, between his recently published capital budget for 2025-26 compared with indications in the draft budget in January 2025.
answer_text: There is no £100m discrepancy in the allocation for Social Housing. My Department has directed £168.5m of its 2025-26 capital budget to the delivery of new social homes in line with the Programme for Government. This includes £100m of Reinvestment and Reform Initiative funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work his Department is carrying out to ensure an independent tribunal to resolve disputes around maintenance fees between leaseholders and tenants.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer given to AQW 28129/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any information is held as to whether the Boeing C-17A Globemaster III aircraft, which left Belfast International Airport on 26 May 2025, contained supplies going to Israel.
answer_text: My Department holds no information in relation to this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale for her Department granting approval for mineral prospecting licenses, despite over 99 per cent of consultation respondents expressing opposition.
answer_text: Under the Mineral Development Act (NI) 1969, my Department has a legal responsibility to consider any application for a Mineral Prospecting Licence.  Having met the necessary legislative requirements, the seven applications were deemed to be valid and where subsequently issued for public consultation.  Having considered all the issues raised by the respondees to the consultation, it was concluded that there were no lawful barriers to prevent my Department granting all seven Mineral Prospecting Licences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any mechanisms in place to monitor military equipment made in Belfast to be used by Israel.
answer_text: The export or monitoring of military equipment is the responsibility of the British Government under the Export Control Act 2002.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether planned reforms to the liquor licensing system will include social clauses requiring hospitality businesses to provide free and safe transport home for late-night workers.
