answer_text: No, the Department does not have the relevant vires under liquor licensing legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any education provided by schools to make pupils aware of endometriosis.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues regarding the implementation of pay increases recommended by the NHS Pay Review Body.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail: (i) the original projected cost of the Land and Property Services Revenues and Benefits Implementation Project; and (ii) its estimated cost as of today.
answer_text: ANSWERThe purpose of the Revenue and Benefit Partner Project is to enable service improvement through modernised IT capability. The original projected costs, as per the existing full business case, are £10.81m capital and £248m resource. These amounts reflect costs across the 18-year life of the business case including relevant operational staff.In late 2024, Land & Property Services (LPS) and its delivery partner agreed to a pause on project delivery allowing for a reassessment of approach to optimise early achievement of project outcomes while mitigating risk.Agreement on the revised approach is at an advanced stage and the project remains within financial tolerance levels. The financial considerations are currently subject to internal scrutiny and approval of the business case, which is still outstanding.I am unable to provide updated figures until the updated business case is approved.The modernisation project continues to align with my Department’s strategic objectives while safeguarding critical rate collection services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to set out her Department's rationale when drafting Part 7 of the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, which exempts local councils from making timely Environmental Impact Assessment determinations on their own planning applications, by omitting the requirement to comply with the provisions of Regulation (i) 8(8); (ii) 8(9); (iii) 8(10); and (iv) 8(11).
answer_text: The rationale for the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 to disapply certain procedural requirements, specifically regulations 8(8) to 8(11) for councils, was to acknowledge the unique administrative position of councils when determining their own applications. The omission of these provisions was intended to provide procedural flexibility, recognising that the formal requirements may not be applicable in such cases. I am content that the Regulations provide appropriate legislative coverage to enable councils to carry out EIA screening determinations on their own planning applications. Councils, in their role as local planning authorities, are fully aware of their statutory EIA obligations and the need to carry out these responsibilities in a competent, timely, and objective manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what plans his Department has to introduce legislation similar to the Republic of Ireland which would see management companies provide annual accounts to leaseholders.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer given to AQW 28129/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the measures being taken forward as part of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill to ensure workers can retain their tips in full.
answer_text: I intend to legislate to ensure that tips left to workers are passed on to them in full. My Bill will contain provisions that: ensure that, where tips are controlled or under significant influence of the employer, those tips will go to workers in full, aside from any deductions required by law;require employers to keep records of tips received and distributed; andrequire employers to set out their policy on dealing with tips and make this available to workers.Where an employer has not complied with the legislation, a worker will be able to seek redress via an industrial tribunal.  The measures will be supported by a statutory code of practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) remedies; and (ii) mechanisms, for the enforcement of policies protecting workers from harassment.
answer_text: Workplace policies, and legislation, protecting workers from harassment can be enforced at several stages including through the employer, via the early conciliation process managed by the Labour Relations Agency and ultimately through the Industrial and Fair Employment Tribunal.Remedies available will depend on the circumstances of each case and the approach taken to enforcement.Guidance on “Harassment and Bullying at Work – Promoting an Inclusive Workplace” has been jointly produced by the Labour Relations Agency and the Equality Commission and is available on their websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the original Land and Property Services Revenue and Benefits project aims and objectives have changed.
answer_text: ANSWERThe aims and objectives of the Revenue and Benefit Partner Project to enable service improvement through modernised IT capability have not changed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any rights that leaseholders have to request accounts from management companies.
answer_text: There are no specific rights in land law, which is the responsibility of my Department, for leaseholders to request accounts from management companies. Such companies may be subject to the requirements of company law, an area which falls outside of the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of introducing statutory caps or reasonableness tests for charges faced by leaseholders.
answer_text: I have recently received a detailed scoping paper from my officials relating to issues faced by leaseholders and the regulation of shared property management. A consideration of service charges forms part of the wider analysis carried out in that document. I am currently considering the options outlined in the paper and will explore further with officials how we can progress this work in the context of available resources and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of The UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and whether it should apply to Departments here.
answer_text: The United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) Committee published a Report in 2023 with recommendations regarding implementation of the UNCRC. Many of the recommendations align with our recently published Programme for Government priorities. The full list of recommendations expands across the remit of several departments and is for respective Ministers to consider.  The Department of Education leads implementation of the UNCRC here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why there are no street lights on Roselands estate on the Andersonstown Road, Belfast; and (ii) what work her Department is carrying out to install street lights.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the provision and maintenance of lighting on adopted public roads and footways.Roselands is a private, unadopted access road and does not meet the criteria for the provision of street lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timeframe for the publication of the responses to the Being Open Framework consultation, including the accompanying analysis.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation on the Framework on 10 December 2024.  The consultation lasted 15 weeks and closed on 28 March 2025.  A total of 270 responses were received. The Department is currently reviewing the responses to identify issues and recurring themes, and to provide analysis of the key aspects of the feedback.  A new Being Open Framework Writing Group is being established to consider the consultation responses and develop a revised Framework document.  In continuation of co-production principles, membership of the Writing Group will come from the Department, HSC Trusts, Service Users and representatives from Health Service Trade Unions.It is anticipated that the implementation of the new Regional Being Open Framework will commence in September 2025 with the new Framework being published at that time.In parallel, I can confirm that a Departmental response, including detailed analysis of the responses to the Being Open Framework consultation, will also be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects the Being Open Framework to be published.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation on the Framework on 10 December 2024.  The consultation lasted 15 weeks and closed on 28 March 2025.  A total of 270 responses were received. The Department is currently reviewing the responses to identify issues and recurring themes, and to provide analysis of the key aspects of the feedback.  A new Being Open Framework Writing Group is being established to consider the consultation responses and develop a revised Framework document.  In continuation of co-production principles, membership of the Writing Group will come from the Department, HSC Trusts, Service Users and representatives from Health Service Trade Unions.It is anticipated that the implementation of the new Regional Being Open Framework will commence in September 2025 with the new Framework being published at that time.In parallel, I can confirm that a Departmental response, including detailed analysis of the responses to the Being Open Framework consultation, will also be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26260/22-27, why the public consultation on a draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site cannot proceed until the completion of court proceedings.
answer_text: I have launched the public consultation on the draft Remediation Strategy, more details are available on DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/consultations/consultation-draft-remediation-strategy-mobuoy-site I refer you to my Oral Statement of 9 June 2025, https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/oral-ministerial-statement-public-consultation-draft-remediation-strategy-mobuoy-site
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will publish the responses to its recent Being Open Framework consultation.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation on the Framework on 10 December 2024.  The consultation lasted 15 weeks and closed on 28 March 2025.  A total of 270 responses were received. The Department is currently reviewing the responses to identify issues and recurring themes, and to provide analysis of the key aspects of the feedback.  A new Being Open Framework Writing Group is being established to consider the consultation responses and develop a revised Framework document.  In continuation of co-production principles, membership of the Writing Group will come from the Department, HSC Trusts, Service Users and representatives from Health Service Trade Unions.It is anticipated that the implementation of the new Regional Being Open Framework will commence in September 2025 with the new Framework being published at that time.In parallel, I can confirm that a Departmental response, including detailed analysis of the responses to the Being Open Framework consultation, will also be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the adequacy of £1 million of additional core funding allocated via the GP Contract for 2025-26, including how this can resolve access issues for patients.
answer_text: The GMS contract for 2025/26 will see an additional investment of £9.5m into core GP services. 	This encompasses £5m to address the issue of GP indemnity; £3.5m to mitigate the cost of the increase in employer’s National Insurance costs, and a further £1m core investment. 		As part of the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services. It is important to note that these measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels. Rather, they are aimed to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike. In many cases, these are things that practices here are already doing and the aim has been to seek to spread that good practice across the region.	Total investment in GP services this year will be more than £414m. This includes pay uplifts from last year, and the recently announced investment in MDTs, which will go to directly to support GPs in meeting the needs of their patients by expanding capacity in practices. My Department has also announced investment of £2.9m in GP Elective Care, which will benefit GPs directly while meeting the needs of patients.I have also stated my intention to see the pay recommendations from the recently published Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body report for 2025/26 implemented in full.  This will see a further £11.5m invested in General Medical Services.Given the extreme budgetary challenges facing my Department, the £9.5m funding was the best and final offer that could be made for 2025/26.I regret that it was not possible to reach agreement in relation to the General Medical Services Contract for 2025/26As I have stated, I remain open to discussion as to how best to secure the future of General Practice so that it can remain a central part of primary care services now and in the future.My Department remains committed to engaging with GPs on how General Practice can play an integral role in the delivery of primary care as part of my agenda to ‘shift left’, both in terms of where in our system care is delivered and how resources are allocated to fund delivery of that care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the safeguards in place to ensure that Fujitsu will not repeat the same errors of the Horizon IT scandal, in its delivery of the contract for a new digital land registry.
answer_text: Safeguards have been put in place within the Department , whilst the contractual details remain commercially sensitive, I am assured that a robust contract is in place. The contract is derived from a model contract developed by Cabinet Office specifically for complex IT delivery services. It contains levers to drive supplier performance and clear governance arrangements. The Department has been building capability in contract management as recommended by external audit. The Land & Property Services (LPS) project implementation team is supported by a dedicated contract management unit.LPS is also continuing to liaise with the Cabinet Office Crown Commercial team who are monitoring Fujitsu’s performance as a strategic supplier.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of transcripts of media interviews given by representatives of non-Executive parties were produced by their Department’s Media Monitoring Unit, for each of the previous 5 years.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the response to AQW 27479/22-29.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department, or any of its arms-length bodies, have requested that Fujitsu continue to bid for contracts.
answer_text: Public contracts are awarded in strict adherence to procurement policy and legislation. I have been advised that officials in the Department, and its arms-length body, have not approached Fujitsu to request they continue to tender for public contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his rational for awarding the contract for a new digital land registry to Fujitsu, despite: (i) the company having publicly promised to pause all public sector bidding in the UK; (ii) the fact that Land & Property Services was not an existing customer; and (iii) the contract not being an extension of an existing deal with Fujitsu.
answer_text: The procurement process for this IT project commenced in Autumn 2021. The initial competition notice was published in December 2021 and following a complex bidding process, the initial intent to award letters were issued in December 2023. Fujitsu issued a statement intending to pause bidding for some new projects in January 2024.The award of the Land & Property Services contract was subject to the Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have made any representation to the Home Office or the British Government in relation to the asylum application for Shahnaaz Khan and Zuhaib Khan.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the operational remit of the Department’s Media Monitoring Unit.
answer_text: The MMU provides monitoring services of newspapers, television and radio to all nine Departments and one external customer - an Arm’s Length Body. They produce digests, transcripts, news bulletins and newsbreak items. The Unit also provides a newspaper cuttings service of daily newspapers which are uploaded to the Executive Information Service Clipping Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how often AP Moller-Maersk related cargo has been logged at Belfast Harbour over the last 20 months.
answer_text: My Department does not have any role in the day-to-day operations of our ports. I am therefore not in a position to advise how often AP Moller-Maersk related cargo has been logged at Belfast Harbour over the last 20 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any checks carried out by Invest NI, prior to awarding funding to Electronic Automation Engineers, in relation to the company’s work on F-35 projects for BAE Systems and Lockheed Martin.
answer_text: All funding applications made to Invest NI are subject to an appraisal and approval process before financial assistance is granted. Financial assistance is provided to incentivise investment by the company to increase its capabilities. Invest NI has not provided any financial support to Electronic Automation Engineers to subsidise its costs of work undertaken for BAE Systems or Lockheed Martin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will expedite Invest NI’s internal review into public funding for companies connected to the F-35 programme.
answer_text: The Invest NI internal review is currently in progress and will be completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether Electronic Automation Engineers will be included in Invest NI’s internal review into public funding for companies connected to the F-35 programme.
answer_text: Electronic Automation Engineers will be included in Invest NI’s internal review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planned residents' parking scheme in Iveagh, West Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to develop the residents' parking scheme already commenced in the Iveagh area of Belfast. Significant work has been taken forward in relation to this scheme and I am currently considering the proposed design.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the date on which the assets of Politics Plus Ltd were transferred to the Assembly Commission; and (ii) the assets received.
answer_text: On 21 December 2021 and 23 January 2025 a total of £71k was granted by Politics Plus to the Assembly Commission, with the stated requirement that it must be used only for the purposes of strengthening the role of women in political and public life, by supporting the work of the Assembly Women’s Caucus.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the process for appointing MLAs as directors of Politics Plus Ltd; and (ii) the body that administered the appointments process.
answer_text: When the Northern Ireland Assembly Legislative Strengthening Trust was first incorporated in June 2010, as an interim measure, the then Speaker and a number of members of the Assembly Commission agreed to act as its initial Trustees. Subsequent to the May 2011 election, the Assembly Commission had no further role in the process for appointing MLAs as directors of Politics Plus and new trustees were appointed from political parties represented within the Assembly and having at least five MLAs.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has provided (i) funding; (ii) accommodation; (iii) staff; and (iv) office resources to Politics Plus Ltd since 2015.
answer_text: In January 2015 the Assembly Commission agreed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Politics Plus for the period from 1 February 2015 to 31 January 2016. Under this MoU the Assembly Commission provided a grant of up to £50k to enable Politics Plus to provide programme management support, as well as staffing resource of 1.8 Full Time Equivalents (FTE) and an office. In November 2017 the Assembly Commission agreed a further MoU with Politics Plus covering the period from 1 December 2017 to 31 December 2021. Under this MoU the Assembly Commission provided a grant of up to £75k in each of 2017/18 and 2018/19 to enable Politics Plus to provide programme management support, as well as staffing resource of 1.3 FTE per annum and an office. Following the resumption of normal Assembly business in January 2020, the level of staffing resource reduced to 0.05 FTE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26904/22-27, (i) whether there is an expectation that her Department would be notified by the contractor if controlled wastes were to be exposed in the course of road construction; and (ii) whether controlled waste has been disturbed by the road works for the Narrow Water Bridge, Warrenpoint.
answer_text: I am advised that there would not be an expectation that the Department would be notified as the project is managed by Louth County Council, however officials are aware of the discovery of controlled waste during the road works for the Narrow Water Bridge.My officials have advised me that the appropriate authority, DAERA’s Environmental Crime Unit, is aware of the incident. They have inspected the site and reviewed the contractor’s waste management plan, including the waste dockets for the material.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the need for legislation similar to Awaab’s Law, given that this will be enacted in Britain in October 2025.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 22292/22 27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether military cargo is handled at Belfast Harbour.
answer_text: My Department does not have any role in the day-to-day operations of our ports. I am therefore not in a position to advise whether military cargo is handled at Belfast Harbour

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26357/22-27, to detail the amount spent by Translink on recruitment and onboarding costs including (i) training; (ii) medical; and (iii) Driver and Vehicle Agency fees, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:In addition to the three areas requested, we have also included access NI costs as a Bus Driver is considered a designated post and subject to an enhance disclosure check.Costs are associated with activity levels each year and based on the rates for 2024/25 year.  Reporting PeriodMedicals (£)Driving Licences (£)Training costs (£)Access NI -Enhanced disclosure (£)01/04/2020 - 31/03/2021360401,4046401/04/2021 - 31/03/202221,5822,40025,2403,84001/04/2022 - 31/03/202353,7755,98061,3989,56801/04/2023 - 31/03/202440,8264,54046,8547,26401/04/2024 - 31/03/202543,8834,88050,2887,808For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 21944/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 21944/22-27 was answered on 11 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the average hours worked per week by full-time Translink drivers, contracted for at least 36 hours per week, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:While the relevant data is recorded within our Driver Allocation System (DAS), the system does not currently support the generation of a report that directly provides the specific breakdown requested. DAS records working hours at an individual level, but it does not automatically exclude periods of absence such as annual leave or sick leave. As a result, producing an accurate average of actual hours worked - excluding absences - would require a manual review of each individual driver’s record. Such an exercise is not feasible within the timeframe for answering Assembly Questions. This may be feasible within the timeframes under the Freedom of Information Act 2000, should the MLA wish to raise a request directly with Translink. We are currently reviewing options to improve the reporting capabilities of this system going forward.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26906/22-27, to detail (i) when the Environmental Crime Unit first received a report of the presence of controlled waste at this location; and (ii) from where the waste currently being exposed by the road works associated with the A2 Narrow Water Bridge has come from.
answer_text: My department first received reports of controlled waste in this area during 2013.It is suspected that the waste being uncovered by the road works has come from the demolition of the Gilbert Ash factory which stood on lands now owned and operated by Warrenpoint Harbour Authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Department keeps a specific record of workers in the Civil Service who lost their job or had to leave their job due to visa changes.
answer_text: The Civil Service has a range of leaver reasons for reporting purposes; however, this does not include visa changes. This level of detail would be retained on personnel files.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any trans or non-binary staff in the Civil Service have expressed concerns about the recent Supreme Court decision around trans rights.
answer_text: Several staff, including trans and non-binary staff, have contacted my department about the recent Supreme Court judgment.The Civil Service is committed to equality, diversity, and inclusion, and to providing a positive, inclusive workplace where everyone can fully participate; perform at their best; reach their full potential; and feel valued, respected and accepted for who they truly are.The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course.This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many single let providers have been contracted by the Department to provide temporary accommodation in the previous 12 months, broken down by: (i) private landlord; (ii) private company; and (iii) estate agent.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of money provided by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts, to Healthcare Ireland in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether Translink record the average number of hours spent by drivers in a bus or glider without a break.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:All Translink bus drivers’ duties and rosters are developed in line with relevant working time and driving regulations (N.I. Domestic Driver Hours Rules/ Assimilated (EU) rules on drivers' hours/ Working Time Directive.  Translink complies with all of these regulations.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail whether she is aware of any concerns regarding enforced office attendance for staff; and (ii) for her assessment the impact of enforced office attendance will have on (a) carers; and (b) people with disabilities.
answer_text: Staffing matters within the Department are the responsibility of the Accounting Officer/Permanent Secretary, Mr Ian Snowden.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of retention numbers for Translink staff over the last five years.
answer_text: As this is an operational matter for Translink, I have not made any assessment of their retention numbers. However, I have sought the requested information from Translink and have been advised as follows:Translink’s staff retention rate for the last five years is shown in the table below.  % Retention Rate 2024/2025 92.22023/2024 91.82022/2023 92.62021/2022 93.52020/2021 95.7Translink also advise this shows that their retention rate for the five-year period has remained between 91%-96%, which market research indicates is a positive indicator of their ability to retain staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of workers in Translink who have become unwell due to working conditions, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink. I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:In relation to the request below the following assumptions have been made:The requestor is seeking information on the number of workers who have had a period of absence from work.The requestor is seeking information where the circumstances associated with the absence were related to a workplace incident where the employee is not deemed responsible, for example. workplace accident (slip, trip, fall), assault or RTC.We have also defined ‘worker’ as employees of Translink as we would not hold the information requested on other categories of workers such as agency worker or contractors.Unfortunately, we are unable to extract the number of employees and can only provide the number of days absence lost.2020/21 2021/22 2022/23 2023/24 2024/25 2025/26 Total Days Lost 1918 1908.25 1679.75 1773 2363 168   For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the difference in workplace rights between 1) Substitute Teachers 2) Teachers without permanent contracts 3) Teachers with permanent contracts.
answer_text: Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the 5 main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and Boards of Governors via TNC Circulars. Teachers’ (Terms and Conditions of Employment) Regulations (Northern Ireland 1987) and (the amendment Regulations of 1988, No. 299) set out terms and conditions of employment of teachers in all grant-aided schools. https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/teachers-salaries-and-conditions-servicehttps://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/teachers-terms-and-conditions-employment-amendment-regs-ni-1988-no-299As detailed in the Regulations many of the terms and conditions of teachers in Northern Ireland are the same regardless of the teacher being in a permanent or temporary contract.There are however a few differences with supply teachers and those with a permanent contract in relation to teachers’, pay, holiday leave periods and sick pay entitlements.Full-time teachers are required to be available to work 195 days per academic year. Permanent teachers are paid monthly, all year round, at a rate of their annual salary divided by twelve. They receive twelve equal monthly payments even though they will be off during periods of school closure.A supply teacher is normally engaged by a school to provide short term immediate cover for an absent teacher and will receive payment for each working day at the rate of 1/195 of the salary which they would receive if they were employed in a permanent capacity.A teacher engaged and paid for each day worked at a rate of 1/195 of the annual salary of a full-time permanent teacher receives a higher daily rate to account for holiday pay. Since 2004 the following statement has been included on the pay slips of supply teachers: ‘Holiday pay is included within your daily rate of pay calculated as annual salary over 195 working days’. In accordance with the Teachers’ Salaries Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993 No. 318 a supply teacher employed for less than 1 year is not entitled to receive occupational sick pay. Depending upon the supply teacher’s earnings they may be entitled to claim statutory sick pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) her assessment of what impact the Data (Use and Access) Bill will have; and (ii) whether she will be laying a Legislative Consent Memorandum for the Bill.
answer_text: The British Government’s domestic data protection framework has potential implications for the EU’s Adequacy Decision, which underpins free data flow from the EU to Britain and the North without additional safeguards. This is vital for the smooth functioning of the all-island economy, which is a point both I and my predecessor have raised at every opportunity with the British Government. The Legislative Consent Memorandum process for this Bill is being led by the Minister of Finance. I have endorsed the principle of the extension of the provisions of the Data (Use and Access) Bill to the North and my officials continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Finance in that regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what maternity and paternity rights exist for agency workers.
answer_text: Eligibility for maternity and paternity leave and pay, and many other statutory leave and pay entitlements, would depend on whether the individual is categorised as an ‘employee’ or a ‘worker’. Employees are entitled to the leave and pay elements if they have worked continuously with the same employer for at least 26 weeks up to the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth and meet the lower earnings limit. Workers are not entitled to maternity or paternity leave but may be entitled to the pay element if they satisfy the same continuous employment and earnings criteria as employees. Statutory Maternity Pay is a matter for the Department for Communities, as is Maternity Allowance, which is payable to mothers who do not qualify for Statutory Maternity Pay but have worked or been self-employed for at least 26 of the 66 weeks before their baby is due and have made sufficient Class 2 National Insurance contributions. My goal is to end this distinction between a worker and an employee but due to the interaction with the tax and social security systems this will require the co-operation of the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any powers she or her Department has to access the algorithms of social media organisations.
answer_text: My Department does not have the vires to access the algorithms of social media organisations. The Online Safety Act 2023 requires providers to specifically consider how algorithms could impact users’ exposure to illegal content.  Ofcom is the independent regulator of Online Safety. More information on the Online Safety Act is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/online-safety-act-explainer/online-safety-act-explainer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has had any engagement regarding a possible company merger between Hovis and Kingsmill.
answer_text: My Department, through Invest NI, has not had any engagement with either Hovis or Kingsmill regarding a possible merger.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what aspects of the work of the Competition Commission applies.
answer_text: The Competition Commission was replaced in 2014, by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). The CMA is the primary competition regulator with powers applying across Britain and the North of Ireland.  My Department has no role in investigating or approving mergers.  Competition is a reserved matter, and the CMA has responsibility for regulating mergers among other competition and markets issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that will be impacted when eligibility for the Limited Capability for Work-Related Activity element of Universal Credit is raised to 22 years old.
answer_text: Since publication of the UK Government’s Green Paper my officials have been working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to assess the proposed changes and their implications for Northern Ireland. This complex and extensive exercise is continuing but at this stage the information requested is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Regional Obesity Management Service will provide any support to people that have lost weight without the use of medical injections.
answer_text: The aim of the Regional Obesity Management Service is to provide support to individuals who are struggling with obesity or being overweight. In its first phase, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. Work is being taken forward by my Department to develop a service specification which will set out access criteria for the service. Anyone who meets that criteria will be deemed appropriate to access the service for support in weight management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out a feasibility assessment of employing GPs as salaried workers.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted a formal feasibility assessment on employing GPs as salaried workers.However in situations where GP practices have handed back their contracts, often due to challenges in recruitment, retention, or financial viability, my Department has implemented temporary measures to ensure continuity of care. These measures include the management of such practices by Health and Social Care Trusts or GP Federations, during which GPs may be employed on a salaried basis to maintain service delivery. These arrangements are intended as interim solutions while efforts are made to secure new contractors for the affected practices.It is important to note that, under the current General Medical Services (GMS) contract, GPs operate as independent contractors. This model has been a foundational aspect of general practice since the inception of the NHS, allowing GP partners to manage their practices with a degree of autonomy while delivering NHS services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24501/22-27, whether the feedback considered will be published.
answer_text: Feedback received during the Women's Health Action Plan Stakeholder engagement events will be published alongside the Women’s Health Action Plan which is anticipated to be published later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the granting of mining licenses meets her Department's climate targets, as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department did not grant any mining licences. However, on the 8 May 2025 my Department did grant seven Mineral Prospecting Licences. Minerals are integral components of renewable energy technologies that will reduce our dependence on fossil fuels and support the delivery of our climate targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the review of mineral licensing policy will be completed.
answer_text: The review of mineral licensing policy, which is enshrined in the Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969 and associated legislation, is ongoing. To inform the scope of the review, independent research was commissioned in 2021 into the economic, social and environmental impact of mineral exploration and mining which identified three key themes for the review to focus on: Research on domestic supply opportunities, Stakeholder Engagement and Updating the regulatory framework for mineral licensing. To ensure compliance with Section 5 (2)(b) Environmental Assessment of Plans and Programmes Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004, the Department is undertaking a Strategic Environmental Assessment and it is expected that this will take up to 18 months to complete. The assessment will help ensure that any new policy will protect the environment and serve local communities. My Department is also planning for a Call for Evidence: it is expected that the Call for Evidence will be issued in September 2025.The review of the minerals policy will also be informed by developments in the EU with the adoption of the Critical Raw Materials Act in March 2024, and the UK Critical Minerals Strategy 2022 and refresh of 2023.  Both set out a range of critical raw materials/minerals which are essential to support the green transition. The review will continue to consider the policy position in terms of both the development of a circular economy in minerals, and the robust regulation of exploration and primary production.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether an assessment has been made of the the impact on children and parents if funding is not provided to Bunscoil an tSléibhe Dhuibh for the expanded Naíonra programme.
answer_text: Release of the £50m for Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) to my department was contingent upon the Executive approving my ELC Strategy plan for 2025-26. I sought Executive approval for that plan in a paper circulated to Ministerial colleagues on the 8 April.  It included a proposal to enhance the Pathway Fund budget.  The Executive approved the plan on 15 May 2025.This additional funding means that all Pathway applicants which have met the eligibility requirements in 2025-26 can receive an award.  Early Years settings, including Naíonra an tSléibhe Dhuibh, received confirmation of their 2025-26 Pathway award from Early Years (EYO) on 20 May 2025.  Naíonra an tSléibhe Dhuibh received the maximum award of £40,000 which I increased from the £30,000 available in previous years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the nature of any concerns they have raised with the UK Government in relation to Mears accommodation for asylum seekers.
answer_text: The provision of asylum accommodation is not devolved; the asylum estate here is managed by Home Office (HO) and their provider Mears Housing. Our Department liaises with HO, Mears and devolved partners to raise and seek to address local issues.Most recently Ministers attended the Home Office Interministerial Group (IMG) for Safety, Security and Migration on Wednesday 21 May 2025 and discussed a range of immigration matters, including asylum and resettlement accommodation and its impact locally.

answer_text: The Assembly Commission has a contract with Pi Communications for the operation and maintenance of broadcasting and related sound and vision services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment as to why there is a disproportionally high number of long remands amongst prisoners in Roe House, Maghaberry Prison.
answer_text: The number of long remands amongst prisoners in Roe House, Maghaberry compares favourably to other accommodation units in the prison.  At 19 May 2025, there were 516 prisoners on remand in Maghaberry Prison, 26 of whom were held in Roe House.  The average remand time in each Unit in Maghaberry ranges from 1 day to 178 days.  The average remand time in Roe House is 112 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the action she is taking to address the issue of protracted court hearings in cases before the Special Courts.
answer_text: There are a range of reasons why jury and non-jury trials can take longer than anticipated to complete including the number of defendants and witnesses involved, and the time required to hear applications to address legal issues.  I recognise the negative impact that delay in the criminal justice system can have on victims, witnesses and on defendants and addressing avoidable delay remains a priority for my Department. The Department and Justice organisations across the system have prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay.  This work is built around five key workstreams and will take forward existing work on committal reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and continue to drive forward other areas for efficiencies such as early engagement, court remits and out of court disposals.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the statistics showing the greatest number of immigration raids carried out in the UK between 2022 and 2023 occurred in Belfast.
answer_text: Immigration, including immigration enforcement, is not devolved and is a matter for the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of claimants that are set to lose their contributory Employment Support Allowance under plans to time limit it with the decision to cut Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: The time limit for contributory Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) is 365 days for claimants placed in the Work-Related Activity Group, including any time spent in the Assessment Phase.  There is no time limit for claimants in the Support Group.  This change was introduced to Northern Ireland on 31 October 2016, with the first claimants being impacted on 28 November 2016.Contributory ESA does not depend on someone receiving Personal Independence Payment (PIP).  If someone loses their PIP entitlement, they would still be entitled to contributory ESA subject to meeting the usual criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of service and maintenance charges in Housing Executive buildings with communal areas.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is required to sell flats under the House Sales Scheme by way of a “Flat Lease”. This lease sets outs the obligations of the Housing Executive as the freehold owner of the premises to maintain and carry out the necessary works and services. This lease also sets out the covenants for the lessee, which includes the payment of apportioned charges for the communal parts of the premises.The communal parts of the premises include the structural parts of the building including the roof, foundations and external parts.The General Service Charges collected for each flat is based on actual costs incurred for services and works on an annual basis and charged monthly to the leaseholders. These General Service Charges in the main include a charge for Insurance, servicing of lights, communal electricity and may include charges for works that are carried out under response maintenance. When delivering Planned Scheme works, the estimated costs of works are advised to the leaseholders in advance of commencement of the scheme on site. These costs will be based on the post tender costs for the works for the block and the apportioned costs will be determined for all of the units that have benefit and requirement of these works.When notifying leaseholders of the estimated planned scheme works, they are advised that they will be billed for the works following completion and are encouraged to make payment in advance of these charges and repayment arrangements are discussed.The Housing Executive offers payment to a Planned Scheme account for these works to allow for such repayment over and above their annual General Service Charges. This Planned Scheme account allows for those leaseholders who do not have the financial means to make payment on demand as set out in the lease, to repay the costs over an agreed period which is based on their individual financial circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department keeps a record of companies that have (i) been in formal dispute with trade unions; and (ii) refused a request for voluntary recognition from a trade union.
answer_text: There is no statutory requirement for companies or trade unions to notify the Department for the Economy, or any other body, of a formal dispute – and records of such disputes are not kept by my Department. The Industrial Court, which is funded by my Department, is the Tribunal Non-Departmental Public Body with a statutory responsibility to adjudicate on trade union recognition disputes.  It will adjudicate when asked to do so by a trade union.  Its decisions are published at www.industrialcourt.gov.uk. The Labour Relations Agency (LRA), also funded by my Department, will provide a conciliation service in relation to trade union recognition if the parties in dispute agree to LRA involvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for a breakdown of the locations of residents' parking schemes; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made of the positive and negative impact of residents' parking schemes.
answer_text: My Department has implemented one residents’ parking scheme in the north within the Rugby Road/College Park Avenue area of Belfast. A review of residents’ parking schemes which considered the Rugby Road scheme as well as our approach to residents’ parking in general was published on 29 November 2024 and is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of civil servants with an annual salary in excess of £100,000.
answer_text: ANSWERThe most recently published data on this subject is available on the NISRA website in Table 1a. It indicates that as of 31 March 2024, there were 65 civil servants earning an annual full-time equivalent salary exceeding £100,000, which constitutes 0.3% of all staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prosecutions of employers in relation to workplace (i) safety breaches; and (ii) deaths in the last ten years.
answer_text: In response to part (i) of the question, information on the total number of prosecutions and convictions for offences under health and safety legislation for the period 2015 – 2024 has been provided.  Departmental prosecutions datasets do not contain information on whether or not the defendant in a case was an employer, or whether the circumstances related specifically to the workplace, so figures for all prosecutions and convictions for offences under relevant legislation have been provided. In relation to part (ii) of the question, there were 10 prosecutions at courts in the period 2015 – 2024, for the offence of corporate manslaughter.  These prosecutions resulted in 3 convictions.Prosecutions and convictions for offences under Health and Safety legislation, 2015 – 2024YearProsecutionsConvictions201521162016231820171413201814112019241720208720211414202221202023171420242624Note:1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures for health and Safety prosecutions relate to cases where there was a prosecution for at least one offence under the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of police and other Government bodies.4. Prosecutions for corporate manslaughter are brought under the Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide act 2007.5. Figures for 2024 are provisional.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of reports that Health and Social Care Trusts have received in relation to patients receiving letters about medical appointments after the appointment occurred, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not receive reports from Royal Mail or Health and Social Care Trusts regarding patients receiving their medical appointment letters in a timely manner or after the appointment has occurred. To minimise any patient inconvenience, Trusts have implemented a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to sending appointment letters to patients, Trusts also use text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments in most services. This approach aims to provide a timely method of communication, especially when postal services may be delayed.With encompass now fully operational across Northern Ireland, all patients and service users will be able to securely access a subset of their health and social care record via My Care, such as appointment letters, clinic letters, medication details, and some test results.  They can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment. My Care provides secure online access to personal health information and to date has approximately 130,000 users.

answer_text: The Department does not receive reports from Royal Mail or Health and Social Care Trusts regarding patients receiving their medical appointment letters in a timely manner or after the appointment has occurred. To minimise any patient inconvenience, Trusts have implemented a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to sending appointment letters to patients, Trusts also use text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments in most services. They can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment. My Care provides secure online access to personal health information and to date has approximately 130,000 users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the number of interviews for the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer post that were noted as being impacted by IT issues in the last 12 months.
answer_text: There have been two Administrative Officer competitions during the last 12 months – one for the Department for Communities and a Generic Administrative Officer Competition attracting over 16,000 applications in total. For the DfC Competition, 446 candidates were unsuccessful due to incomplete interview recordings however we cannot determine if this was due to IT issues.   The Generic Administrative Officer is ongoing with results expected to issue to candidates this week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to codifying the right to access into law as part of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The right to request access to a workplace by trade unions will be contained within the Employment Rights Bill. Practical aspects of this new legal right will be detailed within a supplementary statutory Code of Practice drafted in consultation with key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will ensure all temporary and agency workers in the same job for more than a year will have the right to request permanent employment.
answer_text: I will keep this matter under review and will engage further with stakeholders as we develop our legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to including the reduction of the notice period mandated before a ballot for industrial action in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: My Department sought views in the Good Jobs consultation exercise on the possibility of reducing the notice period for industrial action from seven to five days.Having taken into consideration the consultation feedback I have decided not to reduce the notice period at this time. However, I will improve trade union access to workplaces, lower the threshold for mandatory recognition of trade unions, and strengthen protections for trade union members involved in official strike action.In Britain the current notice period for industrial action is fourteen days. It is intended to reduce this to ten days via its own Employment Rights Bill currently making its way through Parliament.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she has given to reducing the level of information unions are required to submit before taking industrial action in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: Officials are currently identifying options to determine how to reduce the administrative burden on trade unions, when seeking to take industrial action, as indicated in the Way Forward document and consultation response analysis following the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill consultation. My Department will consider what reasonable changes to the administration of the balloting process can be made to reduce bureaucracy for unions and employers alike and take action as required.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of why the Together: Building a United Community strategy to remove peace walls within ten years has not been achieved.
answer_text: Since the T:BUC strategy’s launch in 2013, considerable progress has been made with the removal of interface structures.  Led by the Department of Justice, 22 of the 59 structures have been removed and a further 10 have been partially removed – many substantially. Opening times of vehicular and pedestrian access gates have also been extended at a number of interfaces.The process of removal, reduction or re-imaging of interface security structures is complex, with some structures being in place for up to 50 years.Each project differs depending on the specific location and factors such as the prevalence of inter-community tension, community sentiment, demographics, nearby regeneration, and opportunities for community benefit derived from any planned reduction or removal.Progress continues to be made, and the DoJ Interfaces team are currently engaging with residents and key stakeholders in various location including areas within the Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area, North Belfast, East Belfast and Derry/Londonderry.The International Fund for Ireland (IFI) support projects that develop and deliver a range of confidence and relationship building interventions within and between interface communities to help residents reach a position where they feel it is safe and appropriate to proceed with the removal of Peace Walls within their area. IFI work closely with the DoJ to support interface removal and since 2012 the Peace Barriers Programme (PBP) has engaged thousands of local residents and facilitated intra and inter-community activities. Over the past 10 years, the PBP has identified over 60 sites who desire barrier change and has provided funding of just over £11m. It is currently providing funding to six projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when a ban on zero-hours contracts, as announced under New Decade, New Approach, will come into effect.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly on 28 April 2025, I announced my proposals to end exploitative zero hours contracts.   Subject to the agreement of the Executive and Assembly, I intend to introduce the necessary legislation by January 2026, with a view to the Assembly passing the provisions into law by the end of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any preventative measures in relation to fire and rehire that will be contained in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: I intend to introduce specific measures to strengthen employment protections as they relate to fire and rehire by making it unfair to dismiss an employee in order to alter the terms and conditions of their employment contract except in specific circumstances. The primary exception will be where an employer can demonstrate genuine financial distress and the only other option is job loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to amending procurement policy, given the intention in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill to restrict temporary and agency contracts.
answer_text: My proposals are aimed at ending the use of exploitative zero hours contracts.  I have written to all Ministerial Colleagues, including the Minister of Finance, to seek their endorsement of my proposals for the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill. There are existing requirements to incorporate Social Value in public procurements. I will happily engage further on issues arising.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether banded hours workers will have the same protection as zero-hour contract workers under the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: Workers on banded hour contracts will have the additional protection of minimum guaranteed hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration she or her predecessor gave to removing the ban on secondary strike action as part of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: This area is not in my Bill as my focus is on promoting more constructive relationships between trade unions and employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much square footage of undeveloped land (i) Translink; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company currently holds.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:In terms of development land this would comprise c.18.25 acres of potentially developable land across various sites. This includes Weavers Cross which has outline planning approval. Translink is an umbrella name for NITHC, Ulsterbus, NIR and Metro. NITHC is the ultimate holding company for land holdings. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, to provide a definition of what is meant by genuinely casual or seasonal work.
answer_text: Further detail, including definitions, will be set out in the Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a spending limit for private healthcare provision that he, as Minister, has to approve.
answer_text: My Department will work within the budget which has been agreed, however, decisions around revenue expenditure are fully delegated by the Department to Health & Social Care (HSC) Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how; and (ii) when decisions are made to award public money to private healthcare providers.
answer_text: Decisions around revenue expenditure are fully delegated by the Department to Health & Social Care (HSC) Trusts which can enter into contracts with independent healthcare providers for the provision of healthcare services, funding them from within their own budget.The priority is to build core HSC capacity to reduce the reliance on the independent sector (IS) and when there is additional money available in year, above normal allocations, the HSC will seek to secure additional in-house capacity before seeking additional support from the IS.Bids for work to be fulfilled by the IS through any  additional funding are submitted by Trusts to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) for quality assurance to ensure value for money, in line with public accountability and procurement processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the amount that Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has paid Alliance Healthcare in each of the last ten years; and (ii) for a breakdown on this spend.
answer_text: The total expenditure paid to Alliance Healthcare in each of the last ten years, as reported by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is set out in the following table: Financial YearAlliance Healthcare spend £m2015/166.282016/177.072017/186.452018/196.442019/208.662020/219.802021/2210.252022/2318.412023/2420.312024/2517.62Total111.28Note: reported expenditure excludes any VAT where charged.BHSCT have advised that all spend relates to pharmacy supplies and has experienced an increase in costs due to: An increase in HSC investment in highly specialist medicines in general. An increase in the quantity and value of specialist medicines that are distributed by their Pharma manufacturer through Alliance and very often a solo distributor. An increase in generic medicines procured via Alliance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the sustainability of the field of social work, given that levels of support through the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme have remained unchanged since 2008.
answer_text: The Social Work Student Incentive Scheme was introduced to encourage applications to study for the Degree in Social Work in NI. Due to severe budgetary constraints it has not been possible to provide an uplift to the quantum of the Student Incentive Scheme payments which my Department makes to student social workers. While the number of applications to study social work has fallen in the past five years, this is in keeping with applications to all Further and Higher education courses. However, there continues to be more applicants to study social work in NI than there are places available. Considerable efforts are on going to maximize the number of applications to study social work in future years.My Department has also increased the number of training places for student social workers by commissioning 25 places with the Open University NI between 2021-2023 and has added an additional 40 places across all universities in 2024. This brings the total number of social work training places to 325, the largest number on record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 25789/22-27, to detail the number of prisoners currently on remand for (i) two years; (ii) three years; (iii) four years; (iv) five years; and (v) more than five years.
answer_text: Responses to your queries are set out in the table below. Time on Remand for remand prisoners in Northern Ireland prison custody on 1st May 2025.Time on RemandRemand PrisonersLess than 2 years729(i) At least 2 years but less than 3 years11(ii)(iii) At least 3 years but less than 5 years4(iv)(v) At least 5 years or more0Total Remand Prisoners744Source – PRISM (Management Information)Notes: Information is snapshot data, as at midnight on 1st May 2025.The time periods for Part ii and Part iii have been combined to prevent disclosure of individuals.Time spent on remand includes time spent so far during the current period on remand and excludes any other separate periods of time on remand within the time in custody.Prisoners are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’ and these have been combined.Immigration detainees are another type of unsentenced prisoners in custody but are not included in the remand figures provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to review and uplift the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme allowance to a minimum of £7,000.
answer_text: I am very aware of the difficulties which can arise as a result of financial hardship which social work students can experience while undertaking professional training courses. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, and the need to protect and maintain front-line services, my Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students on professional training courses than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the adequacy of the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme travel cost allowance of £500.
answer_text: Students who are domicile in NI and study social work full-time are provided with a non-means tested allowance of £500 towards their expenses whilst on practice placement. In recent months my Department has been able to make a modest additional payment to social work students who have been assessed as having to travel an excessive distance to/from their mandatory practice learning placement.As a result of the extremely challenging budgetary situation for my Department and the need to protect and maintain essential front-line services, there are no plans to increase the quantum of the allowance at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any current sources of financial support for social work students.
answer_text: Student social workers who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland can access a non-means tested Student Incentive Scheme of £4,000 from my Department, for each year of their study. In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. Payments in relation to the Student Incentive Scheme are in addition to any student finance support which can be applied for separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times housing benefit was reduced or withheld to landlords due to maintenance or repair issues in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Housing Benefit is not reduced or withheld to landlords due to maintenance or repair issues. A customer may ask for their entitlement to Housing Benefit to be paid directly to them or their landlord. Customers contacting Housing Benefit in relation to maintenance or repair issues are referred to Housing Rights for advice. Payment of a rent allowance is made to the person entitled to the allowance unless an appointee arrangement is in place.Reg 91 & 80 of the HB Regs (NI) 2006Reg 72 & 80 of the HB (SPC) Regs (NI) 2006The customer may request that payment is made to their landlord.Reg 91(3) of the HB Regs (NI )2006Reg 72(3) of the HB (SPC) Regs (NI) 2006Alternatively, payment may be made to a landlord where arrears of 6 weeks or more are owed.Reg 92 of the HB Regs (NI )2006Reg 73 of the HB (SPC) Regs (NI) 2006”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of staff in Translink currently employed on G1 pay grades.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:As of 6 May 2025, there is currently a headcount of 84 within the G1 pay grade.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered introducing a rates discount for single occupants, similar to the council tax discount in operation in Britain.
answer_text: ANSWEROur domestic rate support provision already includes a Lone Pensioner Allowance which provides a 20% discount to persons aged 70 or over who live alone – this was provided to nearly 40,000 people here in 2024/25 at a cost of over £9 million to the Executive. I have no current plans to introduce any single occupancy discount within the rating system beyond that Lone Pensioner Allowance provision.Any rate relief given means either foregoing revenue or charging other ratepayers more. An exemption or relief is often viewed from the perspective of who it benefits, but there is a real cost, as every pound raised through the rating system stays here as a resource to help pay for hospitals, schools, and other essential regional services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any work his Department has carried out alongside other agencies to ensure that the collapsed fence at the entrance of Gaelscoil na bhFál and Ionad Uíbh Eachach will be repaired.
answer_text: The Department is currently seeking to establish the boundaries of school site and ownership of the fencing.  Should the fencing be found to be within the school’s boundary remedial works will take place to repair the fencing as soon as possible. If not, the Department will engage with the relevant landowner to seek to have the repairs progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether current Personal Independence Payment deadlines will remain for applicants following the latest changes announced by the Department for Work and Pensions.
answer_text: There are no changes planned to the time allowed for the completion of a Personal Independence Payment application. My Department has already put processes in place to apply flexibility and discretion to return dates. People can be given additional time upon request if they require help to complete their form or to obtain additional medical evidence if they are seeking a review. Similarly, there are no planned changes to award durations for existing claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the high electricity and water demands of data centres and their impact on statutory emissions reduction targets under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, whether his Department will (i) consider classifying large scale data centre proposals as regionally significant developments under Section 26 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011; and (ii) introduce associated planning guidance to ensure cumulative environmental, energy, and climate impacts are properly assessed.
answer_text: Under Section 26 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, developments may be classed as regionally significant where they are of such a scale or nature that they warrant determination by the Department. The Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 set out the current thresholds for regionally significant development.Data centres would generally be treated under Class 8 ‘Business, Industry (Light and General), Storage and Distribution’, depending on their design and function, with a defined major development threshold of 5,000sqm or more gross floor space or where the area of the site exceeds 1 hectare.  There are no corresponding regionally significant thresholds for this class in the Schedule.  As such, a large-scale data centre will typically be processed as a major development by a council, unless the Department exercises its discretion under Section 29 of the Planning Act to call-in the planning application.As part of the Planning Improvement Programme, my Department is currently reviewing the Regulations to ensure the existing classes and thresholds remain appropriate and fit for purpose.  As part of this work, my officials will also be considering whether any changes are required to reflect new and emerging forms of development, to include potential impacts on climate change targets. While the Department is not currently preparing specific planning guidance in relation to data centres, I would advise that all proposals continue to be assessed under the existing planning policy framework and relevant statutory requirements, including the need to consider environmental, infrastructure and climate impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to his Department's vesting orders for the lands forming the illegal Mobuoy dump, to detail (i) the areas of land vested; (ii) the areas of land to be vested; and (iii) the purpose of the vesting orders.
answer_text: My department does not have any vesting orders for the lands forming the Mobuoy site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25406/22-27, to detail when agricultural or environmental commercial interests were first registered in each of the last five years.

The information requested is not in a readily accessible format and collation would require officials from a number of areas to identify where it is located and retrieve the information before collating into an accessible format. I have been advised it is not practicable to provide a response with the necessary level of assurances from an accuracy perspective within the Assembly Questions timescales.  I have therefore asked my officials to contact you and to offer a meeting to discuss this request and explore what assistance and information can be provided.
