question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why the volume of waste at the illegal Mobuoy dump was reported as 600,000 tonnes, when his Department's estimate of waste at the site is in the region of 1.6 million tonnes.
answer_text: In relation to the Mobuoy criminal case, a date for sentencing is to be scheduled by the court. Whilst criminal proceedings are ongoing, I will not be making any comment in relation to the Mobuoy case.Regarding the public consultation, I approved the launch of the public consultation on a draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site with the expectation that it would launch in springtime. However, it has been necessary to delay the launch of the public consultation until after the completion of court proceedings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

The number of staff per Group within my Department who have registered an interest because an immediate family member has an agricultural or environment related commercial interest is as follows:GROUPTOTALClimate Change, Science & Innovation12Environment, Marine & Fisheries (including Forest Service)15Food, Farming & Rural Affairs226Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA)18Strategic Planning & Corporate Services (including Arm’s Length Bodies)38Veterinary Service Animal Health297Finally, I would refer the member to my answers to AQW 26256/22-27 and AQW 26257/22-27 and once again reaffirm my offer of a meeting with my officials to further discuss this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of whether the College Employers’ Forum is fit for purpose.
answer_text: The Colleges Employers’ Forum (CEF) is a collaborative network of the six Further Education (FE) colleges which manages the employers’ relationship with staff at a sectoral level. CEF’s role is to arrange for the negotiation of pay, conditions of service and related issues through regional negotiation committees.The  CEF resumed operation   as a six college model earlier this year and, through its Lecturers’ Negotiating Committee management side, is currently engaging with Trade Unions on the 2024/25 and 2025/26 pay offer and associated conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will honour the full terms of last year’s pay deal for further education lecturers, given that she approved this offer in her role as Minister of Finance.
answer_text: My predecessor provided a budget envelope last year which allowed for an 11.2% pay increase for Further Education lecturers for 2023/24 which aligned with that provided for teachers. The Further Education colleges, as employers, provided a number of terms to be fulfilled in respect of teacher pay parity and the development of an industrial relations model.  I am pleased to update that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer for 2024/25 and 2025/26 as a further step towards parity with teachers. The Further Education college employers have communicated this pay offer with similar terms to their lecturers and to Trade Union officials as part of on-going negotiations. I hope this pay offer will be accepted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department has taken with the College Employers’ Forum to resolve the further education lecturers' pay dispute.
answer_text: My officials have had extensive engagement with College leaders, as employers, on the further education lecturers’ pay dispute. I have also met with representatives of the College Employers Forum seeking an update on the actions being taken to resolve the issue of lecturers’ pay. I am pleased that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer as a further step towards parity with teachers, and I hope that this offer will be accepted and allow the ongoing industrial action to come to an end.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost incurred by Translink for importing hydrogen for its buses in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:As per the response to your earlier question (AQW 23573/22-27), Translink has been operating 23 hydrogen buses in passenger service over the last 3 years. The requested information regarding hydrogen gas cost is regarded as commercially sensitive.              For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that energy security is a key priority for her Department, for her assessment of whether the approval of a number of data centres across the North in recent years will not put at risk or undermine (i) the operational capacity of the national grid; and (ii) the Government's legally binding commitments to reduce greenhouse gas emissions set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Security of Supply is a key priority for my department.  In March 2025, SONI (System Operator for Northern Ireland), published the All-Island Resource Adequacy Assessment (AIRAA), which looks at the balance between electricity demand and supply in Ireland for the next ten years.  This report allows industry, government, regulators and stakeholders to facilitate the transition to renewable energy, whilst ensuring the secure and resilient operation of the electricity system.  It is used to ensure that electricity supplied continues to meet demand (from all sources including data centres) into the future.  From 2025 to 2031, the base scenario assessment shows the system is within standard, meaning there is sufficient capacity to operate the system under normal conditions. In developing these demand forecasts, SONI considers the impact of the Executive’s Energy Strategy and the Climate Change Act 2022. My officials have also been working with DAERA, and across government, to develop the first Climate Action Plan for the region that will meet the greenhouse gas emissions reductions required to meet the first Carbon Budget.My department continue meet regularly with SONI and the Utility Regulator to review, monitor and mitigate any capacity risks around Security of Supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the circumstances when treated effluent from a septic tank or package treatment plant in the countryside will require a consent to discharge from his Department.
answer_text: Under the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, On-Site Wastewater Treatment Systems require the consent from my Department before discharging effluent into a waterway or underground stratum. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) issues such consents on behalf of the Department.Under Article 7(2) of the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, it is an offence to discharge sewage effluent without the appropriate consent. A consent to discharge is required in the following circumstances:When the treated effluent discharges to a waterway or to the underground stratum.When connection to the public sewer network is not possible.All applications are reviewed by NIEA to ensure that the proposed discharge does not pose a risk to public health or the receiving environment.Where consent is granted, monitoring may also be required depending on the discharge volume or the environmental sensitivity of the receiving water. All determinations are made in accordance with the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 and the Water Framework Directive, with the aim of preventing environmental deterioration.Information and application forms are available for both single dwellings and commercial activities on the NIEA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Court of Appeal judgement [2025] EWCA Civ 378 has any implications for how his Department prepares, reviews and consults on River Basin Management Plans in the North, including on the level of information required to be set out for individual water bodies.
answer_text: I am aware of the Court of Appeal’s judgement in the appeal of the case of R (Pickering Fishery Association) v the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs and note that it only applies in England. However, the judgement will be considered in detail before determining whether there are any implications for the River Basin Management Plan process in Northern Ireland. I will continue to prioritise the improvement of water quality in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the deputy First Minister has presented any assessment as to the powers a Climate Commissioner should have.
answer_text: The powers of the Climate Commissioner, as set out within the regulations, are consistent with what has been required by Section 50 of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 23187/22-27, whether a joint position was previously agreed on expedited immigration support for Ukrainians fleeing war.
answer_text: Immigration policy is not devolved. The Ukraine resettlement routes were established by Westminster, set up by the Home Office and administered by MHCLG.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the criteria for organisations to be eligible to access the £250,000 funding to mark the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.
answer_text: Officials are working on the development of the funding scheme, and it will be several months before it is ready to receive applications. A further announcement will be made when preparations are completed, and the scheme opens to applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount that has been spent on consultancy fees by the active travel section within her Department in 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department engages engineering consultancy firms to provide managed professional services in the development and delivery of its capital works programme primarily through the Engineering Services Consultancy Partnering Contract and, when appropriate, through the Major Works and Transport Planning Frameworks.Expenditure in relation to consultancy fees for active travel equates to £0.4m to the end of September 2024. The year-end figures have not yet been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times he has engaged with the Executive Office in relation to the appointment of a Commissioner for Ulster Scots.
answer_text: None

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 24967/22-27, to detail any research her Department has completed on the role the media plays in increasing young men’s propensity to commit gender-based violence.
answer_text: While my Department has not conducted standalone research specifically on the role of the media in increasing young men’s propensity to commit gender-based violence, it has funded and supported work by others that has explored related themes.As previously advised, my Department, in partnership with the Executive Office, provided funding to the PSNI as part of the development of the ‘Power to Change’ campaign, which launched in January this year. This funding enabled the PSNI to commission market research that informed the campaign. The research included interviews with key stakeholders and qualitative focus groups that explored public perceptions of gender-based violence and unwanted behaviours, as well as the attitudes and mindsets that underpin them in Northern Ireland. While not focused solely on media influence, this work considered broader societal influences, which may include media, on behaviours and attitudes. My Department, along with the Department of Health, leads on the implementation of the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.  Under this Strategy, we provided funding through a Small Grants Scheme to Women’s Aid Federation NI to enable them to revise and relaunch ‘Responsible Reporting Matters’ guidelines for the media reporting on domestic abuse and violence against women and girls.  These guidelines encourage journalists to consider the terminology and language they use to prevent a “victim-blaming,” narrative.The Executive Office has responsibility for the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG).  They have advised that, as part of the co-design process for developing the Strategic Framework, the media – both traditional and online was identified as a key sector in shaping public understanding, attitudes and responses to violence against women and girls. A range of external research reports were identified through a literature review on ‘The relationship between masculinity and VAWG’ and also through research shared with the EVAWG team including the voice of lived experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the number of people on remand whilst awaiting trial; and (ii) the length of time people spend awaiting trial.
answer_text: The prevalence and impact of remand presents a significant challenge for individuals involved and their families.  Operational delivery by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is also impacted by the levels of remand.  However, I respect that decisions to remand individuals in custody are for the independent judiciary, who are presented with the facts and evidence for each case.Long case processing times can result in individuals spending longer in custody on remand waiting for trial.  Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system and is one of my key priorities.  The delivery of a Speeding up Justice Programme is a key element of commitments to help deliver Safer Communities under the new Programme for Government.  The programme will seek to reform the way in which criminal cases are handled.  This includes getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, bringing forward proposals regarding the sentencing jurisdiction of the courts, enhancing digital capabilities, and finding better, more efficient ways of dealing with low level offending behaviour.  Reducing the time taken to complete cases is expected to shorten the time that an individual spends on remand, thus helping to reduce the number of unsentenced prisoners on remand at any given point in time.  The number of people on remand whilst awaiting trial; and (ii) the length of time prisoners have spent awaiting trial based on remand prisoners in custody on 8th April 2025 for all Northern Ireland prison establishments combined is detailed in the table on the following page.Time spent on remand by individuals in Northern Ireland Prison Service custody on 8th April 2025.Time on RemandRemand PrisonersLess than 3 months486At least 3 months but less than 6 months136At least 6 months but less than 1 year78At least 1 year but less than 2 years17At least 2 years but less than 3 years6At least 3 years but less than 5 years3Total Remand Prisoners726Average time on remand:98 daysSource – PRISM (Management Information)Notes: There were 726 prisoners on remand across Northern Ireland prison establishments at midnight on 8th April 2025. Time spent on remand includes time spent so far during the current period on remand and excludes any other separate periods of time on remand within the time in custody.Prisoners are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial.’Immigration detainees are not included in the figures provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners on remand in Maghaberry Prison for (i) two to three years; (ii) three to four years; (iii) four to five years; and (iv) six years and over.
answer_text: There were (i) four remand prisoners in Maghaberry Prison on 8th April 2025 who had been on remand for at least two years but less than three years and (ii)(iii) three remand prisoners who had been on remand for at least three years but less than five years.(iv) No remand prisoners on this date had been on remand for six years and over.Source – PRISM (Management Information.Notes: Information is snapshot data, as at midnight on 8th April 2025.There were 629 remand prisoners in Maghaberry Prison on 8th April 2025 who had been on remand for less than two years. No prisoners had been on remand for at ‘least five years but less than six years’ or ‘six years or more’. Time spent on remand includes time spent so far during the current period on remand and excludes any other separate periods of time on remand within the time in custody.The time periods for Part ii and Part iii have been combined to prevent disclosure of individuals.Prisoners are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’ and these are combined for this answer.Immigration detainees are another type of unsentenced prisoners in custody but are not included in the remand figures provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of delays in receiving disability benefits on children and young people with cancer and their families, particularly in relation to (i) financial impacts; (ii) the impact on accessing other benefits support such as Carer's Allowance; and (iii) emotional well-being.
answer_text: No assessment of the impact of delays in receiving disability benefits on children and young people with cancer and their families has been carried out by my Department. While the time taken to make decisions on and pay disability benefits varies over time, and with changes in claim volumes, all awards will be backdated to the date of claim as will the access to other support such as Carer’s Allowance. Recognising the financial challenges that families may experience in these circumstances, my Department has established escalation points for the Royal Victoria Hospital Children's Oncology and Haematology Social Work team and other support organisations to fast track new disability benefit claims where there is urgent need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure children and young people with cancer and their carers receive clear, timely and accessible communication about their disability benefit applications.
answer_text: My Department has a number of measures in place to ensure that all those who need to make an application for Disability Benefits receive clear, timely and accessible communication about their disability benefit applications. These include: Assistance from the respective disability benefit telephony teams for those seeking advice about making an application;The provision of written communications in alternative formats, including braille, British Sign Language, Irish Sign Language, large print and audio CD.  A textphone service is also available;For those needing additional support, the disability benefit branches refer to the Department’s Make the Call service who can provide benefit advice and assistance with application form completion. Benefit branches also signpost to independent advice.    NI Direct provides information on disability benefits which is regularly reviewed and updated. My Department also has escalation point in place for key stakeholders to advocate for customers where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will commit to reviewing and simplifying the disability benefit application process for children and young people diagnosed with cancer, including reducing the application burden through the utilisation of medical evidence to evidence eligibility for these patients.
answer_text: Current legislation dictates that an application must be made for the respective disability benefits. In conjunction with the Department for Work and Pensions, my Department continually reviews the application process to ensure that any improvements needed are delivered for Northern Ireland.Eligibility for Disability Living Allowance (DLA) and Personal Independence Payment (PIP) is based on the information provided by the claimant in their application and any additional evidence provided by the claimant or requested by the Department. Evidence includes information from a range of sources including schools and health care professionals, GPs and Specialist Consultants. The need for further medical evidence is considered on a case by case basis and is not always required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration given to removing the requirement for a three-month qualifying period for disability benefits, such as Disability Living Allowance and Personal Independence Payment, for children and young people diagnosed with cancer, considering (i) the immediate impact and care needs associated with their condition; and (ii) the robust medical evidence of these impacts.
answer_text: Disability benefits such as Disability Living Allowance and Personal Independence Payment provide a measure of financial support towards the additional costs associated with a long-term health condition or disability.	The qualifying period combined with the prospective test are important aspects of disability benefit eligibility rules which help to establish that the health condition is long-term.There are no current plans to make changes to the qualifying period for either Disability Living Allowance or Personal Independence Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any assessment he has made of The Cost of Waiting research from Young Lives vs Cancer, in particular the average seven-month wait time between a child or young person's cancer diagnosis and receiving their first disability benefit payment; and (ii) any steps being taken to reduce these waiting times.
answer_text: No assessment of “The Cost of Waiting” research from Young Lives vs Cancer has been carried out by my Department. My Department’s average processing time for new applications for DLAc from date of receipt in the 2024/2025 year was 35.8 working days*. The latest Personal Independence Payment (PIP) statistics published in February 2025 indicate that the average time taken to process a new application to PIP was 14 weeks. There are special rules in place for someone who is terminally ill and has a life expectancy of less than 12 months to ensure that DLAc or PIP can be paid more quickly. In addition, my Department has established escalation points for the Royal Victoria Hospital Children's Oncology and Haematology Social Work team and other support organisations to fast track new claims where there is urgent need. 		(*The DLAc figures above are taken from operational management information.)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work carried out to consider implementing temporary or modular parking at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: In August 2024 the Belfast Health & Social Care (HSC) Trust undertook a scoping exercise for the addition of a modular style car park on the Royal Victoria Hospital site. Trust staff met with Department of Health officials in October 2024 to discuss delivery options and potential for capital funding.As part of planning discussions between the Belfast HSC Trust and Belfast City Council Planning Service for redevelopment of the Royal Hospitals site in 2018, the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) Road Service, a statutory consultee, did not believe there was sufficient justification to increase parking on the site.In advance of developing more detailed proposals for modular style parking, the Trust have decided it would now be prudent to re-engage with the Planning Service to determine the likelihood of achieving the required planning approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any data his Department holds on (i) the distances travelled; and (ii) the associated costs incurred, by children and young people with cancer to access their treatment and care at the patient treatment centres in Belfast in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust provides a regional oncology service to children and young people in Northern Ireland, however, the Trust information systems do not hold the information that has been requested so unfortunately it will not be possible to provide a response to this enquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that children and young people with cancer and their families are not required to pay hospital parking charges, given reports that many are still incurring parking costs despite the existence of concessionary schemes.
answer_text: The Department’s current Policy for Car Parking Provision and Management includes guidance on free and concessionary parking for patients on particular care paths like cancer. In addition, the Hospital Travel Costs Recovery Scheme allows patients who fulfil specified low-income criteria to reclaim car parking charges.While acknowledging that HSC trusts routinely monitor the uptake of exemptions at their sites, the Department wrote to all Trusts in November 2024 requesting that they remind clinical staff at ward level of their responsibilities to advise patients of their entitlement to free and concessionary parking / transport costs, and that areas where a decrease or low up take of exemptions is noted are targeted. In addition, eligibility criteria is advertised at ward level via leaflets & posters and should be easily accessible on the HSC Trusts’ websites.In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, free hospital car parking comes into operation on the 12 May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners currently on remand in (i) Magilligan Prison; and (ii) Maghaberry Prison.
answer_text: On 8th April 2025, there were:7 prisoners on remand in Magilligan Prison which was 1.4% of the overall population (495).636 prisoners on remand in Maghaberry Prison which was 48.0% of the overall population (1,325).Source: PRISM – Management InformationNotes: Information is snapshot data, as at midnight on 8th April 2025.Prisoners are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’ and these are combined for this answer.Immigration detainees are another type of unsentenced prisoners in custody but are not included in the remand figures provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact of the reconfiguration of the adolescent and young adult cancer services on (i) additional travel needs and costs for patients; and (ii) local hospital services.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the requirements to publish spending through Government procurement cards.
answer_text: ANSWERThere is currently no requirement for Executive Departments to publish spend on Government Procurement Cards. The guidance is currently under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the financial costs for people travelling for cancer treatment were taken account of in relation to the reconfiguration of children and young people’s cancer services.
answer_text: In developing Minimum Standards for Adolescents and Young Adults my Department assessed the potential financial impact that the reconfiguration of services could have on young people who are travelling for treatment and their families.This assessment concluded that only a very small cohort of patients could  face additional travel costs following changes to how the existing service is currently delivered and that any negative impact is clearly outweighed by the benefits of implementing a regionally equitable and sustainable service. Support is available towards travel costs for health and social care treatment via my Department’s Help with Health Care costs scheme which provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help on referral by a doctor or dentist.

answer_text: ANSWERThe total Departmental spend through Government procurement cards in each of the last five financial years, is as follows:	2023-24	£	2022-23	£	2021-22	£	2020-21	£	2019-20	£Total GPC Spend46,38349,63542,17734,64724,062

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 22987/22-27, to detail the number of times the cross-departmental working group has met since it was formed in February 2022.
answer_text: The Cross-Departmental Working Group for the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture strategy and the Irish Language strategy first met in December 2021. Including that meeting, they have met 13 times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of interest being paid on the loan provided for the building of the Value Car Park on Grosvenor Road, Belfast.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Investment Fund provided a loan of £7.8 million to the developers of the car park project. This loan has been repaid in full. The specific terms of the loan are commercially sensitive. However, I can confirm that the level of interest in relation to all loans issued by the Fund is set at a commercial market rate, in accordance with the Operational Agreement that governs the Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of the average additional costs borne by families who have a young person diagnosed with cancer.
answer_text: No assessment of the average additional costs borne by families who have a young person diagnosed with cancer has been carried out by the Department for Communities. The remit of my Department is to administer disability benefits to support families in these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the poverty levels of households before and after a young person has been diagnosed with cancer.
answer_text: The Department has not undertaken any specific assessment in relation to poverty levels before and after a young person has been diagnosed with cancer.The Northern Ireland Poverty and Income Inequality Report is the primary source for data and information about poverty and income inequality in Northern Ireland. The report presents annual estimates of the proportion of people, children, working-age adults and pensioners in Northern Ireland living in poverty, and other statistics on household income and income inequality. Analyses of poverty levels are also presented based on individuals’ characteristics, for example gender, religion, and disability status. The most recent publication can be found at the following link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-poverty-and-income-inequality-report-2023-24

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any barriers preventing the Housing Executive from using funds from its Landlord Reserve Account to purchase properties for use as permanent social homes, rather than temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The use of NIHE Landlord Reserves is restricted. It is primarily used to provide effective management of financial resources and to aid short to medium term investment planning in NIHE stock.  It also provides a means for unexpected and exceptional expenditure to be funded without detriment to in-year business objectives.Using Landlord Reserve funding to purchase homes on the open market for use as permanent social housing would have a requirement to demonstrate value for money and best use of public resources, whilst accounting for the growing investment requirement across the existing stock portfolio. Using Landlord Reserve funding for NIHE to add to supply would represent poorer value for money than the combination of grant and private borrowing that Housing Associations can offer. The recent pilot scheme at Sunningdale Gardens was approved on the grounds of acquiring data with regard to energy efficiency and modern methods of construction which can be shared for learning across the entire NI social housing sector (as opposed to a primary focus of adding to supply only).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of single let providers in the last five years, broken down by (i) private landlord; (ii) private company; and (iii) estate agent.
answer_text: The data is not gathered in the format you have requested and therefore cannot be released.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether ending reliance on temporary accommodation will entail expanding the supply of permanent social housing.
answer_text: My Department is seeking to expand housing supply across all tenures.  The Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy provides the long-term framework for the transformational change needed to tackle our housing supply challenges.  This includes maximising delivery of new social homes through the Social Housing Development Programme and through ramping up delivery of Intermediate Rent homes.  Over the last three years my Department has provided around £520m to build new social homes: currently more than 5,500 social homes are under construction.However, we have an immediate need to accommodate households who are in crisis right now.  Approval for the Housing Executive to purchase up to 600 properties over the next 3 years for use as temporary accommodation will create considerable savings in the Homelessness budget and relieve wider pressures through a reduction in the use of, and reliance on, costly, non-standard accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to his Department’s strategic responsibility to protect and enhance the development of the Irish language at a community level, for his assessment of the importance of teaching Irish in English-medium education settings.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge have legislative responsibility for the promotion of the Irish language in this jurisdiction.In line with the establishing legislation, Foras na Gaeilge supports the Department of Education (DE) with Irish-medium education and the teaching of Irish in an educational setting, however DE retains overall responsibility for educational policy in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will include the teaching of languages at primary school level.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum including languages.  I am aware that Ms Crehan has met with a range of stakeholders to discuss languages including Irish as part of her work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will include the teaching of Irish in English-medium schools.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum including languages.  I am aware that Ms Crehan has met with a range of stakeholders to discuss languages including Irish as part of her work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out an assessment of the impact the loss of core grant funding has had on (i) the community and voluntary sector; and (ii) service delivery.
answer_text: In 2023/24, in response to an increasingly challenging financial position, the Department took the decision to reduce the Core Grant budget by 50%. That decision was covered by a full Equality Impact Assessment, informed by an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) consultation exercise which sought views on the Department’s assessment of the Budget.The Department has taken on board the comments received in the Equality Impact Assessment and will continue to use them to inform future budget decisions, should the opportunity arise to secure additional funding.In terms of the redesign of the Core Grant Scheme in partnership with the community and voluntary sector in 2024, a full Impact Assessment was not deemed necessary. This is primarily on the basis that the Scheme is still intended to contribute to the central administration costs of organisations and not to directly support the provision of health and social care services. The majority of my Department’s investment in the Community and Voluntary Sector is through HSC service delivery contracts, which are separate to the Core Grant Scheme.Delivery of the Scheme in practical terms was based on 12-month agreements with those involved. Each year applicants were advised that funding was subject to the Department’s final budget allocation each year, and there has therefore never been any guarantee of funding from one year to the next. Despite the ongoing budget challenges facing my Department, I remain committed to supporting the Community and Voluntary Sector, and where the wider Health allocation allows me to increase the Scheme budget in future years I will do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of a statement made by his Department to Nuacht TG4 on 14 March 2025 indicating that he would be happy to look at the Scoil Spreagha issue, whether he will accept a request for a meeting with Gael Linn to discuss the Scoil Spreagtha programme.
answer_text: I have agreed to meet with Patsy McGlone MLA and representatives from Gael Linn to discuss the Scoil Spreagtha scheme.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The data provided below reflects the number of complex grievances recorded by HR. Translink does not hold a central record of all informal / early stage grievances which are dealt with at a local level in the business. 2025 - 5 YTD 2024 - 14 2023 - 20 2022 - 16 2021-  31 In terms of the number of grievances received from the Trade Unions, we have recorded 6 in for the period 2020/21 to date, as broken down below: 2020/21 – 4 2024 – 1 2025 - 1 The Trade Unions regularly raise issues with Translink on a range of matters, but they are not normally raised or categorised as grievances.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the average hours completed by a Translink driver in the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Excluding optional overtime, the average weekly working time for a full time driver over the year is: Ulsterbus: 40.09 hours Metro/Glider: 40.48 hours Train:  38.84 hoursFor any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when bus services will commence for the Glenmona site in West Belfast.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25TotalPhysical Assaults112312202490Verbal Assaults413079131005253192937190As well as internal staff training and support on managing incidents of this nature, Translink  engages with key stakeholders including the PSNI and local political representatives as well as delivering its safety education and community outreach programme to help reduce incidents on Glider services. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any mitigations will be put in place to safeguard those organisations who were previously funded through his Department’s Core Grant Funding Scheme and have now lost that source of financial support.
answer_text: The delivery of the Department’s Core Grant Funding Scheme has been based on 12-month agreements with the Community and Voluntary organisations involved. Each year applicants have been advised that funding was subject to the Department’s final budget allocation each year.  There has therefore never been any guarantee of funding from one year to the next. Furthermore, in July 2024, I announced that 2024/25 would be the final year of the old, closed Core Grant Scheme. I made this announcement to offer time for organisations to prepare for the potential loss of Core Grant Funding. Continuing to offer funding to previous recipients would defeat the purpose of redesigning the Scheme to ensure that it is open, transparent and fair to all of those organisations working across the community and voluntary sector.
