question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any consideration he has given to expanding the categories of children eligible for short-term breaks under Article 18 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995; and (ii) whether his Department will introduce legislation or regulations to prescribe the inclusion of other categories of children who would benefit from such support.
answer_text: i. Article 18 of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Children Order) places a general duty on an HSC Trust to provide social care for children in need, their families and others. Section 121 of the Adoption and Children (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) amends Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order. The Act received Royal Assent in April 2022. When commenced, the amendments to the Children Order by way of section 121 of the Act will enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who normally resides at home, outside of the Looked After Children system. ii. Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Children Order will also allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, other categories of children for whom an HSC Trust may provide accommodation outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children. To determine which (if any) other categories of children this provision might be extended to in the future, my Department will consult publicly on any proposals. Section 121 of the Act also amends Article 18 of the Children Order to remove the phrase “exceptional” in relation to cash payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to adapting Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 in the North, to enable the provision of services, including accommodation, to children and families in need.
answer_text: Whilst the provisions of Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 do not apply to Northern Ireland, currently, under Article 18 of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), a Health and Social Care Trust has a duty to provide social care for children in need, their families and others.When commenced, section 121 of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will amend Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order to enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who resides normally at home, outside of the looked after children system.  Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Order will allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, other categories of children to whom accommodation may be provided outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children.  The power to provide accommodation is in addition to the provision of assistance in kind, or in cash.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of Foras na Gaeilge having a real cut of 45 per cent to their budget; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to ensure Foras na Gaeilge receives appropriate funding in the future.
answer_text: Like many ALBs and public sector bodies, the Language Body is facing into significant challenges arising from a difficult financial environment. My department provided around £3.6m in funding to Foras na Gaeilge in 2024 to allow them to meet their statutory responsibilities. I have no plans to reduce this funding in 2025.My Officials are working with Foras, and with our Irish co-sponsors, to understand the challenges that have arisen in Foras, to seek to understand the root cause of the issues and how best to respond in the short-term whilst identifying a longer-term solution that protects the funding ratios that were agreed when the NSLB was established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what proportion of Belfast Harbour’s target of 3,500 new homes by 2035 will consist of social housing.
answer_text: In January 2025, Belfast Harbour launched a five-year (2025-29) strategy, in which it was outlined that the Harbour was collaborating and engaging with Belfast City Council to support its local development plan and the Belfast Agenda.  The strategy outlines the Harbour’s support of the Belfast Agenda’s goal of 3,500 new homes in the Harbour Estate, including Titanic Quarter, by 2035.   This housing will include owner-occupied homes, build-to-rent, affordable homes, and live/workspaces for professionals. Belfast Harbour also plans to develop student accommodation as well as later living options. By the end of 2029, Belfast Harbour has stated that it aims to deliver 325 new homes - including 69 affordable homes - in the City Quays 4 development. A link to the Belfast Harbour Strategy is included for your convenience:Belfast Harbour | Strategy 2025-2029 - Advance Regional Prosperity

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether people who work in both the NHS and the independent sector will have access to the encompass system when they work from a non-NHS site.
answer_text: Currently access to the full encompass platform is only available to HSCNI employees.  Some groups, such as Primary Care professionals will have appropriate access to information held within encompass via EpicCare Link to check Secondary Care admissions, referrals, discharges, Emergency Department attendances, outpatients and some test results. In all cases, encompass can only be accessed using HSCNI verified equipment and registered HSCNI user accounts. Following the publication of the Neurology inquiry, the NI Department of Health recommended that all healthcare providers, both NHS and independent sector, have full and unhindered access to the integrated Electronic Care Record to facilitate patient safety. While there is active consideration of use of the platform for private patients and the independent sector, the encompass programme cannot proceed until there is an agreed policy in place, associate Information Governance issues are resolved and contractual and financial arrangements are in place. This work is being led from a Policy perspective by the DHCNI within the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there has been any oil product outages in Northern Ireland in the last five years.
answer_text: Our three local oil terminals (Puma, Exolum and LSS) have confirmed that there has not been an oil product outage in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration that has been given to data protection measures with the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: Data is stored within the Epic system, which is a secure, integrated digital health and care record platform and will utilise the dual Public Sector Data Centres (alongside other Trust and Regional systems) which are compliant with relevant data protection regulations.The encompass system employs Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to ensure staff can only access data relevant to their roles. RBAC is applied after user completed associated training, identified by Human Resources. All Health and Social Care (HSC) staff are contractually obligated to ensure that personal data is treated with the highest levels of confidentiality. Staff must adhere to Data protection Act 2018, UK GDPR, and the common law Duty of Confidentiality as set out in the HSC Code of Practice on Protecting the Confidentiality of Service User Information. A full audit trail of accesses is maintained within the system.In addition to this the encompass environment is subject to proactive 24/7 security monitoring and reporting to ensure that it is only accessed by known and suitably qualified HSCNI staff and from authorised equipment. The overall security of the encompass solution is independently verified and quality assured by accredited third party vendors on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any environmental impact assessments that take place before deciding whether to import hydrogen.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of the company importing hydrogen to assess the environmental impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the cost of hydrogen fuel for buses in the greater Belfast area, including shipping, in the last three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink has been operating 23 hydrogen buses in passenger service over the last 3 years. The requested information regarding hydrogen gas cost is regarded as commercially sensitive.              For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the spend on Translink Ulsterbus in the last five years; and (ii) the projected spend on Translink Ulsterbus in the next three years.
answer_text: The amount of Bus Route Subsidy for Ulsterbus as provided by the Department for Infrastructure in the last 5 years is outlined below.  Year Ending March 2025 £37.3mYear Ending March 2024 £46.8mYear Ending March 2023 £21.4mYear Ending March 2022 £44.9mYear Ending March 2021 £68.9mTranslink are currently preparing a 3-year corporate plan which will outline the budget required to adequately fund Ulsterbus, this remains work in progress at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how secure the transportation of hydrogen is when coming into the North.
answer_text: The transport of hydrogen by road comes under the remit of my department and is subject to the requirements of ADR (“European Agreement concerning the International Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Road”) which are implemented through The Carriage of Dangerous Goods and Use of Transportable Pressure Equipment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010 (as amended).  HSENI, within my Department, is the Competent Authority with regards to the transport of dangerous goods by road in the North. Enforcement of the Regulations is carried out by HSENI and other enforcement bodies who have a role under the Regulations. Those engaged in the transport of hydrogen are subject to additional ADR requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement and progress that has been made in relation to funding reform for North-South bodies.
answer_text: The paper on the North South Bodies – Alternative Funding Proposal was first circulated to Executive colleagues on 15 October 2024. I requested that the paper be tabled for discussion at the 6 February Executive meeting, and I am aware that my predecessor made a similar request on five occasions from November to January. I continue to press for this matter to be considered as it is a cross-cutting matter that requires Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any direct financial assistance that is available to people who are trying to escape situations of domestic and gender-based violence.
answer_text: My Department’s Discretionary Support scheme provides financial assistance to those in extreme, exceptional or crisis situations which present a significant risk to the health, safety or well-being of either the person making the claim or of their immediate family. This includes financial support for those trying to escape situations of domestic or gender-based violence.Claimants are required to apply to the scheme and satisfy eligibility criteria for consideration of an award. Further information on eligibility and how to apply can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/extra-financial-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any environmentally-friendly local alternatives, including green hydrogen, being considered instead of importing hydrogen.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy, ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’, plots the route to ending the use of fossil fuels and becoming self-sufficient in affordable renewable energy. Increasing levels of renewable electricity can be used for green hydrogen as part of wider measures to make our electricity system more flexible.	I am aware of several innovative, local companies that are working on green or low carbon hydrogen initiatives that could contribute to decarbonising and growing our green economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how data is collated to monitor the number of women who are in emergency accommodation due to a domestic violence situation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE has a statutory duty to provide temporary accommodation as outlined in the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988.All homelessness data is collated using information extracted from a Housing Management System whereby case level data entered as part of the assessment process allows the NIHE to provide overall numbers based on various parameters.Published data on temporary accommodation is accessible via the biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of women and children that have been placed in emergency housing to escape domestic abuse or gender-based violence in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024Section 2 of the accompanying tables provides data on households accepted as homeless by reason.While Section 3 provides data on temporary accommodation, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the advice provided to people who are trying to escape domestic violent situations, but no emergency accommodation is available.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE has a statutory duty to provide temporary accommodation as outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988.This duty is met through a range of options for those experiencing domestic abuse, including Women’s Aid refuges or, where this would not be a suitable placement (i.e. male applicants) or there is no availability, there are other options within the general portfolio of temporary accommodation which can be accessed. This includes options such as Single Lets, NIHE / Housing Association hostels, and other hostels within the voluntary sector which are funded by Supporting People, or, when there are no other options available, non-standard accommodation.In all cases involving domestic abuse the safety of the applicant is the primary consideration.  Referrals to floating support services including, but not limited to, Women’s Aid or Men’s Advisory Project may be made as appropriate to address any support needs arising as a result of domestic abuse.For individuals who are ineligible for assistance or are not otherwise owed the full housing duty under the Housing (NI) Order 1988, advice and assistance would be available. This can include information regarding self-referral hostels and floating support services.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22695/22-27, whether the specific proposal to change the current model of one-to-one support for pupils with special educational needs was made or voted on at SEN Programme Board.
answer_text: As part of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN), my department established a SEN Programme Board.  Flexibility in how Classroom Assistants could be deployed was discussed at those meetings on a number of occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 21278/22-27, for a breakdown of the location of the vacant and derelict properties.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 21278/22-27, I have set out below a breakdown of the location of the vacant, derelict and unused properties.  For the purpose of answering this question, I have defined a vacant property as one that is empty without contents whereas an unused property is defined as one that is empty but items/equipment remain.Vacant property owned or operated by DfC1.Plaza Building, 31-35 Chichester Street, Belfast 2.43-45 Falls Road, Belfast. 3.34 College Street, Belfast 4.Bankmore House, 62 Bedford Street, Belfast 5.Mount Eden War Rooms, 48 Mount Eden Park, Belfast, BT9 6RA6.Kirkistown Cottage, 250b Main Street, Cloughey, Co. Down7.Carrickfergus Depot, 42 Irish Quarter West Street, Carrickfergus8.Grosvenor Barracks, Coleshill Road, Enniskillen. 9.Former Cairn Lodge Boxing Club, Craven Street, Belfast10.208C & 208D Stewartstown Road, Poleglass, Dunmurry11.Unit 6 Stewartstown Road, Poleglass, Dunmurry12.16-24 Castlereagh Street, BelfastDerelict property owned or operated by DfC1.184 North Queen Street, Belfast2.7-7a Marquis Street, Belfast

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of emergency accommodation rooms and properties are available for (i) women; and (ii) women and children escaping domestic violence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE has a statutory duty to provide temporary accommodation as outlined in the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988.The 2024/25 Supporting People budget allocation for women (including those with children) at risk of domestic violence is £3.07m.  This funding is for 14 refuges with 138 units of support and for floating support provision (£2.14m) which delivers 9 services with a total of 1,003 units of support.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that Invest NI has owned the land at the former Mackies site, Belfast, for 25 years without attracting any inward investment, to detail (i) whether the land is still being held for strategic inward investment; and (ii) any steps Invest NI has been taking to secure that investment.
answer_text: The former Mackies lands comprise Invest NI’s Forthriver Business Park and this land is held to support economic growth projects from both indigenous and externally located businesses.  As with all property within the agency’s ownership the available land within the Business Park is marketed to potential investors through the agency’s commercial property database.   Final decisions on investment locations are, however, taken by investors in line with their business requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 21204 /22-27, to detail any correspondence his Department received from the Housing Executive before changing the policy on tenancy succession.
answer_text: The Department has had regular correspondence in relation to the development of an implementation approach and ultimately the final approval of changes to the Housing Selection Scheme in relation to Proposals 17 & 18 of the Fundamental Review of Allocations (Policy Successions).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19552/22-27, to detail any restrictions that will be placed on landlords who attempt to increase rent beyond the once a year limits from ending tenancy or proceeding with an eviction.
answer_text: When my Department commences this legislation, a landlord will not be able to increase rent more than once in any 12-month period from when a tenancy is granted or from the date of the last rent increase. As set out in AQW 19552/22-27, if a landlord attempts to increase rent more than once in every 12-month period, then this action will have no legal effect and a landlord cannot enforce the increase in court, nor can he harass the tenant, or he will be liable in the same way as if he tried to harass the tenant for any other money that is not due to him.Also, as previously detailed, the landlord cannot evict a tenant without following due process in law. This includes the tenant being served with the correct notice to quit, and my Department is developing regulations that will lengthen these notice periods considerably. It may also require the landlord to apply to the Court to commence ejectment proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether funding for house building and house refurbishment can be allocated under Financial Transactions Capital.
answer_text: ANSWERFinancial Transaction Capital funds can only be deployed by the public sector as a loan to, or equity investment in, a private sector entity.  The entity will then use the funding to invest in related infrastructure.  If administered in this way, funding for house building and house refurbishment can be allocated under Financial Transaction Capital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount allocated under the Home Loan Fund when it was open, broken down by organisation.
answer_text: The Department operated 2 separate home loans pilot schemes for Registered Housing Associations (RHAs) using Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) loan funding. The Affordable Home Loans Fund commenced in 2014/15 with funding allocated to 3 RHAs as per the table below. This scheme closed in 2022 and all loan funding has been fully repaid by the RHAs.Affordable Home Loans FundHousing AssociationLoan for new buildsLoan to refurbish empty homesChoice Housing£5m£0Clanmil Housing£3.2m0Apex Housing£6.1m£4.7mTOTAL                                  £19mA separate Empty Home Loans Fund commenced in 2014/15 with £9.2m FTC loan funding allocated to Clanmil RHA aimed specifically at bringing empty homes back into use as affordable homes for sale or rent. This loan is not due to be fully repaid until 2032/33.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has carried out a post-project evaluation of the Empty Home Loan Fund.
answer_text: The Department appointed Business Consultancy Services to carry out an independent Post Project Evaluation of the Empty Home Loan Fund.  This evaluation was completed in February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is able to apply for Financial Transactions Capital funding for the development of housing.
answer_text: My department has allocated £22m of Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding to Co-ownership in 2024-25, which supports the supply of intermediate housing for sale through the Co-Ownership scheme. Currently £158 million of funding, primarily FTC, has been provided to Co-Ownership to support over 4,400 households into homeownership between 2020-21 and 2024-25.In addition, my department has allocated £4m of FTC funding to the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA) scheme in 2024-25, which offers homelessness sector charities in Northern Ireland a low interest loan to purchase and manage affordable move-on accommodation for those who are deemed tenancy ready but may need some support to access or maintain that tenancy.For 2025-26  my department  has submitted the following housing related FTC bids:£37.5m Co-ownership£2m LAMA

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has set time-bound targets to reduce Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service waiting lists.
answer_text: The Department do not hold the information requestedThe demand for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) continues to outstrip capacity which is leading to increased waiting times across Trusts. The Department has not set time bound targets to reduce CAMHS waiting lists as this would require additional expenditure. The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with all Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.Departmental officials continue to monitor CAMHS performance at Trust level through the Support and Intervention Framework. Trusts performance varies due to acuity and workforce pressures.The HSC Support and Intervention Framework was issued in October 2024.  The new Framework sets out the Department’s approach for gaining assurance from HSC organisations in relation to the delivery of ministerial priorities and other key deliverables and the approach to support and intervention where there are matters of concern that need to be addressed.  It promotes a ‘no surprises’ approach and supports early identification of emerging issues and concerns, so that they can be addressed before they have a material impact or performance deteriorates further.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have raised the need for a fast-track scheme for Palestinian asylum seekers with the British Government.
answer_text: Decisions on the matters raised in the question are excepted.There is no joint position on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for patients to begin treatment with the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service following initial assessment in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department do not hold the information requestedThe demand for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) continues to outstrip capacity which is leading to increased waiting times across Trusts. The Department has not set time bound targets to reduce CAMHS waiting lists as this would require additional expenditure. The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with all Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.Departmental officials continue to monitor CAMHS performance at Trust level through the Support and Intervention Framework. Trusts performance varies due to acuity and workforce pressures.The HSC Support and Intervention Framework was issued in October 2024.  The new Framework sets out the Department’s approach for gaining assurance from HSC organisations in relation to the delivery of ministerial priorities and other key deliverables and the approach to support and intervention where there are matters of concern that need to be addressed.  It promotes a ‘no surprises’ approach and supports early identification of emerging issues and concerns, so that they can be addressed before they have a material impact or performance deteriorates further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients referred to the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service that have been waiting for longer than a year to begin treatment in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department do not hold the information requestedThe demand for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) continues to outstrip capacity which is leading to increased waiting times across Trusts. The Department has not set time bound targets to reduce CAMHS waiting lists as this would require additional expenditure. The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with all Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.Departmental officials continue to monitor CAMHS performance at Trust level through the Support and Intervention Framework. Trusts performance varies due to acuity and workforce pressures.The HSC Support and Intervention Framework was issued in October 2024.  The new Framework sets out the Department’s approach for gaining assurance from HSC organisations in relation to the delivery of ministerial priorities and other key deliverables and the approach to support and intervention where there are matters of concern that need to be addressed.  It promotes a ‘no surprises’ approach and supports early identification of emerging issues and concerns, so that they can be addressed before they have a material impact or performance deteriorates further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any oversight and monitoring mechanisms in place to ensure the impartiality of decisions taken in relation to agri-foods.
answer_text: My Department has an extensive co-design process in place with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key food and environmental stakeholders to ensure the impartiality of decisions in relation to the development of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). In particular, the Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group (APSG) brings together representatives across food, farming and the environment to ensure that stakeholder views are understood and properly considered during the development of the Programme. Importantly the policy development being undertaken is science and evidence led with a firm focus on data to help with decision making. As policy proposals within SAP are developed, they presented to APSG to seek its views and the Terms of Reference for this Group requires that Members declare any conflict of interest. Proposals that have been subject to this co-design process are presented to me for final decision. This process has brought a robustness and buy-in to the development process and has given me assurance, when I make the final decisions, that this programme will provide the right support for the industry and demonstrate good value for money for taxpayers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why the review of Invest NI’s investments is being carried out in-house rather than by an independent body.
answer_text: I am confident that Invest NI will carry out the review of investments in a robust and diligent manner, and that there is no need to allocate this task to an independent body.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all Public Private Partnership projects, including Private Finance Initiatives, completed in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the PFI contracts which ended in each of the past five years. YearProject NameProcuring Authority2019None2020None2021Managed Equipment Service (MES) / ATICSBelfast Health and Social Care Trust2022Classroom 2000 Local Area Network servicesDepartment of Education2023None2024Kinnegar WwTWNorthern Ireland Water LtdData on PFI projects is published annually by HM Treasury and the Infrastructure and Projects Authority. The most recent data is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/pfi-and-pf2-projects-2024-summary-data. Public Private Partnership (PPP) is a non-specific term covering a broad range of contractual arrangements between the public and private sectors. PFI contracts are the only form of PPP for which this information is centrally recorded by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of Belfast Harbour’s plan to be reclassified as a private corporation.
answer_text: Responsibilty for the reclassification of ports lies with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). I am aware that  DfI has a live consultation on ports policy and reclassification is one of the issues covered. Any  reclassification is an Office of National Statistics (ONS) classification change only, with no change to the status of the Harbour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the discrepancy between the number of vacant domestic properties recorded by Land and Property Services and the number recorded in the 2021 NI Census.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the Member to AQW 20193/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of times his departmental officials, previous Ministers or representatives of Invest NI have met with representatives of (a) RLC Group; (b) Survitec; and (c) Moyola Precision Engineering; and (ii) whether F-35 components were discussed during any of these meetings.
answer_text: Since I came into office, Invest NI officials have met RLC Group once and Moyola once but have not met with representatives of Survitec. 		At both these meetings media coverage in relation to F35 Components was discussed. Invest NI does not provide financial support for the manufacturing costs of specific products or components.  		The previous Economy Minister did not meet these companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the withdrawal of bus services in the Colin area of West Belfast due to concerns over drivers’ safety, whether any funding is being set aside to assist with community outreach to address the problem.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the First Minister or her junior Minister discussed any concerns with officials in relation to attending Washington DC on St. Patrick’s Day.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide further detail on the planned review of Invest NI’s investments, including (i) the scope of the review; and (ii) a timeline for completion.
answer_text: I have asked Invest NI to review the projects it has funded, to confirm that it has not supported projects involving the supply of arms to Israel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is considering re-establishing the Law Commission.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Law Commission has not functioned as a fully constituted body since April 2015, following the decision of the then Minister of Justice to wind it down due to the financial pressures facing the Department at that time. Significant budgetary pressures remain. While I very much want to see a fully functioning Commission, the wider financial environment facing my Department means that, in the short term, this is not possible. I continue to keep this issue under review and have recently met with the Lady Chief Justice and Justice Schofield, who is the current Chair of the Law Commission, to discuss the future of the Commission. I have asked my officials to undertake a feasibility study into the re-establishment of the Commission, which will be completed before the end of the current mandate and will assist in future decision making on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times the cross-departmental working group considering the Irish language strategy and action plan has met in the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times he has engaged with the Executive Office in relation to the appointment of an Irish Language Commissioner.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will publish his assessment of the report and recommendations produced by the Independent Review of Welfare Mitigations.
answer_text: The updated costs were shared with the Executive and the Communities Committee on 10 March 2025, and presented to the Assembly on 13 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any Irish language projects in the North can be funded outside of Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge is the primary body responsible for the promotion of Irish language in Northern Ireland and receives the majority of DfC Irish language funding.However, The North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, which established the North/South Language Body (NSLB), does not restrict language funding to the agencies of the NSLB therefore it is possible for DfC to provide funding for Irish language projects outside of Foras na Gaeilge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farmers that have had grant and subsidy payments reduced as a result of repeat pollution offences in each of the last five years.

department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he will publish details of the new draft framework, as agreed at the North South Ministerial Council in September 2024, for groups including Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: ANSWERThe alternative funding proposal was approved by the previous Finance Minister Dr Caoimhe Archibald.As it is a cross-cutting issue a paper was circulated to the Executive on 15 October 2024 and has been requested for inclusion at a number of subsequent Executive meetings.Once agreed, the Assembly will be advised of the new arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action his Department is taking to increase rates of prosecution for agriculture-related water pollution incidents.
answer_text: All reports of water pollution incidents continue to be investigated by NIEA. Where a source is identified, for Medium and High severity incidents, formal action is initiated according to the DAERA enforcement policy and, where the circumstances warrant, a file may be prepared for consideration by the Public Prosecution Service (PPS). Prosecution is one only option available to my Department in tackling water pollution incidents. Where issues are identified during Cross compliance farm inspections,  a monetary penalty may be applied which is linked to the environmental impact of the breach, and results in a reduction in the Basic Payment. In High and Medium severity incidents a prosecution may be taken forward under the Water Order (NI) 1999 as well as applying a monetary breach under Cross-Compliance.My Officials are liaising with the Department of Justice on a review of fines and penalties which is designed to ensure that fines reflect the harm caused to the environment. This is informing policy development for a forthcoming Fisheries Bill, which will be issued for public consultation in late Spring.The new Farm Sustainability Standards, which will replace the Cross-Compliance regime, includes a review of the penalty matrix to ensure that breaches of the Standards will result in monetary penalties which reflect the environmental impact.I hope to publish the consultation on the Nutrients Action Programme in the coming months. This will include additional measures to protect water quality from agricultural activities and applies to all agricultural land.I provided additional funding  to employ additional staff in regulation and enforcement roles in response to the blue-green algae issues in Lough Neagh. New catchment officers have undertaken over 120 inspections within the Lough Neagh catchment since October 2024. A number of discharges from a variety of sources have been identified and referrals made to relevant enforcement teams to take forward appropriate action under the DAERA enforcement policy.  Additional staff have also been employed to undertake focused cross compliance inspections within areas of highest risk.I have also included a number of actions in relation to education and incentivisation within the Lough Neagh Action Plan which are relevant across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Planning Appeals Commission public inquiry into the proposed gold mine in the Sperrins will be livestreamed when it resumes in March 2025.
answer_text: When a Departmental planning application is referred to the Planning Appeals Commission, responsibility for organising and putting administrative procedures in place for a public local inquiry or hearing is a matter for the Commission. Whilst the Commission did broadcast the pre inquiry meeting of March 2024, in a document published on the Commission website titled ‘Procedures for the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian Gold) conjoined Public Local Inquiries (Revised)’ dated September 2024, in part of paragraph [21] the Commission advise that  ‘there will be no live coverage on radio or television. The session will not be broadcast, nor will a recording be undertaken by PACWAC and it is not permitted for any other party to record or broadcast the event.’Any consideration whether to reinstate a livestream option is a matter for the Commission.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any correspondence received from (a) the First Minister; and (b) Junior Minister Reilly in relation to not attending St Patrick's Day events in Washington in March 2025; and (ii) any discussions that have taken place in their Department in relation  to this issue.
answer_text: No such correspondence exists. As with all invitations, discussions took place with officials regarding attendance and logistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of any role the agri-food sector has in relation to pollution in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) undertakes an extensive water quality monitoring programme within Northern Ireland’s rivers and lakes.NIEA’s river water body monitoring, indicates that 51% of river water bodies are impacted by agriculture, 26 % by sewage related pressures and a further 22 % impacted by both by sewage pressures and agricultural land use.  Research led by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute was published in October 2020 which examined substance flow analysis for phosphorus in the Northern Ireland’s food system.  Of all the phosphorus lost to water it was estimated that 62% was due to agricultural sources.Incidents recorded on NIEA’s Pollution Incidents Management System (PIMS), attributable to Farms and the Agri-Food Sector in the Lough Neagh Catchment in the last 5 years, are represented in the Table below.  The Lough Neagh Catchment incorporates 6 major river catchments including the Ballinderry River, the Moyola River, the River Blackwater, the Upper Bann River, the Braid/Maine Rivers and the Six Mile Water River. 	IndustryHigh*Medium*Low*TotalFarm16117327460Food industry0122941Farm + Food industry combined16129356501*environmental impact
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s plans to prevent agricultural planning fraud, including falsified soil samples.
answer_text: The prevention of planning fraud is primarily a matter for the planning authorities. However, as a statutory consultee to the NI planning process, my Department recognises that we have a part to play. Therefore my Department has set out a range of requirements for applicants to follow in the submission of information to support agricultural developments. This is designed to assist my officials in determining the authenticity of information submitted. These requirements are set out on the DAERA website at:Nutrient Management Plan | Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the membership of the steering group that advises the independent panel on the Review of Environmental Governance; (ii) the terms of reference for the group; (iii) the role of the group; and (iv) the organisations the group represents.
answer_text: The Stakeholder Reference Group (SRG) was established in January 2025 by the independent panel leading the review of environmental governance and comprises the following members/organisations:Ulster Farmers Union – Aileen LawsonMid Ulster District Council - Anne-Marie CampbellNI Environment Link - Max BryantGreen Alliance - Ruth ChambersMaynooth University - Mary DobbsBusiness in the Community NI - Keelin McConeDairy Council NI - Ian StevensonNature Friendly Farming Network - Cormac DolanNo terms of reference were provided to the SRG as their sole purpose is to support the work of the independent panel by sharing knowledge and expertise and by providing input on any relevant matters, when requested by the panel. I would highlight again that the review of environmental governance is being led by the independent panel and I will not be seeking to influence this in any way, including how the panel engages and utilises the SRG.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to work with the Minister for Communities to collect data about poor housing conditions, including damp, mould and overcrowding, in order to analyse impacts on public health.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount paid to the independent sector and private sector for procedures to reduce hospital waiting lists in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Our health and social care system remains under significant pressure, with the growing demand and capacity gap leading to unacceptably long waiting times for patients across many of our services.Expenditure on the independent health care sector in each of the last five years as advised by HSC Trusts is set out in the table below.   BHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTSpend £ (m)2019/20£13.5£2.6£5.8£2.2£0.22020/21£9.6£1.8£9.6£4.6£1.62021/22£22.8£5.1£14.6£7.5£9.02022/23£22.2£12.6£14.9£12.1£13.02023/24£17.3£10.9£15.7£11.1£8.6Where relevant, this will include diagnostic reportingThis investment has delivered almost 550,000 direct patient care assessments, treatments and diagnostic tests in the independent sector over this five year period.My Department will continue to seek every opportunity to secure additional funding to address long waiting times. However, investment is fundamental to addressing the backlog problem we are facing.  We need a long-term collective effort with sustained and substantial investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consultation he has undertaken with education unions and education support workers in relation to his proposals to change the current model of one-to-one support for pupils with special educational needs.
answer_text: As part of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN), my department established a SEN Programme Board which included representatives from teaching unions.  Flexibility in how Classroom Assistants could be deployed was discussed at those meetings.Also, a programme of stakeholder engagement was undertaken which included workshops being held – Classroom Assistants and SEN Co-ordinators (SENCos) attended. Officials also delivered a presentation to teaching and non-teaching unions on the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department gathers information of contract works not completed by contractors on Housing Executive properties.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Housing Executive maintenance and improvement contracts contain clauses where works are only paid upon completion of each individual property. The Housing Executive carries out sample of inspections before any work is deemed to be complete and payment is made.   If any anomalies are found in these assurance checks, further inspections are carried out. Monies will be held or recovered from the contractor in these situations. If the member, or a tenant is aware of any instance where work has not been completed, they should contact the Housing Executive directly.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20493/22-27, to detail (i) the planned installation timeline of Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) technology at the (a) Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children; and (b) Belfast City Hospital; (ii) whether the expedited installation of ANPR technology at specialist cancer treatment centres for children and young people is being considered due to the significant cost implications for these patients; and (iii) any interim measures being explored to alleviate parking costs for children and young people up to the age of 25 with cancer and thei
answer_text: (i) (a) Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children – Site works began on 19 February 2025 and will be completed by 28 April 2025, totalling 49 days of works on site. Installation of ANPR technology will occur during the period 21 April 2025 and 23 April 2025.(b) Belfast City Hospital – Site works will begin on 5 March 2025 and will be completed on 9 May 2025, totalling 48 days of works on site. Installation of ANPR technology will occur during the period 29 April 2025 and 2 May 2025. (ii) In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, free hospital car parking comes into operation on the 12 May 2026. All relevant equality impact assessments have been completed to date in relation to ANPR enforcement. The Belfast Trust will communicate directly with MLAs in the near future regarding an invitation to meet to discuss the Trust’s plans in relation to the implementation of ANPR technology and to discuss the potential impact of ANPR controls on constituents.(iii) The existing departmental policy for Car Parking Provision and Management (2012) includes guidance on free and concessionary parking provision for patients on particular care paths. Free parking for patients, next of kin / partner, relatives / visitors for radiotherapy / chemotherapy, renal dialysis and critical care / high dependency care paths is currently provided. HSC Trusts are also expected to exercise discretion to apply the eligibility criteria in other situations where the duration and / or frequency of visits lead to significant parking charges. In addition, the Hospital Travel Costs Recovery Scheme allows patients who fulfil specified low-income criteria to reclaim car parking charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of repairs for Housing Executive properties are currently outstanding.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The total number of repairs currently outstanding on Housing Executive properties as at 17th February, 2025 is 18,703.This figure has been exacerbated by the aftereffects of Storm Eowyn (24th January 2025) where the NIHE received 13,958 repair requests in week following the storm. This was an increase of 135% above the average weekly total for Jobs issued to Contractors over the previous 52 weeks. To add further context the NIHE carried out approximately 254,000 maintenance repairs to its 83,000 properties during the financial year 23/24.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the proportion of children living in poverty after housing costs in 2023-24, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for Northern Ireland (NI) are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Due to the sample size of the FRS my Department does not publish poverty levels for children below the NI level.  The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) produce the “Children in Low Income Families: Local Area Statistics” (CiLIF). The CiLIF provides information on the number and proportion of children living in relative and absolute poverty before housing costs by local area across the United Kingdom. The latest published statistics for CiLIF relate to the 2022/23 financial year. Alongside the release of 2023/24 CiLIF statistics in March 2025, DWP plan to publish a paper on proposals for developing After Housing Costs measures of local area child poverty statistics and will be seeking users’ views.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the proportion of children living in poverty after housing costs in 2022-23, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for Northern Ireland (NI) are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Due to the sample size of the FRS my Department does not publish poverty levels for children below the NI level.  The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) produce the “Children in Low Income Families: Local Area Statistics” (CiLIF). The CiLIF provides information on the number and proportion of children living in relative and absolute poverty before housing costs by local area across the United Kingdom. The latest published statistics for CiLIF relate to the 2022/23 financial year. Alongside the release of 2023/24 CiLIF statistics in March 2025, DWP plan to publish a paper on proposals for developing After Housing Costs measures of local area child poverty statistics and will be seeking users’ views.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why the Housing Executive's policy on exceptional circumstances in relation to succession is not publicly available.
answer_text: The policy on exceptional circumstances in relation to succession is publicly available on the Housing Executive’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GPs that have been trained in suicide prevention in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) Local Commissioning Group area; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department do not hold lists or information regarding GPs and their training.  As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including mental health.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.  GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his comments in the Assembly on 10 February 2025 that the latest estimated cost for a carer recognition payment is £90.18 million over a three-year period from 2025-26, to detail the source of this figure.
answer_text: The source of the estimate is internal analysis provided by officials from DfC Analytics Division.

To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on progress of the Intermediate Rent Scheme, launched in May 2024, including the next steps for the scheme.
answer_text: I refer you to my statement on 5th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will ensure that the extended Protect Life 2 Strategy targets resources in line with objective need.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Strategy will continue to ensure suicide prevention services and support are delivery appropriately in deprived areas where suicide and self-harm rates are highest.In developing the new Action Plan for the Strategy there was very strong support to retain the existing Strategy principles. These highlight the importance of actions being person-centred, tailored to diverse populations and are evidence based where possible. The new Action Plan will contain a wide range of actions to reduce the rate of suicide in Northern Ireland. The actions will be split into five new themes: A Whole of Government and Society Approach; Awareness and Prevention; Supporting Compassionate Responses to Suicide; Supporting Compassionate Responses to Self Harm; and finally, Research, Evidence and Planning. The actions underpinning these themes will contain a wide range of deliverable actions over the coming years and which are targeted to make as much impact as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will work with other Departments and relevant agencies to provide a deliverable commitment to raise the parapets on (i) the Divis and Westlink Bridge, Belfast; and (ii) the Foyle Bridge, Derry, including information on time frame for commencement and completion of work.
answer_text: With regards to measures for the Westlink Bridges at Clifton Street and Divis Street, I can confirm that officials are currently reviewing the business case for the project which indicates an estimated investment of £1.8m.  Although budgets for next year are not known, should funding be available, it is hoped that measures could be in place early 2026. In relation to Foyle Bridge, my Department recognises how raising the parapet height at this location could contribute to the wider health objectives of improving the safety and wellbeing of vulnerable individuals within local communities.  However, before any work can be carried out on the bridge, a detailed structural model will need to be developed to ensure that any changes to the parapets do not compromise the structural integrity of the bridge. The model is estimated to cost around £420,000, and the subsequent works to install a barrier may cost in the region of £10m.The Department will continue to work with all stakeholders to identify funding to complete the structural assessment to determine if increasing the barrier heights at Foyle Bridge is possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will urgently publish a revised reduction target for the Protect Life 2 Strategy.
answer_text: Yes, a new baseline will shortly be set as part of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan which is currently being finalised. The existing 10% reduction target will then apply to that revised baseline. I believe every suicide is preventable up to the point of completion, so while we may set realistic targets, the only acceptable number is zero. I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will make a statement to the Assembly on NISRA’s Suicide Statistics 2023, including an analysis of overall patterns.
To ask the Minister of Health how he will ensure that the extended Protect Life 2 Strategy is fully funded.
answer_text: The Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention has been re-established and the first meeting is scheduled to take place soon. This group will provide a renewed focus for all Ministers on suicide and self-harm prevention. It is my clear intention to seek to work collaboratively with Ministers across all Departments in these matters as the social determinants of mental health are the same as those which determine physical health outcomes. It is essential to address these social determinants to improve both the physical and mental health outcomes of our population. Responsibility for many of these determinants fall to other Executive Ministers, and so a joined up, co-ordinated approach is vitally important. I have made it very clear that additional funding is critical for both the Protect Life 2 and Mental Health Strategies, as these are closely interlinked. I will continue to press across the Executive Table to secure this vital funding.Unfortunately, the 2025-26 draft budget allocation to Health won't be sufficient to cover the significant increases in cost which we will face as a result of pay and price inflation, increased National Insurance contributions for GPs, pharmacists and social care providers and rising demand. This means we are faced with a significant shortfall in funding and while I would like to be able to direct additional investment in services such as the Protect Life 2 Strategy, instead we will again be fighting to identify sufficient savings to meet the pay expectations of our workforce and protect the services that we currently have.I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an overview of issues pertaining to the PSNI that are not classed as operational matters and are within her Ministerial remit to comment on.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is responsible for the strategic policy, legislative and resourcing framework within which policing operates.  It is also the sponsor Department for the PSNI, the Northern Ireland Policing Board and the wider family of policing-related organisations.  It sets long-term objectives for the policing of Northern Ireland which must be taken into account by the Policing Board in setting its own objectives for policing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on introducing right to roam legislation.
answer_text: I support responsible access to the countryside and the provision of opportunities for recreation given the range of benefits this provides. However, it is important we balance the provision of access and outdoor recreation opportunities with the needs of farmers and other rural dwellers, given the potential for disturbance. Given the shortened mandate, I am unable to introduce new access legislation in the time remaining.  The Environmental Improvement Plan Outcome 2 is for a ‘healthy and accessible environment and landscapes everyone can connect with and enjoy’.  It includes a series of actions and targets relating to outdoor recreation including the development of a new outdoor recreation strategy and the development of revised/new outdoor recreation legislation by 2028.  I plan to begin scoping and preparatory work during this mandate, in collaboration with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether suicide is a major and urgent public health crisis.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has collected any data on increased notice to quit periods and the impact on tenants.
answer_text: The Department commissioned independent research from the Chartered Institute of Housing (CIH) on the Notice to Quit periods, with a report published in November 2021. https://www.cih.org/media/kdkch00v/private-rented-tenancies-ntq-report-ni-final.pdfDuring the passage of the Private Tenancies Bill, now the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022, an 8-week consultation was held on length of notice to quit periods including the need for exceptions. Link to consultation, including the final report below: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/consultations/consultation-notice-quitThe Department commissioned further research into exceptions to longer notice to quit periods, including circumstances when shorter notice periods would apply and what these notice periods should be. My Department will publish this report when the draft regulations are issued for consultation. I anticipate the consultation will commence in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of buy to let mortgages currently in place.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether an assessment has been made of the Community Right to Buy scheme in Scotland; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to introduce a similar scheme.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the Community Right to Buy Scheme in Scotland but has not made any formal assessment of it.  The introduction of a similar scheme would be a matter for consideration by the Executive Committee. An evaluation of the impact of Community Asset Transfer in Northern Ireland will be commissioned by my Department during 2025/26.  The evaluation will examine the impact and effectiveness of the Community Asset Transfer process and make recommendations to inform the future delivery of this Executive policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of land that is privately owned.
answer_text: Land Registry does not retain records on land that is privately owned and is unable to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact that the increase in the cost of land has had on the cost of (i) rent; and (ii) mortgages.
answer_text: The purchase price of suitable land is a component of the overall development costs of a new housing development, and a developer will factor this into feasibility work to identify if a development is viable and deliverable.  A long development pipeline means many factors will impact the housing market between when land is purchased, and property is available to rent or buy to make an irrefutable link with the cost of land. Rents and mortgages are influenced by a wide range of factors including location, supply and demand, property condition and size, interest rates, mortgage interest rates, inflation, the general state of the economy, and the financial status of applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total number of staff currently working in Land and Property Services, broken down by section.
