question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of land owned by the public sector that has been (i) sold; and (ii) transferred to other bodies in the last ten years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) can confirm that since the financial year 2017/2018 a total number of 615 public sector assets which were declared surplus have been transferred through the Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property (D1) process as follows:141 within the public sector146 on the open market328 to former/adjoining ownerLPS is unable to confirm the full extent of land owned by the public sector but is collating this data under the Government Land & Property Register (GLPR) project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has an aggregate value of land.
answer_text: No. Land values at any one time reflect normal market forces of supply, demand, planning permission, quality, location and other economic factors. Land values are only one element of a compensation claim which is submitted and negotiated by an agent on behalf of a claimant who has had land acquired under compulsory purchase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Land Compensation Act 1961 applies in the North.
answer_text: The Land Compensation Act 1961 does not apply here.The procedures for assessing compensation here are derived from what is commonly referred to as the “Compensation Code”. This is made up of Acts of Parliament, case law and established practice including The Lands Tribunal and Compensation Act (NI) 1964, the Planning and Land Compensation Act (NI) 1971, the Land Acquisition and Compensation (NI) Order 1973, the Land Compensation (NI) Order 1982 and the Planning Blight (Compensation) (NI) Order 1981.My Department publishes compulsory purchase and compensation guides on its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 applies in the North.
answer_text: The Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 extends to England and Wales only.The Planning (Use Classes) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 applies in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to the consultations on revenue raising, to outline what steps his Department has taken to ensure the measures are fair and equitable.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any requirements in place to notify the public in relation to the buying and selling of land.
answer_text: The provisions of the Stormont Regulation and Government Property Act (Northern Ireland) 1933 (The Stormont Act) enables Government departments to acquire and dispose of property. Land & Property Services (LPS) manages, oversees and provides advice in relation to the disposal process.There are two pathways to disposal, initially via the internal market to a Public body or Third Sector Organisation (TSO), and if no potential ‘internal’ purchaser is identified, then via the external market either to a former owner or adjoining owner in the private sector, or by way of an open market sale.Open market sales will include full exposure of the asset to the open market including online and press advertising. Details of surplus assets being sold on the open market are also published quarterly on the Department of Finance website.  The acquisition of land from the public, by Government departments and other Public sector bodies, is not managed by the Department of Finance or LPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the establishment of a land commission, similar to the body in Scotland, to ensure land is owned and used in ways that are fair, responsible and productive.
answer_text: The Scottish Land Commission is a non-departmental public body established under the Land Reform (Scotland) Act 2016. Its role is primarily advisory. It seeks to provide leadership and advice in reforming the ownership and use of land in Scotland. There is no equivalent body in this jurisdiction. Certain functions relating to land ownership have been carried previously by the Law Commission, as part of its broader law reform remit. However, the Commission has not been active since 2016 and decisions to restore its full functions are a matter for the Minister of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of the cost of land; and (ii) to detail any measures he is considering for a reduction or freeze in the cost of land.
answer_text: The cost of land at any one time reflects normal market forces of supply, demand, quality, location and other economic factors. I have no plans to bring forward measures to the Executive aimed at reducing or freezing the cost of land.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff in Land and Property Services in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Please see the table below for the number of staff in Land & Property Services on 31st March for each of the last five years.YearTotal Number of Staff31st March 2024119431st March 2023118831st March 2022120231st March 2021110431st March 20201135

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether Land and Property Services hold a list of all individuals who own farmland.
answer_text: Farmland is not rateable in local rating legislation, so Land and Property Services (LPS) does not hold any information for rates purposes of individuals who own farmland.Where land is registered with the Land Registry, it is possible, on payment of the relevant search fee, to confirm the title owner for individual parcels of land. The use to which land is put is not designated in Land Registry records.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the reserves held by Land and Property Services.
answer_text: Land and Property Services (LPS) operates as a business area within the Department of Finance (DoF) and as such its income is recorded in the Annual Departmental Resource Accounts. LPS is allocated opening Resource and Capital budgets by the Department as part of the annual budget allocation process each financial year. Allocated Budget will take account of forecast income from LPS products and services and planned expenditure. Under spend in LPS budget is returned to the Consolidated Fund, via the Department. LPS also prepares an annual Trust Statement to account for revenue generated through the Rating system. Note 8 of the Trust Statement of Rate Levy 2023/24 discusses the balance due to the Consolidated Fund. At the end of March 2024 this consisted of the net value of assets, less liabilities and amounted to £104,918K.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of cases of damp and mould that were found in homes in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and Housing Associations for social homes. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Across NIHE’s 83,000 homes, within the last financial year (1/4/23 – 31/3/24) they have received around 556,000 repair requests of which 17,198 mentioned issues with condensation or damp. This represents 3.1% of all repair requests. Please see table below which outlines cases reported to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive of Mould, Damp and Condensation, by the tenant, or someone acting on their behalf, as requested for the past five financial years, broken down by Council Area.  Council Area Reported Date - Financial Year  2019/2020 2020/2021 2021/2022 2022/2023 2023/2024 Grand Total ANTRIM AND NEWTOWNABBEY 820 640 860 1,088 1,242 4,650 ARDS AND NORTH DOWN 827 622 790 924 1,078 4,241 ARMAGH CITY BANBRIDGE AND CRAIGAVON 956 718 894 1,132 1,652 5,352 BELFAST 2,915 2,496 2,731 3,481 4,776 16,399 CAUSEWAY COAST AND GLENS 744 566 790 964 1,307 4,371 DERRY CITY AND STRABANE 1,043 462 1,140 1,479 1,550 5,674 FERMANAGH AND OMAGH 353 266 367 500 679 2,165  LISBURN AND CASTLEREAGH  590  522  558  703  1,225  3,598 MID AND EAST ANTRIM 756 625 805 1,242 1,791 5,219 MID ULSTER 358 265 368 428 484 1,903 NEWRY MOURNE AND DOWN 779 594 773 976 1,414 4,536 Grand Total 10,141 7,776 10,076 12,917 17,198 58,108 For any future queries relating to NIHE operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.”Registered Housing Associations reported that 2,687 cases of damp and mould were found in homes in 2023/24. The Department does not hold this information for previous years. The NIHE also undertakes the House Condition Survey which assesses the condition and energy efficiency in properties across all tenures. The survey was last published in 2018. Further details on the current status of the HCS can be accessed at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/house-condition-survey

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Privately Owned Public Spaces.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) does not hold information in relation to Privately Owned Public Spaces and is unable to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of land not registered with Land and Property Services.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 14730/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the health impact on people who have been evicted is being monitored.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely record data on patients who are evicted or have been made homeless.A GP practice may record details about homelessness/ eviction within the free text of clinical notes, but these are not coded specifically.  As a result, it would not be possible to gauge the impact of evictions or homelessness through data from GPs clinical systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered implementing a scheme for property swapping beyond Housing Executive tenants to deal with the housing crisis.
answer_text: A property swapping scheme exists for social housing tenants. Details of the scheme are available at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/apply-for-a-home/transfers-and-exchangesI have not considered implementing any scheme beyond this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 20600/22-27, to detail any role the security services have in the (i) administration; and (ii) determination of the Victims' Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Scheme (TPDPS) is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice and is an arm’s-length body of The Executive Office.  My Department is responsible for ensuring that the necessary administration arrangements are in place to support the VPB in making determinations on applications to the TPDPS.    The Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020, which set out the administration arrangements for the TPDPS, were introduced by the previous UK Government in 2020.  I cannot comment on matters relating to the Security Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of new build homes since 2016 that are (i) second homes; (ii) Airbnb properties; and (iii) another form of holiday home.
answer_text: My Department does not hold any data to answer this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of homes that are (i) overcrowded; and (ii) undercrowded.
answer_text: Census 2021 reported 768,810 occupied households.  Of those, 85,362 were classified as overcrowded, with 535,520 classified as undercrowded – based on the Occupancy Rating measure.The Occupancy Rating measure utilises the ages of household members and their relationships to each other to derive the number of rooms they require. This is then combined with the number of rooms in a household's accommodation to obtain the rating. For example, an occupancy rating of -1 or less implies that a household has fewer rooms than required (i.e. overcrowding) – whereas a rating of 1 or more implies that they have more rooms than required (i.e. undercrowding).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration he has given to reviewing the rating policy toward landlords who have vacant homes that are not in use.
answer_text: For a person to be referred to as a landlord of a property this entails that the property is either let or being actively marketed for let with a view to occupation. For properties not actively let, and which are deemed vacant for rating purposes, full rates liability will apply under either rating of empty homes legislation or landlord liability provisions within the rating legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the increasing numbers of children growing up in temporary accommodation, including impacts on the (i) education; (ii) physical health; and (iii) mental health of children.
answer_text: A safe and secure home is the foundation for everything else, including good physical and mental health and better educational attainment. Prolonged stays in temporary accommodation are not ideal, particularly for families and children. While there has been an increase in the number of children in temporary accommodation, to mitigate against the worst impacts of homelessness, the Housing Executive have sought to ensure the vast majority of families are placed in ‘own front door accommodation’ such as Single Lets or supported placements such as voluntary sector hostels. 97% of children in temporary accommodation are currently in own front door accommodation. For those who are not, the Housing Executive will seek to identify alternative options at the earliest possible opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink is accountable to the Public Services Ombudsman.
answer_text: I can confirm that Translink is not accountable to the Public Services Ombudsman

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when a Translink passenger engagement session last took place.
answer_text: In September 2024 Translink s recently introduced a new twice yearly online focus group for engaging with passengers.  The last of the old Passenger Panel meetings took place in August 2024.  In addition, Translink has a Youth Forum with the latest engagement session taking place across 5 days in five locations across the north in November 2024. The programme is in a transitional period, with Translink recruiting young people to join the youth forum. The first session is due to take place in March 2025. Aswell as the focus groups and Youth forums Translink also has a Continuous Customer Satisfaction Monitoring Programme, as part of the Public Service Agreement with my Department. This seeks independent views through ongoing customer surveys with 6,000 responses per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department’s discussions with Belfast city council on using vacant properties for accommodation.
answer_text: LPS have been supporting the work of Belfast City Council on the H.O.U.S.E Programme (Homes On Upper Spaces for Everyone). Details of the scheme are included below:https://yoursay.belfastcity.gov.uk/house-programme The Expression of Interest phase has now closed and Belfast City Council are now undertaking further engagement, including with social housing associations, statutory organisations and potential funders to determine how they could play a part in future delivery.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) whether the Financial Assistance for Political Parties Scheme 2024 was subject to any assessment in relation to its impact on smaller political parties and the fair allocation of resources; and (ii) whether any other options were considered.
answer_text: Different approaches have been taken to the Financial Assistance to Political Parties (FAPP) Scheme during its existence.  The changes approved by the Assembly on 4 June 2024, followed a long process of consideration by the Assembly Commission.  As part of its consideration, the Assembly Commission also reviewed the arrangements in other legislatures.		Officials consulted with Assembly Commission Members during March and April 2021.  Responses were first considered at the Assembly Commission Meeting on 9 September 2021.  Alongside the general provision of funding to parties, the Assembly Commission also had to consider the provision of funding to the Opposition.  It decided to bring forward a scheme which allocated funding based on the number of Members per party.	The impact on smaller political parties was considered throughout the review process and for that reason, it was agreed that the introduction of the 2024 Scheme should not negatively impact smaller political parties.  The 2024 Scheme included financial protection for any party that received a higher amount under extant 2016 Scheme. 		An Equality Impact Assessment was not required on this, or previous occasions when the FAPP Scheme was reviewed.  FAPP is a statutory Scheme that provides financial assistance for political parties under an Act of the Assembly.  		Revisions to the FAPP Scheme are subject to a political agreement at the Assembly Commission with the involvement of all of the main parties.  It is then subject to a vote on the floor of the Assembly.  All of the main 	stakeholders are involved in the decision and are able to make a direct contribution throughout the process, including on the floor of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to provide details on their preparedness plan for Storm Éowyn; (ii) whether they will release the plan; and (iii) to detail the number of staff in their Department working on future plans for extreme weather conditions.
answer_text: The Executive Office co-ordinates civil contingencies planning across the Executive.At the regional level, the Framework lays out arrangements for effective emergency management regarding preparing for, responding to, and recovering from an emergency across Government, Local Government and responding agencies. It is underpinned by operational plans, owned by each Department which are specific to each potential emergency.  There are 4 staff in the Civil Contingencies Policy Branch working with Government Departments on future plans for extreme weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount Translink has spent on (i) contractors; (ii) legal fees; and (ii) consultants fees associated with the removal of the Boyne Bridge, Belfast.
answer_text: Costs associated with the removal of the Boyne Bridge (including contractors, legal fees and professional services) are in the region of £700,000

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount (i) his Department; and (ii) Translink spent on legal fees in 2024.
answer_text: The amounts spent by my Department and Translink on legal costs in the 2023-24 financial year are set out in the table below. The amounts represent the cost of legal services provided by the Department of Finance’s Departmental Solicitors Office, external solicitors and barristers, the cost of outlays associated with legal cases and plaintiffs’ legal costs for out of court settlements or where costs were awarded against my Department.Legal costs2023/24£000Department4,889Translink354

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the 2021 census figures detailing there are 52,300 vacant households, to detail the number of buildings this encompasses.
answer_text: The Census 2021 reported 52,300 vacant household spaces (i.e. household spaces with no usual residents). This figure corresponds to approximately 47,400 buildings. Since a building can contain multiple household spaces (e.g. an apartment block), the count of buildings refers to those where there is at least one vacant household space.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths of children living in temporary accommodation in each of the last five years, broken down by cause of death.
answer_text: The information requested is not available. NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. While address at time of death is captured as part of the registration process, there is no way to determine if this was a temporary accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the role of the proposed New Homes Ombudsman; and (ii) whether the Ombudsman will prioritise the delivery of homes for people with the greatest objective need.
answer_text: The New Homes Ombudsman Service (NHOS) which will operate across the UK, was established in 2022. It was created as part of the Building Safety Act 2022.  The NHOS is overseen by the New Homes Quality Board (NHQB) which is a non-profit organisation dedicated to improving the quality of new build private homes and customer service provided by housing developers. The NHOS provides a free, independent service to help customers resolve issues with new build private homes which the Registered Developer has been unable or unwilling to fix. This can include the Reservation, Legal Completion and complaints management processes, or issues or defects that have arisen at or after occupation and which are not major defects during the first two years of a new home purchase.The NHOS has no wider role in the delivery of homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive is developing, or has developed, a business case in relation to the buyback of former Housing Executive homes to expand the supply of permanent social housing stock.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is not developing such a business case.I have recently provided approval for the Housing Executive to start a process to buy up to 600 homes over a three-year period from the private market to address current Temporary Accommodation pressures across Northern Ireland.The primary aim of that work is on reducing the use and high cost of expensive Temporary Accommodation such as hotel and B&Bs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the Housing Executive’s tenancy succession policy was last subject to an equality impact assessment.
answer_text: The main provision for succession is statutory in nature and stems from the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1983.  Prior to the Fundamental Review of Allocations, the Housing Executive advises that the Scheme had been subject to full Equality Impact Assessment in 2007. In 2017 the Department published a draft EQIA to accompany the public consultation on the proposals for change under the Fundamental Review of Allocations. The final EQIA was published alongside the consultation outcome report in 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide examples of the very exceptional circumstances that a Housing Executive Designated Officer will examine when deciding to exercise discretion in granting a policy succession of a tenancy.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “As the name would indicate it is not possible to be prescriptive about what will constitute very exceptional circumstances, and it is case and facts specific.  It is, however, important to note the inclusion of the word very here and it is considered where individuals have no entitlement whatsoever under the specified statutory or policy criteria.Set alongside this, the preferred and fairest way to allocate tenancies is through assessment via the Housing Selection Scheme which ranks all applicants on the basis of housing need. In short very exceptional circumstances awards are and should be used very sparingly and reserved for cases where there is compelling evidence to, in effect, ignore the claims of all those on the wating list to award a tenancy outside of normal procedures and practice.  All the information and evidence supplied by the customer alongside that available on Housing Executive systems and records will be considered. All of this must be balanced against the needs of those on the waiting list to come to a decision.Examples of information which may be included (it is important to note that this list is not exhaustive or designed to cover every eventuality and that any of the things noted below will be considered on a cumulative basis):The length of any established or evidenced residence at the property.The occupation level of the property.The size, design of and demand for the property in question.The reasons why succession is not possible under statutory or policy criteria.The circumstances of the applicant which may include any vulnerabilities and how this makes the current property particularly suitable for them.If the applicant has a high housing need.Any other relevant factor.As noted above all this will be considered against needs of people on the waiting list.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether Housing Executive tenants applying for a tenancy succession (i) receive adequate notification and information about the evidence needed to grant a policy succession; and (ii) are granted sufficient time to provide this evidence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “The information which is required or may be relevant with regard to each applicant will differ depending on the circumstances of the applicant in question.  Following application an interview takes place with every succession applicant where the process is explained to them. In most cases prior notice is given that evidence of residency will be required but other information or evidence is case and fact dependent. As the precise circumstances emerge during the succession interview the applicant is informed of what information or evidence will be required to help evaluate their case. We understand that some of this information may take time to assemble and there is no specified time frame to provide evidence in relation to an initial decision. It must of course be provided within a reasonable time but staff have discretion to determine what a reasonable time is, depending on the circumstances of the case and the nature and type of evidence required.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider ring-fencing Housing Association Grant funding for areas of acute housing need.
answer_text: I have no plans to consider ring-fenced grant funding for housing associations. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive have a Strategic Guidelines framework for identifying social housing need across Northern Ireland, by geographic area, urban or rural location and by customer grouping.  The primary function of which is to facilitate programme development and planning to ensure the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) is targeted objectively and distributed through an evidence-based approach whilst allowing budget flexibility. Ring fencing of budget is therefore not Departmental policy as it would restrict part of the SHDP budget so that it may only be spent on schemes in certain areas. An arrangement such as this is unlikely to expand the programme’s activity in designated areas and would reduce the flexibility the SHDP currently has to stretch its already-challenged capacity to deliver both against starts’ and spend targets. A Ring fencing approach risks delivering fewer units and underspending budget whilst not achieving greater targeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration (i) his Department; and (ii) the Housing Executive has given to buying back former Housing Executive homes.
answer_text: I have recently provided approval for the Housing Executive to start a process to buy up to 600 homes over a three-year period from the private market to address current Temporary Accommodation pressures across Northern Ireland. The identification of any properties deemed suitable for purchase through this scheme will be considered and determined by NIHE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications for joint-tenancies of Housing Executive tenants that occurred in West Belfast in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “Please see the table below, which details the number of approved applications for a joint tenancy over the past 5 financial years, in West Belfast:YearNumber of Sole to Joint Tenancies Created1st April 2020 – 31st March 2021        251st April 2021 – 31st March 2022       321st April 2022 – 31st March 2023        601st April 2023 – 31st March 2024        571st April 2024 – 30th January 2025     76Total250It should be noted that the Housing Executive does not record refusals regarding applications for a joint tenancy.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children living in poverty in 2023-24, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for Northern Ireland (NI) are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). Due to the sample size of the FRS my Department does not publish poverty levels for children below the NI level.  The Department for Work and Pensions produce the “Children in Low Income Families: Local Area Statistics” (CiLIF). The CiLIF provides information on the number and proportion of children living in relative and absolute poverty before housing costs by local area across the United Kingdom.The latest published statistics for both the FRS and CiLIF relate to the 2022/23 financial year. Figures for the 2023/24 financial year are due to be published in Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why the final Housing Supply Strategy has omitted the commitment contained in the 2021 draft Strategy to consult on the future of the Housing Executive House Sales Scheme.
answer_text: A consultation on the future of the Housing Executive’s House Sales Scheme was not an Executive commitment and therefore is not included in this Executive strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number; and (ii) the nature of Housing Executive legal cases in the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 21416/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases involving the Enforcement of Judgments Office in relation to housing in West Belfast in each of the last five years.
answer_text: A manual trawl of the Enforcement of Judgments Office records would be required to determine the number of cases which relate specifically to housing in West Belfast and would incur a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) remit; and (ii) powers of the Rent Officer.
answer_text: The function of the Rent Officer is to determine an appropriate rent for protected/statutory tenancies and for those where the property was built pre 1945 and found to be unfit for human habitation.A protected tenancy is one where the original contract of tenancy was registered under The Rent (NI) Order 1978. A statutory tenancy is a tenancy where the original tenant has died, and the tenancy has transferred to the tenant’s ‘successor’ (either spouse/child/other family member). Having considered all the circumstances of the tenancy, the Rent Officer will set a rent based on the facts provided, such as, the general condition and state of repair of the property, whether the property is fit or unfit, the rents of similar properties let by NIHE, and the quality, quantity and condition of any furnishings (if provided). The Rent Officer currently sets the rent of 474 properties.The functions of the Rent Officer are funded by the Department. All decisions made by the Rent Officer are independent of the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered reviewing the restriction on Irish language on the Glider outside of West Belfast.
answer_text: I am committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services and in our society.  I am continuing to engage with Translink in relation to Irish language signage on the public transport network.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the projected £6.8 million budget cover transfer from the Home Office will be spent.
answer_text: Of the £10.1 million received via Budget Cover Transfers in financial year 2024/25, from the Home Office and the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), £6.3million was allocated to The Executive Office and £3.8million to other Departments.TEO funding will be utilised to meet the conditions of the associated Memorandums of Understanding (MOUs) in place for relevant schemes and cohorts and will primarily be spent as follows:Administration of the Homes for Ukraine Scheme locally, including delivery of thank you payments and safeguarding and accommodation checks through the NI Refugee Resettlement Consortium; Administration of the Syrian and Afghan resettlement schemes locally to include support for arrivals through the NI Refugee Resettlement Consortium; Delivery of dispersal funding to each local council to improve support to and integration for asylum seekers settling in their area;Delivery of Refugee Integration Funding to key partners to support orientation and integration for refugees; Funding for the provision of immigration advice for refugees and asylum seekers; andDelivery of Strategic Migration Partnership type functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of Belfast Harbour investing £90 million to improve the port's facilities for cruise ships.
answer_text: Belfast Harbour plays a crucial role as the region’s principal maritime gateway and logistics hub and is a critical infrastructure asset in the north. Belfast Harbour’s vision to develop new infrastructure has the potential to not only boost trade and tourism, but also to help meet the Executive’s net zero targets by facilitating new renewable energy capacity across the island.The Review of Trust Ports Consultation is currently ongoing and available at:  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/consultations/review-trust-ports.  Your comments on this important consultation would be welcomed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure to improve transport links into Derry for students attending Ulster University's Magee campus, especially those living in rural areas such as Tyrone, Fermanagh and Donegal.
answer_text: The Magee Expansion Taskforce has developed an action plan to expand Ulster University in Derry to 10,000 students. This plan considers the necessary transport and public infrastructure improvements required to support a successful expansion.In the development of this action plan, officials from the Department for Infrastructure presented their travel initiatives for the North West to the Taskforce in October.  Additionally, the Minister for Infrastructure attended a Taskforce meeting in December to discuss these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the calls from the local community in Derry to invest in dedicated, high-quality and affordable four-year student housing for those enrolled at Ulster University’s Magee campus.
answer_text: I recognise the calls by the local community in Derry regarding the need for dedicated, high-quality, and affordable accommodation for all students enrolled at Ulster University Magee. Ensuring that students have access to suitable housing is essential for their well-being and academic success.I fully support the findings of the Magee Expansion Taskforce’s report, which highlights that additional affordable accommodation, strategically located alongside broader housing developments, is a critical requirement for the university’s growth.My officials will continue to collaborate with Ulster University and other key stakeholders to advance the development of affordable, high-quality student accommodation that meets the needs of the growing student population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the wider economic benefits of the performing arts industry.
answer_text: While the arts sector is the responsibility of the Department for Communities, I recognise the importance of the wider creative industries to the local economy. The performing arts are a key contributor to the screen industry, one of my Department's priority sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has had any discussions with Cardiff Council in relation to the council's plans to charge a premium on empty properties.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department has engaged with officials at Welsh Government level about the discretionary powers it has granted to Welsh Local Authorities regarding empty properties. Our domestic rating system does not permit sub-delegation of decision making on rating policy at council level and this is determined through statutory provision made by the Assembly. As outlined in the evidence provided to the Finance Committee on 22 January 2025, my policy officials are carefully monitoring the use of discretionary powers in Wales for second and empty homes, particularly in relation to any substantive issues in relation to the impact on the wider housing market, and the potential impact on debt levels, evasion and avoidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on short-term and long-term rating policy proposals to build a progressive rates system.
answer_text: Proposals in this area were laid out prior to Christmas in the Strategic Roadmap for Rating Policy:-https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/minister-finance-oral-statement-rating-policy-strategic-roadmap My Department has now launched supplementary consultation in relation to domestic rating policy as part of that process. Further details can be found at:- https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/consultations/supplementary-consultation-domestic-rating-measures

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount that the Housing Executive spent on legal cases in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Please see the table below for the amount the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has spent in legal costs in the last five financial years:2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Total£k£k£k£k£k£kTotal Estimated NIHE Legal Costs1,971 1,098 1,327 1,509 1,412 7,317 NotesThe above reflects all NIHE legal costs associated with the following:Land and house sales,Contract procurement legal costs,Urban renewal,Public liability claims,General Administrative legal costs,Tenant related legal costs.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any arrangements in place to facilitate engagement and communication between their Department and UK security forces.
answer_text: The Executive Office has no arrangements in place to facilitate engagement with UK security forces. The Ministry of Defence (MOD) is a standing member of the TEO led C3 Leads Network which supports the delivery of civil contingencies here and meets a minimum of 3 times per year. Officials may also engage with the MOD via strategic co-ordination groups during emergency responses in the event their assistance would be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of vacant, derelict and unused properties that are owned or operated by his Department.
answer_text: I can advise that there are 12 vacant, 2 derelict and no unused properties that are owned or operated by my Department. For the purpose of answering this question, I have defined a vacant property as one that is empty without contents whereas an unused property is defined as one that is empty but items/equipment remain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus, to detail any urgent actions he is taking to address the lack of housing in Derry.
answer_text: Action to address locally specific issues that may impact on housing requirements in an area, is primarily taken forward by Local Councils working closely with the Housing Executive Place shaping teams. This includes through the delivery of Local Development Plans, which are responsible for establishing the zonings and policies to ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.   In addressing the wider issue of housing supply, I published an Executive Strategy on 9th December which provides a long-term framework for policies and actions required to deliver more supply across Northern Ireland. Within the Derry and Strabane district council area there are a significant number of social housing units currently under construction as well as a significant number planned under the current Social Housing Development Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times the Housing Executive met with the Minister holding office between January 2020 and October 2022 to discuss the development of the Housing Executive’s tenancy succession policy, prior to its introduction.
answer_text: The provisions for a statutory succession have been in place since 1983. The current Housing Selection Scheme (amended 2nd September 2024) articulates the requirements for any policy succession. No meetings were held between the Minister and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive regarding tenancy succession issues between January 2020 and October 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether the Lighthouse Building in Belfast is currently empty; and (ii) to detail any consideration that has been given to utilising the building for other usage.
answer_text: My Department’s lease for the Lighthouse Building in Belfast expired on 3 June 2019 and the building was handed back to the Landlord when the lease expired.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether his Department will be open to legal challenge if it does not spend 10 per cent of its transport budget on active travel in 2025-26, as specified in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Since taking up office I have repeatedly stated my commitment to increasing investment in active travel in order to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act. My Department has been developing plans to support the increased investment necessary to meet the requirements. This work has included the development of an Active Travel Delivery Plan which will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (2022) and the Strategic Plan for Greenways (2016) to provide a firm basis for future prioritisation of active travel infrastructure investment and delivery. Public consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan is ongoing, and I would encourage the public to engage with the consultation before the closing date of 28 February 2025. My Department has also taken a number of important steps to increase capacity to support the delivery of active travel projects, including filling key posts within the Active Travel team and increasing supply chain capacity to facilitate an increased delivery pipeline. My officials work closely with colleagues across the Department and delivery partners to ensure that overall active travel spend is in keeping with the intent of the Act.Ramping up an ambitious programme of investment of this nature will take years. My Department is working on the basis of increasing overall active travel investment to meet our Climate Change Act obligations by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how his Department’s position on gas caverns at Islandmagee takes into consideration its climate obligations.
answer_text: AQO 1490/22-27Answer: The three decisions within the Department’s remit, the marine licence, the water discharge consent and the abstraction licence, have been quashed through the Judicial Review process.  The applications will be reconsidered within the current legislative and policy context, which now includes the Executive’s Energy Strategy and the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  My department is engaging with the applicant on the information that will now be required to allow for the consideration of the application to re-commence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of cycling journeys (i) he has undertaken as part of his ministerial engagements to date; and (ii) he plans to take as part of his ministerial engagements.
answer_text: During his time in office as Minister for Infrastructure,  Minister O’Dowd undertook two cycling journeys as part of his ministerial engagements.  There are currently no scheduled commitments in my diary that would offer the opportunity for me to cycle.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how Land and Property Services, or her Department, determines whether a home is occupied.
answer_text: Whether a home is deemed occupied for rating purposes is determined in line with long established caselaw. Critically what constitutes occupation under that caselaw for rating purposes is very specific and is not limited to whether a person resides within the property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how the delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022-31 will help relieve congestion in Belfast city centre; and (ii) what estimates his Department has made of the impact its delivery will have on traffic flows.
answer_text: In Belfast City Centre, our latest 2023 cordon survey confirms that private car travel is the most dominant form of travel, and this is a contributor to the traffic congestion in the city centre during peak times. The latest average transport statistics highlight that at least 40% of these car journeys could be short trips (less than 5 mile) and could be replaced by cycling or other forms of sustainable travel for many people.Increasing the attractiveness and viability of more sustainable means of travel will be key to helping address the issue of traffic congestion in Belfast city centre. I recognise that the delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network will play an important role in the creation of a cleaner, greener and more resilient transport network, providing many people with the choice to walk, wheel or cycle for many of their shorter everyday journeys.Within the Belfast Plan of the Eastern Transport Plan, there is an ambition to re-prioritise and reallocate existing space and road spaces in our urban areas, and gear these towards public transport and active travel. Development of the Belfast Plan of the Eastern Transport Plan will be supported by robust data and modelling on which informed transport decisions for Belfast can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her Department's policy in relation to keeping a record of empty properties.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has a specific and statutory role in relation to the collection of information on the occupation of a property as it relates to the assessment and collection of rates. Critically, what constitutes occupation for rating purposes is very specific and is not necessarily the same as a property having someone reside within it or conduct trade from it. My response to AQW 20861/22-27 set out the required circumstances in which a vacant property is identified for the purposes of rates administration. Land & Property Services will most often update occupancy status through changes in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates. Related records will be held in rating administration systems in line with Departmental data protection and information management policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a breakdown of the tenure type of current vacant homes.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information by tenure type for vacant homes.Where a property is unoccupied and the intention is for that property to be let in future, it is not possible to definitively categorise tenure as “rented”, even if it would be expected to fall within that category when next in use.My response to AQW 20861/22-27 set out the limited circumstances in which a vacant property is identified for the purposes of rates administration. Land & Property Services (LPS) will most often update occupancy status through changes in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates.In addition for social sector rental properties (rated under Article 21 of the Rates (NI) Order 1977) under statute those landlords have to pay full rates regardless of whether a property is occupied or vacant, and there is no statutory requirement for LPS to hold records in relation to this as rates liability continues to be the responsibility of the landlord.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans she has to compel owners of properties that are empty, vacant or derelict to register them with Land and Property Services.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has a specific and statutory role in relation to the collection of information on the occupation of a property as it relates to the assessment and collection of rates. Critically, what constitutes occupation for rating purposes is very specific and is not necessarily the same as a property having someone reside within it or conduct trade from it.LPS will update the occupancy status of a property held for rating purposes in required circumstances, for example where this has been reported in the context of a change in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates. If a ratepayer considers that their property is derelict and not capable of beneficial occupation, LPS will consider an application to review the property valuation for the purposes of rating.LPS does not gather additional information for purposes outside those associated with its statutory function. I would also refer the member to the link to the recent Assembly motion on Belfast City Centre Dereliction where I outlined in detail my Department’s position on the issue of derelict properties which do not fall within the remit rating system.https://aims.niassembly.gov.uk/officialreport/report.aspx?&eveDate=2024-11-18&docID=417532#4656698

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Land and Property Services staff working directly to identify vacant and derelict properties.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has a specific and statutory role in relation to the collection of information as it relates to the assessment and collection of rates.My response to AQW 20861/22-27 set out the limited circumstances in which a vacant property is identified for the purposes of rates administration. LPS will update the occupancy status of a property within rating records where this has been reported in the context of a change in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates. If a ratepayer considers that their property is derelict and not capable of beneficial occupation, LPS will consider an application to review the property valuation for the purposes of rates. Following that review, if it qualifies under existing rating legislation, the property will be removed from the Valuation List.  These activities are carried out in response to a request from a ratepayer as part of general rates administration activities. No LPS staff work directly on identifying vacant and derelict properties where this does not align with their statutory remit.LPS focuses upon its statutory duties, including those pertaining to valuation for rating, identifying liability to rates, assessment to rates, rate collection and recovery action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to set out the successful grounds for challenge in High Court judgment [2024] NIKB 84 against his Department; and (ii) whether he will review previous minerals prospecting licences issued to determine the extent to which his Department has, or has not, been complying with the law.
answer_text: (i) 	The High Court judgment [2024] NIKB 84 is publicly available on the Judiciary NI website at Derry City and Strabane District Council's Application.pdf(ii)	This case has not yet reached a final conclusion and therefore it would not be appropriate for me to comment at this stage. When the case is concluded my Department will consider what, if any, impact there is on other Mineral Prospecting Licences currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Renters (Reform) Bill in Westminster, whether he plans to introduce any new legislation to strengthen the rights of private tenants.
answer_text: I have already introduced numerous additional protections for tenants and will continue to do so during the rest of the mandate.  My programme of PRS reform strikes a balance between the rights and responsibilities of landlords and tenants. It includes restricting the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12-month period, increasing notice to quit periods, establishing minimum energy efficiency standards, and progressing work to maximise the potential of the Land Registration Scheme to improve standards. Some of my proposals require new subordinate legislation, while others require amendments to existing subordinate legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how often Land and Property Services staff carry out site visits on vacant and derelict properties.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answers to AQW 20861/22-27 and AQW 20961/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how a derelict property is identified.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) does not hold records on derelict property, only those that are rateable. LPS is responsible for the maintenance of the Valuation List in accordance with Rating Legislation.Under Rating Legislation, both vacant domestic and non-domestic properties are liable to rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recent statistics in the Use of Force by the Police in Northern Ireland 1 October 2023 to 30 September 2024 report, which show that the use of spite and bite guards and attenuating energy projectiles increased from October 2023 to September 2024.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on the use of force by them.  Issues around the use of spit and bite guards and of attenuating energy projectiles by police is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational responsibility of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to statistics on the use of force by police, as raised in the question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the statistics in the Use of Force by the Police in Northern Ireland 1 October 2023 to 30 September 2024 report which shows that force was used against children aged 12 and under 56 times between October 2023 to September 2024, whether she plans to review the necessity and proportionality of police force against children.
answer_text: I have no plans for such a review.  The use of force by police in Northern Ireland is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of tenancy successions granted by the Housing Executive in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “Housing Executive tenancies are recorded on the Housing Management System and are assigned a reason code (why the tenancy has been created or awarded).  There are 1686 tenancies with a reason code of Succession (Statutory) with tenancy commencement dates between April 2019 and Dec 2024 and a further 381 with a reason code of Succession (Policy) within the same time parameters.In addition, there are 3263 tenancies with a reason code of Joint to Single.  The 1983 Housing (NI) Order provides the statutory provision for succession and details that any deceased tenant is a successor if they were previously the joint tenant and then became the sole tenant.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications for Discretionary Support were refused due to exhaustion of the allocated budget in each of the last five years.
answer_text: No applications for Discretionary Support were refused due to exhaustion of the allocated budget in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how a vacant property is identified.
answer_text: The most effective means for property to be identified as vacant within the rating system is through confirmation from ratepayers. Land & Property Services (LPS) will most often update occupancy status through changes in rating liability or when a ratepayer makes an application for exclusion from rates. Since the introduction of the Rating of Empty Homes legislation on 1 October 2011, rate liability for all domestic properties has been assessed at 100% whether occupied or not. Information on the status of occupation of a domestic property is subject to ratepayers informing LPS that the property is unoccupied, unfurnished and not used for storage, or as part of an application for Rating of Empty Homes Exclusion.Any empty Non-Domestic property with a rateable NAV (Net Annual Value) below £2,000 will not be charged rates. In these circumstances an exclusion is applied to the rate account following notification that the property is vacant. For Non-Domestic properties with a rateable NAV above £2,000 the ratepayer can apply for a Non-Domestic Vacant Rating exclusion. Without an application, no exclusion will be applied. LPS will verify applications for exclusion through property inspections and checks on information provided through the application process. Desk based checks and periodic physical inspections on eligibility for continued exclusion are carried out throughout the rating year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the number of occasions that Attenuating Energy Projectiles were used by the PSNI in situations of public disorder involving young people in 2024; and (ii) for her assessment of the use of Attenuating Energy Projectiles in situations of public disorder involving young people.
answer_text: Attenuating Energy Projectiles (AEP) were used by the PSNI on two (2) occasions in situations of public disorder in 2024 and it is suspected that young persons were involved in this public disorder.The use of AEP by the PSNI is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland is notified of all instances where AEP is used.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown, by local council area, of the 18,148 incidents where the PSNI had to use force between 1 October 2023 and 30 September 2024.
answer_text: The recording of information in relation to use of force is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational responsibility of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail all occasions that Attenuating Energy Projectiles were used by the PSNI in 2024, including the rationale for each use.
answer_text: In 2024, the PSNI discharged Attenuating Energy Projectiles (AEPs) on eight (8) occasions, with 13 AEPs discharged in total. Nine (9) AEPs were discharged as a less lethal option and four (4) AEPs were discharged in a public order situation.The use of AEP by the PSNI is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The table below details the number of Discretionary Housing Payments awarded in each of the last five full financial years, broken down by month. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) allocated budget; and (ii) actual spend for Discretionary Housing Payments in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The table below provides details of both the allocated budget; as well as actual spend for Discretionary Housing Payments in each of the last five full financial years.Year2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24Allocated Budget£7,290,000£6,100,000£6,100,000£6,490,000£6,490,000Total spend£4,762,880.00£4,525,302.64£4,380,297.59£6,297,557.07£6,200,587.15”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.
