question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the delivery of 100,000 homes, set out in the Housing Supply Strategy 2024-2039, will be funded.
answer_text: The delivery of ‘at least 100,000 homes’ across all tenures set out in the Strategy, is intended as a long-term ambition over a 15-year period to meet our housing needs and demands.The Strategy provides a framework for the whole system changes needed but will only deliver if Executive Departments prioritise the policy changes and budgetary allocations required.The publication of the Housing Supply Strategy by the Executive, alongside the inclusion of Housing as a priority in the Programme for Government, demonstrates the commitment to meeting this ambition, and with that, to ensure that funding is allocated to actions identified to deliver the supply we need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has demonstrated any trauma-informed practice when meeting the needs of asylum seeker children and refugees.
answer_text: The Department works alongside the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES) to provide support to Asylum Seeker and Refugee pupils.The Education Authority’s lead officer for Asylum Seeker family support is a Trauma Informed Practice (TIP) trainer and three of the other five staff are TIP Level 2 trained, with the two most recently arrived staff completing their TIP Level 1 training before the end of January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her engagement with the Criminal Bar Association, ahead of planned industrial action in January 2025.
answer_text: My Department has been engaging with professions on an ongoing basis since the outset of the access to justice reform programme. I also met the representatives of the professional bodies in advance of my statement to the Assembly in December which highlighted my intention to implement an uplift in fees as soon as possible. Officials met again with representatives of the Bar and CBA when the latest withdrawal of services was announced at the end of December. I am now reflecting on issues raised in the course of that discussion and will of course make every effort to address concerns, where they are within my gift, and where it is appropriate to do so.  While I fully support the need to ensure fair proportionate remuneration for work undertaken and intend to issue a consultation on proposed fees changes at the end of January as planned,  I must follow proper process which takes time. I will, however, continue to engage with the Criminal Bar Association in interim in the interests assuaging their concerns and ending the withdrawal of services and the consequent disproportionate impact on victims and witnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any analysis that has been carried out on the STAR service and its implementation in the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: No analysis has been carried out as the STARS service did not operate in the 2024/25 academic year, due to a shortage of Educational Psychologist provision with the Education Authority (EA). The EA Intercultural Education Service (IES) remain open to re-starting the service when additional Education Psychologist time can be prioritised for this area of work.  In the absence of STARS, schools may still avail of the EA’s regular Educational Psychologist provision for all children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any communication his Department and the Education Authority have issued to (i) families and those who advocate on their behalf; and (ii) schools, in relation to the review of Intercultural Education Service provision for children living with their families in initial or contingency asylum accommodation.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) has agreed to inform families and support organisations when any further changes are made to their regular support service provision for children living with their families in initial contingency accommodation. As no new changes have happened, there is no need to communicate differently at the present time. IES will be inviting advocates and support groups to a presentation on the role of IES within the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will remind councils of their obligations to enable public participation and transparency in the planning decision-making process, particularly if public authorities do not disclose Environmental Impact Assessment Determinations on the planning portal and, therefore, are in breach of Article 5(3)(d) of the Aarhus Convention.
answer_text: Key requirements in relation to Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) are set out in The Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.In recent years my Department has supported the development and delivery of EIA training for planning staff in all councils, in their role as local planning authorities, to enhance capacity and support sound environmental decision-making. Transparency is a central focus in the land use planning system, in relation to all development proposals not just EIA development, with the Planning Portal a key element in supporting effective public access. However, whilst regulation 44 (2)(4) indicates the requirement for councils to keep a register it also indicates that ‘where the registers kept under this regulation are kept using electronic storage, the council may make the registers available to the public for inspection by the public on a website maintained by the council for that purpose’.I am committed to supporting the effective and efficient operation of the regional planning system but recognise that under the two-tier planning system, councils are independent, autonomous organisations responsible for meeting their statutory responsibilities, including those under the 2017 EIA Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14705/22-27, (i) when an update was shared with the Education Authority's Joint Consultative Forum and with Refugee and Asylum Forum; and (ii) to provide a copy of this update.
answer_text: The Service Leads of the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) and DE’s Additional Educational Needs Team (AENT) carried out a review of the temporary operational arrangements pertaining to families seeking sanctuary arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation on 5th November and again on 19th December.  Following these reviews agreement was reached to maintain temporary arrangements as the average length of time families are placed in initial hotel contingency accommodation remains unchanged, i.e. around 4 weeks before being moved to dispersal accommodation within the community.All  new families arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation are met by IES as soon as possible after arrival and informed about the support on offer and are supported throughout early school enrolment procedures.The EA undertook equality screening of the original temporary change in operational procedure in September.  Equality screening was concluded on 31st October with the document published on the EA webpages.EA IES presented a full update on the temporary arrangements to the Joint Consultative Forum on 17th September 2024.  The presentation and minutes of the meeting have been shared subsequently with members of the Forum.There was no separate update to the Refugee and Asylum Forum requested or provided.There was no direct prior consultation with individual families or external groups as service provision is a system-wide decision for EA.  Operational decisions around how resources are deployed to help meet the needs of children and young people with additional educational needs are based on existing policies, procedures and resource allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when Intercultural Education Service provision for children living with their families in initial or contingency asylum accommodation was last reviewed; (ii) who conducted the review; and (ii) any equality screening and child rights impact assessments that were carried out as part of the review.
answer_text: The Service Leads of the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) and DE’s Additional Educational Needs Team (AENT) carried out a review of the temporary operational arrangements pertaining to families seeking sanctuary arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation on 5th November and again on 19th December.  Following these reviews agreement was reached to maintain temporary arrangements as the average length of time families are placed in initial hotel contingency accommodation remains unchanged, i.e. around 4 weeks before being moved to dispersal accommodation within the community.All  new families arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation are met by IES as soon as possible after arrival and informed about the support on offer and are supported throughout early school enrolment procedures.The EA undertook equality screening of the original temporary change in operational procedure in September.  Equality screening was concluded on 31st October with the document published on the EA webpages.EA IES presented a full update on the temporary arrangements to the Joint Consultative Forum on 17th September 2024.  The presentation and minutes of the meeting have been shared subsequently with members of the Forum.There was no separate update to the Refugee and Asylum Forum requested or provided.There was no direct prior consultation with individual families or external groups as service provision is a system-wide decision for EA.  Operational decisions around how resources are deployed to help meet the needs of children and young people with additional educational needs are based on existing policies, procedures and resource allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14705/22-27, (i) when his Department and the Education Authority reviewed this arrangement; (ii) who conducted the review; and (iii) to detail any equality screening and child rights impact assessment carried out as part of the review.
answer_text: The Service Leads of the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) and DE’s Additional Educational Needs Team (AENT) carried out a review of the temporary operational arrangements pertaining to families seeking sanctuary arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation on 5th November and again on 19th December.  Following these reviews agreement was reached to maintain temporary arrangements as the average length of time families are placed in initial hotel contingency accommodation remains unchanged, i.e. around 4 weeks before being moved to dispersal accommodation within the community.All  new families arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation are met by IES as soon as possible after arrival and informed about the support on offer and are supported throughout early school enrolment procedures.The EA undertook equality screening of the original temporary change in operational procedure in September.  Equality screening was concluded on 31st October with the document published on the EA webpages.EA IES presented a full update on the temporary arrangements to the Joint Consultative Forum on 17th September 2024.  The presentation and minutes of the meeting have been shared subsequently with members of the Forum.There was no separate update to the Refugee and Asylum Forum requested or provided.There was no direct prior consultation with individual families or external groups as service provision is a system-wide decision for EA.  Operational decisions around how resources are deployed to help meet the needs of children and young people with additional educational needs are based on existing policies, procedures and resource allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14705/22-27, to detail the outcome of the review, including whether it has been communicated to families and stakeholders who support those families.
answer_text: The Service Leads of the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) and DE’s Additional Educational Needs Team (AENT) carried out a review of the temporary operational arrangements pertaining to families seeking sanctuary arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation on 5th November and again on 19th December.  Following these reviews agreement was reached to maintain temporary arrangements as the average length of time families are placed in initial hotel contingency accommodation remains unchanged, i.e. around 4 weeks before being moved to dispersal accommodation within the community.All  new families arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation are met by IES as soon as possible after arrival and informed about the support on offer and are supported throughout early school enrolment procedures.The EA undertook equality screening of the original temporary change in operational procedure in September.  Equality screening was concluded on 31st October with the document published on the EA webpages.EA IES presented a full update on the temporary arrangements to the Joint Consultative Forum on 17th September 2024.  The presentation and minutes of the meeting have been shared subsequently with members of the Forum.There was no separate update to the Refugee and Asylum Forum requested or provided.There was no direct prior consultation with individual families or external groups as service provision is a system-wide decision for EA.  Operational decisions around how resources are deployed to help meet the needs of children and young people with additional educational needs are based on existing policies, procedures and resource allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the outcome of the review of the Intercultural Education Service provision for children living with their families in initial or contingency asylum accommodation; and (ii) whether the outcome has been communicated to families and stakeholders across all sectors who support families.
answer_text: The Service Leads of the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) and DE’s Additional Educational Needs Team (AENT) carried out a review of the temporary operational arrangements pertaining to families seeking sanctuary arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation on 5th November and again on 19th December.  Following these reviews agreement was reached to maintain temporary arrangements as the average length of time families are placed in initial hotel contingency accommodation remains unchanged, i.e. around 4 weeks before being moved to dispersal accommodation within the community.All  new families arriving into initial hotel contingency accommodation are met by IES as soon as possible after arrival and informed about the support on offer and are supported throughout early school enrolment procedures.The EA undertook equality screening of the original temporary change in operational procedure in September.  Equality screening was concluded on 31st October with the document published on the EA webpages.EA IES presented a full update on the temporary arrangements to the Joint Consultative Forum on 17th September 2024.  The presentation and minutes of the meeting have been shared subsequently with members of the Forum.There was no separate update to the Refugee and Asylum Forum requested or provided.There was no direct prior consultation with individual families or external groups as service provision is a system-wide decision for EA.  Operational decisions around how resources are deployed to help meet the needs of children and young people with additional educational needs are based on existing policies, procedures and resource allocations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there are any aspects of international law that their Department has responsibility to uphold.
answer_text: International relations are an excepted matter under the Northern Ireland Act 1998. However, the observation and implementation of international obligations, and obligations under the Human Rights Convention are devolved matters and apply to all Northern Ireland departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, as part of their work on international relations in relation to securing investment, trade and tourism, if they have discussed with the (i) Ministry of Defence; and (ii) Prime Minister whether any military planes from the United States of America or Israel use Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: The operation of military aircraft and its use of airports is not a devolved matter and does not fall under the remit of the First Minister and deputy First Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications for public immunity interest in relation to hospital deaths that have been granted since the year 2000.
answer_text: There is no central record kept of PII applications which have been granted since 2000 in a retrievable format within the case management systems of the Department, the Trusts or the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO). Therefore, without a very extensive and time-consuming check of all litigation cases handled for DoH it is not possible to determine the number of:Cases where there has been a PII granted; orPIIs granted specifically in relation to hospital deaths.Given the time and resource that would be required to carry out such an exercise, it is not considered that this would be an appropriate use of public money.Even if such an exercise were to be attempted, it is unlikely that case records would be available for all casework dating back to 2000 as some records would have been destroyed in accordance with Retention and Disposal Policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of public immunity interest applications granted since 2000.
answer_text: There is no central record kept of PII applications which have been granted since 2000 in a retrievable format within the case management systems of the Department, the Trusts or the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO).Therefore it is not possible to determine the number of past PIIs granted within my Department without a very extensive and time-consuming check of all litigation cases handled for DoH.Given the time and resource that would be required to carry out such an exercise, it is not considered that this would be an appropriate use of public money.Even if such an exercise were to be attempted, it is unlikely that case records would be available for all casework dating back to 2000 as some records would have been destroyed in accordance with Retention and Disposal Policies. I can however confirm that there are currently no medical negligence cases relating to any of the HSCNI bodies which have a Public Interest Immunity certificate attached.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any transgender advocacy groups he met or engaged with before implementing the ban on puberty blockers.
answer_text: On 12 November and 9 December, I met with representatives from local transgender representative organisations including the Rainbow Project, HereNI, Cara-Friend, Mermaids NI, and the Belfast Trans Resource Centre to outline my Department's plans for reforming gender affirming care services in Northern Ireland.My officials continue to meet with the Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group and the Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which includes clinicians, transgender representative organisations, and service users to discuss the implications of the ban and seek their views. The reach of these groups and the service users is extensive, both regionally and nationally, and they actively liaise with other transgender groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18028/22-27, to detail the number of women who resided within the Causeway Hospital locality that experienced birth before arrival at hospital, in each year between 2018 and 2023.
answer_text: The information you have requested is listed below:2018201920202021202220235<5512<56My officials will continue to monitor births before arrival to hospitals to identify if there has been any increase as a result of the reconfiguration of maternity services in the Northern Trust locality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of times she has met with her officials to discuss public sector contracts with Fujitsu since taking office in February 2024.
answer_text: The Finance Minister received a written briefing in relation to a government contract and Fujitsu on 9th February 2024, and met with officials on 12th February 2024 to discuss the briefing.  The Minister was also briefed on 26th March 2024 to discuss an Assembly oral question relating to awarding public contracts to Fujitsu.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the circumstances when her Department can intervene to amend or cancel public sector contracts with other Departments.
answer_text: The Department of Finance provides a Legal and Procurement Shared Service for  Departments and may be asked to provide advice on contractual matters. The decision to amend or cancel a contract would be for the relevant department in accordance with the relevant procurement or contract legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) why there is to be no prosecution for the major pollution incident on the Crumlin River in February 2023 which killed around 3,500 fish; and (ii) whether his Department has identified the cause of this pollution incident.
answer_text: In relation to the pollution incident you refer to, despite a very rapid response and investigation following notification of the incident, unfortunately it was not possible to secure sufficient evidence to enable a file to be forwarded to Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to take forward a prosecution.The precise cause of this pollution incident has not been established.  It appears that a pollutant travelled down the waterway at an unknown time over the previous 24 hours.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any Health and Social Care Trusts that have received legal advice that delegated healthcare tasks are not permitted to be delivered by personal assistants under Direct Payments legislation.
answer_text: Belfast Trust: No legal advice has been received.  The Trust is mindful of relevant Departmental policy regarding Direct Payments, which is clear that they cannot be made for health services, including delegated healthcare tasks and that they are to cover assessed personal social services.South Eastern Trust: No legal advice has been received that delegated healthcare tasks are not permitted to be delivered by personal assistants under Direct Payments legislation.Southern Trust: The Trust has sought legal advice regarding interpretation of the Carers and Direct Payments [Northern Ireland] Act 2002 and the Health and Social Care [Reform] Act [Northern Ireland] 2009).  The Southern Trust’s position remains that Direct Payments should only be used for social care tasks and cannot be used to fund  health care related tasks.Northern Trust: The Northern Trust has received legal advice which states that delegated tasks are not permitted to be delivered by personal assistants under direct payment legislation.Western Trust: No legal advice has been received that delegated healthcare tasks are not permitted to be delivered by Personal Assistants under Direct Payments legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his announcement that parents and carers are being urged to consider other options before bringing children to hospital for treatment over the winter period, to detail any extra resources his Department has provided to out-of-hours GP services.
answer_text: GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services provide primary care for urgent conditions that cannot wait until the patient’s own GP surgery is next open.   GP OOHs will play an important role in helping to manage additional demand for the coming winter period. This winter, the Department will provide an additional £900k to GP OOHs service providers to support providers to increase capacity to meet the expected increase in demand over the winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how he will determine the growth of multidisciplinary team roll-outs from 30 per cent to 50 per cent of the population; and (ii) whether deprivation will form a part of his consideration.
answer_text: A road map for the full implementation of multi-disciplinary teams across all 17 Federation areas, was developed and approved by Minister Swann in March 2022.  The roadmap ensures that the benefits of implementation of the model are as equally distributed as possible, while being mindful of current challenging workforce and financial constraints.Deprivation was considered when determining the running order for further implementation; planned new areas include a mix of urban and rural areas with some of the highest levels of deprivation in Northern Ireland.I want to be able to further invest in the Primary Care MDTs, so more people can benefit from prompt access to a range of support across primary care and as such my Department has submitted a proposal to the Transformation Fund to underpin the roll-out of MDTs across Primary Care and provide essential additional university training places in key professions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his statement to the Assembly on 10 December 2024, for a breakdown of how the £17 million announced for mental health will be spent.
answer_text: Mental health remains a priority for me but to date we have not been able to secure the additional funding required to deliver the ambition set out in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. My three-year plan commits to an additional £17million to support priority areas under the Strategy which will make the greatest difference.  This includes greater integration of the voluntary and community sector in service delivery and improving services, including crisis interventions and the new Regional Mental Health Service.  However, I must reiterate that this commitment is subject to the Department receiving additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his announcement that parents and carers are being urged to consider other options before bringing children to hospital for treatment over the winter period, how (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts have communicated other means for treatment.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of court cases for people in possession of a personal amount of cannabis in the last five years; and (ii) the average length of sentence.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for possession of cannabis.  Possession of cannabis is prosecuted as ‘possession of a class B drug’.  However, convictions datasets do not hold information in relation to the specific drug involved, where convictions have been made for this offence.  Therefore, information has been provided for all cases involving ‘possession of a class B drug’ offences that were dealt with in the period 2019 to 2023, the most recent five years for which information is available.  In addition to prosecutions and convictions at courts, these cases are often dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposal, so those figures have also been provided (Table 1).  For those who received a custodial sentence for such offences, the average (mean) length of sentence is reported in Table 2.Table 1: Prosecutions and convictions at courts, and out-of-court diversionary disposals for ‘possession of a Class B drug’ offences, 2019 to 2023YearProsecutionsConvictionsDiversions20192,2522,04983020201,6931,50970720212,2912,03775820222,1771,91072420232,2051,936707Note:1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to defendants who were prosecuted/convicted at court, or received an out of court diversion for at least one count of the offence ‘possession of a class B drug’.3. Figures for diversionary disposals include Cautions, Informed Warnings and Youth Conference Plans.Table 2: Average (mean) length of custodial sentence imposed following conviction at courts for ‘possession of a Class B drug’ offences, 2019 to 2023YearAverage length of sentence (days)201991202094202190202284202392Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to defendants who were convicted at court and received a custodial sentence for at least one count of the offence ‘possession of a class B drug’.3. Figures for average length of disposal include time spent on licence as well as time in custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of organisations that will avail of the £150,000 goodwill grant as part of the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: The Goodwill Grant is used to provide a range of events and small gifts to those using homeless services at Christmas time.  It is unrelated to the Housing Supply Strategy.Homeless Connect distribute the monies on an equitable basis amongst the homeless organisations they represent, however, the breakdown for the 2024 grant has not yet been made available to the Department.In 2023 a total of 45 organisations availed of the Goodwill Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the organisations working in homeless prevention that he engaged with before finalising the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My Department developed a draft Housing Supply Strategy following extensive consultation with a wide range of bodies, groups and individuals from within the housing and related sectors which included a number of homelessness organisations.Prior to the publication of the Strategy, I met with the following organisations to discuss homelessness issues;Simon Community;The Welcome Organisation;Teen Challenge Northern Ireland;Housing Rights;Beyond the Battlefield;Registered Housing Associations; Housing Executive; andMACS Supporting Children & Young People.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how people on low incomes can avail of co-ownership as part of the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: An allocation of £158 million in Financial Transactions Capital funding is approved to Co-Ownership to help over 4,400 working people and families on lower incomes into homeownership over the period 2020/21 and 2024/25. A new business case is currently under development to ensure continued funding of this essential intervention.Details on how to avail of Co-Ownership products and the relevant eligibility criteria can be found on its website: www.co-ownership.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he will enforce the restriction of rent increases to once every 12 months.
answer_text: If a landlord attempts to increase the rent more than once in every 12-month period or does not give the right notice period, then the landlord’s action has no legal effect and the rent remains unchanged. A landlord cannot enforce the increase in court, nor can he harass the tenant, or he will be liable in the same way as if he tried to harass the tenant for any other money that is not due to him. Compliance with the restriction on rent increases will be underpinned by a comprehensive engagement strategy undertaken by my officials. This strategy will be aimed at informing landlords and tenants, representative groups and wider stakeholders of the legislative change. It will also advise landlords and tenants where further advice, and guidance can be found on this and other key aspects of private rented sector legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a no fault eviction ban will be included as part of his Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: A no-fault eviction ban is not included as part of my Housing Supply Strategy.My Department is taking forward work, as mandated by the Assembly, to increase security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods.  This is included as an enabling activity under Objective 3 of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure every patient can access cardiac rehabilitation services.
answer_text: Cardiac Rehabilitation services are one of the key priorities of Department’s Cardiac Network and therefore it established a Cardiac Rehabilitation Strategic Leadership Group who are working on devising a standardised cardiac rehabilitation pathway for service users who would benefit from cardiac rehabilitation in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will ensure appropriately commissioned and staffed heart failure services in order to ensure speed of diagnosis and access to specialist input.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of the Department’s Cardiac Network. A Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group was established to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. To date, the Group has devised a proposed heart failure model, which facilitates a regionally- standardised approach with timely diagnosis and access to heart failure services; giving consideration to primary, community and secondary care setting provision. The proposed streamlined pathway includes specialist heart failure team input, which is enhanced by the multidisciplinary team.  Work in this regard is in progress and will continue in 2025 in relation to a more detailed workforce analysis for the purposes of informing commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.In terms of preventative work for people who experience heart failure, patients are provided with advice on modifiable risk factors, for instance smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight through a balanced diet, as well as the appropriate use of relevant medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to ensure access to wider multidisciplinary team input for people with heart failure so that they can access the necessary care across all primary, community and secondary-based settings.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of the Department’s Cardiac Network. A Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group was established to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. To date, the Group has devised a proposed heart failure model, which facilitates a regionally- standardised approach with timely diagnosis and access to heart failure services; giving consideration to primary, community and secondary care setting provision. The proposed streamlined pathway includes specialist heart failure team input, which is enhanced by the multidisciplinary team.  Work in this regard is in progress and will continue in 2025 in relation to a more detailed workforce analysis for the purposes of informing commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.In terms of preventative work for people who experience heart failure, patients are provided with advice on modifiable risk factors, for instance smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight through a balanced diet, as well as the appropriate use of relevant medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to remove regional differences in the commissioning of specialist input services for heart failure.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of the Department’s Cardiac Network. A Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group was established to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. To date, the Group has devised a proposed heart failure model, which facilitates a regionally- standardised approach with timely diagnosis and access to heart failure services; giving consideration to primary, community and secondary care setting provision. The proposed streamlined pathway includes specialist heart failure team input, which is enhanced by the multidisciplinary team.  Work in this regard is in progress and will continue in 2025 in relation to a more detailed workforce analysis for the purposes of informing commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.In terms of preventative work for people who experience heart failure, patients are provided with advice on modifiable risk factors, for instance smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight through a balanced diet, as well as the appropriate use of relevant medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any preventive work he is undertaking for people who experience heart failure.
answer_text: Heart failure services are one of the key priorities of the Department’s Cardiac Network. A Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group was established to progress a standardised heart failure pathway. To date, the Group has devised a proposed heart failure model, which facilitates a regionally- standardised approach with timely diagnosis and access to heart failure services; giving consideration to primary, community and secondary care setting provision. The proposed streamlined pathway includes specialist heart failure team input, which is enhanced by the multidisciplinary team.  Work in this regard is in progress and will continue in 2025 in relation to a more detailed workforce analysis for the purposes of informing commissioning decisions. Any proposed service developments identified as part of the Group’s work requiring additional financial investment will be subject to the availability and subsequent approval of funding to enable implementation.In terms of preventative work for people who experience heart failure, patients are provided with advice on modifiable risk factors, for instance smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight through a balanced diet, as well as the appropriate use of relevant medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has been made aware of any concerns in relation to the delay of legal aid payments to solicitors and the particular impact this has on young women outside of Belfast.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the concerns in relation to the timeliness of legal aid payments to all legal practitioners. I have met representatives of the Law Society and Bar and am alive to concerns expressed about younger female barristers. My department has been consistently underfunded and pressure on resources is increasing. I have repeatedly highlighted the pressure on the justice system including legal aid, and I will continue to argue for more money. £114 million was paid to legal professionals in 2023/24, the highest level in the history of legal aid. We are already on track to exceed that spend this financial year. Legal aid payments are made to solicitor firms, not individual solicitors, in chronological order of the date requests are submitted. Payments to barristers are also made in chronological order. Barristers primarily work out of the Bar Library in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her rationale for not granting the outstanding pay award for 2023-24 for NI Water workers.
answer_text: You will be aware that this issue has been satisfactorily resolved and industrial action has been averted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of legal service users she engaged with before making changes to legal aid.
answer_text: The programme has been informed by widespread evidence gathering and views and experiences have been captured in a number of ways including through the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid led His Honour Judge Burgess, a public call for evidence for the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services, research commissioned by the Department in fulfilment of section 29 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021; and a Review and Rapid Priority Setting Exercise of Civil Legal Aid for cases of Domestic Abuse jointly commissioned by the Department and the Commissioner designate for Victims of Crime. Stakeholders had the opportunity to submit views in writing, in meetings with officials, through representative organisations and through focus groups and engagement will continue.  Changes have not yet been made to the legal aid framework. The programme I announced on 2 December 2024 sets the direction of travel not the final destination and each of the proposals I am considering will be subject to further detailed consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the Bar of Northern Ireland in relation to her proposed changes to legal aid.
answer_text: The programme has been informed by widespread evidence gathering and detailed engagement with stakeholders who have had the opportunity to meet directly with officials and to respond to the call for evidence for the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services and to meet and submit evidence to His Honour Judge Burgess who led the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid. That includes the Bar Council with whom my Department enjoys regular contact, both on an ad hoc basis and through monthly stocktake meetings. I also had the opportunity to meet directly with representatives of the Bar in advance of my statement to the Assembly. The Programme represents the totality of evidence gathered through the engagement process and other sources, but engagement will continue as I consult on the programme delivery plan and each of the proposals therein.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the last time legal aid payments to solicitors and legal teams were increased.
answer_text: Legal aid payments for criminal cases were last increased in 2021 by The Magistrates’ Courts and County Court Appeals (Criminal Legal Aid) (Costs) (Amendment) Rules (Northern Ireland) 2021 with effect from 6 January 2022.The amendment enhanced fees payable in cases appealed to the county court and Newton Hearings.Fees for providers of civil legal services were put in statute by the Civil Legal Services (Remuneration) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 and have not increased since that time.  Most of the fees were in place at the current level for a number of years before the introduction of the 2015 Order and have not therefore been increased for ten years or more.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children that were removed from Irish-medium education in each of the last five years due to appropriate special educational needs and disabilities support and assistance not being in place.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of special educational needs and disabilities (SEND) assessment meetings held in each of the last five years for children with SEND who wanted to attend Irish-medium education.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this information.  This data is held at school level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the information the Education Authority and his Department provides to parents of children who have expressed interest in attending Irish-medium education and have received a diagnosis of special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: Parents may seek a place in an Irish-medium school through the general schools admissions process for children who have or may have special educational needs (SEN), but who do not have a statement.   Information on all schools and their published admissions criteria is available on the Education Authority’s (EA) website.Where SEN have been formally identified through a statement of SEN, parents are signposted to the list of mainstream schools and mainstream schools with specialist provisions Educational Settings | SEND Plan.Where a parent expresses a preference for an Irish-medium school in response to their child’s statement of SEN, the EA will consult with that school regarding the child’s placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of how bilingualism can assist people with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: As reported in Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta’s (CnaG) recent report ‘Provision for Special Educational Needs and Disabilities in Irish Medium Education’ (November 2024), the area of special educational needs (SEN) and bilingualism is under-researched.  However, some research indicates that there can be benefits of bilingualism for children with SEN in relation to the development of speech and language and social skills and improved employment opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQO 1222/22-27, which Department is responsible for legislating to regulate property management companies.
answer_text: This subject does not lie within the remit of my Department. I am aware that there is ongoing engagement between DoF and DfC on this matter. As such, it is for DoF and DfC Ministerial colleagues to confirm the position in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether concerns have been raised with his Department in relation to a perceived bias in the recruitment process within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department is not aware of any concerns raised in relation to a perceived bias in the recruitment process within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. Recruitment is the responsibility of HSC Trusts as employing organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any international best practice work his Department has carried out on the implementation of bilingualism or multilingualism in special educational needs settings.
answer_text: My department is a member of the European Agency for Special Needs and Inclusive Education (the Agency), whose main aim is to help member countries improve their inclusive policy and practice in the field of education.  The Agency provides member countries and stakeholders at European level with evidence-based information and guidance on implementing inclusive education systems.  My department will continue to access the research available from the Agency as it moves into delivery of change for learners with special educational needs (SEN), including those who are bilingual and multilingual.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any representations from Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta to his Department have been reflected in the Education Authority SEND Transformation Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) have been active members of the governance structure throughout its Special Educational Needs/Disability (SEND) Transformation Project and that they have strived to factor in the Irish-medium perspective and action CnaG’s recommendations wherever feasible within the scope of the project.  Irish-medium schools are also represented on the SEND Transformation Project Practitioner Forum and their views have been taken into account.  My department recognises the need to include all sectoral bodies in shaping transformation for learners with SEN.  A working group is being established, which will involve CnaG, to address any gaps in provision for children with SEN in Irish-medium education (IME).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff that are (i) currently working in encompass; and (ii) projected to work in encompass in the next five years.
answer_text: From September 2023 the equivalent of 310 staff were employed in the Regional team to deliver the deployment of encompass to the Trusts.  The planed optimisation phase, post deployment in all 5 Trusts, will reduce staffing to 190 posts by 2027. These are funded through to December 2033.Across the HSC Trusts, a total of 243 posts were built into the business case during the implementation phase of the programme.  These posts were expected to fall to 25 posts by 2027.  However discussions on final numbers, to ensure the new capabilities of the system are embedded and the system optimised to meet organisational and clinical priorities, continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the legally binding obligations contained in the Climate Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to reduce emissions and the high energy demands from data centres that could impact emissions targets, whether his Department is considering the introduction of (i) specific policy; and (ii) guidance for councils on planning and data centres.
answer_text: All planning applications will be assessed against the provisions of the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, Local Development Plans, guidance, the views of statutory consultees, and all other material considerations. Whilst I have no plans at the present time to bring forward new regional strategic planning policy or guidance specifically for data centres, my Department will keep the matter under review. As you are aware, responsibility for local planning policies, rests with Councils, under the two-tier, plan-led system. Councils are, therefore, free to bring forward local policies and guidance to address certain planning matters in their areas, such as specific categories of development, if considered necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to reports that NI Water and NI Water Alpha workers are balloting to take industrial action, when her Department will approve the business case for the pay award.
answer_text: The 2023-24 NI Water Pay Remit Business case was approved by my Department on 4 December, and an approval letter was issued to the Department for Infrastructure Permanent Secretary this morning (5 December).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Translink will receive its budget.
answer_text: My Department allocated the 2024-25 budget to Translink earlier this year. The 2025-26 budget has yet to be agreed by the Executive and the Assembly. Consequently, it is not possible to provide a timeframe for the confirmation of a 2025-26 budget to Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff working within medical records in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust does not have a specific "Medical Records" category on their current HR system nor are staff working on such duties centrally managed in one organisational structure.  My officials therefore cannot provide a definitive number of staff engaged in such activities. However, Trusts and the encompass programme have agreed that there will be no redundancies amongst staff working with medical records because of the implementation of encompass.  Trusts through their Management of Change processes will redeploy or otherwise support affected staff to move into other, suitable roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of posts within medical records are projected to be lost due to the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: There will be no redundancies amongst medical records staff because of the implementation of encompass. Trusts have agreed, through their Management of Change processes, to redeploy affected staff to other, suitable roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are standardised practices for those working in student support services.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that the six regional colleges all adhere to circulars from the Department for the Economy, which outline processes for supporting students. In effect, this means that students attending any of the Further Education colleges will broadly have the same working practices resulting in positive outcomes for the students they work with.	Responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA) is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Providers. As such, the classification of student support providers is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the social housing waiting list for each Common Landlord Area in East Belfast in the last five years, broken down by (i) those with Full Duty Applicant status; and (ii) those in housing stress.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19033/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the social housing waiting list for each Common Landlord Area in North Belfast in the last five years, broken down by (i) those with Full Duty Applicant status; and (ii) those in housing stress.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19032/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the social housing waiting list for each Common Landlord Area in South Belfast in the last five years, broken down by (i) those with Full Duty Applicant status; and (ii) those in housing stress.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19031/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the social housing waiting list for each Common Landlord Area in Derry and Strabane in the last five years, broken down by (i) those with Full Duty Applicant status; and (ii) those in housing stress.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19030/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether the current remit of note-takers within student support services reflects the full duties that they have to undertake.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that the current remit of note-takers within Further Education colleges Student Supports Services does reflect the full duties that they have to undertake. Their job descriptions outline the role that they have to play in supporting the students to whom they are assigned. Responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA) is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Institutions. As such, the remit of note-takers within student support services is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the training opportunities available for student support providers.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that within Further Education colleges Student Support Providers attend mandatory training and induction which includes a full overview of student support services relevant to their College setting. These Support Providers have a dedicated line manager who will organise relevant internal and external training. In each of the six Further Education colleges, Student Support Providers are provided with individual training, advice and guidance relevant to the needs of the students they are supporting. These Support Providers can also avail of specific and individual staff development opportunities relevant to their role, or training provided at College CPD days in line with all other College employees.Responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA) is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Institutions. As such, the training opportunities available for student support providers is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, when deciding how student support providers are recruited and retained, to detail how their payment is decided.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that college Student Support Provider positions are recruited by advertising through normal college advertising procedures using GetGot and the College websites.  Pay scales and rates are determined through the Further Education Non-teaching staff negotiating committee on an annual basis. Student Support Staff are placed on a pay band commensurate with the duties and level of responsibility involved in their role. 	With regard to Higher Education Institutions, responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Institutions. As such, recruitment and retention of student support providers is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the recruitment process for employing student support providers.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that recruitment is managed by the relevant College Human Resources team in conjunction with the appropriate Head of Department.  Positions are advertised both internally and externally through normal College advertising procedures using GetGot and the College website. Shortlisting and recruitment are managed directly by each College Human Resources department. Where positions have not been filled, and are required as a matter of urgency, these are temporarily managed using recruitment agency staff. 		Responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA) is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Providers. As such, the recruitment process for employing support providers is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether student support providers are classed as workers, employees or self-employed.
answer_text: My officials have contacted the Further Education colleges and have been advised that Student Support Providers in the Further Education colleges - including roles such as note-takers, Learning Support Assistants, Learning Support Officers, Inclusive Learning Assistants, Inclusive Learning Mentors or 1:1 Specialist Support Tutors- are classed either as workers or employees. Student Support Providers may be employed by the College on a support staff contract or may be agency workers engaged via recruitment agencies.  With regard to Higher Education Institutions, responsibility for administration of Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA) is delegated to the Education Authority, with local arrangements delivered by the Higher Education Institutions. As such, the classification of student support providers is a matter for individual universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the fire safety systems that are fitted in Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The fire safety systems installed in Belfast Grand Central Station are fitted in accordance with building control requirements and relevant standards for a public building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15500/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of practice fire drills that Translink has carried out in Belfast Grand Central Station in 2024.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that Translink emergency evacuation exercises and fire drills were carried out in advance of opening both the bus and rail elements of the building to the public.  The fire alarm is tested weekly and drills carried out every six months. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the cost of the fire safety systems installed in Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) how often these systems have been tested.
answer_text: I can advise that:The total cost for the fire safety system in Belfast Grand Central Station is approximately £1.5m.The various elements of the fire safety system are tested at the legally required intervals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why some Direct Payment carers are not being permitted to undertake delegated healthcare tasks, despite having received relevant training from Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The legislation covering the use of Direct Payments (DPs) to address identified care needs stipulates the type of care provision for which DPs can be used.  My Department is aware that issues have arisen in some Health and Social Care Trusts around the delegation of healthcare tasks and regrets any disruption to care, or distress experienced, by those individuals and families affected.  My Department is urgently undertaking a review of the process of such care provision and is engaging with stakeholders and families to address the issues that have arisen. It is anticipated that recommendations on the way forward will be brought to my attention early in the New Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any sources of financial support available to social work students undertaking unpaid work placements.
answer_text: Student social workers who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland can access a non-means tested Student Incentive Scheme of £4,000 from my Department, for each year of their study. In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. Payments in relation to the Student Incentive Scheme are in addition to any student finance support which can be applied for separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of personal assistants employed via the Direct Payments system that are permitted to provide social care only, as a proportion of total personal assistants, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide the number of Personal assistants employed across the region via the Direct Payment system nor the number that are permitted to provide social care only tasks.  The reason for this is that the recipient (service user) of a Direct Payment is the employer and may choose to have more than one Personal Assistant deliver their care and they manage and decide this themselves.Direct Payments are commissioned following an assessment of need by the Health and Social Care Trust.I can provide however, the number of people who are in receipt of a Direct Payment across the region at quarter ending 30th June 2024, see table below:BHSCT1067NHSCT1049SEHSCT1522SHSCT1488WHSCT1774TOTAL6900

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 21 November 2024, to detail what reduced sickness and associated backfill through more effective sickness and backfill management entails.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 21 November 2024, to detail what pursuit of procurement efficiencies entails.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has a number of active directorate procurement groups, which work closely with BSO PALS (Procurement and Logistics) colleagues to ensure effective procurement of goods and services across the Trust and to identify and deliver on opportunities for cost containment and procurement cost savings via benchmarking and tendering processes.“Pursuit of procurement efficiencies” refers to this ongoing work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 21 November 2024, how enhanced staff skill-mix initiatives will work alongside his Department’s commitment to safe staffing.
answer_text: Multidisciplinary and inter-professional working and training are a key theme within the Department’s current Workforce Strategy which states:Health and social care teams have the right skills mix to provide the right care and support efficiently, effectively and with compassion.The proposals within the Safe and Effective Staffing legislation consultation outlined plans to place a range of staffing matters on a statutory footing. This includes workforce planning to meet the needs of multiprofessional working to provide the correct staffing skills-mix.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 21 November 2024, to detail where discretionary spend including estates savings will be made.
answer_text: The Written Ministerial Statement dated 21 November 2024, contained specific references to savings plans including discretionary spend for two HSC Trusts; namely Belfast and Southern.Belfast Trust report that targets for estates and discretionary spend savings contained within the Belfast Trust Savings Plan 2024/25 include the following:A range of estates energy and building efficiency initiatives such as building analytics, battery storage and water usage.  The first of these initiatives are planned to commence from mid-December 2024.Discretionary spend savings commenced in September 2024 and involves reducing spend across the Trust in categories such as Furniture & Fittings, Paper & Card, Postage and Printing & Stationery.  The areas were selected due to low risk and low impact on staff and service users.Southern Trust report the following savings targets in 2024/25 in respect of discretionary spend (including estates):A reduction in discretionary spend of £300k with targeted cost reduction in Advertising, IT Consumables, Furniture & Fittings and Printing/Stationery costsReduction in Energy costs of c£620k due to efficiencies gained from implementation of Combined Heat and Power Units, Roof and Cavity Insulation, LED Lighting and Photovoltaic (Solar) Cells (under Invest to Save capital investment schemes)Reduction of the amount spent on maintenance and minor works of £750k with patient facing areas and high risk, critical and urgent work being prioritised

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work she has undertaken to reduce the number of workers on insecure, temporary and zero-hours contracts.
answer_text: The increased use of non-permanent workers in recent years is due to a number of factors for example a previous embargo on recruiting permanent staff, uncertainties around budgets, servicing a contract with the Department of Work and Pensions (DWP), and pressures arising from Covid and the Windsor Framework.To reduce reliance on non-permanent workers, my department has launched number of high volume openly advertised recruitment campaigns (for which non-permanent workers could apply) to employ permanent staff and nearly 150 recruitment competitions are currently being progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work she has undertaken to increase the number of workers earning the Real Living Wage.
answer_text: As a Living Wage employer since 2021, all Civil Service staff here receive at least the real Living Wage. In addition, through Public Sector Pay Policy, which my Department is responsible for setting, I strongly encourage other public sector employers to do likewise. Further, Public Procurement Policy requires that payment of the real Living Wage must be included as a condition of contract for all tenders from June 2022. I am also pleased the Economy Minister, Conor Murphy MLA, has supported Advice NI to set up Living Wage NI to promote the real Living Wage and accredit Living Wage employers in the North.  In that regard, I was particularly pleased to hear that it has recently celebrated the milestone of 100 accredited employers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of forced outages that have occurred as a result of the shortage of supply of fuel in the last five years.
answer_text: There have been no forced outages of the electricity system as a result of the shortage of supply of fuel in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the amount of oil stored in Northern Ireland is sufficient to cover foreseeable supply chain disruptions such as adverse weather events, industrial action, war, sanctions on countries supplying oil to the UK and the blockade of shipping routes.
answer_text: My officials work closely with the various stakeholders within the North’s Energy Industry to collaborate on emergency planning for such events.The North is served by three oil terminals: Puma, Exolum and LSS. The number of days of oil storage in the north will vary based on supply/demand and commercial purchasing strategies.My Department undertakes an annual Winter Preparedness Assessment with the Norths energy industry in advance of the winter period. This includes liaison with all three oil terminals in which assurances are sought on a number of factors, including that of fuel stocks and contingency mitigations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of days of oil is in storage in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The North is served by three oil terminals: Puma, Exolum and LSS. The number of days of oil storage in the North will vary based on supply/demand and commercial purchasing strategies.My officials remain in contact throughout the year with the three oil terminals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount Capita will be awarded for the (i) Northern Ireland Functional Assessment Service; and (ii) Health Assessment Advisory Service.
answer_text: The total cost of the Functional Assessment Service Contract with Capita is £112.2m. This cost may fluctuate depending on claimant demand, inflation and any future changes required to the contract.The Health Assessment Advisory Service is the name of the service provided under the Functional Assessment Service Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what was contained in the revitalisation bid for the Housing Executive that was submitted to the Public Sector Transformation Board.
answer_text: The interim board is currently working through the proposals, and it would not be appropriate to share details of this until this process has concluded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of property management companies' compliance with existing companies legislation.
answer_text: Question 10 – AQO 1222/22-27There is a robust framework in place for ensuring compliance with existing companies legislation and there are a variety of agencies with remits for tackling non-compliance, for example Companies House, the Financial Conduct Authority and the Police.Nevertheless, compliance with companies legislation does not always guarantee fair and scrupulous behaviour, and where this is the case the responsible Departments will consider if additional regulation or other action may be necessary.My Department is willing to cooperate with other Departments in that regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the potential cut in the out-of-hours homeless service for the Housing Executive; and (ii) when a final decision will be made.
answer_text: Following the October Monitoring Round, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has been allocated an additional £3.7 million for homelessness and an additional £3 million to support service delivery.The Housing Executive has confirmed that given the critical importance of the Homeless Out of Hours service there are no plans to either scale down or stop the service during 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for removing the provision of Irish language classes from HMP Maghaberry.
answer_text: Provision of prison educational programmes is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Prison Service.  The curriculum offered is continually reviewed to ensure it meets the needs of people in prison, and any decisions on changes to delivery are the responsibility of the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service.I can however confirm that Irish Language has not been removed from the current curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the operational challenges preventing the laying of regulations in relation to (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Notices; and (ii) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders.
answer_text: Ensuring that there are effective protections in place for victims of domestic abuse is a key priority for me as Justice Minister.  Whilst my Department has undertaken significant preparatory work to bring forward Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders (DAPNs & DAPOs) in progressing this work with operational partners a number of significant operational challenges have been identified. I am clear that it is important that we get the model right and avoid creating an unworkable or ineffective system.   Operational challenges that have been identified relate to:the timeframe within which a DAPO must be brought before the court following a DAPN being issued. PSNI has advised that the timeframe that the Home Office has set for England and Wales is not operationally viable within Northern Ireland. Given the ECHR considerations involved I want to explore this further, including by learning emerging lessons from a new pilot that the Home Office has launched in England; andthe high volume of anticipated cases. My assessment is that without significant additional resource being made available to fund them, DAPNs and DAPOs could not be introduced in Northern Ireland without placing unworkable and unsustainable pressures on PSNI and constraining PSNI’s ability to carry out its other functions.  There would also be a significant impact on court time.In addition, whilst I recognise the potential value that DAPNs and DAPOs could bring if we get the model right, I do not believe that the current model, which is based on the Home Office model, would deliver sufficient added protections for victims in Northern Ireland. Without further additional funding to invest in programmes that could be attached as positive obligations to DAPOs (e.g. to address domestic abuse drivers such as substance misuse, or to promote behavioural change), DAPNs and DAPOs would offer little more protection or intervention than the measures that are already currently available through restraining orders, Non-Molestation Orders or police bail conditions.  It is important that we do not rush to put in place new and as yet untested measures that may provide a false illusion of added protection, which could inadvertently increase the risk posed to victims.   	I understand that the Home Office launched a DAPN / DAPO pilot on 27 November 2024, which is expected to run for at least a year across a number of areas in England.  I think it is important that we use this pilot to test the model and to benefit from valuable learning, including around the benefits for victims and the operational viability of the 48 hour window to bring a DAPO to the court. 	In parallel, my officials will continue to work with operational partners to explore all options with a view to developing the most appropriate model for new protective measures that will help to protect victims of domestic abuse in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, as part of the awarding of the Northern Ireland Functional Assessment Service contract, to detail (i) the number of organisations that (a) applied; and (b) expressed interest in providing the service; and (ii) any consideration his Department gave to providing this service in-house.
answer_text: The number of organisations that applied and expressed an interest for the Functional Assessment Service contract for Northern Ireland was 3, and 5 respectively.A Business Case concluded that a single external provider represented best value for money, as in-housing the service would cost an additional £93m over a ten-year period. The Department do however complete PIP award reviews in-house where sufficient evidence is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of providing purpose-built accommodation to support women who have been through domestic violence or abusive situations.
answer_text: Purpose-built accommodation is one of the options available to support women who have been through domestic violence or abusive situations. Suitability may vary depending on circumstances. Capital funding from the Social Housing Development Programme can be provided for the provision of new purpose-built accommodation or re-modelling of existing accommodation to support people experiencing or at risk of domestic violence or abusive situations.  The commissioning of suitable and affordable accommodation is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought information from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:‘The Supporting People (SP) Programme grant funds the provision of housing support services to enable vulnerable people to live as independently as possible in the community.  The ‘Women at risk of Domestic Violence’ client group is supported through SP funded short-term accommodation-based refuge services, providing support for those in housing need.  SP expenditure (2024/25) on accommodation-based refuge provision is £3.05m. Funding 14 refuges with a total of 138 units. Proposals for services grant funded through the SP programme are required to come through the associated SP commissioning structures. These proposals may or may not include capital for new build or redevelopment.’The NIHE also offer its Sanctuary Scheme free of charge to all their tenants. This is a multi-agency, victim-centred initiative to enable households at risk of domestic abuse to remain safely in their own homes by installing a ‘sanctuary’ in the home, once the perpetrator has left.The primary objectives of the scheme are to:Reduce repeat incidents of domestic violence;Minimise the disruption of having to move home and/or becoming homeless;Enable families to remain in familiar areas and close to support networks, friends and family; andEnable any children to remain in their schools and to maintain friendships and support networks.
