answer_text: The Intercultural Education Service (IES) have implemented a temporary adjustment to their operational processes based on its first-hand experience of working with families who have achieved the right to remain and are relocated from Home Office provided accommodation to Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) non-standard emergency accommodation.  The decision is based on the fact that significant progress has been made in recent months to reduce the length of time spent in non-standard NIHE accommodation and current trends now show families usually receive an offer of a more permanent housing solution within the community within a short number of weeks.  In light of this it is not considered in the best interest of the child to relocate from the school attended during their period seeking asylum to another school in the vicinity of their non-standard NIHE accommodation, only for further relocation being required upon a housing solution being arrived at.  As the change in IES practices is operational, temporary in nature, and in the best interests of families and children involved, consultation was not undertaken.The approach adopted does not preclude families approaching schools directly to register children, should they wish, rather it only refers to the service offered by IES. Where a family requests an immediate placement, they will be provided with guidance on how to approach schools and signposted to support from school admissions colleagues in the EA.An update on this process will be shared with the Education Authority’s Joint Consultative Forum next week and with the Refugee and Asylum Forum so that all of their members are briefed on the temporary arrangement. The temporary arrangement will be reviewed at the mid-term point at the end of October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to communication received from the Education Authority, to provide the source that indicates that the average stay for migrant, refugee and asylum-seeking families in hotel accommodation has fallen to four to five weeks.
answer_text: The main source of this estimate is the Home Office, and data available from its accommodation provider MEARS, as well as data on accommodation trends reported to the inter-agency ‘Move-Ons’ coordination group. The available data provides the opportunity to track changes in move-on trends on a weekly basis.  The accommodation provider has confirmed to the EA that they have improved availability of housing stock and  families should receive offers of housing in a timely period after arrival into a contingency hotel.Further to this, the EA’s front-line Intercultural Education Service (IES) hotel team have witnessed a quicker turnaround period in recent months.  This has led to the situation where initial school placements agreed by IES quickly becoming inappropriate as families are moved.   The needs of every child are considered on an individual basis and IES will immediately place children where it is clear that their stay in temporary hotel accommodation will be for a longer period of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans he has in place to assist elderly people in paying for the delivery of their medical prescriptions.
answer_text: Community pharmacy services are commissioned in accordance with a 3-year commissioning plan for 2022-2025, developed by the Department and agreed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI).Medicines delivery services are not a commissioned service in the current commissioning plan.Decisions as to whether to offer, withdraw or charge for such non-commissioned services are a commercial matter for individual pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of empty homes, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. The Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services (LPS) maintain a register of Vacant Domestic Properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the creation of the role of a housing commissioner.
answer_text: No assessment has been carried out on the creation of the role of a housing commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the new track and signalling, installed as part of the Belfast Grand Central Station project, was approved for use by trains.
answer_text: The new track and signalling, installed as part of the Belfast Grand Central Station project, has not yet been approved for entry into service for passenger trains. Translink commissioned the track and signalling for testing and training purposes on 12 August. This a necessary stage in the process for Translink to demonstrate compliance with technical and safety requirements. Once this is complete the safety authority will be in a position to provide authorisation for the rail systems and passenger services will commence thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount Translink has spent on (i) designing; and (ii) fitting 26 access doors to Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The cost for design, supply and fitting of the 26 access doors at Belfast Grand Central Station is approximately £294,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount Translink has spent on private hire buses to provide rail substitution bus services since Great Victoria Street station closed on 10 May 2024.
answer_text: The cost of private hire buses to provide rail substitution bus services, since the closure of Great Victoria Street Railway Station is part of the overall project and was £1,183,565, as of 13th September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure on what date Babcock handed over Belfast Grand Central Station rail services to Translink.
answer_text: Babcock handed over the railway track at Belfast Grand Central Station to Translink for operational training on 12th August 2024.  The date for the transfer of the station platforms is still to be agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that have received the (i) NSPCC Talk PANTS programme; and (ii) NSPCC Speak out Stay safe programme, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Housing Supply Strategy will be (i) completed; and (ii) published.
answer_text: I am in the process of finalising the Housing Supply Strategy and it is my intention to bring it to the Executive for its consideration shortly.  Publication will be dependent on any forthcoming approval by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of correspondence he has received from NIPSA in relation to the Housing Executive or housing issues since taking office.
answer_text: Since I took office, I have received two items of correspondence from NIPSA in relation to the Housing Executive or housing issues, dated 12th February 2024 and 29th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the impact the 2024-25 budget will have on the Housing Executive’s out of hours service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.‘The Housing Executive recently published a Response to Department for Communities Budget 2024-25 Equality Impact Assessment https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/f70cd07c-4ddc-4ae3-93d2-119e042322ec/NIHE-Response-to-DFC-Budget-EQIA-24-25.pdf which noted ‘The Housing Executive provides an ‘out of hours’ service to people experiencing homelessness, to ensure services are available around the clock. Demand for this service has grown by 1,370% in the past five years. The current indicative allocation puts the provision of this service at risk.’ For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made of the potential impact on excess winter deaths as a result of the decision to means test the Winter Fuel Payments.
answer_text: The decision to implement changes to the Winter Fuel Payments was taken by the Executive based on a recommendation brought by the Communities Minister which outlined the severe financial implications of not aligning to the decision taken by the Westminster Government.  No assessment has been made by the Department of Health of the potential impact on excess winter deaths, given the timescale this change was brought forward.  The Department for Communities has also confirmed they did not undertake an assessment as a result of the Government’s decision to means test the Winter Fuel Payments and advised any assessment on the impact of the Winter Fuel Payment would be a matter for the Department of Work and Pensions.While the priority and focus will continue to be improving health inequalities across Northern Ireland, there continue to be significant financial pressures facing my Department, and difficult decisions need to be taken as a consequence of these pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of making prevention of homelessness a specific new statutory obligation.
answer_text: My officials are actively engaging with the Housing Executive to determine whether legislative change is required in respect of Homelessness. Any legislative change would need prioritised for inclusion in a Legislative Programme, agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether contracts with GP practices can be amended to ensure they provide training for frontline staff in supporting refugees and people seeking asylum to access their services.
answer_text: This is not a contractual matter; GPs are independent contractors.I am content that training is available to all GP practices to support all new comers to Northern Ireland to access their services if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being undertaken to ensure that providers of accommodation for asylum seekers share written and verbal information on accessing GP services with people seeking asylum soon after they have settled into accommodation.
answer_text: I am content that the providers of accommodation for asylum seekers share written and verbal information on accessing GP services with people seeking asylum, during their orientation process. For example, an information booklet has been produced by the HSC, which explicitly outlines how to access a GP. It is available on-line and in multiple languages and is provided to all asylum seekers when they arrive in Northern Ireland. Multiagency Support Hubs are also available across Northern Ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of implementing a specialist service for people seeking asylum that provides person-centered, tailored information support and casework to enable greater access to health and social care services.
answer_text: In addition to full and equal access to universal health and social care services, asylum seekers, who arrive in Northern Ireland, are provided with targeted services. For example, in 2023/24, approximately £1m was allocated to Trusts, GPs, Interpreting Services and Multi-Disciplinary Teams to provide additional support to refugees and asylum seekers, with around 4000 additional interventions delivered by Trusts alone. Each Trust now has a Social Inclusion Worker to support refugees and asylum seekers to access HSC services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Protect Life 2 Strategy has met its target to reduce the rate of suicide by 10 per cent by 2024.
answer_text: The 10% reduction target in the Protect Life 2 Strategy is indexed against a baseline, calculated from the 3 year rolling average annual rate of suicide for the period 2015-2017 (16.5 deaths per 100,000 of population). That 10% reduction equated to a target of 14.9 deaths per 100,000 to be measured for the period 2021-23. The most recent 3 year figures relate to the period 2020-22 and show a suicide rate of 11.5 deaths per 100,000.However, this reduction is largely due to NISRA publishing a revised suicide series following the completion of a review of suicide statistics in Northern Ireland. This review has reduced the number of deaths classed as undetermined with these more likely to be found as either intentional self-harm or as accidental. The number of deaths classified as suicide has therefore reduced in recent years. This has made the original target inaccurate.With the NISRA review now complete a more appropriate baseline will be set as part of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan which is currently in development. The 10% reduction target will then apply to that revised baseline. I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans in place to simplify and implement a uniform scheme around food labelling.
answer_text: Food labelling policy represents multiple stakeholder interests and divisions of responsibilities across the United Kingdom (UK).  In Northern Ireland, both my Department and the Food Standards Agency (FSA) own certain aspects of food labelling policy.  Requirements of labelling are tailored to the type of commodity and where the commodity rests in the food supply chain.  Simplification of general requirements where feasible, is achieved through ongoing liaison across the UK with Competent Authorities at a number of specific “Common Framework” groups, which meet to review and ensure alignment. The FSA in Northern Ireland is responsible for food labelling policy relating to the provision of food information to consumers.  The provision of food information ensures a high level of protection by providing a basis for consumers to make informed choices and to make safe use of food.  Food information rules cover a broad range of information presented to consumers, which must not mislead consumers and must also be accurate, clear and easy to understand. Policy development for this area falls under the UK’s provisional Common Framework arrangements, which ensures a four-nation approach to policy.DAERA has responsibility for the regulation of aspects of beef labelling at slaughter and cutting plants, labelling of eggs at production, packing, wholesale premises and labelling associated with marketing standards that falls within DAERA’s remit.  The labelling requirements are set down in relevant EU legislation and there is no flexibility to simplify these specified elements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the information that patients are provided before being prescribed antidepressants.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). NICE guidance is reviewed locally for its applicability to Northern Ireland and, where found to be applicable, is endorsed by the DoH for implementation in the HSC.The following relevant NICE guidelines have been endorsed for use within HSC NI relating to depression:NG 222: Depression in adults: treatment and managementNG 215: Medicines associated with dependence or withdrawal symptoms: safe prescribing and withdrawal management for adultsThese guidelines outline the information that should be considered and discussed with the patient, including steps to reduce the risk of dependence or withdrawal symptoms before starting antidepressant treatment. They also provide clear guidance on how these medications should be reviewed in practice, taking individual patient clinical need into account.The Transforming Medication Safety (TMSNI) strategy outlines our approach to ensuring the safe use of medicines in Northern Ireland. The roll-out of the WHO 'Know Check Ask' medication safety campaign in 2022 was designed to increase public awareness of the importance of using medication safely and supporting people to be more involved in decisions about their medication. In addition, a Northern Ireland Yellow Card Centre was established to provide a mechanism for patients, care givers and healthcare staff to report suspected safety concerns associated with healthcare products, including medicines and is vital to ensuring patient safety.The Department of Health’s Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework is the key strategic driver of improvement in the safe and effective use of medicines in Northern Ireland. The Framework stipulates that all patients on repeat medication should receive a clinical medication review with a GP or pharmacist at least annually, or more frequently depending on the individual’s care plan or type of medication.  Pharmacy teams are integrated into all care pathways that involve the use of medicines to ensure appropriate help and support is provided to people who are in receipt of medication for their mental ill health.Within primary care all GP practices participate in the Local Enhanced Service which outlines a number of key objectives for GP practice including a requirement to: perform at least an annual medication review for prioritised at risk patients e.g. polypharmacy, care home resident, frail and elderly; andreview medicine use in line with evidence-based guidelines and prescribing policy at a national and local level.Medicines optimisation relies on partnerships between patients and healthcare professionals to support patients to self-manage, to take their medicines correctly, reduce harm, avoid taking unnecessary medicines, cut down on waste and improve medicines safety.  This approach encourages patients to take increased ownership of their treatment and support care closer to home.  Prescribers have access to up

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of administrative workers employed in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust on (i) 1 September 2008; and (ii) 1 September 2024.
answer_text: Information on the number of staff employed in Health and Social Care (HSC) by staff group and organisation at quarter end, including five-year trend data, is published by my Department on a quarterly basis at the link below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/staff-numbersThe table below shows the whole-time equivalent (WTE) of Administrative & Clerical staff employed in the Belfast HSC Trust at the latest date available, quarter ending 30th June 2024, compared to the same position in 2008.  Information at quarter ending 30th September 2024 will be available from 20th November 2024. Administrative & Clerical Staff in Post in Belfast HSC Trust (WTE)Administrative & Clerical Staff in Post (WTE)30 June 200830 June 2024Belfast HSC Trust3,117.33,363.4Source: Information & Analysis Directorate (IAD)Excludes bank staff and staff on career break.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there will be any further fees on top of the initial £250,000 for the magnetic pouch pilot scheme.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the delivery of a small-scale pilot scheme to evaluate the use of mobile phone inhibiting devices in schools will cost a maximum of £250,000.  I do not foresee any further fees or costs relating to the pilot scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what reviews are carried out on patients who are prescribed antidepressants for an extended period of time.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). NICE guidance is reviewed locally for its applicability to Northern Ireland and, where found to be applicable, is endorsed by the DoH for implementation in the HSC.The following relevant NICE guidelines have been endorsed for use within HSC NI relating to depression:NG 222: Depression in adults: treatment and managementNG 215: Medicines associated with dependence or withdrawal symptoms: safe prescribing and withdrawal management for adultsThese guidelines outline the information that should be considered and discussed with the patient, including steps to reduce the risk of dependence or withdrawal symptoms before starting antidepressant treatment. They also provide clear guidance on how these medications should be reviewed in practice, taking individual patient clinical need into account.The Transforming Medication Safety (TMSNI) strategy outlines our approach to ensuring the safe use of medicines in Northern Ireland. The roll-out of the WHO 'Know Check Ask' medication safety campaign in 2022 was designed to increase public awareness of the importance of using medication safely and supporting people to be more involved in decisions about their medication. In addition, a Northern Ireland Yellow Card Centre was established to provide a mechanism for patients, care givers and healthcare staff to report suspected safety concerns associated with healthcare products, including medicines and is vital to ensuring patient safety.The Department of Health’s Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework is the key strategic driver of improvement in the safe and effective use of medicines in Northern Ireland. The Framework stipulates that all patients on repeat medication should receive a clinical medication review with a GP or pharmacist at least annually, or more frequently depending on the individual’s care plan or type of medication.  Pharmacy teams are integrated into all care pathways that involve the use of medicines to ensure appropriate help and support is provided to people who are in receipt of medication for their mental ill health.Within primary care all GP practices participate in the Local Enhanced Service which outlines a number of key objectives for GP practice including a requirement to: perform at least an annual medication review for prioritised at risk patients e.g. polypharmacy, care home resident, frail and elderly; andreview medicine use in line with evidence-based guidelines and prescribing policy at a national and local level.Medicines optimisation relies on partnerships between patients and healthcare professionals to support patients to self-manage, to take their medicines correctly, reduce harm, avoid taking unnecessary medicines, cut down on waste and improve medicines safety.  This approach encourages patients to take increased ownership of their treatment and support care closer to home.  Prescribers have access to up

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the student loan system where students repay their loan when they earn around £22,000, when students in the UK repay their loan when they earn £27,500; and (ii) whether he has made any representation to the UK government in relation to this issue.
answer_text: While my preference is for free higher education and to keep costs as low as possible, we are required to work within the frameworks and existing parameters set by the British Government.The repayment threshold against which borrowers from here will start to repay loans increased from £22,015 to £24,990 in financial year 2024/25 and will increase further to £26,065 for financial year 2025/26. New borrowers in England (from 2023) are required to repay on earnings above £25,000.    The repayment terms and conditions for borrowers from here continue to be favourable compared to other administrations, taking account of the repayment threshold, interest rate charged and write off-period.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the violent attacks and disorder in August 2024, to detail any actions being taken to protect racial and religious minority groups.
answer_text: Officials continue to deliver the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025. Of the eleven proposed actions in the Strategy, the majority of actions have been completed, with the remaining actions intended to continue throughout the lifetime of the Strategy.In addition to the strategy and its outcomes a Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG) was formed and is chaired by the Executive Office, in partnership with the NI Housing Executive, PSNI, and other government departments, to consider the preventative measures and interventions that can be delivered to create an inclusive environment for all. A number of options were identified, including an area-based approach potentially funded under PEACE Plus, and work is ongoing in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the violent attacks and disorder in August 2024, to detail any action being taken to mitigate a rise in anti-racial sentiments and prevent such incidents occurring again.
answer_text: One of my priorities as Justice Minister has been to strengthen current provisions on hate crime and improve the confidence of victims of hate crime in the system that brings offenders to justice.Existing legislation is being used to sanction hate crime offenders who have been engaged in the recent anti-migrant disorder and expressed anti-racial sentiments. Addressing hate and reducing anti-racial sentiment requires sustained cross-government action, with an emphasis on preventative actions, focused on building social and community cohesion. It is my intention to work with the Executive Office, who have primary responsibility for race relations, to ensure communities are integrated within society, and negative attitudes and behaviours are tackled, including how good relations work can be broadened to create cohesive and inclusive communities where people of all faiths and ethnicities are valued and respected.My officials have been working with other Departments to tackle the enabling factors of hate crime and align respective strategies and policies, for example, with The Executive Office (TEO) to connect the TEO Racial Equality Strategy and Good Relations Programme with the Department’s work on Community Safety.The Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025 provides a framework for government departments (and others) to tackle racial inequalities, to eradicate racism and hate crime and along with Together: Building a United Community, to promote good race relations and social cohesion.My Department also supports PCSPs whose work includes awareness raising and education initiatives on reducing hate crime within local communities such as providing bilingual advocacy services and hate crime prevention sessions. There is no short-term solution to tackling these complex issues but one of the starting points is to show strong political and community leadership which condemns the targeting and threatening people from ethnic minority communities and rejects anti-migrant sentiment. My Department is committed to playing its part in delivering a safer community for all, including victims of race hate crimes, and in partnership with others in Government and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of whether there is economic inequality in West Belfast compared to other areas in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any actions being taken to ensure an overall equitable distribution of wealth in the region.
answer_text: There are economic inequalities across the North, including West Belfast.  My actions to increase the number of people in Good Jobs and to promote Regional Balance will help ensure that everyone shares in the benefits of prosperity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the anti-racist protests on 9 August and 10 August, (i) who funded the 150 additional police officers brought in from other parts of the UK; and (ii) to detail the overall cost.
answer_text: The requesting force is responsible for meeting the costs associated with mutual aid deployments. In a recent information gathering exercise the Police Service of Northern Ireland submitted a bid to the Department of £2.75m of which £543,800 is the estimated cost of the mutual aid assistance provided by 80 officers from Police Scotland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the violent attacks and disorder in August 2024, to detail any actions being taken to protect members of the LGBTQ+ community.
answer_text: I share and understand your concerns about the recent violent attacks on members of minority ethnic communities and the escalation in racially motivated hate crimes, threats and disorder all of which I unequivocally condemn.  Racist attacks on members of minority ethnic communities, as with all kinds of intolerance, is a blight on our society that needs to be addressed by all those within it.   I also understand the impact this has on all minority communities, including the LGBTQIA+ community, and the intersectional nature of these impacts.Work is already ongoing within my Department to combat hate crimes generally; improve the criminal justice system for victims of hate crime through the development of foundational hate crime legislation; and to support victims of all crime, including LGBTQIA+ hate, through the Victims and Witnesses Strategy.  The Hate Crime Advocacy Service (HCAS) supports victims of hate crime through the criminal justice process, signposts them to relevant support services, and can assist with third party reporting to the PSNI. Providing guidance and practical assistance in the aftermath of a hate crime offers a vital level of support to victims at a critical time.  There is a dedicated LGBTQIA+ hate crime advocate based in The Rainbow Project. Their work in providing one to one support to victims of hate crime when they may be feeling at their most vulnerable and powerless has proved to be a very valuable resource for victims in their engagement with the criminal justice system.  However, it is clear that there needs to be a cross-departmental approach with an important emphasis on preventative actions, focused on societal attitudes and community cohesion.  This preventative work includes building relationships and support within communities to develop more cohesive communities, which can challenge attitudes to behaviours that may lead to LGBTQIA+ hate crimes and prevent them happening in the first place.  The Executive’s LGBTQIA+ Strategy sits under the umbrella of the Sexual Orientation Strategy and is led by the Department for Communities (DfC).   I can assure you that my Department is committed to playing its part in delivering a safer community for all in partnership with others in Government and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the wealth inequality gap in relation to life expectancy; and (ii) to detail any work being undertaken to address this.
answer_text: Health inequalities, including the gap in life expectancy between the most and least deprived areas of Northern Ireland, remain a particular challenge, which my Department actively seeks to address through all its activity.The Health Inequalities Annual Report produced by my Department assesses deprivation inequality gaps in health outcomes using the Multiple Deprivation Measure (MDM) which is made up of 7 domains of deprivation: Income, Employment, Health Deprivation & Disability, Education, Skills & Training, Access to Services, Living Environment, and Crime & Disorder. The Health Inequalities Annual Report can be found at www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/health-inequalities-annual-report-2024 .Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address these inequalities. The framework was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. It is important to note that research has shown that only 20% of health outcomes are driven by the clinical care we receive, with the rest related to the socio-economic and physical environment in which people live and a range of health and lifestyle behaviours. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing.MLB is also underpinned by a number of strategies designed to help improve population health and wellbeing and reduce inequalities through empowering people to live healthier lives. These include strategies to address substance use and smoking, suicide and self-harm prevention, breastfeeding promotion, obesity prevention and skin cancer prevention.The work undertaken under MLB also aligns with the development of the Integrated Care System for NI which will be underpinned by both population health and Outcomes-Based Accountability approaches to planning, focusing on how we can improve the health and wellbeing of the population and reduce health inequalities. This will be reflected through cross-cutting strategic considerations such as prevention and early intervention, wider social determinants of health and reducing health inequalities.You will be aware from my recent announcement in the Assembly that health inequalities are one of my main priority areas as Health Minister and consideration is currently being given to a targeted, place-based approach to help address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is aware if the PSNI has shared any files or documentation in relation to the Dublin and Mongahan bombings in 1974 with those undertaking Operation Denton.
answer_text: PSNI’s cooperation with Operation Denton is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work being carried out to increase the local housing allowance rate.
answer_text: Responsibility for reviewing Local Housing Allowance lies with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions who has committed to an annual review of Local Housing Allowance. I wrote to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions on 5 March 2024 requesting that, going forward, Local Housing Allowance rates are considered with a view to maintaining pace with private sector rents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the affordability of the private rented sector.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the affordability challenges in the Private Rented Sector. Research carried out by my Department concluded that one of the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters was by ensuring the benefit system properly takes account of the cost of housing.The increase in Local Housing Allowance rate from April 2024 should then go some way to assist many renters with affordability of rent costs. I will be advocating for these rates to keep track of the market rents, so this does not prove to be a very temporary fix for our lower income households.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals in non-standard temporary accommodation that had to leave that accommodation in the last five years due to the venue increasing the cost of accommodation approaching a holiday period.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  As such, I have sought a response from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that the Northern Ireland Housing Executive does not record the information you have requested.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11590/22-27, and in light of the information provided by the Appeals Commission in a letter dated 27 June 2024, how his Department will provide clarity to the public in relation to their advice that that there is no requirement to repeat or resubmit representations on the new Dalradian abstraction and impoundment applications if they had previously made representations on previous applications.
answer_text: My Departmental officials considered the Appeals Commission letter, of 27 June 2024, and subsequently responded on 3 July 2024 to seek the Commissions views on an acceptable way forward which includes all previously submitted representations being accepted by the Commission and being included within the inquiry process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13384/22-22, whether he took legal opinion on the lawfulness of his Department’s position that there is no necessity for members of the public to repeat or resubmit representations on the new Dalradian abstraction and impoundment applications if they had previously made representations on previous applications.
answer_text: My Department's position was informed by consideration of all relevant factors, including legal advice.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff in his Department that are currently working on future pandemic preparation.
answer_text: A dedicated Pandemic Resilience and Response Branch sits within the Department’s Emergency Resilience and Protecting Health Directorate. The branch was established in 2023 and currently has 5 staff members reporting to the Director of Emergency Resilience and Protecting Health. The branch leads and coordinates work in this area engaging regularly with the wider Directorate as well as the operational side of the Department (Strategic Planning and Performance Group).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of unsuccessful tenancy successions of Housing Executive homes that have occurred in West Belfast since 2020 due to a succession already having taken place.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive does not hold or collate the information in the format requested.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as part of the footpath extension scheme at Belfast Grand Central Station, what consideration has been given to (i) those with mobility issues; and (ii) pram users.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with Translink in considering the needs of various user groups, including individuals with mobility issues and pram users, during the design of the new station, to ensure that access is compliant with Disability Discrimination Act (1995) regulations. The new footpaths will create an inclusive and accessible environment for all users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out any research into the health benefits of a four-day working week.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any research into the health benefits of a four-day working week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths recorded in West Belfast in the last five years where mental health illness was cited as the main cause of death.
answer_text: NISRA produces official statistics on death registration information as collected by the General Register Office. The information requested is presented in Table 1 for the years 2018 to 2022. Detailed geographic and cause of death data for 2023 will become available in autumn this year after the release of the Annual Report of the Registrar General. To represent West Belfast, the Assembly Area boundaries from 2008 were used, and ‘Belfast West’ was selected. Geographical information associated with death registrations is based on the usual address of the individual, as supplied by the informant of the death. In the absence of usual address information, place of death is used. Cause of death information provided through the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death is standardised using the World Health Organisation (WHO) ICD-10 framework to facilitate comparisons across countries. For this response ICD-10 codes starting with ‘F’ (codes representing ‘Mental illness and behavioural disorders’) were selected. This group of causes incorporates, but is not limited to, conditions such as dementia, schizophrenia, behavioural and emotional disorders, personality disorders and mental and behavioural disorders due to psychoactive substance use.Table 1: Number of deaths in Belfast West Assembly Area where the underlying cause of death was a mental illness or behavioural disorder.YearNumber of deaths201881201981202081202171202299Note: year is based on the year the death was registered which may not always match the year the death occurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13031/22-27, when the report on the review of arm's-length bodies will be issued to the Executive.
answer_text: The timing of the report to the Executive will depend upon the progress in conducting reviews within departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that NSPCC job cuts in its Childline service will have on children and young people.
answer_text: My Department sought further information from NSPCC after learning of the proposed changes to the Childline service.  While staffing decisions are solely a decision for NSPCC, it has advised that the proposed new approach will be able to support significantly more children and young people across the UK. My Department will continue to engage with NSPCC to ensure that it continues to support the maximum number of children and young people as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13266/22-27, to detail the engagement he has had with arm's-length bodies in relation to the commissioned reviews.
answer_text: New Decade, New Approach reviews which have been completed for the NI Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY) and the Commissioner for Older People NI (COPNI).  The reviewers engaged with Commissioners and other stakeholders during the course of the reviews. Review reports have been provided to me for consideration and my officials have engaged with and will continue to engage with both Commissioners.  The review of the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC) is nearing report stage.  The reviewer has engaged closely with NILGOSC throughout the review process.  My officials have also engaged closely with NILGOSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 12973/22-27, whether all reviews of arm's-length bodies by Departments must be completed prior to the introduction of a Public Bodies Bill.
answer_text: The introduction of a Public Bodies Bill will depend upon the decision of the Executive.  This will be informed by the status of Departments’ reviews of arm’s-length bodies but is not determined by the completion of all reviews.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the actions being taken to support the existence and growth of grassroots music venues.
answer_text: My Department supports the existence and growth of grassroots music venues primarily through the funding distributed via my Department’s Arm’s Length Body, the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.  In 2023/24, with both Government and Lottery funding, the Arts Council were able to provide £600k to 7 venues across Northern Ireland.The Arts Council recently supported the Oh Yeah Music Centre’s successful application to the National Lottery Community Fund to strengthen the organisation and develop strategic plans.The Music Venue Trust’s recent development of a network of venues in Northern Ireland in partnership with Help Musicians UK and in its 2023 Pipeline Investment Fund Scheme, in partnership with Belfast City Council, is notable for the support of five Belfast venues for a range of capital and training needs.  This demonstrates the important role local councils play in supporting music venues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of recent comments made by the Earl of Shaftesbury, to detail his plans in relation to publicly acquiring Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I am on record as stating that my own preference is for community ownership of Lough Neagh and I am currently seeking a meeting with the Earl of Shaftesbury to discuss his comments in more detail. However, I welcome the Lough Neagh Heritage Resilience Project currently underway, led by the Lough Neagh Partnership, which is considering the future management of Lough Neagh including ownership.  I believe that it is appropriate for this work to be undertaken whilst also personally engaging with the Earl of Shaftesbury.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of times the issue of Lough Neagh has been discussed or been on the agenda at Executive meetings in the last five years.
answer_text: The content of Executive papers and all aspects of Executive business are regarded as confidential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of occasions he and his officials have met with the Earl of Shaftesbury over concerns around Lough Neagh in the last five years.
answer_text: I have met once with the Earl of Shaftesbury on 21 February 2024 at his request, to discuss the blue green algae situation in Lough Neagh.  I am currently in the process of scheduling a further meeting with the Earl following his recent comments on the potential for transfer of ownership of Lough Neagh.  I am advised by my officials that no other meetings with the Earl of Shaftesbury have taken place in the last 5 years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13266/22-27, to detail the number of arm's-length bodies within his Department that are to be reviewed, in line with commitment set out in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: In line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment my Department has identified nine Arm’s Length Bodies for inclusion in the review process. Three of the reviews have been commissioned to date - the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY), the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland (COPNI) and the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC).  Consideration will be given to the remaining six reviews in line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work being carried out to ensure healthcare workers, who are in receipt of Universal Credit, will not financially lose out when receiving their back pay.
answer_text: The amount of Universal Credit a person receives is likely to be reduced if their earnings increase, including the receipt of backpay. However, based on individual circumstances, a person may be eligible for a work allowance enabling them to earn a certain amount before their Universal Credit payments are reduced.  Claimants will also keep £0.45 of every £1.00 they earn over the work allowance. Further information on how Universal Credit treats earnings can be found at Universal Credit if you're employed | nidirect.		My Department is not carrying out any work to review or change the rules on the treatment of earnings, which apply equally to all Universal Credit claimants who receive earned income.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that Translink fare increases will have on households with lower incomes.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While public fares were frozen between 2019 and 2023, Translink continued to deliver services within a constrained budget and an increasing cost base. Consequently, an increase in fares has been required to ensure the future delivery of services. Nevertheless, the Concessionary Fares Scheme remains available to provide free and discounted fare travel to some of the most vulnerable in our society.  For example, Translink offer reduced bus and rail travel for the unemployed and those who are currently claiming Jobseekers allowance.Translink also offer promotional fares at weekends and for families, such as the Translink Family and Friends ticket which offers unlimited travel on all Translink services for up to 2 adults and 4 children.  In the meantime, the value of our public transport services cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases of wrongful arrest of (i) refugees; and (ii) asylum seekers that have occurred in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons arrested wrongly by police.  The recording of information in relation to persons arrested by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the need for a tool library for apprentices to access; and (ii) what role his Department can take in creating a tool library.
answer_text: My Department is undertaking research to identify how to best support individuals, including those from low income backgrounds, throughout their participation in the apprenticeship programmes. An enhanced range of support interventions, including provision of tools and equipment, is under consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) when his Department will bring forward legislation to establish a climate commissioner’s office; and (ii) the date by which his Department aims to appoint the commissioner.
answer_text: Development of the Regulations required under Section 50 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 is progressing well. These Regulations will be a first step in establishing the NI Climate Commissioner.We will consider the draft Regulations in the weeks ahead at which point next steps can be determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases of stop and search by the PSNI, involving (i) refugees; and (ii) asylum seekers under the age of 18, that have occurred in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons stopped and searched by police.  The recording of information in relation to persons stopped and searched is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the plans to host 2028 UEFA European Football Championship matches, what action his Department has taken to address (i) traffic issues; (ii) parking issues; and (iii) noise pollution around Casement Park.
answer_text: The application for Casement Park redevelopment (LA04/2017/0474/F) was accompanied by an environmental impact assessment. Planning approval was granted on 28 July 2021 subject to 46 conditions and a planning agreement under section 76 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011. Conditions attached to the permission include mitigation for working hours, noise impacts, and traffic management. The development is to be carried out subject to a Sustainable Traffic Plan to encourage the use of alternative modes of transport to the private car; an Event Management Plan in the interest of sustainability and to encourage the use of transport other than the private car; and a Service Management Plan to ensure the development is adequately serviced in the interests of road safety and traffic progression.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the discussions that have taken place with external agencies in relation to procedures for the installation of percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes.
answer_text: No discussions have been held with external agencies or independent sector providers on the potential for their involvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that are awaiting a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube.
answer_text: Information on the number of children in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) that are awaiting a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube is not held centrally. The Belfast HSCT was, therefore, asked to provide a response.The Belfast HSCT responded that, on 24 June 2024, there were 89 children (up to and including 16 years of age) on the waiting list for a percutaneous endoscopic gastronomy tube.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the actions his Department is taking to build more houses in West Belfast.
answer_text: The very constrained budget for new social housing starts in 2024/25 means that almost all resources will be targeted towards completion of homes that are currently under construction. Across Northern Ireland, there are 5,700 social homes currently under construction: 1,114 of which are in West Belfast Constituency.I have also been advised by the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) as follows:“There have been 560 social homes completed in the West Belfast Constituency in the last 5 years. There are currently 656 social homes programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27 in the Constituency, subject to Budget availability”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the actions his Department is taking to reduce housing waiting lists in West Belfast.
answer_text: The principal means of meeting social housing need is through the re-let of existing social housing accommodation (stock turnover).  Where the volume of re-lets is insufficient to meet housing need, then the construction or acquisition of further (new) social housing is required. The delivery of social housing remains a priority for me but, although my Department will continue to spend most of its capital budget on the delivery of new social homes, the funding available this year will severely constrain the number of new starts possible this year in all areas of Northern Ireland including West Belfast. NIHE, as the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, will now consider how the budget may best be used to meet identified housing need.My Department continues to work with the NIHE to bring forward measures to maximise the Social Housing Development Programme.  One of the critical issues is the provision of suitable land in areas of housing need.  My officials are working to identify public lands held by Departments that can potentially be used for the development of social housing.  They are also looking at ways in which we can: make delivery more timely; bring more stability to the programme; and to explore how issues and pressures associated with existing delivery might be addressed.Work is ongoing to implement changes to the Housing Selection Scheme to make the best use of our existing stock by improving and streamlining processes; enabling the waiting list to move more effectively.My Department also provides funding for intermediate housing, to complement social housing supply.  My Department is overseeing the development of a broad range of intermediate housing products, the purpose of which is to increase the overall housing supply and reduce housing stress.In parallel, my Department has developed a draft Housing Supply Strategy that aims to provide a long-term framework for policies that can increase supply across all tenures. I am currently reviewing the Strategy which has been refreshed to ensure it remains accurate and aligns with current policy. I will bring a finalised Strategy to the Executive for its consideration and approval as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to assist people in West Belfast to get an appointment with a GP.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic.  Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in response to increasing pressures. In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. With regard to individual GP practice arrangements, GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to expand (i) clinical and office space; and (ii) Picture Archiving and Communication System access, to accommodate clinical oncology, diagnostic radiology and interventional radiology trainees, as recommended by the Royal College of Radiologists.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss the findings of these reports in detail, including their predictions of shortfalls in the current and future number of clinical oncologists and clinical / interventional radiologists.My Department is committed to the provision of effective cancer and imaging services in Northern Ireland, ensuring patient safety and quality of care.  A Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022 – 2032 sets the overall direction.  By 2024/25, £10.88m will have already been invested to stabilise HSC Trusts’ oncology and haematology services.  This funding has secured an additional 167.6 WTE posts across the full range of healthcare disciplines delivering oncology and haematology services.  The resultant growth in whole time equivalent (WTE) Consultant Radiologists, Specialty Doctors, Consultant Radiographers, Specialist Radiographers and Radiographers is shown in the following table.Stabilisation of HSC Trusts’ Oncology and Haematology ServicesIncrease in WTE funded posts: Consultants/Specialty Doctors/Consultant Radiographers/Specialist Radiographers/RadiographersStaff typeOncologyHaematologyConsultant9.75 WTE7.58 WTESpecialty Doctor8.0 WTE8.0 WTEConsultant Radiographer/Specialist Radiographer6.5 WTERadiographer3.0 WTETOTAL27.25 WTE15.58 WTEGRAND TOTAL42.83 WTESpecific cancer pathways are currently being developed, which will inform future strategic workforce planning to address workforce requirements for clinical oncologists and other professions.  In respect of imaging services, my Department’s Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.Following a robust options appraisal process by an independent expert panel, the Mater Infirmorum Hospital site in Belfast HSC Trust has been identified as the optimum location for the propos

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to encourage pupils from a working-class environment in West Belfast to participate in further education.
answer_text: My Department funds the Careers Service, which is an all age, all ability service that provides careers information, advice and guidance.  Furthermore, Further Education colleges within or close to West Belfast have linkages with local community groups to encourage those from all backgrounds to access further education provision through a variety of training, reskilling and upskilling programmes, which are offered at different levels. Promotional activities by colleges include School outreach, advertisement billboards, recruitment events and college open days. My Department also funds a number of student support services which are available to eligible students, with an aim to remove financial barriers to those who wish to avail of further and higher education opportunities across the region, including West Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated to the redevelopment of schools in West Belfast in the last five years.
answer_text: Capital Expenditure in Schools in the West Belfast Constituency over last 5 YearsRedevelopment of Schools Capital Expenditure2019/20 £000s2020/21£000s2021/22    £000s2022/23   £000s2023/24 £000sTotal      £000s8,7338,0323,7026,6716,15333,292The above table comprises capital expenditure through the Department’s Major Works, School Enhancement, Minor Works and Fresh Start Agreement programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the recommendations made by the Royal College of Radiologists (RCR) in their 2023 Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology Census report, whether he will (i) put in place a plan for staff retention in radiology and oncology; and (ii) meet with the RCR to discuss the findings of their report.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss the findings of these reports in detail, including their predictions of shortfalls in the current and future number of clinical oncologists and clinical / interventional radiologists.My Department is committed to the provision of effective cancer and imaging services in Northern Ireland, ensuring patient safety and quality of care. This funding has secured an additional 167.6 WTE posts across the full range of healthcare disciplines delivering oncology and haematology services. In respect of imaging services, my Department’s Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to ensure Health and Social Care Trusts have sufficient funding to maintain and expand the number of training places they offer for clinical oncology and clinical radiology trainees.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss the findings of these reports in detail, including their predictions of shortfalls in the current and future number of clinical oncologists and clinical / interventional radiologists.My Department is committed to the provision of effective cancer and imaging services in Northern Ireland, ensuring patient safety and quality of care.  A Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022 – 2032 sets the overall direction.  By 2024/25, £10.88m will have already been invested to stabilise HSC Trusts’ oncology and haematology services.  This funding has secured an additional 167.6 WTE posts across the full range of healthcare disciplines delivering oncology and haematology services.  The resultant growth in whole time equivalent (WTE) Consultant Radiologists, Specialty Doctors, Consultant Radiographers, Specialist Radiographers and Radiographers is shown in the following table.Stabilisation of HSC Trusts’ Oncology and Haematology ServicesIncrease in WTE funded posts: Consultants/Specialty Doctors/Consultant Radiographers/Specialist Radiographers/RadiographersStaff typeOncologyHaematologyConsultant9.75 WTE7.58 WTESpecialty Doctor8.0 WTE8.0 WTEConsultant Radiographer/Specialist Radiographer6.5 WTERadiographer3.0 WTETOTAL27.25 WTE15.58 WTEGRAND TOTAL42.83 WTESpecific cancer pathways are currently being developed, which will inform future strategic workforce planning to address workforce requirements for clinical oncologists and other professions.  In respect of imaging services, my Department’s Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.Following a robust options appraisal process by an independent expert panel, the Mater Infirmorum Hospital site in Belfast HSC Trust has been identified as the optimum location for the propos

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the evidence presented to him by representatives of the Royal Town Planning Institute during the meeting on 12 June 2024 to discuss addressing misinformation about the planning system; and (ii) whether he will initiate steps to address these claims.
answer_text: I met with representatives of the RPTI on 12 June, at its request, to discuss a range of issues such as the ongoing Planning Improvement Programme, the issues that the planning system here is currently experiencing and how to ensure that there are better informed discussions around planning in the future. The issue of wellbeing of members was also a discussion topic, and the RTPI advised that their member surveys are consistently telling them that the wellbeing of planners, particularly public sector workers, is a current concern with issues such as constant public criticism and public abuse on social media particularly relevant.To that end, I am happy to support the RTPI to help raise awareness of the planning profession and the work that it undertakes, and how all of those involved in planning in any way can work collectively to achieve better, more efficient ways of working and communicating.
