question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what measures they can take to promote human rights considerations for journalists among public authorities.
answer_text: We are committed to the protection of human rights of everyone in our community.  The Human Rights Act 1998 provides the key legal protections of conventional human rights. This requires each public authority – including departments and the PSNI - to act compatibly with convention rights laid out in that Act.  If a citizen considers that these rights are not being protected, the Act itself ensures that there is a remedy.  Our department seeks to promote the rights of the public in a number of ways including working with the NI Human Rights Commission to provide training, advice, and guidance to NICS staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what measures he is taking to ensure no child is without a school placement in September 2024.
answer_text: Almost 44,000 children applied to primary and post-primary schools this year through the open enrolment admissions procedures and as of now there are 43 children remaining to secure a placement.To enable us to get to this point my Department has allocated over 900 additional places across both phases to ensure that where oversubscription occurred, that demand could be met.Furthermore, I recently approved 97 additional Year 8 places and 61 further Year 1 places through the Normalisation programme.  This has secured additional capacity at oversubscribed schools not just this year, but also for future years.My Department will continue to create additional places where required to ensure every child can access a place.  Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) (almost 40% of all children with SEN) follow a different process for admission whereby their school placement is named in their final statement of SEN.  This process is highly individualised and is based on a statutory assessment and consultation process between the Education Authority, schools and parents.  There are no opening and closing dates as with the school admissions processes, meaning that comparison with the general admission process is inaccurate.It is my aim that all children have an appropriate school placement for September 2024.  However, the position in relation to placements for children with special educational needs remains challenging for September 2024.  The Education Authority, supported by my Department, is continuing its programme of engagement with schools to create sufficient additional specialist education places to meet demand for September 2024.   A Taskforce led by the interim Chief Executive of the Education Authority meets on a weekly basis and is leading on a very focussed programme of work to address the issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's review of arm’s-length bodies.
answer_text: In line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment my Department has commissioned reviews for three of its Arm’s Length Bodies so far - the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY), the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland (COPNI) and the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC).  Consideration will be given to further reviews in line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who he directly consulted with regarding the implementation of an application fee for a Translink SmartPass.
answer_text: The option on whether to introduce an application fee for a SmartPass was part of the Department for Infrastructure’s recent public consultation on the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme. In addition to an online survey, the Department published pdf, Easyread, sign language and subtitled versions of the consultation. Officials also held face to face and online focus groups with older people, people with disabilities and minority ethnic groups and engaged directly with councils and representative organisations, including the NI Consumer Council, Age NI, the Older Person’s Commissioner, IMTAC and the Refugee and Asylum Forum.On 12th June, I announced my intention to introduce application fees in relation to the SmartPass in order to protect the Concessionary Fares Scheme for the future.  However, the final decision is subject to the consultation on the EQIA on the budget for DfI for 2024/25, which is available on the Department’s website. I would encourage all those who have any further comment to make on the introduction of fees for the SmartPass to respond to the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has requested a full breakdown of the journalists and others who had their communications data accessed by the PSNI.
answer_text: I have been clear that I fully respect the independent oversight of the Chief Constable by the Policing Board.   As the ongoing work on this matter should continue without political interference I have not, at this point, requested the details of any person involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the findings of the NASUWT survey into the special education sector, to detail how schools in West Belfast have been impacted.
answer_text: Officials have requested additional information from NASUWT in relation to the survey to enable detailed consideration of the matters raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to list the organisations that his Department (i) has consulted with; and (ii) will consult with as part of his Good Jobs policy.
answer_text: Good jobs policy cuts across work done throughout my Department and Arm’s Length Bodies. As such good jobs is an issue that has been raised in a range of contexts with a wide range of individuals and organisations as part of the normal departmental policy work and I cannot list all of these. This work has also been informed by Lisa Wilson who I have asked to provide advice on this issue. Specifically in relation to planned legislation, my officials have engaged with the following organisations to take their initial views on matters that may be subject of my upcoming Good Jobs Consultation:Carers NIChartered Institute of Personnel and DevelopmentConfederation of British Industry Employment Lawyers Group NIEquality Commission NIFederation of Small BusinessesHospitality UlsterInstitute of DirectorsIrish Congress of Trade UnionsLabour Relations AgencyLRA Engagement ForumManufacturing NINevin InstituteNI Chamber of CommerceNI Retail ConsortiumRetail NITiny LifeWorker Info Exchange/GMBThe Good Jobs Consultation will seek input from the public and stakeholders and it is hoped that a wide variety of organisations will take the opportunity to respond. A proactive communications strategy will also be deployed throughout the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the number of children and young people in West Belfast who are in housing with no adequate space to play.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the requested information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the extent to which the Housing Executive and housing associations take room to play for children into consideration when collating housing points.
answer_text: Assessment for points under the Housing Selection Scheme is based on housing need. Play space for children is not considered in the awarding of points.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeframe for when Health and Social Care Trusts are required to make agency workers permanent.
answer_text: Agency Workers are contracted and paid by the Agency who employs them. As they are not directly employed by Health and Social Care Trusts they do not have the right to request permanency with the HSC in accordance with the Fixed Term employees regulations (NI) 2002.  This would only apply to staff directly employed by Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to reduce corporate profit margins and rebalance them towards workers.
answer_text: Taxation, including setting corporate tax rates, is a reserved matter under the control of the British government.My Department is developing various initiatives to enhance workers' rights and economic well-being.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the increased corporate profits of Randox Holdings.
answer_text: Randox reported profits of £225m in 2021 and £171m in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans in place to roll-out further electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available EV charge points here. The British Government publishes periodic updates of the number of publicly available EV charge points by both Local Council area, and Parliamentary constituency which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below.Zero emission and electric vehicles - GOV.UK (www.gov.uk)Latest statistics show that there are 564 publicly available EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 45% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023. Relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points within your constituency.

answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercially led operation. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people in West Belfast that have been subjected to social sector criteria charge for having an extra bedroom in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of households in West Belfast parliamentary constituency impacted by the social sector criteria charge for having an extra bedroom in each of the last five years is shown below. However, it is important to note that Social Sector Size Criteria mitigation has been in place in Northern Ireland since 2017 and following amendments to the legislation in February 2022, all households impacted are fully mitigated.Households with SSSC deduction20192020202120222023Housing Benefit4,6004,0903,7303,4103,120Universal Credit7301,1001,3801,5701,690All5,3305,2005,1104,9804,820NotesData Source: Single Housing Benefit Extract (SHBE) - 2019 to 2023; Universal Credit Full Service (UCFS) system - 2019 to 2023.Figures refer to households that have had a Social Sector Size Criteria (SSSC) deduction at some point during the year.Note that Housing Benefit and Universal Credit housing element are paid at household level.In producing the geographical analysis, records were attributed to the parliamentary constituency based on their postcode. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of unsuccessful applications to the Affordable Warmth Scheme in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“During the five-year period - 1 April 2019 to 31 March 2024 - the Housing Executive has had to cancel 1,807 (10.8%) Affordable Warmth Scheme enquiries prior to approval. Reasons for cancellation vary; the three main reasons are:Applicant’s income exceeded the threshold for eligibility.All scheme energy efficiency measures were present in the property.Failure to respond to requests for eligibility information.For the period 1 April 2019 to 31 August 2023 Affordable Warmth was a targeted scheme, where Local councils had responsibility for targeting, assessing eligibility and referring customers to the Housing Executive. The scheme’s enquiry process changed in 2023.  Since 1 September 2023 all private sector households who contact the Housing Executive’s freephone Energy Advice Service are assessed for potential eligibility.  Those who present as eligible, and therefore may benefit from the Affordable Warmth Scheme, are transferred to the grants team to pursue a possible application.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has been notified of recent incidents of asbestos being burnt in the vicinity of Kennedy Way in West Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that the NIEA, who would investigate such incidents, has not been notified of the recent incidents you refer to. 		NIEA often relies on information provided by members of the public to initiate enquiries and investigations surrounding potential waste offences. We would therefore advise that if any member of the public has information which would help with the detection, prevention, remediation, and prosecution of environmental waste crime, to report the matter to the NIEA’s incident hotline: Telephone 0800 807 060. 		Alternatively, you can contact the NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit by email at: environmentalcrime@daera-ni.gov.uk or Telephone 028 90569453.		You may also report environmental crime through CrimeStoppers, 0800 555 111.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the BBC Spotlight programme broadcast on 4 June 2024, (i) for his assessment of whether his Department's decision not to proceed with a public inquiry into illegal waste disposal has had a detrimental impact on illegal dumping; and (ii) whether he will consider bringing the matter of a public inquiry back to the Executive for consideration.
answer_text: The BBC Spotlight programme broadcast on 4 June 2024 raised a number of serious issues with Lough Neagh of which I am acutely aware and which I am working to address urgently.In relation to your questions around a public inquiry I must point out that it was not my Department but the Executive that on two occasions in 2014 and 2016 did not reach a decision to proceed with a public inquiry. The former Agriculture and Environment Minister in response to subsequent questions from one MLA advised that he preferred to focus on using DAERA’s resources to further the recommended improvements in the Mills review.However, I will consider whether it is appropriate to bring the matter of a public inquiry back to the Executive taking account of the likely costs and benefits of the exercise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any representations she has made to the (i) Chief Constable; and (ii) Policing Board in relation to releasing the names of journalists who had their communications data accessed by the PSNI.
answer_text: The Policing Board, as the independent oversight body, holds the Chief Constable to account and I have been clear that this should continue without political interference. I have not made any representations to either the Chief Constable or the Policing Board requesting the release of any names.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of community funding provided by his Department to groups in West Belfast in (i) 2009; and (ii) 2024.
answer_text: My officials have reviewed data from the European Structural Investment Fund Database (ESIF) for the Rural Development Programme (RDP) 2014-2020, the Microsoft database for RDP Programme 2007-2013, and the Government Funding Database for payments made to community and voluntary organisations, and there was no funding provided by DAERA to community groups in West Belfast in 2009 and 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of applications for rates relief that were unsuccessful in the last five years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) administer a range of application based rate relief schemes for domestic rates. These include Lone Pensioner Allowance, Disabled Persons Allowance and the Rate Rebate Scheme. Until June 2022, LPS also administered Housing Benefit for those that owned and occupied their own homes. LPS makes awards to around 470,000 properties per rating year after assessing significant numbers of paper based applications.Unsuccessful applications do not result in a credit transaction to the rate account, so it is not possible for LPS to provide statistics on applications that were unsuccessful for all domestic rate relief schemes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Home Office in relation to the Hostile Environment Policy.
answer_text: Although immigration policy is an excepted matter falling to Westminster, we recognise the significant impact of these policies locally.  Our officials meet regularly with Home Office on immigration matters to represent local issues and concernsAdditionally, we are members of the Interministerial Group on Safety, Security and Migration alongside Ministerial colleagues in Home Office and the other Devolved Administrations. Immigration policies were scheduled to be discussed at the meeting in May, however this was postponed due to the pre-election period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 10153/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department maintains and publishes a record of arm's-length bodies reviews and outcomes, as outlined in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain and publish a record of arm's-length bodies reviews and outcomes. The review of arm's-length bodies as outlined in New Decade, New Approach is the responsibility of individual departments and Ministers.  My Department will be requesting departments to advise on the current status of their reviews of arm’s-length bodies and it is intended that this will inform a report to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of officials in their Department that provide input to answers to Assembly Written Questions.
answer_text: Assembly Written Questions may be asked in relation to any of our departmental responsibilities.   All officials may therefore potentially be required to provide input to the answers to those questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of street lights currently not working in West Belfast; and (ii) his Department's plans to repair them.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain this information by electoral constituency, however the number of reported street light outages for Eastern Division, which includes West Belfast, as of the 11 June 2024 was 93.Currently we are providing a full street lighting maintenance service, with the majority of outages being repaired within the established target of five working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total amount spent on the encompass programme.
answer_text: To date committed spend equates to £91.4m. Total expenditure will be approximately £300m (the bulk of which is coming from DoH capital funding) over the course of the next 10 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of investment by Invest NI in West Belfast in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23, ending 31st March 2023, being the most recent year available.   The table below details the amount of assistance that Invest NI offered towards projects in the West Belfast Parliamentary Constituency in 2022-23, and the amount of total planned investment that this support has secured for the area.  Total planned investment is the combination of business investment and assistance offered by Invest NI in support of a businesses’ project.In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.Information relating to performance on a Parliamentary Constituency basis in 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release at the end of summer 2024.Invest NI assistance offered and investment secured in West Belfast Parliamentary Constituency (2022-23)YearAssistance Offered £mTotal Planned Investment £m2022-230.502.34

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department's preparation of legislation that will enable Departments to make the changes required as a result of the review of arm's-length bodies, as set out in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: Drafting of a Public Bodies Bill has been started.  Completion of the drafting and introduction of the Bill is dependent upon the completion of departmental reviews of arm's-length bodies and the decision of the Executive to an agreed approach.  Timing of any legislation will be subject to prioritisation by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any offers he has made to junior doctors to help avert industrial action.
answer_text: My officials have been actively engaged with BMA representatives to seek to find an acceptable path forward. Independent arbitration has also been offered to facilitate these talks. That offer has not been taken up to date. The 2023/24 pay award, which involves an average pay increase of 9.07% for junior doctors, will be in June pay packets. The budgetary settlement provided to my Department does not afford any scope for a pay deal which might go beyond this above-inflationary award, rather it proposes a real-terms cut of 2.3%. I shall, however, pursue any and all avenues to improve upon the financial opportunities which might be presented to my Department.My officials have been clear in their conversations with BMA that the best route to securing additional funding is via a value for money business case supporting a modernised contract. There has been no further movement from the Junior Doctor Committee, who have reiterated their wish for full pay restoration outside of any changes to their contract and terms and conditions.  I am of the mind that a modernised contract, which will realise better outcomes for junior doctors and employers alike, is the key to securing pay additionality, and would urge the BMA JDC to re-engage with my officials to develop contractual proposals. This would facilitate the development of an appropriate business case and a request for approval from the Department of Finance of a negotiating envelope.Until such matters are agreed, and a broad framework of a contract sketched, it is simply not possible to commence any scoping which could then inform a bid to the Department of Finance for a formal negotiating mandate and any associated financial envelope within which pay additionality could be accommodated.I have, however, written to invite the Chair of the BMA (NI) Council and the individual Committee Chairs to discuss the pressures we will collectively face in 2024 and beyond.The health and wellbeing of our population remains the primary concern and I am happy to re-affirm that the door remains open to the BMA (JDC) to engage in further dialogue on these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of media reports of breaches of environmental laws in relation to discharged waste, whether he will review the number of fines issued to Moy Park.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates a number of Moy Park’s processing sites under the Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 (PPC). These PPC permits contain emission limits on discharges to the sewer network and it was breaches of these limits which were mentioned in recent media reports. 		The Pollution Prevention and Control (Industrial Emissions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 do not contain powers to issue fines directly to operators. They do contain powers to take other types of enforcement action, up to and including prosecution.	Breaches of the permitted emission limits on discharges to sewer at the Moy Park sites detailed in the programme were categorised as low risk as the discharge is further treated at the wastewater treatment works before final discharge to the watercourse. NIEA officials have been working with Moy Park and NI Water to address these breaches and ensure plans are in place to ensure compliance at both the Dungannon and Craigavon sites where the majority of the breaches have occurred.A number of additional wider actions are also being explored including reviewing working protocols between NIEA and NI Water in terms of enforcement of trade discharge consent breaches and how to monitor and respond to cases of lower risk but cumulative trade discharge consent breaches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for not introducing a stand-alone hate crime bill.
answer_text: I am committed to providing a robust legislative response to hate crime, to supporting victims and ensuring that action is taken against offenders.The loss of two years of Assembly business time and the constraint on resources in the Department and the wider justice system has significantly restricted the range of legislative reforms possible within in the remainder of this mandate. Departments have been advised that legislation should be restricted to one bill per year with any additional requirements being addressed on a “one in, one out basis”.  In this context, there is insufficient time and resource to accommodate the introduction of a stand-alone Hate Crime Bill, which was to be the fourth bill of the mandate. I therefore have brought forward the key provisions to be delivered in the current mandate as part of existing legislative vehicles.Whether legislation is delivered through a standalone bill or as part of a wider piece of legislation, the provisions will be the same, and the core element of a statutory aggravator model will remain the foundation for all other provisions. It is the content and impact of hate crime legislation and its urgent deliver, rather than the vehicle, that are my primary concerns.There are two options available for legislation that I am considering. One is through the Sentencing Bill, and the other is through the Victims of Crime Bill which is scheduled for introduction towards the end of the mandate. I am committed to ensuring that the foundational new hate crime legislation provisions will be in in place by the end of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has made an assessment of the affordability of student accommodation.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the cost of living pressures, including the cost of accommodation, faced by students. I am engaging with my officials to consider the current support available to students and to determine how this can be improved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of staff that are part of his new team to address environmental problems in Lough Neagh; and (ii) the job roles of these officials.
answer_text: My officials are currently progressing setting up a Lough Neagh Task Force following confirmation of the budget availability. The additional resource funding of £1.6m will fund in the region of 28 posts which will be split across NIEA regulation and enforcement functions to provide additional inspectors on the ground to complete cross compliance inspections and undertake river inspections to identify sources of pollution. The funding will also strengthen investigative capacity and enhance the department’s ability to accept and process information in order to inform wider decision making around enforcement in response to a range of water quality issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has considered reviewing the industrial derating relief scheme for Moy Park.
answer_text: Support is provided for manufacturing firms where premises are used for an eligible industrial process. In order to benefit from industrial derating, premises must be primarily used and occupied as a factory. The definition of a “factory” is found in Section 175 of the Factories Act [Northern Ireland] 1965. As Moy Park meets the criteria for Industrial derating, I have no plans to review the support provided to them. From my meetings with representatives of this sector, I am aware that any move to unilaterally remove this support may put thousands of jobs at risk within this company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of alleyways that remain unadopted by (i) his Department; and (ii) local councils in the Belfast area.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the inspection or maintenance of unadopted alleyways that are in the ownership of other parties and do not hold details on unadoptions. It is the Department’s policy not to adopt alleyways and pathways which are not essential for safe vehicular and pedestrian access and where the area is already served by a good network of public footways.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of Take Back the City’s proposal to build 750 homes on the former Mackies site in Belfast.
answer_text: I have not assessed Take Back the City’s proposal for the former Mackies site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any meetings he and his officials have held in relation to building social housing on the former Mackies site in Belfast.
answer_text: I have not held any meetings in relation to social housing on the former Mackies site. My officials attended three meetings in 2020 and one workshop in 2021 with interested parties where the site and social housing were discussed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether all taxi drivers have to pay for the Certificate of Professional Competence; and (ii) whether any exemptions apply.
answer_text: The Driver Certificate of Professional Competence is a mandatory training requirement for professional bus and lorry drivers.  A similar requirement for taxi drivers, Taxi Driver Periodic Training (TDPT), was introduced here in 2014, through powers in the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008 and the Taxi Drivers’ Licences Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2014.The purpose of TDPT is to raise the standards of the taxi industry and to develop the current skills of taxi drivers.  Its benefits include improvements to road safety, a better understanding of the environmentally friendly driving techniques and to complement and enhance a taxi driver’s professionalism.All taxi drivers are required to complete 35 hours of TDPT during the validity of their five-year taxi driver licence, before they can renew their taxi driver licence.  There are no exemptions.  The training must be delivered by a training provider authorised by my Department for this purpose, and at least 3.5 hours must be Disability Awareness training.  Training providers determine the cost of the courses they deliver with the course fees being paid directly by the taxi driver.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 12265/22-27, which hotels availed of the Investment Fund.
answer_text: The Fund has supported the following hotel projects to date:Room2 Aparthotel, BelfastHamilton Dock Hotel, BelfastAparthotel, Derry Ebrington Hotel, DerryDunluce Lodge, PortrushLisburn Square (incorporating Haslem Hotel), Lisburn

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any requirements in place for new developments to allocate a specified percentage of homes to people with physical disabilities.
answer_text: When considering allocation for people with physical disabilities, there are two forms of accommodation are considered: Ground Floor or all on one level accommodation and Wheelchair standard.These specialised accommodations will only be considered for allocation to those applicants who require a property of that type unless the list of wheelchair applicants has already been exhausted.  Housing Associations work closely with NIHE’s Regional Place Shaping teams to agree appropriate and sustainable housing mixes for all new build schemes to meet identified housing need in a given location. The departmental target states that 10% of all new build general needs social housing should be wheelchair accessible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time from referral to completion of works for occupational therapy services in West Belfast.
answer_text: The department does not hold this information centrally so the information was requested directly from Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.  The Trust has indicated that the service cannot detail the current average waiting time from referral to completion of works for occupational therapy services as the provision of adaptations for people with a disability requires joint working between health and housing providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the changing of hostels to single-let accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has no current or future plans in the changing of hostels to single let accommodation. Presently under the current policy, the Housing Executive uses both hostels and single let properties as a form of temporary accommodation.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have an outstanding assessment for an occupational therapy appointment in West Belfast.
answer_text: The department does not hold this information at the preferred geographical breakdown centrally so information was requested directly from Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.  Please find their response below.The number of patients that are on the waiting list for a Community Occupational Therapy assessment in west Belfast as of the 30th May 2024 is 328.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work that has taken place to provide an uplift for the Supporting People Programme.
answer_text: I have taken the initial decision in relation to my 2024-25 budget to protect the Supporting People Programme with increased funding of £4.8m to help ensure continued viability of the programme.  This is despite a heavily constrained Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 9858/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 9861/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11590/22-27, whether his Department will be providing the Appeals Commission with an assessment of any new representations received from the public or passing them to the Appeals Commission to consider.
answer_text: My officials will summarise the responses received and provide a copy of each representation to the Appeals Commission. In accordance with regulation 10(2) of the Water Abstraction and Impoundment (Licensing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006, my Department will have regard to any representations received when deciding whether to grant a licence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11590/22-27, whether his answer was informed by (i) legal opinion; and (ii) confirmation from the Appeals Commission that it accepts there is no necessity for members of the public to repeat or resubmit representations on the new abstraction and impoundment applications if they had previously made representations on the deemed refused applications.
answer_text: The decision was taken having considered all relevant considerations. The Department did not seek the Appeals Commission opinion on the representations previously made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 9768/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any powers he has to implement policies which would have an impact on mortgages.
answer_text: My Department has previously considered the merits of introducing a mortgage rescue scheme in Northern Ireland, with research and stakeholder consultation on the Northern Ireland mortgage market being undertaken by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre. The research found that mortgage rescue schemes are expensive to operate and not particularly effective. It suggested that other interventions have been shown to have a greater impact, for example mortgage holidays, payment deferrals and timely advice services. I recognise the importance of good quality advice and my Department has a long-standing relationship with Housing Rights, providing funding for the delivery of direct advice and assistance to members of the public (including mortgage debt advice), assisting other advice agencies and supporting policy development. The provision of such services is vital, helping many people facing housing problems to find a sustainable solution. Officials will continue to closely monitor the impact of interest rate rises on households and work closely with Housing Rights.The Department, under powers in the Welfare Reform and Work (NI) Order 2016,  administers Support for Mortgage Interest which is a government loan scheme to help with the cost of interest on mortgages and certain other home loans for claimants of any of the means-tested benefits, namely, Income support (IS), income-based Jobseekers Allowance (JSA), income-related Employment Support Allowance (ESA), Pension Credit (PC) and Universal Credit (UC). The primary purpose of the scheme is to provide short-term help to prevent repossession by contributing towards mortgage interest payments.  The loan is only recoverable from any equity in the sale or transfer of ownership of the property or when the recipient dies.  If there is insufficient equity to repay the total of the loan, the Department will write off any amount that cannot be repaid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any planned discussions with (i) the Home Office; and (ii) the Irish government in relation to the Gardaí conducting an increased number of immigration checks along the border.
answer_text: Immigration is not a devolved matter and is the responsibility of the Home Office. As noted in my response to AQW 12575/22-27 I do not anticipate any impact on justice issues in Northern Ireland in relation to the Gardaí conducting immigration checks in Ireland and therefore there are no planned discussions with Home Office or with the Irish Government in this regard.I will, of course, continue to raise the potential impact of Home Office immigration policy on areas which are devolved, such as human trafficking and modern slavery, and have also discussed this specific issue with the Independent Anti- Slavery Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact the increased number of Gardaí immigration checks along the border will have on justice matters in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Immigration is not a devolved matter.  I do not anticipate any impact on justice matters in Northern Ireland as a result of the Gardaí immigration checks which are being carried out in Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any circumstances when (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts advise patients to purchase their own medicine from pharmacies.
answer_text: Guidance from the Northern Ireland Department of Health (DoHNI) and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) advises GPs not to prescribe medicines that can be purchased over-the-counter (OTC) by patients when they are being used to treat minor conditions or self-limiting illnesses.Patients are encouraged to self-care and seek advice from their local community pharmacist to help manage these minor and self-limiting conditions.  Numerous resources are available in the Patient Area  of the Northern Ireland Formulary that can be used to promote self-care.A list of conditions and examples of medicines that are readily available and should be bought OTC has been drawn up by DoHNI and SPPG and is available on the Northern Ireland Formulary. Health and Social Care Trusts have confirmed that inpatients are provided with all prescribed medicines during their admission and issued any medicines  required for discharge. The outpatient setting has no facility to supply medicines and recommendations to prescribe any required medicines are issued to the patient’s GP in the form of a written request. Prescribers in these settings may encourage patients to self-care and seek advice from their local community pharmacist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total amount spent on the Going for Growth strategy.
answer_text: Going for Growth was the report of the industry led Agri-Food Strategy Board (AFSB). It made a series of recommendations which informed Government thinking on support to the agri-food industry. The ‘NI Executive Response to Going for Growth’, published in October 2014, outlined agreed actions to be taken by government to address various AFSB recommendations. DAERA does not hold complete and up to date information on the total costs of delivering the NI Executive Response to Going for Growth’ as the agreed actions spanned a number of departments/agencies and were mainstreamed into their business-as-usual activities. DAERA schemes that address Going for Growth recommendations were delivered largely as part of the NI Rural Development Programme 2014-2020, which is now drawing to a close. Total DAERA spend up to March 2024 via the wider NI Rural Development Programme 2014-2020 is £149m. Spend against the relevant actions within the NI Rural Development Programme 2014-2020, including delivery and evaluation costs, is detailed below:Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS) capital - £58m Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS) non-capital (knowledge transfer, innovation, and cooperation schemes) - £22mEnvironmental Farming Scheme (EFS) - £69m.Expenditure related to AFSB running costs was £173k over the period 2012/2013 to 2017/18.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the introduction of creche and childcare provision for Members and staff.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 November 2019, the Assembly Commission was advised that the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Finance, had liaised with the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive, to explore the feasibility of opening a pre-school childcare facility on the Stormont Estate. The intention was that such a facility could be used by Members, Members’ staff, Assembly Commission staff and Civil Service staff working on the Estate.The Department had advised that, at the time, the feasibility of a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate could have been considered as a potential action for inclusion in a draft Childcare Strategy which was being developed by the Department of Education.  In November 2022, the Department informed the Assembly Commission that work on the Childcare Strategy had been paused due to the pandemic, and that it was unknown if the Strategy would now include the provision of a childcare facility, due to the significant changes of the Civil Service workforce within the Estate, notably with regards to Hybrid working.  Subsequently, the Department confirmed in September 2023 that it now had no immediate plans for a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate.  The Assembly Commission has not considered the matter further since.  The Assembly Commission remains open to exploring cooperation on such a project but without other partners, the viability of the proposal is questionable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she will answer AQW 9972/22-27.
answer_text: A response to AQW 9972/22-27 was issued on 22 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 10250/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 10250/22-27 was answered on 29 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work carried out by his Department to create new places for childcare in line with the childcare strategy.
answer_text: The planned data collection programme set out in my Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 will support an assessment of childcare sufficiency within the context of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  This will be informed by cross departmental working, in particular with the Department of Health (DoH), which has statutory and policy responsibility for the supply and demand of childcare provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she will answer AQW 8485/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 8485/22-27 was answered on 28 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the May 2024 report, Campaign to Increase the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme, the Case for Change.
answer_text: My Department has recently received the report ‘Campaign to Increase the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme, The Case for Change’ and is considering its findings.I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking professional training courses due to the cost-of-living crisis. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, the Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional funding that will be made available to organisations in order to expand the preschool education programme to provide childcare.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 set out my plans for early learning and childcare support measures during 2024/25.£5m will be allocated in the current year to commence the process of transitioning all places provided within the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) to 22.5 hours per week.It will take a number of years for all provision to transition to a 22.5-hour weekly session length.  The speed at which this is achieved will largely depend on the resources available from the Executive at each stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount that will be allocated to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: As set out in my Written Ministerial Statement of 23 May 2024, £9m has been earmarked for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme for the period September 2024 – March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when all children will be able to avail of 22 hours per week of free childcare.
answer_text: My statement of 23 May on Early Learning and Childcare measures is published on the Department of Education website and provides details of the package of measures I plan to implement in 2024/25.Parents take a wide range of issues into account when considering childcare arrangements and all funded provision has the potential to contribute to meeting their requirements. This includes attendance at funded educational provision such as school or pre-school. It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to a 22.5-hour weekly session length.  The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage.  I intend to have up to an additional 2,200 22.5-hour places available for September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline where the £24 million from the Spring Budget Barnett consequentials will be allocated.
answer_text: The Executive will make decisions on the allocation of all available funding, as part of the June Monitoring round.This will include the £24 million arising from the application of the 124% needs-based adjustment factor to the Spring Budget following the agreement of an Interim Fiscal Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she or her officials have had with the UK government in relation to the Vagrancy Act 1824.
answer_text: I can confirm that work on my review of the Vagrancy Act 1824 and the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847 has included liaison between my officials and their counterparts in England and Wales. We are continuing to monitor developments in that jurisdiction and elsewhere, though it is important that any legislative proposals developed are appropriate for the Northern Ireland context. My intention is to repeal the relevant provisions in those Acts in the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the law that requires owners of greyhounds to keep a muzzle on them at all times when outdoors.
answer_text: The Control of Greyhounds etc. Act (Northern Ireland) 1950 requires greyhounds to be kept under control and muzzled when in a public place.  I am considering if this legislation is still fit for purpose, in the context of the need for wider reform of animal welfare laws in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what revenue raising measures are contained in the interim Fiscal Framework.
answer_text: The agreement of the Interim Fiscal Framework marks significant progress on the current position within the financial package that accompanied the restoration of the Executive. The Interim Fiscal Framework, which includes the development and implementation of a Sustainability Plan, is the first step toward a Fiscal Framework, which will set out how the Executive will seek to achieve financial sustainability in the longer term.The financial package included a condition that, to write off the £559 million overspend accrued when the Executive was down, the Executive would raise £113 million in locally generated income; the Executive position remains that the overspend was accrued primarily due to the budget falling below need and therefore no conditions should apply to its write-off. I have been clear that as part of the final fiscal framework, the Executive will look at all options to deliver efficiencies, generate revenue, enhance borrowing powers and examine fiscal devolution, in order to put the Executive’s finances on a more stable footing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether funds from Financial Transactions Capital can be allocated to (i) the Housing Executive; and (ii) housing associations.
answer_text: The distinguishing feature of Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) is that the funds can only be deployed by the public sector as a loan to, or equity investment in, a private sector entity. The entity will then use the funding to invest in related infrastructure. The Housing Executive is classified under two different classifications: the Regional Housing Authority as a Non-Departmental Public Body, while the Landlord has been classified as a quasi-Public Corporation.  It is therefore unable to make use of FTC funds. Housing Associations are classified as private sector bodies, so FTC loans can be made to these bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work he has completed to reinstate the core funding scheme for LGBTQ+ charities, including Cara-Friend.
answer_text: The budget position for Health is extremely challenging.  We are currently facing an estimated funding gap of some £340m.  It is unlikely that we will be in a position to balance the budget without making some significant cuts to services and I will need to consider the position carefully prior to reaching any allocation decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the findings of the Infected Blood Inquiry that some government papers and medical files were destroyed, whether this includes documents relating to patients locally.
answer_text: My Department operates a Retention and Disposal Schedule (Good Management Good Records (GMGR)) developed in collaboration with HSC organisations. Copies are available in the NI Assembly Library and published on the departmental website at  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/gmgr-records-management. The Retention & Disposal schedule supports the Department and HSC organisations to comply with Data Protection legislation covering the retention of material when no longer required for the purpose for which it was collected. In line with that guidance and prior to August 2015, when the HSC suspended the destruction of records due to the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry, certain portions of medical and other records were destroyed as they were no longer required and not eligible for permanent preservation by PRONI.  This may have included portions of the medical records of patients who may have been infected with contaminated blood/blood products.  Evidence of record destruction, when identified via the subject access request process, was provided to the Inquiry.  In 2018, the then Permanent Secretary Richard Pengelly wrote to HSC Chief Executives to ask that all steps were taken to preserve records that may be relevant to the Inquiry and that fees for access to medical records were waived for witnesses. Officials carried out extensive searches to identify all potentially relevant records held in the Department. In December 2019, following visits to PRONI to review historic records, the Inquiry team conducted its own independent search of more recent departmental files held in the Department. In March 2020, the department provided funding for additional legal support to the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) to review large volumes of material held in PRONI, for consideration as evidence to the Inquiry.  The Department provided over 100 paper and 180 electronic records, as well as 15 written statements to the Inquiry. HOCS Jayne Brady, former Health Minister Robin Swann and Director of Population Health Liz Redmond provided oral evidence to the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the purpose of the Northern Ireland Appropriate Adult Scheme; (ii) the level of funding her Department has provided to the scheme in the last five years; and (iii) whether her Department is aware of any challenges in the effectiveness and delivery of the scheme.
answer_text: The statutory Codes of Practice, issued under the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) require an Appropriate Adult to be in attendance to provide safeguards to juveniles and vulnerable adults detained in police custody, to ensure they understand the process and their rights.  This should normally be a parent, relative, guardian, social worker or other suitable appropriate adult.The Northern Ireland Appropriate Adult Scheme (NIAAS) is a Department of Justice sponsored scheme.  It does not replace parent, relative or guardian responsibility, or the statutory responsibility for people within the care and control of one of the Health and Social Care Trusts.  Rather, the NIAAS provides the service of a trained and independent Appropriate Adult to support and advise a young person or vulnerable person to ensure they fully understand the detention process and their rights when in police custody, and particularly during police interviews, when a parent, relative, guardian, social worker or other suitable appropriate adult, cannot attend the police station to perform the role of an Appropriate Adult. The NIAAS became operational on 1 June 2009 and the service has been successfully delivered under contractual arrangements.The Department has provided £2,111,428 of funding to the scheme over the last five financial years (April 2019 to March 24).The Department holds regular governance meetings with the PSNI and the contractor to make sure that any challenges are addressed, ensuring the effective delivery of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department is carrying out to improve the provision of adult ADHD assessment and treatment services.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, currently there are no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision of services between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals having to wait longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability. Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of unspent Financial Transactions Capital that was returned to HM Treasury in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) can only be used to provide loans to or equity investment in the private sector.  The FTC budget is ring-fenced for this purpose and cannot be used for anything else.  In this regard, it is more challenging for departments to identify suitable projects that advance the Executive’s policy aspirations and fulfil the criteria of loan or equity investment.  The Executive is required to repay 80% of the FTC allocations received from the Treasury. The Department of Finance has agreed with Treasury that any FTC underspends can be used to offset the required repayment of 80% of FTC allocations. As part of the 2024-25 Budget, I encouraged all departments to submit bids for the use of FTC and have asked that departments continue to consider options for utilising this funding source. Financial Transactions Capital underspends, carryforward and amounts used to offset future repayments, in the last five years are as follows:-                £000s Financial YearDept Underspend (+) / Over spend (-)UnallocatedTotal UnderspendFTC RepaymentBudget Exchange Carry forwardUsed to Offset Future Loan Repayments2022-23-81219,867219,7866,500213,2862021-2217082,50882,67881,2461,4322020-2132155,30455,62529,22226,4032019-2020891,77791,98520,00071,9852018-191,007170,885171,892171,892*prior to 2021-22 FTC repayments were a cost to the Capital DEL rather than FTC* 2023-24 figures have not been provided as they will not be finalised until departments complete their annual accounts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount spent on green and environmentally friendly projects from the Northern Ireland Investment Fund in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Investment Fund has supported 14 projects between 2019 and May 2024. Of these, 13 schemes requiring assessment have achieved or are forecast to achieve an Energy Performance Certificate ‘A’ or ‘B’ rating, which means they are in the top 5.59% in terms of energy performance; 6 of those are EPC ‘A’ rated which are in the top 1.4% of energy performance.   The Fund has invested £50m supporting 3 energy efficient office development projects during this time, which are estimated to have resulted in an annual CO2 saving of 771 tonnes. There is a further energy efficient office scheme in construction which will add to this total.   The Fund has also invested £11.6m in a hotel scheme which is expected to be awarded the first ‘WELL Platinum’ status (the first hotel in Europe to achieve this), which is run by 100% renewable energy, partly powered by on-roof solar panels and heated with air-source heat pumps and sends zero waste to landfill. The Investment Fund remains open for applications and would welcome further interest for those seeking to take forward low carbon or other environmental projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 7375/22-27, what consideration she has given to Recommendations 1 and 2 of the Northern Ireland Policing Board’s report on Human Rights Review, Children and Young People: Strip Searching in Police Custody, published in June 2023.
answer_text: Recommendations one and two, from the Northern Ireland Policing Board’s report on Human Rights Review, Children and Young People: Strip Searching in Police Custody may require changes to the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) and the PACE Codes.  My officials are currently undertaking a wider review of PACE legislation, which sets the legislative framework for policing powers, and will consider the recommendations as part of the review.  They are working with the Home Office and the Scottish Police Authority in this regard.  They will also be engaging with the PSNI and the NIPB to take into account recommendations from the report.It is unlikely that any changes to primary legislation in relation to strip searching

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether Norbrook Laboratories avails of Industrial derating; and (ii) for her assessment of Industrial derating for multinational corporations
answer_text: Yes.Industrial derating is provided for manufacturing firms where premises are deemed to be used for an eligible industrial process.  Irrespective of whether it is a multinational corporation or an indigenous company, manufacturing as a sector contributes significantly to the local taxbase in terms of the rates liability they do pay and also creates skilled jobs with over 89,000 jobs in the sector contributing to the wider economy here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how NI Water plans to monitor all sewage discharges.
answer_text: The monitoring of sewage discharges from the public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water is taking forward a multi-year investment programme to install Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) across its wastewater network, to monitor and record the times when a spill is occurring from a storm overflow and also identify the duration of a spill. The first phase of this EDM deployment, as agreed with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, is focused on discharges to designated bathing and shellfish waters.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what public information work his Department is carrying out to publicise the symptoms of coeliac disease.
answer_text: There are no immediate plans to publicise the symptoms of coeliac disease.  Such awareness campaigns are usually led by the Public Health Agency.  Patients will, in the first instance, present at their GP practice with symptoms that their primary care professional will identify as potentially coeliac disease.Primary care pathways are in place that involve blood tests and referral to secondary care for outpatient consultation and endoscopic investigations for diagnosis if required.Primary and secondary care professionals across the HSC regularly engage with relevant charitable organisations and voluntary groups to raise awareness among patients and help them manage their condition. Ongoing GP training, ensures that Primary Care professionals are familiar with the symptoms and pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions his Department can take to stop sewage pollution in bathing waters and popular recreation areas by 2030.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates NI Water’s wastewater discharges through discharge consents issued under the Water Order (as amended) 1999.  As the regulator, my Department works with DfI and NI Water to prioritise bathing water sites for protection, with priority directed to works within 2km of bathing and shellfish waters. However, full protection is reliant on the adequate allocation of funds to upgrade sewerage infrastructure, and I alone cannot commit to stopping sewage pollution in bathing waters or recreational sites by 2030. This would require prioritisation of funding by the Executive.	My Department will continue to conduct targeted studies to identify the sources at failing sites and develop the appropriate measures for implementation to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of annual reviews that were carried out in the Belfast Health and Social Trust for patients diagnosed with coeliac disease, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust aims to offer all patients with Coeliac Disease an annual outpatient review, however, with the current waiting lists and limited resources this is not possible at present.   The Belfast HSC Trust is unable to provide the number of annual reviews carried out over the last 5 years for coeliac patients as the information is not held separately from specialty data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the feasibility study for the enhancement of rail services in the North West will be complete; and (ii) when the results of the feasibility study will be published.
answer_text: A feasibility study for the Belfast to Derry route enhancement programme, which is examining the introduction of a half-hourly service, is due to be submitted to my Department by the end of 2024. The outcomes of the feasibility study will be made available once the report has been considered and approved by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the Prevent duty; and (ii) whether she has any plans to introduce similar guidance.
answer_text: The Prevent Duty forms part of the UK Government’s counter-terrorism strategy, CONTEST and therefore is a reserved matter. As such, I have not made any formal assessment of the Prevent Duty. There are currently no plans to introduce similar guidance to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what powers she has to initiate a public inquiry.
answer_text: Section 1 of the Inquiries Act 2005 allows for a Northern Ireland Minister to cause an inquiry to be held where it appears to them that particular events have caused public concern. Section 30 of the Inquiries Act 2005 states the inquiry must not consider evidence or make recommendations about any matter falling within paragraph 17 of Schedule 2 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998 (excepted matters, national security, etc).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she is aware of any Israeli companies involved in the (i) Information Technology sector; (ii) Cyber Security sector; and (iii) Data Analytics sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am not aware of any Israeli companies involved in the Information Technology sector; Cyber Security sector; and/or Data Analytics sector in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the research commissioned by Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta into the current state of Irish medium school buildings.
answer_text: My Department has not yet received a final copy of the report on accommodation in the Irish-medium Education Sector, commissioned by the Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta.  I am therefore not in a position to comment at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what Irish language provision will be in the workforce plan.
answer_text: My officials are in the early stages of developing a sector-wide strategic workforce plan, working closely with the Education Authority and other relevant stakeholders. As one of the first steps, a survey of all schools in Northern Ireland (including Irish-medium schools) has recently been carried out, with the aim of gathering precise information on the challenges that schools are facing in recruiting and retaining teachers in specific subject areas. The survey results will be used to inform the approach to strategic workforce planning. As part of the development of a workforce planning strategy, consideration will be given to the needs of Irish-medium schools and Irish language provision within other schools, where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's progress in implementing a childcare strategy for families of school aged children who require wraparound and holiday childcare.
answer_text: My Department continues to progress the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy as a matter of priority. The affordability and accessibility of childcare for families, alongside other actions to support the early learning and childcare sector, are being considered.  I hope to bring proposals to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has carried out in relation to the financial relief offered to families who require wraparound childcare.
answer_text: My Department continues to progress the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy as a matter of priority. The affordability and accessibility of childcare for families, alongside other actions to support the early learning and childcare sector, are being considered.  I hope to bring proposals to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work he has undertaken to increase the payment for the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: Student social workers who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland can access a non-means tested Student Incentive Scheme of £4,000 for each year of their study. In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. Payments in relation to the Student Incentive Scheme are in addition to any student finance support which can be applied for separately.I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking professional training due to the cost-of-living crisis. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, the Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of employees in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that have had a cycle to work scheme application turned down in the last three years, broken down by reason for refusal.
answer_text: Belfast HSC Trust can only provide data from October 2023 to present in relation to the number of employees that have had a cycle to work (C2W) scheme application declined and the reasons for refusal.  A total of 44 staff applications have been declined. The main reasons include; duplicate application; not in Trust employment; outside HMRC applications; under national minimum wage threshold; application form not completed correctly; temporary contract not eligible and current C2W scheme had not expired.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how intermediate-rent homes will be allocated.
answer_text: Intermediate Rent homes will not be subject to an allocations process.  The Intermediate Rent policy sets out the application process alongside the eligibility criteria and is available at the following link:  https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/intermediate-rent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any land that has been identified for the building of intermediate-rent homes; and (i) the basis this land was identified.
answer_text: No land has been identified for the building of Intermediate Rent homes.Intermediate Rent homes may be delivered via a range of routes including acquisition, renovation, conversion and purpose-build.  Not all these routes require the sourcing of land.  Once funding is awarded, the Intermediate Rent Operator will be responsible for obtaining land (as required) and delivering homes to be operated as Intermediate for Rent properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of funding he will commit in Financial Transactions Capital loans for the establishment of intermediate-rent homes; and (ii) the interest that will be charged on such a loan.
answer_text: The competition to award Financial Transactions Capital funding to an Intermediate Rent Operator launched on 15th May 2024.  It would be inappropriate, during the competition, to commit to a specific sum of funding.  Interest will be charged on any resulting loan, as set out in the applicant guidance published on the Department’s website, available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/funding-deliver-northern-ireland-intermediate-rent-housing-scheme-application-guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how rents for intermediate-rent homes will be set.
answer_text: Intermediate rents will be set at a discount of at least 20% below local market rents.  The Intermediate Rent policy, published on the DfC website, sets out how this will work in practice.  My officials will work with the Intermediate Rent Operator, once funding has been awarded, to ensure they set rents in line with the policy, which is available at the following link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/intermediate-rent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the engagement he has had with housing rights organisations in relation to the development of intermediate-rent homes.
answer_text: My officials have undertaken comprehensive engagement and public consultation with a wide range of stakeholders in preparing the Intermediate Rent policy and the funding model.  Engagement sessions took place during the public consultation phase, and engagement has continued to take place via project specific forums and routine engagement with organisations including housing rights organisations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount their Department has spent on (i) social media; and (ii) sponsoring posts on social media platforms, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Executive Office undertook a number of public information campaigns over the last three years The amount spent by the Department by year, on social media was as follows:2021 – 2022: £83,831.57.2022 – 2023: £13,011.14.2023 – 2024: £4,203.93.No spend held.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the budget allocated for communications and marketing in their Department for the 2024-25 financial year, broken down by (i) internal spend; and (ii) external spend.
answer_text: We continue to develop the detailed budget plan for 2024-25, so allocations have not yet been made at this point.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of media interviews they have given since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: We have conducted 20 joint interviews or press conferences since our appointment on 3 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will bring forward regulations to establish a Just Transition Commission.
answer_text: A business case has been developed to assess the options and the associated costs of establishing the Commission and is in the final stage of receiving approvals. The content of the regulations has also been considered and it is envisaged that a draft set of regulations will be produced as part of a public consultation on the establishment of the Commission which I hope to launch in the summer of 2024. The outcome of this consultation and connected policy development work will inform my consideration of the final content of the regulations which are required to be laid before the Assembly for their debate and approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will lay a climate action plan before the Assembly by 6 June 2024, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The first Climate Action Plan is under development, and I am fully committed to delivering a Climate Action Plan that is transformational, evidence-based and informed by stakeholders.
