Significant progress has been made, however, identifying policies and proposals to meet the carbon budget has been extremely challenging, in the absence of an Executive and with a difficult budget position.In addition to meeting the carbon budget, the CAP must meet a wide range of other statutory requirements as set out in the Climate Change Act. Given the cross-cutting nature of the Climate Action Plan, the draft will need to be agreed by the Executive, prior to its publication for the required 16-week consultation. I am however keen to progress apace and whilst the timeframes involved and absence of government until February 2024 mean that it will not be possible to meet the dates for final publication as set out in the Climate Change Act I am determined that the draft will be published for consultation as soon as possible.Progressing obligations set out within the Climate Change Act remains a strong priority for my Department, working together with Executive colleagues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made any representations to the Minister for the Economy to remove the cap on those studying social work to help resolve the workforce pressures in the sector.
answer_text: The number of places commissioned for student social workers each year is determined by the funding available to the Department of Health.  In recent years, 260 social work training places have been commissioned at Queens University Belfast and Ulster University Magee. In the past four years my Department has worked with the Open University (OU) and other stakeholders to develop an approved OU Degree in Social Work in NI. The Department now commissions 25 additional social work training places via the OU.I am pleased to confirm that I have recently secured a commitment to commission a further 40 social work training places commencing 2024/5, which will, in time, help to alleviate some of the workforce pressures in the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any work has been carried out to consider reducing the cap on people studying social work to help resolve the workforce pressures.
answer_text: Due to the significant level of public funding provided to the universities, my Department must control spending to ensure it stays within its budget allocation. Therefore Queen’s University, Ulster University and the six further education colleges all receive an annual Maximum Student Number (MaSN) allocation from the Department.The MaSN is intended to control the two main costs associated with full-time undergraduate students: student support costs and teaching grant costs. The total number of student places is based on the budget allocation available. While my Department sets each institution’s overall MaSN allocation it does not set a cap on specific subjects or courses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education if he has any plans to increase resource and funding to Altram to assist the organisation to deliver more services and support to the Irish medium sector.
answer_text: My Department has funded Altram to develop and pilot an Irish-Medium Pre-school Immersion Education accredited course for Northern Ireland.  DE also funds them to provide the Irish-Medium Pre-school Support Service.The Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April.The budget outcome for Education falls far short of what the Education sector requires.  Given the size of the funding shortfall faced, I am working with my Department’s officials to determine the options available and what difficult decisions are required to offer the education sector the best chance to operate within budget.  Once complete, all education stakeholders will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations.  I am aware of the challenges which many parts of the early years and childcare sector are currently facing, and the need to stabilise core services in order to create a firm foundation on which expansion can be built.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of Altram’s Irish-Medium Early Years State of the Sector Report, published in 2017; and (ii) what plans his Department has to act upon it.
answer_text: Altram’s Irish-Medium Early Years State of the Sector report recommended the development of a Workforce Strategy for the sector, a review of the arrangements for supporting parents with their children’s language development and a costed and time-bound Workforce Development and Training Plan.My Department has funded Altram to develop and pilot an Irish-Medium Pre-school Immersion Education accredited course for Northern Ireland.  We also fund them to provide the Irish-Medium Pre-school Support Service. Through this, parents receive resources to use in the home environment to support their own Irish language competency and the Irish language competencies of their children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding available to support the expansion of early years programmes in Naíonraí to 0-3 in the Irish medium sector.
answer_text: My Department currently provides funding for the Pathway Fund which provides additional targeted support to children up to age four who are at risk of not reaching their full educational potential. The interim 2024-25 Pathway Fund budget is £3.789m and provisional awards have been allocated on this basis.  All 14 Irish Medium projects which applied and met eligibility requirements are in receipt of a provisional award in 2024-25, totalling £277,456. Interim award letters clarify that 2024-25 awards are subject to confirmation of DE funding.In relation to the budget outcome for Education for 2024-25, it falls far short of what the overall Education sector requires.  Given the size of the funding shortfall faced, I am working with my Department’s officials to determine the options available and what difficult decisions are required to offer the education sector the best chance to operate within budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of trout culled in the last two years; and the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: A cull of 28,250 fish was carried out between 6th and 10th November 2023.Zebra mussels, an invasive species, were detected at the fish farm in June 2023 and a suspension of stocking into the PAE was put in place on 19 July in order to comply with the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. This resulted in fish due to be stocked as part of the 2023 PAE stocking programme being retained on site at Movanagher. The cull was carried out for fish welfare reasons as overcrowding would have led to potential increased cannibalism and disease outbreaks for the remaining fish.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cases, on average, assigned to people employed in social work during their assessed year of employment in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Trust has informed the Department that they follow the NISCC Assessed Year in Practice (AYE) policy for the purposes of ensuring that social work graduates are safe to practise and are competent, responsible practitioners.Social workers in their AYE are allocated cases in line with their initial level of competence and confidence. As they progress through their AYE, their caseload increases reflecting their growing competence and confidence. They will have a comparable caseload to any post-AYE colleague in the team by the end of their AYE.Belfast Trust state that they would be unable to provide an average figure, indicating that social work cases vary significantly, and that numbers alone do not convey the diversity in complexity.  Other factors which determine the size of a newly qualified social worker’s case load will include the particular role within which they are employed, and the nature of the social work service provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that left social work before their assessed year of employment ended in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider a recruitment and retention payment for social workers.
answer_text: Under Agenda for Change a recruitment and retention payment (RRP) is an additional payment made where market pressures would otherwise prevent the employer from being able to recruit or retain staff in sufficient numbers at the normal salary. When considering an RRP, Health and Social Care Trusts can make an application to the Department for approval. In recent months Trusts made an application for an RRP for social workers in some Children’s Services teams.This application was returned for review over concerns in relation to its potential divisive and repercussive impact both within the social work profession and across other professions and services.The Department has not been asked to consider any further application from Trusts.I hope this is helpful in explaining the Department’s actions and decisions inrelation to a recruitment and retention payment for social workers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration that was given to employees' health and wellbeing before his Department's Permanent Secretary notified staff that they would have to increase the number of days working in the office.
answer_text: Staff wellbeing is a key strategic priority as set out in my Department’s People Strategy.  My officials have conducted an extensive review of our application of the NICS Hybrid Working Policy. Health and wellbeing featured strongly and consideration included 23 staff engagement events, 3 TUS engagements, surveys, desk research of international best practice, and inputs from my department’s workplace psychology team. The review was also informed by research that shows continuous remote working is detrimental to the wellbeing of some staff, and in particular new staff.Our commitment to hybrid working recognises the benefit this working model offers to our people and our business.  Phasing the implementation of the review recommendations is also a response to wellbeing preparedness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11145/22-27, (i) why his Department directed the Appeals Commission to hold a public inquiry to hear representations on the new abstraction and impoundment applications before the 28 days for receiving representations had expired; and (ii) to detail the rationale for his Department taking this approach.
answer_text: The Department remains of the view that it made a valid direction to the Commission on holding an inquiry on the new abstraction and impoundment licence applications and that there is no reason for the Commission not to accept this referral. The Department had made it clear to the public that any representations made on the previous applications will be made available to the Commission and do not need to be repeated. The Department, therefore, considers them as representations made under regulation 9 on the new applications. Any additional representations will, of course, also be shared with the Commission for consideration at the inquiry. The deadline for making such representations has been extended to 13 June 2024.In these circumstances, the Department sees no legal reason to await conclusion of the advertising period for receipt of additional representations before making its direction. This does not lead to any possible prejudice to other parties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of risk fish stocking brings in relation to the transmission of zebra mussel.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Zebra Mussel Management Strategy for Northern Ireland 2004-10 states that medium-risk vectors include fisheries and aquaculture activities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the reasons for stopping trout stock going to Movanagher Fish Farm.
answer_text: Following the identification of zebra mussels, an invasive species, at Movanagher fish farm in June 2023, the Department suspended the movement of live fish from Movanagher until appropriate biosecurity protocols can be put in place in order to comply with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered implementing a large-scale trial of non-prescribed zebra crossings.
answer_text: My Department uses its policy document RSPPG E005 Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/controlled-crossings-assessment-procedures-rsppg-e005 to assess the need for a controlled crossing such as a zebra crossing.  Guidance contained in Chapter 6 ‘Traffic Control’ of the Traffic Signs Manual https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/851465/dft-traffic-signs-manual-chapter-6.pdf helps inform the selection of the type of facility to be provided, and the relevant design standards from the Design Manual for Roads Bridges are used for more detailed road design considerations to ensure the facility can be installed and operate safely.My Department has no plans to consider implementing a trial of non-prescribed zebra crossings at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to his Department’s Chief Planner assessment that the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry could proceed after it was confirmed that the abstraction and impoundment elements could no longer form part of the inquiry.
answer_text: It is important to bear in mind that on 22 September 2021 the Department for Infrastructure, pursuant to its powers under s.26 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 caused a public local inquiry into three planning applications. The Department appointed the Commission, as an independent body to examine and to consider representations in respect of those applications. Other government bodies with related consents also caused public local inquiries under their own specific legislation and asked the Commission to hear these in conjunction with the planning applications. The decision to conjoin the various matters was one taken by the Commission.It is my department’s Chief Planner’s assessment that, notwithstanding the issues with the abstraction licenses, the public local inquiries for the planning applications could have proceeded as the initial PLI was caused by the Department prior to the other inquiry directions and was not dependent upon them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he or his officials have had with mental health organisations and representative groups in relation to erecting suicide prevention barriers around the Westlink bridges, Belfast.
answer_text: I updated the Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O'Neill, on the Westlink barriers work at our meeting in February this year. At that meeting I agreed to support the development of an All-Party Group on mental health in the Assembly and I have recently accepted an invitation to the next meeting of the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention. In addition, my officials have provided updates to meetings of the Protect Life 2 Strategy Steering Group and to the New Script for Mental Health, Participation and the Practice of Rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he or his officials have had with the Minister for Infrastructure or his officials in relation to erecting suicide prevention barriers at the Westlink.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact on children's health when they are present at (a) a stop and search of an adult; and (b) a home raid by the PSNI; and (ii) to detail the work his Department is doing to support these children.
answer_text: The impact on children’s health when they are present at either (a) a stop and search of an adult or (b) a home raid by the PSNI cannot be fully understood without those children being referred to appropriate services for an assessment of their individual needs, any trauma they may have experienced and the nature of support they require. Belfast HSC Trust does not have experience of children being referred to it for support after (a) a stop and search of an adult or (b) a home raid by the PSNI.However, such children can be referred for support by their General Practitioner or through the Trust’s Children’s Social Care Services Gateway Service to the appropriate health or social care service or community based service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered implementing a pilot project for an overdose prevention centre.
answer_text: My Department leads on the Northern Ireland Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. There are no proposals in the strategy on introducing such facilities at this time. Currently Overdose Prevention Centres are not permitted under the UK-wide Misuse of Drugs Act 1971. We will however continue to monitor evidence on the effectiveness of different harm reduction interventions, such as the forthcoming pilot overdose prevention facilities in Glasgow and Dublin, and will give due consideration to new interventions as appropriate within the wider legislative framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of under-18s, who were subject to a stop and search by the PSNI, that were (i) arrested; and (ii) charged in the last three years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons stopped and searched by police.  The recording of information in relation to persons stopped and searched is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many representations have been received to abstraction and impoundment applications (i) AIL-2024-0003; and (ii) AIL-2004-0004 made by Dalradian Gold at the time his Department directed the Water Appeals Commission to hold a public inquiry on 2 May 2024 for the purpose of considering those representations.
answer_text: Given the level of public interest in the proposals and the environmental sensitivity of the location, my officials directed the Appeals Commission, on 2 May 2024, to hold a public local inquiry to consider representations received in relation to the newly submitted Abstraction & Impoundment Licences.  One representation had been received at that time.  A total of 156 representations have been received in relation to the previous applications AIL 2020/0105 and AIL 2020/0106, which will also be referred to the PAC to be considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the proportion of under-18s, stopped under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 in the last three years, were found to have drugs in their possession; and (ii) ,on average, the (a) quantity; and (b) type of drugs found.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons stopped and searched by police, or in relation to the amount or quantity of drugs found as a result of such occurrences.  The recording of information in relation to persons stopped and searched is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the community background of under-18s who were stopped and searched by the PSNI in the last three years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons stopped and searched by police.  The recording of information in relation to persons stopped and searched is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) resources; and (ii) powers allocated to the dedicated youth team for homelessness prevention at the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has engaged with partners across the statutory and voluntary sectors to develop a Youth Homelessness Action Plan, recognising the collaboration that is required from organisations such as the Housing Executive and Health and Social Care Trusts in particular, alongside a wide range of organisations in the statutory and voluntary sectors. The Action Plan commits to the introduction of dedicated Housing Advisors and Team Leaders in all Areas to be established to take a lead, in relation to young people presenting as homeless in their area, extending service provision specific to young people currently available as part of the Belfast Housing Solutions TeamThe Youth Homelessness Action Plan aims to provide a coordinated response to ensure the Housing Executive addresses the challenges posed by youth homelessness with a focus on prevention, support and accommodation options.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had with his counterparts in the United Kingdom in relation to (i) ensuring there is regulation in place for the private security sector in Northern Ireland; (ii) the creation of an approved-contractors list for the private security sector in Northern Ireland; and (iii) ensuring no one working for firms in the private security sector in Northern Ireland are being paid below the minimum wage.
answer_text: Regulation of the security industry is carried out by the Security Industry Authority, which is a body sponsored by the Home Office.  National Minimum Wage compliance is regulated by HMRC.  There have been no Ministerial discussions in relation to these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) who sits on the Regional Gender Identity Panel as part of the Gender Identity Service – Pathway Review Group; and (ii) how often the panel meets.
answer_text: The Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group was established in September 2019. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sits under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the panel.  The Gender Review Group includes clinicians from the Regional Gender Identity Services in Belfast Trust, officials in my Department’s relevant policy branch and Strategic Planning and Performance Group, a representative from the Patient and Client Council, a representative from the Royal College of GP’s and service users.The Gender Identity Liaison Panel includes my officials as referenced above, a representative from the Patient and Client Council, representatives from a range of Community and Voluntary organisations representing Transgender people and service users or their advocates.Both groups meet quarterly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department submitted any evidence or research as part of the Cass Review.
answer_text: There was no specific expectation that my Department should submit evidence as the Cass Review was not a formal consultation and my Department did not submit any evidence or research as part of the  Review.My Department has noted the publication of the Cass Review, which was commissioned by NHS England. It includes 32 recommendations for England, encompassing a wide range of subject areas including medical interventions, requirements for further research and data, and safeguarding measures.My Department will carefully consider the Cass Review recommendations and any implications for gender identity services provided in Northern Ireland.  My officials will meet with clinicians and stakeholders in the coming weeks to consider the Review recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a record is kept by (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts of trans people who cannot get bloods taken by their GP.
answer_text: (i) My Department does not keep such records.(ii)In relation to whether a record is kept by Health and Social Care Trusts of trans people who cannot get bloods taken by their GP, as this information is not required by my Department – there may be variance across GP surgeries as to whether this information is recorded. If such information is recorded it would be on individual patient files rather than a central record.   The information you have requested is not readily available and could only be compiled at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being carried out to ban conversion therapy.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) lead the work on conversion therapy, as part of their broader sexual orientation strategy. Progress to ban conversion therapy will be a decision for the Minister for Communities.My officials continue to engage with DfC colleagues and will provide support, as required, to ensure health-related matters are considered as part of any policy progression in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department is carrying out to assist trans and non-binary people get access to GP services.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for all patients across Northern Ireland.GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of their individual patients.  In response to increasing patient demand many GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. With the level of pressure on GP services continuing to rise, this approach helps GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of patients who need their services.   My Department is committed to ensuring that patients will be able to continue to access high quality, sustainable GP services now and in the future and is taking forward a wide range of work with stakeholders across the system to help achieve that.  This includes focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  A GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, has also been established to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for both patients and practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of employees in the Northern Ireland Civil Service that stayed in post for less than one week, including agency workers, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NI Civil Service employees and recruitment agency workers that stayed in post for less than one week, in each of the last 5 financial years (headcount) is provided below.YearNumber of staffNICSRecruitment agency workers2019-20200482020-2021342021-20224272022-20237472023-2024245Source:NICS figures taken from HR Connect and Compass systemsRecruitment Agency Worker figures from the NICSHR agency worker tracker as at 26 April 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work being undertaken by (i) his Department; and (ii) Translink to assist low paid workers make use of public transport.
answer_text: I am currently considering the outcomes of the public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme against the budget allocated to the Department, alongside other priorities and pressures across the Department.  I hope to publish my decision in due course.Discounted Fares and Products Translink offer a range of discounted products to assist passengers to find the best value for their journey and save money compared with paying with cash.  This includes 50% off bus and rail travel in Northern Ireland and up to 50% off Enterprise for 16–23-year-olds when they use a yLink card.  The card can be applied for free of charge.  For Metro / Glider customers, a Smartlink Multi-Journey card or Travelcard are available which offer discounts of up to 25% on standard cash fares.  Customers can also avail of mLink and DayLink to receive discount on day travel. For Ulsterbus customers, a Smartlink Multi-Journey card or Smartlink Town Service Travelcard is available which offers discounts of up to 25% on standard cash fares.  Customers can also avail of 25% off day return travel after 9:30am.For rail customers, the iLink travelcard offers day, week, or month travel across 5 different zones in Northern Ireland.  Customers can also avail of 25% off day return travel after 9:30am. Translink also offer promotional fares at weekends and for families, such as the Translink Family and Friends ticket which offers unlimited travel on all Translink services for up to 2 adults and 4 children.  Jobseekers and Warrants (via Collect Tickets) Translink offer reduced bus and rail travel for the unemployed and who are currently claiming Jobseekers allowance.  The permit holder is entitled to half-fare on standard adult day return tickets on NIR services after 9:30am Monday to Friday.  For Metro and Ulsterbus, the permit holder is entitled to half-fare on standard adult single tickets after 9:30am Monday to Friday.  Translink also offer an arrangement to trusted business organisations whereby travel is permitted on Translink bus and rail services by the issue of an electronic warrant which can be redeemed by clients and customers of warrant issuing businesses for a paper travel ticket.  This can only be redeemed via a Ticket Vending Machine.  The Collect Tickets service involves authorised users in trusted business organisations selecting a suitable ticket product via the Collect Tickets website.  Organisations are billed on the basis of actual redemption of vouchers that occur.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work being undertaken to expand late night bus and rail services.
answer_text: The scheduling and timetabling of train services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered putting additional public transport services in place to coincide with late night sittings in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The scheduling and timetabling of train services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what mechanisms are in place to support retail and hospitality workers who have to deal with individuals with addiction issues.
answer_text: I appreciate the challenge that retail and hospitality workers can face when dealing with individuals with addiction issues.  Enforcement and guidance on health and safety issues within retail and hospitality workplaces is a responsibility of environmental health officers in local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount that the Housing Executive paid to (i) private contractors; and (ii) sub-contractors in each of the last three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) Please see the table below for the total paid to private contractors by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in the last three financial years for Stock Investment and Maintenance purposes.Total Paid to Contractors by NIHE2021/222022/232023/24£k’s£k’s£k’s171,412191,153175,824*Please note 2023/24 figures have not yet been finalised and are subject to year-end audit. (ii) The response to this is nil. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive only makes payments to main contractors, who in turn are required under the Fair Payment Charter to pay their respective sub-contractors / supply chain.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount that the Housing Executive holds in (i) reserves; and (ii) other liquid assets.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive Landlord Services division held circa £286m in its rental income reserve as at 31/3/2024 (pre completion of audit).	In relation to Regional Services, grant in aid is drawn-down at the start of the month to cover that month’s outgoings, with only a significantly low level of operational reserves held to cope with any unexpected payments that may fall due over and above the estimated cashflow requirements for that month.”	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 10461/22-27, whether he will be advising the Water Appeals Commission that the conjoined inquiry is unable to proceed until the matter of the water abstraction and impoundment applications is resolved.
answer_text: Dalradian Gold Ltd submitted 2 abstraction and impoundment applications on the 24th April 2024. I have agreed that my Department directs the Appeals Commission to hold a public local inquiry to consider representation in respect of these applications. My Department has now directed the Appeals Commission accordingly and has advised that the April 2024 applications should be considered in the conjoined public local inquiry. However, the decision regarding conjoining these applications is entirely for the Appeals Commission to determine.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he intends to take to alleviate pressures on social workers, following reports of issues recruiting and retaining staff.
answer_text: My Department has worked collaboratively with HSC Trusts, Universities and Colleges, trade union representatives and other key stakeholders to try to alleviate some of the pressures brought about as a result of recruitment and retention issues in social work teams. A Social Work Workforce Review was undertaken in 2021 and its findings were published in 2022. The Department then established the Social Work Workforce Implementation Board, involving senior leaders and a range of workforce representatives, to plan and deliver real improvements in the short, medium and longer term. Some progress has been made in alleviating pressures and improving stability in the social work workforce, including:in December 2022, a decision was taken to end the use of agency social workers in all HSC services by June 2023.the Trusts recruited around 160 agency social workers into vacant HSC posts between January and June 2023 and all use of agency social workers in HSC has now ceased. the Department created a faster, more efficient route into Trust employment for newly qualified social workers, which resulted in almost 200 vacancies being filled between June and September 2023. It is intended to repeat this exercise in the summer of 2024.the Department has also increased the number of training places for student social workers by commissioning 25 additional places with the Open University NI. The Department continues to make the case for an increase in training places for student social workers, which is entirely dependent upon the availability of additional, recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Housing Executive employees on a temporary contract; and (i) any work being carried out to end this practice.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The majority of NIHE’s temporary arrangements are provided through recruitment agencies and 561 agency workers are currently engaged across the Housing Executive. NIHE’s aim is to reduce the reliance on agency worker arrangements with a target to ensure the percentage of agency workers is 10% or below. NIHE have been able to reduce the percentage of agency workers from 17.7% in March 2022 down to 15.4% by March 2024.NIHE aim to further reduce the number of temporary arrangements across the business, and this is one of the areas of focus in its workforce plan. This is reviewed twice a year and sets out planned resourcing activity both annually and over a 5-year period.  NIHE actively seek to fill vacancies on a permanent basis where possible and during 23/24 conducted 256 recruitment exercises, receiving 4205 applications and resulting in 250 appointments to date.  Over the last 3 financial years NIHE have appointed 726 employees, hence demonstrating its commitment to job creation and filling posts. This year NIHE plan to continue to reduce the number of agency worker arrangements in administration posts across Housing and will continue to fill posts in the Direct Labour Organisation on a permanent basis as NIHE progress with structural reviews under its Evolve programme.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the plans he has to progress an education workforce planning strategy for schools and early years education providers.
answer_text: My Department recognises the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic workforce planning and is working closely with the Education Authority to address this need. A survey for all schools in Northern Ireland is being planned, with the aim of gathering precise information on the challenges that schools are facing in recruiting and retaining school-based staff including teachers in specific subject areas.As part of the workforce planning strategy, consideration will be given to possible strategies to address specific school staff shortages including, for example, teachers for STEM subjects. This will include consideration of alternative employment models such as shared posts across schools and the possibility of carefully managed bursary schemes for Initial Teacher Education programmes to encourage more individuals to pursue teaching careers in identified subject areas including STEM. However, such schemes would have to be tightly targeted and would remain subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the cause of the raw sewage spill and ongoing water issues on Amcomri Street, Belfast; and (ii) what maintenance works his Department is taking to repair damage to the water infrastructure in the area.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what guarantees he can provide to ensure a funding increase for SureStart programmes without increasing their workload.
answer_text: During my recent engagement with Sure Start managers they stressed the detrimental impact that a static budget would have on existing Sure Start services.  I intend to prioritise the stabilisation of current Sure Start provision within the Early Learning and Childcare strategy. I will be looking to Executive colleagues to release the funding required to deliver this.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how much the Good Shepherd Sisters have contributed financially to the redress scheme for historical institutional abuse.
answer_text: At this stage, it is not appropriate to disclose the amount that we have received from the Good Shepherd Sisters.  The ongoing negotiations are sensitive, and at different stages with each institution.  It is important that we give space to allow the negotiations to progress in a constructive manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department last carried out a traffic management inspection on Stockman’s Avenue, Belfast.
answer_text: Officials have confirmed they are not aware of any traffic management related issues within Stockman’s Avenue and there are no records of a traffic management inspection having been carried out on Stockman’s Avenue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children living in West Belfast who are (i) registered as having speech and language issues; and (ii) receiving support assistance for speech and language.
answer_text: The information is not held centrally in my department and has therefore been requested from the Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust. Belfast HSC Trust advised that they do not hold a register of i) children identified as having Speech and Language Therapy issues, however in West Belfast as at 30 April 2024 there were 204 children on the waiting list for Speech and Language Therapy, and ii) 709 children on Speech and Language Therapy caseloads.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work being carried out by the General Register Office to make registration more inclusive of trans and non-binary self-identification.
answer_text: The General Register Office (GRO) is responsible for the re-registration of births here for those in receipt of a gender recognition (GR) certificate from the UK Gender Recognition Panel. Once a GR certificate is received in GRO, officials re-register the birth to record the recognised gender and new name (if required). GRO provides information to interested members of the public on how to apply for the certificate and how the re-registration process operates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work being carried out to provide neighbourhood renewal funding on longer-term basis.
answer_text: I recognise the important role the voluntary and community sector, including groups funded through Neighbourhood Renewal programme play in delivering critical services, support and advice to local communities and I am committed to continuing to support the sector.The current review of the People and Place Strategy will consider how organisations working with those living in the most disadvantaged communities can be better supported to deliver improved outcomes. My officials also continue to support the work of the Joint Forum to develop a set of funding principles aimed at improving the funding relationship across the Voluntary and Community Sector.  In the immediate term whilst the Executive has now agreed a 2024/25 budget, I am working with the senior officials to consider the budget position and the decisions that will be required to live within our 2024/25 allocation. In advance of the Executive’s agreement on the Budget allocations, my Department afforded Neighbourhood Renewal organisations with 3 months interim funding encompassing 1 April to 30 June 2024. Funding awards for the remainder of 2024/25 will be determined following consideration of my Department’s budget position and the decisions that will be necessary to remain within our 2024/25 allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 9974/22-27, to detail the number of complaints that were received.
answer_text: There were 6,367 applications for the DfC DWP AO Competition (IRC298527).There were 13 complaints made to HRConnect about the DfC DWP AO competition citing technology-related issues. Any candidate who wishes to make a complaint about any aspect of their application is encouraged to do so via the published complaint procedure which can be found on the NICS Recruitment website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 9974/22-27, whether all candidates were given access to the complaint’s procedure.
answer_text: The NICS recruitment complaints procedure is published on the website which is accessible by all candidates. The Candidate Information Booklet for this competition (IRC298527) provided guidance to assist candidates throughout their application process including contact details for HRConnect should a candidate have a query relating to this competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of complaints his Department received about people receiving treatment in private hospitals and clinics through the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive in the last three years.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (ROIRS) closed to new applications on 21 September 2022 and 68 applications were received after this date.   Three complaints were received by my Department and these related to the application process or level of reimbursement provided rather than the treatment received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has received any complaints about the HR Connect online application for new jobs in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has received no complaints regarding candidates ability to submit an online application for new jobs in the last 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what payments are in place to compensate healthcare workers who have been attacked or threatened at their place of work.
answer_text: HSC staff who are injured or become ill due to their employment may be entitled to financial assistance if their pay is reduced as a result of their health problems. Injury Allowance is a payment made by HSC employers to eligible staff that tops up sick pay, or earnings when on a phased return to work, to 85 per cent of pay.  Staff may also choose to lodge personal injury claims through a formal legal route.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of the regional rate that is spent on water and sewerage provision.
answer_text: The income received from the regional rate is not hypothecated to fund any particular service or services, but rather it contributes to the overall budget envelope available to be allocated by the Executive, along with funding received from the Block Grant, and other sources. It is from this overall funding envelope that the Executive decides on allocations for specific services, including water and sewerage services, when setting its Budgets. Non Ringfenced Resource DEL(£m)Capital DEL(£m)Total all Departments15,418.52,245.5Water and Sewerage service 178.8335.4Percentage1.16%14.94%The proportion of the Executive’s Non Ringfenced Resource DEL and Capital DEL which were allocated to funding water and sewerage services in the 2023-24 Expenditure Plans as set out in the tables published by my Department, following my WMS on 15 February 2024 are as follows:Details of the 2023-24 Departmental Outcome by Spending Area can be found on my Department’s website at 2023-24 in-year monitoring of public expenditure | Department of Finance (finance-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many applications in relation to the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive his Department received after the scheme closed.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (ROIRS) closed to new applications on 21 September 2022 and 68 applications were received after this date.   Three complaints were received by my Department and these related to the application process or level of reimbursement provided rather than the treatment received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what discussions her Department has had about funding for education staff as a result of the pay and grading review.
answer_text: I am committed to working collectively with the Education Minister to find a resolution for education support workers.I have engaged with the Education Minister regarding the funding to implement the outcome of the pay and grading review of non-teaching support staff, both in correspondence, and when we met to discuss the Executive’s Budget for 2024-25.Recognising the importance of these workers, some of whom are amongst the lowest paid in the sector and doing vital work to support our children and young people, the Executive agreed to seek approval to reprofile some of the funding provided in the financial package for this purpose, and I will be raising this with the Treasury as a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average number, per constituency, of large families on the housing waiting list.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me of the following:“The Housing Executive is unable to provide the average number of large families on the housing waiting list. The total numbers of large family households for each parliamentary constituency at December 2023 are as follows: Dec 23Large Family HouseholdsParl ConstApplicantsApplicants in Housing StressAllocationsBelfast East1239620Belfast East/South2221<10Belfast North29425445Belfast South21818610Belfast South/Strangford1210<10Belfast West32428428East Antrim1258512East Londonderry19012126Fermanagh South Tyrone19511519Foyle26321938Lagan Valley12610010Mid Ulster1228413Newry & Armagh22517026North Antrim17412123North Down1229120South Antrim13410019South Down19315918Strangford14411121Upper Bann27117231West Tyrone13910317Grand Total34162602398”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of homes for large families have been built in West Belfast in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The large family classification is defined as 1 or 2 persons aged 16 or over, and 3 or more children aged 0-15, or 3 or more persons 16 or over and 2 or more children aged 0-15. The table below details the social housing units completed in West Belfast that could accommodate larger families.Year3 Bed4 BedTotal2019/20130132020/21434472021/228082022/23404442023/24591675Total16324187”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any review his Department plans to carry out of the policy to home large families.
answer_text: Where the Housing Executive is satisfied that an applicant is statutorily homeless, it has a duty to secure that accommodation becomes available for their occupation. Given the current constraints in social housing supply across Northern Ireland, it is not always immediately possible to secure accommodation appropriate to the size of larger families. I am, however, content that the Housing Executive has clearly established processes to deal with these situations when they arise and, at this time, a change to policy is not necessitated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of large families on the housing waiting list in West Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that there were “324 large family households on the housing waiting list in West Belfast parliamentary constituency as at December 2023.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of families currently without a permanent home due to the lack of properties big enough to house them.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Allocations are made on the basis of need to the highest pointed relevant applicant on the waiting list. Applicants are allowed to select multiple areas of choice of rehousing, each of which will have an individual stock profile, and factors other than points and bedroom size (e.g. ground floor layout) are taken into consideration when matching properties to applicants. It is therefore not practicable to identify where the absence or shortfall in properties of a certain size is the sole factor preventing allocation. The Housing Executive is, therefore, only able to provide details of family households on the waiting list.The figures for family households on the waiting list as at December 2023 are: ApplicantsHousing Stress Applicants (30 points or more)Full Duty ApplicantsAllocationsFamily households14,96611,50410,1901,897”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the extent to which his Department currently tests waterbodies for levels of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances and forever chemicals; and (ii) the actions his Department has taken, and is taking, to ensure such harmful substances are not reaching water supplies and the food chain.
answer_text: The only legislative driver currently in place for the monitoring of PFAS substances in inland surface waters is the EU Water Framework Directive (WFD), Annex 10 of which lists PFOS (Perfluorooctane sulfonate) as a Priority Hazardous Substance. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency is currently developing a method of analysis for PFOS therefore no data has been collected to date for PFOS.In addition to WFD, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), an Executive Agency of DAERA, regulates a number of activities under environmental legislation aimed at protecting the wider environment and have enforcement powers to take action where necessary. This includes the regulation of Discharges under the Water Order 1999 through the issue of Consents to Discharge to the water environment. Consents include conditions which prevent the discharge of any pollutant which would cause harm to the aquatic environment. A risk-based approach to compliance assessment is implemented where an activity is found to be presenting a risk of pollution/ polluting and the Department’s enforcement policy is applied to protect the aquatic environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether levels of per- and polyflouroalkyl substances and forever chemicals are (i) being tested for; and (ii) present in the water quality sampling being undertaken by his Department on the River Faughan, in the vicinity of the Mobuoy illegal landfill site.
answer_text: A comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme is in place for the Mobuoy Site which includes testing for per- and polyflouroalkyl (PFAS) substances, sometimes referred to as ‘forever chemicals’.PFAS sampling for the Mobuoy site includes testing of the river Faughan upstream and downstream of the site; and sampling of the groundwater underlying the waste site. There have been no detections of PFAS substances to date from samples taken from the river Faughan upstream or downstream of the site. PFAS substances have been detected within the groundwaters underlying the Mobuoy site as would be expected from a site containing landfilled material due to the widespread use of PFASs in everyday products. Under current Northern Ireland and UK legislation there are no environmental quality standards set for PFAS substances; with the exception of PFOS (Perfluorooctane sulfonic acid). There has been no detection of PFOS to date within the site groundwaters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that families are represented in policy issues and decision-making when developing a childcare strategy.
answer_text: Since becoming the Education Minister, I have met with parents, providers and other key stakeholders to hear firsthand the issues to be addressed by an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy. In addition, my officials have carried out significant stakeholder engagement, including commissioned research with almost 5,000 parents and 880 providers to help inform the work to date.  The views of parents and families will continue to be sought and fed into the decision-making process as the work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will meet with Ionad na Fuiseoige to see how the organisation provides Irish language services in a holistic way.
answer_text: I am happy to meet with Ionad na Fuiseoige to see how the organisation provides Irish language services.  I would encourage representatives from the organisation to contact my office with a view to arranging a visit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to developing a green corridor as part of developing cycling infrastructure from Grosvenor Road to Falls Road.
answer_text: My Department aims to deliver a cycling network that is as inclusive as possible so that the maximum number of people feel confident to use that network safely, freely, and comfortably. The Department for Communities is currently developing a public realm scheme on Grosvenor Road which will connect the new Belfast Grand Central Station to the Royal Victoria Hospital and Falls Road. Officials are working together to explore any opportunities for collaboration to deliver improved public realm, pedestrian, and cycling infrastructure as part of this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work has taken place to determine the scale of climate anxiety and other mental health issues connected to the climate breakdown.
answer_text: No data is collected by my Department on climate anxiety and climate-related mental health issues in Northern Ireland.  While it is important to identify the causes of mental ill health, the key focus has been on the delivery of the Mental Health Strategy which aims to support and promote good mental health across the region and to provide the right help and support, at the right time, to those who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a timeline for the prioritisation and implementation of a childcare strategy.
answer_text: My department continues to progress the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy as a matter of priority. The scale of what I can do in 2024/25 has largely been determined by the budget allocated by the Executive.  The £25m which has been earmarked for an ELC Strategy is 50% of what I had bid for, and I am recalibrating the options accordingly.  The timeline for implementing a full Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will require Executive decisions on a number of policy issues and a commitment to allocate significant additional budget for any expanded provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 7917/22-27, (i) when the member of the public raised the concern about the Aggregates Levy Credit Scheme; and (ii) to detail a timeframe for the completion of his Department’s preliminary investigation.
answer_text: The member of the public raised the concern about the Aggregates Levy Credit Scheme in September 2022.  The preliminary investigation is now complete.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will met with the campaign group Melted Parents NI to ensure that the families affected by the childcare crisis are represented and their concerns are considered in the childcare strategy.
answer_text: I met with representatives from the campaign group Melted Parents NI on 18 April, as part of a wider engagement with parents to hear first-hand the varied issues and priorities that parents have in relation to childcare. My Assembly Private Secretary recently met with the group and my officials have also offered to meet with them to talk through their concerns in more detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to legislate for energy price controls.
answer_text: Price controls are in place for monopoly network companies and incumbent electricity and natural gas suppliers in the north of Ireland.  These price controls are the responsibility of the Utility Regulator for Northern Ireland.  My Department and the Consumer Council NI engage with the Utility Regulator during this process to ensure a fair outcome for consumers.  Competition between all electricity and natural gas suppliers, including the incumbent suppliers, then works to provide consumers with value and choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the increase in fees for childcare, for his assessment of how 22.5 hours pre-school provision meets the needs of young families.
answer_text: The Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) is a developmental programme and delivered free at the point of delivery to every target aged child whose parents want it.  There is a strong evidence base for extending provision to 22.5 hours from a child development perspective.  As with time spent in school, time spent by children in funded pre-school education has the potential to contribute to parents’ childcare requirements.  It is one of a number of measures I hope to bring forward to support young families, subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) consultants; and (ii) managers in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that have access to free parking.
answer_text: The Departments policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.1,118 car parking spaces in the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust (BHSCT) are free of charge and available to all staff, patients and visitors, on a first come first served basis. The Trust had no control over who accesses these spaces.As demand for staff car parking spaces is greater than available capacity, the BHSCT are implementing an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The assessment criteria takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.Staff are currently charged for their car parking permits, however as a fair recognition of the hard work and dedication of HSC staff, staff parking permits will become free of charge from 12 May 2024.  Currently the trust only holds data on permit holders on the Belfast City Hospital site.SiteTotal Number of PermitsConsultantsManagersBelfast City Hospital3610259189The ‘managers’ figure above reflects staff who have the term ‘manager’ in their job title, however many managers do not have the term ‘manager’ in their job title, and it therefore may not be an accurate reflection of the total number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the letter from the Water Appeals Commission to his Department dated 18 April 2024 in relation to the public inquiry into gold mining in the Sperrins, (i) whether the Commission was provided with the correct information by his officials at the pre-inquiry meeting; and (ii) to confirm that his Department has the necessary legal authority to call a public inquiry in relation to the water abstraction and impoundment license applications.
answer_text: My officials responded to the WAC letter on 24 April 2024. The letter confirms that in oral questions, from the Commissioner, at the pre-hearing inquiry in March, DAERA officials gave an incorrect answer based on their honest understanding of relevant internal procedures and their honest recollections of the correspondence from some time ago. There was no attempt to mislead the inquiry and a sincere apology has been issued.The letter further confirms that the Applications had been deemed to be refused prior to the direction to the WAC and that there was therefore no jurisdiction to direct the WAC to consider representations in respect of these two Applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) her assessment on the prevalence of land-hoarding; and (ii) any action she is considering to address the issue.
answer_text: As part of my Departments work in developing a draft housing supply strategy, the issue of land banking in relation to housing supply, was included as part of the call for evidence consultation process. The outcome of this call for evidence is summarised on Page 56 of the link below. https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/communities/dfc-housing-supply-strategy_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what percentage of imprisonments were sentenced for summary offences in the last five years; and (ii) for her assessment of whether these imprisonments contribute to the increased pressure on the prison service.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide reliable data for percentage of imprisonments sentenced for summary offences.Information on the number of cases that resulted in a custodial sentence and where the main offence at conviction was a summary offence has been provided below.The most recent five year period for which information is available is 2018 – 2022. Convictions at courts resulting in imprisonment where the main offence was a summary offence, 2018 – 2022:YearConvictions2018554 2019564 2020433 2021547 2022616 Note:Figures relate to initial disposal at court.  Appeals are not included.Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, NCA, Airport Constabulary, Harbour Police or PBNI.Figures relate to cases where the main disposal imposed in a case was a custodial one and where the main offence at disposal was a summary offence.The majority of custodial sentences in the magistrates’ courts are for 12 months or less.  This cohort of prisoners contributes to the overall prison population.  Short sentences allow only a very limited opportunity to engage with individuals to address their offending behaviour whilst in custody.NAOMI LONG MLA

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration has been given to implementing a land value tax.
answer_text: It is essential that the Executive has the necessary fiscal levers at its disposal to deliver on our priorities locally.In the first instance, I will consider the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission, which carried out a comprehensive review of the case for increasing our fiscal powers. Whilst the Fiscal Commission did not consider a land value tax specifically, the Commission did recommend a way forward on the creation of new taxes such as this. Once I have considered the Commission’s recommendations in full, it is my intention to bring proposals to the Executive on the powers we might seek.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has plans to remove and replace anti-homeless architecture.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any instances of anti-homeless architecture on Departmental land or property.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many appeals to the Victims' Payment Scheme have been outstanding for over one year.
answer_text: As of 22 April 2024, 7,498 applications have been made to the Scheme with almost 1,300 determinations made by the Victims’ Payments Board.This includes 166 determinations which have been appealed, none of which are outstanding for over one year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many applications have been (i) rejected; and (ii) awarded in relation to the Victims' Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of 22 April 2024, 7,498 applications have been made to the Scheme with almost 1,300 determinations made by the Victims’ Payments Board.Of these, 830 have been recommended for an award and 453 have been determined as ineligible for an award.  To date, more than £39.6 million has been made in payments to applicants.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many Victims' Payment Scheme applications are outstanding as a result of (i) information not being provided by or engaged with the PSNI; and (ii) evidence not being provided from counselling services due to (a) GDPR issues; and (b) data being destroyed.
answer_text: We are unable to provide a response as the Victims’ Payments Board do not collate data in this manner. Engagement with these external stakeholders remains frequent and ongoing.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many appeals are outstanding in the Victims' Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of 22 April 2024, 7,498 applications have been made to the Scheme with almost 1,300 determinations made by the Victims’ Payments Board. This includes 166 determinations which have been appealed, 76 of which remain outstanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to raise tuition fees.
answer_text: I have no plans to raise tuition fees.Our university undergraduates already make a contribution towards the cost of their university education and I believe that it would not be appropriate to add to the financial pressures they are already facing as a result of cost of living increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in order to help address the GP shortage, whether he has considered amending university admissions criteria to study a medicine degree.
answer_text: Admissions criteria for the medical degree programmes at QUB and UU are set by the universities.I do, however, recognise the importance of medical school curriculums in shaping the future career choices of students. Accordingly, my officials have been working closely with Queens University Belfast to support the re-orientation of their medical programme so that students spend 25% of their clinical placements in high quality training environments in General Practice.  Similarly, we are supporting the very significant emphasis on placements in GP settings under Ulster University’s medical curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale in relation to the decision not to intervene in the expansion of the Cloghan Point oil terminal.
answer_text: Mid and East Antrim Borough Council notified my Department, on 08 September 2023, of its resolution to approve planning application LA02/2018/1145/F (Cloghan Point Oil Terminal).  After careful consideration of the notification from MEABC, as well as all third party ‘call in’ requests, I decided on 26 March 2024 not to ‘call in’ the application to my Department for determination. I concluded that the council had assessed the application in a reasonable and robust manner, and that the application does not raise issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level and the circumstances of this case are not exceptional such as to warrant the use of the Department’s ‘call in’ powers under Section 29 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered the decriminalisation of assisted dying.
answer_text: Suicide or attempting suicide is not, of itself, a criminal offence in this jurisdiction. It is also legal for someone from Northern Ireland to travel elsewhere for the purposes of assisted dying where it is allowed in that jurisdiction. It is an offence, however, to encourage or assist the suicide or attempted suicide of another person. I consider any proposed change to end of life care, including to allow for assisted dying, to be primarily a matter for the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people have appeared in court for theft of basic essentials items in the last 10 years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence related to theft of essential items.  Theft of such items may be prosecuted under the more generic offence description of ‘theft’.    Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the circumstances under which an offence occurred, or in relation to the items stolen, so, from information held, the Department is not able to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the number of attacks having been carried out on healthcare staff in the last five years, what plans he has to protect hospital workers.
answer_text: On 12 December 2023 my Department launched a new Management of Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework. The purpose of this framework is to outline the HSC commitment in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace, and to ensure associated structures, policies and support are in place to enable staff to work safely. This regional framework must form the basis of any policies and processes developed locally by the various HSC bodies in relation to dealing with such incidents in a diverse range of working environments, including lone working. Each HSC employer will have differing arrangements in place, supported by appropriate risk assessments according to the setting.The regional framework is accessible on the Department’s website.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-afc-6-2023_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions his Department has had on increasing funding for Parent Rooms.
answer_text: The Parent Rooms received £107k of funding through the Mental Health Support Fund (MHSF). They also received an additional £7k in 2023, as part of uplifts for existing MHSF grantees. The MHSF was established using Covid-19 monies and was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. The monies allocated to the Mental Health Support Fund have now been exhausted. Unfortunately, due to the financial situation which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend the fund and it has ended, as planned. This has been communicated to the fund administrators, Community Foundation NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of counsellors required to deliver the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: There is no figure for the number of counsellors required to deliver the Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-31.  However, Action 32 of the Strategy committed to a comprehensive workforce review, considering existing workforce need and training, with the aim of developing of a new workforce which is fit for the future.  It was identified as a key enabling action, setting the foundation for the wider implementation of the MHS.The Mental Health Workforce Review Report, published in July 2023, sets out a number of recommendations to address workforce challenges across statutory mental health services, and includes a proposed future workforce profile, covering new and existing professions (including counsellors), for the next 10 years.  Recommendation 7 of the Review proposes that ‘a Psychological Therapies Framework should be developed for NI which should include a taxonomy of all psychological professions, defined career pathways and access routes to training programmes for existing and emerging psychological professions’.  Implementation of the Workforce Review will be subject to prioritisation and future budget availability. The Review report can be found at the following link:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-mental-health-workforce-review-22-32.pdf
