question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has any plans to introduce provisions similar to those included in the Strikes (Minimum Service Levels) Act 2023.
answer_text: I refer the member to the recent response to AQW 7341/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Mental Health Support Fund.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered introducing tree planting at Stockman's Way and Stockman's Avenue to help reduce emissions and pollution.
answer_text: My Department has no remit to plant additional trees at Stockman’s Way and Stockman’s Avenue.My Department often works in partnership with local councils and Department for Communities on environmental improvement initiatives such as this and can confirm that we are not aware of any plans at present in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what decisions have been taken to sustainably fund Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice for the long-term amid a rising demand for its services.
answer_text: I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments. The Minister of Finance had advised that should my department need additional financial support for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice she would be open to a discussion around this.  Senior Departmental representatives met with the NICH on  3rd April 2024 to discuss the current situation in respect of funding, costs and service delivery. They are due to met with NICH on the 9th May and at this time I will have an understanding from NICH about the level of funding they will require to sustain services in the short and longer term. Following this meeting I can make decisions regarding representations for additional funding to the Minister of Finance if this is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions have taken place about investing in GRID training for at least one paediatric consultant.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) currently provides seven training posts across five sub-specialties: Neonatology; Community Child Health; Paediatric Emergency Medicine;  Paediatric Intensive Care; and Paediatric Immunology & Infectious Diseases.The Department is not aware of any discussions regarding investing in further training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department, in the design and development of the new planning portal, ensured that approved drawings can be placed under one section in the portal in the interest of improving public access to planning applications online.
answer_text: The project to deliver the new Planning Portal was a collaborative undertaking between my Department and the 10 participating local councils.  The system has delivered an improved online functionality for users with approved drawing(s) referenced within the relevant Public Decision Notice and accessible via the online Planning Register. The Intelligent Client Function within Belfast City Council will continue to engage with stakeholders to improve functionality where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding he has secured to implement the Strategy for Children’s Palliative and End-of-Life Care 2016-26 in full, including funding for a GRID-trained specialist paediatric palliative care consultant.
answer_text: The issue of access to paediatric palliative care for seriously ill children and young people in Northern Ireland is extremely important to me and to the work that is being done within my department.The Department and then Minister published the Paediatric Palliative and End of Life Care Strategy in 2016.  This strategy was completed after considerable consultation with all relevant stakeholders across the sector in NI and beyond to ensure it was as comprehensive as possible.The Strategy includes 23 ambitious objectives that cover the full range of issues within paediatric palliative care, from diagnosis of a palliative or life limited condition through to bereavement support for families of those children who have passed away.Subsequent to the publication of the strategy and with the availability of the necessary funding, which was made available from 2019 as a result of Transformation Funding, a number of specific innovations were introduced in NI.  This has included the following: -The appointment of a paediatric palliative care lead in each Trust.  Funding for this is for one programmed activity per Trust per week which totals £48,597 regionally;the appointment of a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care consultant who was trained at Great Ormond Street but works at the RBHSC and has input across the region.  The funding for this is £120,000 per year;the appointment of Paediatric Palliative and Life Limited Nurse and service.  The nurse works in RJMH and RBHSC supporting staff and facilitating a move to a non-hospital setting (home or hospice) for step down or step up care, and where appropriate, an end of life care service. As well as the specialist nursing role, this service has access to one 24/7 hospice bed or virtual hospice bed with support available for over 300 bed nights per year. Following a recent uplift in funding £204,515 is now available per annum.As well as the above a further £17,734 per annum was made available to offer administrative support to the Regional Paediatric Palliative Care consultant.  There was also a further £4,189 in 2021/22 to Southern Health and Social Care Trust in respect of a palliative medical lead for Paediatrics Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what consideration they have given to a redress payment to victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses; and (ii) what factors will be taken into consideration to determine the size of any redress payment.
answer_text: The Executive Office has made considerable progress in implementing the 5 key recommendations made in the Truth Recovery Design Panel’s ‘Truth, Acknowledgement and Accountability’ Report into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses. One of the key recommendations relates to redress and includes important non-financial redress measures like support services, which are in place. Financial redress is proposed to include a standardised payment and individually assessed payment. Individually Assessed Payment would relate to the individual’s experience, and would follow the public inquiry. The financial redress schemes need legislation given the potential scale of the scheme, the need for a body or service to administer, and that payment should not impact relevant social security benefits.Before the draft legislation to establish a public inquiry and redress scheme can be finalised, views on the policy proposals must be sought through public consultation. The responses (and other factors, such as comparator redress schemes) will help to shape the scheme.  The consultation will launch as soon as possible, and the requisite legislation will be ready to introduce in the Assembly at the earliest opportunity once it has Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that families receive urgent intervention while they wait for the implementation of a childcare strategy.
answer_text: I have bid for funding to enable me to implement a package of measures in the 2024/25 financial year on which the longer-term strategy can be built.  I hope to be in a position soon to bring forward costed proposals for Executive consideration.  What I am able to do will depend on the funding made available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of (i) the number of counsellors; and (ii) the mental health services provided, to (a) schools; and (b) pupils in West Belfast, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not commission school counselling services and does not collate this information.Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) are available to all children who meet threshold referral criteria. The Department does not collate school level detail for CAMHS referrals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to introduce ethical procurement legislation to target firms who breach human rights.
answer_text: Every firm should uphold human rights. Responsibility for Procurement Legislation lies with DoF. Its approach to addressing this issue has my full support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has received any complaints about the HR Connect online application system.
answer_text: A number of candidates reported some technology related issues in relation to an Administrative Officer competition being run for the Department for Communities for their Department of Work and Pensions (DWP) expansion programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the Earl of Shaftesbury's asking price in the sale of the Lough Neagh bed and soil.
answer_text: The asking price with respect to the sale of the Lough Neagh bed and soil is a matter for the Earl of Shaftesbury.In terms of future ownership of the Lough Neagh bed and soil, I welcome the recent National Lottery Heritage Fund award with respect to the Lough Neagh Heritage Resilience Project, led by the Lough Neagh Partnership. This will include a range of activities including investigation into the feasibility of acquiring and transferring ownership of the bed and soil to public and / or community ownership. I await the outcome of this work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered amending the legislation on access to records in line with recent changes elsewhere.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will (i) publish the number of Community Children's Nurses employed by the health service; and (ii) fund any additional posts needed.
answer_text: Information provided by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of community children’s registered nurses employed (at April 2024) is detailed in the table below.HSC TrustCommunity Children's Registered Nurses employed (whole-time equivalent)Belfast21.55Northern23.99South Eastern16.80Southern22.77Western17.10Total102.14The Department will continue to engage with HSC Trusts to identify pressures and explore mechanisms which can support service delivery more effectively including that of appropriate staffing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's spend on private healthcare providers in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Given the significant pressure on our health and social care system, the growing demand and capacity gap means that over recent years our waiting list activity has become increasingly important in dealing with Red Flag (suspect Cancer) and time critical patients. To achieve the maximum impact, the system relies on use of the independent sector (private healthcare providers) to deliver additional waiting list activity.  With the scale of the backlog of patients waiting for treatment, the HSC capacity secured from independent sector providers has been crucial for the many people that have benefited from it.  The Elective Care Framework is clear that, in addition to multi-year funding to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, sustained recurrent investment is required to address the central issue of demand outstripping the current capacity. The following table summarises expenditure with private healthcare providers in each of the last two financial years, as advised by HSC Trusts.£’mBHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTNI Total2022/2322.212.614.912.113.074.82023/2417.310.915.711.18.663.6Notes:Figures rounded to nearest £0.1m.Expenditure noted in the table relates to spend with private health providers in each of the last two financial years largely for assessment and treatment of patients.Expenditure and funding may differ due to timing differences on payment of invoices.In their response, Western Trust advised that their spend with private healthcare providers excludes the Trust support element (£0.5m) for Endoscopy insourcing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he will take to address the shortfall in social work provision; and (ii) what impact this is having on early years services such as Sure Start.
answer_text: My Department has been working collaboratively with HSC Trusts, Universities and Colleges, trade union representatives and other key stakeholders to try to address some of the difficulties in HSC social work services.A Social Work Workforce Review was undertaken in 2021 and its findings were published in 2022. The Department then established the Social Work Workforce Implementation Board, involving senior leaders and a range of workforce representatives, to plan and deliver real improvements in the short, medium and longer term. Some progress has been made in alleviating pressures and improving stability in the social work workforce, including:in December 2022, a decision was taken to end the use of agency social workers in all HSC services by June 2023.the Trusts recruited around 160 agency social workers into vacant HSC posts between January and June 2023 and all use of agency social workers in HSC has now ceased. the Department created a faster, more efficient route into Trust employment for newly qualified social workers, which resulted in almost 200 vacancies being filled between June and September 2023. It is intended to repeat this exercise in the summer of 2024.the Department has also increased the number of training places for student social workers by commissioning 25 additional places with the Open University NI. The Department continues to make the case for an increase in training places for student social workers, which is entirely dependent upon the availability of additional, recurrent funding.Sure Start services are based in communities to allow families and children ease of access, develop local knowledge and understanding and ensure a multi-disciplinary approach as necessary. Staff employed in Sure Start projects have various professional backgrounds there has been no direct evidence to date that the current social work workforce challenges have impacted on early years services such as Sure Start. In addition, Sure Start settings have the autonomy to create and adjust their staffing structures to ensure this is appropriate to meet locals needs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a breakdown of all institutions included in the guidance for the Truth Recovery Programme.
answer_text: The Truth Recovery Design Panel report published in October 2021 did not outline a specific institutional list for investigation, instead it centred institutional types i.e. Mother and Baby Institutions, Madgalene Laundries and Workhouses and their pathways and practices.The integrated investigation will centre on examining the facts and therefore needs to be sufficiently flexible to respond to the evidence provided. The first stage of the integrated investigation, the non-statutory Independent Panel, have a call for evidence which covers a broad range of institutions and practices.This will help shape the list of institutions for the public Inquiry and redress schemes.  In addition, there is a proposal for the draft Inquiry legislation to include giving the Department (after consulting the Inquiry Chairperson) a power to add other relevant institutions which may potentially be identified for investigation. This provides the Inquiry some further flexibility and allows it to take into account the findings of the Independent Panel.This proposal will be outlined in the upcoming public consultation to seek views.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will appoint an interim advocate for the Truth Recovery Programme.
answer_text: The Truth Recovery Design Panel Report did not recommend a dedicated Advocate role. Instead a series of advocacy interventions have included dedicated and specialist support services, the monthly Victim and Survivor Consultation Forum (with an Independent Chair) and via the Independent Panel Victim and Survivor Representatives.There is currently a series of workshops underway with Consultation Forums members which include views on advocacy. While there is no immediate plan to appoint a single Advocate role, on completion of this there will be opportunity to consider how the current advocacy model may be enhanced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Truth Recovery Programme, why the full implementation of interim instructions on access to records has not been completed.
To ask the Minister of Education when the Department will finalise approved intakes to Initial Teacher Education courses.
answer_text: The number of Initial Teacher Education (ITE) places for the Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Northern Ireland for the 2024/25 academic year has not yet been finally determined. My Department is aware of the need for the HEIs to be notified of their ITE intake allocations at the earliest opportunity and is progressing the matter on this basis, in conjunction with the Department for the Economy, which has responsibility for funding ITE places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a pay and grading review for education support staff will form part of his Department’s 2024-25 budget bid.
answer_text: Yes, as part of the Department’s 2024-25 budget bid, I have sought the necessary funding required to enable the implementation of the Pay and Grading Review for education support staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to take measures to ensure the protection and inclusion of transgender pupils in school.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s non-statutory guidance, Supporting Transgender Young People: Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres, and Youth Service on supporting Transgender Young People, aims to support schools in ensuring the protection and inclusion of transgender pupils.  I am considering the need to review the guidance in light of emerging practice and potential legislative changes elsewhere..In order to ensure the safeguarding and protection of all children and young people in our schools, my Department has statutory guidance in place for schools: Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools.Additional supports to assist schools and teachers in addressing all forms of bullying are available through the EA’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team, which provides support for schools, educational settings and young people to help them understand the requirements of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act and embed cultures that help eradicate bullying.  The work of the team also includes providing programmes of direct support for those who experience all types of bullying.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of any disparity of neonatal deaths in Black and Asian infants; and (ii) how his Department plans to address this.
answer_text: (i) The ethnic profile/population in Northern Ireland is significantly different to that in the rest of the UK, with a very small number of neonatal deaths of Black and Asian ethnicity. However, we acknowledge that this is undoubtedly an important group that need to be appropriately supported throughout their pregnancy journey. (ii) Regardless of ethnicity, improving the health and wellbeing of mothers and babies, particularly those from a socially deprived community, remains a priority. Work aimed at addressing issues that are more prevalent in higher social disadvantaged groups and are known to carry a higher risk of loss such as smoking, substance abuse, obesity, age and other co-morbidities is ongoing within Trusts, with a number of programmes in place regionally to address this. MBRRACE-UK facilitates investigation of any deaths of women and their babies during or after childbirth, and also cases where women and their babies survive serious illness during pregnancy or after childbirth. The aim is to identify avoidable illness and deaths so the lessons learned can be used to prevent similar cases in the future leading to improvements in maternal and newborn care for all mothers and babies. Northern Ireland implements all recommendations, including any learning from all the MBRRACE reports, as appropriate. Other initiatives, such as the Family Nurse Partnership and Continuity of Midwifery Carer Programme enable support for pregnant women, mothers and families to be available at local level. These evidence-based programmes have delivered improvement in care and outcomes across Northern Ireland. A national care bundle Saving Babies’ Lives aims to prevent and reduce stillbirth rates and has been adopted in Northern Ireland. The third iteration has been published and a regional group has been established to support implementation, with additional funding required to take this forward.All HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland review each stillbirth using a structured tool called the Perinatal Mortality Review Tool (PMRT). This is a systematic, multidisciplinary review of the circumstances and care leading up to and surrounding each stillbirth which identifies learning and actions to improve future care. Support for full implementation of the programme is ongoing via SPPG and PHA.  My Department also leads on the work of the Northern Ireland Bereavement Network. One of the priority areas for the Network was the recent launch of a website which will signpost citizens to bereavement support services, including those who have experienced early pregnancy or baby loss NI Bereavement Network - Bereaved NI (hscni.net)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether he is aware of any shortfall in the number of exam markers with fluency in the Irish language; and (ii) what action he will take to address any shortfall.
answer_text: Marking and awarding qualifications is a matter for the relevant awarding organisation in line with the appropriate regulatory framework. I am aware that the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) would ideally like to have a sufficient supply of suitably qualified specialists capable of marking papers in Irish across the full range of qualifications but has faced significant challenges in trying to recruit Irish speaking examiners due to the limited pool of Irish speaking teachers in each specialist subject. To ensure students in Irish Medium settings are able to complete their qualifications in Irish CCEA operates a translation model enabling the wider pool of non-Irish speaking examiners to mark the translated work of Irish Medium pupils.CCEA continues to make every effort to recruit sufficient examiners including through digital marketing recruitment campaigns and broadening the recruitment criteria to try to expand the pool of Irish speakers who may apply to be examiners.  This year CCEA introduced changes to its process for recruitment of examiners to try to identify all teachers who feel they are competent to mark papers completed in Irish for different subjects. CCEA officers are hopeful this will help increase the pool of examiners who can mark papers in Irish, and they continue to work with the Irish Medium sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to facilitate the recovery of drug and alcohol users through the provision of public addiction rehabilitation centres.
answer_text: Under the Substance Use Strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, a range of services are commissioned to provide help and support for people across Northern Ireland. Services range from prevention and awareness raising, to inpatient treatment services, and long-term support for recovery. A list of services available in each area is available online at: https://drugsandalcoholni.info/self-help-resources/ As part of the delivery of the strategy’s outcomes, the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group have been co-producing a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework, covering all tiers of service provision. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, which is currently being finalised for publication in April or May 2024 after public consultation, will: ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; andrecognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.As an early action in the strategy, an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland has been commissioned and is near finalisation. This will help set out the way forward for such services in Northern Ireland. It is important to note however that the full delivery of the strategy and the strategic service delivery plan will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people have entered the criminal justice system in relation to assisted dying in the last five years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence related to assisted dying.  Offences related to assisted dying may be recorded under more generic Violence Against the Person offence descriptions.  One such offence is ‘Assisting or encouraging suicide’.  However, in the period 2018 – 2022, the most recent five year period for which information is available, there were no prosecutions or convictions at courts for this offence.  Departmental datasets do not contain information on the circumstances under which an offence occurred, so, from information held, the Department is not able to provide additional detail beyond that provided above.  However, information on relevant cases entering the justice system may be available from detail contained in case files submitted to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) by police.  Reporting of information held by PPS is a matter for the Director of Public Prosecutions.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the issues raised in the question to the Public Prosecution Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to lower the age of access for gender-affirming healthcare.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust’s Knowing Our Identity Service provides a regional assessment and specific intervention service to those aged under 18 years presenting with distress in relation to their assigned birth gender. The clinical decision for access to gender-affirming healthcare for Gender Dysphoria is linked to the stage of puberty the young person has reached, rather than age.  Each case however is assessed on an individual basis.The Belfast Trust’s Gender services will work collaboratively with my Department and Regional Health and Social Care colleagues to inform the future direction of Gender Identity services and models of service delivery to meet the needs of the population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what funding will be put in place for community organisations and charities providing mental health services, including counselling.
answer_text: While a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set, it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging.My Department is currently forecasting a funding gap of some £1 billon and therefore, our expectation is that we will not be able to do all that we want or need to do and the main focus will be on preserving and protecting existing services, with all the limitations that they currently have. How we tackle mental health in Northern Ireland remains a priority for my Department and will be a key consideration in regards to funding, however, I am unable to confirm any specific funding in the absence of a budget.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will comply with the Home Office changes to the visa scheme for Ukrainians.
answer_text: Immigration policy, which includes the Ukraine Visa Schemes, is not a devolved matter.

The table below provides information on the number of employee jobs in industry Section Q: Human Health and Social Work Activities by private sector jobs. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have visited a hospital in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, including Emergency Departments, due to health issues related to damp and mould, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not currently collect information on attendances at Emergency Departments due to health issues related to damp and mould. It should also be noted that due to regional inconsistencies regarding the level and quality of recording the reason why patients attend Emergency Departments, we do not currently hold any information on why patients attend Emergency Departments.Information is collected by the Department on the reason for admission to hospital from the Patient Administration System used by Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs). As there are no specific codes used for health-related issues due to damp or mould, please find overleaf table with the number of people admitted to hospitals in Belfast HSCT with a causative factor of inadequate housing. Individuals1 admitted to hospitals in Belfast HSCT with a diagnosis of inadequate housing2YearIndividuals2018/1902019/20<52020/21<52021/22<52022/236Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.1Figures represent individuals. Figures across years cannot be summed as an individual may have been admitted across multiple years. Admissions are approximated using deaths and discharges. Admissions to the Mental Health and Learning Disability programmes of care are not included.2The ICD-10 does not have any codes that specifically classify damp or mould as a causative factor. The ICD-10 code “Z59.1” (inadequate housing) has been used in any of the sixteen secondary diagnoses fields of the Hospital Inpatient System. This may be used to indicate damp or mould problems within a patient’s accommodation; however, this code is not specific to these issues, and other problems will also be included.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any complaints about building defects in the apartments in Library Square, Belfast, have been raised with (i) his Department; and (ii) the local council.
answer_text: I am not aware of any complaints about building defects in the apartments in Library Square, Belfast, raised to (i) my Department; and (ii) the local council.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what measures are in place to ensure there are no conflicts of interest in relation to ministerial appointments to senior posts in arm’s length bodies.
answer_text: The Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments is published by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI).  Under this Code, applicants for public appointments are asked to declare any real, perceived, or potential conflicts of interest. The selection panel must consider and explore the answers and assess if there is a conflict of interest and, if so, how it will be handled.  The issue must be recorded and, should the applicant otherwise be suitable for appointment, detailed in the applicant summary sent to the appointing Minister(s) to allow for an informed decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the salary of the Chief Executive Officer of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The salary range for the position of Chief Executive of the Education Authority is £137,216 to £163,986.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered issuing an appeal for people living in defective housing, similar to those in Victoria Square housing, to come forward and seek assistance.
answer_text: Defects in privately owned housing, such as those in the Victoria Square development, are a civil matter between the owners and the developer or builder of their homes.  It would not be appropriate for my Department to consider issuing such an appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what representations he has made to the UK government about the provision of welfare payments to asylum seekers.
answer_text: I have made no such representations to the UK Government. The availability of benefits for persons coming to the United Kingdom depends initially on their immigration status.  Immigration and asylum are excepted matters under Schedule 2 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and do not fall within my Department’s remit; responsibility rests with the Home Office.Under section 115 of the Immigration and Asylum Act 1999, which extends to Northern Ireland, a person subject to immigration control (including asylum seekers) is excluded from receiving specified benefits.  If refugee status or humanitarian protection is granted by the Home Office, social security benefits become available subject to satisfaction of the conditions of entitlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to reform the nursing bank.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG), with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. Work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, as well as, the reform of the Bank system.A number of separate reviews have been undertaken in recent years which included reference to the Nurse/Midwifery Bank service in HSC Trusts. The ARIG have now established a working group to assess the recommendations in each of these reports and to consider their implementation across the HSC Trusts.The recommendations can be summarised into 5 key points:A technology-enabled regional Nursing /Midwifery bank, with local Trust ownership, across the HSCNI;Harmonisation of processes;Pay and Non pay incentives;Introduction of performance measures; andReview of retention approaches for Nursing/Midwifery bank.The working group are currently reviewing these recommendations to establish what actions are required to be taken forward, alongside an associated timeline for implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what advice he sought from health experts in relation to restricting access to puberty blockers.
To ask the Minister for Communities how many homeless people are currently being placed in paid specialist accommodation such as hotels and guest houses.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:As of 31 December 2023, there was a total of 395 households placed in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotel). It should be noted that non-standard accommodation will only be used in the absence of other options such as voluntary sector hostels and will be used for as short a duration as possible. The 395 placements in non-standard accommodation reflects approximately 8.8% of the 4,471 households in temporary accommodation as of 31 December 2023. The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 contains information on the numbers classified as experiencing homelessness and is available here:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 It should be noted that it is not possible to provide any data beyond 31 December until the publication of the next bulletin which is scheduled for June 2023.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to provide inclusive and safe healthcare for transgender people.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with the Belfast HSC Trust to address waiting times, resources, and to develop a model of care for Gender Identity services that meets the needs of transgender people living in NI.  As part of this process, a Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group and a Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sits under the main Pathway Review Group,  were established to provide clinical and service expertise  and encourage  those with lived experience to provide input.  My officials have been working  with both groups to develop a solution which meets the immediate needs of service users and enables the service to expand services and build capacity over a period of time.  This solution would also address the long waiting times for this service. However, given the very significant funding challenges faced by my Department and the lack of an agreed budget it has not been possible to progress this work as we would have liked.  My officials are currently working collaboratively with the Belfast HSC Trust and members of the Review Group and Liaison Panel to develop a business case to enhance existing service provision and it will be delivered as part of normal service planning. Funding of around £660k in 2024/25 will be required recurrently to fund the service development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to take domiciliary care provision away from the private sector.
answer_text: The independent social care sector is an integral part of the health and social care system, in providing the necessary care in conjunction with the services provided by the statutory sector. The independent sector delivers more than 75% of care in this area and we simply could not meet the ever growing care demands without this partnership.Both the independent sector, and the statutory sector currently are facing challenges in service delivery and capacity. The key focus for my Department is to stabilise the sectors, and to enhance and grow capacity in the provision of domiciliary care. Last week I announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this was to ensure independent sector providers had the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April. I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funds that will be required to deliver this.In addition, in May 2023, my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum to commence the delivery of reforms in adult social care, with a number of workstreams established across a range of key issues. As part of these workstreams, work is ongoing to develop a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region; a new model for domiciliary care, and to take forward key aspects of social care workforce development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work undertaken by his Department to fully commence the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (NI) 2022 requires my Department to make regulations providing at least 10 days paid leave in each leave year for workers that are victims of domestic abuse. Work to operationalise this Act is ongoing and I will consult on proposals before the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, as part of their in-kind donation to Queen's University, (i) what data Alteryx will be able to collect and store; and (ii) where the data will be stored.
answer_text: Queens University Belfast has advised my Department that the in-kind donation from Alteryx is for an initial three years and will provide opportunities for Queen’s students to undertake data analytics automation training through the usage of the Alteryx software/platform and other relevant training.  Data collection and storage do not form part of the agreement between the two parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, as part of their in-kind donation to Queen's University, whether Alteryx will open any data centres in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am not aware of any plans that Alteryx have to open data centres here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to meet University and College Union following its rejection of the pay offer.
answer_text: I met with the University and College Union and the National Association of Schoolmasters and Union of Women Teachers on 12 February, during my first week in office, and I intend to have further engagements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what bids he made to support a pay uplift for further education workers.
answer_text: At the first opportunity, in February 2024, I submitted a bid of £16.3 million to address all my Department’s pressures.  This included a bid for Further Education pay.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail who was in attendance at their official meeting with the US President during their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: At our meeting with President Biden on 17 March 2024 our respective Special Advisers were in attendance. The Special Envoy for Economic Affairs, Joe Kennedy III, was also present.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a breakdown of the business organisations they met during their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: During our recent visit to Washington D.C. we were able to address a number of important events where U.S. business organisations were present including the Ireland Funds National Gala, the NI Bureau Breakfast Reception, and the U.S. Special Envoy for Economic Affairs Reception. In addition, we had a roundtable discussion with business organisations faciltated by the American Chamber of Commerce which included representatives of Forescout, Merck, Cigna Healthcare, AIG, Caterpillar, BD, Pfizer and Amazon.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether minutes were taken at their official meeting with the US President during their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: We provided a read out of the meeting to officials.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they met with any representatives of the Israeli government during their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: During our recent visit to Washington D.C. we did not meet with any representatives of the Israeli Government.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what discussions they had in relation to (i) tax incentives; and (ii) grants with US corporations during their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: During our recent visit to Washington D.C. we used the opportunity to deliver a clear message to U.S. businesses and investors that we are an attractive investment opportunity for global companies. We did not have specific discussions on tax incentives or grants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice he has sought from the Commissioner for Children and Young People in relation to banning puberty blockers.
answer_text: Puberty blockers are not banned in Northern Ireland. They are available on NHS prescription for those patients who are under 18 and who are on the Belfast Trust's Knowing Our Identity (KOI) Service endocrine pathway prior to March 2020.  We have not sought advice from the Commissioner for Children and Young People at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department has carried out to source replacements for amalgam fillings.
answer_text: The Statement of Dental Remuneration already lists non-amalgam materials suitable for fillings. Non-amalgam alternatives are not directly comparable and have different properties that need to be considered when restoring a tooth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many individuals have availed of puberty blockers in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is not available.  Drugs typically prescribed to suppress the release of sex hormones in individuals can be for the treatment of a range of conditions and the HSC Business Services Organisation does not hold information on the indication for which drugs have been prescribed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with transgender representative organisations in relation to banning puberty blockers.
answer_text: Puberty blockers are not banned in Northern Ireland. They are available on NHS prescription for those under 18s, who were accepted and remain in the Belfast Trust's Knowing Our Identity Service  endocrine pathway prior to March 2020.  My officials have been working with the Gender Identity Services Pathway Review Group and the Gender Identity Services Liaison Panel (which includes representatives from transgender organisations) to develop the Gender Identity Service.  At the next quarterly meeting, they will have the opportunity to discuss the ban on puberty blockers by NHS England.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the grants issued by Invest NI, including those firms which received them, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23, ending 31st March 2023, being the most recent year available.  Information relating to 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release in June 2024.  To improve transparency around our support, Invest NI publishes five years of data relating to its direct financial support to businesses on the Open Data NI portal, which includes the information requested by the Member. Included on this page are:Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance - A dataset containing details on individual financial offers of assistance issued to businesses between the 1 April 2018 and 31 March 2023. This includes information on the amount of assistance offered by Invest NI, as well as the amount of investment secured and the number of jobs to be created by the offer. The dataset also includes information on the type of grant supported (Conditions of Offer), business location, business sector, business size and whether the offer is made to a locally or externally owned business.  Invest NI Open Data Guidance – A guidance booklet which explains the information included in the dataset.Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2018 and 31 March 2023 - A set of data tables that aggregate the data included on the dataset into several different variables for ease of analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what discussions he has had in relation to the provision of additional financial support to St Gerard’s School and Support Services.
To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of awarding public procurement contracts to Fujitsu.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of how the publication of planning guidance on Unauthorised Environmental Impact Assessment Development, represents progress towards compliance with United Nations Decision VII/8s, Section 4, as is contained in the United Kingdom Progress Report to the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee 2023.
answer_text: I am considering briefing from officials on this matter as part of the ongoing process of engagement with the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee in relation to Decision VII/8s.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what consideration it has given to implementing social clauses in contracts with catering and porter providers.
answer_text: The business case for the provision of the Support Services Contract (SSC) (catering, cleaning, porterage and related helpdesk services), approved by the Assembly Commission, sets out the contract’s objectives. In particular, Objective 11 is set out as “To secure a SSC that embeds environmental, social and economic sustainability and that includes the establishment of partnerships with social enterprises.”In order to inform, test and evaluate the potential suppliers, as part of the tender process, the contract’s specification document includes a section on Social and Economic Sustainability. Furthermore, one of the qualitative criteria used to evaluate each potential suppliers’ bid is specific to social and economic sustainability (AC / Sustainability plan to include social value initiatives).

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) when the current Aramark contract expires; and (ii) whether it has considered bringing staff providing these services under the employment of the Assembly Commission.
answer_text: The current Aramark contract for the provision of catering, cleaning, porterage and related helpdesk services commenced on 9 January 2023 for an initial five-year term ending 8 January 2028. There is also provision for a single 24-month contract extension ending 8 January 2030.A number of contractual options including bringing the staff providing the services under the employment of the Assembly Commission or “in-house” were included in the business case considered by the Assembly Commission at its meetings held on 9 February 2022 and 10 October 2022.  This option was not selected as the Assembly Commission does not possess the expertise, the technology and the external upstream partners required to operate the services, notably with regard to the supply chain.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any concerns have been raised by Assembly staff in relation to flexible working conditions.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has a ‘Flexible Working and Partial Retirement Policy’ which allows a member of staff to adjust their working pattern either permanently or on a temporary basis. Approximately a quarter of Assembly Commission staff currently utilise a permanent flexible working pattern. In relation to any concerns being raised by Assembly staff regarding flexible working conditions, for the period January to December 2023, the Assembly Commission received one appeal following a partial retirement request. The Assembly Commission also has a ‘Hybrid Working Policy’ which offers staff the opportunity to work from home, where possible, carefully balanced against the realities of business need and the importance of colleagues being together for strong team dynamics. The balance between working in Parliament Buildings and working at home varies depending on the post, business need and the preference of the member of staff. In this context, consideration can be given to up to 60% of contracted hours per week being worked from home. During recess periods, greater flexibility may be considered, subject to business need.In terms of general feedback on the Hybrid Working Policy a survey was issued to Assembly Commission staff in April 2023 seeking their views on how well the Policy was working. In summary, the response from the survey was very positive about hybrid working and the impact it was having on staff health and wellbeing, morale and productivity. No change was made to the Hybrid Working Policy on foot of the review however, given that the Policy has only ever operated during a period when the Assembly has not been fully functioning, it was agreed that a further review of the Policy should take place following six months of normal Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has considered having trade union members on the Assembly Commission, including in an observer capacity.
answer_text: Under section 40 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, membership of the Assembly Commission is restricted to the Speaker as Presiding Officer of the Assembly and the Members appointed by the Assembly in accordance with Standing Orders.In addition to a member of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee attending meetings at the invitation of the Assembly Commission, the Assembly Commission has only appointed observers when vacancies in its membership could not be filled when the Assembly was not sitting in order to ensure that the Assembly Commission continued to obtain perspectives from across the Assembly.  The Assembly Commission has not considered the appointment of any further observers.  The Assembly Commission is committed to conducting its employee relations business in partnership with Trade Union Side. To facilitate that partnership, the Assembly Commission has two forums in which to conduct its business – the Employee Relations Group (ERG) and the Employee Relations Board (ERB).The ERG is chaired by the Director of Corporate Services and meets with Trade Union Side every two months to discuss operational matters of importance to both sides.The ERB is chaired by the Clerk/Chief Executive and meets once per year. The ERB undertakes a strategic overview of employee relations matters within the Assembly Commission and has a specific role in dealing with issues that are not resolved at the ERG level.A Joint Agreement sets out the working arrangements between the Assembly Commission and Trade Union Side. In the event of a disagreement which is not resolved at ERB level, the Joint Agreement states that both sides should have the opportunity to agree a joint statement setting out an assessment of the issue(s) and submitting it to the Assembly Commission for consideration. The Assembly Commission may decide to hear representations from both sides in such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for its assessment of the adequacy of sick pay protection for Aramark staff employed in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Staff employed in Parliament Buildings for the delivery of catering, cleaning, porterage and related helpdesk services are directly employed by Aramark. As part of the tender process, the Assembly Commission requires all participating organisations to complete a selection questionnaire which assists the Assembly Commission to identify tenderers who can demonstrate the professional and technical ability and experience to be able to successfully deliver this project for the Assembly Commission. The selection questionnaire is a self-declaration, made by the tenderer, that they do not meet any of the grounds for exclusion as set out in the instructions to tenderers for this contract. Ultimately, this in effect means that the tenderer as an organisation is vetted and this confirms their legitimacy and the fact that they are operating lawfully.  For example, one such requirement in the questionnaire is to confirm whether the tenderer has breached any labour law obligations and if so to provide details.  This tender competition was conducted in accordance with the Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (PCR) 2015) (as amended by the Public Contracts (Amendment) (EU Exit) Regulations 2020).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what legislation they intend to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: Along with other Executive Ministers, we are carefully considering our legislative priorities to inform a paper on the Executive’s legislation Programme for discussion at an Executive meeting over the coming weeks.  Once agreed, the Programme, including Executive Office bills, will be communicated formally to the Assembly as soon as is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what legislation she intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.At this stage, bills to modernise the justice system, sentencing and hate crime and victims issues are under active consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: The following Bills are currently included in the Department’s draft legislative programme, and agreed with the Office of Legislative Counsel (OLC):Title of BillProposed introduction dateAdult Protection May 2024Public HealthSecond half 2024Pharmacy Technician First half 2025Safe Staffing BillFirst half 2025Alcohol Minimum Unit Pricing Bill First half 2026Independent Medical Examiner Second half 2026Reform of Children’s Social Care Services Second half 2026

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what legislation he intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme, to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what legislation she intends to introduce in the current Assembly mandate; and (ii) to detail the timeframe for the introduction of this legislation.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many meetings he has held with officials since taking up post in relation to the legislation allowing councils to conduct business in remote or hybrid format.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7233/22-27, for a breakdown of which constituency the rent controls were implemented in.
answer_text: I am unable to answer this question. This is because the information my Department has on rent controls is not held in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of NIE Networks' increase of profits by 22 per cent.
answer_text: The prices that NIE Networks may charge NI electricity consumers is regulated by the independent Utility Regulator under statutory price controls.  The price control determinations set the return on investment that NIE Networks is allowed to make.  The price controls also have an express sharing mechanism whereby savings and higher than expected profits are shared between NIE Networks and consumers.  My predecessor has written to the Utility Regulator to ask if the increase in profit is consistent with these provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what work his Department has carried out to (i) challenge the Misuse of Drugs Act (1971) with the UK Secretary of State for Health and Social Care; and (ii) call for the devolution of the policy.
answer_text: At this moment in time, the UK-wide Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 remains a reserved matter. Under the Northern Ireland Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, there is an action for DoH to work with the UK Government and the Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs to ensure that the Misuse of Drugs Act reflects the needs of Northern Ireland and supports the delivery of outcomes and indicators in this strategy.  Through ongoing involvement with East/West and North/South bodies, and our representation on the UK Ministerial Drug Summits, Four Nations Meetings and the British-Irish Council Drugs & Alcohol Work Sectoral Group, we will continue to liaise with our counterparts in other administrations and to learn from our neighbouring jurisdictions on related policy and legislation matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he has given to (i) carers; and (ii) people with disabilities in relation to the decision to no longer allow local councils to conduct proceedings in hybrid or remote format.
answer_text: I appreciate there may be a short term impact to councils temporarily being prevented from conducting meetings by hybrid or remote means but I do not believe there is justification in prolonging provisions of the Coronavirus Act which was intended as emergency legislation.  I believe it is more important to ensure that new permanent regulations, enabling councils to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means, are brought forward using the powers in the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.   I will be bringing forward these regulations in as quick a timeframe as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, taking account of net greenhouse gas emissions of major planning projects, (i) how this impacts on his Department's proposed climate action plan; and (ii) for his assessment of whether the approval of major planning projects by local councils is adding to the risk of not complying with legally binding targets set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Action Plan will outline policies and proposals to meet the carbon budget and establish a pathway towards 2030, 2040 and 2050 emissions reduction targets across a range of sectors identified in the Climate Change Act. Policies and proposals will impact emissions in differing ways and their impacts are being considered and quantified on a case-by-case basis. Carbon budgets and targets are set at Northern Ireland level and not on a scheme-by-scheme basis. 		Whilst responsibility for planning in Northern Ireland is shared between local councils and the Department for Infrastructure, meeting Carbon Budgets and emissions targets is the collective responsibility of all departments. I will work closely with all Executive colleagues to deliver effective and joined up climate action that supports achieving our Net Zero target.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether her Department received additional Barnett consequential monies as a result of the cut to National insurance; and (ii) how this will be distributed to Departments.
answer_text: The cut to National Insurance would not generate a direct Barnett consequential.The Executive receives Barnett consequential on changes to the budgets of Whitehall departments. These may be positive or negative depending on whether budgets have increased or reduced.We will not be advised if any of the changes are a result of the reduction in National Insurance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6931/22-27, how many employees across the five Health and Social Care Trusts are working in providing abortion services.
answer_text: Across the five Health and Social Care Trusts there are a total of 71 members of staff working in providing abortion services.  This includes medical, nursing, support staff and administrative staff.  These 71 members of staff are employed either permanently or on a sessional basis but with aligned funding. In addition, there is 1.74 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) staffing input made up of theatre and anaesthetic consultants and nurses working in abortion services in Northern Ireland in line with funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 130/22-27, to detail the percentage of respondents in favour of maintaining free travel for those aged over 65.
answer_text: The Concessionary Fares public consultation did not include an option which would remove free travel for those aged over 65. It did, however, include an option which would raise the age of eligibility of the SmartPass from age 60 to age 65 or State Pension Age.  I am still considering the responses to the public consultation and as such, it would not be appropriate for me to release this information at this stage.Once I have announced my decision on which, if any, of the options to take forward, I will publish a consultation summary report which will detail the support for and against the options proposed, along with key themes raised by respondents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many junior doctors remain working in Health and Social Care Trusts after being fully qualified; and (ii) whether his Department has considered implementing a recruitment and retention campaign to keep junior doctors in Health and Social Care Trusts after their studies are completed.
answer_text: (i) In the last full training year, August 2022 to July 2023, there were 105 doctors awarded a Certificate of Completion of Training (CCT).  As of 21st March 2023, 69 have consultant or specialty doctor employment with the HSC Trusts.(ii) My officials have been developing a suite of measures to increase the attractiveness of Northern Ireland as a place within which to pursue medical education, and to encourage this key group to commit to being an essential part of our workforce of the future, on completion of their training.  These measures include:mandating Protected Educational development time;better and more sensitive organisation of work schedules and rotas;and the provision of quality facilities for trainees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what meetings his departmental officials are having with councils in relation to caravan sites.
answer_text: I can confirm that no meetings are due to take place between my departmental officials and Councils in relation to caravan sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many (i) bathroom replacement schemes; and (ii) kitchen replacement schemes are overdue for Housing executive tenants in West Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Following the release of the planned schemes contractors back in August 22, seven Kitchen/Bathroom/Rewire Projects (599 dwellings) and five Bathroom Only Projects (501 dwellings) were delayed.  All twelve of these projects have subsequently been included in a replacement contract that was awarded in August 2023.  One of the projects has commenced onsite and a further six have been briefed and are progressing through the pre-site approval process.  All works will be completed before September 2025.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the circumstances a caravan site owner can remove or end a lease on site.
answer_text: The Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 details in Part 1, paragraphs 4-6 of the Schedule that an owner of a residential site can terminate a lease following a court ruling for specific circumstances. These circumstances are that: 	an occupier has breached a term of their written agreement; 	the occupier is not occupying the caravan as their only main residence; or 	the court is satisfied that, having regard to its condition, the caravan is having a detrimental effect on the site.		In respect of a holiday site, the Licence Agreement should clearly set out the rights and obligations on both the caravan owner and site owner, regarding termination of the lease.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has the power to set limits or caps on site fees charged by caravan park owners.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has the power to set limits or caps on site fees charged by caravan owners.  This would, however, require an amendment to the existing primary legislation.  My Department however only has policy responsibility for the residential caravan sector.  Any changes that impact on the seasonal (holiday) caravan sector would need to be initiated by the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, under the International Relations Strategy, which countries Ministers will, or plan to, (i) travel to; and (ii) host, as part of trade, peace building and other areas of work in the next two years.
answer_text: We have seen significant changes internationally in the last 10 years since the first International Relations Strategy was published in 2014.  An updated strategy is needed which is reflective of emerging opportunities, maximises delivery through best use of existing resources, and is agile in its response to developing challenges and opportunities. We recognise the importance of our international presence and engagement with strategic partners and are considering how a future strategy could help to deliver the Executive’s priorities. Once developed, the International Relations Strategy will set out the optimal countries and regions for engagement to maximise delivery of our priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 115/22-27, how much of the £70 million was spent on the independent and private sector.
answer_text: From April to December 2023 just over £41m of the £70m has been spent in the independent and private sector resulting in over 88,000 assessments, diagnostic tests and treatments being delivered. Funding is targeted initially at those patients with the highest clinical priority and time critical conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to implement the recommendations of the Independent Review of Education in relation to academic selection.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the Report produced by the Independent Review Panel and carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant Departments and Arm’s-Length Bodies.Following this consultation, officials will produce a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report including those on transitions and progression.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to expand special educational needs provision in the Irish medium sector.
answer_text: There are currently six specialist education classes across three schools in the Irish-medium education (IME) sector.  The Education Authority (EA) is planning for the expansion of specialist education provision in areas of identified need to enable children with special educational needs (SEN) to attend school within their own community where possible, including within Irish-medium schools.  The number of new IME SPiMS classes to be established for September 2024 is not confirmed at this point.The EA offers training to all schools through their SEN Pupil Support Services (PSS) and their Educational Psychology Service (EPS) have established a working group to develop best practice in immersion education and bilingualism. My department engages regularly with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) on the issues facing the IME sector in supporting children with SEN, the specific needs of the sector will be considered as the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7373/22-27, for an update on (i) the Education Authority’s work with St Gerard’s School & Support Services; and (ii) what additional capacity can be provided in the short-term and medium-term.
answer_text: A Business Case completed by the Education Authority (EA) identified the preferred option to provide additional accommodation for St Gerard’s School and Support Services as the purchase of existing modular accommodation from an identified contractor and reconfiguration works to create 14 classrooms, associated support space and School Meals Accommodation on the former Suffolk Primary School site. The Business Case was approved by the Department of Finance on 23 January 2024, in the sum of £4,745,107.  The EA has estimated that it is likely to be late 2025 before a decision on the necessary planning application for the proposed project would be granted and so to expedite the provision of additional school places for children with Special Educational Needs in Greater Belfast the EA proposes to locate eight modular classrooms at the site, as a first phase of works.  These initial eight classrooms are estimated to be in place for December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he will take to ensure children in the special educational needs sector can avail of transport services without disruption at the beginning of the school year.
answer_text: Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) follow a different process for admission to schools, which is based on consultation between the EA, schools and parents. Where a child has a special transport need identified during the statutory assessment process, the process of securing appropriate transport provision commences once a school placement has been confirmed. It is my Department’s aim that the majority of children with SEN should receive confirmation of their school placement at the same time as their peers, as far as possible. We are working towards this goal; however, meeting the significant increase in demand for specialist education provision has been challenging in recent years and remains so for 2024/25.The EA is working at pace on a significant programme of work to establish capacity in special schools and to create more specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) to meet the demand for September 2024.  Home to school transport is provided for a large number of pupils with SEN and the EA makes every effort to ensure that transport arrangements are safe, appropriate and put in place as quickly as possible. It can be challenging to ensure transport is in place by the start of the school year where a child has complex needs that require a bespoke transport solution or where new routes are required for pupils attending SPiMS. Additional services, such as taxi provision, must be publicly procured and where additional staff are needed, such as a bus escort, the relevant recruitment procedures must be followed, AccessNI checks carried out and training delivered to maintain children’s safety.The EA Transport service keep parents/guardians updated on the progress of their child’s SEN transport arrangements and may provide a parental payment in the interim period where it has not been possible to put transport in place at the start of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of meetings he has had about an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: I have had a number of meetings with my officials in which the Anti Poverty Strategy was referred to in the context of broader priorities, and one meeting during which I outlined my preferred approach to progressing work on the Executive’s Anti-Poverty strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when an anti-poverty strategy will be implemented.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and I want to ensure that the actions in the Strategy are deliverable and can make a lasting difference. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publicly consult on the new Draft Seabird Strategy.
answer_text: The Seabird Conservation Strategy is one of several high-profile marine conservation strategies and action plans that I will be considering over the coming months. I intend to consult on a draft Seabird Strategy for Northern Ireland during the second half of 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will (i) publish the Peatland Strategy; and (ii) establish a peatland restoration fund.
answer_text: The draft Peatland Strategy is being finalised and I intend to bring this to the Executive with a view to publishing it later this year.  My officials are currently exploring costs associated with peatland restoration to inform potential funding options.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
