question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will implement a child payment, similar to what is available in Scotland.
answer_text: I have no current plans to implement a child payment in Northern Ireland. The annual cost for the introduction of a Child Payment in Northern Ireland, similar to the scheme implemented in Scotland, would need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has raised any concerns in relation to the pipe infrastructure for sewage in the Falls Road area.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that are significant capacity constraints within the sewerage network in the Falls Road area of Belfast.  Indeed, many parts of the wastewater and drainage infrastructure serving Greater Belfast are now having to operate at, or over, design capacity.My Department’s Living with Water in Belfast Plan aims to address this by working with stakeholders to implement catchment-based drainage solutions which will include investment in wastewater infrastructure alongside sustainable drainage and natural flood management schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to resolve the underfunding in community pharmacy.
answer_text: I can assure the member that we are committed to working on mechanisms to address funding in community pharmacy. Following a meeting with representatives of CPNI on 21st February 2024, I have committed to the inflationary uplift of £6.1m for pharmacy services. Furthermore, an additional £4m was provided in recognition of service pressures for the 2023/24 year. The Business Services Organisation arranged for payment of 10/12 of this (£8.4m) with the February payment. Further work will be taken forward in the coming months with CPNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the findings of the Tamara Bronckaers whistleblowing case, whether he intends to initiate any disciplinary proceedings.
answer_text: Under the NICS disciplinary policy, decisions on disciplinary proceedings are matters for permanent secretaries and not ministers.  The policy is also clear that records relating to disciplinary matters must be carefully safeguarded and kept confidential.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the circumstances which prompted his Department to initiate a preliminary review of the Aggregates Levy Credit Scheme; and (ii) the scope and findings of the review.
answer_text: A preliminary investigation into the Aggregates Levy Credit Scheme (ALCS) was prompted by a member of the public raising a concern.  The review is currently ongoing and it would therefore not be appropriate to comment on the scope and findings of the review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of submissions of interest by parents for Naíscoil and Irish Medium primary schools in Belfast, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the following applications were received by funded Irish medium pre-school settings in the Belfast parliamentary constituency areas during the pre-school admissions process in each of the last three years. Academic year 2023/24SettingParliamentary Constituency1st preferencesNaionra Eanna PlaygroupBelfast North0Naiscoil Bheann Mhadagain PS NU Belfast North32Naiscoil Eanna PS NU (Glengormley)Belfast North47Naiscoil Mhic Reachtain PG Belfast North0Naiscoil na Seolta PG Belfast East0Naiscoil an Droichid PS NU Belfast South36Gaelscoil Na Bhfal PS NU Belfast West42Naiscoil an Lonnain PG Belfast West0Naiscoil an tSleibhe Dhuibh PS NUBelfast West32Naiscoil Bhreandain PS NU Belfast West70Naiscoil Na Fuiseoige PG (Dunmurry)Belfast West0Naiscoil na Mona PS NU Belfast West23Academic year 2022/23SettingParliamentary Constituency1st preferencesNaiscoil Eanna PS NU (Glengormley)Belfast North40Naiscoil Mhic Reachtain PG Belfast North6Naiscoil Bheann Mhadagain PS NU Belfast North43Naiscoil an Droichid PS NU Belfast South29Gaelscoil Na Bhfal PS NU Belfast West39Naiscoil an Lonnain PG Belfast West5Naiscoil an tSleibhe Dhuibh PS NUBelfast West33Naiscoil Bhreandain PS NU Belfast West77Naiscoil Na Fuiseoige PG (Dunmurry)Belfast West35Naiscoil na Mona PS NU Belfast West25Academic year 2021/22SettingParliamentary Constituency1st preferencesNaiscoil Bheann Mhadagain PS NU Belfast North25Naiscoil Eanna PS NU (Glengormley)Belfast North41Naionra Eanna PG (Glengormley)Belfast North0Naiscoil Mhic Reachtain PG Belfast North10Naiscoil an Droichid PS NU Belfast South26Gaelscoil Na Bhfal PS NU Belfast West40Naiscoil an tSleibhe Dhuibh PS NUBelfast West38Naiscoil Bhreandain PS NU Belfast West64Naiscoil Na Fuiseoige PG (Dunmurry)Belfast West34Naiscoil na Mona PS NU Belfast West23Figures for statutory settings may include applications in respect of penultimate pre-school aged children as well as target-aged children.Application information for Irish-medium primary schools is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what communication her Department has had with the Northern Ireland Office in relation to commencing the provision under section 4 of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: Commencement of provisions in the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 is a matter for the Northern Ireland Office. There has been some engagement between officials in my Department and the Northern Ireland Office in relation to the commencement of section 4, with a view to ensuring that the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service and the Lady Chief Justice’s Office are given sufficient notice of commencement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what workforce planning has been carried out in their Department, under its Equality Rights and Identity section, to work on Irish language strategy and commissioners.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is leading on the development of the Irish Language Strategy.TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.TEO officials have taken forward the necessary preparatory work to implement the Act which will be in line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether bilingual signage will be erected as part of the Weaver’s Cross development.
answer_text: The provision of signage at the Weavers Cross development is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many meetings Translink officials have held with groups in relation to bilingual signage at the Weavers Cross development.
answer_text: Details of meetings held by Translink officials with any group would be an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work has taken place to expediate work on the Executive’s Irish language strategy.
answer_text: To date, the strategy co-design process has involved extensive engagement with sectoral organisations, community focus groups, delivery bodies and the public sector, with work progressing firstly through an Expert Advisory Panel and then a Co-Design Group. Both the Panel and the Group were composed of community, voluntary and academic Irish language stakeholders and experts to ensure a broad range of experience. Owing to the cross-cutting nature of the strategy, a Cross-Departmental Working Group is considering the Co-Design Group’s proposed action plan.  It will then be for Executive colleagues to agree next steps. A draft strategy will issue for public consultation following Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work his Department has undertaken to ensure bilingual signage has been erected at (i) Queens University; and (ii) Ulster University, Belfast.
answer_text: Universities are autonomous bodies and their policies, including those relating to signage, are matters for the organisations themselves.However, both Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University fall within the ambit of the functions of the Irish Language Commissioner to enhance and protect the use of the Irish language by public authorities in the provision of their services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration has he given to include Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in his energy strategy, alongside other renewable energy alternatives.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy – The Path to Net Zero Energy, outlines a roadmap to 2030 aiming to deliver a 56% reduction in our energy-related emissions, on the pathway to deliver the 2050 vision of Net Zero carbon emissions, and self-sufficiency in affordable, renewable energy.  A key principle of the Strategy is to replace fossil fuels with renewable energy, ultimately ending the importing of fossil fuels, and utilising renewable sources such as liquid biofuels like HVO is integral to this shift. Aligned to that commitment, my department will soon announce a call for evidence on using renewable liquid biofuels such as HVO and bioLPG as alternatives to fossil fuels for heating. The information gathered will support broader policy work regarding decarbonisation of heat in buildings and industry throughout the region and we will produce a summary of analysis as part of the outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total figure paid out in (i) court fees; (ii) compensation; and (iii) other costs connected to legal cases taken by staff, parents and pupils in the special educational needs sector in the last ten years.
answer_text: This information is not held by the department and has been provided by the Education Authority (EA) in-house legal team, EA Solicitors.Whilst accurate data is not accessible for the entire 10-year period requested, the EA has used the financial year 2015/16 as the baseline year and has provided information to respond to the parts of the question in relation to the following areas:Applications involving the EA before the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST);High Court Judicial Review (JR) applications involving the EA grounded on allegations that the EA has failed to comply with its legal duties in the area of SEN;Employers Liability and Public Liability Claims brought by staff, parents and pupils in Special Schools managed by the EA;Industrial Tribunal claims brought by staff employed by the EA in Special Schools managed by the EA.The EA response to this request is as follows:SENDIST ApplicationsThe EA advises that awards for compensation are not made by SENDIST nor are Court fees incurred by the EA with regard to such applications.SENDIST applications are handled by solicitors employed by the EA Solicitors and on occasion Counsel is instructed.  Since 2015/16, insofar as can reasonably be ascertained, the sum of £76,323.50 plus VAT has been incurred by the EA in respect of fees paid to Counsel in connection with the conduct of SENDIST applications involving the EA. High Court JR Applications Involving EA arising from SEN MattersThe EA advises that it is not the normal practice of the High Court to award compensation in High Court JR matters and, insofar as the EA Solicitors are aware, there are no cases in the last 10 years in which compensation has been awarded in SEN related JR applications.JR applications are handled by the EA in-house legal team, EA Solicitors. Junior and, on occasion, Senior Counsel will be instructed by the EA Solicitors in relation to such matters.Whilst accurate data is not accessible for the entire 10-year period requested, since 2015/16, insofar as can reasonably be ascertained, the sum of £49,371.66 plus VAT has been incurred in Counsel’s fees in dealing with High Court JR applications arising from SEN matters.In a number of SEN related JR applications, the High Court has awarded costs against the EA requiring it to meet the applicant’s legal costs and outlay.  Since 2015/16, insofar as can reasonably be ascertained, the sum of £200,241.76 including VAT has been incurred by the EA in respect of such costs. Public Liability/Employer’s Liability Claims brought by Staff, Parents and Pupils in Special Schools managed by the EAThese claims are handled by the EA on a self-insured basis.Since 2015/16, insofar as can reasonably be ascertained, the EA has paid out:£1,786,590 in respect of compensation/damages; £970,148.44 in respect of plaintiff’s costs (including VAT);£300,263.93 in respect of its own legal costs (excluding VAT). Court fees are included in both plaintiff’s costs and the EA’s costs but

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what percentage of his Department's transport budget has been spent on active travel in (i) 2021-22; (ii) 2022-23; and (iii) 2023-24.
answer_text: The implications of Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 are currently being considered by my Department, including what constitutes the ‘overall transport budgets’ and activities that would fall within active travel.  In the meantime, I would refer the member to AQW 5731 detailing active travel spend until 2022-23. Spend figures are not yet available for the 2023-24 financial year. However, approximately £10.2m of capital and resource spend budget is currently recorded against specific Departmental active travel projects and grants for Councils in the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to appoint more staff to the unit responsible for active travel, to ensure he meets his 10 per cent active travel spend target, as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I am pleased to say that my officials have taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Climate Change Act to increase our future delivery capacity as we strive to meet our ambitions for active travel.	The Active Travel Team reorganised internally in early 2023, with responsibility for Active Travel now under a single Director within the Department. Key posts have been added to the team, including a Deputy Director of Active Travel. A new staff structure has been agreed which doubles the number of Departmental staff in active travel, with work is progressing to fill key posts within that structure. Filling of posts remains subject to budget availability and supply of available personnel at the grades required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Strategic Plan for Greenways 2016 target to have 75 per cent of the primary network delivered by 2026; and (ii) what percentage of the primary network has been delivered.
answer_text: The Strategic Plan for Greenways identified around 1000km of greenway network across the North, including cross border greenways. Delivery of greenways has generally been led by councils with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering a project. Evidence gathered through engagement with councils highlights that a total of 122km of that network is in place. Approximately 117km relates to the primary network, 27% of the target. Acknowledging that progress on delivery of the strategy has been slower than I would like, in 2022 I asked that my Department take a greater role in the delivery of greenways. Officials are currently working closely with councils to better understand their 5-year greenway programme, and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways. Future delivery will be dependent upon availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to provide free public transport to everyone.
answer_text: While I have no plans to provide free public transport to everyone, my Department funds free and discounted travel for those groups of people most at risk of social exclusion, through the Concessionary Fares Scheme. Similar schemes exist across these islands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Public Inquiry into the Dalradian Gold Mine project.
answer_text: Given the contentious nature of this complex project and the widespread concerns which have been raised the decision was taken to call for a Public Local Inquiry and report which independently considers the views of stakeholders, including the local community and other Government Departments, and which will robustly scrutinise the information provided by all interested parties.Once the Inquiry is completed and a report prepared by the Planning Appeals Commission, careful consideration will be given by my Department to its findings before any decision is reached.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of a £15 per hour minimum wage as a means of improving workers’ living standards.
answer_text: I will commission research on how minimum wage powers could be used locally. This research will consider the impact of a higher minimum wage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will allocate at least 10 percent of his Department's transport budget in 2024-25 for active travel, as required by the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The implications of Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 are currently	being considered by my Department but regardless of how the requirement is interpreted it will need significantly more capital investment in active travel in the future in order to meet the requirement. Evidence from elsewhere on these islands tells us that ramping up an ambitious programme of investment of this nature takes years and I am pleased to say that my officials have taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Act to increase the future delivery capacity that would allow us to strive to meet our ambitions for active travel.		As you are also aware, the budgets for next year have not yet been set and we are continuing to work closely with the Department of Finance. It will be for the Executive to decide final budget allocations and I will consider funding for active travel in the context of my Department’s overall budget, taking into consideration factors such as other competing priorities, legislative requirements and deliver capacity. 		I am enthusiastic about the positive change that investment in active travel would make to the lives of people who want to have greater choice in how they travel, particularly for shorter everyday journeys, or in combination with using public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any consideration has been given to the design and delivery of the A5 with regards to future rail development from the North West to Portadown, particularly how the two infrastructure developments can intersect.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) was launched in April 2021 by Minister Mallon and the Minister of Transport for the Irish Government, Eamon Ryan T.D. The Review considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, including to the Northwest, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.The public consultation phase of the Review concluded on 29 September 2023 and consideration of responses is ongoing. Once the Review is finalised it will be presented to the governments of both jurisdictions for approval. Once published, further work will be needed to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions.The AISRR project team was cognisant of the A5 flagship project when developing the AISRR. Specific details on how the A5 project and a potential rail line between Portadown and Derry would interact with each other will be developed should a feasibility study for rail be commissioned along the corridor. In the interim, the A5WTC fundamentally does not preclude the processes of feasibility and conceptual design of the rail corridors and routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions have taken place in relation to the use of the Plantir product Foundary for data analysis.
answer_text: The engagement of Palantir and the use of their product Foundary by NHS England does not apply to information held in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, as part of the development of Casement Park, whether it is proposed to allow standing spectators at Euro 2028 finals matches.
answer_text: UEFA require all Tournament venues to be fully seated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the redevelopment of Casement Park, whether the GAA has planning permission for an all-seater stadium with capacity of more than 30,000.
answer_text: Planning permission is in place for the redevelopment of Casement Park with a stadium capacity of 34,578. This is broken down as: 26,403 seated and 8,175 standing.Engagement with Department for Infrastructure and Belfast City council, to explore the influence of the EURO 2028 requirements on the existing Casement Park Planning Permission, is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the current progress of the Future Planning Model; (ii) to detail the cost so far; and (iii) to detail the projected costs for implementing the Integrated Care System NI.
answer_text: A programme of work to develop the future planning model for health and social care is being taken forward through the development and implementation of the Integrated Care System for Northern Ireland (ICS NI). Work has progressed on defining and testing aspects of the model in preparation of supporting shadow roll-out over the coming months which will enable refinement of the model and the regulations pertaining to Area Integrated Partnership Boards.Further information is available online via https://online.hscni.net/our-work/integrated-care-system-ni/.Following the initial development work taken forward in 2021, the Future Planning Model Project was formally established on 1 April 2022 and then became the Integrated Care System Programme following a strategic review in July 2022. Costs from 1 April 2022 to 31 Jan 2024 are £2,724,000* (22/23 £1,499,000 and 23/24 £1,225,000).Forecasted implementation costs from 1 Feb 2024 to 30 September 2024 total £803,000* (23/24 £171,000 and 24/25 £632,000).  *includes programme and staff costs for period detailed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the redevelopment of Casement Park, (i) how much has been paid in consultancy fees; and (ii) to whom these consultancy fees have been paid.
answer_text: A total of £5,526,232 has been paid in consultancy fees for the Integrated Consultancy Team and has been paid to architects, engineers, cost consultants etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has raised the issue of curtailing strip searches of juveniles and young people by the PSNI with the Chief Constable.
answer_text: The use of strip searches of children and young people by police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  I am committed to respecting both the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.  The NIPB has a statutory duty to monitor the performance of the PSNI and we have well established arrangements for policing oversight set out in legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for their assessment on concerns raised by human rights organisations about the detention of asylum seekers at Larne House; and (ii) whether they have had any engagement with the UK government in relation to this issue.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland Act 1998, matters of nationality and immigration are excepted matters. Consequently, responsibility for Larne House including assessing or addressing concerns relating to its function and operation remains the responsibility of the Home Office. The Executive Office has no locus in this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to address the shortfall in special school places in the context of growing demand.
answer_text: The strategic direction of Special Education area planning is set out in the Education Authority’s (EA) Special Education Provision Strategic Area Plan 2022-27.  The associated Operational Plan 1: 2022-2024 sets out the proposed changes to the current Special School estate and Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS).  Operational Plan 2: 2024-26 is currently being developed.Development Proposals (DPs) have been approved to increase the number of places at Harberton School (North) and to establish a new special school in the Belfast City Council area with an additional 275 places.  The EA is also working with St Gerard’s School & Support Services to identify how additional capacity can be provided in the short- and medium-term.The EA plans to bring forward further proposals to increase the number of special school places for consultation before June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the groups he (i) has met; and (ii) intends to meet as part of his consultation on onshore petroleum licensing.
answer_text: I have not met any groups about the consultation on onshore petroleum licensing. The consultation involves written submissions rather than Ministerial meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plan to deal with insufficient staffing levels in the special educational needs sector.
answer_text: It is critical that we attract and retain staff to take forward roles supporting children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) and this is a key matter for consideration as part of the End to End Review of SEN which is currently underway within the department. The issue of workforce planning is to be considered as part of this; however, I recognise that recruitment and retention issues among teachers and support staff across the system, including those in the SEN sector, are tied to the outcome of any proposed pay and teacher workload reviews.  This is not without its challenges, particularly in relation to any additional financial commitment that will be required from the Executive. In conjunction with our Higher Education Institutes (HEIs), my officials are also considering the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) and Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) offers to increase the quality, consistency and access arrangements of SEN-focussed professional learning for the teaching profession.  This will include increasing the opportunities available to both teachers and the wider educational workforce to meaningfully participate in ongoing professional learning and will help optimise the support being made available to pupils, to ensure that EVERY CHILD is happy, learning and succeeding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the First Minister's statement regarding water charges, for an update on his Department's consultation on water and sewerage charges.
answer_text: This public consultation is ongoing having launched on the 7 December 2023 for a 14 week period. The consultation will close on 13 March 2024 and a report summarising the consultation responses will be compiled and presented to me for consideration. As previously stated I will not be introducing Domestic Water Charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether he intends to proceed with the removal of free travel for people aged over 60 years, as part of the concessionary fare scheme.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme closed on 24th August 2023 and included an option which would raise the age of eligibility of the SmartPass to age 65 or State Pension Age.The Department received over 20,000 responses to the consultation. These responses have been invaluable in understanding the benefits of the Scheme not only for individuals, but for our society and economy as a whole, and I thank everyone who took the time to respond or get involved.In the coming weeks I will be considering the responses the Department has received to the consultation and while I will do all I can to protect the concessions which matter most to people, any decisions on whether to keep free travel for those aged 60-64 must be based on the funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many staff in his Department have worked on the consultation on water and sewerage charges.
answer_text: Two officials worked full time on the water and sewerage consultation and one official worked on it as part of their overall responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to extend the concessionary fares scheme to other citizens.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme closed on 24th August 2023, with the Department receiving over 20,000 responses. The consultation included two options which would extend the Concessionary Fares Scheme to other citizens: Extend the qualifying criteria for a Half Fare SmartPass in line with other jurisdictions; and Free transport for those receiving asylum support and victims of human trafficking.In the coming weeks I will be considering the responses the Department has received to the consultation and, while I fully recognise the potential benefits of the inclusion of both of these Options under the Scheme, any decision to extend the Scheme must be affordable within the budget made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many cases of rent controls have been implemented in the last 5 years; and (ii) the average percentage of reductions.
answer_text: The number of cases of rent controls implemented in the last 5 years is 78. 	I am unable to answer the question. This is because the assessment of the rent applicable in a case of rent control is not influenced by or based on a reduction of the original rental amount. The appropriate rent is instead determined through the allocation of points, which take account of the particular characteristics of the property, such as, type and age, number of rooms and whether it has central heating. Each point has a value calculated with reference to Housing Executive rents and local reference rent for similar dwellings let under similar tenancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps he will take to bring private healthcare under the auspices of public health.
answer_text: Ensuring effective health and social care, addressing the demand/capacity gap, and reducing waiting times are the most pressing challenges facing the Health and Social Care Sector and the Northern Ireland Executive.To achieve the maximum impact, the system relies on use of the independent sector to deliver additional waiting list activity.  With the scale of the backlog of patients waiting for treatment, it is expected that this relationship with independent sector providers will be required for some time.  Given the significant pressure on our health and social care system, the growing demand/capacity gap means that over recent years our Waiting List activity has become increasingly skewed towards dealing with Red Flag (suspect Cancer) and time critical patients and this has impacted our ability to significantly address both breaches of Ministerial access targets and long waiting patients.£70m has been invested non-recurrently for Waiting List Initiatives (both independent sector and in-house) this year to help protect provision for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments. The Elective Care Framework is clear that, in addition to multi-year funding to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, sustained recurrent investment is required to address the central issue of demand outstripping the current capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to expand the rail network.
answer_text: The Department is in the process of finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR). The Review considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, including to the Northwest, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.The draft Review report, which was released to public consultation from 25 July to 29 September 2023, makes 30 recommendations for the rail network, five of which, if implemented, would expand the rail network in the north, namely:Reinstatement of the Lisburn-Antrim line with a station at Belfast International Airport.A new dual-tracked electrified line from Belfast to Newry via Hillsborough, Dromore and Banbridge, with connections to the Lisburn-Antrim line and towards Portadown.A new dual-tracked electrified line between Portadown and Derry via Dungannon, Omagh and Strabane. A new single-track line between Derry and Letterkenny.A new single-track line between Portadown and Mullingar via Armagh.Consideration of responses to the public consultation is ongoing. Once the Review is finalised it will be presented to the governments of both jurisdictions for approval. Once published, further work will be needed to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the interventions expanding the rail network listed above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to bring forward legislation to prevent retaliatory evictions.
answer_text: It is already an offence for a landlord to unlawfully evict a tenant.  This includes harassing the tenant or persistently withholding services which are required by the tenant in a retaliatory manner.  A landlord must also give the correct notice to quit depending on the length of tenancy.   My Department is currently progressing a programme of Private Rented Sector Reform, which includes increasing security of tenure for tenants through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods.In parallel, my Department is considering what Phase 2 of the reform programme should contain, with a focus on what measures will have the most impact in improving the safety, security and standards of the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to bring forward similar legislation to Awaab’s law.
answer_text: At present, the Executive’s legislative programme has not been determined. My Department is aware of the consultation on Awaab’s law and officials are monitoring developments to consider the most appropriate approach here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average fine amount issued to landlords for failure to provide adequate housing in the last five years.
answer_text: Enforcement of standards in the Private Rented Sector is the statutory responsibility of local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures are in place to ensure housing providers present dehumidifiers to people faced with damp or mould.
answer_text: There are no measures in place to ensure housing providers present dehumidifiers to tenants faced with damp and mould. It is an operational matter for social housing providers to determine the causes of damp and mould within their housing stock and to decide on appropriate methods of dealing with these. If private tenants are experiencing damp or associated mould issues, they should contact their landlord in the first instance to try and resolve the issue.  If the issue is not resolved, they should contact their local council who have responsibility for enforcement in the private rented sector. This includes the power to inspect the property, issue certificates of unfitness and disrepair and require the landlord to carry out relevant works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many private landlords served notice in the Belfast area over damp and mould, broken down by Assembly constituency in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not have this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any data collected by his Department of the impact on the mental health of women who do not have access to NHS abortion services.
answer_text: Abortion Services are available in all 5 Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland.  As abortion services are available to NI women and girls the Department of Health does not consider it a requirement to collect data on the impact of women who do not have access to NHS abortion services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to commission abortion services in Health and Social Care Trusts where they are not currently available.
answer_text: Abortion Services are available in all 5 Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered an enhanced rate for nursing bank staff as part of his Reducing Expenditure on Agency Staffing in HSC plans.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ locum staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years I am concerned of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.  Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. Alongside this work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to, a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system.Data for all professional groups shows that in 22/23 the total agency spend was £394m – of which £196m related to off-framework agency/locum spend.   Current estimates, based on grossing up April to December figures to full year estimates on a straight line basis indicate projected spend for 23/24 to be in the region of £373m with total off-framework spend projected to be in the region of £110m.  On this basis, the overall net reduction on agency spend is projected, on a straight line basis, to be in the region of £20m.  It should be noted these figures are estimates and should be treated with extreme caution at this time.  Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority.Agency expenditure by Health and Social Care Trust, for the last five financial years, is given below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what progress his Department has made in taking forward the recommendations of the United Nations' decision VII/8s, Section 4; and (ii) for his assessment on whether the UN decision will be complied with by the deadline of October 2024.
answer_text: I am awaiting further legal advice and briefing from officials in relation to this matter, after which I will consider what action may be taken to address the recommendations of the Aarhus Convention Compliance Committee.
