question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Sands Listening Project report entitled Learning From The Experiences Of Black And Asian Bereaved Parents, what long-term, funded plans there are to eliminate inequalities in pregnancy loss and baby deaths.
answer_text: Action to address health inequalities remains a priority for my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) with more work planned to focus specifically on social complexity. The Stillbirth and Neonatal Charity (SANDS) project helps us understand how systemic issues, along with poor practice and discrimination from some healthcare professionals, may lead to less safe care, with poor outcomes for some Black and Asian families. Evidence tells us that Black and Asian babies are more likely to be stillborn or die as newborns compared to white babies. The Sands Listening Project report entitled Learning From The Experiences Of Black And Asian Bereaved Parents references the work of the MBRRACE-UK programme (Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK) confidential programme. My Department works closely with national researchers within the MBRRACE-UK which is focused on improving the health and outcomes for mothers and newborns. This work has a specific focus on health inequalities and works to support women with complex needs and those of a higher disadvantage.  This will remain a priority area going forward. The Public Health Agency are currently working to update the seven regionally agreed Care Pathways for staff caring for women and their families who have experienced a pregnancy loss. There is also a planned partnership with the Stillbirth and Neonatal Death charity (or “SANDS”) to further develop the care pathways so they align with the national bereavement care pathways, this will include the development of regional training and awareness sessions for all staff who work within maternity & neonatal services. This project is planned for 2 years, starting in 2024.Following a coronial case in a freestanding Midwife Led Unit in Northern Ireland, Professor Mary Renfrew was tasked with undertaking a comprehensive and independent review to inform a consistent approach to the provision of midwifery services throughout Northern Ireland (NI).  The independent report will draw on evidence reviews, data analysis and on consultation and engagement with users and advocates of maternity services and with the professionals who provide and lead the services, culminating in the development of recommendations for policy, practice, education, and research.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Sands Listening Project report entitled Learning From The Experiences Of Black And Asian Bereaved Parents, what plans his Department has for funded research programmes to inform the development of effective interventions to address health inequalities and reduce infant deaths.
answer_text: There is currently no budget associated with research and development for a programme to inform effective interventions to address health inequalities and reduce infant deaths.  Action to address health inequalities remains a priority for my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) with more work planned to focus specifically on social complexity. The Stillbirth and Neonatal Charity (SANDS) project helps us understand how systemic issues, along with poor practice and discrimination from some healthcare professionals, may lead to less safe care, with poor outcomes for some Black and Asian families. Evidence tells us that Black and Asian babies are more likely to be stillborn or die as newborns compared to white babies. The Sands Listening Project report entitled Learning From The Experiences Of Black And Asian Bereaved Parents references the work of the MBRRACE-UK programme (Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK) confidential programme. My Department works closely with national researchers within the MBRRACE-UK which is focused on improving the health and outcomes for mothers and newborns. This work has a specific focus on health inequalities and works to support women with complex needs and those of a higher disadvantage.  This will remain a priority area going forward. My Department also work closely with Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) and the Royal College of Midwives (RCM) in national research. The School of Nursing and Midwifery at Queens and produces internationally excellent research that informs healthcare policy, underpins nursing education and practice, and makes improvements for patients and their families. They have recently been involved in the work of Prof Mary Renfrew.  As part of the Public Health Agency Research and Development Division and in conjunction with UK Research and Innovation, the University of Ulster has recently received funding of £1.9m to further develop a community consortium to tackle health disparity for people living with mental illness and to help address health and social care inequalities in Northern Ireland.   This may provide further evidence for perinatal mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to establish an Overdose Prevention Site to address rising need in Belfast city centre.
answer_text: Currently Overdose Prevention Centres – also known as “Drug Consumption Rooms” or “Safer Injecting Facilities” – are not permitted under the UK-wide Misuse of Drugs Act 1971. The issue of criminal sanctions for those found in possession of drugs is an issue that is frequently raised with this type of facility. This is also governed by the Misuse of Drugs Act and remains a reserved matter. My Department leads on the Northern Ireland Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery which recognises the need to target those most at risk, including those who are homeless, people who inject drugs and those who are in contact with the justice system. This strategy aims to directly reduce the harm for these groups through actions that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures for people who use alcohol and other drugs. There are no proposals in the strategy on “Overdose Prevention Facilities” at this time. However, we will continue to monitor evidence on the effectiveness of different interventions, such as the forthcoming pilot overdose prevention facilities in Glasgow and Dublin, and will give due consideration to new interventions as appropriate within the wider legislative framework.In the meantime, it is important to note that there is currently a full range of services available for those who require help and support with substance use issues, and for their families. A directory of services available in each Trust area across Northern Ireland is available online at: https://drugsandalcoholni.info/self-help-resources/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps will be taken to ensure that maternity services have appropriate staff capacity, skills and resources required to assess and care effectively for women and birthing people.
answer_text: Last year my Department established a Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group to receive assurance that maternity and neonatal services are safe and appropriate for the population of Northern Ireland. As part of this broad programme of work, the Chief Nursing Officer commissioned an independent report on maternity services in Northern Ireland. Professor Mary Renfrew, an external midwifery expert, was appointed to conduct a comprehensive review of maternity services and produce a report on Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland.The Department expects to receive Professor Renfrew’s report shortly and will carefully consider its recommendations, which will help inform a consistent approach to the provision of maternity services in Northern Ireland. This work will be fundamental to improving maternity services, ensuring they have the staff capacity, skills and resources required to assess and care for women and birthing people effectively, and will help inform the future strategic direction for maternity and neonatal services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether a Construction Environmental Management Plan for Casement Park has been completed; and (ii) whether his Department has received a copy of it.
answer_text: UCGAA has commissioned and contracted the works currently on site at Casement Park. Therefore, in this case, the production of a Construction Environmental Management Plan (CEMP) is the responsibility of UCGAA and does not require submission to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to take vacant homes into public ownership.
answer_text: The Department consulted on a new Housing Supply Strategy in 2021/22. It is intended that this Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and adaptive reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently reviewing this Strategy and considering options for progressing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to ban no-fault evictions.
answer_text: Phase one of my Department’s programme of reform of the Private Rented Sector includes work to improve security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods via the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022.  As also specified in the Act, regulations on exceptions must be in place before these longer notice periods are commenced. My officials have taken on board the views of stakeholders and are developing a robust Equality Impact Assessment to inform the drafting of these regulations, with consultation planned for the coming months.My consideration of what Phase 2 of this reform programme should encompass will be focused on what further measures will have the most impact in improving the security, standards, and safety of the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the statutory powers of a Maintenance Officer in the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The duties of Maintenance Officers is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how much the Northern Ireland Civil Service has spent on agency staff in the last five years; and (ii) for a breakdown of this expenditure.
answer_text: Information on the annual cost of hiring recruitment agency workers across the Northern Ireland Civil Service is not held centrally by DoF. This information may be found through published Departmental Annual Report and Accounts or by contacting individual Departments. The table below provides DoF expenditure in relation to temporary workers, contract staff and recruitment agency workers. Although the majority of the expenditure will relate to recruitment agency workers, the total figure may include the recruitment of temporary workers and fixed term contract staff costs across DoF:YearCost (£'000) 1, 22018/19£5,8752019/20£6,8752020/21£6,7152021/22£7,1552022/23£6,5431 Costs include recruitment agency workers in the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency2 Costs may include recruitment agency workers, temporary workers and fixed term contract staff costs across DoF

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to financially support unpaid carers.
answer_text: I recognise the important role unpaid carers play within the wider community as well as the vital support they provide to those who they are caring for.Carer’s Allowance is the main benefit in the social security system targeted towards unpaid carers – its primary purpose is to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care for a severely disabled person.  It was never intended to be a wage for caring or to replace lost or forgone earnings in their entirety.  As part of the annual benefits up-rating exercise the weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance will increase by 6.7% to £81.90 from April 2024.  For claimants who are able to combine their caring responsibilities with some paid employment the earnings limit in Carer’s Allowance will also increase to £151.00 per week.Depending on individual circumstances carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and their associated carer premiums/elements.  The main rates in these benefits will also increase by 6.7% from April 2024.In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.I have also noted the recommendations relating to additional support for carers that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022. The recommendations made in that report and any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how much financial support he will provide for capital build projects in the Irish medium sector.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that all children in schools across all sectors in Northern Ireland are treated equitably.  All children have the right to be educated in schools which are comfortable and safe, are of good quality and are properly designed and resourced to support their learning.Over the last ten years my Department has invested over £51m in capital projects in the Irish medium sector.There are a number of projects either underway or in planning across both the minor and major capital works programmes, this includes five new builds for Irish-medium primary schools with estimated costs in excess of £39m.  On the minor works programme, significant works, with costs in excess of £3m, are underway in Gaelcolaiste Dhoire and business cases are currently going through the approval process for substantial works in four Irish-medium primary schools with estimated costs of £11.5m. My Department will however not be able to invest across all sectors with the current levels of capital funding.  We are at risk of not delivering on the most basic requirements to keep schools open, children safe and to provide places for our most vulnerable learners. As referred to in my statement to the Assembly on capital, substantial additional investment is urgently required, and I will be making the case for sustained capital investment in our education infrastructure and bringing forward a bid to the Executive for an absolute minimum of an additional £100 million of capital funding for education in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department’s assessment on the introduction of prescription charges.
answer_text: The revenue that could be generated by the re-introduction of prescription charges to Northern Ireland would be highly dependent on the model and level at which any charges are set. There would also be a requirement for significant upfront investment to support the development and administration of any new charging regime fully.In addition, it is likely that with any charging regime for prescriptions, there would also be the requirement to introduce certain exemption categories on the basis of low income, age and in relation to those with certain medical conditions in order to alleviate the possible destitution or economic hardships that could result from the reintroduction of prescription charges.My Department’s recent assessment on the possibility of having similar charging arrangements and exemptions to that which are currently in place in England would suggest we would likely see 95% of all prescription items dispensed under any prescription charging regime in Northern Ireland either dispensed free of charge or at a reduced rate to patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will bring forward legislation to enhance workers’ rights.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether GP Federations have met their objectives.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail GP salaries, broken down by GP Federation area.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations. They are independent providers of services and are not employees, partners or agents of the Department. GP practices operate as independent, small businesses with responsibility for employing their own staff including doctors, nurses, receptionists and healthcare assistants.The GP Earnings and Expenses Estimates, published by NHS Digital, provides information on earnings and expenses for GPs working in the UK as either a contractor or salaried GP. GPs who work solely as locums are not included in the report.The estimates are based on a sample of anonymised tax data from HM Revenue and Customs’ Self Assessment tax database.  The most recent GP Earnings and Expenses Estimates report, for 2021/22, was published in August 2023, and is available at the link below:GP Earnings and Expenses Estimates, 2021/22 - NHS Digital

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many case reviews of deceased patients of former doctor Michael Watt have been carried out since 2018; and (ii) what plans he has to address review delays.
answer_text: Two groups of deceased patients’ records which were selected for expert review during Phase Two of the RQIA Deceased Patients Review (DPR), in accordance with the RQIA Proposal to the Department of Health including:Group 1: 29 deceased patients whose family members approached the RQIA with concerns about their care since the Department of Health’s announcement of the Review in May 2018.Group 2: 15 patients whom Belfast Trust identified as part of Cohort 1 of the neurology patient recall in 2018, however, who unfortunately died before they had either the opportunity to take part or before their re-assessment was completed.The RQIA published the overarching Royal College of Physicians (RCP) Reports from Phase Two on 29 November 2022, which are available on the RQIA website - https://www.rqia.org.uk/RQIA/files/2f/2f9d52d7-9ea5-43c4-9b25-9b1998f8ec09.pdf From the outset it was envisaged that the DPR would be undertaken with a phased approach. This approach aims to ensure that the appropriate learning is captured, allowing families to come forward if they wish, and ensuring that any difficulties and challenges arising are given due regard. My Department remains mindful of the delicate nature of this work and the need for a sensitive and ethical approach.My Department and the RQIA are continuing to work together to consider all matters to determine the next steps for the DPR, following the outcome of the engagement with the families who have come forward to date. The pace and extent of the DPR next steps will take all material factors into consideration including: the learnings from the work completed to date (including family expectations and experiences, ethical considerations and systems learning); the time requirements for future work; and the resource requirements (including staff and budget requirements)..

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how much is currently in the Consolidated Fund; and (ii) the process for how departments can draw down from it.
answer_text: ANSWEROn the 15 February the balance in the Consolidated Fund or NICF was £373,105,755.58.  It is normal practice for the NICF to start and end the year with a balance of around £1m.   At any one time it may it hold significant funds or be in an overdraft position.  Cash in the NICF does not mean that there are surplus funds to spend.  The NICF is part of the pool of accounts managed by my department and the funds in the NICF will be offsetting expenditure elsewhere, primarily in the Paymaster General Account.  The main access to funding from the NICF is via the Supply process.  The relevant Budget Act for a particular year authorises the maximum amount of cash that can be paid to each department by the NICF to fund their activities. Departments can access this funding on a daily basis by submitting a funding request to Government Accounts Branch in my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether levels of Per and polyfluoroalkyl substances and forever chemicals are (i) being tested for; (ii) present in; and (iii) being removed from the water abstracted at Carmoney Water Treatment Works, Derry.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total amount paid to Epic as part of the Encompass programme.
answer_text: The value of the contract awarded to Epic, £273.426M, was publicly advertised on OJEU in 2020.  To date, committed spend equates to £91.4m

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what access Epic has to personal records through the Encompass programme; and (ii) whether patients can opt out of their data being shared.
answer_text: (i) EPIC provides technical support to ensure the encompass solution is working effectively. Should access to personal data be necessary, to resolve an issue, HSC data protection, confidentiality and cyber security policies and protocols are followed.  A full audit trail of access to information by Epic staff is maintained.(ii) Information has been held in digital medical records for many years, Clinicians require this information to provide all patients and service users with safe and appropriate care. In very rare circumstances restrictions on access to selected parts of a record have been implemented. Information held on HSC computer systems is used for direct patient care only and any other use, such as research and development, requires additional patient consent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail bonus payments for senior staff in (i) his Department; (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (iii) the Business Services Organisation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) During the last five financial years (2018/19 – 2022/23) two senior civil servants within the Department of Health each received a bonus of £500 gross under the previous NICS Bonus Scheme. One was paid on 20/12/2018 and the other was paid on 26/02/2019.(ii) (iii) The only bonus payment made to HSC senior executives in the last five financial years (2018/19 – 2022/23) the special recognition payment, which was paid to all HSC staff on a pro rata basis up to a maximum of £500 net.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total amount provided by (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts to GP Federations, since their inception.
answer_text: Since the inception of GP Federations to the end of January 2024, the total amount of payments made to the Federations are as follows:£187m paid by The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will set a deterrent against breaches of planning conditions governing commercial sand extraction at Lough Neagh through sanctions or prosecutions.
answer_text: Planning permission for commercial sand extraction on Lough Neagh was granted permission in 2021 subject to several conditions. These conditions are monitored by my department. Within the Planning Act (NI) 2011 there are provisions that allow my department to act against a breach of planning control which includes a breach of a planning condition where it is considered expedient to do so. Whilst a breach of planning control is not an offence, failure to comply with subsequent formal enforcement action is an offence and can result in an unlimited fine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work to strengthen powers of Environmental Health Officers to deal with damp and mould issues in (i) private rented sector: and (ii) social housing.
answer_text: Environmental Health Officers already have powers to deal with damp and associated mould issues that are prejudicial to health through enforcing the statutory minimum fitness standard for housing, as set out in Article 46 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.  This standard is applicable to all tenures. In respect of the private rented sector, my Department is currently progressing a programme of reform which includes the development of regulations that will permit Environmental Health Officers to enforce minimum standards to improve fire and electrical safety for tenants. My consideration of what Phase 2 of this reform programme should encompass will be focused on what further measures will have the most impact in improving the security, standards, and safety of the private rented sector.The housing fitness standard is one of the four pillars of the Decent Homes Standard (DHS) which applies to social housing. My Department is initiating a review of the current DHS which may further consider issues around damp and mould.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any (i) engagement; and (ii) planned engagement they have had with representatives of the Israeli government.
answer_text: We have not had engagement and have no planned engagements with representatives of the Israeli government in our role as FM and dFM.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he will take to prevent refugees and people with secure status being evicted from their homes by Mears Housing and the Home Office.
answer_text: The Management of accommodation for asylum seekers by Mears is an excepted matter which is the responsibility of the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the revenue that would be raised through the introduction of domestic water charges; and (ii) the cost to administer domestic water charges on an annual basis.
answer_text: Section 4.4 of the public consultation directed by the British Secretary of State for Northern on water and sewerage charges considers the potential revenue that could be generated from the introduction of domestic water charges. It estimates that if a charge was introduced and set at a level which matches the cost of providing water and sewerage services to each household, with no requirement to provide Government subsidy, £171.6 million resource and £135 million capital from the Executive’s budget could be available for reallocation to deliver other public services / fund public infrastructure.  It also states that the potential level of revenue generated from any introduction of water and sewerage services would be dependent on a number of policy and implementation decisions. These would include policy decisions on protection of low-income households / vulnerable groups, phasing in charges and the period over which this would happen, the charging policy that would be adopted, the mechanism for billing, collection and enforcement and the associated timeline for implementation. An estimate of the potential annual cost to administer domestic water charges could not be provided without significant policy development.I will not be introducing domestic water charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 is a material planning consideration for (i) his Department; and (ii) councils when determining major planning applications that will become net emitters of greenhouse gases.
answer_text: I recognise that the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 represents a decisive change in the North of Ireland’s approach to the climate emergency, requiring a step change in all our sectors’ activities to achieve the ambitious targets within it. As Minister for Infrastructure I am committed in seeking to ensure the two-tier planning system plays a positive role in responding to the requirements of the Act. Development Management Practice Note 16 The Determination of Planning Applications (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk) sets out the fundamental legislative requirements and powers prescribed by the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and relevant subordinate legislation in relation to the determination of planning applications by the Department and councils. You will note that whilst there is no legal definition for material considerations, case law has determined that there are two main tests and ultimately the relevance of and weight to be given to all material planning considerations is a matter for the decision maker on a case-by-case basis (paragraphs 4.12 - 4.18 refer). The Department will continue to consider the extent to which something, including climate change, is material to a planning decision in light of current case law. It will be a matter for councils, as planning authorities to consider what the relevant material considerations are in determining planning applications for which they are responsible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will expedite the roll-out and funding for the Farming with Nature scheme.
answer_text: Within my Department, work is on-going on a new Farm Support and Development Programme, including a Farming with Nature Package, in co-design with stakeholder organisations. I am considering the overall content and progress of this Programme and how it addresses my priorities for the sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward proposals for a new agriculture act that prioritises public money for public goods.
answer_text: I propose introducing a new Agriculture Bill later in the mandate.  This Bill will underpin the future agricultural policy agenda of my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will bring forward proposals for a nature restoration bill, which would establish targets in law for nature’s restoration.
answer_text: The draft Nature Recovery Strategy contains objectives designed to halt the loss of biodiversity that may require new legislation. However, there are no specific targets in the strategy to take forward a Nature Restoration Bill.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commit to the introduction of an independent environmental protection agency.
answer_text: My immediate priority, reflecting the statutory obligation placed on the Department, is to secure Executive approval for a draft Environmental Improvement Plan. We also need to have the optimal structures and processes in place to enable us to achieve positive environmental outcomes.I have therefore requested that officials commence an environmental governance review, including consideration of the benefits that could accrue from setting up an Independent Environmental Protection Agency, a commitment contained within New Decade New Approach.This is particularly important in light of the altered situation post EU exit and how an Independent Environmental Protection Agency would relate to the changed regulatory landscape including extension of the Office for Environmental Protection’s remit to Northern Ireland in February 2022 and the potential role of a Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of education workers who have not had an Access NI check.
answer_text: Staff working in schools are employed by individual employing authorities and not by the Department of Education.  All education workers employed in a statutory education setting in a regulated activity post are required to complete an Access NI check.  In the case of a short-term temporary hire or emergency cover appointment, it may not be possible to finalise the Access NI check prior to engagement.  In such situations, a risk assessment must be completed, and all necessary steps must be taken to ensure the safeguarding of children and young people.  Given this is an ongoing process within a number of employing authorities, it is not possible to confirm the number of education workers who at a point in time do not have a completed Access NI check.  However, there are processes in place to follow-up and confirm that checks that were not able to be completed prior to engagement have subsequently been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many education workers are employed using Temporary Engagement forms.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that during the 2022/2023 Academic Year there were approximately 25,000 employees engaged using Temporary Engagement Forms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many education workers are employed as (i) general assistants; and (ii) classroom assistants.
answer_text: General Assistants and Classroom Assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA) or directly by schools in the Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated sectors.  The number of individuals in these roles is contained in the table below. General AssistantClassroom AssistantEducation Authority12,59918,253Grant Maintained Integrated2 340765Voluntary Grammar2 348791Total2,68719,809Notes: The EA data includes all active staff head count at 12 February 2024, including permanent and temporary staff.2 VG/GMI figures are based on reference week in the autumn term of 2022/23, includes permanent and temporary staff. Source Annual VG and GMI Support Staff Survey3 Two VG schools and six GMI schools did not respond to the survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on pay negotiations with trade unions.
answer_text: Teachers’ pay is negotiated through the forum of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises the employers, sectoral bodies, DE and the five recognised teachers’ unions in Northern Ireland. TNC engagement has been ongoing to address the pay concerns for teachers and now that the necessary funding is available, formal negotiations have commenced.Support StaffThe vast majority of support staff in Northern Ireland are employed on National Joint Council (NJC) terms and conditions. NJC pay awards are negotiated nationally and the 2023-24 award was agreed on 1 November 2023. This award increased each pay point on the NJC scale by £1,925, or a minimum of 3.88%, and has been implemented for all eligible staff.In addition, a pay and grading review business case has been submitted by the Education Authority to address issues with the current grading structure for support staff.  The business case is currently being reviewed by Department of Education and Department of Finance officials.  I am committed to progressing the business case and will need significant additional funding from the Executive in order to implement its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of assaults on school staff in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Assaults on school staff are not reported to DE as DE is not the employer of school-based staff.  My officials asked Education Authority and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), for information pertaining to their school-based staff.The number of reported assaults on school staff in the last 12 months is as follows:Teaching staff - Number of reported assaults on teaching staff is 517.Support staff - Number of reported assaults on support staff is 1235.The figures provided are for both controlled and catholic maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is any additional support available to dentists, following the recent announcement of additional funding in England.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent announcements regarding investment in General Dental Services in England and I will be discussing these and reform progress in Wales and Scotland with officials as soon as possible. To ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients, officials have been developing investment proposals for General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the environmental impact of missed emissions targets set out in legislation.
answer_text: It is vital that Northern Ireland achieves the statutory emissions reduction targets  set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (‘the Act’). The first target under the Act is for 2030 so to date no emissions reduction targets have been missed. An important step towards meeting our targets will be ensuring that emissions do not exceed carbon budgets and I will shortly be bringing forward legislation to set Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets, as required by the Act. There is a significant body of evidence, including at a global level from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change and, at a UK level, from bodies such as the Met Office Hadley Centre, on the potential negative impacts on the environment and our climate from failing to reduce emissions in line with current net zero targets. My priority and focus is on working closely with my Executive colleagues to deliver effective and joined up climate action to ensure that our targets are met.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his plans to reduce the cost of rent for people in the private rented sector.
answer_text: The powers to control rents set out in Section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022, adding 5C to the 2006 Order, expired on 28 October 2023. I therefore currently have no plans to introduce new rent controls in the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what solutions have been identified and what steps will be taken to address pollution in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: A cross-departmental Water Quality Steering Group is finalising a draft report with evidenced-based recommendations to tackle the problem of blue green algal blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality. I will bring this report to the Executive shortly and the Assembly will be updated as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's consultation on its 2030 and 2040 Emissions Reduction Targets and First Three Carbon Budgets.
answer_text: The public consultation on the proposed first three carbon budgets and the 2030 and 2040 emissions reduction targets was launched on 21 June 2023 and ran for 16 weeks, concluding on 11 October 2023. A full analysis of the consultation responses has been completed and I plan to publish a draft summary report within the next month. The consultation responses showed strong support for following the UK Climate Change Committee’s advice around Northern Ireland’s path to net zero, including in respect of its recommendations on Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets and the 2030 and 2040 emissions reduction targets.I will consider the findings from the consultation alongside other relevant information and will engage with my Executive colleagues to seek their approval on appropriate carbon budgets, and 2030 and 2040 emissions reduction targets for Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to address the levels of polluting phosphorus produced by farms.
answer_text: My Department has a number of measures already in place including regulatory controls. The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations limit the amount of chemical phosphorus fertiliser and livestock manures with a high phosphorus content that can be applied to agricultural land. The NAP Regulations require that soil analysis is undertaken and a fertilisation plan completed to ensure that these fertilisers and manures are applied in quantities that meet, but do not exceed, crop requirements for phosphorus. This is to prevent excess application of nutrients and reduce the risk of pollution. The Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) Regulations require large poultry and pig farms to meet a range of environmental requirements. These include completing a nutrient management plan which must demonstrate that the land on which the manure is spread has sufficient crop requirement to utilise the phosphorus contained in the manure.My Department is also taking forward measures to improve the efficient use of nutrients and export of excess phosphorus. These include the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, which provides farmers, across Northern Ireland, with information and training to help them optimise the use of nutrients, such as phosphorus, to reduce excess application and run-off to watercourses.  Another measure is Phase 2 of the Small Business Research Initiative on the Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry (SULS). It aims to establish centralised demonstrator projects for processing of livestock slurry and anaerobic digestate to reduce surplus phosphorus within NI agriculture and ensure efficient export and recycling of organic nutrients.However, I recognise that there is more work to be done with the agricultural sector to reduce surplus phosphorus. My Department is taking forward a review of the Nutrients Action Programme which is considering additional measures that are needed to help reduce phosphorus losses to waterways from agricultural sources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish an environmental improvement plan.
answer_text: The draft Environment Strategy is intended to become Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan. I shall be carefully considering the contents of the draft Environment Strategy before making a decision to recommend its adoption as an Environmental Improvement Plan to my Executive colleagues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when the Environment Strategy will be implemented; and (ii) for an update on the (a) funding; and (b) timetable for delivery associated with this strategy.
answer_text: In the next few weeks, I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and agreement a new environment strategy which can be adopted as Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). The EIP will take a strategic approach to all the issues facing our environment, which will require collective action across government, private and public sector and in the community.	Whilst acknowledging that the ambition of the EIP will need to be set in the context of the current budget situation, collectively we need to explore all avenues for funding to help address the environmental challenges we are facing, as ultimately availability of finance and resource will have a significant bearing on what we can achieve and the timeframe for doing so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will finalise the establishment of the (i) East Coast (Northern Ireland) Marine Special Protection Area; and (ii) Carlingford Lough Marine Special Protection Area.
answer_text: Nature conservation in Northern Ireland is a devolved matter with the exception of the marine environment, where some functions are reserved matters. Therefore, decisions taken on marine SPAs require the consideration and approval of the Secretary of State for DEFRA.My officials are currently working through a number of issues with DEFRA and the process to classify these Special Protections Areas cannot be completed until these are resolved.  I will provide further updates in due course. It should be noted that new protected areas have ‘policy protection’ from when they are consulted on and, therefore, the marine extended area for Carlingford Lough SPA and East Coast (NI) Marine SPA are considered when assessing the impacts of any new plans, projects, developments, or any other significant activities within or beyond the site, and will continue to be assessed to ensure they have no direct or indirect impact on the birds and the habitats which support them.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will launch a consultation on (i) a climate action plan; (ii) a just transition commission; and (iii) the establishment of a climate commissioner.
answer_text: The relevant timeframes are as follows;The consultation on the draft Climate Action Plan will be launched as soon as possible. Given the cross-cutting nature of the Climate Action Plan, the draft will need to be agreed by the Executive, prior to its publication for consultation.I envisage that proposals around the establishment of the Just Transition Commission will be published for consultation in the second quarter of 2024.Responsibility for establishing the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner rests with TEO. I understand that TEO officials have taken forward policy development work on options for the Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner. The First and deputy First Ministers will need to consider these options before progressing to consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish a biodiversity strategy.
answer_text: A Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy is currently under development, with a view to launching a consultation later this year. A date for publication cannot be set until a consultation has taken place and responses have been analysed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of drivers, who have experienced epileptic seizures in the past, having to (i) pay a greater amount to renew their driving licence; and (ii) renew their licence more frequently.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is required to be satisfied that driving licences are issued only to those drivers who meet the relevant standards in relation to medical fitness to drive.A driver applying for a renewal licence and who suffers from epilepsy will have their condition assessed by the DVA to ensure that they meet the relevant standard in relation to fitness to drive.  Whilst a person with a medically restricted licence will be required to renew their licence more frequently, a licence issued under these circumstances does not attract a fee.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many people who have declared that they have had seizures have been granted a driving licence by the DVA, over the last five years.
answer_text: While the information you have requested is held by the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA), it is not held in a readily accessible format, and I am therefore unable to provide you with the information sought.The DVA’s driver licensing database contains details on driving entitlement and personal details in relation to the individual driver. However, information in relation to medical conditions declared, while associated with the driver record, is held separately, in line with protections under the Data Protection Act 2018 and UK GDPR requirements.   To extract the requested information, the DVA would have to check each record where a medical assessment was conducted to determine whether the driver suffered from seizures and was granted a licence.  In the last five years around seventy thousand cases have been assessed by the medical advisors and therefore extraction of the information is both time and cost prohibitive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions she has had, or intends to have, with the Chief Constable in relation to Pro-Palestine protests that have taken place since August 2023.
answer_text: The policing of protests is an operational matter for the PSNI who are accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Whilst I met the Chief Constable on 7 February this matter was not discussed as I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of Mears Housing for the accommodation asylum seekers.
answer_text: Immigration, including asylum policy, is an excepted matter. Responsibility for the provision of accommodation for Asylum Seekers and for monitoring the contractual arrangements in place with Mears Housing is therefore not devolved and remains with the Home Office.				 It is recognised that contingency accommodation can be unsuitable especially for families.				Under the auspice of the Strategic Planning Group, officials have been coordinating work across Departments and statutory agencies and engaging with the voluntary and community sector on issues impacting those living in contingency accommodation. This has resulted in an agreed action plan aimed at addressing issues, where possible, within the devolved competence.				In parallel, recognising the concerns raised, we welcome that the Independent Chief Inspector of Borders and Immigration (ICIBI) was receptive to the Departments request for a review of contingency accommodation here and await the publication of the resulting report.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what engagement their Department has had with Mears Housing; and (ii) how regularly these engagements occur.
answer_text: ANSWER Whilst immigration, including asylum policy, is an excepted matter and the responsibility of the Home Office, given its coordination role, the Department engages regularly on policy approach, local issues and impact. This also includes engagement with Mears Housing as the authority contracted by Home Office to deliver accommodation to asylum seekers here. Engagement has taken place across the following forums as well as on an ad hoc basis as necessary:Strategic Planning Group (SPG) Subgroup on Asylum Contingency Accommodation: a multi-agency subgroup of the SPG (RAS) established to coordinate work to address issues impacting asylum seekers living in Contingency Accommodation, with a primary focus on the needs of children and their families. Meetings have been scheduled monthly since its establishment in October 2022.Council Engagement Group: a 6-weekly meeting established in September 2022, chaired by TEO and attended by local Councils, Home Office, Mears, and devolved statutory bodies, working on a multi-agency basis to support the successful dispersal and integration of asylum seekers across Council areas.Regional Delivery and Procurement Group (RDG): a cross-agency group consisting of statutory and voluntary partners, chaired by Home Office with a focus on the operational delivery of asylum support. The group convened on a monthly-basis until October 2023 and it is the intention for this to start up again in early 2024 following previous cancellations of the meeting owing to administrative challenges. NI Full Dispersal Governance Meeting: chaired by Home Office and attended by Mears and TEO. A monthly regional governance board, facilitating an end-to-end conversation about asylum. Family Help Working Group: oversees the delivery of the Family Help pilot. The group consists of members from Belfast Health & Social Care Trust, TEO, Education Authority (EA), Mears, Barnardos and Surestart and meets monthly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to (i) ensure safe staffing in our hospitals; and (ii) improve overall staffing levels in the health system.
answer_text: The Delivering Care Policy Framework - for safe and effective staffing was first developed in 2014. This document set out an agreed approach to Nursing and Midwifery workforce planning, giving commissioners and providers of Health and Social Care services principles for planning core nursing and midwifery workforce requirements.The DoH CNO group in conjunction with key stakeholders from across the region, are leading a task and finish group to finalise a refreshed Delivering Care Policy Framework 2024 which will support the provision of safe and effective, high-quality care in hospitals and community settings by providing guidance on workforce and workload planning and aligning to ‘Shaping Our Future’ the five-year vision for nursing and midwifery in NI. To underscore safe staffing and support the Department’s current strategic workforce planning programme, the Department made a commitment to consider safe staffing legislation for Nursing & Midwifery as part of the January 2020 Framework Agreement.  The scope was expanded in October 2022 to include all professions within the Health & Social Care Trusts. I intend to launch a public consultation in the coming weeks to seek views on these legislative proposals. Additionally, the Department’s ambitions for developing the health and social care workforce are outlined in the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People which was published in May 2018.  Implementation of the Strategy’s second action plan which covers the three-year period 2022/23 to 2024/25 is ongoing.  A copy of this document can be found at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/health-and-social-care-workforce-strategy-2026A key area of this work is the Department’s ongoing programme of strategic workforce planning.  The Department continues to identify future workforce needs through this programme and uses the evidence from this analysis to invest in the commissioning of pre-registration education and post registration training, subject to the necessary funding being made available.This strategic approach to workforce development has supported a 13.9% (+7,886) increase in whole time equivalent staff in post across the HSC between March 2018 and March 2023, with a 15.1% (+634) increase in medical and dental staff and a 14.9% (+2,250) increase in nursing and midwifery staff in post.Increased and sustained investment is necessary to support my Department’s ambitions for continued growth and development of the health and social care workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what provisions she will put in place to provide public sector workers with a pay increase in the new financial year.
answer_text: Working together with Ministerial colleagues I want to ensure all our public sector workers receive a pay award as soon as possible reflecting the vital role they play in delivering public services.The amount included for pay in the December financial package only provides funding for one year and falls short of what is required. The Executive will take a united case for a fair and just settlement for public sector workers and public services to Westminster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the introduction of domestic water charges; and (ii) whether he has plans to introduce domestic water charges.
answer_text: On the 20 September 2023 the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland directed the Department for Infrastructure Permanent Secretary to carry out a public consultation on the introduction of domestic water and associated charges. This public consultation is ongoing having launched on the 7 December 2023 for a 14 week period. The consultation will close on 13 March 2024 and a report summarising the consultation responses will be compiled and presented to me for consideration.I will not be introducing domestic water charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to introduce rent controls in the private rented sector.
answer_text: The powers to control rents set out in Section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022 (adding 5C to the 2006 Order) expired on 28 October 2023. I therefore currently have no plans to introduce new rent controls in the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment on cutting corporation tax and whether her Department will consider such measures.
answer_text: I will consider the Fiscal Commission’s recommendations, across the full range of taxes, and will then bring proposals to the Executive on the additional fiscal powers it might seek. The devolution and reduction of Corporation Tax will only be possible if it is affordable, and worthwhile if it ensures that ordinary citizens benefit through the creation of sustainable, well-paying jobs, which furthers economic development

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5380/22-27, for a breakdown of the spend on (i) repairs and maintenance on-site staff; and (ii) reactive repairs and maintenance, from 1 April 2022 to 31 March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5380/22-27, what percentage of maintenance and repairs work was carried out by (i) outside contractors; and (ii) secretariat staff.
answer_text: All maintenance and repair works are carried out by outside contractors under a Department of Finance framework agreement, to which the Assembly Commission is a named party.No maintenance or repair works are carried out by secretariat staff.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what work has been undertaken to source an on-site crèche facility for people who work in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 November 2019, the Assembly Commission was advised that the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Finance (the Department), had liaised with the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive, to explore the feasibility of opening a pre-school childcare facility on the Stormont Estate. The intention was that such a facility could be used by Members, Members’ staff, Assembly Commission staff and Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) staff working on the Estate.While some exploratory options and designs had been developed, considerable work was still needed to prepare a fully-worked business case.  This was being developed by the Department, and it was intended that once a detailed proposal was developed, it would be brought to the Assembly Commission for its consideration.Given the many interruptions to normal work activities since March 2020, arising from the Department’s response to COVID-19, the project was paused at that time. The Department has advised that, at the time, the feasibility of a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate could have been considered as a potential action for inclusion in a draft Childcare Strategy which is being developed by the Department of Education. It is worth noting that since 2017, Members and Assembly staff can avail of HMRC’s Tax-Free Childcare scheme. This scheme is administered centrally by HMRC, and allows the payment for approved childcare (for example, from a childminder or nursery), to be made via an online childcare account. Up to £2,000 per year for each child is available to help with the costs of childcare. As with all schemes set up by HMRC, it has specific rules and regulations that must be met before it can be used.In addition, some Members who met the relevant conditions, were able to purchase childcare vouchers using a salary sacrifice scheme. This arrangement meant that childcare vouchers could be purchased in a tax efficient manner. While the salary sacrifice arrangement ended in 2017, the scheme has remained open to Members who were already included in the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether there was any equality impact assessment on the decision to change in overtime payments for staff working in National Museums Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for National Museums Northern Ireland. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “As noted by National Museums NI [in response to AQW 5329], there has been no change to overtime payments for staff working in National Museums NI. An equality impact assessment was not carried out for the change in terms and conditions introduced in 2022 as it was not deemed to be appropriate.  Following a consultation exercise in early 2021, the change was approved by National Museums NI Board of Trustees, the Department of Finance and the Department for Communities”. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact National Museums NI directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what advice Foras na Gaeilge provided to Belfast City Council on the translation of the Irish language signs that were erected in Andersonstown Leisure Centre.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“Foras na Gaeilge is in regular contact with Belfast City Council due to our ongoing funding relationship with the Council, Foras na Gaeilge discussed the issue of this signage on several occasions with the Belfast City Council Irish language Officer as part of our support role to the officer whose salary is part-funded by Foras na Gaeilge. The Foras na Gaeilge Chief Terminologist also provided advice as to the linguistic accuracy of the signs.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the change in overtime payments for staff working in National Museums Northern Ireland will apply to staff on old contracts.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for National Museums Northern Ireland. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “There has been no change to overtime payments for staff employed by National Museums NI and all staff are remunerated in line with NICS overtime rates. In 2022 National Museums NI introduced new terms and conditions for new staff which saw the removal of enhanced payments for working weekends and revised remuneration for working on public holidays. This was done in order to ensure we have appropriate flexibility in delivering our services to the public and provide terms and conditions more appropriate to the sector in which we operate”. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact National Museums NI directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the change in overtime payments for staff working in National Museums Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for National Museums Northern Ireland. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “As noted by National Museums NI [in response to AQW 5329], there has been no change to overtime payments for staff working in National Museums NI. The new contract terms and conditions have been in operation since 2022. The change to terms and conditions continues to be discussed with employee representatives”. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact National Museums NI directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what government departments and cross-border institutions oversee, scrutinise and assess the work of Foras na Gaeilge; and how this work is undertaken.
answer_text: My Department, together with the Irish Government’s Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media, jointly sponsors the North/South Language Body.  Sponsorship responsibilities include approving the Body’s strategic objectives and the policy and performance framework within which the Body will operate. With regards to overseeing, scrutinising, and assessing the work of Foras na Gaeilge, Para 2.2 of Part 5 (Annex 2) of The North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 confers statutory responsibility for the exercise of the functions, of Foras na Gaeilge, to the Board of the North/South Language Body. However North/South Body Sponsor Departments also have a monitoring role to ensure good governance and sound financial management.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the process adopted by Foras na Gaeilge when advising Belfast City Council on Irish language signage at Andersonstown Leisure Centre; and (ii) whether the signage was submitted to the Terminology Committee for its consideration.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“(i) Foras na Gaeilge did not pursue a formal process in relation to advice provided to Belfast City Council – advice was offered by way of informal engagements.(ii) The role of the Terminology Committee is to approve new and revised terminology. Due to the informal nature of the engagement between Foras and Belfast City Council, and since the terminology being suggested was already in existence, it would not have been appropriate to bring the matter to the Terminology Committee.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Foras na Gaeilge recommended that Belfast City Council consult with the local Irish language community on the signage to be erected at Andersonstown Leisure Centre.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“Belfast City Council consultation policies are a matter for Belfast City Council. Foras na Gaeilge did not make any comments, or recommendations to Belfast City Council around consultation on signage to be erected at Andersonstown Leisure Centre.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the government departments and cross-border institutions which oversee, scrutinise and assess the work of Foras na Gaeilge have training in language planning.
answer_text: The North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 confers legislative responsibility on Foras na Gaeilge to promote the Irish language in this jurisdiction. This includes responsibility for language planning.

answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“Foras na Gaeilge responded to a series of informal requests from Belfast City Council by way of advising as to whether translations of the signs proffered were accurate.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the language planning training Foras na Gaeilge staff receive to assist them in advising local authorities on erecting Irish language signage.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“Foras na Gaeilge staff do not receive any language-planning training on advising local authorities on erecting language signs.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether Foras na Gaeilge provides funding to support the post of the Irish Language Officer at Belfast City Council; (ii) to outline the details of the agreement between Foras na Gaeilge and Belfast City Council; and (iii) whether Foras na Gaeilge has provided Belfast City Council’s Irish Language Officer with training in Irish language planning.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“(i) Foras na Gaeilge provides part funding for the role of Irish Language Officer in Belfast City Council through its Irish Language Officers Scheme. (ii) Foras na Gaeilge is providing £11,094 of funding to Belfast City Council in 2024 to part fund the salary of the Council’s Irish Language Officer.
