question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) what guidance Foras na Gaeilge provides to local authorities to ensure Irish language signage is intelligible; and (ii) where this guidance is published.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“Foras na Gaeilge does not provide or publish formalised guidance to local authorities re the intelligibility of Irish language signage.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Foras na Gaeilge’s language planning policy includes provision for ensuring Irish language signs erected by local authorities are intelligible.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following response:“The intelligibility of signs erected by local authorities is not explicitly covered in any Foras na Gaeilge policies.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will acute mental health inpatient services, which were temporarily removed from the Mater Hospital during COVID, be returned.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consultation has been undertaken with residents in North Belfast about the need for acute mental health inpatient services.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will maternity-led delivery services, which were temporarily removed from the Mater Hospital during COVID, be returned.
answer_text: The Belfast Heath and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) continues to deliver high quality maternity services and is currently providing a midwifery-led service within the Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital (RJMH). All births, apart from home births, are currently taking place there.  Postnatal care is provided in two wards in RJMH as well as postnatal home visits by community midwives.Antenatal care continues to be delivered on the Mater site with midwives based there daily. No date can yet be confirmed for the return of delivery services to the Mater site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consultation has been undertaken with staff and their trade unions about the return of emergency surgery to the Mater Hospital.
answer_text: Earlier this year, I published the review of general surgery, which spells out the challenges of the current configuration of these services in Northern Ireland.  The review of general surgery is however a positive building block for the wider transformation of our health and social care system.   Its implementation will ensure that people across Northern Ireland who require general surgery receive the high quality, sustainable and equitable care and treatment they need, when they need it. It recognises that a higher standard of care will be delivered by reconfiguration of service delivery and cross-organisational working which may result in a change in provisions to ensure better patient outcomes.  In the review, I announced the next phase of the Elective Care Centre model and identified the Mater Hospital in Belfast as the first Elective Overnight Stay Centre for patients requiring intermediate complexity surgery with an overnight stay.   Elective Care Centres are designed to provide a dedicated resource for less complex planned surgery/procedures and are a means to increase productivity, efficiency and reliability of the service.  Such centres can reduce waiting times for elective care and provide a better experience for both patients and staff. Emergency surgery has not been performed at Mater Hospital for a number of years. All staff and Trade Union representatives were engaged with at the relevant time. While the policy direction of Elective Overnight Stay Centres ensures a future for Mater Hospital, decisions around details of service delivery is for the Trust to make.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his Written Ministerial Statement on 19 October 2022, whether he has plans to return emergency surgery to the Mater Hospital.
answer_text: Earlier this year, I published the review of general surgery, which spells out the challenges of the current configuration of these services in Northern Ireland.  The review of general surgery is however a positive building block for the wider transformation of our health and social care system.   Its implementation will ensure that people across Northern Ireland who require general surgery receive the high quality, sustainable and equitable care and treatment they need, when they need it. It recognises that a higher standard of care will be delivered by reconfiguration of service delivery and cross-organisational working which may result in a change in provisions to ensure better patient outcomes.  In the review, I announced the next phase of the Elective Care Centre model and identified the Mater Hospital in Belfast as the first Elective Overnight Stay Centre for patients requiring intermediate complexity surgery with an overnight stay.   Elective Care Centres are designed to provide a dedicated resource for less complex planned surgery/procedures and are a means to increase productivity, efficiency and reliability of the service.  Emergency surgery has not been performed at Mater Hospital for a number of years. While the policy direction of Elective Overnight Stay Centres ensures a future for Mater Hospital, decisions around details of service delivery is for the Trust to make.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what date her Department first became aware of substitute teachers not being paid correctly via the NI Supply Teacher Register.
answer_text: This question relates to events in August 2022.A new and improved Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) was procured by the Education Authority (EA) which administers the system, and it went live in August 2022.A number of issues were identified when the system went live which resulted in late salary payments for some supply teachers. The EA first became aware of the issue following receipt and processing of the first file from the new NISTR system for August 2022 which were due for payment on 16 September 2022.Improvements were made in the booking and sign-off processes to avoid issues which had created problems in the past and NISTR is accepted as the only system through which schools can engage supply teachers in NI. The EA continues to monitor activities through NISTR.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions she has taken in response to the recent reported whistleblowing complaints raised about the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 4700/22-27 issued on 16 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what are his plans for maternity services in Northern Ireland
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to ensure that learning from reviews of maternity safety across the United Kingdom is implemented across the Health and social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what representations he has made to the British government for further financial assistance to deal with the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The support being offered by the British Government is not sufficient to deal with the cost of living crisis that households and businesses are facing, and I have repeatedly called on them for further financial support to be made available. Since the first energy support measures were announced in March, I have had the following meetings with British Government Ministers at which cost of living support was discussed:21st March – Simon Clarke MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury26th May – Simon Clarke MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury15th June – Simon Clarke MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury22nd June – Simon Clarke MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury29th June – Simon Clarke MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury26th August – Nadhim Zahawi MP, Chancellor of the Exchequer, and Kwasi Kwarteng MP, Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy23rd September – Chris Philip MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury13th October – Chris Philp MP, Chief Secretary to Treasury13th October – Nadhim Zahawi, Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster 20th October – Ed Argar MP, Chief Secretary to TreasuryIn addition to the above, myself and the Finance Ministers from Scotland and Wales have twice jointly written to the British Chancellor, in July and September, calling for additional cost of living support. I also wrote directly to the Chief Secretary to Treasury twice in September seeking to address gaps in the support announced and subsequently asking for the £100 oil scheme to be urgently reviewed.The inability of the British Government to deliver adequate support to families and businesses again reiterates the need for a functioning Executive that could have considered tailored support that meets the needs of the most vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much surplus exists within the Housing Executive's budget.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“It is prudent to have the ability to carry forward any unspent funding (rental income receipts) from one financial year to the next using a Stock Investment Reserve, so that unforeseen stock investment delays can be funded in subsequent financial years (rather than unspent funding being lost).  This allows more effective management of the available resources, whilst also providing NIHE with an ability to deal with exceptional expenditure as it arises.The estimated closing balance on the Stock Investment Reserve at the end of the 2023/24 financial year was circa £282.8m.To put this figure in context, the current balance on the Stock Investment Reserve represents circa 5.4% of NIHE’s stock investment requirement during the next 10 years.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the reported recent whistleblowing complaints sent to her Department and the Housing Executive.
answer_text: Both the Department and the Housing Executive have guidance in place to ensure that staff are able to raise their concerns in the knowledge that these will be taken seriously. Under existing arrangements, the Department for Communities’ Arm’s Length Bodies notify the Department when they have received concerns and when their internal investigations have been completed. In December 2022 the Housing Executive notified the Department that their investigations had concluded in relation to the concerns. No actions resulted from this for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what representations he has made to the British government about the devolution of tax powers.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Fiscal Commission’s final report in May 2022, I wrote to the then Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) stressing the importance of formal engagement with Treasury to consider how any new Executive and the British Government might respond to the Commission’s report. I also indicated to the CST that I would be launching a public consultation as a first step to start the public discussion on the devolution of further tax powers, which I subsequently launched on the 4th October (www.finance-ni.gov.uk/consultations/devolution-fiscal-powers).  It will however be for an incoming Finance Minister to bring recommendations on the Commission’s report to a restored Executive for its agreement, before further dialogue with the British Government on the implementation of those.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what lessons are there for maternity services in Northern Ireland from (i) the outcome of the Ockenden Review into Shrewsbury and Telford Hospital NHS Trust; and (ii) other reviews of maternity safety across the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has bid for funding to provide for an uplift in teachers’ pay.
answer_text: Departmental of Education (DE) officials have been engaging with their Department of Finance (DoF) counterparts in relation to the DE indicative baseline and the estimated Resource funding gap for 2022-23.  The range of pressures identified during this exercise includes high level estimates of the potential cost of a teachers’ pay settlement for 2021 and 2022.  However, as these settlements are subject to ongoing negotiations, these estimates will be subject to change.It is worth noting that given the pressures on the Education budget, the teachers’ pay settlements for 2021 and 2022 can only be settled with confirmed additional funds from the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many people in West Belfast, over the last five years, paid for a private diagnosis for autism, ADHD or Asperger’s.
To ask the Minister of Education what autism training her Department provides for workers in the education sector.
answer_text: In response to the NI Assembly motion (February 2020), the Enhanced Autism Training Framework (EATF), was developed in partnership by the Education Authority Autism Advisory and Intervention Service (EA AAIS) and Middletown Centre for Autism (MCA). This training framework is in line with the Department’s Teacher Professional Learning Strategy – Learning Leaders, which offers a tiered approach to support a teacher’s continuous professional development. The EATF also includes a bespoke training programme, for Classroom Assistants, in response to calls for training for the wider educational workforce.I have asked officials to liaise with both the EA, MCA and other key stakeholders, to ensure that the opportunities for training of the educational workforce are maximised through the EATF, ensuring that the Department is meeting its requirements as set out in the Autism (Amendment) Act 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what input she, or her Department, has into teacher pay negotiations.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC). Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and DE.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) comprising the five recognised teachers’ unions. My Department is not responsible for the management of the TNC although it is represented on it.Engagement has been ongoing over the last number of months between Management Side and the NITC to explore options for a teachers’ pay settlement for 2021-22.  Following receipt of the NITC pay claim for 2022-23, these discussions are now considering the options for a multi-year pay deal, which will be dependent on the Education budget and Public Sector Pay Policy for 2022/23. Management Side remains committed to resolving issues regarding teachers’ pay through discussion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant AQW 4172/22-27, how much unallocated funding has been spent on government energy bills.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive it is not possible to allocate additional funding to departments. Therefore I can confirm no unallocated funding has been spent on government energy bills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many people in West Belfast, who have children with autism, receive respite care.
answer_text: Respite care is provided to parents and families who have children with a broad range of care needs. Provision of respite care is not based on diagnosis or disability type of those seeking respite which is not recorded and therefore it is not possible to provide a defined number of people who have children with autism who receive respite care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many people in West Belfast received a diagnosis for (i) Autism; (ii) ADHD; and (iii) Asperger’s, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Part (i) and (iii)My Department publishes adult and children’s autism diagnoses, which incorporates diagnoses of Asperger’s syndrome, on a quarterly basis. The data is routinely collected at HSC Trust Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust level only and cannot be disaggregated to West Belfast. Figures for each of the last five years for the Belfast HSC Trust can be found at the following link: Quarterly autism statistics for NI (April - June 2022) | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).Part (ii)My Department does not routinely collect information in regards to the ADHD diagnoses and there is no regional register or consistent recording of ADHD across the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. Therefore, the number of people diagnosed with ADHD in the last years cannot be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many people in West Belfast are currently waiting for an assessment for (i) Autism; (ii) Asperger's; and (iii) ADHD.
To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to bring forward an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland.	I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what proposals she has brought forward to account for the ending of welfare mitigations in March 2025.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory obligation to produce a report on the operation of the existing Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes before 31 March 2025.The purpose of this report will be to assess the operation of each of the current mitigation payment schemes.  The report will include evidence of the effect of the schemes and an assessment of the scheduled cessation of some payments on 31 March 2025.The final report must include proposals, if there are any, to amend, revoke or introduce new legislation in respect of the schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of plans to prosecute people for non-payment of rates.
answer_text: I have no plans to prosecute any person for non-payment of rates as it is not a criminal offence.  The debt arising from non-payment of rates is a civil matter and not a criminal one.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the NIC-ICTU’s call for a comprehensive Employment Bill as outlined in its Workers Demand Better campaign document.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent launch of NIC-ICTU’s Workers Demand Better campaign.  As you will know I have previously said that I believe an Employment Bill is needed to ensure our employment law framework continues to meet the needs of workers and businesses. Any proposals for inclusion in an Employment Bill will be subject to the views of the Executive, stakeholder engagement, public consultation and the relevant Assembly processes with regard to any legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of costs to her Department incurred by the Land and Property Services' plans to prosecute people for non-payment of rates.
answer_text: Land & Property Services does not prosecute people for non-payment of rates as it is not a criminal offence.  The debt arising from non-payment of rates is a civil matter which is pursued through the Magistrates’ Court.Although LPS meets the cost in the first instance, the legal fees and costs associated with civil proceedings are payable by the debtor and recovered through the rate billing cycle.  The current cost of obtaining a Court Process is £39 and the cost of obtaining a Court Decree is £26.As LPS has not yet initiated Court proceedings this financial year, no new legal fees or costs have been incurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 3779/22-27, (i) whether homophobic incidents in prisons are recorded; and (ii) where they are recorded.
answer_text: All reports and complaints of incidents in prisons are recorded.  However, they are categorised either as being either discrimination or harassment related with the category being determined by how each incident is perceived by the complainant.  “Homophobic” is not an assigned category.  When investigated and completed reports and complaints are saved to the Prison Service Records Management System.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reintroduce free Covid-19 testing.
answer_text: COVID-19 tests remain free to all those for whom testing is recommended or advised.  Future decisions regarding arrangements for COVID-19 testing, including consideration of proportionate use of lateral flow testing, will be informed by a range of factors. These include for example the prevailing COVID-19 disease prevalence, trajectory and impact and the wider public health risk assessment. My Department continues to closely monitor Covid-19 surveillance and epidemiological data. It is likely that case numbers will increase as we progress through the Autumn and Winter period. All advice in relation to testing will continue to be kept under review and a range of measures may be necessary into the future in order to mitigate against severe illness for the most vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to deal with the Covid-19 wave expected to arrive this winter.
answer_text: In planning for the winter period, the Health Service remains focused on maintaining service delivery. In addition to the processes developed in response to Covid being embedded, additional capacity and resources will be put in place over the winter period. Achieving a high uptake of the 1 million people eligible to receive the Covid-19 booster, in addition to the flu vaccination programme, will help minimise pressure on the health service over the winter. Ongoing surveillance will provide an important understanding of the emerging pressures on the health system and support decision making to maintain service delivery during the winter period. Timely public health advice and guidance along with targeted Covid testing, where it will make the most difference, will also support our winter plans. I would ask that everyone continues to follow the latest public health advice, including getting vaccinated when invited to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 81/22-27, to specify a proposed date for the opening of a new women’s only accommodation service.
answer_text: The Welcome Organisation are delivering a new accommodation model called Catherine House which officially opened on March 19th, 2023. Catherine House is a short term (up to 2 years) accommodation service for women experiencing chronic homelessness and supports women with complex needs through their journey out of homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she or her officials has had with the Housing Executive over outstanding pay remits.
answer_text: I would refer you to my previous answer of AQW 3386/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many vacancies currently exist in the allocation section of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive currently has 81 Housing Advisor posts within Belfast Housing Solutions and 12 of these are vacant at present.  They continue to assign a dedicated housing advisor to each new homeless /housing applicant as and when they make contact.They actively seek to fill their vacancies and in December 2023 they advertised ‘open’ recruitment for Housing Advisors in Belfast. They are planning to make a number of appointments from this in the coming weeks. The vacancies remain open at present, and they have received a significant number of applications. Therefore, they plan to conduct the next round of shortlisting/assessments in the near future and would anticipate further appointments will be made.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many change of tenancies are outstanding for Housing Executive tenants as a result of ongoing strike action.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “Thirteen properties (change of tenancies) are currently on hold or yet to start, however these are not attributable to strike action, which concluded in March 2023.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much has been spent by the Housing Executive on private contractors during each of the eight weeks that workers have taken strike action.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:     “The costs incurred to complete work that would have been undertaken by NIHE staff during the strike action by utilising private contractors to perform urgent and emergency repairs in areas where the response maintenance service is provided in-house by our Direct Labour Organisation is estimated to be £564k from the 5th of September 2022 to the 3rd of March 2023. This is offset by the reduction in salary costs of the striking workers.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities on how many occasions have arms length bodies not submitted pay remits for Ministerial approval, over the last five years.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the deadline is for submission of Housing Executive pay remits.
answer_text: I would refer you to the answer I provided for AQW 3385/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many vacancies there are in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance.  Each Civil Service Department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff.  Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a Department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post.  NICSHR liaises with all Departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources.At the end of September 2022, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 3,086 vacancies

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much unallocated Executive funding has been spent on energy costs.
answer_text: The absence of an Executive means that it is not possible to agree a Budget nor to allocate any additional funding which has been provided through the Barnett formula. As a result it has not been possible to consider local support for energy costs.The British Government has committed to providing equivalent support here to the £400 Energy Bills Support Scheme. As a result the £164.8 million of Barnett consequential received as a result of the initial £200 announcement will be returned to Treasury.  I have made it clear to the Chancellor and Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS) that there should be no delay in issuing these payments to citizens here. Despite this, the British Government has yet to provide an update on how or when people here will receive the payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the overall departmental overspend for this financial year.
answer_text: As the Executive had not agreed a Budget before the First Minister resigned in February, departments do not have budgets for 2022-23. It is clearly hugely challenging for departments to operate in the absence of a Budget, and this situation has created a real risk of departments collectively spending considerably more than the funding the Executive has available.  I have written to my ministerial colleagues on this matter, and detailed this position in a Written Minister Statement to the Assembly which can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-2022-23-budgetary-position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what power he has to negotiate and approve Civil Service pay increases.
answer_text: I refer you to the statement that I made on 11 October which sets out the position in relation to Civil Service pay: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-2022-23-budgetary-position

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any current underspend in departmental budgets.
answer_text: As the Executive had not agreed a Budget before the First Minister resigned in February, departments do not have budgets for 2022-23. In the absence of these, it is not possible to provide information on departmental underspends.It is clearly hugely challenging for departments to operate in the absence of a Budget, and this situation has created a real risk of departments collectively spending considerably more than the funding the Executive has available. I have written to my ministerial colleagues on this matter, and provided a Written Minister Statement to the Assembly which can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-2022-23-budgetary-position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the quarry operators, that have been awarded contracts to supply materials for his Department or provide materials for infrastructure projects overseen by his Department, that are operating outside of planning control and environmental impact assessment regulations.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any of its suppliers operating without planning permission or any legally required environmental assessments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement she or her officials have had regarding an increased number of court hearings pertaining to people who will be unable to pay their rates this financial year.
answer_text: Neither I, nor my officials, have had any engagement to discuss any increase in the number of court hearings for people who will be unable to pay their rates during 2022/23.The decision on whether to initiate court proceedings is a matter for the Land and Property Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether a seed sourcing grant or other similar support grant is in place to enhance the quality, quantity, and diversity of tree seed sources.
answer_text: There is no specific Seed Sourcing Grant in operation in Northern Ireland. My Department provides certification of forest reproductive material for local tree growers, including the conservation volunteering sector, who produce trees from seed they collect in Northern Ireland. This certification process enables the trees to be marketed for forestry purposes with assurance of seed source and genetic quality.Forest Service also work in partnership with Future Trees Trust to support improvement in the quality, quantity and diversity of tree seed sources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in the absence of the commencement of the Review of Old Minerals Permissions, whether habitats or species listed in Annex 1 to Habitats Directive 92/43/EEC or Northern Ireland priority habitats or species have been destroyed or harmed.
answer_text: In the North, priority habitats or species listed in Annex 1 to Habitats Directive are protected through the Habitats Directive i.e. the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995 and Northern Ireland priority habitats or species are protected through the Environment (Northern Ireland) Order 2002 and other local legislation. The protection afforded by this legislation remains in place and is not reliant on the un-commenced provisions for the review of old mineral permissions (“ROMPs”) in the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what input he and his Department will have to the UK Covid-19 Inquiry.
answer_text: My department will cooperate fully with the UK Covid-19 Inquiry.  My department has been granted Core Participant (CP) status for Module 1.  The outcome of my department’s applications for CP status for Modules 2 and 2C is awaited.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what meetings he has had to discuss delaying rates payments this financial year for people who are in financial distress.
answer_text: Over the past two months I have held meetings with small business owners, trade bodies and householders to discuss the challenges that they are facing because of the current economic climate. I am very aware from my meetings in the community that many ratepayers will have difficulty paying their rates this year.LPS has a legislative duty to collect rates, which contributes to funding for essential public services such as health, education, infrastructure and local council services, and it is not legally possible for the Department to simply not collect the rates.  However, LPS will enter into payment arrangements that allow ratepayers who are in financial distress to pay outstanding rate bills over a longer period of time. I urge any ratepayer finding it difficult to pay their rate bill to contact LPS.My Department also provides a wide range of rate reliefs aimed at helping meet the cost of rates.  These include the Rate Rebate scheme, available for those entitled to Universal Credit, Housing Benefit and Low Income Rate Relief for non-working age citizens, and Lone Pensioner Allowance for citizens aged over 70 who live on their own.  I would encourage people to contact the Make the Call team to see if they are eligible for any of these rate reliefs and to ensure they are getting all the other financial support and services they are entitled to.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3201/22-27, what correspondence she has had with the Department for Work and Pensions or relevant Ministers on waiving or suspending debt repayments.
answer_text: I have had no correspondence with the Department for Work and Pensions or relevant Ministers on waiving or suspending debt repayments.  The suspension of debt recovery in Northern Ireland would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions and the cost of any divergence requires Executive agreement.The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people may be experiencing because of the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what options currently exist for people to defer paying social security debt; and (ii) how that has been conveyed to claimants.
answer_text: Information on the support for those repaying benefit overpayments and UC advances is available on nidirect (https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/ contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3201/22-27, what would the cost be to diverge from the Department for Work and Pensions on the suspension of debt recovery.
answer_text: Any pause in debt recovery would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions and the cost of any divergence would require Executive agreement.  Costs to diverge are not currently available.The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people are experiencing due to the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the efficacy of the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime programme.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Programme to Tackle Paramilitary Activity, Criminality and Organised Crime is a cross Executive initiative, for which I am the lead Executive minister. It is delivering a wide range of initiatives aimed at ensuring that communities are safer, and more resilient to paramilitarism, criminality and coercive control.Following a detailed review of Phase 1 of the Programme in 2020, Phase 2 began in 2021 with a strong focus on demonstrating outcomes not just outputs. The Programme’s ‘public health approach’ to violence reduction is data driven and based on local, national and international best practice. For the first time, data on the scale of the problem and what is working to address it has been gathered. For example, Programme data suggests that between 15 and 30% of people in NI live in an area that experiences paramilitary fear and intimidation. Through robust evaluation, projects are demonstrating how they are making a difference in dealing with the harm in the here and now as well as helping to break the cycle of harm by building individual and community resilience. All projects must contribute to the achievement of shared programme objectives. This helps to ensure collaboration across sectors as well as shared learning and enhanced best practice. For example, the Engage project, supporting young people at risk, has seen a reduction in the levels of probable PTSD, and a reduction in the risk taking behaviours, including those intending or expecting to be involved in violence. The Developing Women in the Community project has seen improvements in self efficacy, as well as a reduction in anxiety, depression, and probable PTSD. In the last financial year, 354 victims of paramilitary violence were supported through the Programme. Hospital-based interventions are showing the value of innovative methods of supporting those at risk of harm. Thousands more have been supported through other projects with a strong focus on early interventions, individual, family and peer support. The focus in the second Phase of the Programme has been on developing sustainable solutions and systems change and there have been significant developments in learning and best practice that will help to ensure better long-term support for people at risk. For example, the Programme has been at the forefront of sharing learning to inform work across sectors on child criminal exploitation and has been the sole funder of the Safeguarding Board’s Trauma Informed Practice project which is changing practice across the public sector. This is particularly relevant given the high levels of trauma in communities experiencing paramilitary exploitation and harm. Further information on the extensive research base for the Programme’s work can be found on www.endingtheharm.info Thankfully, we may be beginning to see some of the long term outworkings of our efforts. There is a downward trend (seen in multiple data sources) in some of the paramilitary activity that causes th

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what work has been carried out by her Department to investigate the benefits of the decriminalisation of drugs.
answer_text: The policy and legal frameworks relating to the misuse of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and the Psychoactive Substances Act 2016, is reserved to the UK Government.  It is therefore a matter for the Home Office to determine and bring forward any proposals on the benefits of decriminalising drugs.  You may be aware of the recently published Drugs Strategy From harm to hope: A 10-year drugs plan to cut crime and save lives - GOV.UK (www.gov.uk) which advocates a whole system approach.  I have not been made aware of any plans within the Home Office to decriminalise or review the classification of drugs.  Any change to the status quo would require the engagement of all four jurisdictions. For me the priority is to support ongoing work to address substance misuse and dependency in a holistic way, and primarily as a medical issue requiring therapeutic interventions, rather than focussing only on criminal justice outcomes.  I have made this point at a number of UK Ministerial engagement events.  I strongly believe that a cross-Executive approach to tackling substance misuse and engagement on UK-wide drugs policy is vital.  This is, regrettably, hampered by the current impasse.My Department continues to work closely with the Department of Health to deliver on the Northern Ireland Substance Use Strategy in support of a preventative and treatment focussed approach to prevent harm and empower recovery.  The Substance Misuse Court in Laganside Courts is one example of an alternative approach to help individuals where substance abuse is an underlying factor in their offending behaviour.    The Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) Drugs Sub Group provides a forum for engagement between PSNI and other stakeholders in response to the challenges posed by the misuse of drugs and related threats and to identify solutions to effectively deal with enforcement and supply of drugs. We can only succeed in improving outcomes if we see substance misuse in the broader context.  Education and awareness to prevent young and vulnerable people turning to drugs as a solution as well as access to therapeutic interventions are also important.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2866/22-27, to outline how many (i) private rented sector; and (ii) Housing Executive homes still require cavity wall insulation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not hold current data on the number of private rented sector homes that still require cavity wall insulation.  It recently completed a Northern Ireland wide cross tenure House Condition Survey, the results of this work will be available January 2026.  This may provide an insight into cavity wall insulation levels within the private rented sector.  Data from the House Condition Survey completed in 2016 indicated that at that time 27.4% of properties categorised as ‘private rented and others’ had no wall insulation.In the 1980s the Northern Ireland Housing Executive began installing cavity wall insulation in new build schemes and implemented a major cavity wall insulation retrofit programme in the older cavity wall constructed stock.  However, the latter installations were not recorded electronically at that time and, therefore, while the Housing Executive would be confident that all or most of the cavity wall constructed properties would have some level of cavity wall insulation installed, we are unable to confirm if any, and how many, have no cavity wall insulation. This would require a survey of all of the properties and the resources that would be required to do so are not available.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to address the economic problems facing unpaid carers.
answer_text: I remain deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced to help those most impacted by the current economic crisis. 	I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider future measures, including in relation to Carer’s Allowance.		The panel engaged with a wide range of people who access the social security system and with key stakeholders (including carers) so that their voices are heard on these important issues. The panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans she has to address the impact of the latest increase in Firmus Energy prices.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure households are facing, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level.		In regard to the latest price rises, the Energy Price Guarantee which was announced by the Business Secretary on 21st September will help to mitigate against price rises in gas and electricity for households in Northern Ireland. The UK Government has suggested that it will take effect from November with support being backdated to 1st October to ensure NI households benefit from the same support as the rest of the UK. This support is in addition to the £400 to be paid through Energy Bills Support Scheme to every household in Northern Ireland.			The vision of the ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’, the Energy Strategy, is providing a pathway to “net zero carbon and affordable energy”.  Domestic consumers living with more vulnerable circumstances, including those on low incomes, are a priority group whose specific needs will be addressed as part of the delivery of the strategy.		I continue to call on the UK government to implement the support for all Northern Ireland consumers as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions she has had with Ministerial collegues in relation to the cancellation of social security debt repayments.
answer_text: I have not had discussions with Ministerial colleagues in relation to the cancellation of social security debt repayments.Pausing debt recovery in Northern Ireland would require a break in parity from the Department for Work Pensions and the financial cost of any divergence requires Executive agreement.The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people are experiencing due to the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to establish an Overdose Prevention Site to address rising need in Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: At present, such facilities – also known as “Drug Consumption Rooms” or “Safe Injecting Facilities” – are not currently permitted under the UK-wide Misuse of Drugs Act 1971. The issue of criminal sanctions for those found in possession of drugs is an issue that is frequently raised with this type of facility. This is also governed by the Misuse of Drugs Act and remains a reserved matter. I am aware of discussions and proposals on this issue in both Scotland and the Republic of Ireland, and my Department is watching developments closely. We will continue to monitor the evidence in respect of effective interventions and develop new proposals, within the wider legislative framework, as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people will receive sanctions or a reduction in their claim as a result of the recent changes to Universal Credit announced in the mini-budget.
answer_text: No decision has yet been made as to whether changes announced to the Administrative Earnings Threshold (AET) level for Universal Credit will be implemented here. Work is ongoing with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to establish if there are any costs and/or parity issues of having a different AET here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people have been contacted regarding changes to Universal Credit since the Chancellor’s mini-budget on 23 September 2022.

Work is ongoing with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to establish if there are any costs and/or parity issues of having a different AET here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she or her officials have had with Department for Work and Pensions or the Chancellor regarding the recent Universal Credit changes.
answer_text: The British Chancellor made no contact before or following his announcement. Work is ongoing with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to establish if there are any costs and/or parity issues of having a different AET here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of how people with caring responsibilities will be impacted by the new changes to Universal Credit.
answer_text: No decision has yet been made as to whether changes announced to the Administrative Earnings Threshold (AET) level for Universal Credit will be implemented here. Work is ongoing with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to establish if there are any costs and/or parity issues of having a different AET here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work has she completed to waive or delay deductions from payments of those on benefits who have been overpaid.
answer_text: In exceptional circumstances the Department has the discretion to waive recovery of all or part of an over-payment. The Department considers requests for waivers based on individual circumstances. Further information on the waiver process and financial support available for anyone who has a benefit overpayment can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/overpayments-benefits-and-financial-support.The Department for Work and Pensions has confirmed they will not be pausing debt recovery. Pausing debt deductions in NI would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions and the financial cost of any divergence requires Executive agreement. The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people may be experiencing because of the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline her Department's projected spend on youth provision in West Belfast for the next two years.
answer_text: In line with Departmental Youth Policy as set out in the policy document Priorities for Youth, youth service provision is based on assessed need.The Regional Assessment of Need for 2023 - 26 is currently being completed and will inform future funding specifications. Funding opportunities for the period April 2023 to March 2026 are due to be published by the Education Authority Youth Service in November 2022 and can be accessed through the EA Youth Funding website at the following link:   https://eanifunding.org.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) when the general enhanced unit became operational in Magilligan prison; and (ii) what is the purpose of the general enhanced unit.
answer_text: Magilligan Prison does not have a general enhanced unit.

answer_text: Information in the form requested is not readily available and could only be obtained/compiled at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1350/22-27, to ask what the overall spend per year is on autism services not directly provided by Health Trusts, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The response to this AQW is based on the response to AQW 1350/22-27 and the clarity provided by the requestor on what was to be included. The funding totals included are for contracted and commissioned services by Trusts for services not directly provided by Trusts. Whilst all funding included in the response includes service provision to people and families requiring support for Autism not all of the funding is ringfenced for Autism services. Some of the contracts are for early intervention and prevention to people and families with social, communication or developmental delay queries. It is not possible to separate out the Autism element of each contracted service, nor would it be appropriate not to include these contracts in the response. answer_text: The recent announcement lifting the moratorium on shale gas extraction applies to England only. It will be up to a Northern Ireland Executive Committee to determine local onshore petroleum licensing policy.

answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work she and her officials have completed to ensure Housing Executive workers obtain a cost of living support payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, on average, how many bus journeys per day in Belfast have been cancelled over the last five years.
answer_text: Details of the number of bus cancellations over the past five years in Belfast is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

answer_text: Due to absence of legal vires and limited resources my Department would be unable to provide financial assistance for the purchasing of such items.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what support his Department is giving to those who are not eligible for the Cycle to Work Scheme but want to partake in active travel.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 3300/22-27.
