question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is on target to have completed the formulation of the action plan for reform of the planning system within six months as per recommendation 3 of the Public Accounts Committee’s Planning in Northern Ireland report issued in March 2022.
answer_text: My officials are working on a draft Memorandum of Response to the PAC recommendations which will be submitted to the PAC when it is re-formed. Officials continue to work on improvements to the planning system and engage with stakeholders to finalise a joint work programme and action plan. I am committed to improving the planning system and ensuring we have a modern, fit for purpose system which delivers the right socio-economic and environmental outcomes for all citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to establish a Fuel Poverty Commissioner office.
answer_text: Officials have commenced the development of a co-designed Fuel Poverty Strategy. As development progresses and at appropriate stages in the process there will be extensive engagement with key Departments and the broad range of external stakeholders and interested parties which will provide opportunity to influence the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to reduce the debt repayment cap.
answer_text: Limits for deductions from social security benefits are set in legislation to protect people from excessive repayments.  There are currently no plans to revise these limits.The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people may be experiencing because of the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be having difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many applications were (i) received; and (ii) shortlisted for the position to carry out an independent review of the licensing system.
answer_text: Two tenders were received to carry out the independent review of the licensing system, with one meeting the award criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what audits her Department has carried out into the number of homes needing cavity wall and roof insulation.
answer_text: The most recent survey was undertaken in 2016, with a full report on the findings published in May 2018.https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Documents/Research/HCS-2016-Main-Reports/HCS-Main-Report-2016.aspx Planning is currently under way for the next House Condition Survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work his Department has conducted to look at a voucher or discount scheme for households at risk of financial difficulty to buy or repair equipment for active travel.
answer_text: Due to limited resources my Department would be unable to fund such a voucher scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to introduce a moratorium on debt repayments for social security recipients.
answer_text: The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people are experiencing because of the current cost of living crisis and is continuing to explore options to provide both immediate and medium to long-term interventions.Pausing debt recovery would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions who are not currently intending to suspend recovery. The financial cost of any such divergence from parity would require Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to end the Utility Regulator's statutory duty to promote gas.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator operates under an extensive legislative and regulatory framework and is required to exercise its functions in line with a broad range of duties, including that provided under Article 14 of the Energy (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 which states that the principal objective of the Department and the Utility Regulator in carrying out their respective gas functions is “to promote the development and maintenance of an efficient, economic and co-ordinated gas industry in Northern Ireland”. The extent to which new or amended frameworks will be needed to support the delivery of the Executive’s Energy Strategy is under constant review by officials in conjunction with the Utility Regulator.  Findings are reported to Ministers and future legislative proposals would be subject to consultation and impact assessment as part of the wider policy and legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has any plans to implement a rent freeze similar to the one implemented by the Scottish Parliament.
answer_text: The powers to control rents set out in Section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022 (adding 5C to the 2006 Order) expired on 27 October 2023. I currently have no plans to introduce new rent controls in the private rented sector.Research carried out by my Department concluded that the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters was by ensuring the benefit system properly takes account of the cost of housing and by increasing housing supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy if he or his officials have explored options for public ownership of energy providers.
answer_text: Neither I, nor my Departmental officials, have explored options for public ownership of energy providers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to end the Utility Regulator’s statutory duty to ensure the financial viability of energy companies.
answer_text: The extent to which new or amended legislative and regulatory frameworks will be needed to support the delivery of the Energy Strategy, ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’, is being assessed by my officials, in conjunction with the Utility Regulator.  The Utility Regulator currently operates under an extensive legislative and regulatory framework and is required to exercise its functions in line with a broad range of duties. These include an obligation to exercise its functions in a way best calculated to secure a diverse, viable and environmentally sustainable long-term energy supply.The Utility Regulator and my Department have a duty to ensure the regulated energy suppliers and monopoly network providers are able to finance their activities.  The same requirements do not apply for competing energy supply companies (who therefore have no price regulation and compete against the regulated companies).   It is in the best interests of all consumers that energy companies remain financially viable in order to prevent a loss of energy supply to homes and businesses.  It also prevents the significant additional cost of supplier failure being added to consumer bills. Consumers are protected through price controls carried out by the Utility Regulator which ensure that the prices consumers pay reflects the cost of providing energy to their homes or businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to implementing enhanced pay for overtime instead of paying private agency companies.
answer_text: Current overtime rates are agreed nationally as part of AfC.  Decisions to utilise overtime are for individual trust employers, based on service needs.Reduction of agency expenditure is an extremely complex and multi-faceted issue which can only be addressed through the implementation of a wide range of measures.Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a key priority. My Department has undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC and it is examining all potential options, and the implications, as to how to address the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.The use of off-contract agencies has contributed significantly to an increase in agency staff expenditure over the last decade.Ending the use of off contract agencies has been a major priority for me in that regard. Equally as important is the need to address the impact that large-scale agency use can have on the morale of our health service staff.  Overreliance on agency use has a negative impact on our workforce, placing additional pressures on them to support agency staff who are unfamiliar with systems and wards.  This creates another pressure on the provision of safe and quality care.A new health service procurement process will be launched in the coming days for agencies who wish to provide nursing and midwifery agency staff to HSC. Agencies who have remained outside contractual frameworks now have a clear choice. I would encourage them to work with us on a contractual footing – or face an end to their income from the health service and taxpayers.The sustained drive to reduce agency costs will be accompanied by reform of the HSC Trusts’ Staff Banks to make them a more attractive option for staff wishing to work additional hours.The Agency Reduction Working Group has identified that reform of the Trusts’ Staff Banks and how they operate is necessary to support the move away from Agency usage.  This will include consideration of introducing or adopting a new regional nursing bank. Reforms would include examining the rates paid for undertaking bank shifts and the frequency of payment following completion of a bank shift.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health who sits on the Department’s working group into spending on Agency staff.
answer_text: The Regional Agency Reduction Group is a HSC Working Group which is working to reduce agency spend across HSC and eliminating the use of ‘Off Contract’ agency. The Group is chaired by a Trust Chief Executive and includes Trust HR and Nursing representatives, Business Service Organisation Procurement and Logistics Service, Trade Union Side and representatives from the Department of Health, NI.This is an extremely complex and multi-faceted issue which can only be addressed through the implementation of a wide range of measures. The most recent meeting was Wednesday 7th September. The group aims to meet every 4 to 6 weeks, with additional ad hoc sub group meetings to look at specific pieces of work, as required.The primary aim of Trust expenditure on agency nurses is to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained at all times for patients and clients. There are many reasons for HSC Trusts employing agency/locum staff at different times. These include, for example, cover for sickness, maternity/paternity leave and increases in beds over the winter monthsTransformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a key priority. The Department has undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC and it is examining all potential options, and the implications, as to how to address the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy if he or his officials have explored options to freeze or cap energy prices.
answer_text: On 21st September the UK Government outlined plans to help cut energy bills and gave further details on the support available for households in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. The Energy Price Guarantee limits the price suppliers can charge customers for units of gas and electricity effective from 1st October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many road traffic accidents were reported at the Westlink/Broadway roundabout at the junction of Donegal Road and Boucher Road, over the last five years.
answer_text: In order to assist my Department in developing road safety related improvement works, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) provides my officials with information on collisions involving personal injury only. Road traffic accidents are reported to PSNI who would be better placed to fully respond to your question. To request this information I suggest you may wish to contact the FOI Department of the PSNI at Lisnasharragh Police Station.Should you have any further questions relating to road improvements in this area, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Department’s working group into spending on Agency staff last met; and (ii) how often the working group meets.
answer_text: The Regional Agency Reduction Group is a HSC Working Group which is working to reduce agency spend across HSC and eliminating the use of ‘Off Contract’ agency. The Group is chaired by a Trust Chief Executive and includes Trust HR and Nursing representatives, Business Service Organisation Procurement and Logistics Service, Trade Union Side and representatives from the Department of Health, NI.This is an extremely complex and multi-faceted issue which can only be addressed through the implementation of a wide range of measures. The most recent meeting was Wednesday 7th September. The group aims to meet every 4 to 6 weeks, with additional ad hoc sub group meetings to look at specific pieces of work, as required.The primary aim of Trust expenditure on agency nurses is to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained at all times for patients and clients. There are many reasons for HSC Trusts employing agency/locum staff at different times. These include, for example, cover for sickness, maternity/paternity leave and increases in beds over the winter monthsTransformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a key priority. The Department has undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC and it is examining all potential options, and the implications, as to how to address the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why it took eight months to publish the September 2021 minutes of the Strategic Planning Group and six months to publish the December 2021 minutes; and (ii) whether he will commit to ensuring that the minutes of future meetings will be made available on the Department’s website within four weeks of meetings taking place.
answer_text: Strategic Planning Group meetings are held on a quarterly basis and minutes of these are agreed at the subsequent meeting. Before uploading onto the Department’s website, the agreed minutes must be reviewed to allow publication to the world at large without breaching obligations under the Data Protection Act 2018 and UK GDPR. Whilst it is not possible to make these minutes available on the Department’s website within 4 weeks, I will ask my officials to ensure that future minutes are published as soon as possible following their agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline the main findings and outcomes of his Department’s planning improvement workshop on 6 June 2022, which considered the Public Accounts Committee report on planning in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what records were kept of the views of the invited participants.
answer_text: The planning improvement workshop afforded those present the opportunity to discuss the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report (as well as other reports), and to consider a future vision for planning improvement in the region. In addition, the recommendations which emerged from the various reports were discussed and prioritised into a number of potential actions. Outcomes from the workshop were circulated to attendees and to local government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline his Department’s understanding of the term sub-threshold, in respect of the application of the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017; and (ii) whether he intends to issue guidance to councils on this matter as part of its Planning Environmental Governance Work Programme.
answer_text: The term ‘sub-threshold’ is not a term which is used in extant legislation, notably the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, nor by the Department’s planning teams.  The legislation sets out applicable thresholds and criteria to inform a planning authority’s decision-making process in relation to which developments are appropriately subject to the requirements of environmental impact assessment (EIA).  The interpretation and application of legislation is a matter for the relevant planning authority in the particular circumstances of a development under consideration.  A series of development management practice notes are being developed as part of the ongoing Environmental Governance Work Programme on key elements of the EIA process, including screening development proposals to determine if EIA requirements should apply, but as this term is not used it will not be a feature of proposed advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the high energy and water demands of data centres, whether his Department is assessing or monitoring the cumulative effects of data centres granted planning permission by councils.
answer_text: My Department is not monitoring the effects of data centres granted planning permission by councils. Any such planning applications are determined by the relevant council planning authority based on the individual circumstances and material considerations in each case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities which members of the managerial leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge, who provide advice to local authorities and public bodies in Northern Ireland on Irish language issues, have qualifications or training in language planning.
answer_text: Please refer to my responses to AQW 3144/22-27 and AQW 3145/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many members of staff make up the managerial leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: The NSMC approved leadership structure for Foras na Gaeilge consists of a CEO who is supported by 3 Directors.Further details of Foras na Gaeilge’s leadership team can be found on the organisations website. https://www.forasnagaeilge.ie/?lang=en

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Foras na Gaeilge have a language-planning unit.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge have a Business & Development team, its primary focus is on language planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what members of the managerial leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge have qualifications or training in language planning.
answer_text: The managerial leadership team hold qualifications and have experience appropriate for the specialised areas in which they work. This includes aspects of language planning such as status planning, corpus planning and acquisition planning. In addition the leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge have access to the services of an external language planner who is a highly-regarded academic at professorial level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what are the job titles of the members of the managerial leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: The Foras na Gaeilge leadership team is comprised of a Chief Executive, a Director of Education Services (who is also the Deputy Chief Executive), a Director of Publishing, Lexicography & Terminology Services and a Director of Corporate Services.Further details of Foras na Gaeilge’s leadership team can be found on the organisations website. https://www.forasnagaeilge.ie/?lang=en

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what members of the managerial leadership team of Foras na Gaeilge offer advice to local authorities and public bodies in Northern Ireland on Irish language issues.
answer_text: All members of the managerial leadership may offer advice to local authorities and public bodies on language issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what representations she, or her officials, have made to the PSNI in relation to the use of plastic bullets and attenuating energy projectiles.
answer_text: The use of attenuating energy projectiles is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will place the legal advice he has received in relation Knock Iveagh in the Assembly library.
answer_text: The legal advice received by my Department in relation to the Knock Iveagh case is subject to legal privilege and therefore it will not be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of PSNI spend on informants in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The breakdown of PSNI spend on informants in each of the last five years is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, and as such the Department does not hold these details. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to having a Mental Health Champion with legislative powers.
answer_text: A number of options were considered in the appointment of a Mental Health Champion (MHC) for Northern Ireland, which included the establishment of a MHC with legislative powers.  It was agreed by the Executive to create the role without legislative powers, subject to review should it become apparent that the role is not working without dedicated legislation.  Proceeding on that basis also allowed for a more timely mental health response to the COVID-19 pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of PSNI spend on (i) plastic bullets; and (ii) attenuating energy projectiles, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The breakdown of PSNI spend on plastic bullets and attenuating energy projectiles in each of the last five years is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, and as such the Department does not hold these details. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the completion of the crisis de-escalation centre.
answer_text: Implementation of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Implementation Plan 2021-31 is ongoing, led by the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency.Action 7 of the Implementation Plan includes a commitment to consider the approach to the provision of safe spaces for de-escalation as part of the standardisation of the Crisis Resolution Home Treatment (CRHT) service. An initial service mapping exercise has commenced for this action and is expected to be completed by April 2023.A Strategic Implementation Group has been set up to oversee the delivery of key activities for the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service as set out in the Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan 2022-23:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/mental-health-strategy-delivery-plan-202223 Full implementation of the actions within the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Implementation Plan is dependent on availability of funding and adequate resources to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what action he has taken to provide Civil Service staff with time off work to attend gynaecological screening.
answer_text: HR Policy 3.08 Special Leave  (Sections 3.16 to 3.18) provides staff with paid time off during the working day to attend medical appointments. (This provision may extend to one day’s paid leave where an appointment is particularly lengthy or considerable travel is required).  This provision would include gynaecological screening.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the completion of the Dual Diagnosis Centre.
answer_text: I am fully aware that many mental health issues can be more complex and difficult to address where a person also has a dependency on alcohol or drugs, or vice versa.The treatment of co-occurring mental health and substance use problems is an issue that was raised prominently during the public consultations on the draft Substance Use Strategy and Mental Health Strategy.  Responses highlighted the need to develop a more holistic treatment approach to provide patient-centred care.  The most effective approach to this issue is therefore one where mental health and substance use services work more effectively together to put the needs of the patient first.  This is why the Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 31 includes an action to work with experts in dual diagnosis to support and build capacity in both mental health and substance use services.  The aim is to embed a “no wrong door” approach that focuses on meeting the needs of the individual in order to deliver better health and social outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Mental Health Champion's annual report will be made public, and if so when.
answer_text: The Mental Health Champion’s Annual Report for 2021/22 will be published on the Mental Health Champion’s website, which is expected to be live in October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to address concerns regarding the inspection and regulation of mental health services.
answer_text: The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 (“the 2003 Order”) constitutes the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and provides general duties and responsibilities of the organisation. The 2003 Order does not provide specific duties in respect of mental health services. The duties of RQIA in relation to Mental Health are set out in Article 86 of the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. My Department is aware of the changing health and social care environment and has carried out a fundamental review of health and social care regulatory framework.  My officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy that includes the principles of regulation, along with the broad scope of services to be regulated and the proposal that the regulator should have wider powers of enforcement etc.   This work has been the first phase of the process before moving on to phase 2. As part of my Department’s response to the pandemic the launch of the consultation on phase 1 was delayed.  Before a draft policy is consulted upon it will be subject to further review taking into account any additional lessons from the pandemic and any other regulatory and quality issues that have emerged over the past few years.It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the medical advice he has received that states children are less infectious with Covid-19 than adults.
answer_text: Advice from officials draws on a range of sources including studies endorsed by the Scientific Advisory Group for Emergencies (SAGE) and opinions from experts across the UK. Medical advice in relation to COVID-19 is approved with input from the Chief Medical Officer, public health advisors and the Chief Scientific Adviser.A range of analyses suggests that children’s susceptibility to infection appears less than adults. There is also strong evidence that children and young people are much less susceptible to severe clinical disease than older people. The current advice in relation to COVID-19 seeks to strike the right balance at this stage of the pandemic between reducing transmission, protecting the vulnerable and mitigating against disruption caused by longer periods of isolation, including disruption to children’s education. All measures and advice cannot be viewed in isolation and must be considered in the context of the changing nature of the pandemic.The current guidance seeks to take account of child welfare, as well as medical and scientific advice, with studies showing that the pandemic has negatively impacted the mental health of children and young people and that school closures cause impairment to the physical and mental health of children. There is clear evidence of the negative educational impact of missing school, particularly for young children. The Department continues to keep all advice and guidance under review, taking account of new and emerging scientific and medical evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what safeguards have been put in place to ensure that the review into licensing examines all options for reform.
answer_text: Section 23 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act 2021 sets out the requirements of the independent review.The University of Stirling was required to provide a methodology as part of the procurement process which showed how it intended to meet those requirements.  The contract is legally binding and all necessary governance procedures are in place to ensure the methodology is followed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when appointing the University of Stirling as the independent reviewer into licensing, what assessment was made of the University’s ability to act independently.
answer_text: The procurement process to appoint the University of Stirling to conduct the review of the licensing system was managed by officials from the Department of Finance’s Construction and Procurement Delivery on behalf of the Department, in line with Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended) and Public Procurement Policy.The University of Stirling will manage a multidisciplinary team from four universities which includes international experts with more than 20 years’ experience in research on alcohol licensing, evaluation of alcohol policy and the impacts of policy on diverse health, social and economic outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many vehicles have had SORN declared in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The Department does not hold details of vehicles which are registered as being off the road (SORN).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many people have been fined, in the last two years, for not having road tax due to not being able to avail of an MOT test.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The Department does not hold details of fines issued by DVLA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline when the serious adverse incident into the case involving the individual from Archer Resourcing began and ended.
To ask the Minister of Health what the process is to ensure that background and employment checks for agency workers being sourced from NI and GB take place.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) whether; and (ii) when a summary of the findings into the case involving the individual from Archer Resourcing will become available.
answer_text: A Serious Adverse Incident (Level 1) was reported by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) to the Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) on 10 December 2021.  The HSCB requested submission of a Significant Event Audit Report by 4 February 2022, however it remains overdue.  BHSCT have advised ‘the findings of the Investigation will be shared with relevant stakeholders on completion of the investigation which is expected by November 2022’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work completed by her Department's Fuel Poverty Unit.
answer_text: The work of the Affordable Warmth and Energy Branch in my Department’s Housing Directorate is ongoing.  The Branch has responsibility for, among other things, fuel poverty policy, developing and delivering Schemes, such as Affordable Warmth and Boiler Replacement, which assist people at risk of fuel poverty, taking forward actions relating to climate change obligations and key Strategies such as Energy, Green Growth and Environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline whether recruitment agencies with headquarters in England are registered with the RQIA.
To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the Housing Executive workers' decision to take strike action for four weeks.
answer_text: I am aware Housing Executive workers are taking strike action and the Housing Executive is continuing to negotiate with Trade Unions. I would urge those involved in these negotiations to work urgently to find an agreed resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many homes in the last five years have availed of cavity wall and roof insulation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed that in the period requested there has been 344 cavity wall installations and 7,092 roof insulation upgrades. With regards to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, between 01 April 2017 and 31 August 2022, 1,933 homes have availed of cavity wall insulation and 5,660 homes have availed of loft insulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many senior members of staff in the Housing Executive were awarded a pay rise in the last two years; and (ii) what was the average amount of the pay rise.
answer_text: Details relating to senior staff in the Housing Executive and their salaries are published in the Housing Executive’s Annual Reports, which are available on its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what input does she have into pay awards for Housing Executive workers.
answer_text: I have no input into the negotiations or agreement of the pay awards for Housing Executive Workers.I will be required to approve the pay remits in line with DoF guidance when they have been submitted by the Housing Executive on the basis of an agreed settlement.I would urge those involved in negotiations to work urgently to find an agreed resolution

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many staff at the Housing Executive were awarded emergency COVID-19 payments.
answer_text: No Housing Executive staff were awarded emergency COVID-19 payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities who won the tender for housing women experiencing violence.
answer_text: The Housing Executive completed a Competitive Selection Exercise for the support provision element of the new Supporting People service for Women Experiencing Chronic Homelessness in the Greater Belfast area, which identified a preferred applicant.   Due to commercial sensitivity and ongoing negotiations external to the Housing Executive, I am unable to confirm the name of the preferred applicant at this time.    To clarify, this selection exercise was not a tender and was for Women Experiencing Chronic Homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what conversations she or her officials have had with energy companies in the last 12 months regarding debt recovery.
answer_text: Neither I nor my officials have had conversations with any energy companies in the last 12 months regarding debt recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why there is an underspend in Year 1 of the Mental Health Strategy.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 (MHS) was published in June 2021, alongside a 10 year Funding Plan. The Funding Plan outlined that £1.2bn is needed to fully implement the Strategy over the 10 year period. Within the Funding Plan, indicative spend across each year of the MHS is outlined. The investment required to deliver the Strategy is in addition to existing expenditure in mental health services. While the Executive’s Draft Budget 2022-25 made provision to deliver the MHS over the budget period, no budget was subsequently agreed and therefore no additional funding has been provided to my Department.  My Department has been using its limited existing resources to take forward a range of critical preparatory activities alongside work to complete key enabling actions that were started in 2021/22.   This funding is allowing the continuation of existing projects and other preparatory work, however it is not sufficient to take forward full implementation of other Mental Health Strategy actions.  The Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan for 2022/23 outlines the implementation work that can be taken forward within the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 81/22-27, to outline what specific work has taken place to develop a women’s only hostel service in Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive completed a Competitive Selection Exercise for the new Supporting People service for Women Experiencing Chronic Homelessness in the Greater Belfast area, which identified a preferred applicant. Commercial negotiations external to the Housing Executive are ongoing and both the Housing Executive and my Department are closely monitoring progress.  The Housing Executive has increased peripatetic places in the community for women experiencing chronic homelessness, and are considering further options based on the specific support needs of women experiencing homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when a new fuel poverty strategy will be implemented.
answer_text: Officials are scoping the development of a co-designed Fuel Poverty Strategy which will align with key Executive strategies including Energy and Green Growth. This will ensure a just transition to net zero in line with climate change obligations and that current inequalities are addressed and further inequalities are not created.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Public Accounts Committee report on Planning in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report which was published on 22 March 2022 and produced a wide range of recommendations and actions in relation to improving the planning system in the North, this report is being carefully considered. In response to the report my officials have been working collaboratively with local government and planning stakeholders to bring forward a programme of work to deliver improvements to the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to the publication of the 2022 Public Accounts Committee report, Planning in Northern Ireland, (i) to outline his key priorities to address issues raised in the report; and (ii) to set out the timeframe to implement changes.
answer_text: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report made a number of recommendations in relation to improving the planning system in the North. As a result, my Department has been engaging with stakeholders to assess how best to prioritise the issues raised and deliver improvements.My officials will continue to work collaboratively with councils going forward to agree the timeframes for implementation and deliver improvements to the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure who has been appointed to independently lead the review of the planning system, as recommended in the 2022 Public Accounts Committee report Planning in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report, published on 22 March 2022, has produced a number of recommendations in relation to improving the planning system in the North. In response to the report my officials have been working collaboratively with local government and planning stakeholders to bring forward a programme of work to deliver improvements to the planning system. This will include options for an independently led Regional Commission for planning improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what ecological or biodiversity surveys were undertaken on the wild flower meadow at Magheralin Church before his Department granted an award of £100,000 to turn it into a pollinator garden; and how this will improve the current nature conservation value of the site.
answer_text: The project at Magheralin Church was funded under the Platinum Jubilee Pollinator Garden Award and this award was delivered on behalf of the Department by Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful (KNIB).  The purpose of the Platinum Jubilee Pollinator Garden Award was to support a community group or partnership to:Create a bespoke Pollinator Garden as a legacy of the 2022 Platinum JubileeTransform an underused space into a vibrant and valued community space by adding habitats and increasing access to visitors.Increase the biodiversity value of an area by planting for pollinators.Improve the health and wellbeing of a community and aid biodiversity recovery by helping to improve and/or maintain the quality of a community space.Increase civic pride and improve the quality of local environment through the creation of a quality shared space.Improve access to wildlife by improving infrastructure, installing bespoke features and creating or improving habitats.Applications were considered and assessed by a panel of three judges.  The judging panel also had considerable expertise and experience in the fields of biodiversity and conservation.  The judging panel did not complete a specific ecological or biodiversity survey of the site however, a visual assessment of the site was carried out by the judging panel on the 18 May 2022.  Through the site visit, the judging panel identified a range of issues which helped to highlight the following issues:In recent years the grass had been cut but not lifted.  This increased the fertility of the grass in a way that was not conducive to the creation of a species-rich meadow;There was no standing or fallen deadwood, the removal of which could have had a negative impact in habitats for biodiversity;As the field had been left untended for some time, there was a variety of plant species growing in the field (colonising), but none that required an external survey.  For example, mixed grasses which included various common native flowers; andThe field was in the early stages of colonisation by Grey willow, which, left unchecked, could take over the meadow area. As a result of this investment a dedicated area will be set aside for improvement and will become a shared accessible space for the whole community, where they can become immersed in nature and learn more about the importance of pollinators.  The proposed planting will increase plant species and plant diversity.  New tree planting will provide sources of nectar and pollen for pollinators from spring through to autumn and will also offer new nesting spaces for pollinating insects, thus improving the nature conservation value of the site.  The remainder of the site will be untouched, but will be managed going forward.Learning to value pollinators and nature, and learning to care about pollinators, requires time spent outdoors in suitable environments, this project helps to make that happen.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what interim arrangements are in place for car owners who are unable to tax their cars because their MOT certificate has expired and are waiting for an MOT test appointment.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many carers are only in receipt of Carers Allowance.
answer_text: As at February 2022 there were 29,880 people receiving Carers Allowance who were not in receipt of Jobseeker’s Allowance, Employment Support Allowance, Income Support, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many carers, in receipt of Carers Allowance, do not live with the person they care for.
answer_text: The information requested is not available to the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many carers receive Universal Credit and the Carer Element; and (ii) how many of these carers also receive Carer's Allowance.
answer_text: The Universal Credit carer element is paid at household level. In February 2022 there were 14,640 households receiving Universal Credit with a carer element.		In February 2022 there were 9,730 recipients of Carer’s Allowance who were part of a UC household with a carer element.		A person claiming carer’s element in Universal Credit may not automatically be in receipt of or entitled to Carers Allowance, as earnings may be considered to determine eligibility for Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of media reports that striking teachers could have their pay deducted for taking industrial action.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC).  Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and the Department of Education.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the five main teaching unions (INTO, UTU, NAHT, NASUWT and NEU).I understand that TNC Management Side has undertaken an assessment of the industrial action and determined that participating in the NASUWT’s action amounts to a breach of teachers’ contractual duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many carers are in receipt of Carer's Allowance and Universal Credit, but without the Carer Element.
answer_text: In February 2022, 1,250 Carers Allowance recipients were on Universal Credit but not getting carers element.My department is currently working to identify those cases where a person is in receipt of carer’s allowance without the carer element having been applied for within Universal Credit. Officials are working to update these cases to ensure those customers receive the full support they are entitled to, including any underpayments due.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many carers are in receipt of Carer's Allowance and the Carer Premium.
answer_text: As at February 2022, the date of the most recent published statistics, there are 31,750* people in receipt of Carers Allowance who also receive a Carers Premium in either Income Support or Pension Credit or a Carers Element in Universal Credit.  The Department does not hold information on the number of people in receipt of Carers Allowance who also receive a Carers Premium in either Jobseekers Allowance or Employment Support Allowance.  *Figure is rounded to the nearest 10

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many carers receive Pension Credit and the Carer Addition.
answer_text: As at February 2022, the date of the most recent published statistics, there are 10,430* Pension Credit claimants in in receipt of the Carers Premium*Figure is rounded to the nearest 10

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when all Health and Social Care Trusts will get access to perinatal services.
answer_text: Implementation of the specialist perinatal mental health services is being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams across the region.  Both the Belfast Trust and the South Eastern Trust are operational and offering a full consultancy service while in the remaining Trusts, recruitment is advancing with the aim of commencing full service delivery as soon as possible.  In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region, including engagement with each of the Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people are being treated for a functional neurological disorder.
answer_text: Information on the number of people who are being treated for a functional neurological disorder is not available centrally in the Department.  The following information has been provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on functional neurological disorders (FND). Belfast HSC Trust advised that it does not have a specific consultant functional neurological disorder service.Northern HSC Trust advised that their neurology service is outpatient based, and as outpatients are not coded for specific conditions, they could not provide information requested.South Eastern HSC Trust advised that they have no neurologist  who sub-specialises in functional neurological disorder, and have no functional neurological disorder specialist clinic. Southern and Western HSC Trusts were contacted for input but were unable to provide a response within the required timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what meetings his Department has had on providing services and treatment to people who have a functional neurological disorder.
answer_text: The former Director of Mental Health Disability and Older People within my Department met with a Consultant Stroke Physician at the Belfast HSC Trust on 16th June 2021 to specifically discuss the work of a local community organisation, FND matters.  In a wider capacity, the Regional Review of Neurology Services is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years. Functional Neurology is represented by a psychologist based in the Belfast Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what services are being put in place for former patients of Dr. Watt who have a functional neurological disorder.
answer_text: Each former patient of Dr Watt, including those with a functional neurological disorder, was informed by the clinician who saw them about changes to their diagnoses, management plan or treatment during their clinical review process. Each review appointment was supported by a form completed by a consultant neurologist answering specific questions about each individual’s care, which were based on recommendations by the Royal College of Physicians, and the results were recorded on a database.The Neurology Review Team, which is tasked with the identification of the optimal configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years, has many work streams. Consultant neurologists, and all other professionals involved in the care of patients with neurological conditions are involved with the structuring of future plans for neurology services within Northern Ireland.  This is a regional approach which will ensure that the appropriate services are available in the appropriate areas.  This team approach will also ensure that the entire neurology patient population is considered and appropriate pathways for patients are developed in line with need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many meetings she, or her departmental officials, have had with Belfast City Council, Clonard Residents Association and Intercomm about the former Mackies site in west Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has had numerous meetings with Belfast City Council and one meeting with the Clonard Residents Association, and Participation and Practice of Rights about the former Mackie’s site in west Belfast.  No meetings have taken place with Intercomm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has for social housing on the former Mackies site in west Belfast.
answer_text: I will consider any sites that come forward, particularly in areas where they are needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the Belfast City Council's decision to support the building of the Forth Meadow Greenway on the former Mackies site in west Belfast; and whether she will challenge this decision.
answer_text: I have noted the decision of Belfast City Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many meetings she, or her departmental officials, have held with interested parties about the development of social housing on the former Mackies site in west Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has held three meetings with interested parties about the development of social housing on the former Mackie’s site in west Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the number of teenagers that have been fined on the Glider for not having purchased a ticket, since the Glider service started.
answer_text: The enforcement of fines on the Glider system is the responsibility of Translink. To obtain a breakdown of the numbers of passengers by age who use the Glider service, you should direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink record details of the ethnic background of people who have received fines on the Glider.
answer_text: The enforcement of fines on the Glider service and the statistics around those fines are the responsibility of Translink in its role as our public transport operator. To obtain the information you require, you should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial cost of an adjourned tribunal for (i) Personal Independence Payments; and (ii) Disability Living Allowance.
answer_text: The average cost of an adjourned tribunal for Personal Independence Payments is £373.64.		The average cost of an adjourned tribunal for Disability Living Allowance is £373.64.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what criteria her Department is using to extend Disability Living Allowance awards for people with Down syndrome.
answer_text: All awards are based on the individuals needs arising from a health condition or disability and the likelihood of those needs changing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what criteria is used by her Department to decide to extend a Disability Living Allowance award for a person with Down syndrome up until the age of eligibility for a Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: All awards are based on the individuals needs arising from a health condition or disability.  		Where the evidence shows that a child’s needs are unlikely to change, then an award will be made to their 16th birthday, at which stage they are required to claim Personal Independence Payment.  Award processes are subject to ongoing review and enhancement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people with Down syndrome are waiting for a mandatory reconsideration or an appeal for Personal Independence Payments or Disability Living Allowance.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Minister will uphold the previous Minister’s decision to refuse planning permission for the Hightown Incinerator.
answer_text: A final decision notice refusing this application was issued by my Department on 1 April 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people with Down syndrome have successfully overturned the original decision on (i) Personal Independence Payments; and (ii) Disability Living Allowance, at Mandatory Reconsideration stage, over the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average cost to bring a (i) Personal Independence Payments; and (ii) Disability Living Allowance case to a tribunal hearing.
answer_text: The average cost of a Personal Independence Payment appeal for the period 1 January 2021 - 31 December 2021 was £511.	The average cost of a Disability Living Allowance appeal for the period 1 January 2021 - 31 December 2021 was £511.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people with Down Syndrome have been successful at a (i) Personal Independence Payments; and (ii) Disability Living Allowance tribunal hearing, over the last five years.
answer_text: The Appeals Service is unable to provide this data as Personal Independence Payment and Disability Living Allowance appeals are not broken down by nature of disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people with Down Syndrome have went through a mandatory reconsideration for Personal Independence Payments and Disability Living Allowance over the last five years.
answer_text: In the five years up to February 2022, the date of the latest published figures, fewer than 10 individuals with Down’s Syndrome recorded as the main disabling condition have gone through the Mandatory Reconsideration process.The Department does hold equivalent information relating to Disability Living Allowance Mandatory Reconsiderations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work she has commenced to ensure people with Down Syndrome are awarded Disability Living Allowance and Personal Independence Payment for life.
answer_text: Measures in each benefit area are subject to ongoing review and enhancement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many legal applications have been submitted by former patients of Dr. Watt against the Department or the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that 391 claims (as at 30th June 2022), have been submitted against the Trust, in relation to the neurology recall. It is important to highlight that claims in respect Dr Watt’s private patients fall outside the remit of the Department and the Belfast Trust.  Therefore information on the number of claims for those patients within a Private Patient setting is not available.No claims have been submitted against the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the current legislation allows companies to employ agency workers while their employees are engaging in industrial action.
answer_text: Regulation 7 of the Conduct of Employment Agencies and Employment Businesses Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 prohibits the provision of temporary workers to perform the duties normally performed by a worker who is taking part in a strike or other industrial action.This restriction does not apply if strike action is unofficial or where the employing business which is providing the temporary worker does not know about the industrial action and has no reasonable grounds for knowing about the industrial action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the next grouping of people on legacy benefits are to be transferred on to Universal Credit.
answer_text: The final stage of Universal Credit (UC) implementation, known as Move to UC, involves the managed migration of those who remain in receipt of the legacy benefits being replaced by Universal Credit. A start date for Move to UC in the North has not yet been decided and planning is underway.  Income Support, Income Based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Income Related Employment & Support Allowance, Housing Benefit (Rental), tax credits 	 Income Support, Income Based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Income Related Employment & Support Allowance, Housing Benefit (Rental), tax credits

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1613/22-27, what proportion of the money was spent on private or independent sector organisations.
answer_text: A total investment of £90m non-recurrent funding was made available to the HSC in 2021/22 to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services.  Just over £58m of this was spent on private or independent sector organisations. The £58m delivered approximately 116,000 Outpatient assessments, diagnostic tests and IP/DC procedures in 2021/22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the recommendations in the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry will be implemented.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Inquiry Panel’s report.  It is extensive and detailed.  I am committed to ensuring it receives the careful consideration it deserves. I will provide a full response to the Report’s recommendations, as soon as is practicable. I have asked the Department of Health’s Permanent Secretary to lead a steering group to take forward this work as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Inquiry Panel’s report.  It is extensive and detailed.  I am committed to ensuring it receives the careful consideration it deserves. I will provide a full response to the Report’s recommendations, as soon as is practicable. I have asked the Department of Health’s Permanent Secretary to lead a steering group to take forward this work as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered updating or amending his Department's and the Health and Social Care Trusts' whistleblowing policies.
answer_text: NICS policy covers my Department and is currently under review.  The completion of the NICS policy review will be informed by the outcome of the DAERA independent review which is ongoing.HSC policy has been reviewed.  A public consultation on a draft regional whistleblowing Health and Social Care framework and model policy is underway. The consultation is expected to end on 26 August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, since 2015, what was the average amount paid by individuals who had to pay the bedroom tax.
answer_text: The Housing Executive does not readily hold or record the figures of the average amount paid by individuals who had to pay the “bedroom tax” as a result of not being entitled to a mitigation payment. However, based on research that was carried out between October and December 2018 with a sample of Housing Executive tenants who were of working age and under-occupying their home, the average shortfall was £13.45 a week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1140/22-27, how can the route become part of the public transport network.
answer_text: For the Colin Connect, Blacks Road Park & Ride and Divis Mountain service to become part of the public transport network, Translink would need to seek approval from the Department. In considering such a request, the Department would take a number of factors into account, including the impact on Translink’s financial position and any subsequent impact on the subsidy provided to Translink by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many individuals had to pay the bedroom tax from 2015 to 2020.
answer_text: In the period 1 April 2017 to 31 March 2020 a total of 306 households had their “bedroom tax” mitigation payments stopped.I believe that stopping mitigation payments was wrong and this is why I introduced legislation to close this loophole in the “bedroom tax” mitigation scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the vacant land between 36 and 42 Mooreland was acquired by the GAA for the Casement Park redevelopment.
answer_text: This piece of land was acquired by the GAA for the Casement Park Development. This information is in the public domain as part of the UCGAA’s Planning Application documentation in 2017.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any human rights concerns about the continued use of spit and bite guards.
answer_text: Following the introduction of the PSNI’s use of Spit and Bite Guards, the Member will wish to note that I discussed this issue at my regular stocktakes with the Chief Constable.  While I recognise that the PSNI’s decision to continue to use Spit and Bite Guards is a sensitive area, I understand that the PSNI has developed extensive training and guidance for police officers in accordance with advice received from the Policing Board’s Human Rights Advisor and the Police Ombudsman’s Office.However, the Member will also wish to note that the PSNI’s decision to continue to use Spit and Bite Guards is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what correspondence she has had with the PSNI in relation to the use of spit and bite guards.
answer_text: I have not corresponded with the Police Service of Northern Ireland on the use of Spit and Bite Guards.  The use of Spit and Bite Guards is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the NIPB.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what feasibility work his Department has carried out on a redress scheme for former patients of Dr. Watt.
answer_text: My Department initiated work in 2019 to develop options for a streamlined redress process for affected neurology patients.  This work identified that the option of a neurology redress scheme would take a significant amount of time to set up, including the requirement for a public consultation and legislation in advance of implementation.  Examinations of other redress schemes active at that time found that a redress scheme would not be appropriate for complex cases.  Therefore it was concluded a redress scheme would not represent the best approach for neurology patients and families. Alternative options for neurology compensation were considered, including the potential to establish an Early Resolution Scheme.  This work was paused in March 2020 due to the Covid-19 pandemic, and recommenced in May 2021.The conclusion of this work was that it is not possible to develop a streamlined early resolution scheme through which all neurology recall cases could proceed, and that due to the legal and medical complexities of neurology cases, each claim would require assessment on its own individual merits in order to ensure a just outcome for the patients and families affected.  To deliver the best outcome for neurology patients and families, a streamlined approach has been developed, with a view to processing claims as quickly and easily as possible through a dedicated Neurology Recall Legal Team in the Directorate of Legal Services, acting on behalf of the Belfast Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what involvement departmental and Health and Social Care officials have had with the UK Government's future planning for pandemics.
answer_text: DHSC and Cabinet Office co-chair the UK Pandemic Disease Capabilities Board (PDCB), which includes membership from across Whitehall Departments and the Devolved Administrations to ensure coherence in the approach to future pandemic preparedness across the four nations. While my Department remains at the forefront in planning for the management of the health effects of a severe pandemic, building resilience and ensuring preparedness requires a coordinated approach across all Executive Departments and agencies.  My officials along with TEO have jointly established a PDCB NI sub-group to focus on pandemic preparations in NI, and to contribute to the overall UKG planning. Other Departments will be invited to nominate a representative to this group in due course and resource will need to be assigned to preparations across the NICS. In addition, my officials are working with DHSC to undertake a UK-wide fundamental review of the clinical countermeasures that we need ready access to in event of a future pandemic or emerging infectious disease. This review factors in lessons learnt from COVID-19 alongside engagement with scientific and epidemiological experts to consider future risks the UK might face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the membership of the Planning for Future Pandemic Advisory Board.
answer_text: DHSC and Cabinet Office co-chair the UK Pandemic Disease Capabilities Board (PDCB), which includes membership from across Whitehall Departments and the Devolved Administrations to ensure coherence in the approach to future pandemic preparedness across the four nations. While my Department remains at the forefront in planning for the management of the health effects of a severe pandemic, building resilience and ensuring preparedness requires a coordinated approach across all Executive Departments and agencies.  My officials along with TEO have jointly established a PDCB NI sub-group to focus on pandemic preparations in NI, and to contribute to the overall UKG planning. Other Departments will be invited to nominate a representative to this group in due course and resource will need to be assigned to preparations across the NICS. In addition, my officials are working with DHSC to undertake a UK-wide fundamental review of the clinical countermeasures that we need ready access to in event of a future pandemic or emerging infectious disease. This review factors in lessons learnt from COVID-19 alongside engagement with scientific and epidemiological experts to consider future risks the UK might face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the membership of the Transformation Advisory Board as of May 2022.
answer_text: The last meeting of the Transformation Advisory Board (TAB) occurred on 28 March 2022.  The Board is now due to be reviewed as detailed in its Terms of Reference.  I thanked members for their support, which included representatives from service user groups, trade unions, the community and voluntary sector and Northern Ireland experts from within the fields of economics, commerce and communications.The membership as of May 2022 was:Robin Swann (Minister of Health, Chair);Peter May (Permanent Secretary, Department of Health);Maria Somerville (User Representative);Brian O’Hagan (User Representative);Seamus McAleavey (Community and Voluntary Sector Representative);John Patrick Clayton (Trade Union representative);Ann McGregor (Northern Ireland Chamber of Commerce); andNick Garbutt (Scope).I have indicated that I am establishing an Advisory Board for the next stage of Health Service Improvement and Transformation, including helping shape the future design plan for the reconfiguration of health services that I have asked be taken forward as a priority.  It is essential therefore, that membership will include, as has TAB, clinical leaders as well as voluntary community sector, service users and representative bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hours in total private health bodies have availed of Belfast Health and Social Care facilities in the last five years.
answer_text: The requested information is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the extra £90 million allocated to address waiting lists was spent.
answer_text: A total investment of £90m non-recurrent funding was made available to the HSC in 2021/22 to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services.  This funding was used to secure additional capacity, both in-house and in the independent sector, across a range of elective specialties. This funding was targeted at those patients with the highest clinical priority. The £90m delivered approximately 270,000 Outpatient/AHP assessments, diagnostic tests and IP/DC procedures in 2021/22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients saw a lactation consultant, in each of the last five years, broken down by Belfast constituency.
answer_text: The requested information is not held centrally within my Department, so Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) were approached. BHSCT have informed us that this information is not readily available. They have informed us that the term ‘lactation consultant’ is unprotected and therefore, anyone can claim to be a lactation consultant. Whilst there are a number of International Board Certified Lactation Consultants (IBCLC’s) working within BHSCT, not all of the specialist breastfeeding team with the skills to support breastfeeding, are IBCLC certified. There are also a number of private IBCLC’S working within the BHSCT areas, but there is no readily available data of how many women are supported by them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many meetings he has held about a neurology redress scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will waive the three year rule on litigation to allow recalled neurology patients to seek redress.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why The Gathering is not included in his Department's Planning Improvement Workshop, which is considering the recommendations of the Public Account Committee's report into Planning in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a process of engagement, including meetings and workshops, about improving the planning system following the Public Accounts Committee’s report into Planning. This has included engagement with representatives from industry, the environment and community sectors and local government. It is not possible to have all the many planning stakeholders at these events, but my officials would be happy to meet with The Gathering as part of this process to discuss the recommendations of the Public Accounts Committee's report and how best to improve planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work her Department has engaged in to ensure the provision of Autism training in Education has included people with Autism.
answer_text: The Enhanced Autism Training Framework, has been developed in partnership by the Education Authority Autism Advisory and Intervention Service (EA) and Middletown Centre for Autism (MCA), in response to the NI Assembly motion (February 2020). The training framework has been developed in conjunction with key stakeholders, including autistic young people, who also deliver a part of the EA AAIS programme on a peer to peer basis. Officials are currently working with the EA and MCA to review the training provision offer for the 2022/23 Academic Year to ensure that it meets current and emerging needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 77/22-27, to outline who is responsible for implementing price controls on food items.
answer_text: Presently no Northern Ireland Department has statutory powers to implement price controls on food items.
