question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list organisations and groups that have been contacted in relation to the Chartered Institute of Housing’s survey on the private rented sector.
answer_text: The Chartered Institute of Housing (CIH) is undertaking research on the Department’s behalf to support its responsibilities under Section 7 of The Private Tenancies Act 2022.  As part of this research CIH will engage with a wide range of stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work carried out to bring the services provided by Capita in-house.
answer_text: Since taking up my position as Minister for Communities in January 2020, I have made clear my decision to reverse social security service privatisation. A number of elements of existing service delivery have been amended as a result of my policy decision, specifically:Since March 2021, where sufficient evidence is available to complete assessment, PIP award reviews have been completed in-house, that initiative reducing referrals to the provider by circa 25%. In August 2021 the Department in-housed the Independent Audit function previously undertaken by contracted providers.  That initiative has improved the independence of the function, governance and internal systems of control and assurance by holding providers to account for quality and customer service.I have instructed Officials to continue to engage with DoH to develop the necessary partnership approach to bring all services in-house as soon as practicably possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the new bus route that was piloted in 2021 to Divis and the Black Mountain.
answer_text: The bus route between the Colin Connect, Blacks Road Park & Ride and Divis Mountain did not form part of the public transport network as specified in the public service agreement between my Department and Translink. As it was a contracted arrangement between the National Trust and Translink, you should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on work completed by Translink to provide a permanent bus to Divis and the Black Mountain.
answer_text: The bus route between the Colin Connect, Blacks Road Park & Ride and Divis Mountain did not form part of the public transport network as specified in the public service agreement between my Department and Translink. As it was a contracted arrangement between the National Trust and Translink, you should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the finding of the Royal College of Nursing’s Last Shift Survey that 83 per cent of nursing staff said the numbers of nursing staff on shift was not sufficient to safely and effectively meet all the needs and dependency of the patients and service users.
answer_text: I confirm that my Department has received a copy of the UK wide Royal College of Nursing’s Last Shift Survey Report and I acknowledge the challenges reported by 83% of staff replies. In response to regional staffing pressures, in autumn 2021 I commissioned the Nursing and Midwifery Retention Initiative Report, a regional workforce study. I have approved the recommendations of this report and my Department are addressing these issues in line with both Nursing and Midwifery Task Group and Delivering Care policy. Since January 2020, the Northern Ireland Executive have committed an additional investment of £60m for safe and effective staffing.  To date a total of £25 million has been allocated, delivering approximately 360 whole time equivalent nursing and midwifery posts.  I remain committed to the implementation of the Delivering Care Policy.My commitment to introducing a Safe Staffing Bill early in the new Assembly mandate remains. Despite the current uncertainties, work is continuing in partnership with the co-design group and interested stakeholders to ensure that the policy proposals for the Bill are fully developed and agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work is ongoing to ensure a Mother and Baby Unit is in operation here.
answer_text: Implementation of the specialist perinatal mental health services is being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams. In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region, including engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much funding was provided by her Department or the Education Authority for the Platinum Jubilee children’s book to be made available to primary schools.
answer_text: No funding was provided by my Department or the Education Authority towards the recently distributed commemoration book entitled ‘Queen Elizabeth – A Platinum Jubilee Celebration’.  The book was developed, printed and distributed by DK Books on behalf of the Department for Education in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any discussions have taken place between her Department and officials from the Executive Office regarding funding for a violence against women and girls strategy.
answer_text: The Executive Office lead on the development of the Violence Against Women and Girls (VAWG) Strategy and therefore on any related bid for funding.  However, since advocating for the introduction of a VAWG strategy, I noted that it would require funding in order to support implementation. My officials do however work very closely with colleagues in the Executive Office, given the linkages with the work we jointly lead on in relation to domestic and sexual abuse. The Department of Justice and the Department of Health jointly launched a Call for Views with the Executive Office in January of this year to help develop two important strategies, one to tackle domestic and sexual abuse and one to tackle violence against women and girls.  This was an important and positive step forward for all victims of domestic and sexual abuse and those subject to violent and abusive behaviours.  As part of the development of the new strategies, consideration will need to be given to associated funding, and has been noted as part of wider discussions between officials in both Departments.  A cross Departmental bid for funding the new domestic and sexual abuse strategy was made as part of the draft 2022 – 25 budget process, which cannot be approved at present.  My officials will continue to liaise with their Executive Office colleagues throughout the development of both strategies, to ensure parallels and commonalities are addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on safe staffing legislation.
answer_text: As part of the 2020 Framework Agreement, I made the commitment to develop and introduce robust Safe Staffing legislation with the agreement of TUS, HSC Employers and our Chief Professional Officers. A Safe Staffing Framework Delivery Group was established in early 2020 comprising of Department of Health (DoH), Health & Social Care Trust (HSCT) and Trade Union (TUS) colleagues to consider all aspects of a Safe Staffing Programme including the development of legislation. While the onset of the pandemic delayed progress during 2020, a Bill Team has been established and since June 2021, they have regularly met with stakeholders to discuss and develop all aspects of the legislation and engaged with Scottish and Welsh counterparts to understand the approach taken to the issue in those jurisdictions. Policy position papers from TUS (jointly RCN, UNISON and NIPSA) and BMA have recently been received and are being considered by the Department. Despite the current uncertainties, the Department’s commitment to introducing a Bill early in the new Assembly mandate remains.  Policy development work is ongoing and will continue in partnership with the co-design group to ensure that the policy proposals for the Bill are fully developed and agreed prior to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many times the review group into eligibility for free school meals has met over the last twelve months.
answer_text: The Department commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria in November 2021  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place later in the year.  A Key Stakeholder Forum has been established to provide advice on eligibility criteria and research on FSM & UG provision including in other jurisdictions and provide the voice of those living through/experiencing poverty/hardship. The review group is an internal departmental group and has met with the key stakeholder group in December 2021 and it is planned that another meeting will take place in the near future. A Cross-departmental group supports the development of the Options Paper and comprises representatives from the Department for Economy (DfE), Department for Communities (DfC), Department of Health (DoH), Department for Environment, agriculture and Rural Affairs and the Education Authority (EA). The Department has also met with this group and internal departmental group as well as the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People. The input of key stakeholders and children and young people is vital to ensure that the views of all interested parties are considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Welfare Mitigation Review.
answer_text: The independent review of welfare mitigations has been completed and a final report has been submitted to the Department. This will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work his Department has completed on a women’s employment strategy.
answer_text: Whilst the Department for Communities has policy responsibility for employability, my department has a clear contribution to make, particularly through our skills and education remit. Our recently published Skills Strategy for Northern Ireland - Skills for a 10X Economy - has led to the establishment of the Northern Ireland Skills Council and a Skills Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion Sub-Committee. The sub-committee is currently considering issues of access and participation in skills relating to further and higher education, which may impact on underrepresented groups, including gender. The evidence and actions that will flow from this work will help more females overcome barriers to employment and help them increase their social mobility through skills.Addressing the skills imbalances and specifically the under-representation of women in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) employment is at the heart of my department’s vision for a 10X economy and is a key priority for Northern Ireland in order to deliver the talent required to grow the economy. The Women in STEM Action Plan, codesigned by government and industry was published in March this year. The activities underpinning the action plan are creating an environment that encourages and supports more women to reach their full STEM potential in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he has taken to meet Royal College of Nursing's demand for a 12.5 per cent increase in pay.
answer_text: Annual pay uplifts for all staff groups within Agenda for Change are recommended the UK-wide NHS Pay Review Body (NHSPRB). The NHSPRB considers evidence from all interested parties, including Government and each representative organisation, such as the RCN, when considering their recommendations.These recommendations are expected in the coming weeks and I do not intend to pre-empt them.I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service. However, I must stress, that our pay award decisions can also only be taken in the context of a wider public sector pay policy set by the Department of Finance. Any pay increase will also require to be fully funded by the Executive, given the financial outlook facing my Department in 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Royal College of Nursing's declaration of a trade dispute.
answer_text: My Department and I acknowledge the notification from the Royal College of Nursing (RCN). As RCN will be aware, my Department is awaiting recommendations on 2022/23 pay increases from the UK-wide NHS Pay Review Body. The recommendations are expected in the coming weeks.My Department accepted, in full, the recommendations of the Review Body in 2021/22 for a 3% award, maintaining pay parity with England and Wales. In addition to this, I allocated £29m to support further non-consolidated pay awards for HSC staff, ranging from 1.5% for the lowest paid, to 0.5% to the highest paid – this additional award is currently being processed by the Regional Payroll team.I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work her Department has completed to ensure that the new school builds, announced in the Major Capital Works Programme, will be net zero carbon buildings.
answer_text: The Education Authority is currently procuring Integrated Consultancy Teams to lead the design of the recently announced Major Capital Projects. Following a review of the standards incorporated in the tender documents I can confirm the revised specification for new school builds will be for Net Zero Carbon buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what powers the Utility Regulator has to refuse increased tariffs on energy prices.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator’s (UR) regulatory processes ensure that what consumers pay reflects the cost of providing electricity or gas.  The UR does not have any statutory powers to require energy companies to charge less than it costs them to supply gas and electricity to homes and businesses.The UR monitors wholesale prices to ensure they are fairly reflected in consumer’s bills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what pre-market engagement took place before commencing procurement for the new builds announced in the Major Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: On 21 March 2022 I announced 28 post primary schools that are to advance in planning under the Major Capital Works Programme. The Education Authority commenced the pre-qualification process to appoint Integrated Consultancy Teams for each project. As the procurement exercise is largely similar to others which have been undertaken in recent years, it was considered that pre-market engagement was not necessary on this occasion. Indeed, the decision to proceed without pre market engagement was taken on the basis that the procurement opportunity would provide a welcome opportunity for the wider construction industry in the current challenging times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many times in the last five years the Utility Regulator has prevented an increase to tariffs by energy companies.
answer_text: In the past 5 years, there have been 2 tariff submissions by energy companies that have not resulted in an increase to tariffs following a review by the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment she has made in relation to the number of organisations bidding for Major Capital Works projects; and what steps her Department can take to encourage greater diversity among bidders.
answer_text: The Education Authority has commenced the prequalification stage of the procurement of Integrated Consultancy Teams (ICT) for the recently announced Major Capital projects. In addition to providing much-needed, enhanced school facilities for our young people and teachers, one of the driving forces behind this process has been my Department’s determination to get this work issued to the marketplace to secure employment within the industry in the current and future challenging times.The prequalification process is still live and as such, there is no firm indication of how many economic operators may decide to submit bids. However, I am satisfied that we are fortunate to have a healthy supply of suitably qualified and experienced professional practices in the local market which are able to undertake work of this nature.While my Department is already well-supplied in this field, I am always keen to ensure this market is open to as many economic operators as possible. This will ensure the professional experience and economic benefits are shared more widely within the industry.My Departmental officials, working in conjunction with their EA colleagues, have broken the 28 announced school projects into four tranches of seven projects rather than overloading the market with all 28 projects at one time. The projects in each tranche range in scope and value and I hope this will encourage as many operators as possible to become engaged in the bidding process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how future spending will be impacted by the Climate Change (No. 2) Bill, which is waiting Royal Assent.
answer_text: There is a commitment within the Climate Change Bill to publish a Climate Action Plan (CAP) in 2023, setting out how interim targets and the net zero target will be achieved by 2050. This cross cutting plan and its subsequent implementation is critical for the North in terms of addressing climate change. The costs associated with implementation of the bill will be clarified once the CAP is published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the beef carbon reduction measure will be monitored to ensure that adverse environmental behaviours are not incentivised, particularly on High Nature Value land.
answer_text: Responses received through the formal consultation process on the Future Agricultural Policy Proposals and associated Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report and Habitats Regulation Assessment, provided recommendations on how environmental assessments can be integrated into the policy design.			Key recommendations received by stakeholders included: 	•	Environmental assessments should be integrated into policy development; and	•	A commitment to monitor and review policy implementation and apply mitigation measures, to avoid any adverse impacts on the environment.	My officials are currently taking forward the further development of the beef carbon reduction measure including engaging with stakeholders to ensure, as far as possible, that no adverse behaviours, particularly on HNV land, are incentivised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the cross-departmental working group on biomethane production has been formed; and whether a cost-benefit analysis has been carried out on comparing biomethane from Anaerobic digestion with further electrification of heat and transport systems.
answer_text: DAERA chairs the Inter Departmental Biomethane Group (IDBG), which first convened on 2nd November 2021. The IDBG has met monthly since then and has provided bi-monthly updates to the AERA committee. The IDBG includes representatives from DAERA, DfE, DfI, the Utility Regulator, AFBI, NI Water, Strategic Investment Board and Invest NI. 		Energy and Transport are the remit of DfE and DfI respectively and DAERA has not carried out a cost-benefit analysis to compare biomethane from anaerobic digestion vs further electrification of heat and transport systems. This is primarily as biomethane is not intended by the IDBG as a replacement for electrification of heat or transport, but rather to compliment these technologies and others.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how action to address the climate and nature emergency will be funded.
answer_text: The previous Executive agreed a Green Growth Strategy which is being led by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. Individual Departments are progressing their plans to address the climate emergency. There is also a commitment within the Climate Change Bill to publish a Climate Action Plan (CAP) in 2023, setting out how interim targets and the net zero target will be achieved by 2050. This cross cutting plan and its subsequent implementation is critical for the North in terms of addressing climate change.  Departmental allocations will be confirmed as part of the budget, which requires an Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many circumcisions have been carried out in each of the last five years in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how many people in Belfast are currently waiting for this procedure to be carried out.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many people are currently working in home boarding businesses for pets.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the number of people currently working in home boarding businesses nor does it, or any other statutory agency, have a legal obligation to gather this information.		However, if my Department is made aware of any animal boarding establishment that is operating without a licence, officials visit the relevant premises to explain the legislative requirements and encourage compliance with them where this is possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many home boarding businesses for pets are deemed to be operating illegally.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the number of home boarding businesses currently in operation nor does it, or any other statutory agency, have a legal obligation to gather this information.		However, if my Department is made aware of any animal boarding establishment that is operating without a licence, officials visit the relevant premises to explain the legislative requirements and encourage compliance with them where this is possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what concerns his Department has about the long term safety and care of pets if home boarding businesses are forced to close.
answer_text: Complying with existing licensing requirements can be difficult for home boarding businesses. My Department has not, therefore, required businesses undertaking this activity to close but when notified that they are operating, works with them to encourage adherence to the licencing conditions where they are able to do so. My Department is not aware of any increased risk to the care and safety of the pets that attend these establishments or evidence to suggest that there would be a possible risk if they decided to cease trading of their own violation. In any event, pets continue to be protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 which imposes a duty of care on any person with permanent or temporary control of an animal to ensure that its needs are met.  My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes during this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what emergency legislative provision his Department can take to allow home boarding for pets businesses to remain open.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that home boarding business may find complying with current licensing difficult to achieve. However, my Department has not sought businesses undertaking this activity to cease trading.  Instead, it works closely with operators to encourage them to meet licencing conditions where they are able to do so.My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding in homes, during this mandate.  Any legislation brought forward on this matter will be subject to public consultation in accordance with established practice.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he and his officials have had with home boarding businesses for pets regarding licensing.
answer_text: Where my Department is made aware of a home boarding establishment that is operating without a licence, officials work closely with the operators to encourage compliance with licensing conditions, where these can be satisfied. 		My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many staff in the Civil Service have taken time off work in the last five years due to issues related to menopause.
answer_text: Sickness absence is recorded on the HR Connect system; there is not a specific absence type by which to identify menopause or menopause related absence. The Civil Service recognises that menopause is a transition that can last for a number of years, and that women going through menopause may experience a wide range of physical and emotional health symptoms. The Civil Service Menopause policy, introduced 8 March 2022, provides practical guidance for staff and line managers on adjustments to help staff cope with the impacts of menopausal symptoms. The Civil Service Menopause Policy can be viewed by clicking here. https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/NICS%20Menopause%20Policy.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many requests for adjustments to the workplace environment have been made by women going through menopause.
answer_text: The Civil Service does not hold figures centrally on requests for adjustments in the workplace. Adjustments are agreed at a local level between the staff member and line manager. The Civil Service Menopause policy, introduced 8 March 2022, provides practical guidance for staff and line managers on adjustments to help staff cope with the impacts of menopausal symptoms.  The Civil Service Menopause Policy can be viewed by clicking here. https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/NICS%20Menopause%20Policy.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what work the Civil Service has undertaken to ensure a policy exists across departments to support women going through menopause.
answer_text: The Civil Service Menopause Policy was developed by my Department and was launched on 8 March 2022, to coincide with International Women’s Day.  The policy sets out the Civil Service commitment to supporting women in the workplace, and it is recognition that women may need additional consideration, support and adjustments during the time of change before, during and after menopause.  It is intended that the policy will provide clarity and direction on how staff should deal with menopause related issues in the workplace and how managers can best provide support.  The Civil Service Menopause Policy can be viewed by clicking here. https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/NICS%20Menopause%20Policy.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many health and social care staff are currently receiving support from food banks.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the requested data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action she has taken to extend the application of prudential borrowing rules to the Housing Executive.
answer_text: Options which might enable the Housing Executive to access borrowing under prudential borrowing rules are included in considerations relating to the revitalisation of the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the meetings she or her officials have held with private finance investors about the future of the Housing Executive, including the names of potential investors.
answer_text: I have not held any meetings with private finance investors about the future of the Housing Executive and nor have my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how much was paid to private organisations to carry out maintenance and repair work in the Housing Executive over the last ten years.
answer_text: The total amount paid out to carry out essential maintenance and repair work in Housing Executive properties for local tenants over the last ten years is £1,291,867,000. This includes all planned improvement works, cyclical and response maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many engagements, since November 2020, she or her officials have held with the British Government about the future of the Housing Executive, including allowing it to borrow money as a public body.
answer_text: I am currently considering options to revitalise the Housing Executive. Once a preferred option has been identified then, should it require engagement with the British Government, that will proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the plans to revitalise the Housing Executive, as announced in November 2020.
answer_text: My officials have worked in partnership with the Housing Executive to produce and assess options to progress the revitalisation of the Housing Executive.I am currently considering advice on these options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work she has undertaken to remove or amend the two child limit on Child Tax Credit and Universal Credit.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work, the independent panel were specifically asked to consider support for families affected by the “two-child” policy in Universal Credit, Child Tax Credit and Housing Benefit.The implementation of any new payments will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of Poverty Truth Commission processes, as currently exist in Scotland, as a way to reflect the reality of living in poverty.
answer_text: An Anti-Poverty Truth Commission is one of a range of recommendations contained in the report of the Anti-Poverty Strategy Expert Advisory Panel, which was published on 5 March 2021.  The Panel’s report is being used to inform the development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy, and the recommendation on an Anti-Poverty Truth Commission is being considered in this context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the recommendations from the Discretionary Support and Welfare Mitigations reviews commissioned by her Department will be implemented.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of Discretionary Support has been published on my Department’s website.  I hope to make an announcement soon on how I propose to improve Discretionary Support by making it more accessible and ensuring that emergency financial support is there for people when they need it most.The independent review of welfare mitigations will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what schemes are currently in place to help address food poverty and the increased cost of food.
answer_text: The Department is committed to doing all within its power to support individuals, families and communities who are experiencing hardship during this cost of living crisis. I have instructed Officials to explore all potential support that can be offered by the Department and this work is ongoing. Any new potential measures will need to be supported by an accompanying budget which will require Executive sign off.My Department has been running a Social Supermarket Pilot Programme at five sites since October 2017. A Social Supermarket provides holistic support through the provision of food alongside wraparound services to address the root causes of poverty. This includes, but is not limited to, debt and benefits advice, budgeting, healthy eating, cooking on a budget and training and volunteering opportunities to enhance future employability.An award of short-term financial help may be made through the Discretionary Support scheme to assist with day to day living expenses such as food costs if a person is in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation. More information about the financial support available through the Discretionary Support scheme can be found at: Finance Support | nidirectA package of Cost of Living Support payments was announced by the British Chancellor on 26 May. This includes an additional payment of £650 for people on income-based benefits, £300 for pensioners and a £150 Disability Cost of Living Payment. More information about the Cost of Living Support payments can be found at Cost of Living support | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what conversations has he had with Westminster in relation to implementing an energy price cap.
answer_text: Following the removal of price regulation for electricity and gas, GB introduced a price cap on certain energy tariffs on 1 January 2019 to bring price protection to consumers by ensuring these tariffs reflect the underlying costs of supplying energy. The Northern Ireland energy market is very different from that in the rest of the UK and the current system of pricing for consumers is a combination of price regulation and competitive pricing.  There are no plans to introduce a price cap as our price regulation of the dominant suppliers in electricity and gas ensures that customers pay no more than the efficient costs of purchasing and supplying gas and electricity plus an agreed profit margin set by the NI Utility Regulator.  The regulated price then acts as a “price to beat” for the other suppliers.  My Department is involved in this tariff review process alongside the Utility Regulator, The Consumer Council for Northern Ireland and the respective supply company. The new Energy Strategy has committed to a review of existing consumer protection and redress provisions and my officials’ policy considerations will include looking at practice elsewhere.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many meetings about the commissioning of abortion services has he attended in last two years.
answer_text: In addition to briefings with officials within my Department, I have met with the Northern Ireland Under Secretary of State on seven occasions since May 2020 regarding the commissioning of abortion services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what conversations has he had with Westminster to implement a windfall tax on energy companies.
answer_text: I have not had any conversations with Westminster regarding this matter. Taxation remains a reserved matter and any consideration of a windfall tax on energy companies would be a matter for HM Treasury.		Furthermore, any NI representations to HM Treasury on taxation matters would be for the Department of Finance to lead on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people are waiting for ear wax removal services; and what is the average waiting time.
To ask the Minister for the Economy how many times he and his officials have met with Caterpillar (NI) Ltd. since 2010.
answer_text: A record of my meetings up to March 2022 are available online. From March 2022 to May 2022 I have not met with Caterpillar. 	 	My officials have met with Caterpillar on approximately 10 occasions  since the inception of the Department for the Economy in May 2016. Officials in Arms Length Bodies have also liaised with Caterpillar on numerous occasions since May 2016.		In addition to this, many ad-hoc communications, occasions of engagement and meetings have also taken place during this period, in order to exercise due diligence and undertake monitoring obligations as part of funding agreements and other matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the 2021 National Joint Council pay increase will be awarded.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the total amount of grants that Caterpillar (NI) Ltd., and its subsidiary or arms-length companies, have received from Invest NI since 2010.
answer_text: Since January 2010, Invest NI has offered £3,310,972 of direct financial assistance to Caterpillar (NI) Ltd in support of its operations in Larne, Monkstown and Springvale, Belfast.  Details of this assistance is set out below.Given the nature of Invest NI assistance, not all monies will have been claimed or paid and the values refer to the grant amount offered.	(Note: The company announced the closure of the Monkstown factory in September 2016)		Offer Date/Start DateAmount OfferedSelective Financial Assistance (Capital and Employment)*08-Feb-13£1,275,00018-Feb-15£1,087,050£2,362,050Company Development Programme /Business Improvement Training Programme (Skills)19 May 2014£349,04719 June 2014£599,875£948,922Total£3,310,972*The Selective Financial Assistance offer dated 8 Feb 2013 for £1,275,000 was abandoned in May 2017 at the request of the company as they did not expect to meet the criteria as laid out under the terms of the agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many staff are currently working in audiology services in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information provided by each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust on the number of staff working in audiology teams is detailed below.HSC TrustHeadcountWhole-time equivalentBelfast2521.26Northern 1614.3South Eastern1714.22Southern2621.21Western1110.87

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people are currently on a waiting list for audiology services; and how long is the average wait.
answer_text: Information on the average audiology waiting time is not available. However, information on waiting times (in bands) for active waits is available. The number of active audiology wait times, as at 30th April 2022, for Northern Ireland is shown in the table below. 0-3 Weeks>3 to 6 weeks>6 to 9 weeks>9 to 13 weeks> 13 weeksTotal Active WaitsNorthern Ireland Total3983622352837362,014Source: Health and Social Care TrustsThe Department collects and publishes audiology waiting times annually. The Audiology completed waits 2021/22 report was published on 23rd May 2022.and is available and published at the link provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what arrangements his Department has in place with independent providers or community organisations to provide audiology services.
answer_text: Non-recurrent funding of £46m has been made available to the Health and Social Care Trusts in the first six months of 2022/23 to help reduce waiting times for a range of elective care services, including diagnostic tests. This funding is being used to secure additional capacity across a range of elective services including audiology. Health and Social Care Trusts have been allocated £50k for the first quarter of 2022/23 and a further £87k will be allocated for the second quarter to deliver additional audiology services through waiting list initiatives during the first six months of 2022/23.In addition, there is a range of voluntary organisations that offer advice, information and services for deaf, hard of hearing people and the general public. Information on hearing and ear care can be accessed via the NI Direct website: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/hearing-and-ear-care

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work her Department has commissioned to implement a child payment benefit, similar to that which has been introduced in Scotland.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package for the North. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the impact that the removal of the £20 Universal Credit uplift has had on poverty levels.
answer_text: The removal of the £20 uplift in Universal Credit has no doubt caused hardship for many struggling families. The decision by the British Government was outrageous. I consider the decision unjust and morally reprehensible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of Unite the Union and and GMB Bus Driver Trade Union’s proposed industrial action over below inflation pay offers from Translink.
answer_text: I welcome the constructive engagement between Translink and the representative unions which has led to Translink bringing forward a revised pay offer for its bus drivers, cleaners and shunters for the 2021/22 year.  Public transport services are a very important part of everyday life for our citizens and I am pleased that disruption to our bus services has been averted at this time pending the outcome of a ballot on the revised offer. The offer made by Translink acknowledges Trade Union concerns over increasing inflation and the cost of living, while also supporting wider business needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the need for a windfall tax on energy companies to help with the rise in the cost of living.
answer_text: As taxation remains a reserved matter, any assessement of a windfall tax on energy companies would be a matter for HM Treasury consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work he has undertaken to look at implementing price controls for food given the rising cost of living.
answer_text: It is not within the vires of my Department to introduce price controls for food and therefore no work has been undertaken by my Department to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans to expand and enhance women’s refuge services for homeless women, as announced in March 2022.
answer_text: The safety and well-being of vulnerable women who require this vital service is a key priority for the Department and Officials are working with the Housing Executive to ensure that a new women’s only facility is in place as soon as possible. Officials have been directed to work at speed on the delivery of dedicated accommodation services, particularly for those women experiencing chronic homelessness.For anyone who requires assistance in the interim, there are a range of statutory services in place to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what correspondence he has had with Caterpillar (NI) Ltd. in relation to its staff taking industrial action.
answer_text: I am aware of the ongoing industrial action at Caterpillar sites in Belfast and Larne. To date, I have had no correspondence from either the company or  unions representing the workers on this matter.
